related.title: か
related.meaning: Or
related.comparison: ～なり～なり is a somewhat formal or literary expression used to provide examples, typically implying additional options or suggesting that the speaker doesn't mind which option is chosen. In contrast, the question particle か is used after nouns to present alternatives or offer a choice between two or more items (e.g., 今日か明日休みます = 'I'll take off today or tomorrow').

related.title: たり～たりする
related.meaning: Things like ~ and ~
related.comparison: Both たり～たりする and ～なり～なり list example actions, like 'doing things like A and B.' たり is a neutral, commonly used pattern in casual speech. In contrast, なり～なり is more formal and carries a stronger nuance that the examples are just possibilities, often implying that the speaker doesn't care which one is chosen.

related.title: とか～とか
related.meaning: Among other things, For example, Such as
related.comparison: While both ～なり～なり and とか～とか present examples, they differ in formality and nuance. ～なり～なり is more formal and presents possible choices without preference. とか～とか is casual and used in everyday speech to mean 'things like (A) and (B),' often implying there are other possible examples.

synonyms.title: のなかで
synonyms.comparison: ～のうち(で) and のなかで are similar expressions that both mean 'among', but their usage can differ. ～のうち(で) is most often used with number + counter phrases. For example, 5人のうち4人が合格した ('Out of 5 people, 4 passed.'). With non-numerical groupings, like スポーツの中でどれが一番好き？ ('Among sports, which do you like best?'), のなかで is more common, especially in speech.

related.title: 同士
related.meaning: Fellow, With each other, Mutually, Between, Among, Together, With
related.comparison: Both ～のうち(で) and 同士 describe relationships within a group, but differ in nuance. ～のうち(で) indicates selection from a group, e.g. ３人のうちで誰が一番早い？ ('Who is the fastest among the three?'). 同士 emphasizes mutual relationships or shared characteristics, e.g. 学生同士で助け合う ('Help each other as students').

related.title: のなかで～がいちばん～
related.meaning: Out of ~, ~ is the best/most ~
related.comparison: Both ～のうち(で) and のなかで express the idea of 'among' or 'out of,' used when selecting or describing something from a group. のうち is often (though not exclusively) used with number + counter phrases, as in 10人のうち5人が車を持っている ('Out of 10 people, 5 own a car'). のなかで is more common and casual in speech, while のうち has a slightly more formal tone.

related.title: ～は～の一つだ
related.meaning: 〜 is one of (many) 〜
related.comparison: Both ～のうち(で) and ～は～の一つだ are used to identify things within a group or category. ～のうち(で) is most often paired with number + counter phrases to mean 'among', for example, ５人のうち４人が合格した ('Out of 5 people, 4 passed.'). In contrast, ～は～の一つだ identifies something as part of a group, e.g., バナナは果物の一つだ ('A banana is one type of fruit.').

related.title: ～ばこそ
related.meaning: Precisely because, Only because, Because, Due to, Only if
related.comparison: Both use the emphatic こそ but differ by verb form. ～ばこそ is conditional, meaning only if (A), then (B), as in 忙しければこそ 'precisely because you're busy'. ～てこそ focuses on completion, meaning only after (A) happens, then (B), as in 苦労してこそ成功がある ('Only after hardship comes success'). ～ばこそ can introduce a hypothetical condition, while ～てこそ emphasizes the actual realization of (A).

related.title: あっての
related.meaning: Exist only because of or due to, Owe everything to
related.comparison: These are both emphatic expressions that can be used in similar contexts to express 'it's entirely because of (A) that (B)'. あっての is narrower in use, saying that something was possible only because something else 'exists', e.g. 皆さんの努力あっての成功だ ('This success is entirely because of everyone's effort.'). てこそ can be used with a wider variety of verbs, such as 留学してこそ学べること ('things you can only learn by studying abroad'.).

related.title: からこそ
related.meaning: Emphasized because, Precisely because
related.comparison: Both からこそ and ～てこそ emphasize cause and effect, but they differ in nuance. からこそ highlights a specific reason with a strong emphasis, e.g. 努力したからこそ合格できた ('I was able to pass precisely because I worked hard'). In contrast, ～てこそ indicates that only after a certain condition is fulfilled can something else happen, e.g. 努力してこそ意味がある ('It only has meaning after putting in effort').

related.title: こそ
related.meaning: For sure, For certain, Even more so, Underlined or bolded text
related.comparison: Both involve the same こそ, but one attaches to nouns and the other to verb + て form. こそ emphasizes a specific word or element, often to contrast or highlight, e.g. 彼こそできる ('He, if nobody else, can do it.'). In contrast, ～てこそ focuses a condition that must be met for something to be true, e.g. 大人になってこそ分かる 'understand only after becoming an adult'.

related.title: ～ても～なくても
related.meaning: Whether ~ or not
related.comparison: ～ても～なくても and ようが～まいが are identical in meaning, both expressing the idea of 'whether or not', with the difference that ～ても～なくても is more casual and colloquial, while ようが～まいが is more formal/literary and emphatic. For example, 行っても行かなくても and 行こうが行くまいが mean the same thing but differ in tone.

related.title: てでも
related.meaning: Even if I have to, By any or all means, No matter what
related.comparison: Both can show determination against conditions, but differ in use. てでも expresses strong will to do something even at personal cost, as in 雨に濡れてでも行きたい (I want to go even if I get drenched). ようが～まいが lists two alternatives and insists the result won’t change, as in 雨が降ろうが降るまいが試合は行われる (Whether it rains or not, the match will be held).

related.title: とも
related.meaning: Even if, No matter
related.comparison: Both mean even if or no matter if. とも states a single condition in a slightly formal style, as in 雨が降ろうとも行く (I’ll go even if it rains). ようが～まいが is more emphatic, presenting contrasting alternatives, as in 雨が降ろうが降るまいが行く (I’ll go whether it rains or not).

related.title: たって
related.meaning: Even if, Even though, No matter how
related.comparison: Both show even if, but with different tone. たって is casual and conversational, equivalent to ても, as in 子どもだって分かる (Even a child would understand). ようが～まいが is formal and emphatic, often with contrasting options, as in 成功しようが失敗しようが挑戦する (Whether we succeed or fail, we’ll try).

related.title: ても
related.meaning: Even if, Even though
related.comparison: ても is an extremely versatile expression used to mean 'even if' in a wide variety of contexts, both in everyday speech and writing. In contrast, ようが～まいが is a highly formal expression that specifically means 'whether or not' and occurs primarily in written or academic language. For example 食べようが食べまいが and 食べても食べなくても both mean 'whether or not (you) eat', but the former is far more formal and emphatic.

related.title: です
related.meaning: To be, Is
related.comparison: ～んです・のです are used to clarify context, provide background information, give reasons, or express curiosity. In contrast, です without の or ん is a simple declarative form used to state facts or general statements. Using ～んです・のです adds emphasis, conveys intention, or creates a more explanatory tone by suggesting a larger context.

related.title: んだけど・んですが
related.meaning: But..., And...
related.comparison: ～んです・のです adds an explanatory tone to a statement, like 難しいんです ('It's difficult, I'm telling you.'). When paired with contrastive particles like けど or が, the speaker often implies something left unsaid, as in 難しいんですけど ('It's hard, but...', implying 'and that's why I can't do it', etc.).

antonyms.title: Verb[volitional]とする
antonyms.meaning: Try to, Attempt to, To be about to
antonyms.comparison: These two expressions are opposite uses of the same structure. Verb[volitional]とする means 'Try to do (A)' or 'Make an attempt at (A)', often with the nuance that the attempt may fail, e.g. 食べようとしたけど、無理だった 'I tried to eat it, but I couldn't.' In contrast, 〜ようとしない means 'Not even try to (A)', e.g. うちの息子は、野菜を食べようとしない 'My son doesn't even try to eat vegetables.'

antonyms.title: ようにする
antonyms.meaning: To try to, To make sure to, To endeavor to
antonyms.comparison: ようにする (literally, 'do things in such a way as to X') can express the idea of taking care to perform a certain action and often describes intentions or habits. 〜ようとしない conveys the opposite, not making an effort to do something. For example, 毎日野菜を食べるようにしている ('I'm making a habit of eating vegetables every day.') vs. うちの子どもは野菜を食べようとしない ('My child doesn't even try to eat vegetables.').

related.title: Verb[よう]
related.meaning: Let's, I shall (Casual volitional)
related.comparison: Both of these use Verb [よう], the volitional form. By itself, it expresses a suggestion, e.g. 食べよう ('Let's eat'.). Combined with としない, it means 'doesn't even try to,' e.g. 野菜を食べようとしない ('He/she doesn't even make an effort to eat vegetables.'). The key difference is between making a proposal vs. showing a lack of intent or effort.

synonyms.title: ことができる
synonyms.comparison: Both express potential or ability, like 'can do'. ことができる is more formal and common in writing, e.g. 書くことができる 'can write', while the potential form れる・られる is shorter and used in conversation, e.g. 書ける 'can write'. The meaning is the same, but usage differs by tone and context.

related.title: Verb［れる・られる］
related.meaning: Was done to ~ by ~, Done without consent, Done to one's dismay
related.comparison: Passive and potential forms have different meanings but can be confusing because some forms overlap. For う-verbs, the forms are distinct. For example, 言われる 'to be said' (passive) vs. 言える means 'can say' (potential). For る-verbs, a form like 食べられる can mean either 'to be eaten' (passive) or 'can eat' (potential), and which is intended must be determined from context.

related.title: 聞こえる
related.meaning: To be audible, To sound (like), (Can) hear, To be heard
related.comparison: Both 聞こえる and 聞ける can mean 'can hear,' but differ in nuance. 聞こえる refers to a sound being audible, while 聞ける indicates the ability to hear or listen. For example, a bird chirping nearby would be 聞こえる, but being able to stream a song would be 聞ける.

related.title: 見える
related.meaning: To be visible, To seem, To be in sight
related.comparison: Both 見える and 見られる mean 'can see' (potential form) but their nuances differ. 見える describes something being visible, while 見られる refers to the ability to view something. For example, stars in the sky are 見える, but being able to watch a movie on a streaming service would be 見られる.

related.title: 得る・得る
related.meaning: Is able to, Can, Possible
related.comparison: 得る・得る and the れる・られる (Potential) form both indicate possibility or ability, but differ in nuance. れる・られる is used for general ability, as in 話せる 'can speak', and is conversational. 得る is more formal and written-style, and is typically that something is possible or conceivable, like 起こり得る ('It could happen.').

related.title: れる・られる (Potential)
related.meaning: To be able, Can (Potential)
related.comparison: Passive and potential forms have different meanings but can be confusing because some forms overlap. For う-verbs, the forms are distinct. For example, 言われる 'to be said' (passive) vs. 言える means 'can say' (potential). For る-verbs, a form like 食べられる can mean either 'to be eaten' (passive) or 'can eat' (potential), and which is intended must be determined from context.

related.title: Verb［せる・させる］
related.meaning: To make/let/have (somebody do something)
related.comparison: The passive (Verb［れる・られる］) and causative (Verb［せる・させる］) forms are both fundamental in Japanese. The passive shows that an action was done to someone, while the causative means to make or let someone do something. For example, 食べられた 'was eaten' vs. 食べさせた 'made/let someone eat'.

related.title: Causative-Passive
related.meaning: Verbs (Causative-passive voice), To be made to do something, To be forced to do
related.comparison: The passive (Verb［れる・られる］) and causative-passive are two key verb forms in Japanese. The passive expresses that an action was done to someone, while the causative-passive means being made or forced to do something. For example, 食べられた 'was eaten' vs. 食べさせられた 'were made to eat'.

related.title: れる・られる + ままに
related.meaning: Do as (one is told or ordered…), In accordance with, In compliance with, Just like
related.comparison: The passive form (Verb［れる・られる］) is used to show that an action was done to someone. For example, 言われた means 'was told'. When combined with ままに 'in that state', it can express following instructions exactly, e.g. 言われるままに買った ('I bought it just as I was told.').

related.title: 他動詞・自動詞
related.meaning: Done through action vs. occurring on its own
related.comparison: 他動詞 (transitive) verbs act on something, while 自動詞 (intransitive) verbs describe actions that happen by themselves. The passive form (Verb［れる・られる］) can make transitive verbs behave like intransitive ones. For example, ケーキを食べた ('I ate cake.') vs. ケーキが食べられた ('The cake was eaten.'). But when the speaker is affected, the object stays, as in 兄にケーキを食べられた ('I had my cake eaten by my brother.').

related.title: Adjective + て・Noun + で
related.meaning: And... (Conjunctive)
related.comparison: Both of these patterns involve connecting an adjective or noun phrase to the rest of the sentence using the て form. The meaning of the connection depends on context. Sometimes it simply links words, as in 大きくてきれい ('big and pretty'), while in other cases, the first part may provide a reason for the second, as in 甘くておいしい ('sweet and tasty').

related.title: し～し
related.meaning: And, Giving reasons
related.comparison: し～し lists multiple reasons, similar to 'because of A, B, and so on' in English. In contrast, Adjective + て + B just connects adjectives. While it can imply the first quality supports the second (e.g. 甘くておいしい = 'sweet and delicious'), it doesn't suggest a broader conclusion. し often implies one, as in 甘いし、おいしいし, meaning 'It's sweet and delicious… (so I like it).'

synonyms.title: のはXの方だ
synonyms.meaning: It is...that..., It is...who..., ...is the one that..., ...is the one who...
synonyms.comparison: Both structures single out and highlight something. That is, expressing '(A) is the one that (B)'. Adjective + の(は) is the simpler form, e.g. 新しいのはどっち？ ('Which one is new?') のはXの方だ expands on this to specify and contrast. A common use is in insistent apologies, e.g. いいえ、謝らないといけないのは僕のほうだ ('No, I'm the one who needs to apologize.')

synonyms.title: の
synonyms.meaning: Verb nominalizer, The one who..., That which...
synonyms.comparison: Both of these patterns use の to turn a phrase into a noun. For example, the verb 走る ('to run') becomes 走るのが好き ('I like running') because the subject marker が must follow a noun. The Adjective + の(は) pattern works similarly, turning adjectives into noun phrases (e.g., 赤い ('red') becomes 赤いのはきれいです ('The red one is pretty')).

related.title: Particle + の
related.meaning: Combined particles
related.comparison: Particle + の is used when particles like から, と, へ, で, or まで combine with の to describe a noun. For example, 先生への手紙 means 'a letter to (my) teacher,' and 駅までの道 means 'the road to the station.' Adjective + の(は) is a pattern where の replaces a noun, allowing the adjective to form a descriptive phrase. For example, 高いのはこれです means 'This is the expensive one.'

related.title: Verb［れる・られる］
related.meaning: Was done to ~ by ~, Done without consent, Done to one's dismay
related.comparison: The passive (Verb［れる・られる］) and causative (Verb［せる・させる］) forms are both fundamental in Japanese. The passive shows that an action was done to someone, while the causative means to make or let someone do something. For example, 食べられた 'was eaten' vs. 食べさせた 'made/let someone eat'.

related.title: 他動詞・自動詞
related.meaning: Done through action vs. occurring on its own
related.comparison: 他動詞 (transitive) verbs act on something, while 自動詞 (intransitive) verbs describe actions that happen by themselves. The causative form means to make or let someone do something, so intransitive verbs become transitive when changed, e.g. 人が走る 'a person runs' vs. 人を走らせる 'make a person run'.

related.title: Causative-Passive
related.meaning: Verbs (Causative-passive voice), To be made to do something, To be forced to do
related.comparison: The causative (Verb［せる・させる］) and the causative-passive are two related verb forms. Verb［せる・させる］ is used to express either 'letting' or 'making/forcing' someone do something. For example, the causative of 行く 'go' is 行かせる 'let/make (someone) go'. The causative-passive is the passive form of this, meaning 'to be made to do (A)', e.g., 行かせられた (or the contracted form 行かされた) means 'I was made/forced to go.'.

related.title: Verb + て+ B
related.meaning: And, And then, After that (sequence)
related.comparison: Adjective + て, Noun + で, and Verb + て+ B are the same fundamental pattern, using the て form to connect elements in a sentence. The construction of the て form depends on whether the word is an い-Adjective, な-Adjective, noun, or verb, but the function remains consistent, similar to the English 'and.'

related.title: なくて
related.meaning: To not ~ and ~, Not ~ and ~ (Conjugations)
related.comparison: Adjective + て or Noun + で is used to connect adjective or noun phrases with the rest of the sentence, similar to the English 'and.' なくて is similar, but connects phrases that are in the negative (ない) form. For example, 安くてよかった means 'I'm glad it was inexpensive,' while 高くなくてよかった would mean 'I'm glad that it wasn't expensive'.

related.title: 連用形
related.meaning: Formal Conjunctive
related.comparison: 連用形 also replaces て or で after adjectives and nouns in formal writing. For example, 優しくて becomes 優しく, e.g. とても優しくて、綺麗です 'She's very kind, and pretty.' → とても優しく、綺麗です. (Same meaning, but more formal and literary.)

related.title: Adjective + て + B
related.meaning: And..., Both and (Conjunctive)
related.comparison: Both of these patterns involve connecting an adjective or noun phrase to the rest of the sentence using the て form. The meaning of the connection depends on context. Sometimes it simply links words, as in 大きくてきれい ('big and pretty'), while in other cases, the first part may provide a reason for the second, as in 甘くておいしい ('sweet and tasty').

related.title: Verb[て] + B
related.meaning: And・and so, Due to, Because of, Since, Conjunctive
related.comparison: Both of these patterns involve connecting a word or phrase (adjectives and nouns in one case, verbs in the other) to the rest of the sentence using the て form. The meaning of the connection depends on context. Sometimes it simply links words, as in 大きくてきれい ('big and pretty'), while in other cases, the て form expresses reason or method, as in 歩いて行った ('went by walking').

related.title: あまり
related.meaning: So much...that
related.comparison: Both emphasize extremity, but with different focuses. Adjective + 限りだ expresses a strong feeling at its peak, e.g. 悲しい限りだ ('I'm as sad as can be.') あまり indicates that excess leads to a (usually undesirable) result, e.g. 悲しみのあまり声が出なかった ('I couldn't speak due to extreme sadness.')

related.title: てならない
related.meaning: Very, Extremely, Can't help but do
related.comparison: Both convey extremity of feeling. Adj限りだ attaches to adjectives for maximum intensity, as in 嬉しい限りだ (I’m extremely happy). てならない attaches to adjectives or verbs for uncontrollable emotions, as in 不安でならない (I can’t help but feel uneasy).

related.title: てたまらない
related.meaning: Can't help but want to, Very, Extremely
related.comparison: Both convey extremes but differ in tone. Adj限りだ attaches to adjectives to show feelings at the utmost, often formal, as in 嬉しい限りだ (I’m extremely happy). てたまらない highlights intense emotions or desires that feel unbearable, used more in casual speech, as in 会いたくてたまらない (I miss you so much I can’t bear it).

related.title: Verb［れる・られる］
related.meaning: Was done to ~ by ~, Done without consent, Done to one's dismay
related.comparison: The passive (Verb［れる・られる］) and causative-passive are two key verb forms in Japanese. The passive expresses that an action was done to someone, while the causative-passive means being made or forced to do something. For example, 食べられた 'was eaten' vs. 食べさせられた 'were made to eat'.

related.title: Verb［せる・させる］
related.meaning: To make/let/have (somebody do something)
related.comparison: The causative (Verb［せる・させる］) and the causative-passive are two related verb forms. Verb［せる・させる］ is used to express either 'letting' or 'making/forcing' someone do something. For example, the causative of 行く 'go' is 行かせる 'let/make (someone) go'. The causative-passive is the passive form of this, meaning 'to be made to do (A)', e.g., 行かせられた (or the contracted form 行かされた) means 'I was made/forced to go.'.

related.title: じゃない
related.meaning: Is not, Isn't
related.comparison: い-Adjectives form a negative by replacing い with くない (e.g., 赤い = 'it's red,' 赤くない = 'it's not red'). For nouns and な-Adjectives, the negative is formed by adding じゃない (e.g., 大変じゃない = 'it's not difficult,' バナナじゃない = 'it's not a banana'). Both forms are plain and used in casual conversation or other grammatical patterns.

synonyms.title: Verb + まで
synonyms.comparison: The particle まで has the core meaning of 'as far as.' With nouns, it can mean 'until' with time expressions (e.g. 月曜まで休む = 'take off until Monday') or 'up to' with locations (e.g. 駅まで送る = 'see [someone] off to the station'). When used after a plain-form verb, it means 'until A happens. For example, 友達が来るまでテレビを見ていた = 'I was watching TV until my friend came.'

related.title: まで
related.meaning: Even, To even, To the extent of
related.comparison: The particle まで has the core meaning of 'as far as.' It's commonly used with time expressions to mean 'until' (e.g. 月曜まで休む = 'rest until Monday') and with places to mean 'up to' or 'to (a location)' (e.g. 駅まで送る = 'see [someone] off to the station'). When used with verbs or non-location nouns, it can mean 'even' or 'as much as' (e.g. 骨まで食べる = 'eat even the bones').

related.title: までに
related.meaning: By ~, By the time that ~, Before ~
related.comparison: The particle まで means 'until' when used with a time expression and a verb describing an ongoing action. For example, 8時までテレビを見る means 'watch TV until 8 o'clock.' In contrast, までに means 'by' and is used to express that an action is completed before a certain time. For example, 8時までに終わる means 'finish by 8 o'clock'.

related.title: 風
related.meaning: -style, Way, Fashion
related.comparison: Both describe style or appearance, but with different nuance. 型 refers to a concrete type, model, or category, e.g. 新型のスマホ ('A new model of smartphone') 風 suggests resemblance or outward style, often subjective or superficial, e.g. 昔風の家 ('A house in the old-fashioned style')

related.title: 的
related.meaning: ~ly・~like・~al, Typical
related.comparison: Both attach to nouns to form descriptive modifiers, but differ in meaning. 型 refers to a fixed or functional type, e.g. 日本型の経営 ('The Japanese style of management') 的 turns nouns into な-adjectives meaning 'related to' or 'having the quality of', e.g. 経済 → 経済的な問題 ('An economic problem')

related.title: らしい ②
related.meaning: typical of, -ish・like, appropriate for, becoming of, befitting
related.comparison: Both describe attributes, but with different focuses. 型 emphasizes form or category, e.g. 家族型の会社 ('A family-style company') らしい expresses what seems typical or characteristic of something, often based on impression, e.g. 家族らしい雰囲気 ('A family-like atmosphere', warm and loving, as one might expect from a family)

antonyms.title: Number + も
antonyms.comparison: Both forms emphasize quantity, but with contrasting nuance. Number/Amount + は implies 'at least' that amount, with a contrastive tone. For example, ３０分はかかると思うけど ('I think it'll take at least 30 minutes.', implying that it won't be less, but might be more.). In contrast, Number + も emphasizes the amount as if the speaker believes it is a lot, e.g., ３０分もかかった ('It took thirty whole minutes.').

related.title: なん + counter + か
related.meaning: Some, Several, A few
related.comparison: These patterns are similar in that both combine a counting expression with a particle. Number/Amount + は has a contrastive nuance, implying 'at least' that amount. For example, １０人は来ると思うけど ('I think at least 10 people will come.', implying the speaker is uncertain if any more will.). In contrast, なん + counter + か means 'a few (A)' or 'several (A)', e.g. 何人か 'a few people.'

related.title: は
related.meaning: As for... (Highlights sentence topic)
related.comparison: When は follows a number or amount, it often adds a contrastive nuance like 'at least' or 'no more than.' For example, １回は行ったけど means 'I went once, but no more than that.' This is similar to contrastive uses of は, like in 田中さんは行ったけど ('Tanaka went, but no one else did'). If there's no contrast, the number is usually used without a particle, as in 三つ買った ('I bought three').

antonyms.title: Number + も
antonyms.comparison: Number + も emphasizes a large quantity, like 'as many as (A)' or 'a whole (A)' in English. In contrast, Number + しか〜ない emphasizes a small quantity. For example, おにぎりを３個も食べた ('I ate three whole rice balls.') vs. おにぎり１個しか食べなかった ('I only ate one rice ball.').

related.title: しか～ない
related.meaning: There's only, Nothing but
related.comparison: しか～ない expresses the meaning of 'only' or 'nothing but'. For example, バナナしか食べなかった ('I only ate a banana', or literally 'I didn't eat anything but a banana.'). When used with numbers in the Number + しか〜ない pattern, it emphasizes that the quantity is small, e.g., ５人しか来なかった ('Only 5 people came.', or literally 'No one came but five people.').

antonyms.title: Number + しか〜ない
antonyms.meaning: No more than, Not exceeding, As few as・as little as, Only
antonyms.comparison: Number + も emphasizes a large quantity, like 'as many as (A)' or 'a whole (A)' in English. In contrast, Number + しか〜ない emphasizes a small quantity. For example, おにぎりを３個も食べた ('I ate three whole rice balls.') vs. おにぎり１個しか食べなかった ('I only ate one rice ball.').

antonyms.title: Number/Amount + は
antonyms.meaning: At least, Sort of, Or so
antonyms.comparison: Both forms emphasize quantity, but with contrasting nuance. Number/Amount + は implies 'at least' that amount, with a contrastive tone. For example, ３０分はかかると思うけど ('I think it'll take at least 30 minutes.', implying that it won't be less, but might be more.). In contrast, Number + も emphasizes the amount as if the speaker believes it is a lot, e.g., ３０分もかかった ('It took thirty whole minutes.').

related.title: からする
related.meaning: About, Or more, Over, Starts at
related.comparison: からする can be thought of as a more formal equivalent of もする when following number (or number+counter) expressions, e.g. 100万円からする時計 ('a watch that costs as much as 1,000,000 yen') vs. 100万円もする時計 (same meaning, but more colloquial).

related.title: なん + counter + か
related.meaning: Some, Several, A few
related.comparison: These patterns are similar in that both combine a counting expression with a particle. なん + counter + か combines the question word 何 (なん or なに) with a counter to mean 'a few (A)' or 'some amount of (A)', for example 何人か 'a few people.' In contrast, Number + も emphasizes that the number is large, e.g. １０００人も来た ('A whole 1000 people came.')

related.title: の
related.meaning: Verb nominalizer, The one who..., That which...
related.comparison: In the phrase AのB, の indicates a relationship between A and B, either possession (e.g., ジョンの本 = 'John's book') or description (e.g., 日本人の学生 = 'A Japanese student'). Particle + の is a variation where a phrase with a particle describes another noun (e.g., 先生からの手紙 = 'A letter from the teacher'). Note that particles like に, も, は, が, and を are typically not used in this construction.

related.title: Adjective + の(は)
related.meaning: The 'one' that... (Indefinite pronoun, Adjective nominalization)
related.comparison: Particle + の is used when particles like から, と, へ, で, or まで combine with の to describe a noun. For example, 先生への手紙 means 'a letter to (my) teacher,' and 駅までの道 means 'the road to the station.' Adjective + の(は) is a pattern where の replaces a noun, allowing the adjective to form a descriptive phrase. For example, 高いのはこれです means 'This is the expensive one.'

related.title: かどうか
related.meaning: Whether or not, If...or not
related.comparison: Both are embedded question forms. A question phrase plus か makes an indirect question, like 何時に来るか分からない ('I don't know what time they'll come'). かどうか focuses specifically on yes/no questions, like 来るかどうか分からない ('I don't know whether or not they'll come.').

related.title: 誰か・どこか・誰も・どこも
related.slug: 誰か-どこか-誰も-どこも
related.meaning: Someone, Somewhere, Not anyone, Not anywhere
related.comparison: The 誰か・どこか・誰も・どこも pattern combines question words like 誰 ('who') and どこ ('where') with particles to express ideas like 'someone' or 'somewhere.' These forms are also related to how the particle か is used with question phrases in embedded questions. For example, 誰が来るか分かりますか？ means 'Do you know who will come?'

related.title: か〜ないかのうちに
related.meaning: Right after, As soon as, Just when, Barely
related.comparison: Question-phrase + か embeds a question, e.g. 来るか分からない ('I don't know if they're coming.'). か〜ないかのうちに expresses something happening immediately at a moment of uncertainty, e.g. 来るか来ないかのうちに 'just as they arrived'.

related.title: か
related.meaning: Or
related.comparison: Both uses of か are extensions of its basic function as a question particle. When following a Wh-question word (like who, what, where, why, how), it creates a phrase meaning 'some-(word)' (e.g., 誰か = 'someone,' どこか = 'somewhere,' いつか = 'sometime'). When following regular nouns, it means 'or' (e.g., 今日か明日休みます = 'I'll take off today or tomorrow').

related.title: なにか・なにも
related.meaning: Something, Nothing, Anything, Not anything, Some kind of, Not any kind of
related.comparison: The なにか・なにも pattern combines the question word なに with particles to express ideas like 'something' or 'nothing.' This structure is based on how the particle か is used with question words within a sentence. For example, 何が食べたいか分かりますか？ means 'Do you know what (they) want to eat?'

related.title: Verb［せる・させる］
related.meaning: To make/let/have (somebody do something)
related.comparison: 他動詞 (transitive) verbs act on something, while 自動詞 (intransitive) verbs describe actions that happen by themselves. The causative form means to make or let someone do something, so intransitive verbs become transitive when changed, e.g. 人が走る 'a person runs' vs. 人を走らせる 'make a person run'.

related.title: Verb［れる・られる］
related.meaning: Was done to ~ by ~, Done without consent, Done to one's dismay
related.comparison: 他動詞 (transitive) verbs act on something, while 自動詞 (intransitive) verbs describe actions that happen by themselves. The passive form (Verb［れる・られる］) can make transitive verbs behave like intransitive ones. For example, ケーキを食べた ('I ate cake.') vs. ケーキが食べられた ('The cake was eaten.'). But when the speaker is affected, the object stays, as in 兄にケーキを食べられた ('I had my cake eaten by my brother.').

synonyms.title: Noun + まで
synonyms.meaning: Until, Till,  To (but no further than)
synonyms.comparison: The particle まで has the core meaning of 'as far as.' With nouns, it can mean 'until' with time expressions (e.g. 月曜まで休む = 'take off until Monday') or 'up to' with locations (e.g. 駅まで送る = 'see [someone] off to the station'). When used after a plain-form verb, it means 'until A happens. For example, 友達が来るまでテレビを見ていた = 'I was watching TV until my friend came.'

related.title: までに
related.meaning: By ~, By the time that ~, Before ~
related.comparison: The particle まで means 'until' when used with a verb that can describe a progressive or ongoing action, e.g. 8時までテレビを見る 'watch TV until eight o'clock'. In contrast, までに means 'by', 'before', or 'no later than', and is used with verbs that describe actions with a set completion point, e.g. 8時までに終わる 'to finish by/before eight o'clock.'

related.title: まで
related.meaning: Even, To even, To the extent of
related.comparison: The particle まで has a core meaning of 'as far as' or 'to the extent of.' With verbs, it often means 'until A happens'—for example, 友達が来るまでテレビを見ていた ('I was watching TV until my friend came'). In other cases, it can mean 'even,' as in 骨まで食べた ('He even ate the bones'). Both uses reflect the core idea of reaching a certain extent or limit.

synonyms.title: まま(に)
synonyms.meaning: As is, While remaining, Left in a state
synonyms.comparison: Both patterns use まま, but れる・られる + ままに expresses acting in line with orders or circumstances, e.g. 言われるがままにする ('Do exactly as told'). In contrast, standalone まま(に) is used in a wider range of contexts to express an unchanged state, e.g. 窓が開けたままになっている ('The window is still open'). The compound implies passivity; the base form, a persistent state.

related.title: に沿って
related.meaning: Along, In accordance with, In line with
related.comparison: Both convey doing something in accordance with something else, but differ in nuance. れる・られる + ままに implies doing something exactly as told or forced, with little choice, as in 指示されるがままにやった (I did it exactly as instructed). に沿って simply means in line with a tangible or figurative standard, as in 点線に沿って (along the dotted line) or スケジュールに沿って (in line with the schedule), without implying lack of control.

related.title: とおり
related.meaning: In that way, Exactly, Precisely, Just as
related.comparison: Both convey the idea of doing something in precisely a certain way, but differ in nuance. れる・られる + ままに implies passivity or being compelled by external forces. For example, 言われるがままにやった ('I did as told'). とおり can be used similarly, as in 言われたとおりにやった ('I did it just as I was told'), but also appears in neutral contexts. For example, レシピのとおりに作った ('I made it exactly according to the recipe').

related.title: Verb［れる・られる］
related.meaning: Was done to ~ by ~, Done without consent, Done to one's dismay
related.comparison: The passive form (Verb［れる・られる］) is used to show that an action was done to someone. For example, 言われた means 'was told'. When combined with ままに 'in that state', it can express following instructions exactly, e.g. 言われるままに買った ('I bought it just as I was told.').

related.title: にしたがって
related.meaning: As, Following, As then, According to, In accordance with
related.comparison: れる・られる + ままに implies doing something in accordance with external directions, often with little control, as in 指示されるがままに (exactly as instructed). にしたがって also means in accordance with, as in 規則に従って (in accordance with the regulations), but does not necessarily imply lack of volition.

related.title: ように
related.meaning: So that, In order to, In such a way that
related.comparison: Both structures express doing something in a certain way. れる・られる + ままに is used when someone acts as they're told, e.g. 言われるがままに 'just as told'. ように can express acting of one's own accord, e.g. 間に合うように早めに出た ('I left early so I could make it on time.').

related.title: る-Verb (Dictionary)
related.meaning: る + ます (Ichidan Verbs)
related.comparison: In Japanese, the dictionary form and the non-past form of verbs are identical. For example, 食べる is both the dictionary form and the non-past form of the verb 'to eat.' Unlike in English, this form can be used as is to express future actions ('I will eat'), habitual actions ('I regularly eat / I eat everyday'), or general truths ('Lions eat meat').

related.title: る-Verb (Past)
related.meaning: る - Verbs (Past tense)
related.comparison: The non-past (or dictionary) form expresses habitual or future actions, while the past form expresses completed actions or past states. For る-Verbs, change the final る to た to form the past tense. Both the non-past and past forms are casual, non-polite forms used in conversation or other grammar patterns.

related.title: る-Verb (Negative)
related.meaning: Will/Does/Do (not)
related.comparison: The non-past (or dictionary) form is the base form of a verb, used to express habitual or future actions (e.g., 食べる = 'I eat' or 'I will eat'). To express the negative, use the negative form. For most える/いる-Verbs, change the final る to ない (e.g., 食べない = 'I do not eat' or 'I will not eat'). Both the non-past and negative forms are plain forms used in casual conversation or other grammar patterns.

related.title: う-Verb (Negative)
related.meaning: Will/Does/Do (not)
related.comparison: The non-past or dictionary form is the base form of a verb, used to express habitual or future actions To express the negative, change the final kana and add ない (e.g., 買わない = 'I do not buy' or 'I will not buy'). Both the non-past and negative forms are plain forms used in casual conversation or other grammar patterns.

related.title: Verb + て
related.meaning: And, Then (Linking events)
related.comparison: The non-past or dictionary form is the base form of a verb, used for habitual or future actions. Meanwhile, the Verb + て form connects verbs, indicating actions in sequence or as part of complex grammar patterns. Note that unlike the non-past form, the Verb + て form has no tense or politeness level on its own, which are instead determined by what follows.

related.title: う-Verb (Dictionary)
related.meaning: う + ます (Godan Verbs)
related.comparison: In Japanese, the dictionary form and the non-past form of verbs are identical. For example, 買う is both the dictionary form and the non-past form of the verb 'to buy.' Unlike in English, this form can be used as is to express future actions ('I will buy'), habitual actions ('I regularly buy'), or general truths ('People buy a lot around Christmas').

related.title: う-Verb (Past)
related.meaning: う - Verbs (Past tense)
related.comparison: The non-past (or dictionary) form of a verb expresses habitual or future actions, while the past form indicates completed actions or past states. For う-Verbs, the last kana changes before adding た (or だ) to form the past tense. Both forms are plain and used in casual conversation or other grammar patterns.

antonyms.title: 〜ようとしない
antonyms.meaning: Not willing to, Not make an effort to, To not attempt or try to, Volitional + としない
antonyms.comparison: These two expressions are opposite uses of the same structure. Verb[volitional]とする means 'Try to do (A)' or 'Make an attempt at (A)', often with the nuance that the attempt may fail, e.g. 食べようとしたけど、無理だった 'I tried to eat it, but I couldn't.' In contrast, 〜ようとしない means 'Not even try to (A)', e.g. うちの息子は、野菜を食べようとしない 'My son doesn't even try to eat vegetables.'

related.title: ようにする
related.meaning: To try to, To make sure to, To endeavor to
related.comparison: ようにする (literally, 'do things in such a way as to X') expresses taking care to perform a certain action and often indicates a habitual effort. In contrast, Verb[volitional] + とする implies a one-time attempt to do something, often implying the attempt may not succeed. For example, 毎朝走るようにしている ('I'm making a habit of running every morning.') vs. １０キロ走ろうとしたけど ('I tried to run 10k, but...' implying 'but I couldn't finish', etc.).

related.title: Verb[volitional] + としたが
related.meaning: Tried to ~ but ~, Was about to ~ but ~, Was going to ~ but ~
related.comparison: Both structures use the volitional verb form to show intent, but differ in whether the outcome is explicitly expressed. 'Verb[volitional]とする' expresses an attempt or intention to do something, e.g. '食べようとする' ('try to eat'). 'Verb[volitional]としたが' implies the attempt was interrupted or failed, e.g. '食べようとしたが、無理だった' ('I tried to eat it, but couldn't').

related.title: まだ～ていません
related.meaning: Still haven't done (something)
related.comparison: The grammar point 'Verb[volitional]とする' expresses the speaker's decision or intention to do something, often indicating a plan or goal. In contrast, 'まだ～ていません' is used to express that an action has not yet occurred or a state has not yet been reached, emphasizing the ongoing absence of the action or state.

related.title: るところだ
related.meaning: About to, On the verge of
related.comparison: Both can describe actions about to happen, but with different nuance. るところだ simply states that something is on the verge of occurring. Verb[volitional] + とする adds a sense of intent or effort, and is often used when the action may not actually happen, e.g. 子どもはなんでも食べようとする ('Kids will try to eat anything.'), where 食べるところだ wouldn't be appropriate.

related.title: てみる
related.meaning: To try to
related.comparison: Verb[volitional]とする and てみる are both translated as 'try', but the former implies attempting something that may not succeed, e.g. 話そうとした ('I tried to speak.'), which implies that maybe the attempt was unsuccessful. In contrast,てみる focuses on trying something to see the result, as in 話してみた ('I tried speaking.'), which has the nuance of doing so to see what it was like.

related.title: Verb[よう]
related.meaning: Let's, I shall (Casual volitional)
related.comparison: Both of these use Verb [よう], the volitional form. On its own, it expresses a suggestion, e.g. 食べよう ('Let's eat.'). When combined with とする, it shows an attempt to do something, often one that fails, e.g. 食べようとしたけど ('I tried to eat it, but...'). The key difference is suggestion/proposal vs. attempted action.

related.title: Verb[volitional]とする
related.meaning: Try to, Attempt to, To be about to
related.comparison: Both structures use the volitional verb form to show intent, but differ in whether the outcome is explicitly expressed. 'Verb[volitional]とする' expresses an attempt or intention to do something, e.g. '食べようとする' ('try to eat'). 'Verb[volitional]としたが' implies the attempt was interrupted or failed, e.g. '食べようとしたが、無理だった' ('I tried to eat it, but couldn't').

related.title: い-Adjectives
related.meaning: Adjectives ending in い
related.comparison: い-Adjectives describe the state or characteristics of nouns and can be used at the end of a sentence or directly before a noun. For example, 面白い本 means 'an interesting book.' Similarly, Japanese verbs, unlike English verbs, can also modify nouns. For example, 読んだ本 means 'the book [I] read.'

related.title: な-Adjectives
related.meaning: Adjectives that are also nouns
related.comparison: な-Adjectives describe the state or characteristics of nouns. They can be used at the end of a sentence (followed by だ or です) or precede a noun with な. For example, 大変な仕事 means 'a tough job.' Similarly, Japanese verbs can modify nouns directly, unlike な-Adjectives. For example, 読んだ本 means 'the book (I) read.'

related.title: ている①
related.meaning: Is, Am, Are (~ing)
related.comparison: The ている① form expresses completed actions or past states. Unlike English, verbs can directly modify nouns, similar to い-Adjectives. For example, 読んでいる本 means 'the book [I] am reading.' However, when describing a noun's state, the past form (た) is often preferred over ている. For example, 死んだネズミ ('a dead mouse') is more common than 死んでいるネズミ.

related.title: う-Verb (Past)
related.meaning: う - Verbs (Past tense)
related.comparison: The う-Verb (Past) expresses completed actions or past states. Unlike English, verbs can directly modify nouns, similar to い-Adjectives. For example, 買った本 means 'the book [I] bought.' This typically applies only to the plain/casual verb form, while the polite ます form is used at the end of sentences or clauses.

related.title: る-Verb (Past)
related.meaning: る - Verbs (Past tense)
related.comparison: The る-Verb (Past) expresses completed actions or past states. Unlike English, verbs can modify nouns directly, similar to い-Adjectives. For example, 食べたケーキ means 'the cake [I] ate.' This applies mainly to the plain/casual form, while the polite ます form is used at the end of clauses or sentences.

related.title: ている③
related.meaning: Has/have done, Is ~ing (Repetition)
related.comparison: The ている① form expresses completed actions or past states. Unlike English, verbs can directly modify nouns, similar to い-Adjectives. For example, 行っている学校 means 'the school I am going to.' However, when describing a noun's state, the past form is often preferred over ている. For example, 死んだネズミ ('a dead mouse') is more common than 死んでいるネズミ.

related.title: ている②
related.meaning: State of being, Has (have) done
related.comparison: The ている① form expresses completed actions or past states. Unlike English, verbs can directly modify nouns, similar to い-Adjectives. However, unless emphasizing a changing state, the past form (た) is usually preferred over ている when describing nouns. For example, 死んだネズミ ('a dead mouse') is more common than 死んでいるネズミ.

related.title: だに + しない
related.meaning: Not even, Not at all, Never could have
related.comparison: Both use だに to express an extremely small degree, but the structure and nuance differ. だに + しない is a fixed negative pattern meaning ‘does not even do (the smallest action),’ often used to emphasize complete lack of attention or effort, as in 挨拶だにしない (‘He doesn’t even greet.’). Verb＋だに is a formal, written construction meaning ‘even (the act of)' and is typically followed by negative or limiting expressions to stress how minimal or unlikely the action is, as in 彼の名前を聞くだに緊張する (‘I get nervous just hearing his name.').

related.title: う-Verb (Past)
related.meaning: う - Verbs (Past tense)
related.comparison: The う-Verb (Past) expresses a completed action or one that took place in the past, similar to '-ed' in English. The Verb + て form looks similar but with た replaced by て. It is used to connect actions in chronological order or in other grammar patterns, such as asking a favor (てください) or permission (てもいいか). The て form has no tense; tense is determined by what follows it in the sentence.

related.title: う-Verb (Dictionary)
related.meaning: う + ます (Godan Verbs)
related.comparison: The dictionary form of an う-verb (e.g., 買う) is its base form, describing non-past actions (e.g., 買う = 'I buy' or 'I will buy'). It's used casually or as part of other grammatical patterns. In contrast, the て-form (verb + て) connects verbs to show sequences or to form requests and various other grammar structures (e.g., 買ってください = 'Please buy...').

related.title: 連用形
related.meaning: Formal Conjunctive
related.comparison: Both 連用形 and the て form connect sequential actions, but 連用形 sounds more formal and is often used in writing. For example, 起きて becomes 起き, e.g. ８時に起きて、朝ご飯を食べました 'I got up at 8 o'clock, then ate breakfast.' → ８時に起き、朝ご飯を食べました. (Same meaning, different register.)

related.title: る-Verb (Negative)
related.meaning: Will/Does/Do (not)
related.comparison: The negative form of a る-verb expresses that someone does not or will not perform an action (e.g., 食べない = 'I do not eat' or 'I will not eat'). The Verb + て form, on the other hand, connects verbs with other sentence elements, expressing sequences, requests, or permission. For example, 食べて can mean 'Eat, please' in a casual request.

related.title: ている①
related.meaning: Is, Am, Are (~ing)
related.comparison: The Verb + て form is used to connect a verb to another verb, or another part of the sentence. When combined with いる, it describes an ongoing action or state. For example, 食べている means 'I am eating,' and 死んでいる means 'I am dead.' Whether ている conveys an action (like 'is -ing') or a state depends on the verb. In casual speech, ている is often shortened to てる.

related.title: ている②
related.meaning: State of being, Has (have) done
related.comparison: The Verb + て form connects verbs or parts of a sentence. When an 'instantaneous' verb (one that expresses a single change in state) is combined with the existence verb いる in the ている form, it expresses an ongoing state. For example, 死んでいる means '(It) is dead.' With other verbs, ている conveys a meaning similar to the English 'is...-ing' (see ている①).

related.title: ていた
related.meaning: Was doing ~
related.comparison: The Verb + て form connects verbs or parts of a sentence. When combined with いた, it describes a past state. For example, 食べていた means 'I was eating,' and 死んでいた means 'it was dead.' Whether ていた conveys an action ('was -ing') or a state depends on the verb. In casual speech, ていた is often shortened to てた.

related.title: Verb + て+ B
related.meaning: And, And then, After that (sequence)
related.comparison: The Verb + て form connects verbs or parts of a sentence. One of the most common uses of this form is to describe actions that occur in sequence, similar to the English 'then' or 'and then.' For example, 水を買って、飲んだ means 'I bought (some) water, and then drank it.'

related.title: Verbs (Non-past)
related.meaning: (Non-past)
related.comparison: The non-past or dictionary form is the base form of a verb, used for habitual or future actions. Meanwhile, the Verb + て form connects verbs, indicating actions in sequence or as part of complex grammar patterns. Note that unlike the non-past form, the Verb + て form has no tense or politeness level on its own, which are instead determined by what follows.

related.title: る-Verb (Dictionary)
related.meaning: る + ます (Ichidan Verbs)
related.comparison: The dictionary form of a る-Verb (e.g., 食べる) is its basic, informal form used to describe present or future actions (e.g., 食べる = 'I eat' or 'I will eat'). In contrast, the て form (verb + て) connects verbs, describing sequential actions or appearing in requests and other grammar patterns (e.g., 食べてください = 'Please eat').

related.title: Verb + てもいい
related.meaning: It's okay to, It's alright to, Can/may
related.comparison: The Verb + て form is used to connect a verb to another verb, or another part of the sentence. When combined with もいい (meaning 'even' + 'good' or 'okay'), it asks for or grants permission. For example, 食べてもいい？with a question mark means 'Can I eat this?' (literally, 'Is it okay even if I eat this?'), while 食べてもいい with no question mark means 'you can eat it.'

related.title: ている③
related.meaning: Has/have done, Is ~ing (Repetition)
related.comparison: The Verb + て form connects verbs or parts of a sentence. One common use of this form is to connect it to the existence verb いる to express continuing or repeated actions, like going to work or going to sleep at the same time every day. Note that the specific nuance of ている can depend on the verb and context (see ている① and ている②).

related.title: てから
related.meaning: Once...is done, Once...happens, After doing
related.comparison: The Verb + て form is used to connect a verb to another verb, or another part of the sentence. It's commonly used to list actions in sequence, such as 食べて寝る ('I eat, and (then) sleep'). Adding から after the て form emphasizes the sequence, as in 食べてから寝る ('I eat, and (after that) I will sleep').

related.title: Verb[て]
related.meaning: Please do for me (Casual request)
related.comparison: The Verb + て form connects verbs or parts of a sentence. It can also be used by itself as a casual request. For example, 食べて would mean 'Please eat [this.]' These casual requests are often followed by the sentence-ending particle ね to further soften them. To express a more polite request, the てください form should be used.

related.title: てくれてありがとう
related.meaning: Thank you for ~ ing
related.comparison: The Verb + て form connects verbs or parts of a sentence. A common use is to express thanks by combining it with the giving verb くれる (also in the Verb + て form) and ありがとう. For example, 教えてくれてありがとう means 'Thank you for teaching me.' This is a casual form, and a more polite version would use honorific verbs (くださる or いただく) and add ございます.

related.title: Adjective + て・Noun + で
related.meaning: And... (Conjunctive)
related.comparison: Adjective + て, Noun + で, and Verb + て+ B are the same fundamental pattern, using the て form to connect elements in a sentence. The construction of the て form depends on whether the word is an い-Adjective, な-Adjective, noun, or verb, but the function remains consistent, similar to the English 'and.'

related.title: Verb + て
related.meaning: And, Then (Linking events)
related.comparison: The Verb + て form connects verbs or parts of a sentence. One of the most common uses of this form is to describe actions that occur in sequence, similar to the English 'then' or 'and then.' For example, 水を買って、飲んだ means 'I bought (some) water, and then drank it.'

related.title: と
related.meaning: And
related.comparison: Both the Verb + て form and the particle と can express the meaning of 'and.' The difference is that Verb + て is used to connect verb or verb phrases to the rest of the sentence, while と connects nouns with other nouns. For example, パンとバナナを買った means 'I bought bread and a banana.' To say 'I bought bread, and ate it,' however, you would say パンを買って、食べた.

related.title: Verb[て] + B
related.meaning: Non-sequence, And, But (Contrast), Parallel action/state
related.comparison: Both patterns use the て form to link a verb or verb phrase to the next part of the sentence, but the nuance varies. It can either simply connect actions, as in パンを買って食べた ('I bought bread and ate it'), or suggest a cause or effect, as in 人が多くて入れなかった ('There were too many people, so I couldn't get in'). This interpretation depends on the context.

related.title: で
related.meaning: With, By (using)
related.comparison: The particle で follows a noun to describe the means or method of an action (e.g., ペンで書く = 'to write with a pen'). A verb in the て form can express a similar nuance (e.g., ペンを使って書く = 'to write using a pen,' 歩いて行く = 'to go by walking'). The meaning is the same, but the form differs depending on whether the 'method' is expressed with a noun or a verb.

synonyms.title: てもかまわない
synonyms.meaning: It doesn't matter if, I don't mind
synonyms.comparison: Both Verb + てもいい and てもかまわない are used to give or ask for permission. The difference is tone and formality. てもいい is a basic, everyday expression, while てもかまわない is slightly more formal and often used in writing or professional contexts to mean 'It doesn't matter if…' or 'It's fine if…'

synonyms.title: てもいい
synonyms.meaning: It is alright/fine/okay even if, Can/may, ~ is also okay
synonyms.comparison: The てもいい pattern expresses that something is acceptable or allowed. With verbs, it's used to give or ask for permission, such as 食べてもいい？ (Can I eat this?') It can also be used with nouns or adjectives to ask if a condition is acceptable, like 冷たくてもいい? ('Is it okay if it's cold?) when referring to a drink, for example.

related.title: でよければ
related.meaning: If it is alright, If you don't mind, If you would like, If possible
related.comparison: The ても form means 'even if.' One common use is Verb + てもいい, which gives or asks for permission (e.g. 食べてもいい？ = 'Can I eat this?') A related pattern is でよければ, which uses the conditional form of いい (よければ) to mean 'if it's okay.' It's often used to make polite offers, like 私でよければお手伝いします ('If I'm okay [as a choice], I'll help').

related.title: Verb + て
related.meaning: And, Then (Linking events)
related.comparison: The Verb + て form is used to connect a verb to another verb, or another part of the sentence. When combined with もいい (meaning 'even' + 'good' or 'okay'), it asks for or grants permission. For example, 食べてもいい？with a question mark means 'Can I eat this?' (literally, 'Is it okay even if I eat this?'), while 食べてもいい with no question mark means 'you can eat it.'

related.title: といってもいい
related.meaning: You could say, You might say
related.comparison: The ても form has the core meaning of 'even if.' One common use is Verb + てもいい, which gives or asks for permission—for example, 食べてもいいですか？ literally means 'Is it okay even if I eat this?' but works like 'Can/May I eat this?' in English. Similarly, といってもいい means 'It's okay even if you say...' but is used to express 'You could say' or 'One might say.'

related.title: ても
related.meaning: Even if, Even though
related.comparison: ても has the core meaning of 'even if.' One common use is in Verb + てもいい to ask or give permission. For example, 食べてもいいですか？ means 'Can/May I eat this?' (literally, 'Is it okay even if I eat this?') More broadly, ても can also express contrast. For example, 寒くても行きます means 'I'll go even if it's cold.'

related.title: ないで
related.meaning: Without doing, Not ~ and ~
related.comparison: The ないで form combines a verb in the negative with で, and has two common uses: (1) to make a negative request, e.g. 食べないで ('Don't eat it.'), and (2) to express 'without doing' something, e.g. 晩ご飯を食べないで出かけた ('I went out without eating dinner.').

related.title: ないでください
related.meaning: Please don't (Polite request)
related.comparison: Both Verb[ないで] and ないでください are used to express requests not to do something. While ないでください is polite, and can be used with strangers or people equal in status to you, Verb[ないで] (without ください) is more casual, and typically used to make requests of friends or family members in a casual tone. Requests with てください and especially Verb [て] can be followed by ね (e.g. 食べてね) for a softer, more gentle feeling.

related.title: ていく
related.meaning: To ~ and go, To go on, To start
related.comparison: Verb + にいく combines the verb stem with にいく to express going somewhere to do something (e.g., 食べに行く = 'go to eat'). In contrast, ていく (Verb + て + いく) expresses sequential actions (e.g., 食べて行く = 'eat, then go') or actions that progress over time (e.g., 上手くなっていく = 'get better over time').

related.title: へいく
related.meaning: To go to, To head toward
related.comparison: へいく is used with nouns, typically physical locations or directions, to express 'going' or 'heading' somewhere (e.g., 学校へいく, 西へいく). へ can be replaced with に, which is more colloquial and emphasizes the final destination. にいく can also combine with verb stems to express going to perform an action (e.g., 食べにいく = 'go to eat'). The two patterns can be combined as well (e.g., 店へ食べにいく = 'go to the store to eat').

related.title: なくて
related.meaning: Not ~ and, Not so, Because not (Examples)
related.comparison: Both structures use the て-form to express cause or reason. Verb [て] + B uses the affirmative form to explain a positive reason, e.g. 行けてよかったです ('I'm glad I could go.'). In contrast, なくて is the て-form of the negative ない and expresses a negative reason, e.g. 行けなくて残念でした ('It was unfortunate I couldn't go.').

related.title: Adjective + て・Noun + で
related.meaning: And... (Conjunctive)
related.comparison: Both of these patterns involve connecting a word or phrase (adjectives and nouns in one case, verbs in the other) to the rest of the sentence using the て form. The meaning of the connection depends on context. Sometimes it simply links words, as in 大きくてきれい ('big and pretty'), while in other cases, the て form expresses reason or method, as in 歩いて行った ('went by walking').

related.title: し～し
related.meaning: And, Giving reasons
related.comparison: Both forms can express reasons, but their usage differs. Verb[て] + B shows that the stated action is the direct reason for what follows, while し～し implies that multiple reasons exist, even if only one is mentioned. For example, 犯人が捕まって安心です ('I'm relieved because the criminal was caught.') vs. 犯人が捕まったし、安心です ('The criminal was caught, and I'm relieved.'), where the し version suggests there may be other factors for the relief.

synonyms.title: 命令形
synonyms.comparison: The imperative or command form (命令形) gives blunt commands, e.g. 止まれ ('Stop!'), often sounding forceful. Verb[て] form is softer and used for casual requests, e.g. 見て 'Look!'. Adding よ or ね softens it further, e.g. 見てね ('Take a look, okay?'), which is commonly done in friendly, everyday speech.

related.title: てください
related.meaning: Please do (Polite request)
related.comparison: Both the 〜て  and 〜てください are used to make requests. てください is polite and neutral, and can be used with strangers or people of equal status. In contrast, the 〜て form is more casual and is used when speaking with friends or family. Requests using either form, especially the plain 〜て, can be softened further by adding the sentence-ending particle ね (e.g. 食べてね), which adds a gentle, caring tone.

related.title: Verb + て
related.meaning: And, Then (Linking events)
related.comparison: The Verb + て form connects verbs or parts of a sentence. It can also be used by itself as a casual request. For example, 食べて would mean 'Please eat [this.]' These casual requests are often followed by the sentence-ending particle ね to further soften them. To express a more polite request, the てください form should be used.

related.title: くれる
related.meaning: To give (me), To be given
related.comparison: あげる and くれる are basic verbs for giving. あげる is used when the speaker or their in-group gives something to someone outside the group, or when one out-group member gives to another. くれる is used when someone outside the speaker's group gives something to the speaker or their in-group. In both cases, the subject marked by が is the giver.

related.title: もらう
related.meaning: To receive, To get, To obtain
related.comparison: あげる means 'to give' and is used when the speaker or someone in their in-group gives something to someone outside the group. The subject, marked by が, is the giver. In contrast, もらう means 'to receive' and describes the act from the receiver's perspective. With もらう, the subject marked by が is the one receiving.

related.title: てあげる
related.meaning: To do something for someone, As a favor for
related.comparison: あげる is a basic verb meaning 'to give,' often used when the giver is the speaker or someone in the speaker's in-group, and the receiver is outside that group. For example, プレゼントをあげる means 'to give a present.' When used as てあげる after a verb in the て form, it means doing something for someone's benefit. For example, 英語を教えてあげる means 'to teach English (to someone as a favor).'

related.title: た末・の末
related.meaning: After, As a conclusion of
related.comparison: Both た末・の末 and あげく convey the end of a process, but with subtle differences. た末・の末 often implies a significant sequence of events or actions over an extended period leading to the final result, as in 長い議論の末、結論に達した (After long discussion, we reached a conclusion). あげく, in contrast, is often used when the outcome is disappointing or problematic, as in 迷ったあげく、遅刻してしまった (After much hesitation, I ended up being late).

related.title: そこ
related.meaning: There, That place
related.comparison: あそこ and そこ both indicate locations, differing only by perspective. あそこ refers to a place far from both the speaker and listener, similar to 'over there' (away from both of us). In contrast, そこ refers to a location closer to the listener than to the speaker, similar to 'there' (near you).

related.title: その
related.meaning: That
related.comparison: その refers to something physically or figuratively close to the listener, similar to 'that' in English, and must directly precede a noun (e.g., その本 = 'that book'). In contrast, あそこ refers specifically to a location distant from both the speaker and listener, equivalent to 'over there' in English. Unlike その, あそこ can stand alone without needing a noun.

related.title: あれ
related.meaning: That (over there)
related.comparison: あそこ is used to refer to a location that is far from both the speaker and listener. あれ refers to objects similarly far away, or to ideas known to both people, similar to the English 'that' or 'that thing (over there).' In short, あそこ refers to places, while あれ refers to objects, situations, or ideas.

related.title: それ
related.meaning: That
related.comparison: あそこ refers to a place far from both the speaker and listener, or a known location not close to either. It's similar to 'over there' or 'that place' in English. それ, on the other hand, refers to an object near the listener or something just mentioned, like 'that.' The difference is whether you're referring to a place or a thing, and from whose perspective.

related.title: ここ
related.meaning: Here, This place
related.comparison: あそこ and ここ both indicate locations but differ in perspective. あそこ refers to a place far from both the speaker and the listener, similar to 'over there' in English. On the other hand, ここ refers to a place near the speaker, similar to 'here' or 'this place.'

related.title: あの
related.meaning: That (over there)
related.comparison: あそこ refers directly to a place far from both the speaker and listener, physically or figuratively, similar to the English 'over there.' あの, however, must be followed by a noun and indicates something distant from both parties (e.g., あの本 = 'that book [over there]'). In other words, both あそこ and あの point to things far away, but differ structurally in their usage.

related.title: どこ
related.meaning: Where
related.comparison: どこ is a question word used to inquire about a place, similar to 'where' in English. あそこ, in contrast, refers specifically to a place far from both the speaker and listener, physically or figuratively, similar to 'over there.' For example, トイレはどこですか？ ('Where's the bathroom?') could be answered with あそこです ('It's over there,' indicating a place distant from both speaker and listener).

related.title: この
related.meaning: This
related.comparison: この refers to something close to the speaker, either physically or figuratively, similar to 'this' in English, and must be followed by a noun (e.g., この本 = 'this book'). In contrast, あそこ refers to a location far from both the speaker and listener, similar to 'over there' in English. Unlike この, あそこ can stand alone without a noun.

related.title: ～てこそ
related.meaning: Only if, Because, By, Unless, Until
related.comparison: These are both emphatic expressions that can be used in similar contexts to express 'it's entirely because of (A) that (B)'. あっての is narrower in use, saying that something was possible only because something else 'exists', e.g. 皆さんの努力あっての成功だ ('This success is entirely because of everyone's effort.'). てこそ can be used with a wider variety of verbs, such as 留学してこそ学べること ('things you can only learn by studying abroad'.).

antonyms.title: まえに
antonyms.comparison: あとで and まえに are both used to describe the timing of actions, but with opposite meanings. まえに usually follows a plain (non-past) verb and means 'before doing A' (e.g., 食べる前に = 'before eating'). In contrast, あとで typically follows the past tense form of a verb and means 'after doing B' (e.g., 食べたあとで = 'after eating').

related.title: 上で
related.meaning: After, Upon
related.comparison: Both 上で and あとで express that one action follows another, but with different nuances. あとで simply shows sequence, like 'after doing (A)'. 上で implies the first action is a necessary step for the second, similar to 'upon doing (A)', and is more formal, often used in writing or business contexts.

related.title: たら
related.meaning: When, From when, Just when, If
related.comparison: Both あとで and たら can show that one action follows another, but their usage and nuance differ. あとで simply shows sequence, like 'after doing (A)'. たら adds a conditional nuance and can mean 'when' (temporal) or 'if' (hypothetical). Because of this, they are rarely interchangeable.

related.title: 前者は・後者は
related.meaning: The former ~ the latter, The first ~ the second
related.comparison: 前者は and 後者は, like あとで, relate to sequence, but in different ways. あとで (also written in kanji as 後で) refers to the order of actions, like 'after doing (A)'. 前者 and 後者 mean 'the former' and 'the latter', pointing back to the previously mentioned items.

related.title: てから
related.meaning: Once...is done, Once...happens, After doing
related.comparison: てから and あとで both convey a sequence of events, but their nuances differ. てから uses the Verb + て form to show that one event follows another. In contrast, あとで follows the past tense (た form) and simply indicates that the second event occurred after the first, without implying any connection between the two.

related.title: それ
related.meaning: That
related.comparison: それ refers to something closer to the listener than to the speaker, or something just mentioned, similar to 'that' in English. あの points to something far from both the speaker and listener, or something both people are aware of. あの must be used with a noun, like あの本 ('that book'), while それ can stand alone.

related.title: あれ
related.meaning: That (over there)
related.comparison: Both あれ and あの refer to something far from both the speaker and listener, or to something they both know. あれ is a standalone pronoun that means 'that' or 'that thing.' あの is a modifier that must be followed by a noun, like in あの本 ('that book'). In other words, あれ stands alone, while あの does not.

related.title: ここ
related.meaning: Here, This place
related.comparison: ここ refers to a location close to the speaker or to a place the speaker points to, similar to 'here' in English. あの refers to something far from both the speaker and listener, or something they both know, similar to 'that' in English. Unlike ここ, あの is used for objects or concepts and must directly precede a noun (e.g., あの本 = 'that book').

related.title: この
related.meaning: This
related.comparison: Both この and あの can be used before nouns to describe their position relative to the speaker, similar to 'this' and 'that' in English. The difference lies in perspective: この refers to something close to the speaker (like 'this'), while あの refers to something far from both the speaker and listener, or something known to both (like 'that').

related.title: その
related.meaning: That
related.comparison: Both その and あの are used before nouns to express 'that,' referring to an object or concept away from the speaker. その points to something closer to the listener than the speaker, or something just mentioned. In contrast, あの refers to something far from both the speaker and listener, or something familiar to both but not close to either.

related.title: そこ
related.meaning: There, That place
related.comparison: そこ refers to a place near the listener, similar to 'there' in English. あの, however, points to something distant from both speaker and listener or something familiar to both, like 'that.' Unlike そこ, あの always refers to objects or concepts and must directly precede a noun (e.g., あの本 = 'that book').

related.title: あそこ
related.meaning: Over there
related.comparison: あそこ refers directly to a place far from both the speaker and listener, physically or figuratively, similar to the English 'over there.' あの, however, must be followed by a noun and indicates something distant from both parties (e.g., あの本 = 'that book [over there]'). In other words, both あそこ and あの point to things far away, but differ structurally in their usage.

related.title: どの
related.meaning: Which (of three or more), What (kind)
related.comparison: Both どの and あの are used before nouns. あの refers to something far from both the speaker and listener, or something familiar to both but not close, similar to 'that' in English. In contrast, どの is a question word meaning 'which' when there are three or more choices (e.g., どの色が好きですか？ = 'Which color do you like?').

related.title: すぎる
related.meaning: Too much, Very
related.comparison: Both すぎる and あまり express that something is excessive, but they differ in usage and nuance. すぎる attaches to the stem of verbs and adjectives, while あまり follows plain-form verbs, nouns with の, or な-adjectives with な. Though すぎる can imply something negative, あまり more clearly emphasizes that the excess leads to a negative consequence.

related.title: あまり～ない
related.meaning: Not very, Not much, Not really, Hardly
related.comparison: あまり has the base meaning of 'excess', but its nuance changes with context. Together with a negative word in the form あまり～ない, it means 'not very (A)', e.g. あまり寒くない ('It's not very cold.'). Used affirmatively, it implies something of an excessive degree, e.g. 緊張のあまりうまくできなかった ('I was so nervous I couldn't do it well.').

related.title: なかなか
related.meaning: Very, Quite, Considerably
related.comparison: Both express degree, but with different implications. なかなか implies something is quite or unexpectedly good, e.g. なかなかいい店だね ('This place is surprisingly good.') あまり highlights an excessive degree that leads to an unintended or negative result, e.g. 緊張のあまり声が震えた ('I was so nervous my voice shook.')

related.title: からこそ
related.meaning: Emphasized because, Precisely because
related.comparison: あまり follows a noun or verb phrase and shows that an extreme state leads to an unintended result, e.g. 緊張のあまり声が出なかった ('I was so nervous I couldn't speak.') からこそ attaches after the reason and emphasizes it as the precise cause, e.g. 緊張したからこそ全力を出せた ('It was precisely because I was nervous that I gave it my all.')

related.title: あまりに
related.meaning: So much...that, (Way) too...
related.comparison: Both mean 'too much', but differ in nuance. あまりに emphasizes intensity and doesn't necessarily imply a result, e.g. あまりに暑い日だった ('It was an extremely hot day.') あまり highlights excess that leads to a (usually undesirable) outcome, e.g. 暑さのあまり眠れなかった ('I couldn't sleep due to the extreme heat.')

related.title: Adj限りだ
related.meaning: Extremely, Really, Very, As ... as one can be
related.comparison: Both emphasize extremity, but with different focuses. Adjective + 限りだ expresses a strong feeling at its peak, e.g. 悲しい限りだ ('I'm as sad as can be.') あまり indicates that excess leads to a (usually undesirable) result, e.g. 悲しみのあまり声が出なかった ('I couldn't speak due to extreme sadness.')

related.title: こそ
related.meaning: For sure, For certain, Even more so, Underlined or bolded text
related.comparison: Both express emphasis, but in different ways. あまり is a noun that emphasizes an extreme degree leading to an unintended result, e.g. 悲しみのあまり泣き出した ('She burst into tears from too much sadness.') こそ is a particle that stresses importance or exclusivity, e.g. 今日こそ早く寝よう ('Today, for sure, I'll go to bed early.')

antonyms.title: なかなか
antonyms.comparison: なかなか and あまり～ない both express degree, but in opposite directions. なかなか implies exceeding expectations, e.g. なかなか美味しい ('It's quite tasty.'). In contrast, あまり～ない suggests a lesser degree, or falling short of expectations, e.g. あまり美味しくない ('It's not very tasty.').

antonyms.title: たくさん
antonyms.meaning: Many, A lot of, Plenty, Enough
antonyms.comparison: たくさん is a noun meaning 'many' or 'a lot,' often used with verbs (e.g. たくさん食べた = 'I ate a lot'). To express the opposite idea ('not much' or 'not many'), the pattern あまり～ない is used (e.g. あまり食べなかった = 'I didn't eat much'). Note that あまり originally means 'excess' and can also appear in affirmative forms (see あまりに).

related.title: 別に〜ない
related.meaning: Not really, Not particularly, It's not like...
related.comparison: Both 別に〜ない and あまり〜ない are used to qualify negative statements, but differ in nuance. あまり〜ない expresses a lesser degree, e.g. あまり痛くない ('It doesn't hurt much.'). In contrast, 別に〜ない adds a tone of indifference or dismissal, e.g. 別に痛くない ('It's not like it hurts.', implying that there is no need to worry, etc.).

related.title: あまり
related.meaning: So much...that
related.comparison: あまり has the base meaning of 'excess', but its nuance changes with context. Together with a negative word in the form あまり～ない, it means 'not very (A)', e.g. あまり寒くない ('It's not very cold.'). Used affirmatively, it implies something of an excessive degree, e.g. 緊張のあまりうまくできなかった ('I was so nervous I couldn't do it well.').

related.title: 全く～ない
related.meaning: Not~at all
related.comparison: Both 全く～ない and あまり～ない express negation, but differ in degree. 全く～ない indicates a complete absence, e.g. 全く寒くない ('It's not cold at all.'). あまり～ない suggests a lesser degree, e.g. あまり寒くない ('It's not very cold.').

related.title: なかなか～ない
related.meaning: Not really, Hardly, Not easily or readily, By no means, Far from
related.comparison: なかなか～ない and あまり～ない both express negation, but with different nuance. なかなか～ない implies difficulty or delay despite effort, e.g. なかなか話せない ('I can't speak easily.', implying difficulty despite effort). あまり～ない simply indicates a lower degree, e.g. あまり話せない ('I can't speak very much/well.').

related.title: めったに〜ない
related.meaning: Hardly, Rarely, Seldom
related.comparison: Both めったに〜ない and あまり〜ない are used to qualify negative expressions, but differ in nuance. めったに〜ない means 'rarely' or 'almost never' and focuses on frequency, e.g. めったに行かない ('I rarely go.'). あまり〜ない is less strong, e.g. あまり行かない ('I don't go often.'). It can also qualify a degree or amount, e.g. あまり寒くない ('It's not very cold.'), while めったに cannot.

related.title: すこしも～ない
related.meaning: Not even a little, Not at all, Not in the least, Never
related.comparison: Both あまり～ない and すこしも～ない are used in negative statements, but differ in degree and tone. あまり～ない means 'not very (A)' and is fairly colloquial, e.g. あまり寒くない ('It's not very cold.'). すこしも～ない is more formal and emphatic, e.g. 少しも寒くない ('It's not the least bit cold.').

related.title: そんなに
related.meaning: So (much), That much, Like that
related.comparison: Both あまり～ない and そんなに express a negative degree, but with different nuance. あまり～ない means 'not very' in a general sense, e.g. あまり寒くない ('It's not very cold.'). そんなに (literally 'that much') can imply comparison to a standard and can be used with both negative and affirmative phrases, e.g. そんなに好きなら 'if you like [it] that much...' and そんなに好きじゃない ('I don't like it that much.').

related.title: あまり
related.meaning: So much...that
related.comparison: Both mean 'too much', but differ in nuance. あまりに emphasizes intensity and doesn't necessarily imply a result, e.g. あまりに暑い日だった ('It was an extremely hot day.') あまり highlights excess that leads to a (usually undesirable) outcome, e.g. 暑さのあまり眠れなかった ('I couldn't sleep due to the extreme heat.')

related.title: なかなか
related.meaning: Very, Quite, Considerably
related.comparison: Both emphasize degree, but differ in nuance. なかなか suggests something is quite or surprisingly so, often with a positive tone, e.g. なかなか面白い映画だった ('It was quite an interesting movie.') あまりに means 'too much' or 'excessively', and is often negative in tone, e.g. あまりに寒くて出かけられなかった ('It was too cold to go out.')

related.title: すぎる
related.meaning: Too much, Very
related.comparison: Both すぎる and あまりに express that something is excessive or 'too much.' すぎる attaches to the stem of verbs or adjectives (e.g. 早すぎる), while あまりに comes before them (e.g. あまりに早い). They can also be combined for emphasis (e.g. あまりに早すぎる). Compared to すぎる, あまりに often carries a nuance of surprise or emotional reaction.

synonyms.title: か
synonyms.comparison: Both か and あるいは present alternatives. か is casual and follows nouns to mean 'or' (e.g., 今日か明日休みます = 'I'll take off today or tomorrow'). あるいは is more formal, mainly used in writing or formal speech (e.g., 新品で買うか、あるいは中古で買うか、まだ決めていません = 'I haven't decided yet whether I'll buy it new or perhaps second-hand').

related.title: もしかしたら
related.meaning: Perhaps, Maybe, Possibly
related.comparison: あるいは and もしかしたら can overlap in one usage. あるいは is usually used mid-sentence to present alternatives, e.g. 月曜日あるいは火曜日 'Monday or Tuesday.' However, it can also appear at the start of a sentence to mean 'Perhaps,' e.g. あるいはそうかもしれない 'Perhaps that might be so.' もしかしたら can be used the same way, as in もしかしたらそうかもしれない, though あるいは carries a stronger nuance of weighing other possibilities.

antonyms.title: なし
antonyms.comparison: These two phrases come from the existence verb ある and its negative form, and are opposites meaning 'with (A)' or 'without (A)', e.g. '字幕ありで観る' ('watch with subtitles') and '字幕なしで観る' ('watch without subtitles'). They also have opposite figurative meanings, with あり meaning 'possible' or acceptable, and なし used to reject something as an option.

related.title: あそこ
related.meaning: Over there
related.comparison: あそこ is used to refer to a location that is far from both the speaker and listener. あれ refers to objects similarly far away, or to ideas known to both people, similar to the English 'that' or 'that thing (over there).' In short, あそこ refers to places, while あれ refers to objects, situations, or ideas.

related.title: あの
related.meaning: That (over there)
