related.comparison: Both あれ and あの refer to something far from both the speaker and listener, or to something they both know. あれ is a standalone pronoun that means 'that' or 'that thing.' あの is a modifier that must be followed by a noun, like in あの本 ('that book'). In other words, あれ stands alone, while あの does not.

related.title: その
related.meaning: That
related.comparison: その refers to something closer to the listener than the speaker, like 'that' in English, and must be followed by a noun (e.g., その本 = 'that book'). あれ refers to something far from both the speaker and listener, or something both people know. Unlike その, あれ can stand alone and means 'that' or 'that thing.'

related.title: どれ
related.meaning: Which (of three or more)
related.comparison: どれ is a question word used to ask 'which' or 'which one' from a group of three or more options. あれ, on the other hand, refers to something far from both the speaker and listener, or something they both know, similar to 'that' or 'that thing' in English. In short, どれ is used to ask a question, while あれ refers to a specific, distant thing.

related.title: これ
related.meaning: This
related.comparison: これ and あれ are both used to refer to things, but the difference lies in distance. これ means 'this' and refers to something near the speaker. あれ means 'that' and refers to something far from both the speaker and the listener. Both can refer to physical objects or more abstract things.

related.title: それ
related.meaning: That
related.comparison: それ and あれ are both similar to the English word 'that' and are used to refer to objects or ideas away from the speaker. それ refers to something near the listener or just mentioned in conversation. あれ points to something far from both the speaker and listener, or something both people are aware of. For physical objects, think of それ as 'that near you' and あれ as 'that over there.'

related.title: ここ
related.meaning: Here, This place
related.comparison: ここ refers to a place where the speaker is or is pointing to, similar to 'here' in English. あれ, on the other hand, refers to something far from both the speaker and listener, or something they both know but isn't nearby, similar to 'that (over there).' The key difference is whether you're talking about a place or a thing, and from whose point of view.

related.title: そこ
related.meaning: There, That place
related.comparison: あれ refers to something far from both the speaker and listener, or something both people know but isn't nearby, similar to 'that (over there)' in English. そこ refers to a place near the listener, like 'there.' Both relate to distance, but あれ is used for objects or ideas, while そこ is used for locations.

related.title: この
related.meaning: This
related.comparison: この refers to something near the speaker, like 'this' in English, and must be followed by a noun (e.g., この本 = 'this book'). あれ, on the other hand, refers to something far from both the speaker and listener, or something known to both but not nearby, similar to 'that.' Unlike この, あれ can be used on its own to mean 'that' or 'that thing.'

related.title: でもなんでもない
related.meaning: Not at all, By no means, Definitely not, Nothing like that, Not really
related.comparison: The phrase い-Adj（く） + もなんともない commonly follows an adjective to strongly downplay or deny something, e.g. 怖くもなんともない('I'm not scared or anything like it..'). The phrase でもなんでもない is similar but is used with nouns or な-adjectives to convey a similar sense, e.g. 迷惑でもなんでもない ('It's not a bother or anything.').

related.title: い-Adjectives
related.meaning: Adjectives ending in い
related.comparison: い-Adjectives describe the state or appearance of nouns, like 白いドレス ('a white dress'). They can also form the predicate, describing the main topic of a sentence. For example, 今日は暑い means 'Today is hot.' Unlike English, an い-Adjective can stand alone as a complete sentence (e.g., 暑い means 'It's hot' without needing an equivalent of 'it').

related.title: いい
related.meaning: い-Adjective meaning 'good'
related.comparison: い-Adjectives describe qualities or states. They can modify nouns (e.g., 暑い日 = 'hot day') or end a sentence (e.g., 今日は暑い = 'Today is hot'). いい ('good') is an い-adjective, but it's irregular—it becomes よく when used with verbs, unlike most い-adjectives which don't change their base form.

related.title: Verb［た・ている］+ Noun
related.meaning: Verb modified noun, Relative clause
related.comparison: い-Adjectives describe the state or characteristics of nouns and can be used at the end of a sentence or directly before a noun. For example, 面白い本 means 'an interesting book.' Similarly, Japanese verbs, unlike English verbs, can also modify nouns. For example, 読んだ本 means 'the book [I] read.'

related.title: な-Adjectives
related.meaning: Adjectives that are also nouns
related.comparison: い-Adjectives attach directly to nouns (e.g., 面白い本) and don't use だ in plain form (e.g., この本は面白い). な-Adjectives attach to nouns with な (e.g., 好きな本) and do use だ in plain form (e.g., この本は好きだ). Note that not all adjectives ending in い are い-Adjectives—for example, きれい (綺麗) ends in い but is a な-Adjective, as in きれいな月.

related.title: い-Adjective (Predicate)
related.meaning: い-Adjective (Predicate)
related.comparison: い-Adjectives describe the state or appearance of nouns, like 白いドレス ('a white dress'). They can also form the predicate, describing the main topic of a sentence. For example, 今日は暑い means 'Today is hot.' Unlike English, an い-Adjective can stand alone as a complete sentence (e.g., 暑い means 'It's hot' without needing an equivalent of 'it').

related.title: な-Adjective + Noun
related.meaning: Describing a noun
related.comparison: い-Adjectives connect directly to nouns (e.g., 面白い本) and aren't followed by だ in plain form (e.g., この本は面白い). な-Adjectives, on the other hand, connect to nouns using な (e.g., 好きな本) and are followed by だ in plain form (e.g., この本は好きだ). Note that not all adjectives ending in い are い-Adjectives (e.g., 綺麗, written as きれい in hiragana, is a な-Adjective, e.g., きれいな月).

related.title: い-Adjectives
related.meaning: Adjectives ending in い
related.comparison: い-Adjectives describe qualities or states. They can modify nouns (e.g., 暑い日 = 'hot day') or end a sentence (e.g., 今日は暑い = 'Today is hot'). いい ('good') is an い-adjective, but it's irregular—it becomes よく when used with verbs, unlike most い-adjectives which don't change their base form.

related.title: そんな・こんな・あんな・どんな
related.slug: そんな-こんな-あんな-どんな
related.meaning: Such a・Kind of, Something like ~
related.comparison: そんな・こんな・あんな・どんな are used before nouns and mean 'that/this/what kind of' or 'something like (A)'. This is a common and fairly casual pattern in everyday speech. いかなる is similar to どんな, but with a much more formal or literary tone. For example, いかなることがあっても and どんなことがあっても both mean 'no matter what happens', but the いかなる version sounds much more formal.

antonyms.title: 以上 ①
antonyms.meaning: More than (or equal to), Over・(and) above, Any more・Greater, Exceeding
antonyms.comparison: 以上① and いか (written as 以下) both attach to amounts or nouns like それ, but express opposite meanings: 以上① means 'X or more', while いか means 'X or less'. For example, １００円以上 '100 yen or more' vs. １００円以下 means '100 yen or less'. In both cases, the stated number is included. 以上 and 以下 can also refer to earlier or later content in a document.

related.title: を除いて
related.meaning: Except, With the exception of, Unless
related.comparison: いがい and を除いて both express exclusion, similar to 'other than' or 'with the exception of' in English. Their meanings are very close and often interchangeable. The main difference is that を除いて is more formal and mainly used in writing, while いがい appears in both casual speech and writing. 土日以外毎日働いています and 土日を除いて毎日働いています both mean 'I work every day except Saturday and Sunday.'.

related.title: ほかに(も)・ほか(に)は
related.meaning: Other, Another, Any other, Anything else, As well as, Besides, In addition
related.comparison: Both forms can express the idea of 'other than', but their nuance and usage differ slightly. いがい emphasizes exceptions, like 'except for' or 'other than,' and attaches directly after nouns, e.g. バナナ以外の果物 means 'fruits other than bananas.' In contrast, ほかに highlights the presence of alternatives, takes の when linking to nouns, e.g. バナナの他に 'other than bananas', and can also start a sentence or phrase.

related.title: はともかく
related.meaning: Setting aside, Apart from
related.comparison: はともかく carries the nuance of setting something aside to focus on the rest. For example, 田中さんはともかく、みんな来ると思う means 'Setting Tanaka aside, I think everyone will come.'. In contrast, いがい emphasizes exclusion, 田中さん以外はみんな来ると思う means 'I think everyone but Tanaka will come.' So, はともかく avoids commenting on Tanaka, while いがい explicitly excludes him.

related.title: をおいてほかに〜ない
related.meaning: No...but, Nothing else but, None other than
related.comparison: Both forms express exclusion, but with key differences. いがい (often written in kanji as 以外)  attaches directly to nouns, e.g. サッカー以外 'other than soccer'. It's common in both speech and writing. In contrast, をおいてほかに〜ない is much more formal and academic. It's also used almost exclusively to positively highlight something, while いがい carries no such nuance.

related.title: たちまち
related.meaning: Immediately, Quickly, Suddenly, In a moment or flash
related.comparison: Both convey suddenness. いきなり emphasizes lack of warning, as in いきなり泣き出した (She suddenly burst into tears). たちまち highlights speed or immediacy, as in 話題はたちまち広まった (The topic spread immediately).

related.title: いつの間にか
related.meaning: Suddenly, Unnoticed, Without noticing, Too soon
related.comparison: いきなり highlights the suddenness or surprise of an action, as in いきなり泣き出した (She suddenly burst into tears). いつの間にか describes a change that happened unnoticed, focusing on the speaker’s realization, as in いつの間にか夜になっていた (Before I knew it, night had fallen).

related.title: だす
related.meaning: To suddenly begin, To start doing
related.comparison: Both だす and いきなり express sudden action, but they differ in usage. だす attaches to verb stems to mean 'to suddenly start doing something.' For example, 動き出す 'begin to move'. In contrast, いきなり appears before verbs or phrases, like 'suddenly' in English. They can also be used together, as in いきなり動き出した ('It suddenly started moving.').

related.title: どんなに〜ても
related.meaning: No matter how
related.comparison: Both express 'no matter how', but どんなに〜ても is used with qualities or actions, e.g. どんなに忙しくても行きます ('No matter how busy I am, I'll go'), while いくら〜でも is used with amounts or quantities, e.g. いくら頼んでも無理です ('No matter how much you ask, it's impossible').

related.title: どうしても
related.meaning: Regardless of, No matter what
related.comparison: どうしても and いくら〜でも both express emphasis but in different ways. どうしても is used when something must happen or when the speaker is determined, e.g. どうしても行きたい ('I want to go no matter what'). いくら〜でも means 'no matter how much' or 'how many', often used with amounts or degrees to show that the outcome won't change, e.g. いくら頼んでも無理です ('No matter how much you ask, it's impossible').

related.title: する
related.meaning: To do, To make into
related.comparison: Both いたす and する express performing an action, similar to the English 'do.' The key difference is politeness: する is the standard, neutral term used in everyday contexts, while いたす is a humble expression used to show respect by 'lowering' your actions (or those of your in-group) in relation to another person.

related.title: お〜する
related.meaning: I humbly do, I am obliged to do, Humble speech
related.comparison: These are both humble forms used to describe one's own actions politely. お～する is used with regular verbs, such as 手伝う 'help' becoming お手伝いする (same meaning, but in a deferential sense). In contrast, いたす is the humble form of する and is used instead of お～する when using する itself. For example, 連絡いたします 'I will contact you.' is the humble form of 連絡する (same meaning).

related.title: なさる
related.meaning: To do (Honorific)
related.comparison: いたす is the humble form of する, used when talking about your own actions to someone respected. For example, 連絡いたします means 'I'll contact you.' in a deferential way. なさる is the opposite: the honorific form used when referring to the respected person's actions, as in ゴルフをなさいます ('He/she plays golf.').

related.title: つい
related.meaning: Accidentally, Unconsciously, Against one's better judgement
related.comparison: Both describe actions that occur without full conscious control, but they differ in nuance. いつの間にか emphasizes that something happened without the speaker noticing, e.g. いつの間にか春になっていた ('Before I knew it, spring had come.'). つい highlights that an action was done inadvertently or against one's better judgment, and often pairs with 〜てしまう or 〜ちゃう, e.g. つい甘いものを食べてしまった ('I ended up eating sweets without meaning to.').

related.title: たちまち
related.meaning: Immediately, Quickly, Suddenly, In a moment or flash
related.comparison: たちまち emphasizes immediacy, describing something that happens instantly and visibly, as in 雨がたちまち降り出した (It suddenly started raining). いつの間にか emphasizes that a change occurred without the speaker noticing when, as in いつの間にか眠っていた (At some point I fell asleep without realizing).

related.title: いきなり
related.meaning: For no reason, Suddenly, All of a sudden
related.comparison: いきなり highlights the suddenness or surprise of an action, as in いきなり泣き出した (She suddenly burst into tears). いつの間にか describes a change that happened unnoticed, focusing on the speaker’s realization, as in いつの間にか夜になっていた (Before I knew it, night had fallen).

related.title: お～になる
related.meaning: To do (Honorific)
related.comparison: These are honorific forms used to respectfully describe others' actions. お～になる is used with regular verbs, e.g. 書く 'write' becoming お書きになる (same meaning, but showing respect to the writer). いらっしゃる is a special honorific form that replaces 行く, 来る, and いる, for which the お～になる form is not used.

related.title: がいる
related.meaning: To be, There is,  To remain (in a state)
related.comparison: がいる combines the particle が with the verb いる to express the existence of living or animate beings, like people or animals. いらっしゃる is the honorific equivalent of いる, as well as 行く and 来る. In other words, がいらっしゃる is the honorific version of がいる, and いらっしゃいますか is the honorific equivalent of いますか, 行きますか, or 来ますか.

related.title: くる
related.meaning: To come
related.comparison: Both いらっしゃる and くる express movement toward the speaker, similar to the English 'come.' The key difference is politeness: くる is the neutral verb used in everyday contexts, while いらっしゃる is an honorific verb used to show respect for someone's movement. Note that いらっしゃる also serves as the honorific form of いる, いく, and くる.

related.title: へいく
related.meaning: To go to, To head toward
related.comparison: Both いらっしゃる and いく express movement away from the speaker, similar to the English 'go.' The key difference is politeness: いく is a neutral verb used in everyday contexts, while いらっしゃる is a polite, honorific verb used to show respect for someone's movement. Note that いらっしゃる also serves as the honorific form of いる, くる, and いく.

related.title: という
related.meaning: Called, Named, Known as, As much as
related.comparison: いわゆる and という both convey the idea of something being referred to by a name, but they function differently. いわゆる comes before a noun to mean 'the so-called (A)'. という appears between two nouns in the form AというB ('a B called A'). Because they serve different grammatical roles, they can appear together, e.g. いわゆる外来語というもの ('What is known as a so-called loanword')

related.title: というのは
related.meaning: ~ Known as ~ is, ~ Called ~ is, ~ Means ~, Because
related.comparison: いわゆる and という both express the idea of something being 'called' something, but they are used very differently. いわゆる precedes a noun to mean 'the so-called (A)'. というのは takes a word or phrase and introduces it as a topic to be defined or explained. Because they serve different roles, they can be used together, e.g. いわゆる五月病というのは ('This thing known as the so-called May blues is...')

related.title: 一方だ
related.meaning: More and more, Continue to, Keep on, -er and -er
related.comparison: Both 一方だ and いよいよ express change, but differ in usage. 一方だ follows verbs, often in formal or objective contexts to describe trends, e.g. '物価は上がる一方だ' ('Prices keep rising'). いよいよ suggests something is finally or imminently happening, often with anticipation, e.g. 'いよいよ試合が始まる' ('The match is about to begin').

related.title: 遂に
related.meaning: Finally, At last
related.comparison: Both 遂に and いよいよ can mean 'finally', but with different focus. 遂に indicates that something has occurred at last, often after struggle or delay, e.g. '遂に大学に入った' ('I finally entered university'). いよいよ emphasizes an approaching climax or moment of anticipation, e.g. 'いよいよ卒業の日が来た' ('Graduation day has finally arrived').

related.title: とうとう
related.meaning: Finally, After all
related.comparison: Both とうとう and いよいよ can mean 'finally', but with different nuances. とうとう is used when something expected happens after effort or waiting, e.g. とうとう大学に入った ('I finally got into university'). いよいよ expresses anticipation for something imminent, e.g. いよいよ夏が来た ('At last, summer has come.').

related.title: う-Verb (Past)
related.meaning: う - Verbs (Past tense)
related.comparison: The dictionary form of an う-verb (e.g., 買う) is the base form used to describe current, habitual, or future actions (e.g., 買う = 'I buy' or 'I will buy'). In contrast, the past form (-た form) expresses completed actions or past states (e.g., 買った = 'I bought'). Both forms commonly appear in grammar patterns that require the casual/plain form, such as ～と思います ('I think').

related.title: Verb + て
related.meaning: And, Then (Linking events)
related.comparison: The dictionary form of an う-verb (e.g., 買う) is its base form, describing non-past actions (e.g., 買う = 'I buy' or 'I will buy'). It's used casually or as part of other grammatical patterns. In contrast, the て-form (verb + て) connects verbs to show sequences or to form requests and various other grammar structures (e.g., 買ってください = 'Please buy...').

related.title: る-Verb (Dictionary)
related.meaning: る + ます (Ichidan Verbs)
related.comparison: る-Verbs and う-Verbs are the two main verb types in Japanese. Their dictionary forms are the basic forms used in dictionaries and casual non-past sentences (e.g., 食べる = 'I eat' or 'I will eat'). The difference between these verb types is in their conjugation patterns, not their meaning or nuance. Be careful: not all verbs ending in る are る-Verbs (for example, 帰る 'to return' is actually an う-Verb).

related.title: Verbs (Non-past)
related.meaning: (Non-past)
related.comparison: In Japanese, the dictionary form and the non-past form of verbs are identical. For example, 買う is both the dictionary form and the non-past form of the verb 'to buy.' Unlike in English, this form can be used as is to express future actions ('I will buy'), habitual actions ('I regularly buy'), or general truths ('People buy a lot around Christmas').

related.title: る-Verb (Negative)
related.meaning: Will/Does/Do (not)
related.comparison: The dictionary form is the base form of the verb, used for habitual or future actions. The negative form expresses that an action doesn't happen or a state doesn't exist. For る-Verbs, the negative is formed by changing る to ない (e.g., 食べる → 食べない). う-Verbs have different conjugation patterns. Be careful: not all verbs ending in る are る-Verbs—for example, 帰る ('to return') is actually an う-Verb.

related.title: う-Verb (Negative)
related.meaning: Will/Does/Do (not)
related.comparison: These forms express the negative ('does not' or 'will not') of う-Verbs, with a difference in tense. The う-Verb (Negative) expresses a lack of future or habitual action (e.g., 買わない = 'I don't buy' or 'I won't buy'). The う-Verb (Negative-Past) expresses a lack of past action (e.g., 買わなかった = 'I didn't buy'). Both are plain forms used in casual speech or other grammatical patterns.

related.title: う-Verb (Past)
related.meaning: う - Verbs (Past tense)
related.comparison: These two forms are used with う-Verbs to express the idea of having done or not having done a particular action in the past. The difference is whether the meaning is affirmative ('did') or negative ('did not'). For example, 買った means 'I bought,' while 買わなかった means 'I did not buy.' Both are plain forms used in casual speech or as part of other grammatical forms.

related.title: る-Verb (Negative-Past)
related.meaning: る - Verbs (Negative-past tense)
related.comparison: Both forms express the negative past ('did not'), but the formation differs depending on whether the verb is a る-Verb or う-Verb. For example, 食べなかった (for 食べる, a る-Verb) means 'I did not eat,' while 買わなかった (for 買う, a う-Verb) means 'I did not buy.' Both are plain forms used in casual speech or other grammatical patterns.

antonyms.title: る-Verb (Negative)
antonyms.comparison: The past form of a verb expresses completed actions or past states, while the negative form expresses actions or states that do not occur. る-Verbs form their negative by changing る to ない (e.g., 食べる → 食べない), whereas う-Verbs form the past tense differently (e.g., 買う → 買った). Remember, not all verbs ending in る are る-Verbs, for example, 帰る ('to return') is an う-verb.

related.title: う-Verb (Dictionary)
related.meaning: う + ます (Godan Verbs)
related.comparison: The dictionary form of an う-verb (e.g., 買う) is the base form used to describe current, habitual, or future actions (e.g., 買う = 'I buy' or 'I will buy'). In contrast, the past form (-た form) expresses completed actions or past states (e.g., 買った = 'I bought'). Both forms commonly appear in grammar patterns that require the casual/plain form, such as ～と思います ('I think').

related.title: Verb + て
related.meaning: And, Then (Linking events)
related.comparison: The う-Verb (Past) expresses a completed action or one that took place in the past, similar to '-ed' in English. The Verb + て form looks similar but with た replaced by て. It is used to connect actions in chronological order or in other grammar patterns, such as asking a favor (てください) or permission (てもいいか). The て form has no tense; tense is determined by what follows it in the sentence.

related.title: う-Verb (Negative-Past)
related.meaning: う - Verbs (Negative-past tense)
related.comparison: These two forms are used with う-Verbs to express the idea of having done or not having done a particular action in the past. The difference is whether the meaning is affirmative ('did') or negative ('did not'). For example, 買った means 'I bought,' while 買わなかった means 'I did not buy.' Both are plain forms used in casual speech or as part of other grammatical forms.

related.title: だった・でした
related.meaning: Was, Were (Past tense)
related.comparison: The past form of う-Verbs expresses a completed action or an event that took place in the past, similar to the English '-ed.' In contrast, だった and でした are used after nouns to express the past tense, indicating that something 'was' or 'were' in a certain state. While the meaning (something happened or existed in the past) is the same, the grammatical expression differs depending on the word type (verb vs. noun).

related.title: ていた
related.meaning: Was doing ~
related.comparison: The う-Verb (Past) expresses a completed action or one that took place in the past, similar to '-ed' in English. The ていた form conveys an ongoing action or state in the past, similar to 'was -ing' in English. In other words, ていた indicates an action that was continuous or repeated, while the regular past form describes a single, completed action.

related.title: Verb［た・ている］+ Noun
related.meaning: Verb modified noun, Relative clause
related.comparison: The う-Verb (Past) expresses completed actions or past states. Unlike English, verbs can directly modify nouns, similar to い-Adjectives. For example, 買った本 means 'the book [I] bought.' This typically applies only to the plain/casual verb form, while the polite ます form is used at the end of sentences or clauses.

related.title: る-Verb (Past)
related.meaning: る - Verbs (Past tense)
related.comparison: The past form of verbs expresses a completed action or an action that took place in the past, similar to the English '-ed.' It is a plain form often used in casual conversation or as part of other grammatical structures. The past form differs for る-Verbs and う-Verbs (e.g., 食べる becomes 食べた, and 買う becomes 買った), but the meaning of the conjugation is the same.

related.title: る-Verb (Dictionary)
related.meaning: る + ます (Ichidan Verbs)
related.comparison: The dictionary form of verbs is their base form, expressing habitual or future actions. The past form indicates completed actions or past states. The difference between る-Verbs and う-Verbs is how they conjugate into the past, not in their meaning. For example, る-Verbs change る to た (食べる → 食べた), while う-Verbs follow different patterns (読む → 読んだ). Be aware that not all verbs ending in る are る-Verbs—帰る ('to return') is an う-Verb.

related.title: Verbs (Non-past)
related.meaning: (Non-past)
related.comparison: The non-past (or dictionary) form of a verb expresses habitual or future actions, while the past form indicates completed actions or past states. For う-Verbs, the last kana changes before adding た (or だ) to form the past tense. Both forms are plain and used in casual conversation or other grammar patterns.

related.title: 中
related.meaning: During, While, Throughout, In the process of
related.comparison: Both expressions refer to a continued state, but in different ways. うちに means 'do (B) while (A),' that is, during the ideal time or condition for (A), e.g. 熱いうちに食べてね ('Eat it while it's hot.') In contrast, 中 attaches to nouns and simply means 'during (A)' or 'in the middle of (A)', e.g. 会議中 ('in the middle of a meeting'), 授業中に携帯が鳴った ('My phone rang during class.')

related.title: の間に
related.meaning: While, During, Between, Period
related.comparison: Both の間に and うちに can express 'while' or 'during', but are used in different contexts. の間に simply conveys the idea of 'while (A), (B)', e.g. 映画を観ている間に眠ってしまった ('I fell asleep while/during watching a movie.') In contrast, うちに carries the nuance of doing something during an ideal time or condition, e.g. 明るいうちに帰ろう ('Let's go home while it's light out.')

related.title: ないうちに
related.meaning: Before it becomes, Before ~ occurs, Without
related.comparison: うちに means 'while' a state persists, implying that an action is taken during that time, e.g. 明るいうちに帰る ('Go home while it's still light.') ないうちに means 'before' a negative state ends, emphasizing the importance of acting before something happens, e.g. 雨が降らないうちに出かける ('Leave before it rains.') Both suggest taking action while a condition remains unchanged.

related.title: 最中に
related.meaning: In the middle of
related.comparison: Both can be translated as 'while', but differ in nuance. うちに emphasizes doing something before a condition changes, e.g. 熱いうちに食べてね ('Eat it while it's hot.') 最中に highlights something occurring in the very middle of another action, e.g. 会議の最中に電話が鳴った ('The phone rang in the middle of the meeting.')

related.title: とき
related.meaning: When, At the time of
related.comparison: とき expresses the time of an action or event, like 'when' in English. It can be used with nouns as in 雨の時 'when it rains', verbs as in 父が帰ってきた時 'when Dad came home', or adjectives as in 暑い時 'when it's hot', and can describe one-time or repeated actions. うちに means 'while' or 'during,' often with the nuance that it's the ideal or only time to act, e.g. 熱いうちに食べてね ('Eat it while it's hot.').

related.title: ているところだ
related.meaning: In the process of doing ~ right now/at this moment
related.comparison: ているところだ conveys that an action is in progress at that moment, with the nuance that the speaker is occupied with it, e.g. 晩ご飯を食べているところだ ('I'm in the middle of eating dinner.'). In contrast, うちに describes doing an action while a certain state is ongoing, often with the nuance that it's the ideal time, e.g. 熱いうちに食べましょう ('Let's eat it while it's hot.').

related.title: ついでに
related.meaning: While you are at it, On the occasion
related.comparison: Both can be translated as 'while', but with different implications. ついでに means 'while you're at it' and adds a convenient extra action, e.g. 買い物のついでに銀行に寄った ('I stopped by the bank while shopping.') うちに emphasizes doing something before a condition changes, e.g. 熱いうちに食べてね ('Eat it while it's hot.')

related.title: ているあいだに
related.meaning: While, During the time that ~
related.comparison: Both patterns can express the idea of 'while,' but differ in nuance. ているあいだに refers to something happening during another ongoing action. For example, 勉強しているあいだに電話がかかってきた means 'The phone rang while I was studying.' うちに emphasizes doing something while a particular state still lasts. For example, 早いうちに帰ろう means 'Let's go home while it's still early.'

related.title: ながら
related.meaning: While ~ing, During, As
related.comparison: ながら attaches to verb stems to express performing one action simultaneously with another, e.g. 音楽を聴きながら走る 'listen to music while running'. In contrast, うちに can follow various words or phrases to express doing something while a certain state or condition persists, e.g. 若いうちにマラソンを走りたい ('I want to run a marathon while I'm young.').

related.title: う-Verb (Negative-Past)
related.meaning: う - Verbs (Negative-past tense)
related.comparison: These forms express the negative ('does not' or 'will not') of う-Verbs, with a difference in tense. The う-Verb (Negative) expresses a lack of future or habitual action (e.g., 買わない = 'I don't buy' or 'I won't buy'). The う-Verb (Negative-Past) expresses a lack of past action (e.g., 買わなかった = 'I didn't buy'). Both are plain forms used in casual speech or other grammatical patterns.

related.title: Verbs (Non-past)
related.meaning: (Non-past)
related.comparison: The non-past or dictionary form is the base form of a verb, used to express habitual or future actions To express the negative, change the final kana and add ない (e.g., 買わない = 'I do not buy' or 'I will not buy'). Both the non-past and negative forms are plain forms used in casual conversation or other grammar patterns.

related.title: じゃない
related.meaning: Is not, Isn't
related.comparison: The negative form of an う-verb indicates 'does not' or 'will not' perform an action (e.g., 買わない = 'I don't buy' or 'I won't buy'). On the other hand, じゃない (casual form of ではない, the negative of だ) negates nouns and な-adjectives (e.g., 元気じゃない = 'not feeling good'). Both express negation, but each applies to different word types.

synonyms.title: てください
synonyms.comparison: てください is a polite, neutral way to say 'Please do (something),' and is used with strangers or equals. The honorific form, お〜ください, is used for superiors or customers. However, some verbs like 食べる and 行く have special honorific forms, such as 召し上がる and いらっしゃる, which are used instead.

antonyms.title: ないでください
antonyms.comparison: ないでください is a polite way to ask someone not to do something. It's neutral and can be used with strangers or people of equal status. In contrast, お〜ください is an honorific request form used with superiors, customers, or others of higher status. Some verbs, like 食べる and 行く, have special honorific forms such as 召し上がる and いらっしゃる, which are used instead of the お〜ください structure.

related.title: なさい
related.meaning: Command to do
related.comparison: Both forms ask someone to do something, but the tone is different. なさい is a polite but direct command, often used by parents or teachers, as in これを使いなさい ('Use this.'). お～ください is a respectful request form, used to politely encourage someone like a customer or superior to do something, as in これをお使いください ('Please use this.').

related.title: お～願う
related.meaning: Please, Could you please
related.comparison: お～ください and お～願う are both honorific request forms but differ in nuance and setting. お～ください is often used when politely encouraging someone to take an action, such as お上がりください ('Please come in.'). お～願う is more formal and is typically used in public announcements or formal situations. For example, ご注意願います ('Please be careful.') might be used in a train announcement.

related.title: いらっしゃる
related.meaning: To be (Honorific), To come (Honorific), To go (Honorific)
related.comparison: These are honorific forms used to respectfully describe others' actions. お～になる is used with regular verbs, e.g. 書く 'write' becoming お書きになる (same meaning, but showing respect to the writer). いらっしゃる is a special honorific form that replaces 行く, 来る, and いる, for which the お～になる form is not used.

related.title: なさる
related.meaning: To do (Honorific)
related.comparison: Both are honorific forms. お～になる is used for regular verbs, like 答える 'answer' becoming お答えになる (same meaning, but showing respect to the answerer). なさる is the specific honorific form of する, e.g. ゴルフをなさる  means '(He/she) plays golf.' in an honorific/respectful sense. Since する is irregular, it does not use the お～になる pattern.

synonyms.title: てください
synonyms.comparison: てください is a basic way to express a polite request (e.g., 'Please do…'). It is polite and neutral, and can be used with strangers or people equal in status to you. In comparison, お～願う is a very polite and formal expression, often used in professional contexts (such as to a customer or client) or formal announcements.

antonyms.title: ないでください
antonyms.comparison: ないでください is used to ask someone not to do something in a polite way. It is relatively neutral, and could be used with strangers or people equal in status to you. In contrast, お～願う is an extremely polite and formal way to make a request, often used in professional contexts (such as to a customer or client) or formal announcements.

related.title: なさい
related.meaning: Command to do
related.comparison: Both forms are used to ask someone to do something, but with different levels of formality. なさい is a polite command often used from superiors to subordinates, as in 待ちなさい ('Wait.'). お～願う is extremely humble and formal, used in business or public announcements, as in お待ち願います ('We request that you to wait.').

related.title: お～ください
related.meaning: Please do (Honorific)
related.comparison: お～ください and お～願う are both honorific request forms but differ in nuance and setting. お～ください is often used when politely encouraging someone to take an action, such as お上がりください ('Please come in.'). お～願う is more formal and is typically used in public announcements or formal situations. For example, ご注意願います ('Please be careful.') might be used in a train announcement.

related.title: いたす
related.meaning: To do (Humble), To venerably do
related.comparison: These are both humble forms used to describe one's own actions politely. お～する is used with regular verbs, such as 手伝う 'help' becoming お手伝いする (same meaning, but in a deferential sense). In contrast, いたす is the humble form of する and is used instead of お～する when using する itself. For example, 連絡いたします 'I will contact you.' is the humble form of 連絡する (same meaning).

synonyms.title: だいたい
synonyms.meaning: Most・mostly, Adequately・generally, For the most part, Roughly・approximately, In the first place
synonyms.comparison: Both can mean 'most', as in だいたいの人／おおよその人 'most people'. They can also both mean 'approximately', e.g. だいたい３時間／おおよそ３時間 'about three hours'. However, おおよそ is much more formal and mainly used in writing. だいたい can also express 'in the first place', e.g. だいたいなんでいるの？ ('Why are you here in the first place?)' or 'roughly/adequately', e.g. だいたいでいいから ('Roughly is fine.), while おおよそ cannot.

related.title: だいたい
related.meaning: Most・mostly, Adequately・generally, For the most part, Roughly・approximately, In the first place
related.comparison: These can be used interchangeably to express roughly or about. だいたい is more casual and common in daily speech, as in だいたい百人来た (About a hundred people came). おおよそ sounds slightly more formal or written, as in おおよそ見当がつく (I can roughly guess). Note that だいたい can also appear as a negative interjection, as in だいたい何でいるの？ (Why are you here in the first place?), while おおよそ cannot be used this way.3

related.title: ために
related.meaning: Due to, Because of, As a result of
related.comparison: ために and せいで can both express the idea of 'due to (A)', but they differ in that おかげで almost always describes a desirable result, while ために can be used with either a positive or negative nuance. ために can also, in some cases, mean 'for the purpose of (A)', while おかげで cannot be used in this way.

related.title: につき
related.meaning: Due to, On account of, Each or every
related.comparison: Both express cause, but differ in tone and formality. おかげで implies a positive result with gratitude, e.g. 先生のおかげで合格できました ('Thanks to my teacher, I passed.') につき states a neutral reason, often used in formal notices, e.g. 雨天につき中止します ('Due to rain, it is canceled.') It also only follows nouns.

related.title: せいで
related.meaning: Because of, Due to, Blame
related.comparison: Both mean 'because of', but with opposite tone. おかげで expresses a thankful cause, e.g. 先生のおかげで合格できた ('Thanks to my teacher, I passed.') せいで places blame for something negative, e.g. 雨のせいで試合が中止になった ('The game was canceled because of the rain.')

related.title: そのため(に)
related.meaning: For that reason, Because of that, To that end
related.comparison: Both express cause and effect, but with different tone and usage. おかげで implies a positive result and gratitude, e.g. 先生のおかげで合格できた ('Thanks to my teacher, I passed.') そのため(に) is neutral, objective, and often appears at the start of a sentence. It can also mean 'for that purpose'.

synonyms.title: かもしれない
synonyms.comparison: おそらく and かもしれない both express possibility, but differ in certainty and placement. かもしれない comes at the end of sentences or phrases and means 'might' or 'may', e.g. 行くかもしれない ('I might go.'). おそらく appears at the beginning and expresses a stronger likelihood, e.g. おそらく行くだろう ('Most likely, I'll go.').

synonyms.title: もしかしたら
synonyms.comparison: Both express possibility, but differ in how likely something seems. おそらく suggests a high chance and is often used with だろう or でしょう, e.g. おそらく来るでしょう 'Most likely, he will come.' もしかしたら suggests a lower chance or speculation, and pairs with かもしれない or かもしれません, e.g. もしかしたら雨が降るかもしれない 'Perhaps it will rain.'

related.title: ～ずつ
related.meaning: One at a time, Per, Each, Gradually
related.comparison: Both deal with repetition or intervals, but in different ways. ～ずつ means 'each' or 'at a time' and attaches to quantity expressions, e.g. 一つずつください 'Give me one of each.' おきに follows time or distance intervals to mean 'every (A)', e.g. ５分おきに電車が来る 'Trains come every five minutes.'

related.title: たびに
related.meaning: Each time, Every time, Whenever
related.comparison: Both おきに and たびに describe repeated occurrences, but are used differently. おきに attaches to time expressions to mean 'at intervals of (A)', e.g. '５分おきに電車が来る' ('Trains arrive every five minutes'). たびに describes a response that recurs whenever a particular thing happens, e.g. '見るたびに笑ってしまう' ('I end up laughing every time I see it').

related.title: に (Frequency)
related.meaning: Times (In each time period), Every, Frequency of
related.comparison: Both expressions show frequency, but in different ways. The に (frequency) construction combines a time period with a number to mean '(A) times per (B),' e.g. 一週間に２回 'twice a week'. おきに attaches to a number + counter to mean 'at intervals of (A),' e.g. 二日おきに学校に行く ('I go to school every other day.' meaning 'every two days').

related.title: ごとに
related.meaning: Each, Every
related.comparison: ごとに and おきに both express regular intervals, but with a key difference. ２日ごとに means 'every two days', i.e. the event happens on the second day. ２日おきに means 'at intervals of two days', or in other words every third day. A helpful tip: おきに comes from 置く, which can have the meaning of leaving a gap or space.

related.title: につけ
related.meaning: Whenever, Every time something happens
related.comparison: Both おきに and につけ describe repeated occurrences, but are used differently. おきに attaches to time expressions to mean 'at intervals of (A)', e.g. '５分おきに電車が来る' ('Trains arrive every five minutes'). につけ is used with verbs and nouns to express a habitual response, e.g. '彼の声を聞くにつけ' ('whenever I hear his voice').

related.title: まい～のように
related.meaning: Almost every ~, Nearly every ~, On a ~ basis
related.comparison: Both まい～のように and おきに express frequency, but in different ways. まい～のように is used with 'every~' time expressions to mean 'almost every (A)', e.g. 毎日のように 'almost every day'. In contrast, おきに attaches to a number + counter expression to mean 'at intervals of (A),' e.g. 一日おきに 'every other day' or 'at intervals of one day'.

related.title: 上に
related.meaning: As well, Besides, In addition to
related.comparison: 上に and おまけに both introduce additional information, but differ in tone. 上に is neutral and formal, as in 彼は親切な上に、頭もいい (He is kind, and on top of that, smart). おまけに often emphasizes worsening conditions, as in 雨だった。おまけに風も強かった (It was raining. On top of that, the wind was strong).

related.title: それに
related.meaning: And, Besides, Moreover, In addition
related.comparison: それに and おまけに both add information. それに is neutral and just adds a point that logically follows. おまけに adds something extra or unexpected, often with a tone of surprise or bonus, e.g. おいしかったし、おまけに安かった ('It was delicious, and cheap too!').

related.title: その上
related.meaning: Besides, In addition, Furthermore
related.comparison: その上 and おまけに both introduce additional information, but differ in nuance. その上 is neutral and simply adds or emphasizes information. おまけに often conveys a negative situation or unexpected outcome, sometimes with a nuance of irritation or frustration, as in 雨だった。おまけに風も強かった (It was raining. On top of that, the wind was strong).

related.title: しかも
related.meaning: Moreover, Furthermore
related.comparison: Both add extra information. しかも is neutral, adding notable facts, as in 彼は優秀だ。しかも親切だ (He’s talented, and moreover, kind). おまけに can add positive or negative on top of that nuance, as in 雨だった。おまけに風も強かった (It was raining, and on top of that, the wind was strong).

synonyms.title: と
synonyms.comparison: および and と both connect nouns, similar to the English 'and,' but differ in formality. および is very formal, mostly used in written documents (such as business or academic writing) or formal speeches. In contrast, と is common in everyday conversation and casual writing. Both particles can list items, people, or abstract concepts.

related.title: や
related.meaning: Things like, And the like
related.comparison: Both および and や are used to list items, but they differ in nuance and formality. および is a highly formal expression similar to と, often implying a complete list. It is used almost exclusively in writing and structured, formal speech. In contrast, や is less formal and suggests a partial list, such as 'A, B, and things like that.'

antonyms.title: はじめる
antonyms.comparison: はじめる and おわる can be attached to the stem of verbs to express the contrasting ideas of 'beginning' and 'finishing' an action, respectively. For example, 読みはじめる means 'to begin reading', while 読みおわる means 'to finish reading'.

antonyms.title: だす
antonyms.meaning: To suddenly begin, To start doing
antonyms.comparison: おわる and だす both attach to verb stems but express opposite ideas, like finishing vs. starting an action. おわる is used for completing almost any action, e.g. 本を読み終わった ('I finished reading the book.'). だす often describes actions that begin suddenly or unexpectedly, e.g. 雨が降り出した ('It started to rain.').

related.title: てしまう・ちゃう
related.meaning: To do something by accident, To finish completely・totally, Unfortunately
related.comparison: おわる attaches to verb stems to mean 'to finish doing (A)', e.g. 食べおわった ('I finished eating.'). In contrast, てしまう・ちゃう uses the て-form and can also mean doing something completely, but often adds a nuance of regret, finality, or lack of intention, e.g. 食べてしまった・食べちゃった ('I ate it and now it's gone.' or 'I didn't mean to eat it.').

related.title: 切る
related.meaning: To do something completely to the end, Utterly, Resolutely
related.comparison: Both 切る and おわる attach to verb stems to express finishing an action, but with different nuances. おわる simply marks completion, e.g. 使いおわった ('I finished using it.'). In contrast, 切る often implies using something up, e.g. バターを使い切った ('I used up all the butter.') or doing something to the very end with full effort, e.g. ゴールまで走り切った ('I ran all the way to the finish line.').

related.title: 上がる・上げる
related.meaning: Something is finished, To finish something, Something has come to an end, To do something completely, To do something through
related.comparison: While おわる and 上がる・上げる both attach to verb stems to express finishing an action, their nuances differ. おわる can describe completing any action, e.g. ケーキを食べおわった ('I finished eating the cake.') or 映画を観おわった ('I finished watching a movie.'). 上がる・上げる is typically used when the action results in some completed product, e.g. 論文を書きあげた ('I finished writing the paper.').

related.title: 込む ②
related.meaning: Remain, To do thoroughly, Deeply, Intensely
related.comparison: Both 込む and おわる attach to verb stems but express different nuances. おわる simply marks the end of an action, e.g. ケーキを食べおわった ('I finished eating the cake.') or 映画を観おわった ('I finished watching a movie.'). 込む adds the sense of doing something thoroughly and staying in that state, e.g. 黙り込んだ ('He fell completely silent and stayed that way.').

synonyms.title: か
synonyms.comparison: か is the standard question particle, used at the end of sentences to form questions (e.g., どんな本が好きですか？ = 'What kind of books do you like?'). It also appears in the middle of other grammar patterns. かい is a casual, informal question particle often used by older men at the end of yes/no questions (e.g., 元気かい？ = 'Are you feeling okay?').

synonyms.title: かと思ったら・かと思うと
synonyms.slug: かと思ったら-かと思うと
synonyms.meaning: Then again, Just when I thought, No sooner than
synonyms.comparison: か〜ないかのうちに indicates that one action happens immediately after another, focusing on the brief moment, as in ベルが鳴るか鳴らないかのうちに学生が飛び出した (The students rushed out the moment the bell rang). かと思ったら・かと思うと also shows quick succession, but emphasizes suddenness or the speaker’s perception, as in 雨が降ったかと思ったら止んだ (No sooner had it started raining than it stopped).

related.title: 直ちに
related.meaning: At once, Immediately, As soon as
related.comparison: Both express immediacy between actions, but with different focus. 直ちに stresses promptness, often used for direct commands or instructions, e.g. '直ちに医者を呼んでください' ('Call a doctor immediately'). か〜ないかのうちに highlights how closely two actions occur in time, e.g. 'ドアを開けるか開けないかのうちに猫が飛び出した' ('No sooner had I opened the door than the cat jumped out').

related.title: Question-phrase + か
related.meaning: Including questions within sentences
related.comparison: Question-phrase + か embeds a question, e.g. 来るか分からない ('I don't know if they're coming.'). か〜ないかのうちに expresses something happening immediately at a moment of uncertainty, e.g. 来るか来ないかのうちに 'just as they arrived'.

synonyms.title: かい
synonyms.comparison: か is the standard question particle, used at the end of sentences to form questions (e.g., どんな本が好きですか？ = 'What kind of books do you like?'). It also appears in the middle of other grammar patterns. かい is a casual, informal question particle often used by older men at the end of yes/no questions (e.g., 元気かい？ = 'Are you feeling okay?').

antonyms.title: よ
antonyms.comparison: よ is a common sentence-ending particle used for emphasis, often indicating that the speaker is providing new information (e.g., 面白いですよ = 'It's interesting, I'm telling you!'). In contrast, か is the standard question particle, which has the opposite function, asking for information (e.g., 面白いですか？ = 'Is it interesting?').

related.title: っけ
related.meaning: What is…again?, Was it... that... ?, Did you say...?
related.comparison: か is the standard question particle, used at the end of sentences to ask questions (e.g., どんな本が好きですか？ = 'What kind of books do you like?') and also within other grammar patterns. っけ, on the other hand, is a casual sentence-ending particle used when trying to recall something (e.g., 名前はなんだっけ？ = 'What was his name again?').

related.title: るところだ
related.meaning: About to, On the verge of
related.comparison: ところだ and かけ both express incomplete actions, but with a key difference. るところだ emphasizes that the action is just about to happen, e.g. リンゴを食べるところだ ('I'm about to eat an apple.'). かけ indicates the action has started but isn't finished, e.g. 食べかけのリンゴ ('a half-eaten apple').

related.title: たて
related.meaning: Freshly ~ed, Just ~ed
related.comparison: Both かけ and たて describe transitional states of actions, but with opposite timing. かけ refers to something that is incomplete or in progress, often implying interrupted action, e.g. 食べかけのパン 'half-eaten bread'. たて, by contrast, refers to something freshly completed, emphasizing newness and immediacy, e.g. 焼きたてのパン 'freshly baked bread'.

related.title: はじめる
related.meaning: To start, To begin to
related.comparison: はじめる attaches to verb stems to mean 'to begin' or 'start' doing something, e.g. 食べはじめる 'to start eating'. In contrast, かけ also attaches to verb stems but indicates something incomplete or in progress, e.g. 食べかけ 'half-eaten'. Forms with かけ function grammatically as nouns, as in 食べかけのバナナ 'a half-eaten banana'.

related.title: だす
related.meaning: To suddenly begin, To start doing
related.comparison: かけ and だす both attach to verb stems and can express the start of an action, but their nuance differs. かけ emphasizes that the action has begun but remains incomplete. For example, 書きかけの小説 'a partially written/incomplete novel.' In contrast, だす highlights the beginning of the action, often with a sense of suddenness. For example, 書き出した ('He suddenly started writing.').

related.title: たほうがいい
related.meaning: It'd be better to, Should do
related.comparison: かた and たほうがいい are distinct grammar patterns both containing 方 (which can be read as かた or ほう) but have different meanings and structures. かた attaches to the verb stem and means 'way of doing (A)' or 'how to (A)', e.g. 書き方 'way of writing' or 'how to write'. In contrast, たほうがいい connects to the past tense of a verb and is used to give advice or make suggestions.

related.title: かな
related.meaning: I wonder
related.comparison: Both かな and かしら can express doubt, similar to the English 'I wonder if (A)'. Comparing the two, かな is the more common and casual expression, used by both genders in a wide variety of contexts and settings. In contrast, かしら has a softer tone that can come off as more polite and feminine.

related.title: ～のだろうか
related.meaning: I wonder...
related.comparison: Both express uncertainty or internal questioning, like 'I wonder'. ～のだろうか (or the polite ～のでしょうか) is neutral and used by all genders. かしら has a softer, feminine tone and is rarely used by men. The explanatory の adds a nuance of seeking an answer, which かしら does not.

related.title: を兼ねて
related.meaning: And also, Also (partly) to, Also (partly) for (the purpose of), At the same time, To double as, When … also, While … as well, And as
related.comparison: Both are similar in that they describe doing something 'along with' or 'incidentally' to another action. かたがた is particularly formal and used especially in writing, e.g. お礼かたがたお伺いしたく存じます ('I hope to visit you, in part to thank you.'), while を兼ねて (and variant forms like も兼ねて) are still somewhat formal but used more widely, e.g. ダイエットも兼ねて毎朝散歩する 'to walk every morning, in part for purposes of a diet'.

related.title: どんなに〜ても
related.meaning: No matter how
related.comparison: Both are similar in that they describe doing something 'incidentally' to another action. かたがた is particularly formal and used especially in writing, e.g. お礼かたがたお伺いしたく存じます ('I hope to visit you, in part to thank you.'), while ついでに is far more casual and can be used for even simple daily expressions, e.g. 買い物のついでに郵便局に寄る 'to stop at the post office while going out shopping'.

related.title: 一方で
related.meaning: On the other hand, At the same time, While
related.comparison: かたわら and 一方で both describe two actions or conditions, but differ in nuance. かたわら implies doing something secondary alongside a main activity, e.g. '彼は本業のかたわら小説を書いている' ('He writes novels alongside his main job'). 一方で marks contrast or coexistence between broader situations, e.g. '一方で都市化が進んでいる' ('Meanwhile, urbanization is advancing').

related.title: ながら
related.meaning: While ~ing, During, As
related.comparison: かたわら and ながら are similar in that both can describe performing two tasks at once, but differ in nuance and usage. かたわら follows noun + の or the dictionary form of verbs, and typically implies performing a secondary action alongside a main one. It is often used with habitual actions, such as doing a side job or hobby along with a main job. In contrast, ながら is a more versatile way of expressing two simultaneous actions.

related.title: なぜなら〜から
related.meaning: Because, The reason is, That is because
related.comparison: These phrases are related in that they can be used similarly, as なぜなら〜から could be rephrased as なぜかというと. They are different in that なぜなら〜から always states the reason for something previously said, like 'as for why that is, it's because (A)', while ～かというと① can be used to clarify what you are explaining, for example by saying なぜ嫌いかというと 'as for why I dislike it'.

related.title: ～かというと ②
related.meaning: If you ask me..., If I had to say, If I were to say, If I were to choose one, I'd rather
related.comparison: The pattern ～かというと has two main uses. ～かというと① introduces reasoning or a cause mid-sentence by echoing a question, e.g. なぜ行かなかったかというと 'If you're asking why I didn't go...' ～かというと② qualifies a personal opinion in response to an implied question, like 'If I were asked' or 'If I had to say,' e.g. どこに住みたいかというと、やっぱり大阪がいい 'If you're asking where I'd like to live, I have to go with Osaka.'

related.title: なぜなら〜から
related.meaning: Because, The reason is, That is because
related.comparison: These phrases are related in that they can be used similarly, as なぜなら〜から could be rephrased as なぜかというと. They are different in that なぜなら〜から always explicitly states a reason for something, like 'as for why, it's because (A)', while ～かというと② can simply be used to comment on something, e.g. 好きか嫌いかというと 'if I were to say whether I like it or dislike it'.

related.title: ～かというと ①
related.meaning: Why? Well..., Why? Because..., If you ask me why..., The reason why is..., It is because...
related.comparison: The pattern ～かというと has two main uses. ～かというと① introduces reasoning or a cause mid-sentence by echoing a question, e.g. なぜ行かなかったかというと 'If you're asking why I didn't go...' ～かというと② qualifies a personal opinion in response to an implied question, like 'If I were asked' or 'If I had to say,' e.g. どこに住みたいかというと、やっぱり大阪がいい 'If you're asking where I'd like to live, I have to go with Osaka.'

synonyms.title: か〜ないかのうちに
synonyms.meaning: Right after, As soon as, Just when, Barely
synonyms.comparison: か〜ないかのうちに indicates that one action happens immediately after another, focusing on the brief moment, as in ベルが鳴るか鳴らないかのうちに学生が飛び出した (The students rushed out the moment the bell rang). かと思ったら・かと思うと also shows quick succession, but emphasizes suddenness or the speaker’s perception, as in 雨が降ったかと思ったら止んだ (No sooner had it started raining than it stopped).

related.title: なり
related.meaning: As soon as, The moment, When
related.comparison: Both describe actions happening immediately after another. かと思ったら・かと思うと adds surprise or contrast, as in 雨が降ったかと思うと止んだ (No sooner had it started raining than it stopped). なり emphasizes immediacy without surprise, as in ドアを開けるなり走り出した (He opened the door and immediately ran out).

related.title: と思いきや
related.meaning: Thought...but, When...thought that...but, Despite having thought...
related.comparison: Both show overturned expectations. かと思ったら・かと思うと stresses suddenness, as in 会ったかと思ったらすぐ帰った (He came and left immediately). と思いきや focuses on realization after expectation was proven wrong, as in 勝つと思いきや負けた (I thought they would win, but they lost).

related.title: 直ちに
related.meaning: At once, Immediately, As soon as
related.comparison: Both involve rapid transitions, but differ in usage. 直ちに is formal and emphasizes urgency, often used in official or written language, e.g. '直ちに避難してください' ('Please evacuate immediately'). かと思ったら・かと思うと is more colloquial and marks an abrupt or surprising change just after a perceived moment, e.g. '転んだかと思ったらすぐ立ち上がりました' ('Just when I thought he'd fallen, he got right back up').

related.title: たとたんに
related.meaning: The instant or moment, As soon as, Just as
related.comparison: Both express actions occurring in close sequence, but differ in focus. たとたんに emphasizes the precise moment with suddenness, e.g. 'ドアを開けたとたんに猫が飛び出した' ('The moment I opened the door, the cat jumped out'). かと思ったら・かと思うと marks a quick change with surprise, e.g. '晴れたかと思ったら、すぐに雨が降り出した' ('Just when I thought it cleared up, it started raining again').

synonyms.title: か否か
synonyms.comparison: Both か否か and かどうか express 'whether or not,' but differ in tone and usage. か否か is highly formal and mostly seen in legal documents, academic writing, or formal reports. かどうか is more common in daily conversation and general writing, making it the default choice in most situations.

related.title: にしても～にしても
related.meaning: Regardless of whether
related.comparison: Both can suggest contrastive options, but with different usage. かどうか focuses on 'whether or not' something is true. にしても～にしても means 'regardless of which', and is used for comparing two alternatives. For example, 楽しいにしてもつまらないにしても行く ('Whether it's fun or boring, I'll go.').

related.title: 〜でも 〜でも
related.meaning: Whether ~ or, Neither ~ or
related.comparison: かどうか expresses uncertainty, as in 来るかどうか分からない ('I don't know whether or not they'll come.'). 〜でも〜でも lists multiple alternatives and suggests the outcome is unchanged. For example, 雨でも晴れでも行く ('Whether it rains or shines, I'll go.').

related.title: Question-phrase + か
related.meaning: Including questions within sentences
related.comparison: Both are embedded question forms. A question phrase plus か makes an indirect question, like 何時に来るか分からない ('I don't know what time they'll come'). かどうか focuses specifically on yes/no questions, like 来るかどうか分からない ('I don't know whether or not they'll come.').

related.title: か何か
related.meaning: Or something, Or something like that
related.comparison: か何か and かどうか look similar but are used differently. か何か follows a noun and means 'or something,' as in お茶か何か飲みますか？ ('Would you like some tea or something?'). かどうか means 'whether or not' and expresses uncertainty, like 面白いかどうか分からない ('I don't know whether or not it's interesting.').

related.title: ～ても～なくても
related.meaning: Whether ~ or not
related.comparison: かどうか expresses uncertainty, like 'whether or not', as in 行くかどうか分からない ('I don't know whether or not they'll go.'). ～ても～なくても presents two opposite possibilities and says the result is unchanged, like 降っても降らなくても行く ('Whether it rains or not, I'm going.').

related.title: にしても
related.meaning: Even though, Even if, Even when, Also
related.comparison: かどうか expresses binary uncertainty, like 'whether or not', as in 行くかどうか分からない ('I don't know whether or not they'll go.'). にしても adds a tone of concession or contrast, like 'even if'. For example, どこに行くにしても 'no matter where (you) go'. The former asks a yes/no-type question, the latter implies acceptance of a situation.

related.title: にしろ～にしろ
related.meaning: Whether… or
related.comparison: Both express the sense of 'whether', but in different ways. かどうか is used to present uncertainty, as in 面白いかどうか分からない ('I don't know whether or not it's interesting.'). にしろ～にしろ expresses that something applies regardless of the case, like 面白いにしろつまらないにしろ観たい ('Whether it's interesting or boring, I want to watch.').

related.title: ～のだろうか
related.meaning: I wonder...
related.comparison: Both かな and ～のだろうか express doubt or curiosity, like 'I wonder if (A)' かな is casual and common in everyday speech or texting. ～のだろうか also can be used in conversation but conveys a more thoughtful or serious tone, suggesting deeper consideration as compared to かな, which can be used for offhand comments.

related.title: かしら
related.meaning: I wonder
related.comparison: Both かな and かしら can express doubt, similar to the English 'I wonder if (A)'. Comparing the two, かな is the more common and casual expression, used by both genders in a wide variety of contexts and settings. In contrast, かしら has a softer tone that can come off as more polite and feminine.

related.title: はたして
related.meaning: Wonder if ~ (really)?, Wonder how ~ (actually)?, Sure enough, As expected
related.comparison: Both かな and はたして express doubt, but their usage differs. かな is a casual sentence-ending particle meaning 'I wonder,' e.g. 寒いかな？ ('I wonder if it's cold?'). はたして appears at the beginning of a sentence for emphasis, often with かな or ～のだろうか, e.g. はたして来るのだろうか？ ('Will they really come, I wonder?').

synonyms.title: なかなか
synonyms.comparison: When used in affirmative sentences, なかなか and かなり both convey the nuance that something exceeds expectations, e.g. なかなか美味しい ('It's quite tasty.') or かなり美味しい (essentially the same meaning). The main difference is that なかなか is often used in negative expressions to suggest that something is not easily done, e.g. なかなかうまくいかない ('It just won't go well.'), while かなり is not used this way.

antonyms.title: かねる
antonyms.meaning: Cannot, Be unable to, Hard to, Hesitant to
antonyms.comparison: かねる and かねない are the same pattern in negative and positive forms. かねる implies being unable to do something, so its affirmative form carries a negative meaning, as in 分かりかねる (I cannot understand). かねない expresses a potential undesirable outcome, as in 誤解を招きかねない (It might invite misunderstanding). Both are formal/polite and often used in business contexts to soften assertions.

related.title: 恐れがある
related.meaning: There is a risk or fear that, Be in danger of, Possibility of
related.comparison: かねない is a polite expression often used in business settings to indicate a possible undesirable outcome, as in 誤解を招きかねない (It might invite misunderstanding). 恐れがある also expresses possibility, but with a more objective focus on risk, as in 誤解を招く恐れがある (There’s a danger it will invite misunderstanding). The former feels more deferential, the latter more factual.

related.title: なくはない
related.meaning: It's not that…, Kind of, might, Slightly, Somewhat, In some respects
related.comparison: かねない conveys possibility with a negative or worrisome nuance. なくはない means it’s not that (A) is impossible, softening the statement, as in できなくはない (It’s not that I can’t do it [though maybe not perfectly]). かねない would not be used in this context because its implication is negative, not concessive.

synonyms.title: おそらく
synonyms.comparison: おそらく and かもしれない both express possibility, but differ in certainty and placement. かもしれない comes at the end of sentences or phrases and means 'might' or 'may', e.g. 行くかもしれない ('I might go.'). おそらく appears at the beginning and expresses a stronger likelihood, e.g. おそらく行くだろう ('Most likely, I'll go.').

related.title: じゃないか
related.meaning: Isn't it?, Is it not?
related.comparison: かもしれない and じゃないか can both appear at the end of a sentence, but differ in nuance. かもしれない expresses uncertainty, e.g. おいしいかもしれない ('It might be tasty.'). じゃないか is more emphatic and seeks agreement, e.g. おいしいじゃないか ('It's tasty, isn't it?' or 'I'm telling you it's tasty, don't you agree?').

related.title: だろう
related.meaning: Right?, Probably, Isn't it?
related.comparison: かもしれない (polite form かもしれません) and だろう (polite form でしょう) are both casual phrases used to express uncertainty or possibility, but with different degrees of certainty. かもしれない is less certain, similar to 'maybe' or 'might,' while だろう expresses a higher degree of certainty, like 'probably' or 'I guess.'

related.title: でしょう
related.meaning: Right?, Probably, Isn't it?
related.comparison: かもしれない (polite form かもしれません) and でしょう both express uncertainty or possibility, softening a statement. The main difference is the degree of certainty: かもしれない expresses less certainty, similar to 'maybe' or 'might,' while でしょう (casual だろう) expresses a higher degree of certainty, similar to 'probably' or 'I guess.'

related.title: もしかしたら
related.meaning: Perhaps, Maybe, Possibly
related.comparison: かもしれない and もしかしたら both express possibility, but differ in placement and nuance. かもしれない appears at the end of sentences or phrases and means 'might' or 'may', e.g. 行くかもしれない ('I might go.'). もしかしたら comes at the beginning to emphasize the uncertainty, e.g. もしかしたら行くかもしれない ('Perhaps I might go.').

related.title: ようだ
related.meaning: It seems that, It appears that, It looks like
related.comparison: Both ようだ and かのようだ express appearance or resemblance, but with different nuance. ようだ means 'It seems' or 'It appears,' and can be used whether the speaker believes the thing is actually as it appears or not. かのようだ adds a stronger, often figurative tone like 'just as if (A)', and is typically used when the speaker knows it is it not actually that thing.

related.title: ふうに
related.meaning: ~ way, In a way that suggests, Like ~
related.comparison: Both express resemblance, but with different nuance. かのようだ is used at sentence-end to compare to a hypothetical or exaggerated situation, as in 夢かのようだ (It’s like a dream). ふうに is more neutral and connective, describing manner or appearance, as in こんなふうに書く (Write it like this).

related.title: という風に
related.meaning: As if to say, As if to suggest that, In such a way that, Things like
related.comparison: Both describe how something appears, but differ in tone. かのようだ often conveys imagination or unreality, as in 幻かのようだ (It’s like an illusion). という風に shows how something visibly seems, as in 分かっているというふうに笑った (He smiled as if he knew).

related.title: まるで…ようだ
related.meaning: It is entirely as if, It is just as though
related.comparison: These are minor variations of the same basic expression meaning 'as if it were (A)'. まるで adds an emphatic nuance, implying it's 'just like' the thing it's being compared to, e.g. まるで夢のようだ 'It's just like a dream.' If the question particle か is present, it adds a nuance of uncertainty, highlighting that though it seems like that thing, the speaker doubts it is (or knows it is not) that thing, e.g. 日本にいるかのようだ 'It's like I'm in Japan (though I'm not).'

related.title: ように・ような
related.meaning: As・like, Just like
related.comparison: かのようだ and ように・ような both use よう, meaning 'seems', but have different specific uses. かのようだ carries a strong hypothetical nuance and is often used to mean 'just like' or 'as if' when comparing to obviously different things. In contrast, ように・ような expresses comparison in a more direct or neutral way and can be used whether the similarity is hypothetical or real.

antonyms.title: かねない
antonyms.meaning: Might, Could be, Capable of, Possible
antonyms.comparison: かねる and かねない are the same pattern in negative and positive forms. かねる implies being unable to do something, so its affirmative form carries a negative meaning, as in 分かりかねる (I cannot understand). かねない expresses a potential undesirable outcome, as in 誤解を招きかねない (It might invite misunderstanding). Both are formal/polite and often used in business contexts to soften assertions.

related.title: 〜得ない
related.meaning: Unable to, Cannot
related.comparison: Both 〜得ない and かねる express inability, but differ in nuance. 〜得ない indicates objective impossibility, as in あり得ない話 (an unthinkable or impossible story). かねる reflects personal or emotional hesitation, often used in polite refusals in business contexts, as in お答えできかねます (I’m unable to answer).

related.title: を兼ねて
related.meaning: And also, Also (partly) to, Also (partly) for (the purpose of), At the same time, To double as, When … also, While … as well, And as
related.comparison: を兼ねて and かねる come from the same verb, but their uses differ. を兼ねて expresses that something serves two purposes simultaneously, as in 運動を兼ねて散歩してきた (I went for a walk, also to get some exercise). かねる attaches to verb stems to express inability politely or indirectly, as in 私には分かりかねます (I’m afraid I don’t understand).

related.title: わけにはいかない
related.meaning: Cannot afford to, No way, Impossible to do
related.comparison: Both express inability to do something, but differ in tone and usage. わけにはいかない follows the non-past form of a verb and implies a moral or social reason for being unable to act, e.g. 約束を破るわけにはいかない 'I can't break my promise.' かねる attaches to the verb stem and is more formal, often used in business settings for polite refusals or hesitations, e.g. 私には分かりかねます 'I'm afraid I'm not sure.'

related.title: 切れない
related.meaning: Being unable to finish or complete
related.comparison: Both 切れない and かねる express inability to do something, but differ in tone and nuance. 切れない describes physical or practical inability to complete an action, e.g. '食べ切れない' ('I can't finish eating it'). かねる conveys emotional or psychological reluctance and often appears in formal or polite refusals, e.g. '分かりかねます' ('I'm afraid I don't know').

related.title: 次第だ・次第で
related.meaning: Depending on, So
related.comparison: The grammar point 次第だ／次第で is used to indicate that a situation or course of action depends on something specified, often involving uncontrollable events or conditions. On the other hand, 〜かは〜によって違う expresses that the result or state changes depending on certain conditions or variables, focusing on how outcomes differ based on those factors.

related.title: によって・による
related.meaning: By means of, According to, Depending on, Because of, Due to
related.comparison: Both express variation depending on conditions, and 〜かは〜によって違う includes によって as part of its structure. The key difference lies in grammatical structure: 好きか嫌いかは人によって違う ('Whether someone likes it depends on the person.') vs. 好き嫌いは人による ('Likes and dislikes depend on the person.')

synonyms.title: ものだから
synonyms.comparison: から expresses a reason or cause, similar to 'because' or 'so' in English, with the structure (A) から (B). In contrast, ものだから is more emphatic or emotional, often used to explain or justify actions. Both can be used in subjective statements, where the speaker presents their perspective or judgment.

synonyms.title: ものですから・もので
synonyms.meaning: Because, My excuse is, My explanation is
synonyms.comparison: Both から and ものですから・もので express reasons or explanations. から is more straightforward and commonly used, similar to '(A), so (B)' in English. ものですから・もので places more emphasis on the reason, often used to explain one's own actions. Note that ものですから is more polite, while から is used in more casual speech.

synonyms.title: ので
synonyms.meaning: Because, So, Since, The reason being
synonyms.comparison: から and ので both express a cause or reason, similar to the English 'A, so B.' The reason comes first, followed by から or ので, and then the result. から is more colloquial, used in everyday speech, and often for personal or emotional statements. ので is slightly more formal and tends to be used for more objective statements.

synonyms.title: もの・もん
synonyms.comparison: から expresses a cause or reason, similar to 'A, so B' in English. もの or もん (with もん being more casual) can also express an emphatic or emotional reason, similar to 'because' in English. The main difference is that から can be used mid-sentence, while もの or もん typically appears at the end of a sentence and conveys a more personal or emotional tone.

related.title: したがって
related.meaning: Therefore, Thus, As a result
related.comparison: Both したがって and から express a cause or reason. The difference is that から is common in casual, everyday speech, while したがって is more formal, used in writing or structured speech. Additionally, から appears in the middle of a sentence, while したがって comes at the start. To use から at the start, you would use ですから or だから.

related.title: からこそ
related.meaning: Emphasized because, Precisely because
related.comparison: から expresses a reason or cause, similar to 'because of A, B' or 'A, so B' in English. The reason comes before から, with the result after. からこそ is the same から followed by the emphatic こそ, highlighting that the given reason is precisely or solely responsible for what follows. It's used for strong arguments or emphatic points.

related.title: なぜなら〜から
related.meaning: Because, The reason is, That is because
related.comparison: Both から and なぜなら〜から express reasons or causes. から is straightforward and casual, used in both casual conversations and formal speech, similar to 'A, so B.' In contrast, なぜなら〜から emphasizes the reason more strongly, often used in writing or formal speech with the nuance of 'As for why, it's because...' and typically when explaining a point in detail.

related.title: し～し
related.meaning: And, Giving reasons
related.comparison: Both し～し and から express reasons, similar to 'so' or 'because' in English. The reason (A) for outcome (B) is always followed by し or から, e.g. (A) し/から (B). The key difference is that から focuses on a single reason, while し～し lists multiple reasons. Even with one reason, し implies other unstated or implied reasons.

related.title: からする
related.meaning: About, Or more, Over, Starts at
related.comparison: These are essentially the same grammar point, both used with amounts to express either the idea of 'at least' or 'X or more', or simply to emphasize that the amount is a lot. からする is used e.g. for prices 100万円からする時計 ('a watch that costs a whole 1,000,000 yen'), and からある with e.g. weights (20キロからあるダンベル 'a dumbbell weighing as much as 20 kg') or distances 10キロから散歩道 ('a walking trail of as much as 10 km'). The related grammar point からの can be used more generally, including with quantities of people.

related.title: 以上 ①
related.meaning: More than (or equal to), Over・(and) above, Any more・Greater, Exceeding
related.comparison: からある and 以上① both emphasize large numbers or amounts, but differ in nuance and usage. からある means 'as many as', usually with inanimate objects (due to the use of ある), and attaches directly to nouns, e.g. ５００枚からあるチケット ('as many as 500 tickets'). 以上① means 'X or more', can be used with people, and attaches to nouns with の, e.g. 500人以上の学生 = '500 or more students'.

related.title: に応じて
related.meaning: Depending or based on, According to, In response to, Suitable for or to
related.comparison: に応じて shows flexible adjustment in proportion to conditions, as in 状況に応じて対応する (Respond according to the situation). からして emphasizes the basis for judgment, meaning judging from or even just from, often with emphasis, as in 態度からして気に入っていない (Judging from his attitude, he doesn’t like it).

related.title: にしたら
related.meaning: From the point of view of
related.comparison: からして judges from a single strong piece of evidence, often critically, as in 言葉遣いからして失礼だ (Even just from his wording, it’s rude). にしたら centers on a person’s standpoint, neutral or sympathetic, as in 子供にしたら楽しい経験だ (From a child’s perspective, it’s a fun experience).

related.title: ところを見ると
related.meaning: Judging from, Seeing that
related.comparison: Both indicate judgment, but differ in form. からして follows nouns, as in 顔つきからして疲れている (Judging from his face, he’s tired). ところを見ると follows verbs, focusing on directly observed actions, as in 帰るところを見ると用事が終わった (Seeing him leave, his business must be finished).

related.title: からすると・からすれば
related.slug: からすると-からすれば
related.meaning: Judging from, Considering
related.comparison: Both follow nouns and can mean judging from (A), as in 外見からして／外見からすると綺麗だ (Judging from her appearance, she’s beautiful). However, からすると・からすれば can also express perspective, as in 学生からすると難しい (From a student’s perspective, it’s difficult). からして is stronger and often evaluative, focusing on a single piece of evidence.

related.title: にもとづいて
related.meaning: Based on, In accordance with
related.comparison: While both patterns can be translated into English as 'based on', they have very different meanings. からして means 'based on (A), (B)', and expresses the logical basis for the speaker's judgment or opinion. For example, 匂いからして美味しそう = 'From the smell, it seems tasty.' In contrast, にもとづいて expresses the foundation or basis for another action. For example: 経験に基づいて判断する = 'Make a decision based on experience.'

related.title: にしては
related.meaning: (Even) considering, (Even) for
related.comparison: にしては and からして both use a reference point, but にしては highlights a result that defies expectations, e.g. 子供にしては詳しい ('He knows a lot for a child'), while からして draws a conclusion from one part that suggests the whole, e.g. みんなの感想からして面白そう ('From everyone's impressions, it seems interesting').

related.title: ことから
related.meaning: From the fact that
related.comparison: ことから and からして both express reasoning, but in different ways. ことから means 'from the fact that (A)' and is used to explain a cause or basis, e.g. 声がふるえていることから緊張していると分かる ('From the fact their voice is trembling, I know they're nervous'). からして highlights a telling detail as evidence, e.g. 見た目からして運動していそう ('Judging from her appearance, it looks like she exercises').

related.title: から見ると
related.meaning: From the point of view of, By the look of
related.comparison: Both can mean from the perspective of, but differ in nuance. から見ると emphasizes observation or evaluation, as in 医者から見ると簡単な病気だ (From a doctor’s point of view, it’s a simple illness). からして presents (A) as the basis of a judgment, often emphatic, as in 態度からして失礼だ (Judging from his attitude, he’s rude).

synonyms.title: ～ばこそ
synonyms.meaning: Precisely because, Only because, Because, Due to, Only if
synonyms.comparison: Despite appearing different, ～ばこそ and からこそ are essentially synonymous in meaning, both expressing an reason for something, i.e. 'it is precisely because of (A) that (B)'. The main difference is that ～ばこそ is a highly formal and literary expression found primarily in writing, while からこそ is far more common and used in everyday speech as well as writing.

related.title: あまり
related.meaning: So much...that
related.comparison: あまり follows a noun or verb phrase and shows that an extreme state leads to an unintended result, e.g. 緊張のあまり声が出なかった ('I was so nervous I couldn't speak.') からこそ attaches after the reason and emphasizes it as the precise cause, e.g. 緊張したからこそ全力を出せた ('It was precisely because I was nervous that I gave it my all.')

related.title: ことだから
related.meaning: It is exactly because
related.comparison: からこそ and ことだから are similar in that both can emphatically state a reason. However, ことだから is typically used to make a prediction or assumption based on a person's known behavior or character, e.g. 田中さんのことだからできると思う ('Because it's Tanaka, I think he can do it'). In contrast, からこそ is more broadly used to emphasize a specific reason, e.g. 努力したからこそできた ('I could do it precisely because I worked hard').

related.title: ～てこそ
related.meaning: Only if, Because, By, Unless, Until
related.comparison: Both からこそ and ～てこそ emphasize cause and effect, but they differ in nuance. からこそ highlights a specific reason with a strong emphasis, e.g. 努力したからこそ合格できた ('I was able to pass precisely because I worked hard'). In contrast, ～てこそ indicates that only after a certain condition is fulfilled can something else happen, e.g. 努力してこそ意味がある ('It only has meaning after putting in effort').

related.title: ものだから
related.meaning: Because, The reason is
related.comparison: からこそ and ものだから are similar in that both can be used in expressing a reason.
