They differ in that からこそ is used to specifically emphasize the reason, like saying 'it is precisely because of (A) that (B)', while ものだから is not as emphatic, and is generally used when providing an explanation or excuse for one's actions or a situation.

related.title: こそ
related.meaning: For sure, For certain, Even more so, Underlined or bolded text
related.comparison: Both use こそ for emphasis, but からこそ highlights a reason or cause, meaning 'precisely because', e.g. 努力したからこそできた ('I could do it precisely because I worked hard.'). こそ on its own emphasizes the word it follows, framing it as precisely the one, e.g. 彼こそできる ('He, if no one else, can do it.')

related.title: から
related.meaning: Because, Since
related.comparison: から expresses a reason or cause, similar to 'because of A, B' or 'A, so B' in English. The reason comes before から, with the result after. からこそ is the same から followed by the emphatic こそ, highlighting that the given reason is precisely or solely responsible for what follows. It's used for strong arguments or emphatic points.

related.title: からある
related.meaning: As much as, As many as, At least
related.comparison: These are essentially the same grammar point, both used with amounts to express either the idea of 'at least' or 'X or more', or simply to emphasize that the amount is a lot. からする is used e.g. for prices 100万円からする時計 ('a watch that costs a whole 1,000,000 yen'), and からある with e.g. weights (20キロからあるダンベル 'a dumbbell weighing as much as 20 kg') or distances 10キロから散歩道 ('a walking trail of as much as 10 km'). The related grammar point からの can be used more generally, including with quantities of people.

related.title: Number + も
related.meaning: Emphasis, As ~ as, Not even
related.comparison: からする can be thought of as a more formal equivalent of もする when following number (or number+counter) expressions, e.g. 100万円からする時計 ('a watch that costs as much as 1,000,000 yen') vs. 100万円もする時計 (same meaning, but more colloquial).

related.title: ばかりに
related.meaning: Simply because, Only because, Just because, Simply on account of
related.comparison: They may seem similar because both can be translated as 'just because', but mean very different things. ばかりに expresses, often with regret, a negative outcome caused by a single reason, e.g. 一回遅刻したばかりにクビになった ('I was fired just because I was late once.'). In contrast, からといって means 'just because (A), it doesn't mean (B)', e.g. お金持ちだからといって幸せとは限らない ('Just because someone is rich doesn't mean they're happy.').

antonyms.title: ことにはならない
antonyms.comparison: からには shows that once a condition is met, a natural consequence or obligation follows, as in 約束したからには守るべきだ (Since I made a promise, I should keep it). ことにはならない shows that fulfilling a condition does not guarantee the expected result, as in 読んだからといって理解したことにはならない (Just because you read it doesn’t mean you understood it).

related.title: 以上 ②
related.meaning: Since, Now that, As long as, If
related.comparison: 以上② and からには both mean since or now that, but differ slightly in nuance. からには adds a stronger sense of personal resolve or obligation, as in 約束したからには守らなければならない (Since I made a promise, I must keep it). 以上② is more neutral and objective, as in 参加する以上、遅刻するわけにはいかない (If we’re going to participate, we can’t be late).

related.title: 上は
related.meaning: Once, Since, Now that, As long as
related.comparison: 上は and からには both present a situation or condition, then express something deemed necessary or natural based on that situation. For example: 日本に来たからには、たくさんの日本人と話したい (Since I came to Japan, I want to speak to many Japanese people). The primary difference is formality: 上は is used almost exclusively in formal writing, while からには is common in both writing and everyday conversation.

related.title: さえ〜ば
related.meaning: If only…then, As long as..., Provided..., Only thing ... needs for ... is ...
related.comparison: These expressions both connect a condition to a result, but in different ways. さえ〜ば emphasizes that the condition alone is enough, e.g. お金さえあれば大丈夫 ('As long as I have money, I'll be fine'). からには highlights an obligation or natural consequence once a condition is true, e.g. 仕事を引き受けたからには最後までやる ('Since I took on the task, I'll see it through').

related.title: 限り
related.meaning: As long as, While… is the case, Limited to
related.comparison: Both express conditional relationships but with different focus. からには conveys cause-and-effect or obligation, as in 約束したからには守るべきだ (Since I made a promise, I should keep it). 限り emphasizes temporal or situational boundaries, meaning as long as or while, as in 生きている限り希望はある (As long as you live, there is hope).

related.title: にしたら
related.meaning: From the point of view of
related.comparison: から見ると is used for objective judgment from a certain perspective, often based on observable facts, as in 専門家から見ると簡単だ (From an expert’s point of view, it’s easy). にしたら emphasizes a personal, subjective perspective, showing how it feels from someone’s position, as in 子供にしたら大問題だ (From a child’s standpoint, it’s a big problem).

related.title: から言うと
related.meaning: Speaking in terms of, From the point of view of
related.comparison: から言うと and から見ると both express viewpoint, but differ in nuance. から言うと emphasizes interpretation or judgment from language or opinion, e.g. 経験から言うとそれは無理だ ('Speaking from experience, that's impossible'). から見ると highlights observation or appearance, e.g. 外国人から見ると奇妙に見える ('From a foreigner's perspective, it appears strange').

related.title: にしては
related.meaning: (Even) considering, (Even) for
related.comparison: にしては and から見ると both reflect viewpoint, but にしては highlights a result that departs from expectations for a category or role, e.g. 新人にしては落ち着いている ('She's calm for a newcomer'), while から見ると gives a neutral assessment from a given perspective, e.g. プロから見ると ('From a professional's viewpoint').

related.title: からして
related.meaning: Even..., Judging from, Based on
related.comparison: Both can mean from the perspective of, but differ in nuance. から見ると emphasizes observation or evaluation, as in 医者から見ると簡単な病気だ (From a doctor’s point of view, it’s a simple illness). からして presents (A) as the basis of a judgment, often emphatic, as in 態度からして失礼だ (Judging from his attitude, he’s rude).

related.title: からすると・からすれば
related.slug: からすると-からすれば
related.meaning: Judging from, Considering
related.comparison: Both express judgments based on something, but differ slightly. から見ると emphasizes observation, as in 専門家から見ると容易だ (From an expert’s perspective, it’s easy). からすると・からすれば can also express viewpoint or position, as in 外国人からすると珍しい (From a foreigner’s perspective, it’s unusual).

related.title: にしたら
related.meaning: From the point of view of
related.comparison: からすると・からすれば are objective, drawing conclusions from evidence, as in 態度からすると本気だ (Judging from his attitude, he’s serious). にしたら highlights a subjective, inner viewpoint, as in 親にしたら心配だろう (From a parent’s perspective, they must be worried).

related.title: からして
related.meaning: Even..., Judging from, Based on
related.comparison: Both follow nouns and can mean judging from (A), as in 外見からして／外見からすると綺麗だ (Judging from her appearance, she’s beautiful). However, からすると・からすれば can also express perspective, as in 学生からすると難しい (From a student’s perspective, it’s difficult). からして is stronger and often evaluative, focusing on a single piece of evidence.

related.title: に言わせれば・に言わせると・に言わせたら
related.meaning: If you ask, In … opinion, According to
related.comparison: Both express a viewpoint, but the source and nuance differ. に言わせれば・に言わせると・に言わせたら explicitly attributes an opinion to a specific person and often carries a subjective or critical tone, emphasizing that this is that person’s way of seeing things, as in 専門家に言わせればその計画は甘い ('If you ask an expert, that plan is naive.'). からすると・からすれば presents a perspective based on position, role, or circumstances and sounds more neutral or analytical, focusing on standpoint rather than personal opinion, as in 学生の立場からすると高すぎる ('From a student’s point of view, it’s too expensive.').

related.title: にしては
related.meaning: (Even) considering, (Even) for
related.comparison: にしては and からすると・からすれば both express judgment, but にしては highlights a state or result that departs from expectations, e.g. 初心者にしては上手だ ('He's good for a beginner'), while からすると・からすれば shows reasoning from a standpoint, e.g. 私の経験からすると ('Based on my experience').

related.title: から言うと
related.meaning: Speaking in terms of, From the point of view of
related.comparison: から言うと and からすると・からすれば are similar in that both introduce perspective. から言うと explicitly means 'speaking from (A)', offering a basis for opinion, e.g. 経験から言うと簡単ではない ('Speaking from experience, it's not easy'). からすると・からすれば has a broader 'based on (A)' meaning, often implying inference, e.g. 態度からすると反省していない ('Judging from his attitude, he isn't sorry').

related.title: ことから
related.meaning: From the fact that
related.comparison: ことから and からすると・からすれば both describe drawing conclusions, but differ in tone and focus. ことから shows cause and effect based on a fact, e.g. 足音がしたことから誰かがいると分かる ('From the fact we heard footsteps, we know someone is there'). からすると・からすれば show judgment from a certain viewpoint, e.g. 足音からすると一人の女性だったみたい ('Judging from the footsteps, it seems like it was one woman').

related.title: 上
related.meaning: From the standpoint of
related.comparison: 上 and からすると・からすれば both indicate a perspective or basis for judgment, but differ in tone and scope. 上 is formal and often tied to institutional or thematic standpoints, as in 理論上の問題 (a problem from a theoretical standpoint). からすると・からすれば is more subjective and flexible, used with personal or situational cues, as in 表情からすると彼は怒っている (Judging from his expression, he’s angry).

related.title: から見ると
related.meaning: From the point of view of, By the look of
related.comparison: Both express judgments based on something, but differ slightly. から見ると emphasizes observation, as in 専門家から見ると容易だ (From an expert’s perspective, it’s easy). からすると・からすれば can also express viewpoint or position, as in 外国人からすると珍しい (From a foreigner’s perspective, it’s unusual).

related.title: ところを見ると
related.meaning: Judging from, Seeing that
related.comparison: Both describe conclusions based on evidence, but differ in usage. ところを見ると attaches to verbs, drawing inferences from observed action, as in 人が並んでいるところを見ると人気だ (Seeing people lining up, it must be popular). からすると・からすれば follow nouns, as in 成績からすると合格できる (Judging from his grades, he can pass), and can also express personal perspective.

related.title: から見ると
related.meaning: From the point of view of, By the look of
related.comparison: から言うと and から見ると both express viewpoint, but differ in nuance. から言うと emphasizes interpretation or judgment from language or opinion, e.g. 経験から言うとそれは無理だ ('Speaking from experience, that's impossible'). から見ると highlights observation or appearance, e.g. 外国人から見ると奇妙に見える ('From a foreigner's perspective, it appears strange').

related.title: にしたら
related.meaning: From the point of view of
related.comparison: から言うと and にしたら both express perspective, but differ in nuance. から言うと gives an opinion or evaluation from a stated viewpoint, e.g. 医者から言うとこれは異常だ ('From a doctor's perspective, this is abnormal'). にしたら puts the speaker in someone else's position to make a subjective judgment, e.g. 子供にしたら怖かっただろう ('It must have been scary from the child's perspective').

related.title: からすると・からすれば
related.slug: からすると-からすれば
related.meaning: Judging from, Considering
related.comparison: から言うと and からすると・からすれば are similar in that both introduce perspective. から言うと explicitly means 'speaking from (A)', offering a basis for opinion, e.g. 経験から言うと簡単ではない ('Speaking from experience, it's not easy'). からすると・からすれば has a broader 'based on (A)' meaning, often implying inference, e.g. 態度からすると反省していない ('Judging from his attitude, he isn't sorry').

related.title: ことから
related.meaning: From the fact that
related.comparison: ことから and から言うと both express basis or reasoning, but differ in tone and usage. ことから is generally used for factual inference or deduction, e.g. 電気が付いていないことから、誰もいないと分かった ('Judging from the fact the lights are off, I could tell no one is there'). から言うと provides a basis for a stated opinion, e.g. 経験から言うと注意が必要だ ('Speaking from experience, caution is required').

related.title: で言うと
related.meaning: In, Of, According to
related.comparison: で言うと and から言うと both present a frame of reference, but differ in nuance. で言うと highlights category or classification, often for comparison, e.g. 車で言うとポルシェみたいなものだ ('If we speak in terms of cars, it's like a Porsche'). から言うと gives a basis for a stated opinion, e.g. 私の経験から言うと、もうどうしようもない ('Speaking from my experience, there's nothing to be done').

related.title: 上
related.meaning: From the standpoint of
related.comparison: から言うと and 上 both indicate viewpoint, but から言うと introduces a subjective opinion from a specific angle, e.g. 学生の立場から言うと反対だ ('Speaking from a student's viewpoint, I'm against it'). 上 expresses evaluation based on a defined category, e.g. 法律上問題はない ('There's no problem in terms of the law').

related.title: でも
related.meaning: Or something,  Any... (with question words), No matter (who)
related.comparison: Both か何か and でも can suggest alternatives, like 'or something' in English. か何か places more emphasis on the idea of other specific options, e.g. お茶か何か飲みませんか？ ('How about some tea or something else?'), while でも is vaguer and implies the suggestion is just one of many possibilities, e.g. お茶でも飲みませんか？ ('How about some tea or something?').

related.title: かどうか
related.meaning: Whether or not, If...or not
related.comparison: か何か and かどうか look similar but are used differently. か何か follows a noun and means 'or something,' as in お茶か何か飲みますか？ ('Would you like some tea or something?'). かどうか means 'whether or not' and expresses uncertainty, like 面白いかどうか分からない ('I don't know whether or not it's interesting.').

synonyms.title: かどうか
synonyms.comparison: Both か否か and かどうか express 'whether or not,' but differ in tone and usage. か否か is highly formal and mostly seen in legal documents, academic writing, or formal reports. かどうか is more common in daily conversation and general writing, making it the default choice in most situations.

synonyms.title: がいる
synonyms.meaning: To be, There is,  To remain (in a state)
synonyms.comparison: Both がある and がいる combine the particle が with a verb to express existence or possession. The difference is that ある refers to inanimate objects or intangible concepts (e.g., 車がある = 'I have a car' or 'There is a car,' アイデアがある = 'I have an idea'), while いる is used for living or animate things (e.g., 猫がいる = 'I have a cat' or 'There is a cat').

related.title: だ
related.meaning: To be, Is
related.comparison: がある indicates the existence or possession of something. For example, ペンがある means 'There is a pen (on the table, etc.)' or 'I have a pen.' On the other hand, だ expresses assertion or determination similar to 'is/are' in English. In contrast to the がある example, ペンだ would mean 'It (=the thing we are looking at) is a pen.'

related.title: がある + Noun
related.meaning: Noun (B) that has Noun (A), Noun (B) with Noun (A)
related.comparison: がある expresses the existence or possession of inanimate objects, similar to 'there is' or 'I have' in English (e.g., ピアノがある = 'There is a piano' or 'I have a piano'). When combined with a noun, it describes the noun, much like an adjective (e.g., ピアノがあるレストラン = 'A restaurant with a piano'). Remember, ある changes to いる when referring to animate objects.

related.title: である
related.meaning: Formal or literary だ, Authoritative, Copula
related.comparison: がある expresses the existence or possession of inanimate objects, similar to saying 'there is' or 'I have' in English. In contrast, である is comparable to (and is, in fact, an alternate form of) the casual だ or polite です, and is therefore equivalent to the English "it is". In other words, ピアノがある means 'There's a piano' or 'I have a piano', but ピアノである means 'It's a piano' in formal/written language (the spoken polite equivalent would be ピアノです).

related.title: です
related.meaning: To be, Is
related.comparison: The phrase ～がある is used to show existence or possession. For example, 本がある means 'There is a book' or 'I have a book.' On the other hand, です is used to identify something, like 本です ('It is a book'). がある is more casual and often used with close friends or in certain grammar patterns, while です is polite.

related.title: ございます
related.meaning: To be, To exist (Polite)
related.comparison: ある and ございます are both verbs of existence. ある is the plain form used in everyday conversation, while ございます is the polite form used in formal settings or speeches. For example, 本がある means 'I have / there is a book' in casual speech, while 本がございます is the same phrase in a more polite and formal context (note that が must be used in both cases).

synonyms.title: ものの
synonyms.comparison: ものの expresses contrast, similar to 'although' in English, showing that while one thing is true, another contradicts it. が also expresses contrast, but its nuance is weaker, and in some cases, it merely introduces a statement without implying contrast. が is more general, while ものの is more specific. Both are formal and more common in writing than in casual speech.

synonyms.title: けれども
synonyms.comparison: Both が and けれども express contrast, similar to 'but' in English, or sometimes simply indicate a transition to added information (like 'and'). Both are somewhat formal, used in writing or formal speech, compared to the casual けど. Note that while が and けれども follow immediately after verbs or い-Adjectives, だ or です is required after a な-Adjective or noun.

synonyms.title: だが・ですが
synonyms.comparison: The particle が in both patterns expresses contrast, similar to 'but' in English, or sometimes indicates a transition to additional information (like 'and'). The difference is that が follows directly after an い-Adjective or verb, while it requires だ or です after a な-Adjective or noun. が is somewhat formal and often used in writing or formal speech.

synonyms.title: けど・だけど
synonyms.comparison: Both が (or だが/ですが after a noun or な-adjective) and けど・だけど express contrast or transition, similar to 'but' in English (e.g., '見た目は美味しそうだけど、味がない' = 'It looks tasty, but has no flavor'). The difference is that が is more formal or written-style, while けど is more colloquial. Both can also be used at the end of a sentence to suggest an implied contrast, even if not explicitly stated.

related.title: んだけど・んですが
related.meaning: But..., And...
related.comparison: Both express contrast, but が is more formal and used in writing, like 面白いが難しい ('It's interesting, but difficult'). When combined with ～んだ・～んです, as in 行きたいんですが, it softens the sentence and invites a response or action from the listener.

related.title: ところが
related.meaning: Even so, However, Even though, But
related.comparison: Both ところが and が express contrast or contradiction. ところが is used at the beginning of a sentence to emphasize a contrast between expectation and reality. In contrast, が is used after a phrase or at the end of a sentence to express simple contrast, or sometimes to add information or context without implying surprise or expectation.

related.title: くせに
related.meaning: Even though, And yet, Despite
related.comparison: くせに expresses disapproval, similar to 'in spite of' or 'although' in English. In contrast, が is a neutral connecting particle, often expressing contrast (like 'but') or adding information (like 'and'). Unlike くせに, which carries a negative nuance, が is neutral. After な-Adjectives and nouns, くせに requires な or の, while が requires だ or です.

related.title: の
related.meaning: Indicates possession
related.comparison: がある + Noun expresses possession, using a phrase with a noun, the particle が, and the verb ある to describe another noun (e.g., ピアノがあるレストラン = 'a restaurant with a piano'). In these cases, が can be replaced with の without changing the meaning (e.g., ピアノのあるレストラン).

related.title: がある
related.meaning: To be, There is
related.comparison: がある expresses the existence or possession of inanimate objects, similar to 'there is' or 'I have' in English (e.g., ピアノがある = 'There is a piano' or 'I have a piano'). When combined with a noun, it describes the noun, much like an adjective (e.g., ピアノがあるレストラン = 'A restaurant with a piano'). Remember, ある changes to いる when referring to animate objects.

synonyms.title: がある
synonyms.comparison: Both がある and がいる combine the particle が with a verb to express existence or possession. The difference is that ある refers to inanimate objects or intangible concepts (e.g., 車がある = 'I have a car' or 'There is a car,' アイデアがある = 'I have an idea'), while いる is used for living or animate things (e.g., 猫がいる = 'I have a cat' or 'There is a cat').

related.title: いらっしゃる
related.meaning: To be (Honorific), To come (Honorific), To go (Honorific)
related.comparison: がいる combines the particle が with the verb いる to express the existence of living or animate beings, like people or animals. いらっしゃる is the honorific equivalent of いる, as well as 行く and 来る. In other words, がいらっしゃる is the honorific version of がいる, and いらっしゃいますか is the honorific equivalent of いますか, 行きますか, or 来ますか.

related.title: 途中に・途中で
related.meaning: On the way, Partway, In the middle of
related.comparison: がけに and 途中に・途中で are close in meaning and often interchangeable. The main difference is grammatical connection. がけに attaches to the stem of verbs, as in 帰りがけに (on the way home). 途中に・途中で attaches to the dictionary form of verbs, as in 帰る途中で (on the way home), or to nouns with の, as in 会議の途中に (in the middle of the meeting).

related.title: ついでに
related.meaning: While you are at it, On the occasion
related.comparison: ついでに and がけに both relate to doing something while engaged in another action. ついでに expresses a second action done while convenient, e.g. '買い物のついでに郵便局に寄った' ('I stopped at the post office while shopping'). がけに is used with movement verbs and emphasizes doing something during a transition, e.g. '帰りがけに電話した' ('I called on my way home').

related.title: きらいがある
related.meaning: Have a tendency to, Tend to, Be slightly~
related.comparison: Both express tendencies or patterns of behavior, but they differ in tone and usage. がち attaches to verb stems, and is neutral, used for tendencies that happen often, e.g. つい忘れがちだ ('I tend to forget.'). きらいがある follows the plain form of verbs and is typically used only for undesirable tendencies. Unlike がち, it can also express a tendency to not do something, e.g. 人の話を聞かないきらいがある ('He tends to not listen to others.').

related.title: っぽい
related.meaning: -ish, -like, Characteristic of, Typical of, Tendency to
related.comparison: Both がち and っぽい describe tendencies or characteristic qualities, but differ in nuance. がち is neutral and used for tendencies that happen often, e.g. つい忘れがちだ ('I tend to forget.'). っぽい expresses an inherent quality or the appearance of something, similar to the English '~ish', e.g. 子供っぽい性格 ('a childish personality').

related.title: にみえる
related.meaning: To look, To seem, To appear
related.comparison: がする and にみえる (often written in kanji as に見える) both describe sensations, but differ in usage. がする is used with sensory words, e.g. 音がする 'I hear', 匂いがする 'I smell', and 味がする 'I taste' (literally, 'It gives off a sound/smell/taste.'). に見える specifically describes visual sensations and means 'looks like', e.g. 石に見える ('It looks like a rock.'), without implying that it is the thing it visually resembles.

related.title: 聞こえる
related.meaning: To be audible, To sound (like), (Can) hear, To be heard
related.comparison: がする and 聞こえる both express sensory experiences, but differ in nuance.  がする is used with sensory words, e.g. 音がする 'I hear', 匂いがする 'I smell', and 味がする 'I taste' (literally, 'It gives off a sound/smell/taste.'). 聞こえる focuses specifically on sound and means 'to be heard' or 'to sound like', e.g. 音楽が聞こえる ('I hear music.') or 音楽に聞こえる ('It sounds like music.').

related.title: にくい
related.meaning: Difficult to, Hard to
related.comparison: がたい and にくい both attach to verb stems and express the idea of 'difficult to do', but with different nuance and formality levels. にくい is used in everyday speech and reflects the speaker's personal sense of difficulty or discomfort. がたい is more formal/written-style and implies something is nearly impossible or extremely difficult due to factors beyond the speaker's control.

related.title: ようがない・ようもない
related.slug: ようがない・ようもない
related.meaning: There's no way to, It's impossible to
related.comparison: Both ようがない・ようもない and がたい express difficulty or unfeasibility of an action, but differ in nuance. ようがない・ようもない conveys that there is no way or means to do something. For example, 連絡のしようがない ('There's no way to contact him'). がたい, on the other hand, indicates that something is very difficult to do, often due to factors beyond the speaker's control. For example, 信じがたい話だ ('It's a story that is hard to believe').

synonyms.title: ついでに
synonyms.meaning: While you are at it, On the occasion
synonyms.comparison: Both express simultaneous actions, but differ in nuance. ついでに implies convenience, e.g. '買い物のついでに郵便局に寄った' ('I stopped at the post office while shopping'). がてら implies doing something while moving, e.g. '散歩がてら郵便を出した' ('I mailed a letter while taking a walk'). They also differ grammatically: がてら connects directly to nouns, while ついでに requires の.

related.title: ながら
related.meaning: While ~ing, During, As
related.comparison: がてら and ながら both convey performing two actions together, but differ in nuance. がてら expresses a secondary or incidental activity done in addition to a main one, e.g. 犬の散歩がてら、コンビニに寄った ('I stopped at the convenience store while taking my dog for a walk.'). In contrast, ながら typically describes two actions of relative importance performed simultaneously, e.g. テレビを観ながらご飯を食べた ('I watched TV while eating dinner.').

related.title: 見える
related.meaning: To be visible, To seem, To be in sight
related.comparison: Both みられる and 見える mean 'can see' (potential form) but differ in nuance. 見える means something is visible, while 見られる refers to the ability to watch or view. For example, stars on a clear night would be 見える, but being able to watch a movie on a streaming service would be 見られる.

related.title: ひつようがある
related.meaning: It is necessary to
related.comparison: Both expressions use 必要 'necessary' to show that something is needed. が必要 attaches to nouns or nominalized verbs, as in 金が必要 ('Money is necessary.') or 覚えることが必要 ('It's necessary to memorize.'). 必要がある connects only to verbs and uses the form 覚える必要がある ('There is a need to memorize.'), which means essentially the same thing but fits more naturally with verb phrases.

related.title: たがる
related.meaning: To want to (Third person)
related.comparison: がる attaches to adjectives to describe the observed feelings or desires of another person, e.g. 寒がる 'seems cold', ほしがる 'seems to want (A)', and so on. When combined with the たい-form of verbs, it becomes たがる, expressing observed desire, e.g. 飲みたがる 'seems to want to drink'.

related.title: たい
related.meaning: Want to do
related.comparison: The たい form expresses personal desires, like バナナが食べたい ('I want to eat a banana'). がる, on the other hand, is used to show that someone else seems to want something, like 食べたがっている ('[He/she] seems like they want to eat'). The たい form is generally not used for others' desires unless the subject has explicitly made their desire clear.

related.title: がほしい
related.meaning: To want something, To be in need of
related.comparison: がほしい and がる both express desire but differ in usage. がほしい straightforwardly expresses the speaker's own wants, e.g. 車がほしい ('I want a car.'). In contrast, がる attaches to adjectives (e.g. ほしい becomes ほしがる) to describe what someone else appears to feel, e.g. 田中さんは車をほしがっている ('Mr. Tanaka seems to want a car.').

related.title: てほしい
related.meaning: I want you to
related.comparison: てほしい and がる both relate to expressing desire but are used differently. てほしい is used when you want someone to do something, e.g. これを買ってほしい ('I want you to buy this.'). がる describes what another person appears to feel, e.g. 田中さんは車を買いたがっている ('Mr. Tanaka seems to want to buy a car.').

related.title: ように～てほしい
related.meaning: Want ~ to ~ like ~, Want ~ to be able to ~, Want ~ to ~ in the way of ~
related.comparison: These two patterns using ほしい to express a 'want' differ in meaning and usage. がほしい follows nouns to express the speaker's desire for something, whether physical (金がほしい = 'I want money.') or figurative (自由がほしい = 'I want freedom.'). ようにしてほしい combines ようにする and てほしい to express a desire for someone to act in a certain way, e.g. 忘れないようにしてほしい ('I want you to make sure not to forget.').

related.title: たい
related.meaning: Want to do
related.comparison: Both the たい form and がほしい express desires, similar to the English 'want.' The difference is that たい is used with verbs to express one's desire to perform some action, while がほしい is used after nouns to express one's desire for an object. For example, 日本に行きたい means 'I want to go to Japan' (desire to do something), while お金がほしい means 'I want money' (desire for an object).

related.title: がる
related.meaning: To feel, To think, To act as if, To want, To desire
related.comparison: がほしい and がる both express desire but differ in usage. がほしい straightforwardly expresses the speaker's own wants, e.g. 車がほしい ('I want a car.'). In contrast, がる attaches to adjectives (e.g. ほしい becomes ほしがる) to describe what someone else appears to feel, e.g. 田中さんは車をほしがっている ('Mr. Tanaka seems to want a car.').

related.title: たがる
related.meaning: To want to (Third person)
related.comparison: がほしい and たがる both express desire but differ in usage. がほしい is used for the speaker's own wants, e.g. 車がほしい ('I want a car.'). たがる combines the たい-form of verbs with がる to describe a third person's apparent desire to do something, e.g. 田中さんは車を買いたがっている ('Tanaka seems to want to buy a car.').

related.title: てほしい
related.meaning: I want you to
related.comparison: Both がほしい and てほしい express a want, but differ in meaning and usage. がほしい follows nouns to express the speaker's desire for something, whether physical, e.g. 金がほしい ('I want money.') or figurative, e.g. 自由がほしい ('I want freedom.'). In contrast, てほしい uses the て form of a verb to express a desire for someone else to do something, e.g. これを見てほしい ('I want you to look at this.').

related.title: や否や
related.meaning: As soon as, No sooner than, The minute
related.comparison: Both express that one action is followed immediately by another, meaning ‘no sooner than,’ and are often interchangeable. や否や is more formal and literary, commonly used in written narratives to describe near-simultaneous events, as in ベルが鳴るや否や生徒が教室を飛び出した ('No sooner had the bell rung than the students rushed out of the classroom.'). が早いか is slightly more vivid and action-focused, often used to emphasize the suddenness of the second action, as in ドアを開けるが早いか子どもが走り出した ('No sooner had I opened the door than a child ran off.').

related.title: ～ところに・～ところへ
related.meaning: At the time, When, While, Timing
related.comparison: Both can express the meaning of 'as soon as (A), (B)', but with different usage and nuance. が早いか marks an action that is followed immediately by another, often sudden or dramatic, meaning ‘no sooner than,’ as in ドアを開けるが早いか、子どもが飛び出してきた ('No sooner had I opened the door than a child burst out.'). ～ところに・～ところへ describes another event occurring at a certain stage of an action, focusing on coincidence in time rather than instant reaction, as in 出かけようとしたところに電話がかかってきた ('Just as I was about to go out, I got a phone call.').

antonyms.title: 好き
antonyms.comparison: 好き and 嫌い are な-Adjectives used to express 'like' and 'dislike.' The object of like or dislike is typically marked with が, though を can appear in some cases. Adding 大 (e.g., 大好き, 大嫌い) intensifies the sentiment, transforming 'like' and 'dislike' into something more akin to 'love' and 'hate.'

related.title: に伴って・に伴い
related.meaning: As, Due,  Along with, Hand-in-hand with, Resulted in, Be consequent upon
related.comparison: Both can describe one event influencing another, but with very different nuance. きっかけ refers to something that triggered or led to a change, as in 入院をきっかけにタバコをやめた (I gave up smoking on the occasion of my hospitalization). に伴って・に伴い is more formal, often expressing a broader causal relationship, as in 高齢化に伴い人口が激減した (The population rapidly declined along with the aging of the population).

related.title: を契機に
related.meaning: Lead to, As a good opportunity to, As a result of, As a trigger
related.comparison: Both indicate events that lead to change, but differ in register. を契機に is formal and often marks major turning points, as in 結婚を契機に転職した (Marriage became the occasion for changing jobs). きっかけ is casual and broad, used for any kind of trigger, as in 友達の紹介がきっかけで知り合った (We met thanks to a friend’s introduction).

related.title: がち
related.meaning: Apt to do, Tend to do, Prone to do
related.comparison: Both express tendencies or patterns of behavior, but they differ in tone and usage. がち attaches to verb stems, and is neutral, used for tendencies that happen often, e.g. つい忘れがちだ ('I tend to forget.'). きらいがある follows the plain form of verbs and is typically used only for undesirable tendencies. Unlike がち, it can also express a tendency to not do something, e.g. 人の話を聞かないきらいがある ('He tends to not listen to others.').

related.title: 傾向がある
related.meaning: Tendency, Trend, Inclination
related.comparison: Both mean to have a tendency, but differ in nuance. 傾向がある is neutral and objective, used for groups or broad trends, as in 日本人は魚を食べる傾向がある (Japanese people tend to eat fish). きらいがある is often used about individuals and carries a critical tone, as in 彼は決断が遅いきらいがある (He has a tendency to be slow in making decisions).

related.title: にもかかわらず
related.meaning: Despite, In spite of, Regardless, Nevertheless
related.comparison: Both くせに and にもかかわらず convey contradiction between two facts, but with different nuance and tone. くせに is a casual expression with a blaming or critical tone, e.g. 知っているくせに何で教えてくれないの？ ('You know, so why won't you tell me!?'). In contrast, にもかかわらず is a more neutral and formal way to say 'in spite of (A)', e.g. 雨にもかかわらず出かけた ('We went out despite the rain.').

related.title: つつ(も)
related.meaning: Even while ~ing, Even though ~ing, Despite ~ing, Although ~ing
related.comparison: Both くせに and つつ(も) express contradiction, but differ in tone. くせに is a casual expression with a blaming or critical tone, e.g. 知っているくせに何で教えてくれないの？ ('You know, so why won't you tell me!?'). つつ(も) is neutral and used to describe conflicting simultaneous actions, e.g. 危険だと知りつつも行った ('I went despite knowing at the same time that it was dangerous.').

related.title: のに
related.meaning: Despite, Although, Even though
related.comparison: Both のに and くせに express contrast, but with different nuance. のに is relatively neutral, like 'although', while くせに adds a strong negative or critical tone. For example, 頭がいいのにやる気がない and 頭がいいくせにやる気がない both mean 'Although (he's) smart, (he) has no motivation.', the のに version is just stating a fact, while the くせに version implies that his lack of motivation is a negative trait and not befitting of him.

related.title: が
related.meaning: But, However
related.comparison: くせに expresses disapproval, similar to 'in spite of' or 'although' in English. In contrast, が is a neutral connecting particle, often expressing contrast (like 'but') or adding information (like 'and'). Unlike くせに, which carries a negative nuance, が is neutral. After な-Adjectives and nouns, くせに requires な or の, while が requires だ or です.

related.title: ながらも
related.meaning: But, Even while, Even though, Despite, Although
related.comparison: ながらも and くせに both express contradiction, but differ in nuance. ながらも simply expresses two seemingly contradictory states coexisting in a neutral fashion, e.g. 緊張しながらもわくわくしている ('While feeling anxious, I'm also excited.'). In contrast, くせに implies blame or criticism, e.g. 知っているくせに教えてくれない ('Even though he knows, he won't tell me.').

related.title: それなのに
related.meaning: And yet, Despite this
related.comparison: Both express contrast, but differ in usage and implication. くせに follows a phrase and has a critical tone, e.g. 知っているくせに教えてくれない ('He knows but won't tell me.'), implying that the speaker is angry at the person. それなのに comes at the beginning of a sentence, is neutral, and expresses simple contradiction, e.g. 彼は真実を知っている。それなのに、教えてくれない ('He knows. And yet, he won't tell me.') with an objective nuance.

related.title: それでも
related.meaning: But still, And yet, Even so, Nevertheless
related.comparison: それでも and くせに both express contrast, but differ in tone. それでも is neutral, like the English 'even so'. くせに adds a critical nuance, often pointing out something negative or unjustified, e.g. 若いくせにやる気がない ('Even though he's young, he has no motivation.'), which implies disapproval.

related.title: っぽい
related.meaning: -ish, -like, Characteristic of, Typical of, Tendency to
related.comparison: Both ぎみ (often written in kanji as 気味) and っぽい describe tendencies or characteristics, but with different nuance. ぎみ suggests a slight or subtle tendency, often related to physical or emotional states, e.g. 疲れ気味 ('a bit tired') or 風邪気味 ('have a bit of a cold'). っぽい implies a more innate trait that gives the appearance or impression of something, e.g. 子どもっぽい ('childish') or 忘れっぽい ('forgetful').

related.title: 的
related.meaning: ~ly・~like・~al, Typical
related.comparison: 的 and ぎみ (気味) are similar in that both can be attached to nouns to turn them into adjectives, but they differ in nuance and usage. 的 has a wider range of uses, similar to English endings like '-al', '-ly', etc., e.g. 感情 'emotion' vs. 感情的 'emotional'. In contrast, ぎみ has the specific meaning of 'slightly (A)' or 'showing tendencies of (A)', and typically expresses a subjective judgment about a condition, e.g. 疲れ気味 'a bit tired'.

related.title: ような気がする
related.meaning: Have a feeling that
related.comparison: Both ような気がする and ぎみ (from 気味) use 気 expressions to convey subtle states or impressions, but with different nuance and usage. ような気がする follows a phrase to express a subjective hunch like 'Have a feeling that (A)', e.g. 来るような気がする 'I have a feeling they'll come.' ぎみ attaches to nouns to express a slight tendency or hint of something observable, often (but not always) negative, e.g. 疲れ気味だ 'I'm feeling a bit tired.'

related.title: げ
related.meaning: Seemingly, Giving appearance of, [Expresses emotions or feelings]
related.comparison: ぎみ (often written in kanji as 気味) and げ both describe observable states, but differ in focus. ぎみ suggests a slight or subtle tendency, often related to physical or emotional states, e.g. 疲れ気味 ('a bit tired') or 風邪気味 ('have a bit of a cold'). げ expresses perceived emotions of others, e.g. 寂しげな顔 ('a lonely-looking face').

related.title: まま(に)
related.meaning: As is, While remaining, Left in a state
related.comparison: きり often marks an action done once and left that way, as in 去年会ったきり連絡がない ('I haven't heard from them since we met last year'). まま(に) marks an ongoing unchanged state, as in 靴を履いたまま入らないで ('Don't enter with your shoes on'). Both can imply continuation, but きり emphasizes a cutoff point.

related.title: を限りに
related.meaning: As of (the end of), (Starting) from, (No longer) after, By (the), Ending with
related.comparison: Both を限りに  and きり mark a point after which something stops or changes, but they differ in formality and nuance. を限りに is a fixed, formal expression used mainly in written or public contexts to indicate a clear and definitive final point, often for events, careers, or official actions, as in 本日を限りに閉店します (‘As of today, we will close for good.’). In contrast, きり is more casual and flexible, commonly used in everyday speech to say that something has not happened again since a certain point, and often used with number + counter expressions such as 一度きりしか会っていない ('I haven't met him except that one time.')

related.title: だけしか
related.meaning: To emphasize '~only', Nothing but
related.comparison: きり and だけしか both express limitation, but with different nuance. きり often implies that something has happened only once or hasn't changed since, e.g. 去年会ったきり一度も連絡が来ない 'I haven't heard from them even once since we met last year.' だけしか means 'Nothing but', typically in a way that emphasizes lack or even disappointment, e.g. 千円だけしか持っていない 'I only have 1000 yen.'

related.title: だけ
related.meaning: Only, Just
related.comparison: だけ means 'only' or 'just,' while きり has a more specific nuance. After a past tense verb, きり suggests a permanent change after an event. For example, 日本に行ったきり、戻ってこなかった means 'Once (he) went to Japan, he never returned.' When used after a number + counter (e.g. 一人きり), it emphasizes 'nothing more, nothing less.'

related.title: たなり・なり
related.meaning: While... ~ing, Remaining, Not or ever since, Keep or leave
related.comparison: たなり・なり and きり are very similar in meaning, both expressing that a certain state persists without the expected result occurring, and are often interchangeable. For example, in '東京に行ったきり地元に帰って来なかった' ('Once he went to Tokyo, he never came back to his hometown') and '東京に行ったなり地元に帰って来なかった' both convey the same lingering, unresolved situation.

related.title: しか～ない
related.meaning: There's only, Nothing but
related.comparison: きり and しか～ない can both express the idea of 'only,' but in different ways. きり is used primarily with number+counter expressions, like 一度きり 'only one time' or 二人きり 'only/just the two of us'. In contrast, しか～ない is used with a noun and negative verb to mean 'only' or 'nothing but', e.g. １００円しかない ('I have only 100 yen.'). In some cases, the two can be used together, e.g. 一度きりしか行っていない ('I only went just the once.').

antonyms.title: へいく
antonyms.meaning: To go to, To head toward
antonyms.comparison: くる and いく are both movement verbs often used with directional particles to express moving toward a particular location. The difference lies in the perspective of the speaker and directionality: くる typically expresses movement toward the speaker's location, while いく expresses movement away from the speaker's location.

related.title: てくる
related.meaning: To come to, To become, To continue, To be starting to, Has been ~ing
related.comparison: The verb くる commonly expresses movement toward the speaker, similar to the English 'come.' When combined with the て form of a verb, it can be used literally (e.g., 持ってくる = 'bring') or figuratively to express progression or change over time, like the English 'come to be' (e.g., 雨が降ってくる = 'it will come to rain').

related.title: いらっしゃる
related.meaning: To be (Honorific), To come (Honorific), To go (Honorific)
related.comparison: Both いらっしゃる and くる express movement toward the speaker, similar to the English 'come.' The key difference is politeness: くる is the neutral verb used in everyday contexts, while いらっしゃる is an honorific verb used to show respect for someone's movement. Note that いらっしゃる also serves as the honorific form of いる, いく, and くる.

related.title: くらい ①
related.meaning: About, Approximately
related.comparison: くらい (often ぐらい in speech) expresses an approximate degree or extent. It's commonly used with number + counter expressions (the くらい① form), as in 100円くらい = 'about 100 yen.' It can also be used like ほど to express extent or degree, as in 足が動けなくなるくらい走った = 'I ran so much my legs couldn't move anymore.'

related.title: ほど
related.meaning: The more, About, To the extent that, So much… that
related.comparison: Both くらい and ほど can express extent in the sense of 'so much that (A)' or 'to the extent of (A)', and are often interchangeable, e.g. 驚くくらい静かだった / 驚くほど静かだった ('It was quiet to a surprising degree.') くらい (also written as ぐらい) tends to sound slightly more colloquial, and each word has other uses where they may not be interchangeable.

related.title: まで
related.meaning: Even, To even, To the extent of
related.comparison: Both まで and ほど share the core meaning of 'to the extent of,' but are used differently. まで emphasizes inclusivity or a surprising limit, e.g. 骨まで食べた ('He even ate the bones.'). In contrast, くらい② means 'to the point of' and often expresses degree, e.g. 叫びたいくらい怖かった ('It was so scary I wanted to scream.').

synonyms.title: ほど
synonyms.meaning: The more, About, To the extent that, So much… that
synonyms.comparison: くらい (often ぐらい in speech) expresses an approximate degree or extent. It's often used with number + counter expressions (the くらい① form), as in 10分くらいかかった = 'It took about ten minutes.' ほど can be used similarly, but is more formal and carries the nuance of an upper limit—e.g., 10分ほど implies 'about, but no more than ten minutes.'

synonyms.title: ごろ
synonyms.comparison: くらい (often ぐらい in speech) expresses an approximate degree or extent. A common use is with number + counter expressions, as in 10分くらいかかった ('It took about 10 minutes'). Unlike くらい, which can be used with durations or points in time (e.g. 10分くらい, 10時くらい), ごろ can only be used with points in time (e.g. 10時ごろ = 'around 10 o'clock').

related.title: くらい ②
related.meaning: To the extent that, So…that, About the only thing that...
related.comparison: くらい (often ぐらい in speech) expresses an approximate degree or extent. It's commonly used with number + counter expressions (the くらい① form), as in 100円くらい = 'about 100 yen.' It can also be used like ほど to express extent or degree, as in 足が動けなくなるくらい走った = 'I ran so much my legs couldn't move anymore.'

related.title: は～くらいです
related.meaning: About the only, To the extent that
related.comparison: くらい (or ぐらい in speech) expresses an approximate degree or amount, like the English 'about.' It's commonly used with numbers and counters (the くらい① form), e.g. 100円くらい = 'about 100 yen.' A related pattern, は～くらいです, means 'just about the only X is Y.' For example, 食べ物で嫌いなのはピーマンくらいです = 'The only food I dislike is peppers.'

related.title: てくれる
related.meaning: To do something for someone (usually you)
related.comparison: くれる means 'to give' and is used when someone outside the speaker's group gives something to the speaker or their in-group. For example, プレゼントをくれた means '(someone) gave me a present.' As てくれる, it shows someone did something for the speaker's benefit, as in 日本語を教えてくれた ('(someone) taught me Japanese').

related.title: あげる
related.meaning: To give (away), To present, To provide
related.comparison: あげる and くれる are basic verbs for giving. あげる is used when the speaker or their in-group gives something to someone outside the group, or when one out-group member gives to another. くれる is used when someone outside the speaker's group gives something to the speaker or their in-group. In both cases, the subject marked by が is the giver.

related.title: もらう
related.meaning: To receive, To get, To obtain
related.comparison: くれる means 'to give' and is used when someone from the speaker's out-group gives something to the speaker or their in-group. The subject, marked with が, is the giver. In contrast, もらう means 'to receive' and describes the action from the receiver's perspective. With もらう, the subject marked with が is the one receiving.

related.title: てくれてありがとう
related.meaning: Thank you for ~ ing
related.comparison: くれる is a basic verb meaning 'to give,' usually used when someone from outside the speaker's group gives something to the speaker or their in-group. For example, プレゼントをくれた means '(someone) gave me a present.' It also appears in the pattern てくれてありがとう to thank someone for a favor. For example, 日本語を教えてくれてありがとう means 'Thank you for teaching me Japanese.'

related.title: なかなか
related.meaning: Very, Quite, Considerably
related.comparison: Both なかなか and けっこう are adverbs used to express extent. In affirmative sentences, they can both mean 'quite' or 'rather.' A key difference is that なかなか is often used in negative sentences to express difficulty in doing something. Meanwhile, けっこう can be used alone (especially as けっこうです) to politely decline something, like saying 'I'm fine.'

related.title: っぽい
related.meaning: -ish, -like, Characteristic of, Typical of, Tendency to
related.comparison: Both っぽい and げ describe perceived qualities, but differ in nuance. っぽい refers to innate behaviors or traits that give a certain impression. For example, 子どもっぽい ('childish') or 怒りっぽい ('quick to anger'). In contrast, げ highlights perceived emotions in others. For example, 寂しげな顔 ('a lonely-looking face').

related.title: み
related.meaning: ~ness, Used to indicate conditions or nature
related.comparison: み and げ are adjective endings that serve different roles. み turns adjectives into abstract nouns that describe a quality, e.g. '深み' ('depth') or '苦しみ' ('suffering'). げ turns adjectives or verbs into な-adjectives with the nuance of seeming to possess an emotion, e.g. '嬉しげな顔' ('a happy-seeming face') or '意味ありげな表情' ('a seemingly meaningful expression').

related.title: ような気がする
related.meaning: Have a feeling that
related.comparison: Both ような気がする and げ (from 気) use 気 expressions to convey subtle states or impressions, but with different nuance and usage. ような気がする follows a phrase to express a subjective hunch like 'Have a feeling that (A)', e.g. 来るような気がする 'I have a feeling they'll come.' げ attaches to emotion-related stems to describe appearance based on sensory perception, e.g. 悲しげな顔 'A face with a sorrowful air.'

related.title: ぎみ
related.meaning: Looking, Feeling a little, Touch of, Tending to, Slightly
related.comparison: ぎみ (often written in kanji as 気味) and げ both describe observable states, but differ in focus. ぎみ suggests a slight or subtle tendency, often related to physical or emotional states, e.g. 疲れ気味 ('a bit tired') or 風邪気味 ('have a bit of a cold'). げ expresses perceived emotions of others, e.g. 寂しげな顔 ('a lonely-looking face').

related.title: そうに・そうな
related.meaning: Seem, Look like, Sound
related.comparison: そうに・そうな and げ both describe appearances, but with some differences. Both can be used with adjectives like 優しそうな目 'kind-looking eyes' and 優しげな目 (same meaning), though げ is more subjective and poetic. Unlike げ, そう can also describe things that seem about to happen, e.g. 雨が降りそうな空 'a sky that looks like it's about to rain'.

synonyms.title: が
synonyms.comparison: Both が (or だが/ですが after a noun or な-adjective) and けど・だけど express contrast or transition, similar to 'but' in English (e.g., '見た目は美味しそうだけど、味がない' = 'It looks tasty, but has no flavor'). The difference is that が is more formal or written-style, while けど is more colloquial. Both can also be used at the end of a sentence to suggest an implied contrast, even if not explicitly stated.

related.title: だが・ですが
related.meaning: But, Still, However, Nevertheless
related.comparison: Both けど・だけど (and the polite ですけど), as well as だが・ですが, express contrast, similar to 'but,' or sometimes just indicate a transition (like 'and'). The key difference is that だが is more formal and used in written contexts, while けど is more casual. Both can be used at the end of a sentence to imply something is left unsaid.

related.title: ところが
related.meaning: Even so, However, Even though, But
related.comparison: Both ところが and けど・だけど express contrast or contradiction. The key difference is that ところが is used at the beginning of a sentence to emphasize a contrast between the expected result and reality, and is more formal. In contrast, けど・だけど are used after a phrase or at the end of a sentence to express simple contrast or contradiction.

related.title: んだけど・んですが
related.meaning: But..., And...
related.comparison: Both introduce contrast, like the English 'but'. けど・だけど are common in speech, as in 難しいけど面白い ('It's difficult, but interesting.'). Adding ～ん to this pattern adds an explanatory nuance, often used to imply a request or hesitation, e.g. 難しいんですけど 'It's difficult, but...(do you want me to do it anyway?, etc.)'.

related.title: けれども
related.meaning: But, Although
related.comparison: けど and けれども are essentially the same expression, with けど being a shortened form of けれども. Both express contrast, like 'but' in English, or soften a statement. The difference is in their formality: けど is casual and colloquial, while けれども is more formal and is most often used in writing or formal speech.

synonyms.title: ものの
synonyms.comparison: Both ものの and けれども can appear at the end of a clause to show contrast. けれども (or the casual けど) usually expresses a simple contrast or adds background information, and is used in a wide range of situations. In contrast, ものの has the specific nuance of 'While A is true, B is not,' and is less colloquial, more commonly found in writing or formal speech.

synonyms.title: が
synonyms.comparison: Both が and けれども express contrast, similar to 'but' in English, or sometimes simply indicate a transition to added information (like 'and'). Both are somewhat formal, used in writing or formal speech, compared to the casual けど. Note that while が and けれども follow immediately after verbs or い-Adjectives, だ or です is required after a な-Adjective or noun.

related.title: だが・ですが
related.meaning: But, Still, However, Nevertheless
related.comparison: Both of these are linking phrases used to express contrast in Japanese. だが and ですが sound a bit stiffer than けれども or the more casual けど, and must follow a noun or な-adjective (in contrast, けれども would become だけれども in this case). Besides showing contrast, both が and けれども (or けれど・けど) can also be used to add background information without directly meaning 'but'.

related.title: ところが
related.meaning: Even so, However, Even though, But
related.comparison: Both ところが and けれども are linking phrases used to express contrast. けれども (or the casual form けど) shows a simple contrast or transition, similar to 'but' in English, and can appear mid-sentence or at the end. In contrast, ところが is typically used at the beginning of a sentence and adds a strong nuance of surprise or something unexpected.

related.title: けど・だけど
related.meaning: But, However
related.comparison: けど and けれども are essentially the same expression, with けど being a shortened form of けれども. Both express contrast, like 'but' in English, or soften a statement. The difference is in their formality: けど is casual and colloquial, while けれども is more formal and is most often used in writing or formal speech.

related.title: これ
related.meaning: This
related.comparison: これ refers to something close to the speaker, either a physical object or figurative idea, similar to 'this' in English. On the other hand, ここ refers to a location or place close to the speaker, like 'here' in English. In other words, the primary difference between the two is that これ refers to things, while ここ refers to locations.

related.title: それ
related.meaning: That
related.comparison: ここ refers to a location where the speaker is, or is pointing to (like on a map), and is similar to 'here' in English. それ refers to something near the listener or something just mentioned, like 'that.' The key difference is that ここ refers to a place, while それ refers to a thing.

related.title: あの
related.meaning: That (over there)
related.comparison: ここ refers to a location close to the speaker or to a place the speaker points to, similar to 'here' in English. あの refers to something far from both the speaker and listener, or something they both know, similar to 'that' in English. Unlike ここ, あの is used for objects or concepts and must directly precede a noun (e.g., あの本 = 'that book').

related.title: その
related.meaning: That
related.comparison: その refers to something near the listener, similar to 'that' in English, and must directly precede a noun (e.g., その本 = 'that book'). In contrast, ここ refers to a location near or indicated by the speaker, similar to 'here.' Unlike その, ここ stands alone as 'this place' and doesn't require a noun.

related.title: この
related.meaning: This
related.comparison: ここ refers directly to a place close to the speaker and can stand alone, similar to 'here' or 'this place' in English. この, however, must be followed by a noun to specify an object or person near the speaker (e.g., この本 = 'this book'). Thus, ここ and この differ in both usage and what they refer to.

related.title: あれ
related.meaning: That (over there)
related.comparison: ここ refers to a place where the speaker is or is pointing to, similar to 'here' in English. あれ, on the other hand, refers to something far from both the speaker and listener, or something they both know but isn't nearby, similar to 'that (over there).' The key difference is whether you're talking about a place or a thing, and from whose point of view.

related.title: あそこ
related.meaning: Over there
related.comparison: あそこ and ここ both indicate locations but differ in perspective. あそこ refers to a place far from both the speaker and the listener, similar to 'over there' in English. On the other hand, ここ refers to a place near the speaker, similar to 'here' or 'this place.'

related.title: そこ
related.meaning: There, That place
related.comparison: そこ and ここ are both used to indicate locations, with the difference being in the speaker's perspective. そこ refers to a location closer to the listener than to the speaker, similar to 'there' or 'that place' in English. On the other hand, ここ refers to a location closer to the speaker, similar to 'here' or 'this place.'

related.title: どこ
related.meaning: Where
related.comparison: どこ is a question word used to ask about locations, similar to 'where' in English. In contrast, ここ refers specifically to a place close to the speaker, either physically or figuratively, equivalent to 'here.' For example, トイレはどこですか？ ('Where is the bathroom?') could be answered by ここです ('It's here'—near the speaker or indicated location).

related.title: のはXの方だ
related.meaning: It is...that..., It is...who..., ...is the one that..., ...is the one who...
related.comparison: Both structures single out and highlight something, but in different ways. のはXの方だ highlights contrast between options and is often used in apologies, e.g. 悪いのは僕の方だ ('I'm the one that's at fault.') こそ emphasizes exclusivity or importance, e.g. 私こそ謝るべきだ ('I, and no one else, should apologize.')

related.title: なんか・なんて
related.meaning: Such as, Things like, Emphasis
related.comparison: Both add emphasis, but with opposite nuance. なんか・なんて often downplays or dismisses, while こそ highlights importance or exclusivity. The former can feel self-deprecating or casual, while the latter stresses that something uniquely applies. Compare 私なんかできるのかな？ ('I wonder if someone like me could do it?') versus 彼こそできる ('Him, if no one else, can do it.')

related.title: からこそ
related.meaning: Emphasized because, Precisely because
related.comparison: Both use こそ for emphasis, but からこそ highlights a reason or cause, meaning 'precisely because', e.g. 努力したからこそできた ('I could do it precisely because I worked hard.'). こそ on its own emphasizes the word it follows, framing it as precisely the one, e.g. 彼こそできる ('He, if no one else, can do it.')

related.title: より
related.meaning: More than, Over
related.comparison: より expresses a general comparison, like 'more than (A)' or '~er,' e.g. 月曜日より水曜日がいい ('Wednesday is better than Monday.') or 走るより野球をするのが好き ('I prefer playing baseball to running.'). In contrast, こそ adds emphasis, highlighting something as especially important, e.g. 月曜日こそ図書館に行きたい ('Monday is precisely when I want to go to the library.').

related.title: ～てこそ
related.meaning: Only if, Because, By, Unless, Until
related.comparison: Both involve the same こそ, but one attaches to nouns and the other to verb + て form. こそ emphasizes a specific word or element, often to contrast or highlight, e.g. 彼こそできる ('He, if nobody else, can do it.'). In contrast, ～てこそ focuses a condition that must be met for something to be true, e.g. 大人になってこそ分かる 'understand only after becoming an adult'.

related.title: なら
related.meaning: Were it, If, If it's the case, As for (Conditional)
related.comparison: こそ and なら can look similar after a noun, e.g. 彼こそできる vs. 彼ならできる, but differ in nuance. こそ is emphatic, suggesting that something someone is 'the one', e.g. 彼こそできる ('He's exactly the one who can do it.'). なら implies a conditional focus, e.g. 彼ならできる ('If it's him we're talking about, he can do it.'), without the nuance of excluding other possibilities that こそ can have.

related.title: あまり
related.meaning: So much...that
related.comparison: Both express emphasis, but in different ways. あまり is a noun that emphasizes an extreme degree leading to an unintended result, e.g. 悲しみのあまり泣き出した ('She burst into tears from too much sadness.') こそ is a particle that stresses importance or exclusivity, e.g. 今日こそ早く寝よう ('Today, for sure, I'll go to bed early.')

related.title: のがすき
related.meaning: Like doing, Love doing
related.comparison: This phrase combines the nominalizer の with the な-Adjective 好き to express liking an activity (e.g., 本を読むのが好き = 'I like to read books'). の can be replaced with こと, another nominalizer. The difference is subtle: の implies a concrete or tangible action, while こと is more abstract (e.g., 本を読むことが好き implies liking the 'act of reading').

related.title: ことなの
related.meaning: (A) is (Description of A), (A) means that (Description of A)
related.comparison: These related expressions both use the noun こと, or 事 in kanji. On its own, こと turns a verb or adjective into a noun phrase, e.g. 食べる 'to eat' becomes 食べること 'the act of eating'. When combined with the explanatory/emphatic なの, it conveys meaning or definition, e.g. 'It means that (A)' or '(A) is how it is.'

related.title: の
related.meaning: Verb nominalizer, The one who..., That which...
related.comparison: Both こと and の turn verbs or adjectives into noun phrases. こと is used for intangible or conceptual ideas, while の is used for more tangible or physical concepts. For example, 走るのが好きです and 走ることが好きです both mean 'I like running,' with の feeling more physical and こと more conceptual. However, they aren't interchangeable in set phrases like ことができる or ことがある.

related.title: ということ
related.meaning: ~ing, The ~ that ~ (Nominalization)
related.comparison: こと attaches to the dictionary form of verbs to turn them into nouns, e.g. 行くこと '(the act of) going'. ということ has a similar function, but adds the quoting phrase という, so it's often used to clarify information or reported speech, e.g. 日本に行くということ 'the fact that (he) is going to Japan'.

related.title: ものか
related.meaning: Definitely not, Absolutely not, Impossible, Do you really think~?
related.comparison: Both ことか and ものか express strong emotion, but differ in purpose and tone. ことか is a rhetorical exclamation used to convey deep feelings like admiration or regret, e.g. どんなに嬉しかったことか 'How happy I was!' ものか expresses emphatic denial or rejection, e.g. 行くものか 'As if I'd go!' ものか can also appear as the casual contraction もんか, while ことか is typically more formal or literary.

related.title: 一体
related.meaning: (What) the heck?, (Who) in the world?, (Where) on earth?, (How many) f*ing...
related.comparison: 一体 and ことか both add emphasis to questions, but differ in nuance. 一体 strengthens genuine questions with a tone of surprise or frustration, like 'What in the world?', e.g. 一体何があったんですか？ 'What in the world happened?' ことか appears in rhetorical questions or exclamations to express strong emotion, e.g. 彼がどんなに努力したことか 'How hard he worked!'

related.title: 点
related.meaning: Aspect, Respect, Point, Way, In terms of, In that, From the point of view
related.comparison: ことから is used to draw a logical conclusion from a fact or observation, e.g. 顔色が悪いことから風邪を引いていると分かった 'From the fact that his face was pale, I could tell he had caught a cold.' 点 comments on a specific point without implying a conclusion, e.g. 安さという点でこのスマホのほうがいい 'This smartphone is better from the perspective of low price.'

related.title: から言うと
related.meaning: Speaking in terms of, From the point of view of
related.comparison: ことから and から言うと both express basis or reasoning, but differ in tone and usage. ことから is generally used for factual inference or deduction, e.g. 電気が付いていないことから、誰もいないと分かった ('Judging from the fact the lights are off, I could tell no one is there'). から言うと provides a basis for a stated opinion, e.g. 経験から言うと注意が必要だ ('Speaking from experience, caution is required').

related.title: からして
related.meaning: Even..., Judging from, Based on
related.comparison: ことから and からして both express reasoning, but in different ways. ことから means 'from the fact that (A)' and is used to explain a cause or basis, e.g. 声がふるえていることから緊張していると分かる ('From the fact their voice is trembling, I know they're nervous'). からして highlights a telling detail as evidence, e.g. 見た目からして運動していそう ('Judging from her appearance, it looks like she exercises').

related.title: からすると・からすれば
related.slug: からすると-からすれば
related.meaning: Judging from, Considering
related.comparison: ことから and からすると・からすれば both describe drawing conclusions, but differ in tone and focus. ことから shows cause and effect based on a fact, e.g. 足音がしたことから誰かがいると分かる ('From the fact we heard footsteps, we know someone is there'). からすると・からすれば show judgment from a certain viewpoint, e.g. 足音からすると一人の女性だったみたい ('Judging from the footsteps, it seems like it was one woman').

related.title: ことだから
related.meaning: It is exactly because
related.comparison: ことから and ことだから both express reasons, but differ in usage. ことから explains a fact-based conclusion, e.g. 顔が赤いことから熱があると分かった ('From the fact their face is red, I knew they had a fever'). ことだから gives a reason based on someone's known traits, e.g. 田中さんのことだから、もう宿題は終わっているだろう ('Since we're talking about Tanaka, he's probably finished his assignment already').

synonyms.title: れる・られる (Potential)
synonyms.comparison: Both express potential or ability, like 'can do'. ことができる is more formal and common in writing, e.g. 書くことができる 'can write', while the potential form れる・られる is shorter and used in conversation, e.g. 書ける 'can write'. The meaning is the same, but usage differs by tone and context.

antonyms.title: ことはない
antonyms.meaning: There is no need to, Never happens
antonyms.comparison: These two expressions can be opposite in meaning. ことはない means that something never happens or is not necessary, e.g. 彼と話すことはない ('There are no occasions where I talk to him'), while ことがある indicates occasional occurrence, e.g. 道に迷うことがある ('I sometimes get lost'). Note that ことはない can also express 'no need to', e.g. 心配することはない ('There's no need to worry'), which is unrelated to any use of ことがある.

related.title: たことがある
related.meaning: Have done before
related.comparison: With a non-past verb, ことがある expresses that something happens occasionally or from time to time, similar to saying 'There are times when…' In contrast, たことがある uses the past tense to describe a completed experience. For example, 日本に行ったことがある means 'I've been to Japan.' It refers to something that has happened at least once, without implying it occurred repeatedly.

related.title: とき
related.meaning: When, At the time of
related.comparison: とき marks the time of an action or event, like 'when' in English, e.g. 雨の時 or 雨が降るとき 'when it rains'. ことがある expresses something that happens occasionally, e.g. 雨が降ることがある ('There are occasions when it rains.'). Because they express different concepts, the two can be combined, as in: 時間がないとき、コンビニ弁当を食べることがある ('When I don't have time, there are occasions when I eat a convenience store lunch box.').

synonyms.title: たほうがいい
synonyms.meaning: It'd be better to, Should do
synonyms.comparison: たほうがいい is a common expression used to give advice or make suggestions, often reflecting the speaker's personal opinion on what would be better. ことだ may appear similar but is more formal, carrying a stronger nuance of expectation, implying 'this is how things are (or should be,' rather than a personal suggestion.

antonyms.title: べきではない
antonyms.meaning: Should not, Must not , Ought not to
antonyms.comparison: ことだ suggests what one should do as advice or a general guideline, e.g. 無理をしないことだ ('You should take it easy.') べきではない expresses the opposite, indicating what one should not do based on moral or social expectations, e.g. 人を見た目で判断すべきではない ('You shouldn't judge people by appearances.')

antonyms.title: ことはない
antonyms.meaning: There is no need to, Never happens
antonyms.comparison: ことはない downplays the need for action and is often used to reassure, e.g. 焦ることはない ('There's no need to rush.') ことだ gives firm advice and carries an authoritative tone, e.g. 焦らずに進めることだ ('You should proceed calmly.') ことはない can also mean that something never occurs, which is a usage unrelated to ことだ, e.g. 彼と会うことはない ('I never see him.')

antonyms.title: てはならない
antonyms.comparison: Both carry an authoritative tone, as if stating a rule, but have opposite meanings. ことだ gives strong advice or recommendations, e.g. 約束は守ることだ ('You should keep your promises.') てはならない expresses strict prohibition, e.g. 約束を破ってはならない ('You must not break your promises.')

antonyms.title: ないほうがいい
antonyms.meaning: It'd be better not to, Shouldn't do
antonyms.comparison: ないほうがいい is used to suggest not doing something, similar to 'You shouldn't…' in English. It's a common expression that often reflects the speaker's personal or subjective opinion. In contrast, ことだ expresses the opposite idea, meaning 'should' or 'ought to,' and is more formal. It carries the nuance of stating a rule or general expectation rather than personal advice.

related.title: 命令形
related.meaning: (Verb)!, Do (something)!
related.comparison: The imperative or command form  (命令形) gives direct, blunt commands, e.g. 止まれ ('Stop!'). It can sound harsh unless softened with particles like よ, e.g. 頑張れよ ('Try harder, okay?'). In contrast, ことだ offers soft advice, e.g. 早起きすることだ ('You should wake up early.'), and is often used when giving recommendations or suggestions.

related.title: ばいい
related.meaning: Can, Should, It'd be good if
related.comparison: Both can be used to give advice or suggestions, but the tone differs. ことだ sounds more formal or authoritative, as if stating a rule or guideline. In contrast, ばいい is more casual and conversational, often used among friends. Additionally, ばいい can be used in contrary-to-fact suggestions, such as 行けばいいのに ('You should go but you won't'). ことだ cannot be used this way.

related.title: ものだ
related.meaning: Supposed to, Should, That's the way it is
related.comparison: Both express what should be done, but with different nuance. ことだ is used to give firm advice, e.g. 諦めずに続けることだ ('You should keep going without giving up.') ものだ expresses general expectations or norms, e.g. 仕事は地道に頑張り続けるものだ ('One is expected to keep working steadily.')

related.title: べき
related.meaning: Must, Should , Ought to
related.comparison: Both express obligation, but differ in tone. ことだ is used to offer advice or general suggestions, e.g. 無理をしないことだ ('You should take it easy.') べき is stronger, implying moral or logical necessity, e.g. 約束は守るべきだ ('You should keep your promises.')

related.title: わけだ
related.meaning: For that reason, No wonder, As you'd expect, Naturally, Thus, This is why
related.comparison: Both express reasoning in a broad sense, but are used differently. わけだ explains why something is the case, e.g. 暖房が壊れているのか。だから寒いわけだ ('So the heater's broken. That explains why it's cold.') ことだ gives strong advice or instruction, e.g. 寒いなら暖房をつけることだ ('If it's cold, you should turn on the heater.')

related.title: なければならない
related.meaning: Must do, Have to do
related.comparison: ことだ is used for light advice ('should' or 'ought to'), e.g. 毎日早起きすることだ ('You should get up early every day.'). なければならない expresses stronger obligation, like 'must' or 'have to', often implying consequences. ７時に起きなければならない ('You must get up at 7.').

related.title: ものだから
related.meaning: Because, The reason is
related.comparison: Both give reasons, but differ in tone. ことだから emphasizes a reason that is obvious or characteristic, as in 田中さんのことだから大丈夫だろう (Since it’s Tanaka, it should be fine). ものだから is often used to explain or excuse, as in 熱があるものだから休みます (I have a fever, so I’ll take a day off).

related.title: もの・もん
related.meaning: Because, Cause
related.comparison: Both もの・もん and ことだから can be used to give reasons, but they differ in tone and intent. もの・もん is casual, colloquial, and subjective, used to express personal feelings or justifications, like 'because (A)'. ことだから, on the other hand, has a more objective and emphatic feel, often meaning 'it is precisely because (A) that (B)'. In some contexts, ことだから can also imply 'considering (A), then (B)'.

related.title: なぜなら〜から
related.meaning: Because, The reason is, That is because
related.comparison: These phrases are similar in that both express a reason with a degree of certainty, but they differ in specific usage and nuance. なぜなら〜から is used at the beginning of a sentence to provide a reason for something previously said, explicitly saying 'as for why, (A)'. ことだから is used at the end of a phrase with the reason following, conveying 'it is precisely because (A) that (B)'.

related.title: からこそ
related.meaning: Emphasized because, Precisely because
related.comparison: からこそ and ことだから are similar in that both can emphatically state a reason. However, ことだから is typically used to make a prediction or assumption based on a person's known behavior or character, e.g. 田中さんのことだからできると思う ('Because it's Tanaka, I think he can do it'). In contrast, からこそ is more broadly used to emphasize a specific reason, e.g. 努力したからこそできた ('I could do it precisely because I worked hard').

related.title: ことから
related.meaning: From the fact that
related.comparison: ことから and ことだから both express reasons, but differ in usage. ことから explains a fact-based conclusion, e.g. 顔が赤いことから熱があると分かった ('From the fact their face is red, I knew they had a fever'). ことだから gives a reason based on someone's known traits, e.g. 田中さんのことだから、もう宿題は終わっているだろう ('Since we're talking about Tanaka, he's probably finished his assignment already').

related.title: し～し
related.meaning: And, Giving reasons
related.comparison: Both give reasons, but the structure and emphasis differ. ことだし presents a reason as a settled or obvious circumstance that naturally justifies a decision, often sounding calm and explanatory, as in 雨も降っていることだし、今日は家にいよう ('Since it’s raining anyway, let’s stay home today.'). By contrast, し～し lists multiple reasons side by side, emphasizing accumulation rather than inevitability, as in 雨だし寒いし、出かけたくない ('It’s raining and cold, so I don’t want to go out.').

related.title: ので
related.meaning: Because, So, Since, The reason being
related.comparison: Both express cause, but the stance is different. ことだし frames the reason as an accepted situation that makes the conclusion feel reasonable or timely, often used when gently proposing or deciding something, as in 時間もないことだし、先に始めよう ('Since we don’t have much time, let’s start first.'). ので is a neutral causal connector focusing on objective explanation, commonly used to state factual reasons without added nuance, as in 雨が降っているので、試合は中止です ('Because it is raining, the game is canceled.').

related.title: べくして
related.meaning: As expected, Meant to, Supposed to, Destined to, Sure to
related.comparison: Both suggest that a result follows naturally, but the viewpoint is different. ことだし presents a reason as a settled circumstance that makes a decision reasonable at the moment, often used for gentle justification or proposals, as in 雨も降っていることだし、今日は家にいよう ('Since it’s raining anyway, let’s stay home today.'). べくして is a formal, written expression meaning 'bound to' or 'inevitably,' describing an outcome that follows naturally from prior conditions, often with hindsight, as in 彼は失敗すべくして失敗した ('He failed as was inevitable.').

related.title: こととて
related.meaning: Due to, Since, On account of, Because
related.comparison: Both give reasons based on circumstances, but the nuance and formality differ. こととて is a formal, written expression used to justify an outcome by presenting the reason as unavoidable or understandable, often in apologies or explanations, as in 不慣れなこととて、ご迷惑をおかけしました ('As I am inexperienced, I caused you trouble.'). ことだし presents a reason as a known or settled situation that makes a decision feel reasonable or timely, and is common in spoken language, as in 雨も降っていることだし、今日は家にいよう ('Since it is raining anyway, let’s stay home today.').

related.title: ずに
related.meaning: Without doing
related.comparison: Both ずに and ことなく mean 'without doing (A)' and can often be used interchangeably. ずに is more common and used in both writing and conversation, e.g. '宿題をせずに寝た' ('I went to bed without doing my homework'). ことなく is more formal and tends to appear in writing, e.g. '彼は休むことなく働き続けた' ('He continued working without rest').

related.title: ということ
related.meaning: ~ing, The ~ that ~ (Nominalization)
related.comparison: ことなの combines こと with the explanatory ～んです／のです form and is used to give or ask for background with an emphatic, explanatory tone. ということ also turns a phrase into a noun, meaning 'the fact that' or 'the act of (A)', but is typically used to clarify something without the added emphasis of ん／の.

related.title: ということだ
related.meaning: I heard that, Rumor has it that, It is said that, It means that
related.comparison: Both use こと to summarize information, but with different nuances. ことなの means 'it's that (A)' and is often used to clarify or define something, as in '(A) means (B)' or 'that means (A).' ということだ can also be used in this way, but additionally functions to report hearsay, e.g. 'I hear / They say (A).' ことなの, without the quoting function of という, is not used to report what others have said.

related.title: というのは
related.meaning: ~ Known as ~ is, ~ Called ~ is, ~ Means ~, Because
related.comparison: ことなの and というのは can both be used in explanations, but differ in position and role. ことなの appears at the end of a sentence to emphasize a conclusion or explanation. Literally, 'it's that it's (A)'. というのは follows a noun or phrase to introduce it as a topic to be explained. They can be used together, e.g. 人生というのはこういうことなんだ ('This thing we call life... this is what it means.')

related.title: ということは
related.meaning: That means, That is to say
related.comparison: These two expressions are similar in that both can be used when offering or seeking explanations. ことなの is used at the end of a phrase or sentence, often to clarify a particular point, e.g. 今日は来ないということなの？ ('So does that mean he's not coming today?'), while ということは begins a sentence and sets up a conclusion based on what was just said, e.g. ということは、今日は来ないんだ ('So that means he's not coming today').

related.title: こと
related.meaning: Converting a verb into a noun
related.comparison: These related expressions both use the noun こと, or 事 in kanji. On its own, こと turns a verb or adjective into a noun phrase, e.g. 食べる 'to eat' becomes 食べること 'the act of eating'. When combined with the explanatory/emphatic なの, it conveys meaning or definition, e.g. 'It means that (A)' or '(A) is how it is.'

related.title: ことだし
related.meaning: (And) so, Because, Since
related.comparison: Both give reasons based on circumstances, but the nuance and formality differ. こととて is a formal, written expression used to justify an outcome by presenting the reason as unavoidable or understandable, often in apologies or explanations, as in 不慣れなこととて、ご迷惑をおかけしました ('As I am inexperienced, I caused you trouble.'). ことだし presents a reason as a known or settled situation that makes a decision feel reasonable or timely, and is common in spoken language, as in 雨も降っていることだし、今日は家にいよう ('Since it is raining anyway, let’s stay home today.').

antonyms.title: そうにない
antonyms.meaning: Unlikely to, Showing no signs of
antonyms.comparison: ことに emphasizes a strong emotional or subjective reaction to an event, while そうにない expresses objective improbability. For example, 驚いたことに、巨人が負けた 'To my surprise, the Giants lost.' expresses emotional impact, whereas 雨は降りそうにない 'It doesn't look like it will rain.' simply states an unemotional likelihood.

related.title: てたまらない
related.meaning: Can't help but want to, Very, Extremely
