related.comparison: Both ことに and てたまらない express emotion, but in different ways. ことに highlights the speaker's emotional response to a situation, usually following an adjective or verb, e.g. 悲しいことに、誰も来なかった 'Sadly, no one came.' てたまらない conveys an overwhelming personal feeling, often physical or emotional, e.g. 会いたくてたまらない 'I want to see you so badly I can't stand it.'

related.title: てしょうがない
related.meaning: Extremely, Very, Really, It can't be helped
related.comparison: Both can express emotion, but ことに highlights the emotional tone of a situation, e.g. 嬉しいことに、試験に合格しました 'To my delight, I passed the test.' てしょうがない expresses a strong, almost uncontrollable personal feeling, e.g. 帰りたくてしょうがない 'I want to go home so badly I can't help it.'

related.title: てならない
related.meaning: Very, Extremely, Can't help but do
related.comparison: ことに and てならない both express emotional intensity, but differ in focus. ことに emphasizes the emotional tone of the situation itself, e.g. 悔しいことに、負けてしまった 'Regretfully, I lost.' てならない expresses a deeply felt, uncontrollable emotion, e.g. 心配でならない 'I can't help but worry.'

related.title: 幸い・幸いなことに
related.meaning: Luckily, Fortunately
related.comparison: 幸いなことに (sometimes shortened to just 幸い) means 'Fortunately', and is a fixed phrase, e.g. 幸い(なことに)、大事には至らなかった 'Fortunately, it didn't become serious.' It's a variation of ことに, which highlights emotional reactions. While 幸い expresses a positive outcome, ことに can also appear with other emotion words, like 驚いたことに 'To my surprise' or 悲しいことに 'Sadly'.

synonyms.title: にする
synonyms.meaning: To decide on, To choose, To make something into, To view something as
synonyms.comparison: Both にする and ことにする share the same core meaning: making a choice or decision. にする is used when choosing a thing and follows a noun, like ビールにする ('I'll have a beer'). ことにする follows plain-form verbs and expresses the speaker's decision to do or not do something, like 行くことにする or 行かないことにする.

related.title: ことになる
related.meaning: It will end up being, It is the result of, It has been decided that
related.comparison: These expressions are similar in that both are used to express decisions. ことになる conveys that something has been decided or will happen due to external factors, e.g. 来月転勤することになった ('It's been decided that I'll be transferred next month'), while ことにする describes something the speaker has decided of their own power, e.g. 来月仕事を辞めることにした ('I've decided to quit my job next month').

related.title: ～にする・～くする
related.meaning: Adjective + する, To make something/someone (more)
related.comparison: ～にする・～くする and ことにする both involve making something a certain way, but differ in usage. ～にする and ～くする are used with nouns and な-Adjectives, e.g. 綺麗にする 'make clean' and 甘くする 'make sweet'. ことにする, used with verbs, implies deciding on an action, e.g. いくことにする 'decide to go'.

related.title: ようにする
related.meaning: To try to, To make sure to, To endeavor to
related.comparison: ことにする and ようにする are closely related but have different nuances. ことにする is used to express a resolution or decision, e.g. 来年、マラソンを走ることにした ('I decided to run a marathon next year.'). In contrast, ようにする expresses making an effort to carry out an action and is often used for ongoing intentions or habits, e.g. 毎朝走るようにしている ('I'm trying to run every morning.').

related.title: ことになる
related.meaning: It will end up being, It is the result of, It has been decided that
related.comparison: These are closely related patterns with a slight but clear nuance difference. ことになる expresses that something has been decided or will happen, often beyond the speaker's control, e.g. 転勤することになった ('It's been decided that I'll be transferred'), while ことになっている is typically used to refer to an established or habitual rule, e.g. 会議を行うことになっている ('It's been decided we'll hold a meeting').

related.title: はずだ
related.meaning: Bound to (be), Expected to (be), Should be (able)
related.comparison: Both can describe future events, but differ in source. はずだ shows the speaker's expectation, like 来るはずだ ('He should come.'). ことになっている refers to something officially decided, often outside the speaker's control. For example, 来ることになっている ('It has been decided that he will come.')

related.title: にする
related.meaning: To decide on, To choose, To make something into, To view something as
related.comparison: Both にする and ことになる relate to decisions but differ in agency. にする is used when the speaker actively chooses something, often in daily situations like deciding what to eat, similar to 'I'll go with X.' ことになる, on the other hand, expresses decisions made by others or by circumstance, like saying 'It's been decided that X.'

related.title: ことにする
related.meaning: To decide on, Have decided to
related.comparison: These expressions are similar in that both are used to express decisions. ことになる conveys that something has been decided or will happen due to external factors, e.g. 来月転勤することになった ('It's been decided that I'll be transferred next month'), while ことにする describes something the speaker has decided of their own power, e.g. 来月仕事を辞めることにした ('I've decided to quit my job next month').

related.title: ことになっている
related.meaning: To be expected to, To be scheduled to
related.comparison: These are closely related patterns with a slight but clear nuance difference. ことになる expresses that something has been decided or will happen, often beyond the speaker's control, e.g. 転勤することになった ('It's been decided that I'll be transferred'), while ことになっている is typically used to refer to an established or habitual rule, e.g. 会議を行うことになっている ('It's been decided we'll hold a meeting').

related.title: に決まっている
related.meaning: Surely, Bound to, Certainly, Be decided
related.comparison: These patterns are related in that both center around the idea of something being decided. ことになる expresses that something has been decided or will happen, often beyond the speaker's control, e.g. 転勤することになった ('It's been decided that I'll be transferred'), while に決まっている expresses that something is decisively true, e.g. 勝つに決まっている ('They'll definitely win').

related.title: ようになる
related.meaning: To reach the point that, To come to be that, To turn into
related.comparison: Both ようになる and ことになる express something becoming a certain way, but with different nuances. ようになる describes a change in state, e.g. 話せるようになった ('I became able to speak.') ことになる, on the other hand, conveys a decision made, often outside the speaker's control, e.g. 辞めることになった ('It was decided that I/he/she would quit.').

antonyms.title: からには
antonyms.comparison: からには shows that once a condition is met, a natural consequence or obligation follows, as in 約束したからには守るべきだ (Since I made a promise, I should keep it). ことにはならない shows that fulfilling a condition does not guarantee the expected result, as in 読んだからといって理解したことにはならない (Just because you read it doesn’t mean you understood it).

related.title: というものでもない
related.meaning: There is no guarantee that…, Not necessarily
related.comparison: Both mean just because (A), it doesn’t mean (B). ことにはならない emphasizes that one condition doesn’t guarantee a result, as in 勉強したからといって合格することにはならない (Just because you studied doesn’t mean you’ll pass). というものでもない carries a stronger nuance that the claim cannot be taken as an absolute truth, as in お金があれば幸せになれるというものでもない (Having money doesn’t necessarily mean you’ll be happy).

related.title: とは限らない
related.meaning: Not necessarily so, Is not always true, There is no guarantee that
related.comparison: While both deny expected conclusions, ことにはならない expresses that one thing doesn't necessarily lead to another, e.g. '話し合ったからといって解決したことにはならない' ('Just talking about it doesn't mean it's resolved'). とは限らない expresses that something isn't necessarily the case, e.g. '高いものが必ずしも良いとは限らない' ('Expensive things aren't always good').

antonyms.title: ことだ
antonyms.comparison: ことはない downplays the need for action and is often used to reassure, e.g. 焦ることはない ('There's no need to rush.') ことだ gives firm advice and carries an authoritative tone, e.g. 焦らずに進めることだ ('You should proceed calmly.') ことはない can also mean that something never occurs, which is a usage unrelated to ことだ, e.g. 彼と会うことはない ('I never see him.')

antonyms.title: ことがある
antonyms.meaning: Something is possible, Sometimes, Occasionally, There are times when
antonyms.comparison: These two expressions can be opposite in meaning. ことはない means that something never happens or is not necessary, e.g. 彼と話すことはない ('There are no occasions where I talk to him'), while ことがある indicates occasional occurrence, e.g. 道に迷うことがある ('I sometimes get lost'). Note that ことはない can also express 'no need to', e.g. 心配することはない ('There's no need to worry'), which is unrelated to any use of ことがある.

antonyms.title: ひつようがある
antonyms.comparison: These forms are opposites in meaning. ひつようがある shows something must be done, as in 行く必要がある ('You need to go.'). ことはない reassures that something doesn't need to be done, as in 行くことはない ('You don't need to go.'). One expresses necessity, the other expresses lack of it.

related.title: すこしも～ない
related.meaning: Not even a little, Not at all, Not in the least, Never
related.comparison: ことはない and すこしも～ない are both emphatic negatives, but with different nuances. ことはない means 'There's no need to (A).' or 'There are no instances of (A).'. For example, 心配することはない ('There's no need to worry.'), and 彼と話すことはない ('There are no occasions when I talk to him.'). In contrast, すこしも～ない emphasizes absence of degree or amount, e.g., すこしも心配していない ('I'm not even a little bit worried.').

related.title: はずがない
related.meaning: Hardly possible, Cannot be, Highly unlikely, Improbable
related.comparison: ことはない has two primary uses: one is to express that there is no need to do something, e.g. 心配することはない ('There's no need to worry.'), and the other is to say that something simply doesn't happen, e.g. 彼と会うことはない ('There are no cases where I meet him.'). In contrast, はずがない expresses the speaker's strong denial or disbelief regarding some possibility, e.g. 負けるはずがない ('There's no way / It's highly unlikely we will lose.').

related.title: までもない
related.meaning: There is no need to, Do not have to, It is not necessary to, Needless to
related.comparison: Both can serve as emphatic ways to express 'there is no need to', e.g. 心配する（までもない/ことはない） ('There is no need to worry.'). Note however that ことはない can also be used to mean that something never happens (lit. 'there are no cases of'), e.g. 彼と会うことはない ('I never meet him.'), while までもない is not used in this sense and will always have the 'no need' meaning.

related.title: 決して〜ない
related.meaning: Never, By no means
related.comparison: Both express negation, but differ in tone and nuance. ことはない suggests that something never happens, or literally that 'there are no cases of (A)', e.g. 忘れることはない ('There's no way I'll forget'), while 決して〜ない adds strong emphasis, meaning 'never' or 'by no means', and could even be used together with ことはない, e.g. 決して忘れることはない ('I will by no means forget').

related.title: わけがない
related.meaning: There is no way, There is no reason, It is impossible, Cannot be
related.comparison: These expressions are similar in that both emphatically express negation, but they differ in nuance. ことはない implies something never happens, or literally 'there are no cases of (A)', e.g. 二度と会うことはない ('I'll never talk to him again / a second time.'). In contrast, わけがない expresses strong denial or impossibility, e.g. 負けるわけがない ('There's no way I can lose.')

related.title: なくてもいい
related.meaning: Don't have to
related.comparison: ことはない and なくてもいい can overlap when expressing 'There is no need to...' or 'You don't have to...' For example, 心配することはない and 心配しなくてもいい both mean 'There's no need to worry.' However, ことはない can also be used to express that something never happens. For example, 彼と話すことはない means 'I never talk to him,' which is not a use for なくてもいい.

related.title: ここ
related.meaning: Here, This place
related.comparison: これ refers to something close to the speaker, either a physical object or figurative idea, similar to 'this' in English. On the other hand, ここ refers to a location or place close to the speaker, like 'here' in English. In other words, the primary difference between the two is that これ refers to things, while ここ refers to locations.

related.title: それ
related.meaning: That
related.comparison: これ and それ are both used to refer to things, but the difference lies in distance. これ means 'this' and refers to something near the speaker. それ means 'that' and refers to something closer to the listener than the speaker. Both can refer to physical objects or more abstract things.

related.title: あれ
related.meaning: That (over there)
related.comparison: これ and あれ are both used to refer to things, but the difference lies in distance. これ means 'this' and refers to something near the speaker. あれ means 'that' and refers to something far from both the speaker and the listener. Both can refer to physical objects or more abstract things.

related.title: この
related.meaning: This
related.comparison: これ and この both refer to things close to the speaker, like the English 'this.' これ is used by itself to mean 'this' or 'this thing.' この must be used with a noun, like in この本 ('this book'). In other words, これ stands alone, while この comes before a noun.

related.title: どれ
related.meaning: Which (of three or more)
related.comparison: これ refers to something close to the speaker, like 'this' in English, and can be a physical object or an idea. どれ is a question word that means 'which one?' and is used when choosing from a set of options. In short, これ identifies something specific, while どれ asks about a choice.

related.title: それ
related.meaning: That
related.comparison: この points to something near the speaker, like 'this' in English, and must be followed by a noun (e.g., この本 = 'this book'). それ, on the other hand, refers to something near the listener or recently mentioned, like 'that.' Unlike この, それ can be used on its own to mean 'that' or 'that thing.'

related.title: そこ
related.meaning: There, That place
related.comparison: この refers to something physically or figuratively close to the speaker, similar to 'this' in English, and must directly precede a noun (e.g., この本 = 'this book'). In contrast, そこ refers specifically to a location closer to the listener than to the speaker, similar to 'there' in English. Unlike この, そこ can stand alone without a noun.

related.title: あの
related.meaning: That (over there)
related.comparison: Both この and あの can be used before nouns to describe their position relative to the speaker, similar to 'this' and 'that' in English. The difference lies in perspective: この refers to something close to the speaker (like 'this'), while あの refers to something far from both the speaker and listener, or something known to both (like 'that').

related.title: その
related.meaning: That
related.comparison: Both この and その are used before nouns to describe their position relative to the speaker, similar to 'this' and 'that' in English. The difference lies in perspective: この refers to something close to the speaker (like 'this'), while その refers to something closer to the listener or something just mentioned (like 'that').

related.title: どの
related.meaning: Which (of three or more), What (kind)
related.comparison: Both どの and この are used before nouns. この refers to something physically or figuratively close to the speaker, similar to 'this' in English (e.g., この色 = 'this color'). In contrast, どの is a question word used to ask 'which' when there are three or more choices (e.g., どの色が好きですか？ = 'Which color do you like?').

related.title: ここ
related.meaning: Here, This place
related.comparison: ここ refers directly to a place close to the speaker and can stand alone, similar to 'here' or 'this place' in English. この, however, must be followed by a noun to specify an object or person near the speaker (e.g., この本 = 'this book'). Thus, ここ and この differ in both usage and what they refer to.

related.title: これ
related.meaning: This
related.comparison: これ and この both refer to things close to the speaker, like the English 'this.' これ is used by itself to mean 'this' or 'this thing.' この must be used with a noun, like in この本 ('this book'). In other words, これ stands alone, while この comes before a noun.

related.title: あれ
related.meaning: That (over there)
related.comparison: この refers to something near the speaker, like 'this' in English, and must be followed by a noun (e.g., この本 = 'this book'). あれ, on the other hand, refers to something far from both the speaker and listener, or something known to both but not nearby, similar to 'that.' Unlike この, あれ can be used on its own to mean 'that' or 'that thing.'

related.title: あそこ
related.meaning: Over there
related.comparison: この refers to something close to the speaker, either physically or figuratively, similar to 'this' in English, and must be followed by a noun (e.g., この本 = 'this book'). In contrast, あそこ refers to a location far from both the speaker and listener, similar to 'over there' in English. Unlike この, あそこ can stand alone without a noun.

related.title: がある
related.meaning: To be, There is
related.comparison: ある and ございます are both verbs of existence. ある is the plain form used in everyday conversation, while ございます is the polite form used in formal settings or speeches. For example, 本がある means 'I have / there is a book' in casual speech, while 本がございます is the same phrase in a more polite and formal context (note that が must be used in both cases).

related.title: に (Frequency)
related.meaning: Times (In each time period), Every, Frequency of
related.comparison: Both expressions show frequency, but in different ways. The に (frequency) construction combines a time period with a number to mean '(A) times per (B),' e.g. 一週間に２回 'twice a week'. ごとに attaches to a number + counter to mean 'every X,' e.g. 二時間ごとに薬を飲む 'take medicine every two hours'.

related.title: たびに
related.meaning: Each time, Every time, Whenever
related.comparison: ごとに and たびに both express frequency, and can be interchangeable in cases like 失敗するたびに／ごとに 'every time (you) fail'. However, ごとに can directly follow time or distance expressions (e.g. ３時間ごとに), while たびに requires a verb indicating an action or event, e.g. ３時間が経つたびに 'every time 3 hours pass'. Their noun forms also differ, e.g. 発表の度に vs. 発表ごとに (both meaning 'with every announcement').

related.title: につけ
related.meaning: Whenever, Every time something happens
related.comparison: ごとに and たびに both express frequency, but differ in usage. In phrases like 失敗するごとに or 失敗するにつけ 'every time (you fail)', the meaning is similar, though the grammatical attachment is different. につけ is also more formal and literary, while ごとに is common attaches directly to time or distance expressions, e.g. ３時間ごとに 'every 3 hours'.

related.title: おきに
related.meaning: Repeated at intervals, Every other, Every, At intervals of
related.comparison: ごとに and おきに both express regular intervals, but with a key difference. ２日ごとに means 'every two days', i.e. the event happens on the second day. ２日おきに means 'at intervals of two days', or in other words every third day. A helpful tip: おきに comes from 置く, which can have the meaning of leaving a gap or space.

related.title: ～ずつ
related.meaning: One at a time, Per, Each, Gradually
related.comparison: Both express regular repetition, but in different structures. ～ずつ attaches to quantity phrases to mean 'each' or 'little by little', e.g. 一つずつください 'Give me one of each.' ごとに follows nouns and means 'every (A)', often time-based, e.g. ３０分ごとに体を動かす 'I move my body every thirty minutes.'

related.title: まい～のように
related.meaning: Almost every ~, Nearly every ~, On a ~ basis
related.comparison: Both phrases express frequency but differ in usage. ごとに attaches to time expressions, nouns, or verbs to mean 'every (A),' e.g. ３時間ごとに 'every three hours' or 失敗するごとに 'every time (you) fail'. まい～のように is used only with time words that take 毎 and adds emphasis, meaning 'almost every (A)', e.g. 毎日のように 'nearly every day'.

synonyms.title: くらい ①
synonyms.comparison: くらい (often ぐらい in speech) expresses an approximate degree or extent. A common use is with number + counter expressions, as in 10分くらいかかった ('It took about 10 minutes'). Unlike くらい, which can be used with durations or points in time (e.g. 10分くらい, 10時くらい), ごろ can only be used with points in time (e.g. 10時ごろ = 'around 10 o'clock').

related.title: とき
related.meaning: When, At the time of
related.comparison: Both ごろ (or ころ) and とき indicate when something happened, but with key differences. ごろ is less specific and means 'around that time', as in 子どもの頃 'in my childhood days'. It also works as a suffix with time words, e.g. 11時ごろ 'around 11 o'clock', while とき cannot be used this way. By contrast, とき has a more precise nuance, as in 子どもの時 'when I was a child'.

related.title: み
related.meaning: ~ness, Used to indicate conditions or nature
related.comparison: さ and み both nominalize adjectives, similar to '~ness' in English, but differ in nuance and usage. さ works with both い and な-adjectives and expresses measurable degree, e.g. 高さ 'height' or 大変さ 'difficulty'. み is typically used with い-adjectives and focuses on quality or characteristics rather than degree, e.g. 面白み 'that which makes (something) interesting'.

related.title: さ - Interjection
related.meaning: All right, There we go, Now, You see, Well (I don't know, let me see)
related.comparison: Both can be used to express emotion in casual conversation. The interjection さ is often used at the beginning of sentences and can express encouragement, e.g. さあ、行こう！ ('All right, let's go!'), or hesitation/uncertainty, e.g. さあ、分かりません ('Let's see... I don't know.'). The other usage appears at the end of phrases and functions like a casual よ, e.g. できるさ！ ('You can do it, I'm telling you!').

related.title: さ - Filler
related.meaning: Like, You know, You see, Listen, Uhh
related.comparison: Both uses of さ are characteristic of casual conversation. The 'casual よ' usage comes at the end of sentences and, like よ, often emphasizes or confirms something to the listener, like 'I'm telling you'. The 'filler' usage of さ appears mid-sentence and ties phrases together, similar to English expressions like 'like' or 'you know', e.g. 昨日さ、ケンジ君に会ったよ ('So yesterday, like, I saw Kenji!').

related.title: では・それでは・じゃあ
related.meaning: If so, Well (then), In that case
related.comparison: Both can appear at the beginning of a sentence to mark a transition. では・それでは・じゃあ signal a shift or next step based on context, e.g. じゃあ、行こうか ('Well then, shall we go?') さ (often extended as さあ) adds an encouraging or prompting tone, e.g. さあ、そろそろ行こうか！ ('Come on, let's get going!')

related.title: さて
related.meaning: Well, Well then
related.comparison: These are two similar-looking interjections with different uses. さて is a transitional phrase often used at the beginning of sentences to change the topic or move the conversation into a new phase, like 'well then' or 'now then'. In contrast, さ is a casual interjection that serves multiple functions, such as encouragement (e.g. 'all right!', 'come on!') or hesitation ('well, you see...').

related.title: さ - Filler
related.meaning: Like, You know, You see, Listen, Uhh
related.comparison: さ is a common expression in casual speech with multiple distinct usages. One is as an interjection for encouragement, e.g. さあ、行こう！ ('All right, let's go!'), or hesitation, e.g. さあ、分かりませんね ('Let's see... I don't know.'). The other is as a filler that ties parts of a sentence together, e.g. 昨日さ、図書館に行ったらさ、ケンジくんがいたよ ('Yesterday, like, I went to the library, right? And Kenji was there!').

related.title: さ - Casual よ
related.meaning: Casual よ
related.comparison: Both can be used to express emotion in casual conversation. The interjection さ is often used at the beginning of sentences and can express encouragement, e.g. さあ、行こう！ ('All right, let's go!'), or hesitation/uncertainty, e.g. さあ、分かりません ('Let's see... I don't know.'). The other usage appears at the end of phrases and functions like a casual よ, e.g. できるさ！ ('You can do it, I'm telling you!').

related.title: さ - Interjection
related.meaning: All right, There we go, Now, You see, Well (I don't know, let me see)
related.comparison: さ is a common expression in casual speech with multiple distinct usages. One is as an interjection for encouragement, e.g. さあ、行こう！ ('All right, let's go!'), or hesitation, e.g. さあ、分かりませんね ('Let's see... I don't know.'). The other is as a filler that ties parts of a sentence together, e.g. 昨日さ、図書館に行ったらさ、ケンジくんがいたよ ('Yesterday, like, I went to the library, right? And Kenji was there!').

related.title: さ - Casual よ
related.meaning: Casual よ
related.comparison: Both uses of さ are characteristic of casual conversation. The 'casual よ' usage comes at the end of sentences and, like よ, often emphasizes or confirms something to the listener, like 'I'm telling you'. The 'filler' usage of さ appears mid-sentence and ties phrases together, similar to English expressions like 'like' or 'you know', e.g. 昨日さ、ケンジ君に会ったよ ('So yesterday, like, I saw Kenji!').

related.title: すら
related.meaning: Even
related.comparison: すら and さえ are both somewhat formal intensifiers that can be used in similar ways. For example, 子どもですらできる and 子どもでさえできる are both emphatic ways to say 'even a child could do it'. Though they can overlap in some usages, one difference is that さえ can be used with ば conditionals in expressions such as これさえすれば 'as long as you do this', while すら is not used in this way.

related.title: まで
related.meaning: Even, To even, To the extent of
related.comparison: Both さえ and まで mean 'even,' but with different nuances. さえ is often used with negatives to express 'not even X,' e.g. 食べることさえできなかった ('I couldn't even eat.'). まで is usually affirmative and highlights something surprising or extreme, e.g. 骨まで食べた ('He even ate the bones.').

related.title: 割に
related.meaning: Although, Despite, Rather...for, Unexpectedly, Comparatively, Considering
related.comparison: Both さえ and 割に convey, in some sense, a characteristic or event contrary to expectations. さえ is a strong 'even' that shows emphatic extremity or surprise, e.g. 子どもさえ分かる ('Even a child understands'). 割に expresses contrast with expectations, e.g. 年の割に元気だ ('He's energetic for his age').

related.title: さえ〜ば
related.meaning: If only…then, As long as..., Provided..., Only thing ... needs for ... is ...
related.comparison: Both expressions use さえ, but their usages and nuances differ. さえ〜ば shows that one condition is sufficient, like 'as long as (A)', e.g. お金さえあれば何とかなる ('As long as I have money, things will work out'). さえ alone emphasizes extremity, surprise, or unexpectedness, e.g. 彼さえ知らない ('Even he doesn't know').

related.title: ても
related.meaning: Even if, Even though
related.comparison: さえ and ても can both express the meaning of 'even' and sometimes overlap, especially with nouns. For example, 子どもでもできる and 子どもさえできる both mean 'Even a child can do it.'. However, they diverge when ても expresses permission, such as in 行ってもいい ('You can go.') or when さえ means 'if only', e.g. お金さえあれば 'as long as you have money'. In these cases, the two expressions are not interchangeable.

related.title: さえ
related.meaning: Even
related.comparison: Both expressions use さえ, but their usages and nuances differ. さえ〜ば shows that one condition is sufficient, like 'as long as (A)', e.g. お金さえあれば何とかなる ('As long as I have money, things will work out'). さえ alone emphasizes extremity, surprise, or unexpectedness, e.g. 彼さえ知らない ('Even he doesn't know').

related.title: からには
related.meaning: As long as, Since, Given
related.comparison: These expressions both connect a condition to a result, but in different ways. さえ〜ば emphasizes that the condition alone is enough, e.g. お金さえあれば大丈夫 ('As long as I have money, I'll be fine'). からには highlights an obligation or natural consequence once a condition is true, e.g. 仕事を引き受けたからには最後までやる ('Since I took on the task, I'll see it through').

related.title: 限り
related.meaning: As long as, While… is the case, Limited to
related.comparison: Both expressions can be translated as 'as long as (A)', but they differ in nuance. さえ〜ば indicates that a single condition is sufficient, e.g. 努力さえすればうまくいく ('As long as you try, things will go well'). 限り means 'as long as' in the sense of duration or ongoing state, e.g. 生きている限り夢を追いたい ('As long as I live, I want to chase my dream').

related.title: はたして
related.meaning: Wonder if ~ (really)?, Wonder how ~ (actually)?, Sure enough, As expected
related.comparison: はたして conveys doubt or skepticism, questioning whether something is really true, as in はたして成功するだろうか (Will it really succeed?). さすが affirms expectations, showing that someone or something lives up to reputation, as in さすが先生だ (That’s just like the teacher).

related.title: では・それでは・じゃあ
related.meaning: If so, Well (then), In that case
related.comparison: Both mark transitions, but with slightly different nuance. さて is used to shift to a new topic or proceed to the next step, e.g. さて、次の話題に入りましょう ('Now then, let's move on to the next topic.') では・それでは・じゃあ reference the previous context and often lead into a decision or conclusion, similar to 'well then' or 'in that case', e.g. それでは、始めましょう ('Well then, let's begin.')

related.title: さ - Interjection
related.meaning: All right, There we go, Now, You see, Well (I don't know, let me see)
related.comparison: These are two similar-looking interjections with different uses. さて is a transitional phrase often used at the beginning of sentences to change the topic or move the conversation into a new phase, like 'well then' or 'now then'. In contrast, さ is a casual interjection that serves multiple functions, such as encouragement (e.g. 'all right!', 'come on!') or hesitation ('well, you see...').

related.title: ざるを得ない
related.meaning: Can't help doing, Have no choice but
related.comparison: 〜ざる is the classical negative form of a verb, functioning like ない when modifying nouns, as in 知られざる名作 (an unknown masterpiece). It also forms the base of ざるを得ない, which conveys inevitability or lack of choice, as in 行かざるを得ない (I have no choice but to go). While 〜ざる alone is literary or archaic, ざるを得ない remains common in formal modern Japanese.

related.title: 上に
related.meaning: As well, Besides, In addition to
related.comparison: 上に and 更に both add information, but their nuance differs. 上に connects additive facts or qualities, often with a static or descriptive tone, as in 彼は真面目な上に礼儀正しい (He is serious, and also polite). 更に emphasizes progression or intensification, as in 更に速く走った (He ran even faster).

related.title: その上
related.meaning: Besides, In addition, Furthermore
related.comparison: その上 and 更に are both relatively formal expressions used at the beginning of a sentence to add information. While often interchangeable, they differ subtly: 更に can imply that the added point is of the same type as what came before, while その上 can highlight that the following point is particularly notable or worthy of emphasis.

related.title: しかも
related.meaning: Moreover, Furthermore
related.comparison: Both add information, but differ in nuance. しかも emphasizes reinforcement or surprising addition, as in この料理はおいしい。しかも安い (This dish is delicious. And what’s more, it’s cheap). 更に is more formal, indicating progression or escalation, as in 明日は更に寒くなるでしょう (Tomorrow it will get even colder).

related.title: また
related.meaning: Also, As well, Moreover, Again, Additionally
related.comparison: また is common in both speech and writing, meaning 'again' or introducing additional information, like 'moreover' or 'additionally'. 更に is more formal and used in writing or structured speech, with the nuance of 'even more' or 'beyond that', often indicating further change or escalation.

related.title: 再び
related.meaning: Again, Once more, A second time
related.comparison: Both 再び and 更に can involve repetition or continuation, but they differ in meaning. 再び means 'again' or 'once more', and is used when something is done a second time after a pause, e.g. '再び会う' ('to meet again'). 更に means 'further' or 'more', and emphasizes intensification or accumulation, e.g. '更に深まる' ('to deepen further').

related.title: ますます
related.meaning: Increasingly, More and more, Decreasingly, Less and less, Keep -ing
related.comparison: 更に and ますます both suggest addition or progression, but with different nuances. 更に emphasizes adding something on top of what already exists, e.g. '更に三人が参加した' ('Three more people joined'). ますます highlights a state intensifying over time, e.g. 'ますます寒くなってきた' ('It's gotten colder and colder').

related.title: てならない
related.meaning: Very, Extremely, Can't help but do
related.comparison: Both てならない and ざるを得ない convey inevitability, but differ in source. てならない expresses uncontrollable emotion or sensation, as in 会いたくてならない (I can’t help wanting to see them), while ざるを得ない indicates being forced to act due to circumstance or necessity, as in 行かざるを得ない (I have no choice but to go).

related.title: ないわけにはいかない
related.slug: ないわけにはいかない
related.meaning: Can't not, No way to avoid
related.comparison: ざるを得ない and ないわけにはいかない both express unavoidable action, but differ in tone and motivation. ざるを得ない emphasizes external compulsion or lack of options, as in 行かざるを得ない (I have no choice but to go), while ないわけにはいかない conveys a sense of moral or social obligation, as in 手伝わないわけにはいかない (I can’t not help).

related.title: ～ざる
related.meaning: To not ~, Un~
related.comparison: 〜ざる is the classical negative form of a verb, functioning like ない when modifying nouns, as in 知られざる名作 (an unknown masterpiece). It also forms the base of ざるを得ない, which conveys inevitability or lack of choice, as in 行かざるを得ない (I have no choice but to go). While 〜ざる alone is literary or archaic, ざるを得ない remains common in formal modern Japanese.

related.title: てたまらない
related.meaning: Can't help but want to, Very, Extremely
related.comparison: ざるを得ない expresses unavoidable action due to external circumstances, as in 行かざるを得ない (I have no choice but to go). By contrast, てたまらない conveys an intense emotional feeling that is hard to endure, as in 会いたくてたまらない (I can’t help wanting to see you). Both imply compulsion, but one is situational, the other emotional.

related.title: てしょうがない
related.meaning: Extremely, Very, Really, It can't be helped
related.comparison: ざるを得ない and てしょうがない both express inevitability, but differ in cause and nuance. ざるを得ない conveys compulsion due to external pressure or lack of alternatives, as in 行かざるを得ない (I have no choice but to go), while てしょうがない expresses an overpowering personal emotion or state, as in 寂しくてしょうがない (I can’t help feeling lonely).

related.title: ずにはいられない
related.meaning: Cannot help doing something, Cannot resist doing something
related.comparison: Both express uncontrollable actions or feelings, but differ in tone. ずにはいられない often has a more subjective or emotional feel, e.g. '泣かずにはいられなかった' ('I just couldn't help but cry'). ざるを得ない has a more objective tone and is used to express external necessity or obligation, e.g. '行かざるを得ない' ('I have no choice but to go').

related.title: を余儀なくさせる
related.meaning: Force to, To compel, Make unavoidable
related.comparison: Both express an obligation, but from different perspectives. を余儀なくさせる implies forcing someone into a difficult situation, e.g. 退去を余儀なくさせる 'it (e.g. the situation) forced them to leave' vs. 退去せざるを得ない '(we/I) have no choice but to leave'.

related.title: しかない
related.meaning: Have no choice but, Only, Just
related.comparison: しかない and ざるを得ない both express having no choice, but しかない is more neutral and conversational, e.g. やるしかない ('I have no choice but to do it'). ざるを得ない has a similar meaning but is more literary, and is found mostly in writing or formal speech, as in 行かざるを得なかった ('I was forced to go').

related.title: ことだし
related.meaning: (And) so, Because, Since
related.comparison: Both give reasons, but the structure and emphasis differ. ことだし presents a reason as a settled or obvious circumstance that naturally justifies a decision, often sounding calm and explanatory, as in 雨も降っていることだし、今日は家にいよう ('Since it’s raining anyway, let’s stay home today.'). By contrast, し～し lists multiple reasons side by side, emphasizing accumulation rather than inevitability, as in 雨だし寒いし、出かけたくない ('It’s raining and cold, so I don’t want to go out.').

related.title: じゃあるまいし
related.meaning: You are not ~ so..., it isn't ~ so...
related.comparison: じゃあるまいし is a combination of the まい form that is equivalent to ないだろう and し used to list reasons. Technically, it could be rephrased as じゃないだろうし, or even simply じゃないし for a more direct way of expressing the sentiment of 'You're/It's not (A), so...', e.g. 子どもじゃあるまいし ('You're not a child, so...').

related.title: Adjective + て + B
related.meaning: And..., Both and (Conjunctive)
related.comparison: し～し lists multiple reasons, similar to 'because of A, B, and so on' in English. In contrast, Adjective + て + B just connects adjectives. While it can imply the first quality supports the second (e.g. 甘くておいしい = 'sweet and delicious'), it doesn't suggest a broader conclusion. し often implies one, as in 甘いし、おいしいし, meaning 'It's sweet and delicious… (so I like it).'

related.title: から
related.meaning: Because, Since
related.comparison: Both し～し and から express reasons, similar to 'so' or 'because' in English. The reason (A) for outcome (B) is always followed by し or から, e.g. (A) し/から (B). The key difference is that から focuses on a single reason, while し～し lists multiple reasons. Even with one reason, し implies other unstated or implied reasons.

related.title: とか～とか
related.meaning: Among other things, For example, Such as
related.comparison: し～し and とか～とか both list things, but with a different nuance. し～し lists reasons or causes, like 'because of things like (A) and (B)'. とか～とか simply lists items without implying they are a cause for something else, i.e. 'things like (A), (B), and so forth'. Also, とか～とか is very casual, while し～し will be found in both speech and writing.

related.title: Verb[て] + B
related.meaning: And・and so, Due to, Because of, Since, Conjunctive
related.comparison: Both forms can express reasons, but their usage differs. Verb[て] + B shows that the stated action is the direct reason for what follows, while し～し implies that multiple reasons exist, even if only one is mentioned. For example, 犯人が捕まって安心です ('I'm relieved because the criminal was caught.') vs. 犯人が捕まったし、安心です ('The criminal was caught, and I'm relieved.'), where the し version suggests there may be other factors for the relief.

related.title: ので
related.meaning: Because, So, Since, The reason being
related.comparison: Both し～し and ので express reasons, similar to 'so' or 'because' in English, with the reason coming before し or ので. The main difference is that ので focuses on a single, objective reason, while し～し lists multiple reasons and often has a more personal or subjective feel. Even with one reason, し implies that there are other unstated or implied reasons.

related.title: 上に
related.meaning: As well, Besides, In addition to
related.comparison: 上に (literally, 'on top of') can be used as a slightly formal way to express 'in addition to (A), (B)', e.g. 安い上に、美味しい ('In addition to being inexpensive, it's tasty.'). While し～し is similar in that it lists multiple things, it specifically does so in the context of giving reasons for something, e.g. 安いし、美味しいし、一番好きなお店です ('It's inexpensive, it's tasty too, and so it's my favorite shop.').

related.title: よりしかたがない
related.meaning: There is no choice but, Can't be helped, Inevitable
related.comparison: While しかない and よりしかたがない both express having no choice, しかない is more neutral and conversational, while よりしかたがない is more literary and formal, often adding a nuance of resignation. Compare: そうするしかない ('We have no choice but to do that') and 待つよりしかたがない ('There's nothing to be done but wait').

related.title: よりほかない
related.meaning: To have no choice but, No other way than, Have to
related.comparison: しかない and よりほかない are similar in that both mean 'there's no choice but to (A)', but よりほかない is considerably more formal than the colloquial しかない, and can carry a nuance that other options exist but aren't feasible due to external pressure or circumstance.

related.title: ざるを得ない
related.meaning: Can't help doing, Have no choice but
related.comparison: しかない and ざるを得ない both express having no choice, but しかない is more neutral and conversational, e.g. やるしかない ('I have no choice but to do it'). ざるを得ない has a similar meaning but is more literary, and is found mostly in writing or formal speech, as in 行かざるを得なかった ('I was forced to go').

related.title: しか～ない
related.meaning: There's only, Nothing but
related.comparison: When used with a noun and a negative verb, しか～ない expresses the idea of 'only' (literally, 'nothing but'), e.g. １００円しかない ('I only have / have nothing but 100 yen.') and バナナしか食べなかった ('I only ate / ate nothing but a banana.'). When used at the end of a sentence following a verb in the affirmative form, しかない has the related meaning of 'there's no choice but to do (A)', e.g. 行くしかない ('I have no choice but to go.').

related.title: もっとも
related.meaning: Although, But, However, For all that, Provided that
related.comparison: Both もっとも and しかしながら are formal expressions that can begin a sentence to convey contrast, but differ in nuance. しかしながら is a literary and emphatic variant of しかし, e.g. 'しかしながら、例外があります' ('Nevertheless, there are exceptions'). もっとも adds a qualification to the previous statement, e.g. '全員来ました。もっとも、田中さんは遅れましたが' ('Everyone came. However, Tanaka was late').

related.title: それなのに
related.meaning: And yet, Despite this
related.comparison: Both introduce contrast. しかしながら is formal and neutral, used in writing or speeches, as in 実験は失敗した。しかしながら多くを学んだ (The experiment failed. Nevertheless, we learned a lot). それなのに is conversational and conveys surprise or disappointment, as in 約束した。それなのに来なかった (He promised, and yet he didn’t come).

related.title: それにしても
related.meaning: Nevertheless, Even so
related.comparison: Both can mean nevertheless. しかしながら is formal and used only for explicit contrasts, as in 雨だった。しかしながら試合は行われた (It rained. Nevertheless, the match was held). それにしても is common in everyday speech and can also serve to shift topics.

related.title: それでも
related.meaning: But still, And yet, Even so, Nevertheless
related.comparison: しかしながら and それでも both mean 'however' or 'nevertheless'. but differ in formality. しかしながら is very formal, often seen in academic or business writing. それでも is used in conversation and everyday writing. Both acknowledge one fact, then present a contrasting one.

synonyms.title: だけ
synonyms.comparison: だけ conveys 'only' or 'just.' しか～ない combines the particle しか with a negative verb to express 'nothing but.' While the two have similar meanings, their structures differ. For example, the equivalent of バナナだけ食べた ('I ate only a banana') would be バナナしか食べなかった ('I ate nothing but a banana'). Compared to だけ, しか～ない emphasizes the limitation or lack of other options more strongly.

related.title: Number + しか〜ない
related.meaning: No more than, Not exceeding, As few as・as little as, Only
related.comparison: しか～ない expresses the meaning of 'only' or 'nothing but'. For example, バナナしか食べなかった ('I only ate a banana', or literally 'I didn't eat anything but a banana.'). When used with numbers in the Number + しか〜ない pattern, it emphasizes that the quantity is small, e.g., ５人しか来なかった ('Only 5 people came.', or literally 'No one came but five people.').

related.title: にほかならない
related.meaning: Nothing but, None other than, Simply
related.comparison: しか～ない and にほかならない are similar in that both can be translated as 'nothing but' or 'only', but with a different nuance. しか～ない means 'nothing but' in the sense of 'nothing else exists', e.g. １００円しかない ('I only have 100 yen.'). In contrast, にほかならない means 'nothing but' in the sense of 'simply' or 'definitely', e.g. 彼は犯罪者に他ならない ('He's simply / nothing but a criminal.').

related.title: だらけ
related.meaning: Completely covered in, Full of
related.comparison: だらけ and しか～ない are used differently and express contrasting meanings. だらけ follows nouns and expresses the idea of 'covered with' or 'full of', e.g. 泥だらけ 'covered in mud'. In contrast, しか～ない is used with a noun and negative verb to mean 'only' or 'nothing but', e.g. １００円しかない ('I have only 100 yen.'). They are similar in that both can often carry a negative or undesirable nuance.

related.title: だけしか
related.meaning: To emphasize '~only', Nothing but
related.comparison: だけしか is essentially an emphatic version of しか～ない, combining だけ 'only' and しか～ない 'nothing but' to strongly emphasize that there is nothing else. For example, １００円だけしかない ('I only have / have nothing but 100 yen.) is a more emphatic version of 100円しかない (same meaning), and 日本語だけしか話せない ('I can only speak / can speak nothing but Japanese.') is a more emphatic version of 日本語しか話せない (same meaning).

related.title: を余儀なくされる
related.meaning: To be forced to do something
related.comparison: を余儀なくされる and しかない are similar in that both express having no choice but to do something, though their usage differs. を余儀なくされる follows nouns and has a nuance of being forced by circumstances, e.g. 中止を余儀なくされる 'be forced to cancel.' In contrast, しかない follows verbs and focuses on lack of choice rather than external pressure, e.g. 行くしかない ('There's no choice but to go.').

related.title: をおいてほかに〜ない
related.meaning: No...but, Nothing else but, None other than
related.comparison: Both convey the idea of 'nothing but,' but with different nuances. をおいてほかに～ない almost always has a positive nuance, suggesting that the thing in question is the only one that possesses some good quality, e.g. 日本語を学ぶなら文プロをおいてほかにない ('If you want to learn Japanese, there's nothing but Bunpro.'). In contrast, しか～ない emphasizes lack and is often used with a negative nuance, e.g. １００円しかない ('I only have 100 yen.').

related.title: ばかり
related.meaning: Just, Only, Nothing but
related.comparison: ばかり and しか～ない can both mean 'only' or 'nothing but', but their usage and nuance differ. ばかり is used with affirmative verb forms and can imply excess. For example, 漫画ばかり読む 'to read only manga' has a sense of reading manga all the time. In contrast, しか～ない is used with negative verbs and expresses 'nothing but' in a more objective sense. For example, 漫画しか読まない simply states that nothing else is read.

related.title: きり
related.meaning: Only, Just, Since
related.comparison: きり and しか～ない can both express the idea of 'only,' but in different ways. きり is used primarily with number+counter expressions, like 一度きり 'only one time' or 二人きり 'only/just the two of us'. In contrast, しか～ない is used with a noun and negative verb to mean 'only' or 'nothing but', e.g. １００円しかない ('I have only 100 yen.'). In some cases, the two can be used together, e.g. 一度きりしか行っていない ('I only went just the once.').

related.title: でしかない
related.meaning: Merely, Nothing but, No more than
related.comparison: しか～ない uses the particle しか and a verb in the negative (ない) form to convey the idea of 'only' (literally, 'nothing but'), e.g. １００円しかない ('I only have / have nothing but 100 yen.') and バナナしか食べなかった ('I only ate / ate nothing but a banana.'). でしかない is a version of this using a form of だ・です 'is', and means 'it is only' or 'it's nothing but', e.g. ゴミでしかない ('It's nothing but garbage.').

related.title: しかない
related.meaning: Have no choice but, Only, Just
related.comparison: When used with a noun and a negative verb, しか～ない expresses the idea of 'only' (literally, 'nothing but'), e.g. １００円しかない ('I only have / have nothing but 100 yen.') and バナナしか食べなかった ('I only ate / ate nothing but a banana.'). When used at the end of a sentence following a verb in the affirmative form, しかない has the related meaning of 'there's no choice but to do (A)', e.g. 行くしかない ('I have no choice but to go.').

related.title: それに
related.meaning: And, Besides, Moreover, In addition
related.comparison: それに and しかも both add information, but しかも is stronger. それに is neutral: it adds a point that logically follows. しかも emphasizes what comes next, like 'what's more' or 'on top of that', e.g. 安い。それに美味しい。しかも体にいい ('It's cheap. And tasty. And what's more, it's good for your body.').

related.title: その上
related.meaning: Besides, In addition, Furthermore
related.comparison: その上 and しかも can both appear at the beginning of a sentence to introduce additional information. They are often interchangeable, but しかも is somewhat more colloquial and can imply that the added information is unexpected. その上 is more neutral and formal, with no such implication.

related.title: おまけに
related.meaning: In addition, On top of that, What is more, To make matters worse
related.comparison: Both add extra information. しかも is neutral, adding notable facts, as in 彼は優秀だ。しかも親切だ (He’s talented, and moreover, kind). おまけに can add positive or negative on top of that nuance, as in 雨だった。おまけに風も強かった (It was raining, and on top of that, the wind was strong).

related.title: 更に
related.meaning: Even more or further, Again, More and more
related.comparison: Both add information, but differ in nuance. しかも emphasizes reinforcement or surprising addition, as in この料理はおいしい。しかも安い (This dish is delicious. And what’s more, it’s cheap). 更に is more formal, indicating progression or escalation, as in 明日は更に寒くなるでしょう (Tomorrow it will get even colder).

related.title: 又〜も
related.meaning: Moreover, Additionally, And also, In addition, Furthermore, Or
related.comparison: Both express addition like the English 'moreover', but differ in usage. しかも starts a sentence to add information that emphasizes the previous point, e.g. '彼は優秀だ。しかも努力家だ' ('He's talented. Moreover, he's hardworking'). 又〜も, because of も, must be used with another noun that also applies and adds to the previous statement, e.g. '姉はピアノが弾ける。また、歌も歌える' ('My older sister can play piano. She can also sing songs').

related.title: なお②
related.meaning: In addition, Incidentally, Furthermore, Moreover
related.comparison: しかも and なお② both add additional information, but differ in nuance. しかも often strengthens the previous statement with surprising or emphatic detail, as in 彼は優秀だ。しかも努力家だ (He is talented, and what’s more, hardworking). なお② is more neutral and formal, used to supply further facts without emphasis, as in 今回の件について、なお調査中です (Regarding this matter, we are still investigating further).

related.title: ゆえに
related.meaning: Because, The reason is, Due to, Therefore, So
related.comparison: Both express logical consequence, but the tone and register differ. ゆえに is a formal, written connector meaning ‘therefore’ that presents a reason–result relation in a stiff, explanatory style, as in 天候が悪化した。ゆえに計画を変更した (‘The weather worsened; therefore, we changed the plan.’). したがって also means ‘therefore’ but is more neutral and common in explanations or academic writing, focusing on logical progression rather than formality, as in 条件が満たされない。したがって実行できない (‘The conditions are not met; therefore, it cannot be carried out.’).

related.title: から
related.meaning: Because, Since
related.comparison: Both したがって and から express a cause or reason. The difference is that から is common in casual, everyday speech, while したがって is more formal, used in writing or structured speech. Additionally, から appears in the middle of a sentence, while したがって comes at the start. To use から at the start, you would use ですから or だから.

related.title: まま(に)
related.meaning: As (one likes, feels or pleases), Spur of the moment, Wherever one fancies, Spontaneously, On a whim
related.comparison: まま(に) describes being left in a certain state, often implying lack of freedom or control, as in 言われるままに従った (I obeyed just as I was told). したがって is a formal connector meaning therefore or thus, used to show logical consequence, as in 天気は悪い。したがって中止する (The weather is bad. Therefore, it will be canceled).

related.title: その結果
related.meaning: As a result
related.comparison: その結果 and したがって are similar in that both express a result or consequence with a relatively formal tone. その結果 emphasizes the resulting situation or condition, similar to the English 'as a result of that', while したがって implies a logical conclusion, which may or may not necessarily be a 'resulting' situation.

related.title: そこで
related.meaning: Accordingly, Now, Therefore, To that end
related.comparison: そこで and したがって are both used at the beginning of a sentence to express one action following another, but differ in nuance and tone. そこで is a general-purpose expression usable in various situations and contexts, like the English 'so' or 'as such'. In contrast, したがって is more formal and has a stricter 'following from that' or 'thus' nuance.

synonyms.title: る-Verb (Negative)
synonyms.comparison: The negative form of a る-verb indicates 'does not' or 'will not' do something (e.g., 食べない = 'I don't eat' or 'I won't eat'). In contrast, じゃない (a casual form of ではない, the negative of だ) negates nouns and な-adjectives (e.g., 元気じゃない = 'not feeling good'). While both express negation, they apply to different types of words.

antonyms.title: だ
antonyms.comparison: だ follows nouns and な-adjectives, with a meaning similar to the English 'is.' For instance, これはペンだ translates to 'This is a pen.' On the other hand, じゃない (a contraction of ではない) is used to create the negative of such expressions. For example, これはペンじゃない translates to 'This isn't a pen.'

antonyms.title: です
antonyms.comparison: です works like 'is' or 'to be' in English and is used to politely state something in everyday conversation. じゃない is the casual negative form meaning 'is not,' and it's often used in relaxed speech or in phrases like ～じゃないと思います ('I don't think it is...').

related.title: じゃないか
related.meaning: Isn't it?, Is it not?
related.comparison: じゃない follows a noun or な-Adjective to express 'is not' or 'are not.' じゃないか, with the addition of か, changes the meaning to 'Isn't it...?' as in 本じゃない ('It isn't a book') vs. 本じゃないか ('It's a book, isn't it?') While じゃない is used only after nouns or な-Adjectives, じゃないか can follow い-Adjectives and verbs as well.

related.title: う-Verb (Negative)
related.meaning: Will/Does/Do (not)
related.comparison: The negative form of an う-verb indicates 'does not' or 'will not' perform an action (e.g., 買わない = 'I don't buy' or 'I won't buy'). On the other hand, じゃない (casual form of ではない, the negative of だ) negates nouns and な-adjectives (e.g., 元気じゃない = 'not feeling good'). Both express negation, but each applies to different word types.

related.title: い-Adjectives くない
related.meaning: Not (Adjective)
related.comparison: い-Adjectives form a negative by replacing い with くない (e.g., 赤い = 'it's red,' 赤くない = 'it's not red'). For nouns and な-Adjectives, the negative is formed by adding じゃない (e.g., 大変じゃない = 'it's not difficult,' バナナじゃない = 'it's not a banana'). Both forms are plain and used in casual conversation or other grammatical patterns.

related.title: じゃなかった
related.meaning: Was not, Wasn't
related.comparison: じゃない and じゃなかった both follow nouns or な-adjectives to express the negative, but differ in tense. じゃない is present tense ('is not'), while じゃなかった is past tense ('was not'). For example, 静かじゃない means 'It isn't quiet,' while 静かじゃなかった means 'It wasn't quiet.'

related.title: し～し
related.meaning: And, Giving reasons
related.comparison: じゃあるまいし is a combination of the まい form that is equivalent to ないだろう and し used to list reasons. Technically, it could be rephrased as じゃないだろうし, or even simply じゃないし for a more direct way of expressing the sentiment of 'You're/It's not (A), so...', e.g. 子どもじゃあるまいし ('You're not a child, so...').

related.title: まい
related.meaning: Won't, Intend not to, Probably not
related.comparison: まい was previously introduced with verbs as a condensed (and somewhat more formal) way of saying ないだろう. This is that same まい used with the negative form じゃない following nouns, and combined with し to list reasons, resulting in a phrase meaning 'You're/It's not (A), so...' , e.g. 子どもじゃあるまいし  ('You're not a child, so...').

related.title: かもしれない
related.meaning: Might, Maybe, Probably
related.comparison: かもしれない and じゃないか can both appear at the end of a sentence, but differ in nuance. かもしれない expresses uncertainty, e.g. おいしいかもしれない ('It might be tasty.'). じゃないか is more emphatic and seeks agreement, e.g. おいしいじゃないか ('It's tasty, isn't it?' or 'I'm telling you it's tasty, don't you agree?').

related.title: でしょう
related.meaning: Right?, Probably, Isn't it?
related.comparison: でしょう, in addition to expressing conjecture, can also seek confirmation or agreement from the listener (e.g., 面白いでしょう = 'Interesting, right?'). じゃないか is more forceful and casual, implying the speaker strongly believes something and expects the listener to agree (e.g., 面白いじゃないか = 'Interesting, isn't it!'). Note that でしょう is polite, while じゃないか is very casual.

related.title: のではないだろうか
related.meaning: I think ... isn't it?, It might be that..., Don't you agree?
related.comparison: Both じゃないか and のではないだろうか are negative questions similar to the English '...isn't it?' but differ in nuance. じゃないか is used to confirm something the speaker believes to be true, often with a confrontational or challenging tone, e.g. 'It's (A), isn't it!?' In contrast, のではないだろうか is more tentative, expressing uncertainty and seeking confirmation, e.g. 'Do you suppose it's (A)?'.

related.title: じゃない
related.meaning: Is not, Isn't
related.comparison: じゃない follows a noun or な-Adjective to express 'is not' or 'are not.' じゃないか, with the addition of か, changes the meaning to 'Isn't it...?' as in 本じゃない ('It isn't a book') vs. 本じゃないか ('It's a book, isn't it?') While じゃない is used only after nouns or な-Adjectives, じゃないか can follow い-Adjectives and verbs as well.

related.title: ～のだろうか
related.meaning: I wonder...
related.comparison: Both ～のだろうか and じゃないか seek confirmation, but with different levels of confidence. のだろうか expresses uncertainty, used for low-confidence guesses or suppositions, e.g. 'I wonder if (A)?'. In contrast, じゃないか is used to confirm something the speaker believes to be true, often with a confrontational or challenging tone, e.g. 'It's (A), isn't it!?'.

related.title: ね
related.meaning: Isn't it?, Right? (Sentence ending particle)
related.comparison: じゃないか and ね both seek agreement at the end of a sentence, but they have different nuances and levels of politeness. じゃないか is casual and assertive, often strongly insisting on an opinion, and can feel confrontational, making it best suited for use among close friends. In contrast, ね is softer and used widely to seek gentle agreement or confirmation in various contexts.

related.title: だろう
related.meaning: Right?, Probably, Isn't it?
related.comparison: だろう, in addition to expressing conjecture ('It's probably…'), can seek confirmation or agreement from the listener (e.g., 面白いだろう = 'It's interesting, right?'). じゃないか is more forceful and assertive, suggesting the speaker strongly believes something and expects agreement (e.g., 面白いじゃないか = 'It's interesting, isn't it!'). Both are casual forms and should be avoided in polite conversation.

related.title: じゃない
related.meaning: Is not, Isn't
related.comparison: じゃない and じゃなかった both follow nouns or な-adjectives to express the negative, but differ in tense. じゃない is present tense ('is not'), while じゃなかった is past tense ('was not'). For example, 静かじゃない means 'It isn't quiet,' while 静かじゃなかった means 'It wasn't quiet.'

related.title: あまり
related.meaning: So much...that
related.comparison: Both すぎる and あまり express that something is excessive, but they differ in usage and nuance. すぎる attaches to the stem of verbs and adjectives, while あまり follows plain-form verbs, nouns with の, or な-adjectives with な. Though すぎる can imply something negative, あまり more clearly emphasizes that the excess leads to a negative consequence.

related.title: あまりに
related.meaning: So much...that, (Way) too...
related.comparison: Both すぎる and あまりに express that something is excessive or 'too much.' すぎる attaches to the stem of verbs or adjectives (e.g. 早すぎる), while あまりに comes before them (e.g. あまりに早い). They can also be combined for emphasis (e.g. あまりに早すぎる). Compared to すぎる, あまりに often carries a nuance of surprise or emotional reaction.

antonyms.title: きらい
antonyms.comparison: 好き and 嫌い are な-Adjectives used to express 'like' and 'dislike.' The object of like or dislike is typically marked with が, though を can appear in some cases. Adding 大 (e.g., 大好き, 大嫌い) intensifies the sentiment, transforming 'like' and 'dislike' into something more akin to 'love' and 'hate.'

antonyms.title: すこしも～ない
antonyms.meaning: Not even a little, Not at all, Not in the least, Never
antonyms.comparison: These expressions look similar but have opposite meanings. すくなくない is a double negative meaning 'not a few', which implies 'many' or 'a lot'. For example, そういう人はすくなくない ('There are many people like that.', or literally 'not just a few'.). In contrast, すこしも～ない emphasizes absence. For example, すこしも面白くない ('It's not interesting at all.'), which implies 'not even a little bit'.

synonyms.title: もう
synonyms.comparison: Both もう and すでに express the idea of 'already.' The difference is their level of formality and tone. While もう is commonly used in everyday conversation and can have a personal or emotional nuance, すでに is more objective and found primarily in writing or formal speech. もう can also be used alone as an expression of exasperation or frustration, while すでに cannot.

antonyms.title: すくなくない
antonyms.meaning: Quite a few, Quite a lot, Many, Not a few, Not a little
antonyms.comparison: These expressions look similar but have opposite meanings. すくなくない is a double negative meaning 'not a few', which implies 'many' or 'a lot'. For example, そういう人はすくなくない ('There are many people like that.', or literally 'not just a few'.). In contrast, すこしも～ない emphasizes absence. For example, すこしも面白くない ('It's not interesting at all.'), which implies 'not even a little bit'.

related.title: ぜんぜん
related.meaning: (Not) at all
related.comparison: ぜんぜん and すこしも～ない both emphasize negation and mean 'not at all.' A key difference is that ぜんぜん, especially in casual speech, can also appear in affirmative phrases, e.g. ぜんぜん大丈夫 ('It's totally fine.'). In contrast, すこしも～ない is more formal and strictly used in negative contexts, e.g. すこしも寒くない ('It's not even a little cold.').

related.title: あまり～ない
related.meaning: Not very, Not much, Not really, Hardly
related.comparison: Both あまり～ない and すこしも～ない are used in negative statements, but differ in degree and tone. あまり～ない means 'not very (A)' and is fairly colloquial, e.g. あまり寒くない ('It's not very cold.'). すこしも～ない is more formal and emphatic, e.g. 少しも寒くない ('It's not the least bit cold.').

related.title: ことはない
related.meaning: There is no need to, Never happens
related.comparison: ことはない and すこしも～ない are both emphatic negatives, but with different nuances. ことはない means 'There's no need to (A).' or 'There are no instances of (A).'. For example, 心配することはない ('There's no need to worry.'), and 彼と話すことはない ('There are no occasions when I talk to him.'). In contrast, すこしも～ない emphasizes absence of degree or amount, e.g., すこしも心配していない ('I'm not even a little bit worried.').

related.title: とても～ない
related.meaning: Not ~ at all, Cannot ~ at all
related.comparison: とても～ない is an emphatic negative used to show that something is far beyond one's ability. For example, とてもできない ('I can't do that at all.'). In contrast, すこしも～ない means 'not even a little bit (A)' and isn't limited to ability. For example, すこしも面白くない ('It's not interesting at all.'). Unlike すこしも, とても can be used without ない if the meaning is still negative, e.g. とても無理だ ('It's completely impossible.').

related.title: 全く～ない
related.meaning: Not~at all
related.comparison: 全く～ない and すこしも～ない are emphatic negatives that can both mean 'not at all' or 'not even a little'. For example, 全く飲まない and 少しも飲まない both mean 'I don't drink at all.' or 'I don't drink even a little.'. While often interchangeable, すこしも may sound unnatural when there's no nuance of amount or degree. For example, 全く行ったことがない ('I haven't gone at all.'), which wouldn't fit with すこしも.

related.title: なかなか～ない
related.meaning: Not really, Hardly, Not easily or readily, By no means, Far from
related.comparison: なかなか～ない expresses that something is not easily done, often implying difficulty or repeated effort. For example, なかなかうまく話せない ('I just can't speak well.') has the nuance that the speaker is trying but not succeeding. In contrast, すこしも～ない means 'not even a little.' 少しも話せない means 'I can't speak at all.', focusing on the absence of ability without any implication of effort or struggle.

related.title: 決して〜ない
related.meaning: Never, By no means
related.comparison: 決して〜ない and すこしも～ない are both emphatic negatives, but differ in nuance and formality. 決して〜ない is more formal and mainly used in writing, with the sense of 'by no means' or 'never'. For example, 決して危険ではない ('It is by no means dangerous.'). すこしも～ない is less formal and emphasizes absence of degree or amount, e.g., 少しも危険ではない ('It's not even a little bit dangerous.').

related.title: いたす
related.meaning: To do (Humble), To venerably do
related.comparison: Both いたす and する express performing an action, similar to the English 'do.' The key difference is politeness: する is the standard, neutral term used in everyday contexts, while いたす is a humble expression used to show respect by 'lowering' your actions (or those of your in-group) in relation to another person.

related.title: なさる
related.meaning: To do (Honorific)
related.comparison: Both なさる and する express performing an action, similar to the English 'do.' The key difference is politeness: する is the standard, neutral term used in everyday contexts, while なさる is an honorific expression used to 'elevate' someone else's actions with respect to you (or your in-group).

related.title: そうすると
related.meaning: Having done that..., If that is done..., (And/just) then, Thereupon, If so
related.comparison: すると is often considered an abbreviated form of そうすると, and both are typically used at the beginning of a sentence to express something that occurred or logically followed from what came before, similar to 'upon (A), (B)' in English. Both can also be used to summarize or draw a conclusion from the previous statement, like saying 'so that means (A).'

related.title: そのため(に)
related.meaning: For that reason, Because of that, To that end
related.comparison: The two Japanese phrases, すると and そのため(に), are used in different contexts. すると is used to indicate a natural or logical outcome or consequence of a certain action, it is often translated as 'and then', 'if', or 'when'. On the other hand, そのため(に) is commonly used to express the cause or reason for a certain outcome, it is often translated as 'for that reason', 'because of that' or 'hence'.

related.title: と
related.meaning: If, When, Whenever, And, [Conditional]
related.comparison: と marks a natural result, while すると is a transition word meaning 'thereupon' or 'and then'. It's often used at the beginning of a new sentence to show what happened next. For example, ドアを開けた。すると、猫がいた ('I opened the door. And there was a cat.'

related.title: そこで
related.meaning: Accordingly, Now, Therefore, To that end
related.comparison: Both describe what follows from a previous situation, but with different nuance. そこで introduces an intentional response to a situation, e.g. 雨が降ってきた。そこで傘をさした ('It started raining, so I opened my umbrella.') すると shows a natural or immediate result, e.g. 雨が降ってきた。すると子供が泣き出した ('It started raining. Then the child started crying.')

related.title: それに
related.meaning: And, Besides, Moreover, In addition
related.comparison: すると and それに are both sentence starters but differ in usage. すると shows result or reaction, e.g. 雨が降った。すると傘を差した ('It started raining. Then he opened an umbrella.'). それに is additive and neutral: 雨が降っていた。それに寒かった ('It was raining. And cold too.').

related.title: さえ
related.meaning: Even
related.comparison: すら and さえ are both somewhat formal intensifiers that can be used in similar ways. For example, 子どもですらできる and 子どもでさえできる are both emphatic ways to say 'even a child could do it'. Though they can overlap in some usages, one difference is that さえ can be used with ば conditionals in expressions such as これさえすれば 'as long as you do this', while すら is not used in this way.

related.title: も
related.meaning: Also, Too, As well, Even, Either, Neither
related.comparison: すら and も are fundamentally similar in that both can serve to add emphasis. In cases like 子どもですらできる 'even a child can do it' 子どもでもできる (same meaning) they are essentially interchangeable except that すら is more formal and more emphatic. In examples where も is simply serving to add additional information, e.g. 田中さんも来た ('Mr. Tanaka also came.'), も could not be replaced with すら (unless there is some reason to believe that Tanaka coming would be an extremely rare or unexpected happening).

related.title: だらけ
related.meaning: Completely covered in, Full of
related.comparison: Both indicate that something is filled with many similar elements, but the evaluation differs. だらけ means ‘full of’ with a negative or undesirable nuance, used when the items are unwanted or messy, as in ゴミだらけの部屋だ ('The room is full of trash.'). ずくめ means ‘nothing but’ with a positive or favorable nuance, used when the elements are all good or fortunate, as in いいことずくめの旅行だった ('It was a trip full of good things.').

related.title: まま(に)
related.meaning: As is, While remaining, Left in a state
related.comparison: Both relate to a state continuing without change. まま describes a condition or state that remains as it is, neutral in tone, and when used as ないまま it means an action did not happen and that non-action continued, as in 行かないままだった ('I remained in a state of not going.'). ずじまい is especially close to ないまま but emphasizes the outcome that, after time or circumstances, the action never happened at all, often with regret or resignation, as in 結局行かずじまいだった ('In the end, I never went.').

related.title: ずっと ②
related.meaning: By far, Way, Far...er, Far...more
related.comparison: ずっと is an adverb with two main uses that are related but differ in nuance. ずっと① emphasizes a continuous action or state over time, for example, ずっと好きだった ('I always liked you/her/etc.'). In contrast, ずっと② is used in comparisons to show degree, for example, バスケより野球のほうがずっと好き ('I like baseball much more than basketball.').

related.title: ないで
related.meaning: Without doing, Not ~ and ~
related.comparison: ないで and ずに both express the idea of 'without doing (A)' and are used in similar ways. For example, 朝ご飯を食べないで学校に行った and 朝ご飯を食べずに学校に行った both mean 'I went to school without eating breakfast.', but ずに has a slightly more formal or literary tone, while ないで is more conversational. Unlike ないで, ずに cannot be used as a negative request form to say 'Don't (A).'.

related.title: ずにはいられない
related.meaning: Cannot help doing something, Cannot resist doing something
related.comparison: ずに has the core meaning of 'without doing', while ずにはいられない is an idiomatic form meaning 'I can't help but (A)'. Compare '宿題をせずに寝た' ('I went to bed without doing my homework'), which describes inaction, to '彼の話に笑わずにはいられなかった' ('I couldn't help but laugh at his story'), which expresses an uncontrollable response.

related.title: ずに済む
related.meaning: Get by without doing something, To get away with
related.comparison: ずに has the core meaning of 'without doing', and ずに済む is an idiomatic usage of meaning 'not having to do (A)' (literally, 'it works out without doing it'). Compare 宿題をせずに寝た ('I went to bed without doing my homework.'), which simply describes lack of action, to e.g. 新しいスマホを買わずに済んだ ('It worked out so that I didn't have to buy a new smartphone.').

related.title: ことなく
related.meaning: Without ~ing
related.comparison: Both ずに and ことなく mean 'without doing (A)' and can often be used interchangeably. ずに is more common and used in both writing and conversation, e.g. '宿題をせずに寝た' ('I went to bed without doing my homework'). ことなく is more formal and tends to appear in writing, e.g. '彼は休むことなく働き続けた' ('He continued working without rest').

related.title: 当たり
related.meaning: Per~, A~, Apiece, Each
related.comparison: Both express the idea of 'each', but with different nuance and usage. ～ずつ attaches to quantity expressions like numbers or 少し to mean 'each' or 'at a time', e.g. 一つずつください 'Give me one of each.' or ちょっとずつよくなってきた 'It's gotten better a little at a time.' 当たり also attaches to Number + Counter, but means 'per' or 'apiece', e.g. １個当たり５００円です 'They're 500 yen apiece.'

related.title: それぞれ
related.meaning: Each, Respectively
related.comparison: Both convey the meaning of 'each', but differ in structure. ～ずつ attaches to quantity expressions to mean 'each' or 'little by little', e.g. 一つずつください 'Give me one of each.' or ちょっとずつよくなってきた 'It's gotten better a little at a time.' それぞれ appears before a phrase or with の and emphasizes individuality, e.g. 人はそれぞれ個性がある 'People each have unique qualities.'

related.title: おきに
related.meaning: Repeated at intervals, Every other, Every, At intervals of
related.comparison: Both deal with repetition or intervals, but in different ways. ～ずつ means 'each' or 'at a time' and attaches to quantity expressions, e.g. 一つずつください 'Give me one of each.' おきに follows time or distance intervals to mean 'every (A)', e.g. ５分おきに電車が来る 'Trains come every five minutes.'

related.title: ごとに
related.meaning: Each, Every
related.comparison: Both express regular repetition, but in different structures. ～ずつ attaches to quantity phrases to mean 'each' or 'little by little', e.g. 一つずつください 'Give me one of each.' ごとに follows nouns and means 'every (A)', often time-based, e.g. ３０分ごとに体を動かす 'I move my body every thirty minutes.'

related.title: 各
related.meaning: Each, Every, Respective, Various
related.comparison: Both mean 'each', but with different usage. ～ずつ follows quantity expressions to mean 'each' or 'at a time', e.g. 一つずつください 'Give me one of each.' or ちょっとずつよくなってきた 'It's gotten better a little at a time.' 各 appears before nouns and means 'every' or 'each', e.g. この電車は各駅にとまります 'This train stops at every station.'

related.title: ずっと ①
related.meaning: Continuously, All throughout, Entire time, Ever (since), The whole time, All the way
related.comparison: ずっと is an adverb with two main uses that are related but differ in nuance. ずっと① emphasizes a continuous action or state over time, for example, ずっと好きだった ('I always liked you/her/etc.'). In contrast, ずっと② is used in comparisons to show degree, for example, バスケより野球のほうがずっと好き ('I like baseball much more than basketball.').

related.title: なくてはならない
related.meaning: Must do, Have to do
related.comparison: Both express necessity, but the source of that necessity is different. ずにはすまない means ‘cannot avoid doing’ and implies moral pressure, social responsibility, or consequences that make inaction unacceptable, often based on feelings or circumstances rather than rules, as in 迷惑をかけた以上、謝らずにはすまない ('Having caused trouble, I can’t avoid apologizing.'). なくてはならない expresses objective or logical necessity such as rules, requirements, or unavoidable conditions, without emotional pressure, as in 10時までに行かなくてはならない ('I must go by 10 o'clock.').

related.title: わけにはいかない
related.meaning: Cannot afford to, No way, Impossible to do
related.comparison: Both express that an action cannot be avoided for similar but slightly different reasons. ずにはすまない means ‘cannot avoid doing’ due to moral obligation, responsibility, or consequences, often implying inner pressure or remorse, as in 迷惑をかけた以上、謝らずにはすまない ('Since I caused trouble, I can’t avoid apologizing.'). わけにはいかない also means ‘cannot do,’ but focuses on social rules, common sense, or one’s position making the action inappropriate, rather than moral necessity, as in 今日は大事な会議があるから休むわけにはいかない ('I can’t take the day off because there is an important meeting.').

related.title: わけにはいかない
related.meaning: Cannot afford to, No way, Impossible to do
related.comparison: These express internal conflict, but from opposite angles. わけにはいかない implies being unable to act due to duty or social constraint, e.g. 断るわけにはいかない 'I just can't refuse.' ずにはいられない expresses an inability to stop oneself from acting, e.g. 笑わずにはいられない 'I couldn't help but laugh.'

related.title: ずに
related.meaning: Without doing
related.comparison: ずに has the core meaning of 'without doing', while ずにはいられない is an idiomatic form meaning 'I can't help but (A)'. Compare '宿題をせずに寝た' ('I went to bed without doing my homework'), which describes inaction, to '彼の話に笑わずにはいられなかった' ('I couldn't help but laugh at his story'), which expresses an uncontrollable response.

related.title: てしょうがない
related.meaning: Extremely, Very, Really, It can't be helped
related.comparison: Both express uncontrollable actions or feelings, but differ in nuance. ずにはいられない is about being compelled to act (lit. 'unable not to act'), e.g. '彼を応援せずにはいられない' ('I can't help but root for him'). てしょうがない emphasizes a state or feeling that overwhelms you, e.g. '暑くてしょうがない' ('It's unbearably hot') or '会いたくてしょうがない' ('I want to see you so much I can't bear it').

related.title: ないではいられない
related.meaning: Cannot help doing something, Cannot resist doing something
related.comparison: These are nearly synonymous expressions that both mean 'can't help but do (A)'. ずにはいられない feels slightly more formal or literary, while ないではいられない is more typical of speech. Compare '笑わずにはいられない' and '笑わないではいられない' (same meaning, but the latter sounds less literary).

related.title: てならない
related.meaning: Very, Extremely, Can't help but do
related.comparison: Both express uncontrollable actions or feelings, but differ in nuance. ずにはいられない is about being compelled to act (lit. 'unable not to act'), e.g. '彼を応援せずにはいられない' ('I can't help but root for him'). てならない emphasizes a state or feeling that overwhelms you, e.g. '暑くてならない' ('It's unbearably hot') or '会いたくてならない' ('I want to see you so much I can't bear it').

related.title: ざるを得ない
related.meaning: Can't help doing, Have no choice but
related.comparison: Both express uncontrollable actions or feelings, but differ in tone. ずにはいられない often has a more subjective or emotional feel, e.g. '泣かずにはいられなかった' ('I just couldn't help but cry'). ざるを得ない has a more objective tone and is used to express external necessity or obligation, e.g. '行かざるを得ない' ('I have no choice but to go').

related.title: ないわけにはいかない
related.slug: ないわけにはいかない
related.meaning: Can't not, No way to avoid
related.comparison: Both imply that something must be done, but differ in the nature of the obligation. ずにはいられない arises from emotional urgency, e.g. '泣かずにはいられなかった' ('I just couldn't help but cry'). ないわけにはいかない often reflects social duty or pressure, e.g. '謝らないわけにはいかない' ('I have to apologize').

related.title: てたまらない
related.meaning: Can't help but want to, Very, Extremely
related.comparison: Both express uncontrollable actions or feelings, but differ in nuance. ずにはいられない is about being compelled to act (lit. 'unable not to act'), e.g. '彼を応援せずにはいられない' ('I can't help but root for him'). てたまらない emphasizes a state or feeling that overwhelms you, e.g. '暑くてたまらない' ('It's unbearably hot') or '会いたくてたまらない' ('I want to see you so much I can't bear it').

related.title: につき
related.meaning: Due to, On account of, Each or every
related.comparison: Both せいで and につき express cause, but with different nuance and tone. せいで is more colloquial and typically introduces a cause for a negative result, e.g. 雨のせいで試合が中止になった ('The game was canceled because of the rain.'). につき states a reason more neutrally and is often used in formal written announcements, e.g. 工事中につき通行止め ('Closed to traffic due to construction.').

related.title: ために
related.meaning: Due to, Because of, As a result of
related.comparison: ために and せいで can both mean 'due to (A)', but with a difference in tone. せいで almost always implies a negative or undesirable result. ために can be used with either positive or negative nuance. Also, ために can mean 'for the purpose of (A)', which せいで cannot.

related.title: おかげで
related.meaning: Thanks to, Because of
