related.comparison: Both mean 'because of', but with opposite tone. おかげで expresses a thankful cause, e.g. 先生のおかげで合格できた ('Thanks to my teacher, I passed.') せいで places blame for something negative, e.g. 雨のせいで試合が中止になった ('The game was canceled because of the rain.')

related.title: そのため(に)
related.meaning: For that reason, Because of that, To that end
related.comparison: Both せいで and そのため(に) show cause and effect, but せいで follows a word or phrase and implies a negative result or blame, e.g. 台風のせいで試合が中止になった ('The game was canceled because of the typhoon'), while そのため(に) is used at the beginning of the sentence and neutrally shows a logical result of whatever was previously stated. そのため(に) can also express 'for the purpose of (A)', which is a nuance せいで cannot have.

related.title: なくてはならない
related.meaning: Must do, Have to do
related.comparison: Both ずにはおかない and なくてはならない express inevitability, but they differ in source and nuance. なくてはならない states an objective or logical necessity such as rules, duties, as in 法律を守らなくてはならないパスポートがなくてはならない (‘One must obey the law.'). By contrast, ずにはおかない expresses an inevitable result caused by someone’s actions or by a situation’s inherent force, often implying emotional impact or consequence rather than obligation, as in 彼の言葉は人を感動させずにはおかない (‘His words are bound to move people.’).

related.title: ずに
related.meaning: Without doing
related.comparison: ずに has the core meaning of 'without doing', and ずに済む is an idiomatic usage of meaning 'not having to do (A)' (literally, 'it works out without doing it'). Compare 宿題をせずに寝た ('I went to bed without doing my homework.'), which simply describes lack of action, to e.g. 新しいスマホを買わずに済んだ ('It worked out so that I didn't have to buy a new smartphone.').

related.title: わざわざ
related.meaning: Expressly, Especially, Go out of one's way, Take the trouble to
related.comparison: 折角 and わざわざ both convey effort or intentionality, but differ in nuance. 折角 highlights the value of an effort or opportunity, often implying it would be wasteful not to use it. For example, 折角来たのだから食べましょう ('We came all this way, so let's eat it'). わざわざ emphasizes that someone deliberately went out of their way. For example, わざわざ届けてくれてありがとう ('Thank you for going out of your way to deliver it to me').

related.title: 全く～ない
related.meaning: Not~at all
related.comparison: Both ぜんぜん (with a negative verb) and 全く～ない mean 'not at all,' e.g. ぜんぜんできない and 全くできない both mean 'I can't do it at all.', with ぜんぜん being more casual in tone. In modern speech, both can occasionally appear with affirmative forms, e.g. ぜんぜん大丈夫 ('It's completely fine.'), though this was traditionally considered improper use.

related.title: とても～ない
related.meaning: Not ~ at all, Cannot ~ at all
related.comparison: Both ぜんぜん (with a negative form) and とても～ない can mean 'not at all', but differ slightly in nuance. とても～ない emphasizes impossibility, e.g. とてもできない and ぜんぜんできない both mean 'I absolutely can't do it.'. With adjectives like 美味しくない, where there is no sense of potential, 全然 is preferred, e.g. 全然美味しくない ('It's not tasty at all.').

related.title: すこしも～ない
related.meaning: Not even a little, Not at all, Not in the least, Never
related.comparison: ぜんぜん and すこしも～ない both emphasize negation and mean 'not at all.' A key difference is that ぜんぜん, especially in casual speech, can also appear in affirmative phrases, e.g. ぜんぜん大丈夫 ('It's totally fine.'). In contrast, すこしも～ない is more formal and strictly used in negative contexts, e.g. すこしも寒くない ('It's not even a little cold.').

related.title: だけましだ
related.meaning: At least, It's better than..., One should feel grateful for
related.comparison: Both can express the meaning of at least, but with different nuance. せめて expresses a minimal hope or consolation in an otherwise unfavorable situation, as in せめて一日は休みたい (I want to take at least one day off). だけましだ evaluates a situation as better than something worse, as in 忙しいけど、土日休めるだけましだ (I’m busy, but at least I can rest on the weekends).

related.title: よ
related.meaning: Emphasis (Sentence ending particle)
related.comparison: よ is a sentence-ending particle used to add emphasis and indicate the speaker is sharing new information (e.g., 面白いですよ = 'It's interesting, I'm telling you!'). ぜ is a casual, masculine particle that also adds strong emphasis. Unlike ぞ, it can sound a bit more friendly, but it's still informal and should be avoided in polite situations or with people you don't know well.

antonyms.title: そうもない
antonyms.meaning: Very unlikely to, Does not seem that, Does not appear that
antonyms.comparison: そう is used to express that something seems likely based on perception or indirect signs, e.g. 雨が降りそう ('It looks like it's going to rain.'). In contrast, そうもない (or そうにない) indicates that there are no signs of something happening, e.g. 雨が降りそうもない ('It doesn't seem like it'll rain at all.').

antonyms.title: そうにない
antonyms.meaning: Unlikely to, Showing no signs of
antonyms.comparison: そう expresses that something appears a certain way based on perception or indirect signs, e.g. 雨が降りそう ('It looks like it'll rain.'). In contrast, そうにない (or そうもない) indicates that something shows no signs of happening, e.g. 雨が降りそうにない ('It doesn't seem like it'll rain at all.').

related.title: らしい ①
related.meaning: Seems like, Apparently, I heard
related.comparison: The main difference between そう and らしい① lies in the source of information. そう implies speculation based on personal observation, e.g. 面白そう ('It looks interesting.'). らしい is based on secondhand information or inference, e.g. 面白いらしい ('I hear it's interesting.' or 'Apparently, it's interesting.').

related.title: ような気がする
related.meaning: Have a feeling that
related.comparison: ような気がする and そう both express impressions, but with different nuance. ような気がする conveys personal intuition or vague feeling, i.e. something you sense even without clear reason. On the other hand, そう is typically based on observable clues or appearance. In that sense, ような気がする is more subjective and less confident than そう.

related.title: っぽい
related.meaning: -ish, -like, Characteristic of, Typical of, Tendency to
related.comparison: Both っぽい and そう express appearance or impression, but differ in nuance and usage. そう attaches to word stems and is neutral and often based on observation, e.g. 雨が降りそう ('It looks like it'll rain.'). っぽい is casual and attaches to nouns to mean '~ish' or '~like', e.g. 子供っぽい 'childish'. Unlike そう, it often carries a negative or demeaning tone.

related.title: のように・のような
related.meaning: Like (Noun), Similar to (Noun)
related.comparison: のように・のような and そう both express impressions, but with different nuance. のように・のような show direct comparison, like 'like' or 'as if,' e.g. 春のように暖かい ('It's warm like spring.'). そう expresses a perceived impression based on observation, e.g. 暖かそう ('It seems warm.').

related.title: みたい
related.meaning: Like, Similar to, Resembling
related.comparison: Both みたい and そう can mean 'seems like', but differ in nuance and usage. みたい compares something to another, e.g. 春みたい ('It's like spring.') or is based on reliable evidence, e.g. 面白いみたい ('Apparently it's interesting.' i.e. based on something you've heard). そう is based on appearance or sensory impression, e.g. 面白そう ('It looks/sounds interesting.').

related.title: ように・ような
related.meaning: As・like, Just like
related.comparison: ように・ような and そう both convey impressions, but with different nuance. ように・ような expresses direct comparison or similarity, like 'as if' or 'like,' e.g. 春が来たように暖かい ('It's warm as if spring had come.'). そう is based on the speaker's observation or feeling, e.g. 暖かそう ('It seems warm.').

related.title: たい
related.meaning: Want to do
related.comparison: The たい form expresses personal desires, such as 日本に行きたい ('I want to go to Japan'), though, the たい form is typically not used for expressing others' desires. One way that we can comment on another person's desires, however, is to use the そう form, meaning 'appears to.' For example 日本に行きたそう can be used to mean 'They seem to want to go to Japan.'

related.title: にみえる
related.meaning: To look, To seem, To appear
related.comparison: にみえる and そう both express that something 'seems' a certain way, but with different nuance. にみえる specifically refers to visual appearance and can be combined with そう, e.g. 面白そうにみえる ('It looks interesting.'). そう alone expresses general impression and doesn't necessarily rely on visual cues, e.g. 面白そう ('It seems interesting.'), which could be based on hearing about it, etc.

related.title: そんな・こんな・あんな・どんな
related.slug: そんな-こんな-あんな-どんな
related.meaning: Such a・Kind of, Something like ~
related.comparison: そういう (and its variants こういう, ああいう, どういう) and そんな・こんな・あんな・どんな both modify nouns to mean 'that sort of,' 'this sort of,' etc. While often similar in meaning, そういう tends to be a bit more formal or polite. In contrast, depending on context そんな can be used with a negative or dismissive tone, which is not true of そういう.

related.title: と
related.meaning: If, When, Whenever, And, [Conditional]
related.comparison: と marks a natural condition, while そうすると is used to explain what would happen in that case. It means 'if that happens' or 'then...' and often introduces a result. For example, 早く寝なさい。そうすると、明日は元気になるよ ('Go to bed early. Then you'll feel good tomorrow.')

related.title: すると
related.meaning: Having done..., If...is done..., (And/just) then, Thereupon, If so
related.comparison: すると is often considered an abbreviated form of そうすると, and both are typically used at the beginning of a sentence to express something that occurred or logically followed from what came before, similar to 'upon (A), (B)' in English. Both can also be used to summarize or draw a conclusion from the previous statement, like saying 'so that means (A).'

related.title: ようだ
related.meaning: It seems that, It appears that, It looks like
related.comparison: Both forms express observations similar to 'seems' or 'appears', but differ in nuance. そうだ is based on sensory clues, e.g. 雨が降りそうだ ('It looks like it'll rain.', because the sky is dark and the air is damp). ようだ is based on more concrete information or experience, e.g. 雨が降るようだ ('It seems it'll rain.' because people are carrying umbrellas, or the weather report said so).

related.title: ということだ
related.meaning: I heard that, Rumor has it that, It is said that, It means that
related.comparison: Both convey hearsay, but differ in tone and formality. そうだ is used to directly pass on something you've heard, e.g. 明日は休みだそうだ ('I heard he has tomorrow off.') ということだ is more formal and often sounds more confident or declarative. It can also carry a nuance of explanation or conclusion, e.g. 明日は休みになるということだ ('So that means / They say we'll have tomorrow off.')

related.title: んだって
related.meaning: I heard that, He or She said that
related.comparison: Both express hearsay, but with different tone and usage. んだって is very casual, often used in everyday conversation, and carries an explanatory tone due to んだ, e.g. 明日休みなんだって ('They said tomorrow's a day off, you know.') そうだ is more neutral and indirect, with no explanatory nuance, e.g. 明日休みだそうだ ('I heard tomorrow's a day off.')

related.title: とか
related.meaning: I heard that… something like, They said ... or something
related.comparison: Both can relay hearsay, but with different levels of certainty. とか adds a nuance of vagueness or uncertainty, e.g. 来月引っ越すとか ('I heard they might be moving next month, or something.') そうだ also conveys hearsay, but with less ambiguity, e.g. 来月引っ越すそうだ ('I heard they're moving next month.')

related.title: らしい ①
related.meaning: Seems like, Apparently, I heard
related.comparison: らしい ① and そうだ both convey information heard from another source, but with different nuances. らしい ① suggests hearsay with some degree of certainty, implying the speaker believes it to be true, e.g. 'Apparently it's (A)'. In contrast, そうだ is more neutral, simply meaning 'I heard (A).' or 'They say (A).'. They also connect differently to nouns and な-Adjectives, e.g. 大変らしい vs. 大変だそうだ (both meaning 'It seems difficult.').

related.title: ときいた
related.meaning: I heard that
related.comparison: Both ときいた and そうだ express hearsay, but with different nuances. ときいた means 'I heard (A).' and conveys information directly heard by the speaker, e.g., 高いときいた ('I heard it was expensive.'). In contrast, そうだ reports hearsay in a more abstract fashion, similar to 'They say (A).' in English.

related.title: といわれている
related.meaning: It is said that, Is known as
related.comparison: といわれている and そうだ both report something heard, but differ in nuance. といわれている is more general and formal, e.g. 'It is said that (A)'. On the other hand, そうだ often implies someone specific said it and is more casual, e.g., 雨が降るそうだ ('I heard it's going to rain.').

related.title: といえば
related.meaning: Speaking of, If it were the case that
related.comparison: These expressions are similar in that both can be used to shift the topic, but といえば is typically used following a noun to introduce something connected to that topic, while そういえば recalls something the speaker just remembered. For example, 夏といえば花火だね ('Speaking of summer, fireworks come to mind') vs. そういえば、明日は休みだった ('Oh right, we have a holiday tomorrow').

related.title: ところで
related.meaning: By the way, Incidentally
related.comparison: Both can be used at the beginning of a sentence to introduce a new topic, but with different nuance. ところで marks a clear, unrelated shift, similar to 'by the way' in English, e.g. ところで、週末は何してた？ ('By the way, what did you do this weekend?') そういえば recalls something related to the current topic, e.g. そういえば、田中さんに会ったよ ('Speaking of which, I saw Tanaka.')

related.title: ようだ
related.meaning: It seems that, It appears that, It looks like
related.comparison: そうに・そうな and ようだ can both mean 'seems' or 'appears', but differ in nuance and usage. そうに・そうな is based on sensory observation and attaches to verbs/adjectives (in the case of そうに) or nouns (in the case of そうな). In contrast, ようだ is based on information or inference and typically appears at the end of a sentence, e.g. 雨が降るようだ ('It looks like it will rain.').

related.title: ように・ような
related.meaning: As・like, Just like
related.comparison: Both そうに・そうな and ように・ような describe appearances, but with different nuances. そうに・そうな conveys a subjective impression or an event about to happen, e.g. 優しそうな目 'kind-seeming eyes' or 雨が降りそうな天気 'weather that looks like it's about to rain'. ように・ような, in contrast, expresses comparison, e.g. 春が来たような天気 'weather that feels like spring has arrived'.

related.title: のように・のような
related.meaning: Like (Noun), Similar to (Noun)
related.comparison: Both そうに・そうな and のように・のような describe appearances, but with different nuances. そうに・そうな conveys a subjective impression or an event that seems about to happen, e.g. 優しそうな目 'kind-seeming eyes' or 雨が降りそうな天気 'weather that seems like it's about to rain'. ように・ような, in contrast, expresses comparison, e.g. 春のような天気 'spring-like weather'.

related.title: みたい
related.meaning: Like, Similar to, Resembling
related.comparison: Both みたい and そうに・そうな can mean 'seems like', but differ in nuance and usage. みたい expresses resemblance to something else, e.g. 春みたいな天気 'spring-like weather'. そうに・そうな is based on sensory cues and often used with verbs, e.g. 雨が降りそうな天気 'weather that looks like it's about to rain'.

related.title: みたいに・みたいな
related.meaning: Like, Similar to, Resembling
related.comparison: そうに・そうな and みたいに・みたいな both attach to verbs and adjectives and express appearance, but differ in nuance. そうに・そうな is based on observable signs, e.g. 雨が降りそうな天気 'weather that looks like it's about to rain'. みたいに・みたいな compares something to another thing, e.g. 春みたいな天気 'spring-like weather' in a season that is not actually spring.

related.title: にみえる
related.meaning: To look, To seem, To appear
related.comparison: Both そうに・そうな and にみえる express appearance, but with different nuances. そうに・そうな conveys a subjective impression of a state, e.g. 優しそうな目 'kind-seeming eyes', or an event about to happen, e.g. 雨が降りそうな天気 'weather that seems like it's about to rain'. にみえる, used with そう or alone, e.g. 優しそうにみえる ('He/she seems kind.') or 虫にみえる ('It looks like a bug.'), specifically emphasizes visual appearance.

related.title: げ
related.meaning: Seemingly, Giving appearance of, [Expresses emotions or feelings]
related.comparison: そうに・そうな and げ both describe appearances, but with some differences. Both can be used with adjectives like 優しそうな目 'kind-looking eyes' and 優しげな目 (same meaning), though げ is more subjective and poetic. Unlike げ, そう can also describe things that seem about to happen, e.g. 雨が降りそうな空 'a sky that looks like it's about to rain'.

synonyms.title: っこない
synonyms.meaning: There is no chance of, There is no way of, Impossible
synonyms.comparison: っこない and そうにない both indicate improbability, but differ in strength. っこない is categorical and emphatic, rejecting any possibility, as in こんなに弱い選手ばかりでは、勝てっこない (There’s no way we can win with such poor athletes). そうにない is more tentative, suggesting something seems unlikely based on current signs, as in 勝てそうにない (It doesn’t seem like we can win).

antonyms.title: ことに
antonyms.comparison: ことに emphasizes a strong emotional or subjective reaction to an event, while そうにない expresses objective improbability. For example, 驚いたことに、巨人が負けた 'To my surprise, the Giants lost.' expresses emotional impact, whereas 雨は降りそうにない 'It doesn't look like it will rain.' simply states an unemotional likelihood.

antonyms.title: そう
antonyms.meaning: Look like, Appear, Seem, Have a feeling that
antonyms.comparison: そう expresses that something appears a certain way based on perception or indirect signs, e.g. 雨が降りそう ('It looks like it'll rain.'). In contrast, そうにない (or そうもない) indicates that something shows no signs of happening, e.g. 雨が降りそうにない ('It doesn't seem like it'll rain at all.').

related.title: そうもない
related.meaning: Very unlikely to, Does not seem that, Does not appear that
related.comparison: そうもない and そうにない are extremely similar, both expressing that something is unlikely or shows no signs of happening. The difference is that そうもない feels stronger and more confident due to も adding emphasis, e.g. '雨が降りそうにない' vs. '雨が降りそうもない' (same meaning, but the latter sounds more confident).

related.title: あそこ
related.meaning: Over there
related.comparison: あそこ and そこ both indicate locations, differing only by perspective. あそこ refers to a place far from both the speaker and listener, similar to 'over there' (away from both of us). In contrast, そこ refers to a location closer to the listener than to the speaker, similar to 'there' (near you).

related.title: それ
related.meaning: That
related.comparison: そこ and それ both refer to something previously mentioned or something closer to the listener than the speaker. そこ refers to a place and is similar to 'there' in English. それ refers to a thing or idea, like 'that.' Both are used when the speaker and listener share an understanding of what is being referred to, so the name doesn't need to be repeated.

related.title: この
related.meaning: This
related.comparison: この refers to something physically or figuratively close to the speaker, similar to 'this' in English, and must directly precede a noun (e.g., この本 = 'this book'). In contrast, そこ refers specifically to a location closer to the listener than to the speaker, similar to 'there' in English. Unlike この, そこ can stand alone without a noun.

related.title: その
related.meaning: That
related.comparison: その must always precede a noun and refers to something physically or figuratively closer to the listener (e.g., その本 = 'that book'). In contrast, そこ stands alone and refers specifically to a place near the listener, similar to 'there' or 'that place' in English. Thus, both その and そこ point to things near the listener but differ in usage (noun vs. place).

related.title: ここ
related.meaning: Here, This place
related.comparison: そこ and ここ are both used to indicate locations, with the difference being in the speaker's perspective. そこ refers to a location closer to the listener than to the speaker, similar to 'there' or 'that place' in English. On the other hand, ここ refers to a location closer to the speaker, similar to 'here' or 'this place.'

related.title: どこ
related.meaning: Where
related.comparison: どこ is a question word used to ask about locations, similar to 'where' in English. In contrast, そこ refers specifically to a place closer to the listener, physically or figuratively, equivalent to 'there.' For instance, トイレはどこですか？ means 'Where is the bathroom?' and could be answered with そこです, meaning 'It's there (near you, or a place I'm pointing to).'

related.title: あれ
related.meaning: That (over there)
related.comparison: あれ refers to something far from both the speaker and listener, or something both people know but isn't nearby, similar to 'that (over there)' in English. そこ refers to a place near the listener, like 'there.' Both relate to distance, but あれ is used for objects or ideas, while そこ is used for locations.

related.title: あの
related.meaning: That (over there)
related.comparison: そこ refers to a place near the listener, similar to 'there' in English. あの, however, points to something distant from both speaker and listener or something familiar to both, like 'that.' Unlike そこ, あの always refers to objects or concepts and must directly precede a noun (e.g., あの本 = 'that book').

antonyms.title: ないことはない
antonyms.meaning: Is not impossible, It doesn't mean that it isn't
antonyms.comparison: Both そうもない and ないことはない express uncertainty, but in opposite directions. そうもない shows strong doubt or improbability, e.g. '雨が降りそうもない' ('It doesn't look like it will rain'). ないことはない uses a double negative to express a less confident positive, e.g. '行けないことはない' ('It's not that I can't go'), which implies that while not impossible, it might be with some difficulty.

antonyms.title: そう
antonyms.meaning: Look like, Appear, Seem, Have a feeling that
antonyms.comparison: そう is used to express that something seems likely based on perception or indirect signs, e.g. 雨が降りそう ('It looks like it's going to rain.'). In contrast, そうもない (or そうにない) indicates that there are no signs of something happening, e.g. 雨が降りそうもない ('It doesn't seem like it'll rain at all.').

related.title: わけがない
related.meaning: There is no way, There is no reason, It is impossible, Cannot be
related.comparison: そうもない and わけがない are similar in that both express the unlikelihood of an event. そうもない suggests low probability based on observation, e.g. 雨が降りそうもない ('It seems unlikely that it'll rain'), while わけがない is an emphatic denial based on the speaker's logical conclusion, e.g. 勝てるわけがない ('There's no way we can win').

related.title: っこない
related.meaning: There is no chance of, There is no way of, Impossible
related.comparison: Both そうもない and っこない express unlikelihood, but differ in intensity and tone. そうもない implies doubt but leaves room for possibility, e.g. '間に合いそうもない' ('It doesn't seem like we'll make it in time'). っこない is stronger and more absolute, suggesting impossibility, e.g. 'そんなことできっこない' ('There's no way I could do that'). っこない is also more casual and colloquial, while そうもない is more neutral.

related.title: ようがない・ようもない
related.slug: ようがない・ようもない
related.meaning: There's no way to, It's impossible to
related.comparison: While both express impossibility, そうもない focuses on visual or situational cues suggesting unlikelihood, e.g. '雨が降りそうもない' ('It doesn't look like it'll rain'). ようがない・ようもない implies there is no method or way to perform the action, e.g. '連絡のしようがない' ('There's no way to contact them').

related.title: そうにない
related.meaning: Unlikely to, Showing no signs of
related.comparison: そうもない and そうにない are extremely similar, both expressing that something is unlikely or shows no signs of happening. The difference is that そうもない feels stronger and more confident due to も adding emphasis, e.g. '雨が降りそうにない' vs. '雨が降りそうもない' (same meaning, but the latter sounds more confident).

related.title: はずがない
related.meaning: Hardly possible, Cannot be, Highly unlikely, Improbable
related.comparison: そうもない and はずがない both express the improbability of something happening. そうもない is the less confident of the two and carries the nuance that something seems unlikely, e.g. 雨が降りそうもない ('It doesn't appear that it's going to rain.'). In contrast, はずがない is a stronger denial, with a nuance of disbelief or surprise, e.g. 負けるはずがない ('There's no way / It's highly unlikely [we] will lose.').

related.title: その結果
related.meaning: As a result
related.comparison: These expressions are similar in that both are used at the beginning of a sentence and can express the outcome of a situation, but they differ in nuance. そこで is relatively vague about the connection between the two events, like the English 'as such' or 'accordingly', while その結果 more specifically conveys that a situation or condition results from the previously mentioned cause.

related.title: そのため(に)
related.meaning: For that reason, Because of that, To that end
related.comparison: Both are typically used at the beginning of a sentence to show what follows from a certain cause or situation. そのため(に) more clearly states the relation and can mean either 'due to that' or 'for that purpose', while そこで is less clear about the relationship between the previous thought and what follows, similar to the English 'as such' or 'in light of that'.

related.title: すると
related.meaning: Having done..., If...is done..., (And/just) then, Thereupon, If so
related.comparison: Both describe what follows from a previous situation, but with different nuance. そこで introduces an intentional response to a situation, e.g. 雨が降ってきた。そこで傘をさした ('It started raining, so I opened my umbrella.') すると shows a natural or immediate result, e.g. 雨が降ってきた。すると子供が泣き出した ('It started raining. Then the child started crying.')

related.title: したがって
related.meaning: Therefore, Thus, As a result
related.comparison: そこで and したがって are both used at the beginning of a sentence to express one action following another, but differ in nuance and tone. そこで is a general-purpose expression usable in various situations and contexts, like the English 'so' or 'as such'. In contrast, したがって is more formal and has a stricter 'following from that' or 'thus' nuance.

related.title: それ
related.meaning: That
related.comparison: その and それ both refer to something closer to the listener than the speaker, like 'that' in English. それ is used by itself to mean 'that' or 'that thing.' その must be followed by a noun, as in その本 ('that book'). In other words, それ stands alone, while その comes before a noun.

related.title: あそこ
related.meaning: Over there
related.comparison: その refers to something physically or figuratively close to the listener, similar to 'that' in English, and must directly precede a noun (e.g., その本 = 'that book'). In contrast, あそこ refers specifically to a location distant from both the speaker and listener, equivalent to 'over there' in English. Unlike その, あそこ can stand alone without needing a noun.

related.title: あれ
related.meaning: That (over there)
related.comparison: その refers to something closer to the listener than the speaker, like 'that' in English, and must be followed by a noun (e.g., その本 = 'that book'). あれ refers to something far from both the speaker and listener, or something both people know. Unlike その, あれ can stand alone and means 'that' or 'that thing.'

related.title: ここ
related.meaning: Here, This place
related.comparison: その refers to something near the listener, similar to 'that' in English, and must directly precede a noun (e.g., その本 = 'that book'). In contrast, ここ refers to a location near or indicated by the speaker, similar to 'here.' Unlike その, ここ stands alone as 'this place' and doesn't require a noun.

related.title: そこ
related.meaning: There, That place
related.comparison: その must always precede a noun and refers to something physically or figuratively closer to the listener (e.g., その本 = 'that book'). In contrast, そこ stands alone and refers specifically to a place near the listener, similar to 'there' or 'that place' in English. Thus, both その and そこ point to things near the listener but differ in usage (noun vs. place).

related.title: この
related.meaning: This
related.comparison: Both この and その are used before nouns to describe their position relative to the speaker, similar to 'this' and 'that' in English. The difference lies in perspective: この refers to something close to the speaker (like 'this'), while その refers to something closer to the listener or something just mentioned (like 'that').

related.title: あの
related.meaning: That (over there)
related.comparison: Both その and あの are used before nouns to express 'that,' referring to an object or concept away from the speaker. その points to something closer to the listener than the speaker, or something just mentioned. In contrast, あの refers to something far from both the speaker and listener, or something familiar to both but not close to either.

related.title: どの
related.meaning: Which (of three or more), What (kind)
related.comparison: Both どの and その are used before nouns. その refers to something physically or figuratively closer to the listener than the speaker, or something that was just mentioned, similar to the English 'that.' In contrast, どの is a question word used to ask 'which' when there are three or more choices (e.g., どの色が好きですか？ = 'Which color do you like?').

related.title: すると
related.meaning: Having done..., If...is done..., (And/just) then, Thereupon, If so
related.comparison: The two Japanese phrases, すると and そのため(に), are used in different contexts. すると is used to indicate a natural or logical outcome or consequence of a certain action, it is often translated as 'and then', 'if', or 'when'. On the other hand, そのため(に) is commonly used to express the cause or reason for a certain outcome, it is often translated as 'for that reason', 'because of that' or 'hence'.

related.title: それで
related.meaning: Because of that, So?, And then?, Therefore, With that
related.comparison: そのため(に) and それで both show cause and effect. それで is casual and used in everyday speech: 道が混んでた。それで遅れた ('The road was crowded, so I was late.'). そのため is more formal and can also express purpose, e.g. 健康のため、運動している 'I'm exercising for my health.').

related.title: ため(に)
related.meaning: For, For the sake of, In order to
related.comparison: These share the same core construction, using ため(に) to express 'in order to (A)' or 'due to (A)'. ため(に) is used within a sentence after a noun, verb, or adjective phrase. そのため is used at the beginning of a sentence to mean 'for that reason' or 'due to that', referring back to what was just said.

related.title: ために
related.meaning: Due to, Because of, As a result of
related.comparison: そのため(に) can be seen as a more specific version of ために. ために follows a noun, verb, or adjective phrase to express purpose or reason. そのため(に) appears at the beginning of a sentence and means 'due to that' or 'in order to do that', referring back to what was previously said.

related.title: なぜなら〜から
related.meaning: Because, The reason is, That is because
related.comparison: Both expressions relate to cause and effect, but in opposite directions: なぜなら〜から gives the reason for something previously stated, while そのため(に) introduces the result of a prior situation. Additionally, そのために can also express purpose, a meaning なぜなら〜から does not carry.

related.title: せいで
related.meaning: Because of, Due to, Blame
related.comparison: Both せいで and そのため(に) show cause and effect, but せいで follows a word or phrase and implies a negative result or blame, e.g. 台風のせいで試合が中止になった ('The game was canceled because of the typhoon'), while そのため(に) is used at the beginning of the sentence and neutrally shows a logical result of whatever was previously stated. そのため(に) can also express 'for the purpose of (A)', which is a nuance せいで cannot have.

related.title: そこで
related.meaning: Accordingly, Now, Therefore, To that end
related.comparison: Both are typically used at the beginning of a sentence to show what follows from a certain cause or situation. そのため(に) more clearly states the relation and can mean either 'due to that' or 'for that purpose', while そこで is less clear about the relationship between the previous thought and what follows, similar to the English 'as such' or 'in light of that'.

related.title: おかげで
related.meaning: Thanks to, Because of
related.comparison: Both express cause and effect, but with different tone and usage. おかげで implies a positive result and gratitude, e.g. 先生のおかげで合格できた ('Thanks to my teacher, I passed.') そのため(に) is neutral, objective, and often appears at the start of a sentence. It can also mean 'for that purpose'.

related.title: という理由で
related.meaning: For (that) reason; on the grounds of (that), With the reasoning, Under the pretext of, Due to
related.comparison: Both introduce reasons, but そのため(に) shows a direct consequence or effect from what was previously stated, while という理由で introduces a reason more abstractly and with a greater degree of formality. They also differ in how they connect the reason to the consequence, with そのため(に) always appearing at the beginning of a sentence, and という理由で being able to appear either at the beginning or in the middle of a sentence.

related.title: その結果
related.meaning: As a result
related.comparison: These expressions are similar in that both are used at the beginning of a sentence to express the effect of a cause mentioned just previously. そのため(に) implies a direct, logical cause, like the English 'due to that', while その結果 focuses on the ultimate condition or situation brought about by the cause, like 'as a result of that'. そのため(に) can also express 'for the purpose of (A)', which is a nuance その結果 cannot have.

synonyms.title: なお②
synonyms.comparison: なお② and その上 are both formal expressions used at the start of a sentence to add information. They can overlap in meaning, but その上 often implies that the following point is notable, while なお② simply adds on. Additionally, requests or instructions can follow なお②, but usually do not follow その上.

related.title: それに
related.meaning: And, Besides, Moreover, In addition
related.comparison: それに and その上 both add information. それに is neutral and casual. その上 has a slightly more formal tone and implies building upon the previous statement, e.g. 彼は優秀だ。その上、性格もいい ('He's capable, and what's more, he's kind too.').

related.title: 上に
related.meaning: As well, Besides, In addition to
related.comparison: その上 and 上に both convey the idea of in addition, but differ in structure and tone. その上 is a conjunctive adverb used between sentences, as in 彼は遅刻した。その上、宿題も忘れた (He was late. On top of that, he forgot his homework). 上に functions within a sentence to link clauses, and is often used in more structured or descriptive contexts.

related.title: おまけに
related.meaning: In addition, On top of that, What is more, To make matters worse
related.comparison: その上 and おまけに both introduce additional information, but differ in nuance. その上 is neutral and simply adds or emphasizes information. おまけに often conveys a negative situation or unexpected outcome, sometimes with a nuance of irritation or frustration, as in 雨だった。おまけに風も強かった (It was raining. On top of that, the wind was strong).

related.title: しかも
related.meaning: Moreover, Furthermore
related.comparison: その上 and しかも can both appear at the beginning of a sentence to introduce additional information. They are often interchangeable, but しかも is somewhat more colloquial and can imply that the added information is unexpected. その上 is more neutral and formal, with no such implication.

related.title: 更に
related.meaning: Even more or further, Again, More and more
related.comparison: その上 and 更に are both relatively formal expressions used at the beginning of a sentence to add information. While often interchangeable, they differ subtly: 更に can imply that the added point is of the same type as what came before, while その上 can highlight that the following point is particularly notable or worthy of emphasis.

related.title: そこで
related.meaning: Accordingly, Now, Therefore, To that end
related.comparison: These expressions are similar in that both are used at the beginning of a sentence and can express the outcome of a situation, but they differ in nuance. そこで is relatively vague about the connection between the two events, like the English 'as such' or 'accordingly', while その結果 more specifically conveys that a situation or condition results from the previously mentioned cause.

related.title: したがって
related.meaning: Therefore, Thus, As a result
related.comparison: その結果 and したがって are similar in that both express a result or consequence with a relatively formal tone. その結果 emphasizes the resulting situation or condition, similar to the English 'as a result of that', while したがって implies a logical conclusion, which may or may not necessarily be a 'resulting' situation.

related.title: 結果・の結果
related.meaning: As a result of
related.comparison: Both expressions use 結果 to express a resulting situation or condition, like the English 'as a result', but differ in how they connect to the cause. その結果 is used at the beginning of a sentence and points back to something just mentioned, while 結果・の結果 is used in mid-sentence and connects directly to an explicitly mentioned cause, either by attaching to a verb or to a noun with の.

related.title: ために
related.meaning: Due to, Because of, As a result of
related.comparison: ために and その結果 both express a cause/effect relationship, but in different ways. ために is used within a sentence following a noun, verb, or adjective, and can mean either 'due to (A)' or 'in order to (A)'. その結果 comes at the beginning of a sentence and always means 'as a result of that', referring back to the previous statement.

related.title: そのため(に)
related.meaning: For that reason, Because of that, To that end
related.comparison: These expressions are similar in that both are used at the beginning of a sentence to express the effect of a cause mentioned just previously. そのため(に) implies a direct, logical cause, like the English 'due to that', while その結果 focuses on the ultimate condition or situation brought about by the cause, like 'as a result of that'. そのため(に) can also express 'for the purpose of (A)', which is a nuance その結果 cannot have.

related.title: あの
related.meaning: That (over there)
related.comparison: それ refers to something closer to the listener than to the speaker, or something just mentioned, similar to 'that' in English. あの points to something far from both the speaker and listener, or something both people are aware of. あの must be used with a noun, like あの本 ('that book'), while それ can stand alone.

related.title: その
related.meaning: That
related.comparison: その and それ both refer to something closer to the listener than the speaker, like 'that' in English. それ is used by itself to mean 'that' or 'that thing.' その must be followed by a noun, as in その本 ('that book'). In other words, それ stands alone, while その comes before a noun.

related.title: どれ
related.meaning: Which (of three or more)
related.comparison: どれ is a question word used to ask 'which' or 'which one' when choosing from three or more options. それ, on the other hand, refers to something near the listener or something already mentioned, similar to 'that' in English. Unlike どれ, それ is not a question word.

related.title: これ
related.meaning: This
related.comparison: これ and それ are both used to refer to things, but the difference lies in distance. これ means 'this' and refers to something near the speaker. それ means 'that' and refers to something closer to the listener than the speaker. Both can refer to physical objects or more abstract things.

related.title: ここ
related.meaning: Here, This place
related.comparison: ここ refers to a location where the speaker is, or is pointing to (like on a map), and is similar to 'here' in English. それ refers to something near the listener or something just mentioned, like 'that.' The key difference is that ここ refers to a place, while それ refers to a thing.

related.title: そこ
related.meaning: There, That place
related.comparison: そこ and それ both refer to something previously mentioned or something closer to the listener than the speaker. そこ refers to a place and is similar to 'there' in English. それ refers to a thing or idea, like 'that.' Both are used when the speaker and listener share an understanding of what is being referred to, so the name doesn't need to be repeated.

related.title: この
related.meaning: This
related.comparison: この points to something near the speaker, like 'this' in English, and must be followed by a noun (e.g., この本 = 'this book'). それ, on the other hand, refers to something near the listener or recently mentioned, like 'that.' Unlike この, それ can be used on its own to mean 'that' or 'that thing.'

related.title: あれ
related.meaning: That (over there)
related.comparison: それ and あれ are both similar to the English word 'that' and are used to refer to objects or ideas away from the speaker. それ refers to something near the listener or just mentioned in conversation. あれ points to something far from both the speaker and listener, or something both people are aware of. For physical objects, think of それ as 'that near you' and あれ as 'that over there.'

related.title: あそこ
related.meaning: Over there
related.comparison: あそこ refers to a place far from both the speaker and listener, or a known location not close to either. It's similar to 'over there' or 'that place' in English. それ, on the other hand, refers to an object near the listener or something just mentioned, like 'that.' The difference is whether you're referring to a place or a thing, and from whose perspective.

related.title: 各
related.meaning: Each, Every, Respective, Various
related.comparison: Both 各 and それぞれ express the idea of 'each' or 'every', but they differ in usage. 各 always goes before a noun, for example, 各部屋 'each room', 各階 'each floor', and so on. それぞれ, on the other hand, can stand alone at the start of a sentence or be attached to a noun with の. For example, 人はそれぞれ違う意見がある ('People each have differing opinions.').

related.title: ～ずつ
related.meaning: One at a time, Per, Each, Gradually
related.comparison: Both convey the meaning of 'each', but differ in structure. ～ずつ attaches to quantity expressions to mean 'each' or 'little by little', e.g. 一つずつください 'Give me one of each.' or ちょっとずつよくなってきた 'It's gotten better a little at a time.' それぞれ appears before a phrase or with の and emphasizes individuality, e.g. 人はそれぞれ個性がある 'People each have unique qualities.'

related.title: 当たり
related.meaning: Per~, A~, Apiece, Each
related.comparison: 当たり and それぞれ can both convey a sense of 'each', but their nuances are different. 当たり attaches to numbers to mean 'each' in the sense of 'apiece', e.g. １個当たり１００円 ('100 yen each' / '100 yen apiece'). In contrast, それぞれ means 'each' in the sense of 'to each their own', e.g. 人はそれぞれ個性がある ('People each have unique traits').

related.title: そのため(に)
related.meaning: For that reason, Because of that, To that end
related.comparison: そのため(に) and それで both show cause and effect. それで is casual and used in everyday speech: 道が混んでた。それで遅れた ('The road was crowded, so I was late.'). そのため is more formal and can also express purpose, e.g. 健康のため、運動している 'I'm exercising for my health.').

related.title: だが・ですが
related.meaning: But, Still, However, Nevertheless
related.comparison: Both それでも and だが・ですが express contrast, similar to 'but' or 'however' in English, but with different nuances. それでも highlights a result or situation that goes against expectations. だが・ですが is more neutral and often used to transition to a contrasting point. Structurally, だが・ですが can appear mid-sentence or at the end of a clause, while それでも typically begins a sentence.

related.title: のに
related.meaning: Despite, Although, Even though
related.comparison: のに expresses contrast, similar to 'although,' and follows verbs, adjectives, or nouns (with な for nouns and な-adjectives). It's typically used at the end of a clause or sentence. それでも also shows contrast, but appears at the beginning of a sentence. The それ refers to the previous statement, giving it the meaning of 'Even so' or 'But still.'

related.title: それにしても
related.meaning: Nevertheless, Even so
related.comparison: それでも and それにしても both express contrast. それでも adds a contradictory point neutrally. それにしても accepts the previous idea, but adds what the speaker really thinks, often a more critical or stronger opinion, e.g., 暑いね。それにしてもよく歩くね ('It's hot. Still, you sure walk a lot.').

related.title: だって
related.meaning: I heard/was said, Because, But, Even or also, Even if or even though
related.comparison: だって and それでも both express contradiction. だって is casual and often used at the start of a sentence to offer an objection, e.g., だって疲れたもん ('But I'm tired!'). それでも is less casual and shows contrast with a neutral tone, e.g., 雨だった。それでも行った ('It was rainy, but I still went.').

related.title: ところが
related.meaning: Even so, However, Even though, But
related.comparison: それでも and ところが both show contrast, like 'even so' or 'and yet'. それでも has a neutral tone and simply adds something that goes against the previous statement. ところが implies something unexpected or surprising happened, especially outside the speaker's control.

related.title: くせに
related.meaning: Even though, And yet, Despite
related.comparison: それでも and くせに both express contrast, but differ in tone. それでも is neutral, like the English 'even so'. くせに adds a critical nuance, often pointing out something negative or unjustified, e.g. 若いくせにやる気がない ('Even though he's young, he has no motivation.'), which implies disapproval.

related.title: しかしながら
related.meaning: However, Nevertheless, But
related.comparison: しかしながら and それでも both mean 'however' or 'nevertheless'. but differ in formality. しかしながら is very formal, often seen in academic or business writing. それでも is used in conversation and everyday writing. Both acknowledge one fact, then present a contrasting one.

related.title: それなのに
related.meaning: And yet, Despite this
related.comparison: それでも and それなのに both show contrast. それでも means 'even so' and sounds neutral, e.g. 雨だった。それでも行った ('It was rainy, but I went anyway.'). それなのに has a stronger emotional tone, often of disbelief or disapproval, e.g. 優しくした。それなのに怒られた ('I was nice and still got yelled at!').

related.title: それにしても
related.meaning: Nevertheless, Even so
related.comparison: Both introduce contradiction. それにしても can mean even so or nevertheless, and can also be used to change topics, as in それにしても暑いね (Even so, it’s hot, isn’t it?). それなのに marks direct contrast, closer to and yet, as in 一生懸命勉強した。それなのに落ちた (I studied hard, and yet I failed).

related.title: もっとも
related.meaning: Although, But, However, For all that, Provided that
related.comparison: Both can begin a sentence to introduce contrast, but differ in tone and nuance. もっとも is more formal and adds a condition that qualifies the previous statement, e.g. 全員来ました。もっとも、田中さんは遅れましたが 'Everyone came. That said, Tanaka was late.' それなのに is more casual and expresses surprise at an unexpected outcome, e.g. 雨だった。それなのに、試合は行われた 'It rained. And yet, the match was held.'

related.title: ながらも
related.meaning: But, Even while, Even though, Despite, Although
related.comparison: Both express contrast, but with different nuances and usage. ながらも attaches to verbs, adjectives, or nouns and conveys contradictory states, e.g. '緊張しながらもわくわくしている' ('While feeling anxious, I'm also excited'). それなのに is used at the start of a sentence to mean 'and yet', often with surprise, e.g. '８時間も寝た。それなのに眠い' ('I slept eight whole hours. But still, I'm sleepy').

related.title: しかしながら
related.meaning: However, Nevertheless, But
related.comparison: Both introduce contrast. しかしながら is formal and neutral, used in writing or speeches, as in 実験は失敗した。しかしながら多くを学んだ (The experiment failed. Nevertheless, we learned a lot). それなのに is conversational and conveys surprise or disappointment, as in 約束した。それなのに来なかった (He promised, and yet he didn’t come).

related.title: くせに
related.meaning: Even though, And yet, Despite
related.comparison: Both express contrast, but differ in usage and implication. くせに follows a phrase and has a critical tone, e.g. 知っているくせに教えてくれない ('He knows but won't tell me.'), implying that the speaker is angry at the person. それなのに comes at the beginning of a sentence, is neutral, and expresses simple contradiction, e.g. 彼は真実を知っている。それなのに、教えてくれない ('He knows. And yet, he won't tell me.') with an objective nuance.

related.title: のに
related.meaning: Despite, Although, Even though
related.comparison: のに expresses contrast, similar to 'although,' and follows verbs, adjectives, or nouns (with な for nouns and な-adjectives). It's typically used at the end of a phrase or sentence. それなのに is a related form used at the beginning of a sentence, meaning 'and yet' or 'despite that,' with それ referring back to the previous statement.

related.title: それでも
related.meaning: But still, And yet, Even so, Nevertheless
related.comparison: それでも and それなのに both show contrast. それでも means 'even so' and sounds neutral, e.g. 雨だった。それでも行った ('It was rainy, but I went anyway.'). それなのに has a stronger emotional tone, often of disbelief or disapproval, e.g. 優しくした。それなのに怒られた ('I was nice and still got yelled at!').

synonyms.title: か
synonyms.comparison: Both か and それとも present alternatives. か directly follows nouns to mean 'or' (e.g., 今日か明日休みます = 'I'll take off today or tomorrow'). In contrast, それとも means 'or' as well, but is used at the start of sentences or questions, not directly after nouns (e.g., 今日休む？それとも明日にする？ = 'Will you take off today? Or [will you do it] tomorrow?').

related.title: むしろ
related.meaning: Rather, Instead, Over
related.comparison: Both むしろ and それとも involve choices, but in different ways. むしろ is used to clarify or emphasize a preferred option, e.g. ワインよりむしろビールが飲みたい ('I'd rather have beer than wine'). それとも offers neutral choices in questions, e.g. ワインにする？それともビール？ ('Will you have wine? Or beer?').

related.title: では・それでは・じゃあ
related.meaning: If so, Well (then), In that case
related.comparison: それなら and それでは can both be used at the start of a sentence as transition markers meaning in that case or well then. それなら expresses a response to a specific condition, as in それなら行きません (In that case, I won’t go). それでは can overlap in usage, but it can also mean well then or and with that, often used to wrap up a topic, as in それでは、失礼します (Well then, excuse me), which それなら cannot convey.3

related.title: なら
related.meaning: Were it, If, If it's the case, As for (Conditional)
related.comparison: なら follows a phrase or noun to express a condition, as in 田中さんが行かないなら、私も行かない (If Tanaka isn’t going, I won’t go either). The なら of それなら is the same particle, but the phrase それなら is used sentence-initially to refer back to something previously mentioned, as in 行かないの？それなら私も行かない (You’re not going? In that case, I won’t go either).

related.title: たら
related.meaning: When, From when, Just when, If
related.comparison: それなら expresses a response to a condition, as in それなら行きません (In that case, I won’t go). The conditional たら form can also mark conditions at the end of verbs, adjectives, or nouns. それだったら can convey a similar meaning to それなら, but with a slight nuance: それなら assumes the condition will or has happened, while それだったら tends to be more hypothetical.3

synonyms.title: なお②
synonyms.comparison: なお② and それに both add information, but differ in tone. それに is neutral and used in everyday speech. なお② is formal and often written, with a tone of explanation or emphasis, e.g. 商品の仕様は変更される場合があります。なお、返品はできません ('The product specifications may be changed. Furthermore, returns are not accepted.').

related.title: その上
related.meaning: Besides, In addition, Furthermore
related.comparison: それに and その上 both add information. それに is neutral and casual. その上 has a slightly more formal tone and implies building upon the previous statement, e.g. 彼は優秀だ。その上、性格もいい ('He's capable, and what's more, he's kind too.').

related.title: おまけに
related.meaning: In addition, On top of that, What is more, To make matters worse
related.comparison: それに and おまけに both add information. それに is neutral and just adds a point that logically follows. おまけに adds something extra or unexpected, often with a tone of surprise or bonus, e.g. おいしかったし、おまけに安かった ('It was delicious, and cheap too!').

related.title: しかも
related.meaning: Moreover, Furthermore
related.comparison: それに and しかも both add information, but しかも is stronger. それに is neutral: it adds a point that logically follows. しかも emphasizes what comes next, like 'what's more' or 'on top of that', e.g. 安い。それに美味しい。しかも体にいい ('It's cheap. And tasty. And what's more, it's good for your body.').

related.title: また
related.meaning: Also, As well, Moreover, Again, Additionally
related.comparison: Both それに and また can be used to provide added information, like the English 'in addition' or 'moreover', but with slightly different nuances. また has a slightly formal tone, and adds information in a neutral sense. それに is more colloquial and has the nuance of adding information that supports or reinforces the previous statement.

related.title: すると
related.meaning: Having done..., If...is done..., (And/just) then, Thereupon, If so
related.comparison: すると and それに are both sentence starters but differ in usage. すると shows result or reaction, e.g. 雨が降った。すると傘を差した ('It started raining. Then he opened an umbrella.'). それに is additive and neutral: 雨が降っていた。それに寒かった ('It was raining. And cold too.').

related.title: ところが
related.meaning: Even so, However, Even though, But
related.comparison: Both express contrast, but with different nuance. ところが introduces an unexpected result, e.g. 試合に勝つと思った。ところが負けた ('I thought we'd win the game. But we lost.') それにしても acknowledges the situation but adds an emotional reaction or further judgment, e.g. 仕方がない。それにしても悔しい ('It can't be helped. Even so, it's frustrating.')

related.title: それでも
related.meaning: But still, And yet, Even so, Nevertheless
related.comparison: それでも and それにしても both express contrast. それでも adds a contradictory point neutrally. それにしても accepts the previous idea, but adds what the speaker really thinks, often a more critical or stronger opinion, e.g., 暑いね。それにしてもよく歩くね ('It's hot. Still, you sure walk a lot.').

related.title: それなのに
related.meaning: And yet, Despite this
related.comparison: Both introduce contradiction. それにしても can mean even so or nevertheless, and can also be used to change topics, as in それにしても暑いね (Even so, it’s hot, isn’t it?). それなのに marks direct contrast, closer to and yet, as in 一生懸命勉強した。それなのに落ちた (I studied hard, and yet I failed).

related.title: しかしながら
related.meaning: However, Nevertheless, But
related.comparison: Both can mean nevertheless. しかしながら is formal and used only for explicit contrasts, as in 雨だった。しかしながら試合は行われた (It rained. Nevertheless, the match was held). それにしても is common in everyday speech and can also serve to shift topics.

related.title: ～たまでだ
related.meaning: Just, Merely, Simply, Only
related.comparison: Both それまでだ and たまでだ use までだ to mark a limit, but the type of limit is different. それまでだ expresses a harsh evaluation meaning “that’s all there is” or “that’s the end,” often used to judge that nothing more can be done if a condition is lost, as in 信頼を失ったらそれまでだ (‘If you lose trust, that’s the end.’). By contrast, たまでだ uses the past tense to downplay an action and means “I only did (A),” emphasizing that the action was small or not worth blaming, as in 俺はただ自分の意見を言ったまでだ (‘I only expressed my opinion.’) or 困っていたから助けたまでだ (‘I just helped because she looked troubled.’).

related.title: ～るまでだ
related.meaning: Just, Merely, Simply, Only
related.comparison: Both involve a limit, but the type of limit is different. それまでだ is an evaluative expression meaning “that’s the end,” used to judge that nothing more can be done if a crucial condition is lost, as in 信頼を失ったらそれまでだ (‘If you lose trust, that’s the end.’). By contrast, Vるまでだ (and までのことだ) expresses resignation, meaning “one can only but (B),” often used with なら to show that (B) is the only remaining option in a negative situation, as in 誰にも教えてもらえないなら、ネットで調べてみるまでだ (‘If no one will teach me, I’ll just have to look it up online.’).

related.title: そういう
related.meaning: Like that, Kind of
related.comparison: そういう (and its variants こういう, ああいう, どういう) and そんな・こんな・あんな・どんな both modify nouns to mean 'that sort of,' 'this sort of,' etc. While often similar in meaning, そういう tends to be a bit more formal or polite. In contrast, depending on context そんな can be used with a negative or dismissive tone, which is not true of そういう.

related.title: いかなる
related.meaning: What kind of, No matter what kind of, No … (with a negative verb), Any... (with a negative verb)
related.comparison: そんな・こんな・あんな・どんな are used before nouns and mean 'that/this/what kind of' or 'something like (A)'. This is a common and fairly casual pattern in everyday speech. いかなる is similar to どんな, but with a much more formal or literary tone. For example, いかなることがあっても and どんなことがあっても both mean 'no matter what happens', but the いかなる version sounds much more formal.

related.title: そんなに
related.meaning: So (much), That much, Like that
related.comparison: そんな・こんな・あんな・どんな come before nouns and mean 'that/this/what kind of (A)' or 'something like (A)'. そんなに is the same そんな, but followed by に. そんなに (as well as こんなに, あんなに, どんなに) is used with verbs or adjectives to add emphasis, similar to 'that much' or 'to that extent' in English. For example, そんな本 means 'a book/books like that,' and そんなに面白い means 'that interesting' or 'so interesting'.

related.title: あまり～ない
related.meaning: Not very, Not much, Not really, Hardly
related.comparison: Both あまり～ない and そんなに express a negative degree, but with different nuance. あまり～ない means 'not very' in a general sense, e.g. あまり寒くない ('It's not very cold.'). そんなに (literally 'that much') can imply comparison to a standard and can be used with both negative and affirmative phrases, e.g. そんなに好きなら 'if you like [it] that much...' and そんなに好きじゃない ('I don't like it that much.').

related.title: そんな・こんな・あんな・どんな
related.slug: そんな-こんな-あんな-どんな
related.meaning: Such a・Kind of, Something like ~
related.comparison: そんな・こんな・あんな・どんな come before nouns and mean 'that/this/what kind of (A)' or 'something like (A)'. そんなに is the same そんな, but followed by に. そんなに (as well as こんなに, あんなに, どんなに) is used with verbs or adjectives to add emphasis, similar to 'that much' or 'to that extent' in English. For example, そんな本 means 'a book/books like that,' and そんなに面白い means 'that interesting' or 'so interesting'.

related.title: よ
related.meaning: Emphasis (Sentence ending particle)
related.comparison: よ is a sentence-ending particle used to add emphasis and indicate the speaker is sharing something new (e.g., 面白いですよ = 'It's interesting, I'm telling you!'). ぞ is a rougher, more masculine sentence-ending particle that adds strong emphasis. It has a blunt tone and should be avoided in polite conversation or with strangers, as it lacks the softness or friendliness of よ.

synonyms.title: たがる
synonyms.comparison: The たい form expresses personal desires, such as 日本に行きたい ('I want to go to Japan'). がる, on the other hand, is a suffix that shows when someone else appears to want something, like 日本に行きたがっている ('They seem like they want to go to Japan'). The たい form is typically not used for expressing others' desires unless quoting the subject.

related.title: そう
related.meaning: Look like, Appear, Seem, Have a feeling that
related.comparison: The たい form expresses personal desires, such as 日本に行きたい ('I want to go to Japan'), though, the たい form is typically not used for expressing others' desires. One way that we can comment on another person's desires, however, is to use the そう form, meaning 'appears to.' For example 日本に行きたそう can be used to mean 'They seem to want to go to Japan.'

related.title: ませんか
related.meaning: Won't you, Would you not, Why don't we
related.comparison: ませんか is a polite way to extend an invitation, similar to 'Wouldn't you like to...?' in English. For example, 何か食べませんか？ ('Won't you eat something?') might be used when politely offering food to a guest. Note that unlike English, the たい form ('Do you want to…?') isn't typically used to invite someone in Japanese—何か食べたいですか？ simply asks if the person wants to eat something.

related.title: がほしい
related.meaning: To want something, To be in need of
related.comparison: Both the たい form and がほしい express desires, similar to the English 'want.' The difference is that たい is used with verbs to express one's desire to perform some action, while がほしい is used after nouns to express one's desire for an object. For example, 日本に行きたい means 'I want to go to Japan' (desire to do something), while お金がほしい means 'I want money' (desire for an object).

related.title: がる
related.meaning: To feel, To think, To act as if, To want, To desire
related.comparison: The たい form expresses personal desires, like バナナが食べたい ('I want to eat a banana'). がる, on the other hand, is used to show that someone else seems to want something, like 食べたがっている ('[He/she] seems like they want to eat'). The たい form is generally not used for others' desires unless the subject has explicitly made their desire clear.

related.title: てほしい
related.meaning: I want you to
related.comparison: Both たい and てほしい express a desire, with one key difference. The たい form is used to express your own desires, similar to the English 'I want to...' while てほしい expresses your desires for other people, or in other words, 'I want you to…' For example, 見たい would express your desire to see something, while 見てほしい would be used if you want someone else to take a look.

synonyms.title: たい
synonyms.comparison: The たい form expresses personal desires, such as 日本に行きたい ('I want to go to Japan'). がる, on the other hand, is a suffix that shows when someone else appears to want something, like 日本に行きたがっている ('They seem like they want to go to Japan'). The たい form is typically not used for expressing others' desires unless quoting the subject.

related.title: がる
related.meaning: To feel, To think, To act as if, To want, To desire
related.comparison: がる attaches to adjectives to describe the observed feelings or desires of another person, e.g. 寒がる 'seems cold', ほしがる 'seems to want (A)', and so on. When combined with the たい-form of verbs, it becomes たがる, expressing observed desire, e.g. 飲みたがる 'seems to want to drink'.

related.title: がほしい
related.meaning: To want something, To be in need of
related.comparison: がほしい and たがる both express desire but differ in usage. がほしい is used for the speaker's own wants, e.g. 車がほしい ('I want a car.'). たがる combines the たい-form of verbs with がる to describe a third person's apparent desire to do something, e.g. 田中さんは車を買いたがっている ('Tanaka seems to want to buy a car.').

related.title: てほしい
related.meaning: I want you to
related.comparison: てほしい and たがる both express desire but in different ways. てほしい is used when the speaker wants someone else to do something, e.g. これを買ってほしい ('I want you to buy this.'). たがる combines the たい-form with がる to describe a third person's apparent desire, e.g. 田中さんは車を買いたがっている ('Tanaka seems to want to buy a car.').

antonyms.title: あまり～ない
antonyms.meaning: Not very, Not much, Not really, Hardly
antonyms.comparison: たくさん is a noun meaning 'many' or 'a lot,' often used with verbs (e.g. たくさん食べた = 'I ate a lot'). To express the opposite idea ('not much' or 'not many'), the pattern あまり～ない is used (e.g. あまり食べなかった = 'I didn't eat much'). Note that あまり originally means 'excess' and can also appear in affirmative forms (see あまりに).

related.title: ことがある
related.meaning: Something is possible, Sometimes, Occasionally, There are times when
related.comparison: With a non-past verb, ことがある expresses that something happens occasionally or from time to time, similar to saying 'There are times when…' In contrast, たことがある uses the past tense to describe a completed experience. For example, 日本に行ったことがある means 'I've been to Japan.' It refers to something that has happened at least once, without implying it occurred repeatedly.

related.title: たものだ
related.meaning: Used to
related.comparison: Both expressions describe past experiences but with different nuances. たことがある states that something has happened before, as in 日本に行ったことがある ('I've been to Japan'). たものだ adds a nostalgic tone and refers to things done regularly in the past, as in 子どもの頃、よくこの公園で遊んだものだ ('I often played at this park when I was a child').

related.title: 僅かに
related.meaning: Only, Slightly, A little, Barely, Merely
related.comparison: Both express small amounts, but differ in usage. 僅かに is adverbial, meaning barely, as in 僅かに聞こえる (It’s barely audible). たった(の) precedes quantity to stress insignificance, as in たったの100円しかない (I have only 100 yen).

related.title: 直ちに
related.meaning: At once, Immediately, As soon as
related.comparison: Both 直ちに and たちまち mean 'immediately', but differ in tone and context. 直ちに is formal and emphasizes urgency, often used in official or written language, e.g. '直ちに避難してください' ('Please evacuate immediately'). たちまち suggests a spontaneous change out of the speaker's control, e.g. '空がたちまち暗くなった' ('The sky suddenly turned dark').

related.title: いきなり
related.meaning: For no reason, Suddenly, All of a sudden
related.comparison: Both convey suddenness. いきなり emphasizes lack of warning, as in いきなり泣き出した (She suddenly burst into tears). たちまち highlights speed or immediacy, as in 話題はたちまち広まった (The topic spread immediately).

related.title: いつの間にか
related.meaning: Suddenly, Unnoticed, Without noticing, Too soon
related.comparison: たちまち emphasizes immediacy, describing something that happens instantly and visibly, as in 雨がたちまち降り出した (It suddenly started raining). いつの間にか emphasizes that a change occurred without the speaker noticing when, as in いつの間にか眠っていた (At some point I fell asleep without realizing).

related.title: ても
related.meaning: Even if, Even though
related.comparison: ても and たって both mean 'even if' or 'even though,' with the main difference being tone and formality. ても is widely used in both everyday speech and writing, while たって is more casual and emphatic, typically found in informal conversation. Aside from tone, their meaning is nearly identical, e.g., 行っても and 行ったって both mean 'Even if I/you/etc. go...'

related.title: ては
related.meaning: If, With, Since, Because
related.comparison: ては often marks a condition with a negative or cautionary result, as in 無駄遣いしては貯金できない (If you waste money, you can’t save). たって means even if or no matter, used casually like ても but with a rougher tone, as in 誰に聞いたって分からない (Even if you ask anyone, they won’t know).

related.title: だって
related.meaning: I heard/was said, Because, But, Even or also, Even if or even though
related.comparison: These are two expressions that can look very similar, or even identical in some cases, as たって when used with nouns will become だって. As an overall guideline, だって is a casual expression used to express 'I hear (A)' or 'they say (A)', or as a colloquial 'because', while たって is one way to say 'even though (A)' or 'even (A)', similar to ても or でも.

related.title: にしても
related.meaning: Even though, Even if, Even when, Also
related.comparison: にしても and たって are related in that たって is a more casual form of ても, meaning 'even if'. にしても could be rephrased as にしたって, which sounds more informal and emotional. For example, どこに行くにしても ('No matter where you go') can also be expressed as どこ行くにしたって.

related.title: たとえ〜ても
related.meaning: Even if…is the case, Supposing that
related.comparison: Both express contrastive conditionals. たとえ〜ても is neutral or formal, e.g. たとえ雨に降られても気にしない 'Even if I get rained on, I won't care.' たって is casual and conversational, often used with emotional insistence, e.g. そんなこと言ったってしょうがないよ 'Even if you say that, there's nothing we can do.'

related.title: どうしても
related.meaning: Regardless of, No matter what
related.comparison: どうしても and たって are similar in that both express ideas like 'even' or 'no matter', but they are used differently. どうしても appears at the beginning of a sentence to convey 'no matter what'. For example, どうしても勝ちたい ('I want to win no matter what'). たって is a casual verb ending that functions like ても. In fact, どうしても can be expressed more colloquially as どうしたって.

related.title: たところで
related.meaning: Even if ~ cannot or will not, Even though ~ not
related.comparison: Both translate as even if, but differ in nuance. たって is a casual equivalent of ても, as in 雨が降ったって行く (I’ll go even if it rains). たところで appears in negative or futile contexts, as in 頑張ったところで無駄だ (Even if you try hard, it’s useless).

related.title: ようが～まいが
related.meaning: Even if, No matter what, Whether or not, [Emphatic], Volitional + が
related.comparison: Both show even if, but with different tone. たって is casual and conversational, equivalent to ても, as in 子どもだって分かる (Even a child would understand). ようが～まいが is formal and emphatic, often with contrasting options, as in 成功しようが失敗しようが挑戦する (Whether we succeed or fail, we’ll try).

related.title: たばかり
related.meaning: Just did, Something just happened
related.comparison: Both express recency but differ in usage. たばかり follows た-form verbs to show something just happened, e.g. 焼いたばかり 'just baked'. たて attaches to stems and means 'freshly done', e.g. 焼きたてのパン 'freshly-baked bread'.

related.title: かけ
related.meaning: Half-, Not yet finished, About to, Begin to
related.comparison: Both かけ and たて describe transitional states of actions, but with opposite timing. かけ refers to something that is incomplete or in progress, often implying interrupted action, e.g. 食べかけのパン 'half-eaten bread'. たて, by contrast, refers to something freshly completed, emphasizing newness and immediacy, e.g. 焼きたてのパン 'freshly baked bread'.

related.title: たところだ
related.meaning: Just finished doing, Was just doing
related.comparison: たところだ and たて are similar in that both refer to something that has just happened, but they differ in usage and nuance. たところだ uses the past tense of a verb and simply describes the timing, e.g. パンを焼いたところだ ('I just baked some bread.'). In contrast, たて attaches to verb stems and has the nuance of 'freshly done/finished', e.g. 焼きたてのパンが一番美味しい ('Freshly baked bread is the most delicious.').

related.title: ても
related.meaning: Even if, Even though
related.comparison: たとえ～ても is a stronger and more limited version of ても used in hypothetical situations. For example, たとえどこに行っても 'no matter where you might go' is a more emphatic version of どこに行っても 'wherever you go'. However, in cases where ても expresses permission, e.g. てもいい 'you can (A)', たとえ would not be used, since the meaning is not strongly hypothetical.

related.title: もし
related.meaning: If (For emphasis)
related.comparison: もし and たとえ are similar in that both can be used at the beginning of a sentence to emphasize a hypothetical situation or condition. The primary difference is that while もし can be followed by a wide variety of conditionals, including なら, たら, ば, and ても, たとえ is almost always followed by a ても expression, giving it the meaning of 'even if (A)' or 'even supposing that (A)'.

related.title: にしても
related.meaning: Even though, Even if, Even when, Also
related.comparison: にしても and たとえ〜ても both mean 'even if', but にしても leans toward conditions that are reasonably likely, e.g. 難しいにしてもやってみたほうがいい ('Even if it's difficult, you should give it a try'). たとえ〜ても is far more hypothetical, e.g. たとえ失敗してもあきらめない ('Even if I fail, I won't give up').

related.title: とも
related.meaning: Even if, No matter
related.comparison: Both express strong contrary hypotheticals, similar to 'Even if (A), still (B)'. たとえ〜ても is less formal and more common in speech, e.g. たとえ一人でも、ちゃんとやる 'Even if it's just me, I'll do it right.' とも is more literary and formal, and takes the volitional form, e.g. 死のうとも構わない 'Even if I die, I don't mind.'

related.title: たって
related.meaning: Even if, Even though, No matter how
related.comparison: Both express contrastive conditionals. たとえ〜ても is neutral or formal, e.g. たとえ雨に降られても気にしない 'Even if I get rained on, I won't care.' たって is casual and conversational, often used with emotional insistence, e.g. そんなこと言ったってしょうがないよ 'Even if you say that, there's nothing we can do.'

related.title: としても
related.meaning: Even if (we assume), Assuming,  Also as
related.comparison: Both express hypothetical conditions, but with different implications. たとえ〜ても presents contrast, like 'Even if (A), still (B)', e.g. たとえ雨が降っても行きます 'Even if it rains, I'll go.' としても assumes (A) as a premise for argument or stance, e.g. 行くとしても一人では行かない 'Even if I go, I won't go alone.'

related.title: もしも～なら・もしも～でも
related.slug: もしも～なら・もしも～でも
related.meaning: If, Supposing
related.comparison: Both introduce hypothetical situations, but with different nuances. たとえ〜ても sets up contrast, like 'Even if (A), still (B)', e.g. たとえ負けても悔いはない 'Even if I lose, I'll have no regrets.' もしも～なら・～でも expresses a basic hypothetical and its result, e.g. もしも彼が来たら、話します 'If he comes, I'll talk to him.'

related.title: にせよ・にしろ
related.meaning: Even if, Even though, No matter, Whether… or
related.comparison: Both show that something remains unchanged regardless of conditions. たとえ〜ても presents a contrasting hypothetical, e.g. たとえ雨に降られても気にしない 'Even if I get rained on, I won't care.' にせよ・にしろ is more concrete and neutral, often listing options, e.g. 行くにせよ行かないにせよ、連絡してほしい 'Whether you go or not, I want you to contact me.'

related.title: とか～とか
related.meaning: Among other things, For example, Such as
related.comparison: たとえば and とか～とか both introduce examples, but differ in usage. たとえば comes at the start of a sentence or clause, like 'for example'. In contrast, とか～とか is used to list multiple items and follows each noun. Because they differ in function, they can be used together in the same sentence, e.g. たとえば本とか雑誌とか ('For example, things like books, magazines, and so forth.').

related.title: るところだ
related.meaning: About to, On the verge of
related.comparison: たところだ and るところだ share the same structure, with the tense indicating whether the action is complete or not. るところだ uses the non-past form to mean 'about to do' something, e.g. 晩ご飯を食べるところだ ('I'm just about to eat dinner.'). たところだ uses the past form to mean 'just did' something, e.g. 晩ご飯を食べたところだ ('I just ate dinner.').

related.title: たとたんに
related.meaning: The instant or moment, As soon as, Just as
related.comparison: たところだ and たとたんに are similar in that both refer to something that has just happened, but with different usage and nuance. たところだ simply describes the timing, e.g. 家に帰ってきたところだ ('I just got home.'). In contrast, とたんに expresses that something else happened immediately at that moment. For example, 家に帰ってきたとたんに雨が降ってきた ('The instant I got home, it started to rain.').

related.title: たばかり
related.meaning: Just did, Something just happened
related.comparison: たところだ and たばかり are similar in that both refer to something that has just happened, but with slightly different nuances. Both 晩ご飯を食べたところです and 晩ご飯を食べたばかりです essentially mean 'I just ate dinner.', but while the version with ところ focuses solely on the timing, the version with ばかり carries a nuance of continued relevance, e.g. implying '...and that's why I'm not hungry now' or '...and so I want to rest before working out'.

related.title: たて
related.meaning: Freshly ~ed, Just ~ed
related.comparison: たところだ and たて are similar in that both refer to something that has just happened, but they differ in usage and nuance. たところだ uses the past tense of a verb and simply describes the timing, e.g. パンを焼いたところだ ('I just baked some bread.'). In contrast, たて attaches to verb stems and has the nuance of 'freshly done/finished', e.g. 焼きたてのパンが一番美味しい ('Freshly baked bread is the most delicious.').

related.title: たところだ
related.meaning: Just finished doing, Was just doing
related.comparison: たところだ and たとたんに are similar in that both refer to something that has just happened, but with different usage and nuance. たところだ simply describes the timing, e.g. 家に帰ってきたところだ ('I just got home.'). In contrast, とたんに expresses that something else happened immediately at that moment. For example, 家に帰ってきたとたんに雨が降ってきた ('The instant I got home, it started to rain.').

related.title: たばかり
related.meaning: Just did, Something just happened
related.comparison: Both refer to something that just happened, but differ in nuance. たばかり describes the state after completion, e.g. 帰ってきたばかり 'just got home'. とたんに means something happened the moment another did, e.g. 帰ってきたとたんに雨が降った ('The moment I got home, it started to rain.').

related.title: と同時に
related.meaning: At the same time as, The instant, The moment, As well as
related.comparison: Both と同時に and たとたんに describe closely linked events, but they differ in focus. と同時に emphasizes simultaneity or coordination, while たとたんに conveys a sudden, often unexpected result following a completed action. Their grammar structure also differs: と同時に follows the non-past form, while たとたんに follows the past tense.

related.title: 直ちに
related.meaning: At once, Immediately, As soon as
related.comparison: 直ちに and たとたんに both indicate swift timing, but differ in tone and structure. 直ちに is formal and emphasizes urgency, e.g. '直ちに避難してください' ('Please evacuate immediately'). たとたんに conveys something happening instantly after another event, often with surprise, e.g. '顔を見たとたんに泣き出した' ('She started crying the moment she saw his face').

related.title: 次第
related.meaning: As soon as
related.comparison: Both describe sequences where one action quickly follows another, but differ in tone and formality. 次第 is formal and indicates that an action will follow as soon as another is completed, e.g. '会議が終わり次第、報告します' ('I'll report as soon as the meeting ends'). たとたんに emphasizes sudden or unexpected results, often in conversation, e.g. 'ドアを開けたとたんに猫が飛び出した' ('The moment I opened the door, the cat jumped out').

related.title: なり
related.meaning: As soon as, The moment, When
related.comparison: Both express immediacy following another action, but differ in nuance and tone. たとたんに stresses suddenness or unexpectedness, sometimes with emotion, e.g. '立ち上がったとたんに倒れた' ('He collapsed the moment he stood up'). なり also marks instantaneousness, but feels more formal or literary, and implies a natural cause-effect link, e.g. '席に着くなり、食べ始めた' ('As soon as he sat down, he started eating').

related.title: かと思ったら・かと思うと
related.slug: かと思ったら-かと思うと
related.meaning: Then again, Just when I thought, No sooner than
related.comparison: Both express actions occurring in close sequence, but differ in focus. たとたんに emphasizes the precise moment with suddenness, e.g. 'ドアを開けたとたんに猫が飛び出した' ('The moment I opened the door, the cat jumped out'). かと思ったら・かと思うと marks a quick change with surprise, e.g. '晴れたかと思ったら、すぐに雨が降り出した' ('Just when I thought it cleared up, it started raining again').

synonyms.title: にせよ・にしろ
synonyms.meaning: Even if, Even though, No matter, Whether… or
synonyms.comparison: Both share an even if nuance. にせよ・にしろ asserts that the conclusion doesn’t change, as in 成功しないにせよ挑戦すべきだ (Even if you don’t succeed, you should try). たところで adds futility, as in 今さら謝ったところで遅い (Even if you apologize now, it’s too late).

related.title: たって
related.meaning: Even if, Even though, No matter how
related.comparison: Both translate as even if, but differ in nuance. たって is a casual equivalent of ても, as in 雨が降ったって行く (I’ll go even if it rains). たところで appears in negative or futile contexts, as in 頑張ったところで無駄だ (Even if you try hard, it’s useless).

related.title: まま(に)
related.meaning: As is, While remaining, Left in a state
related.comparison: Both expressions refer to a persisting state, but differ in nuance. With たなり・なり, there's often (but not always) an implication that the situation is undesirable or unexpected, as in 部屋に入ったなり出てこない ('Ever since going in his room, he hasn't come out'). まま(に) also marks an unchanged state, e.g. 窓が開けたままになっている ('The window is still open'), but is more neutral in tone.

related.title: きり
related.meaning: Only, Just, Since
