related.comparison: たなり・なり and きり are very similar in meaning, both expressing that a certain state persists without the expected result occurring, and are often interchangeable. For example, in '東京に行ったきり地元に帰って来なかった' ('Once he went to Tokyo, he never came back to his hometown') and '東京に行ったなり地元に帰って来なかった' both convey the same lingering, unresolved situation.

related.title: ごとに
related.meaning: Each, Every
related.comparison: ごとに and たびに both express frequency, and can be interchangeable in cases like 失敗するたびに／ごとに 'every time (you) fail'. However, ごとに can directly follow time or distance expressions (e.g. ３時間ごとに), while たびに requires a verb indicating an action or event, e.g. ３時間が経つたびに 'every time 3 hours pass'. Their noun forms also differ, e.g. 発表の度に vs. 発表ごとに (both meaning 'with every announcement').

related.title: おきに
related.meaning: Repeated at intervals, Every other, Every, At intervals of
related.comparison: Both おきに and たびに describe repeated occurrences, but are used differently. おきに attaches to time expressions to mean 'at intervals of (A)', e.g. '５分おきに電車が来る' ('Trains arrive every five minutes'). たびに describes a response that recurs whenever a particular thing happens, e.g. '見るたびに笑ってしまう' ('I end up laughing every time I see it').

related.title: につけ
related.meaning: Whenever, Every time something happens
related.comparison: These are similar expressions that both express the idea that every time one thing happens, so does something else. While they are interchangeable in some cases, につけ can only be used for naturally occurring events, e.g. '見るにつけ笑ってしまう' ('I laugh every time I see it'). たびに can also be used for volitional actions, e.g. '出張に行くたびにお土産を買う' ('I buy souvenirs every time I go on a business trip').

related.title: まい～のように
related.meaning: Almost every ~, Nearly every ~, On a ~ basis
related.comparison: Both まい～のように and たびに express frequency and the idea of 'every,' but with different nuances. まい～のように is used with 'every~' time expressions to mean 'almost every (A)', e.g. 毎日のように 'almost every day'. In contrast, たびに follows verbs or noun phrases to mean 'every time (A)', e.g. 彼に会うたびに 'every time I see him' or 旅行のたびに 'every time I take a trip'.

related.title: たて
related.meaning: Freshly ~ed, Just ~ed
related.comparison: Both express recency but differ in usage. たばかり follows た-form verbs to show something just happened, e.g. 焼いたばかり 'just baked'. たて attaches to stems and means 'freshly done', e.g. 焼きたてのパン 'freshly-baked bread'.

related.title: たとたんに
related.meaning: The instant or moment, As soon as, Just as
related.comparison: Both refer to something that just happened, but differ in nuance. たばかり describes the state after completion, e.g. 帰ってきたばかり 'just got home'. とたんに means something happened the moment another did, e.g. 帰ってきたとたんに雨が降った ('The moment I got home, it started to rain.').

related.title: たところだ
related.meaning: Just finished doing, Was just doing
related.comparison: たところだ and たばかり are similar in that both refer to something that has just happened, but with slightly different nuances. Both 晩ご飯を食べたところです and 晩ご飯を食べたばかりです essentially mean 'I just ate dinner.', but while the version with ところ focuses solely on the timing, the version with ばかり carries a nuance of continued relevance, e.g. implying '...and that's why I'm not hungry now' or '...and so I want to rest before working out'.

synonyms.title: ことだ
synonyms.comparison: たほうがいい is a common expression used to give advice or make suggestions, often reflecting the speaker's personal opinion on what would be better. ことだ may appear similar but is more formal, carrying a stronger nuance of expectation, implying 'this is how things are (or should be,' rather than a personal suggestion.

synonyms.title: ばいい
synonyms.meaning: Can, Should, It'd be good if
synonyms.comparison: Both たほうがいい and ばいい are used to give suggestions. たほうがいい is a straightforward 'You should…', while ばいい adds a nuance of 'All you have to do is...' or 'Why don't you just...?' Depending on context, ばいい can sound more friendly, but it can also sound a bit pushy. Unlike たほうがいい, ばいい is commonly to be paired with のに to express 'It would be good if (A), but (A) isn't possible).'

synonyms.title: べき
synonyms.comparison: たほうがいい is used to give advice or make a suggestion, often expressing the speaker's subjective opinion on what they believe would be better. It's commonly used in everyday conversation. べき has a similar meaning, but is more formal and has a stronger nuance of expectation about what 'should' be done, rather than a personal opinion.

antonyms.title: ないほうがいい
antonyms.meaning: It'd be better not to, Shouldn't do
antonyms.comparison: たほうがいい and ないほうがいい are common expressions used to make suggestions, with opposite meanings. たほうがいい expresses a positive suggestion ('You should'), while ないほうがいい is a negative one, ('You shouldn't'). Both are used in everyday conversation, and often reflect the speaker's personal opinion about the best course of action.

antonyms.title: べきではない
antonyms.meaning: Should not, Must not , Ought not to
antonyms.comparison: たほうがいい is used to give advice or make a suggestion, often expressing the speaker's subjective opinion on what they believe would be better. It's commonly used in everyday conversation. In contrast, べきではない has the opposite meaning and is more formal. It conveys an expectation or rule about what 'should not' be done, rather than a personal opinion.

related.title: かた
related.meaning: Way of ~ing, How to ~, Manner of ~ing
related.comparison: かた and たほうがいい are distinct grammar patterns both containing 方 (which can be read as かた or ほう) but have different meanings and structures. かた attaches to the verb stem and means 'way of doing (A)' or 'how to (A)', e.g. 書き方 'way of writing' or 'how to write'. In contrast, たほうがいい connects to the past tense of a verb and is used to give advice or make suggestions.

related.title: より～のほうが
related.meaning: To be more ~ than ~
related.comparison: These are two common expressions using ほう ('alternative'). より～のほうが is used for comparisons—for example, XよりYのほうが means 'Y is more... than X.' In contrast, たほうがいい is used to give advice or suggestions. 野菜を食べたほうがいい literally means 'It would be better if you ate vegetables,' but carries the nuance of 'You should eat vegetables.'

related.title: たらいい・といい
related.meaning: It would be nice if, It would be good if, Should, I hope
related.comparison: たほうがいい is a common expression used to give advice or make suggestions, often reflecting the speaker's personal opinion on what would be better. たらいい・といい carry similar meanings, but often expresses the speaker's wish for something to happen, similar to 'I hope' or 'It'd be nice if…' Because of this, only たらいい・といい can be used with things out of a person's control.

related.title: たらどう
related.meaning: Why don't you...?, What if you did...?
related.comparison: Both たらどう and たほうがいい can be used to give advice or suggestions. The key difference is that たほうがいい has a strong nuance that the speaker is advising the listener on what to do or what would be the best course of action ('You should...'), while たらどう is more gentle, as if suggesting a potential option but not strongly insisting on it ('How about if you...?')

synonyms.title: ～るまでだ
synonyms.comparison: These are essentially the same grammar pattern, with the difference in nuance coming from the verb tense. ～たまでだ is typically used to downplay a completed action, e.g. 自分の意見を述べたまでだ ('All I did was express my opinion.), while ～るまでだ generally implies the speaker will 'just do (something)' in the face of a bad situation, e.g. 誰も教えてくれないなら、自分で調べるまでだ ('If no one will teach me, I'll just look it up myself'). Both are typically used to refer to one's own actions or those of someone in one's in-group.

related.title: それまでだ
related.meaning: If… then it is all in vain, It's done, It is useless, No point, It is a waste, That's it, It is all gone,
related.comparison: Both それまでだ and たまでだ use までだ to mark a limit, but the type of limit is different. それまでだ expresses a harsh evaluation meaning “that’s all there is” or “that’s the end,” often used to judge that nothing more can be done if a condition is lost, as in 信頼を失ったらそれまでだ (‘If you lose trust, that’s the end.’). By contrast, たまでだ uses the past tense to downplay an action and means “I only did (A),” emphasizing that the action was small or not worth blaming, as in 俺はただ自分の意見を言ったまでだ (‘I only expressed my opinion.’) or 困っていたから助けたまでだ (‘I just helped because she looked troubled.’).

antonyms.title: な
antonyms.comparison: The dictionary/non-past form of a verb + な expresses a blunt command not to do something. In contrast, たまえ expresses a strong, authoritative command to do something. For example, 食べるな！ means 'Don't eat it!' while 食べたまえ means 'I command you: eat it!' な is more casual than the formal たまえ, but both are used toward someone of lower status whom you have power over.

related.title: のに
related.meaning: In order to
related.comparison: Both can mean 'in order to' when expressing purpose and are often interchangeable in that usage, e.g. 健康維持のために運動する / 健康を維持するのに運動する ('I exercise in order to maintain my health.') However, ために can also express cause in certain contexts, which のに cannot, e.g. 雨のために試合が中止になった ('The game was canceled due to rain.')

related.title: そのため(に)
related.meaning: For that reason, Because of that, To that end
related.comparison: These share the same core construction, using ため(に) to express 'in order to (A)' or 'due to (A)'. ため(に) is used within a sentence after a noun, verb, or adjective phrase. そのため is used at the beginning of a sentence to mean 'for that reason' or 'due to that', referring back to what was just said.

related.title: わけだ
related.meaning: For that reason, No wonder, As you'd expect, Naturally, Thus, This is why
related.comparison: わけだ and ため(に) are similar in that both can be used when expressing a reason, but they mean quite different things. わけだ is used when drawing a conclusion, like the English 'so that's why (A)', while ため(に) on the other hand means 'for the sake of (A)' or 'due to (A)'.

related.title: 向き
related.meaning: Suitable for, Facing
related.comparison: Both can be translated as 'for (A)', but differ in nuance. 向き attaches directly to a noun and suggests suitability, e.g. 子ども向きの本 ('A book suitable for children') ため(に) connects to nouns with の and suggests intended benefit or purpose, e.g. 子どものための本 ('A book for children [designed to benefit them]')

related.title: んがため(に)
related.meaning: In order to, To, For the purpose of
related.comparison: んがため(に) can be considered an old-fashioned, literary form of ため(に). While both can express the idea of 'for the purpose of (A)' or 'in order to (A)', for example 生きんがために食べる 'eat (in order) to live' sounds like a much more formal and poetic version of 生きるために食べる (same meaning).

related.title: には
related.meaning: In order to, In regard to, For
related.comparison: Both can express purpose, similar to 'in order to' or 'for that reason', e.g. 海外に行くためにパスポートを取った ('I got a passport in order to go abroad.') 海外に行くにはパスポートが必要だ ('A passport is necessary to go abroad.') Unlike ため(に), には also has broader uses, such as marking scope or perspective, e.g. 子どもには難しい ('It's difficult for children.')

related.title: によって・による
related.meaning: By means of, According to, Depending on, Because of, Due to
related.comparison: Both can express cause, but with different perspectives. ために often reflects the speaker's point of view, e.g. 試合は台風のために中止になった ('The game was canceled in light of the typhoon.') によって typically conveys an external or objective cause, e.g. 台風によって多くの家が壊れた ('Many houses were damaged due to the typhoon.')

related.title: ゆえに
related.meaning: Because, The reason is, Due to, Therefore, So
related.comparison: Both relate cause and result, but their structures are different. ゆえに can be used as a sentence-level connector meaning ‘therefore’, drawing a conclusion a stated reason, as in 予算が不足している。ゆえに延期した (‘The budget was insufficient; therefore, it was postponed.’). ために is a grammatical form meaning ‘because of' or 'due to’ comes within a sentence to similarly express purpose, e.g. 雨のために試合が中止になった (‘The game was canceled due to the rain.’).

related.title: せいで
related.meaning: Because of, Due to, Blame
related.comparison: ために and せいで can both mean 'due to (A)', but with a difference in tone. せいで almost always implies a negative or undesirable result. ために can be used with either positive or negative nuance. Also, ために can mean 'for the purpose of (A)', which せいで cannot.

related.title: おかげで
related.meaning: Thanks to, Because of
related.comparison: ために and せいで can both express the idea of 'due to (A)', but they differ in that おかげで almost always describes a desirable result, while ために can be used with either a positive or negative nuance. ために can also, in some cases, mean 'for the purpose of (A)', while おかげで cannot be used in this way.

related.title: そのため(に)
related.meaning: For that reason, Because of that, To that end
related.comparison: そのため(に) can be seen as a more specific version of ために. ために follows a noun, verb, or adjective phrase to express purpose or reason. そのため(に) appears at the beginning of a sentence and means 'due to that' or 'in order to do that', referring back to what was previously said.

related.title: につき
related.meaning: Due to, On account of, Each or every
related.comparison: Both ために and につき can express 'due to (A)', but differ in tone and usage. ために requires の to connect to nouns, e.g. 悪天候のため ('Due to bad weather') につき connects directly and sounds stiffer or more formal, e.g. 悪天候につき (Same meaning) Unlike ために, につき cannot be used to express purpose.

related.title: その結果
related.meaning: As a result
related.comparison: ために and その結果 both express a cause/effect relationship, but in different ways. ために is used within a sentence following a noun, verb, or adjective, and can mean either 'due to (A)' or 'in order to (A)'. その結果 comes at the beginning of a sentence and always means 'as a result of that', referring back to the previous statement.

related.title: にあって
related.meaning: At, In the (place or situation), Under ~ conditions or circumstances, During, Suited to
related.comparison: Both express reason or cause, but with different constraints. ために connects to nouns with の and means 'due to (A)' or 'for the purpose of (A)'. にあって attaches directly to a noun and is limited to situations, positions, or conditions, e.g. 非常時にあって ('in a state of emergency') It cannot be used with general nouns the way ために can.

related.title: たことがある
related.meaning: Have done before
related.comparison: Both expressions describe past experiences but with different nuances. たことがある states that something has happened before, as in 日本に行ったことがある ('I've been to Japan'). たものだ adds a nostalgic tone and refers to things done regularly in the past, as in 子どもの頃、よくこの公園で遊んだものだ ('I often played at this park when I was a child').

antonyms.title: ないほうがいい
antonyms.meaning: It'd be better not to, Shouldn't do
antonyms.comparison: ないほうがいい is used to advise against doing something, similar to 'You shouldn't…' in English. たらいい and といい can also be used to give advice, but they often express the speaker's wish or hope for something to happen, similar to 'I hope' or 'It'd be nice if…'. Unlike ないほうがいい, they can be used for situations outside a person's control.

related.title: ばいい
related.meaning: Can, Should, It'd be good if
related.comparison: All three expressions share the base meaning of 'it would be good if (A)' and can be used to express wishes or give suggestions. たらいい・といい tend to sound stronger or more assertive than ばいい, which feels lighter or less serious in tone. While often interchangeable, in questions like どうすればいい？・どうしたらいい？ ('What should I do?'), といい is not usable.

related.title: たらどう
related.meaning: Why don't you...?, What if you did...?
related.comparison: たらどう is a gentle, open-ended suggestion, e.g. 休んだらどう？('Why don't you take a break?'). たらいい・といい can also suggest, but sounds more like advice, e.g. 休んだらいいよ ('You should take a break.'). It can also express hope, like 雨が止むといいね = 'I hope the rain stops.'.

related.title: といい
related.meaning: I hope, I wish, You should, It would be good
related.comparison: Both といい and たらいい can express 'if you do (A), it would be good'. but differ in nuance. たら is less certain, often used for things that may or may not happen, e.g. 'It would be nice if (A).'. と is more definite and can imply hope, e.g. 'I hope (A).'. Depending on the context, といい can also suggest 'You should (A).'.

related.title: たほうがいい
related.meaning: It'd be better to, Should do
related.comparison: たほうがいい is a common expression used to give advice or make suggestions, often reflecting the speaker's personal opinion on what would be better. たらいい・といい carry similar meanings, but often expresses the speaker's wish for something to happen, similar to 'I hope' or 'It'd be nice if…' Because of this, only たらいい・といい can be used with things out of a person's control.

related.title: はずだ
related.meaning: Bound to (be), Expected to (be), Should be (able)
related.comparison: はずだ expresses confident expectation, as in 雨が降るはずだ ('It should rain.'). たらいい and といい express hope or desire without implying it will happen, e.g. 雨が降るといい ('I hope it rains.'). The difference lies in expectation vs. wish. Thus, といい is often used even when the speaker is not confident the thing will happen.

related.title: ばよかった
related.meaning: Should have, I wish, It would have been better to
related.comparison: ばよかった and たらいい・といい are similar in that both express the speaker's wish or hope. ばよかった is used when expressing regret over something that happened (or didn't happen) in the past, e.g. 行けばよかった ('I wish I had gone.') which implies 'but I didn't'. In contrast, たらいい・といい typically expresses something the speaker hopes will happen in the future, e.g. 明日晴れるといいけど ('I hope it's sunny tomorrow.').

related.title: と
related.meaning: If, When, Whenever, And, [Conditional]
related.comparison: たら and と are both conditional forms, but they differ in nuance and usage. たら expresses both 'if' and 'when', and can describe hypotheticals or one-time events, such as 雨が降ったら行きません ('If it rains, I won't go.'). と is used for regular or expected outcomes, as in 春になると桜が咲く ('When spring comes, the cherry blossoms bloom.').

related.title: もし
related.meaning: If (For emphasis)
related.comparison: もし and たら are often used together to form conditional ('if') sentences. もし typically comes at the start of a sentence to emphasize the hypothetical nature of what follows, while たら gives the verb its conditional form. For example, もし宝くじが当たったら車を買おうと思います means 'If I win the lottery, I think I'll buy a car.'.

related.title: あとで
related.meaning: After, Later
related.comparison: Both あとで and たら can show that one action follows another, but their usage and nuance differ. あとで simply shows sequence, like 'after doing (A)'. たら adds a conditional nuance and can mean 'when' (temporal) or 'if' (hypothetical). Because of this, they are rarely interchangeable.

related.title: ば
related.meaning: If… then, (Conditional)
related.comparison: ば and たら are both conditional forms, but with different nuances. ば emphasizes the condition itself, often with an 'if and only if' feel, e.g. 田中さんが来てくれれば何とかなる ('If Tanaka comes, it'll work out.'). たら sets up an hypothetical condition, which can be followed by a result, comment, or request, e.g. 田中さんが来てくれたら教えてね ('If/when Tanaka comes, let me know.').

related.title: 上で
related.meaning: After, Upon
related.comparison: Both 上で and たら can describe sequences or conditions, but with different nuance. 上で implies that one action is a prerequisite for another, such as 社長と相談した上で決めましょう ('Let's decide after discussing it with the president.'). たら is more general and covers a wider range of temporal and conditional uses, as in 晴れたら散歩に行こう ('If it's sunny, let's go for a walk.').

related.title: なら
related.meaning: Were it, If, If it's the case, As for (Conditional)
related.comparison: なら and たら are both conditional expressions but differ in nuance and usage. なら means 'if it's the case that (A)', often implying the speaker believes it will happen, e.g. 来るなら 'if it's the case that (they) come'. たら covers the ideas of both 'if' and 'when', expressing both hypothetical conditions or guaranteed events, e.g. 駅に着いたら電話してください ('Call me when/if you arrive at the station.').

related.title: それなら
related.meaning: Then, If that's the case
related.comparison: それなら expresses a response to a condition, as in それなら行きません (In that case, I won’t go). The conditional たら form can also mark conditions at the end of verbs, adjectives, or nouns. それだったら can convey a similar meaning to それなら, but with a slight nuance: それなら assumes the condition will or has happened, while それだったら tends to be more hypothetical.3

related.title: たらどう
related.meaning: Why don't you...?, What if you did...?
related.comparison: たら expresses a condition or hypothetical, and when combined with どう, forms a casual suggestion. For example, 散歩に行ったらどう？ means 'How about going for a walk?'. Similar to the English phrase 'Why don't you (A)?' or 'How about (A)?', it isis common in informal conversation.

related.title: てから
related.meaning: Once...is done, Once...happens, After doing
related.comparison: てから and たら both convey a sequence of events, but their nuances differ. てから simply indicates that one event follows another. In contrast, たら is similar to the English 'when' or 'if' and suggests that the second event is a result of the first. たら can be used in both hypothetical situations ('if A, then B') and past events ('When I did A, B happened').

related.title: ませんか
related.meaning: Won't you, Would you not, Why don't we
related.comparison: ませんか is a polite way to extend an invitation, similar to 'Won't you…?' or 'Wouldn't you like to...?' in English. In contrast, たらどう has a stronger tone and is used to suggest or advise a course of action. For example, 何か食べませんか？ is a polite offer (e.g. to a guest), while 何か食べたらどう？ implies the speaker thinks the listener should eat something.

related.title: たらいい・といい
related.meaning: It would be nice if, It would be good if, Should, I hope
related.comparison: たらどう is a gentle, open-ended suggestion, e.g. 休んだらどう？('Why don't you take a break?'). たらいい・といい can also suggest, but sounds more like advice, e.g. 休んだらいいよ ('You should take a break.'). It can also express hope, like 雨が止むといいね = 'I hope the rain stops.'.

related.title: たら
related.meaning: When, From when, Just when, If
related.comparison: たら expresses a condition or hypothetical, and when combined with どう, forms a casual suggestion. For example, 散歩に行ったらどう？ means 'How about going for a walk?'. Similar to the English phrase 'Why don't you (A)?' or 'How about (A)?', it isis common in informal conversation.

related.title: 命令形
related.meaning: (Verb)!, Do (something)!
related.comparison: The imperative or command form  (命令形) is used for blunt orders, e.g. 止まれ ('Stop!'), which can be harsh. In contrast, たらどう is a soft suggestion, similar to 'Why don't you (A)?' in English, e.g. 行ってみたらどう？ ('Why don't you try going?'). It gives the listener more freedom and therefore sounds more gentle.

related.title: たほうがいい
related.meaning: It'd be better to, Should do
related.comparison: Both たらどう and たほうがいい can be used to give advice or suggestions. The key difference is that たほうがいい has a strong nuance that the speaker is advising the listener on what to do or what would be the best course of action ('You should...'), while たらどう is more gentle, as if suggesting a potential option but not strongly insisting on it ('How about if you...?')

related.title: 以来
related.meaning: Since, Henceforth
related.comparison: Both mark a point after which something changes, but the meaning and outlook are very different. たら最後 expresses a strong, often negative inevitability, meaning “once (A) happens, that’s it,” and implies that things will go badly or cannot be undone, as in 一度言ったら最後、もう取り消せない (‘Once you say it, that’s it, you can’t take it back.’). By contrast, 以来 is a neutral, factual expression meaning “since,” used to state that a situation has continued from a certain point in the past without emotional judgment, as in 日本に来て以来、日本語を勉強している (‘I’ve been studying Japanese since I came to Japan.’).

related.title: とか～とか
related.meaning: Among other things, For example, Such as
related.comparison: Both たり～たりする and とか～とか are used to give examples of actions, similar to the English '(doing) things like A and B.' The key difference is how they attach to words. たり is made by adding り to the た (past tense) of a verb, while とか follows the dictionary form. Also, とか can follow nouns directly, while たり requires だ to become だったり.

related.title: やら～やら
related.meaning: Whether or, Things like, And … so on
related.comparison: Both たり～たりする and やら～やら list examples of actions, like saying 'doing things like A and B' in English. たり is more neutral, while やら adds a nuance of uncertainty or a wider, less-defined range, suggesting the speaker is listing just some of many possible actions or things.

related.title: ～なり～なり
related.meaning: Or, Either or
related.comparison: Both たり～たりする and ～なり～なり list example actions, like 'doing things like A and B.' たり is a neutral, commonly used pattern in casual speech. In contrast, なり～なり is more formal and carries a stronger nuance that the examples are just possibilities, often implying that the speaker doesn't care which one is chosen.

related.title: や
related.meaning: Things like, And the like
related.comparison: Both たり～たりする and や can be used to provide or list examples, similar to the English 'things like A and B.' The main difference is that たり can only be used with verbs, adjectives, or noun phrases, while や can only be used to list nouns. Note that both grammar points suggest that the speaker is listing just some of many possible actions or things.

related.title: である
related.meaning: Formal or literary だ, Authoritative, Copula
related.comparison: Both are formal forms of 'to be' used in writing. である is the standard formal equivalent of だ, e.g. 彼は学生である ('He is a student.') たる emphasizes qualification or the qualities appropriate to a role, e.g. 指導者たる者 ('A person worthy of being a leader')

related.title: としては
related.meaning: As a, For a, As for
related.comparison: Both relate to identity or role. たる is classical and formal, emphasizing the qualities expected of that role, as in リーダーたる者は責任感が必要だ (A leader must have responsibility). としては emphasizes perspective from a role or position, as in 教師としては当然だ (As a teacher, it’s natural).

related.title: として
related.meaning: As (i.e. in the role of), For
related.comparison: たる and として both mean 'as (A)' or 'in the role of (A)', but たる is formal and often used when describing what someone in that position should be or do, e.g. 指導者たる者 ('a person in the role of leader'). として is more neutral and can be found in everyday speech, e.g. 学生として ('as a student').

related.title: に足る
related.meaning: To be worthy of, To be enough, To be sufficient
related.comparison: While these two grammar points appear similar in form, they differ in origin and are used quite differently. たる primarily follows nouns and most often occurs in patterns such as リーダーたる者 'as a leader,' to express qualities or characteristics befitting someone in a certain role. In contrast に足る, can follow nouns or verbs to express the idea of being sufficient or worthy of something, e.g. 信頼に足る友人 'a trustworthy friend' or 語るに足りる発明 'an invention worthy of discussion'.

synonyms.title: です
synonyms.comparison: だ and です both function like the English 'is.' The main difference is politeness: だ is casual and used in plain speech, while です is polite. だ can't be used after い-adjectives, but です can, acting as a general politeness marker. You may still see だ in set expressions like ～だと思います, even in polite sentences.

synonyms.title: である
synonyms.meaning: Formal or literary だ, Authoritative, Copula
synonyms.comparison: The main difference between だ and である is formality. だ is casual and common in conversation, while である is more formal and mainly used in writing. Both だ and である follow nouns or noun phrases, but である has a more literary tone. である slight differs from the verb ある (to exist) in that である is a fixed connector.

synonyms.title: でございます
synonyms.comparison: だ conveys a casual, informal, or blunt tone, except when required by certain grammatical patterns. In contrast, でございます is a highly polite form, primarily used in business settings or when addressing someone of higher social status such as a boss or customer.

antonyms.title: じゃない
antonyms.comparison: だ follows nouns and な-adjectives, with a meaning similar to the English 'is.' For instance, これはペンだ translates to 'This is a pen.' On the other hand, じゃない (a contraction of ではない) is used to create the negative of such expressions. For example, これはペンじゃない translates to 'This isn't a pen.'

related.title: がある
related.meaning: To be, There is
related.comparison: がある indicates the existence or possession of something. For example, ペンがある means 'There is a pen (on the table, etc.)' or 'I have a pen.' On the other hand, だ expresses assertion or determination similar to 'is/are' in English. In contrast to the がある example, ペンだ would mean 'It (=the thing we are looking at) is a pen.'

related.title: よ
related.meaning: Emphasis (Sentence ending particle)
related.comparison: よ is a common sentence-ending particle used to add emphasis, often indicating the speaker is sharing new information (e.g., 面白いよ = 'It's interesting, I'm telling you!'). だ is a grammatical form expressing assertion, similar to 'is' or 'am.' These two are often combined, especially in casual or masculine speech (元気だよ = 'I'm feeling good!'). In softer or more feminine speech, the だ is frequently omitted (e.g., 元気よ).

related.title: だった・でした
related.meaning: Was, Were (Past tense)
related.comparison: だった・でした and だ are all used to assert or identify something. The difference among them is in formality and tense. だ is casual and present tense (similar to the English 'is'/'am'), while だった・でした is past tense ('was'), with だった being casual (the past tense of だ) and でした being polite (the past tense of です).

related.title: あげく
related.meaning: After, In the end, After all, Eventually
related.comparison: Both た末・の末 and あげく convey the end of a process, but with subtle differences. た末・の末 often implies a significant sequence of events or actions over an extended period leading to the final result, as in 長い議論の末、結論に達した (After long discussion, we reached a conclusion). あげく, in contrast, is often used when the outcome is disappointing or problematic, as in 迷ったあげく、遅刻してしまった (After much hesitation, I ended up being late).

related.title: 上で
related.meaning: After, Upon
related.comparison: Both can express the idea of 'after doing (A), (B)', but 上で focuses on process or sequence (lit. 'upon' doing), e.g. 話し合った上で決めましょう ('Let's decide after discussing it.'). In contrast, た末・の末 emphasizes an outcome after prolonged effort or struggle, e.g. 悩んだ末に転職した ('After much deliberation, I changed jobs.').

synonyms.title: だけのことはある
synonyms.meaning: No wonder, Not surprising that, As expected
synonyms.comparison: Both express results in line with expectations. だけのことはある is sentence-final and expresses admiration, as in 優勝するなんて、さすが努力しただけのことはある (Winning is just what you’d expect from his effort). だけあって is followed by a phrase stating the result, as in 彼は有名人だけあって態度が堂々としている (As expected of a celebrity, his manner is confident).

related.title: だけに
related.meaning: As might be expected, Because, All the more because, It's only natural
related.comparison: Both mean as might be expected. だけあって usually leads to a positive evaluation, as in 有名なだけあって味は素晴らしい (As expected of a famous restaurant, the taste is excellent). だけに can be positive or negative, often stressing proportionality, as in 苦労しただけに喜びも大きい (Because I struggled, the joy is even greater).

related.title: だけで
related.meaning: Just by, Just with
related.comparison: だけあって and だけで may look similar, but they have distinct meanings. だけあって is used when a result is seen as natural or expected, similar to 'as you'd expect from (A)'. This is close in nuance to a separate expression, だけに. In contrast, だけで expresses that something is 'enough' to accomplish something else, like 'just by (A)' or 'only with (A)' in English.

synonyms.title: おおよそ
synonyms.comparison: Both can mean 'most', as in だいたいの人／おおよその人 'most people'. They can also both mean 'approximately', e.g. だいたい３時間／おおよそ３時間 'about three hours'. However, おおよそ is much more formal and mainly used in writing. だいたい can also express 'in the first place', e.g. だいたいなんでいるの？ ('Why are you here in the first place?)' or 'roughly/adequately', e.g. だいたいでいいから ('Roughly is fine.), while おおよそ cannot.

related.title: おおよそ
related.meaning: Approximately, Roughly, About, Outline, Gist
related.comparison: These can be used interchangeably to express roughly or about. だいたい is more casual and common in daily speech, as in だいたい百人来た (About a hundred people came). おおよそ sounds slightly more formal or written, as in おおよそ見当がつく (I can roughly guess). Note that だいたい can also appear as a negative interjection, as in だいたい何でいるの？ (Why are you here in the first place?), while おおよそ cannot be used this way.3

related.title: ほとんど
related.meaning: Almost all, Most, Hardly any, Few (Adverbial noun)
related.comparison: Both だいたい and ほとんど can express 'most' or 'almost all,' but they also have distinct uses. For example, だいたいの人 and ほとんどの人 both mean 'most people', and だいたい一緒 and ほとんど一緒 both mean 'almost the same'. However, they diverge when ほとんど is used negatively as in ほとんどない 'almost none', as well as when だいたい means 'generally' or 'approximately' (だいたい５時に起きる), as ほとんど cannot be used here.

synonyms.title: が
synonyms.comparison: The particle が in both patterns expresses contrast, similar to 'but' in English, or sometimes indicates a transition to additional information (like 'and'). The difference is that が follows directly after an い-Adjective or verb, while it requires だ or です after a な-Adjective or noun. が is somewhat formal and often used in writing or formal speech.

related.title: それでも
related.meaning: But still, And yet, Even so, Nevertheless
related.comparison: Both それでも and だが・ですが express contrast, similar to 'but' or 'however' in English, but with different nuances. それでも highlights a result or situation that goes against expectations. だが・ですが is more neutral and often used to transition to a contrasting point. Structurally, だが・ですが can appear mid-sentence or at the end of a clause, while それでも typically begins a sentence.

related.title: けど・だけど
related.meaning: But, However
related.comparison: Both けど・だけど (and the polite ですけど), as well as だが・ですが, express contrast, similar to 'but,' or sometimes just indicate a transition (like 'and'). The key difference is that だが is more formal and used in written contexts, while けど is more casual. Both can be used at the end of a sentence to imply something is left unsaid.

related.title: ところが
related.meaning: Even so, However, Even though, But
related.comparison: Both だが・ですが and ところが express contrast, but with different nuances. だが・ですが is a neutral transition similar to 'but' or 'however,' and can appear in various positions within a sentence. ところが usually comes at the start of a sentence and emphasizes an unexpected or surprising result, often appearing in storytelling or narration.

related.title: けれども
related.meaning: But, Although
related.comparison: Both of these are linking phrases used to express contrast in Japanese. だが and ですが sound a bit stiffer than けれども or the more casual けど, and must follow a noun or な-adjective (in contrast, けれども would become だけれども in this case). Besides showing contrast, both が and けれども (or けれど・けど) can also be used to add background information without directly meaning 'but'.

synonyms.title: だけしか
synonyms.comparison: だけ conveys the meaning of 'only' or 'just.' だけしか is a more emphatic expression, comparable to the English 'nothing but.' In addition to the increased emphasis, だけしか has more limited usage. While だけ can be used after nouns, verbs, and adjectives, だけしか is typically used only after nouns and is almost always followed by a negative verb (similar to the base しか～ない pattern).

synonyms.title: しか～ない
synonyms.comparison: だけ conveys 'only' or 'just.' しか～ない combines the particle しか with a negative verb to express 'nothing but.' While the two have similar meanings, their structures differ. For example, the equivalent of バナナだけ食べた ('I ate only a banana') would be バナナしか食べなかった ('I ate nothing but a banana'). Compared to だけ, しか～ない emphasizes the limitation or lack of other options more strongly.

antonyms.title: のみならず
antonyms.meaning: Not only...but, Not just...but, What is more
antonyms.comparison: だけ conveys 'only' or 'just.' In contrast, のみならず is a formal expression similar to だけじゃなく, meaning 'not only A, but also B.' While だけ expresses limitation, のみならず emphasizes a lack of restriction. Note that while だけ is commonly used in both everyday speech and writing, のみならず is more formal and typically limited to written language or formal speech.

antonyms.title: だけでなく
antonyms.comparison: だけ conveys the meaning of 'only' or 'just'. だけでなく (or variant forms like だけではなく, だけじゃなくて, etc.) is a negation of this, combining だけ with で (the conjuctive form of だ) and なく (the conjunctive form of ない) to express the meaning of ‘not only (A)...but also (B)’. Unlike だけ, だけでなく will always be followed by a continuation of the sentence providing additional information, e.g. バナナだけでなく、リンゴも食べました (‘I ate not only a banana, but also an apple.’).

related.title: ばかり
related.meaning: Just, Only, Nothing but
related.comparison: Both ばかり and だけ express limitation, similar to 'only,' but with different nuances. 漫画だけ読んだ means '(He) read only comic books,' while 漫画ばかり読んだ implies '(He) read nothing but comic books,' with a suggestion that other reading would be better. だけ connects to the dictionary form, while ばかり connects to the verb's て form.

related.title: きり
related.meaning: Only, Just, Since
related.comparison: だけ means 'only' or 'just,' while きり has a more specific nuance. After a past tense verb, きり suggests a permanent change after an event. For example, 日本に行ったきり、戻ってこなかった means 'Once (he) went to Japan, he never returned.' When used after a number + counter (e.g. 一人きり), it emphasizes 'nothing more, nothing less.'

related.title: だけで
related.meaning: Just by, Just with
related.comparison: だけ conveys the meaning of 'only' or 'just.' だけで is the combination of だけ with the particle で, conveying the idea of the manner or means by which something is done. だけで expresses 'just by' or 'merely with' (literally, 'with/using only A'). It typically follows either nouns or verbs, suggesting that the thing or action was the only thing needed to achieve a certain result.

synonyms.title: だけ
synonyms.comparison: だけ conveys the meaning of 'only' or 'just.' だけしか is a more emphatic expression, comparable to the English 'nothing but.' In addition to the increased emphasis, だけしか has more limited usage. While だけ can be used after nouns, verbs, and adjectives, だけしか is typically used only after nouns and is almost always followed by a negative verb (similar to the base しか～ない pattern).

related.title: しか～ない
related.meaning: There's only, Nothing but
related.comparison: だけしか is essentially an emphatic version of しか～ない, combining だけ 'only' and しか～ない 'nothing but' to strongly emphasize that there is nothing else. For example, １００円だけしかない ('I only have / have nothing but 100 yen.) is a more emphatic version of 100円しかない (same meaning), and 日本語だけしか話せない ('I can only speak / can speak nothing but Japanese.') is a more emphatic version of 日本語しか話せない (same meaning).

related.title: ばかり
related.meaning: Just, Only, Nothing but
related.comparison: ばかり and だけしか are similar in that both can express limitation, but ばかり emphasizes the idea of excessiveness of a single thing, e.g. 漫画ばかり読む 'do nothing but read manga', while だけしか emphatically stresses insufficiency, e.g. 千円だけしかない ('I have only 1,000 yen.').

related.title: きり
related.meaning: Only, Just, Since
related.comparison: きり and だけしか both express limitation, but with different nuance. きり often implies that something has happened only once or hasn't changed since, e.g. 去年会ったきり一度も連絡が来ない 'I haven't heard from them even once since we met last year.' だけしか means 'Nothing but', typically in a way that emphasizes lack or even disappointment, e.g. 千円だけしか持っていない 'I only have 1000 yen.'

related.title: だけ
related.meaning: Only, Just
related.comparison: だけ conveys the meaning of 'only' or 'just.' だけで is the combination of だけ with the particle で, conveying the idea of the manner or means by which something is done. だけで expresses 'just by' or 'merely with' (literally, 'with/using only A'). It typically follows either nouns or verbs, suggesting that the thing or action was the only thing needed to achieve a certain result.

related.title: だけあって
related.meaning: Is only natural, …Being the case, Because, As might be expected
related.comparison: だけあって and だけで may look similar, but they have distinct meanings. だけあって is used when a result is seen as natural or expected, similar to 'as you'd expect from (A)'. This is close in nuance to a separate expression, だけに. In contrast, だけで expresses that something is 'enough' to accomplish something else, like 'just by (A)' or 'only with (A)' in English.

synonyms.title: のみならず
synonyms.meaning: Not only...but, Not just...but, What is more
synonyms.comparison: のみならず and だけでなく(て)～も are nearly identical in meaning, both meaning 'Not only (A), but also (B)'. のみならず is by far the more formal expression, while だけでなく (especially in the alternate form だけじゃなくて) is more common in everyday speech. For example, 日本語のみならず、韓国語も話せる 'He can speak not only Japanese, but also Korean.' vs. 日本語だけじゃなくて、韓国語も話せる (Same meaning, but far more casual).

synonyms.title: はもとより
synonyms.meaning: Not only... but also, Let alone, Of course
synonyms.comparison: Both express that something applies to more than one item, but differ in nuance and tone. だけでなく(て)～も is a neutral 'Not only (A), but also (B)', e.g. 日本語だけでなく、韓国語も話せる 'He can speak not only Japanese, but also Korean.' はもとより is more literary and formal, and suggests the first item is obvious, e.g. 親はもとより先生にも感謝しています 'I'm grateful not only to my parents, but also to my teachers.'

related.title: ばかり
related.meaning: Just, Only, Nothing but
related.comparison: ばかり emphasizes exclusivity, meaning 'only' or 'nothing but', e.g. 肉ばかり食べている ('He eats nothing but meat'). だけでなく～も expresses inclusion, meaning 'not only…but also', e.g. 肉だけでなく野菜も食べている ('He eats not only meat but also vegetables'). Compared to だけ, ばかり often carries a stronger nuance of excess or doing something too much.

related.title: をはじめ
related.meaning: Not only...but also..., Starting with
related.comparison: をはじめ gives a representative example and implies that others follow, e.g. 東京をはじめ、全国でイベントが行われた ('The event was held across the country, starting with Tokyo'). だけでなく(て)～も lists multiple elements without suggesting one is more representative, e.g. 東京だけでなく大阪でも人気だ ('It's popular not only in Tokyo but also in Osaka').

related.title: ばかりでなく
related.meaning: Not only…but also, As well as
related.comparison: ばかりでなく and だけでなく(て)～も are nearly identical in meaning, both meaning 'Not only (A), but also (B)'. ばかりでなく is the more formal expression, while だけでなく (especially in the alternate form だけじゃなくて) is more common in everyday speech. For example, 日本語ばかりでなく、韓国語も話せる 'He can speak not only Japanese, but also Korean.' vs. 日本語だけじゃなくて、韓国語も話せる (Same meaning, but more casual in tone).

related.title: はもちろん
related.meaning: Not only but also, Let alone, Not to mention, Of course
related.comparison: Both express 'not only (A), but also (B)', but the nuance differs. はもちろん stresses (A) as obvious, e.g. 寿司はもちろん納豆も食べる ('He eats not only sushi, but also natto'). だけでなく gives equal weight to both, e.g. 映画だけでなくドラマも好きだ ('I like not only movies, but also TV dramas').

related.title: だけでなく
related.meaning: Not only… but also
related.comparison: だけでなく (or だけでなくて, だけじゃなくて, etc.) is a common expression that means 'not only'. It is often followed by another noun + も in the expression だけでなく(て)～も, which has the meaning of 'not only (A), but also (B)', e.g. 肉だけでなく、魚も食べる ('I eat not only meat, but also fish.'). In other words, だけでなく(て)～も is an extension of the だけでなく pattern when you want to specify the additional thing or quality.

related.title: だけあって
related.meaning: Is only natural, …Being the case, Because, As might be expected
related.comparison: Both mean as might be expected. だけあって usually leads to a positive evaluation, as in 有名なだけあって味は素晴らしい (As expected of a famous restaurant, the taste is excellent). だけに can be positive or negative, often stressing proportionality, as in 苦労しただけに喜びも大きい (Because I struggled, the joy is even greater).

related.title: わけだ
related.meaning: For that reason, No wonder, As you'd expect, Naturally, Thus, This is why
related.comparison: Both だけに and わけだ can be used to draw conclusions, but differ in tone and focus. だけに emphasizes an expected result based on a known condition, e.g. 忙しい人だけに来ないと思った ('Precisely because he's a busy man, I thought he wouldn't come'), while わけだ indicates the speaker's realization based on something previously expressed or observed, e.g. だから来ないわけだ ('So that's why he's not coming').

related.title: だけのことはある
related.meaning: No wonder, Not surprising that, As expected
related.comparison: Both imply results in line with expectations. だけのことはある expresses admiration, always at sentence-end, as in 練習しただけのことはあって優勝した (He won, as you’d expect from his practice). だけに is more flexible, attaching to another phrase that states the result, and can be used positively or negatively.

synonyms.title: のみならず
synonyms.meaning: Not only...but, Not just...but, What is more
synonyms.comparison: のみならず and だけでなく are essentially synonymous in that they both express non-exclusivity (i.e. 'not only'), but differ in formality. While だけでなく is common in both everyday speech (often appearing as だけじゃなくて) and writing, のみならず is a highly formal expression used primarily in writing and academic contexts. Both phrases are often followed by a noun + も to convey 'not only (A), but also (B)'.

antonyms.title: だけ
antonyms.comparison: だけ conveys the meaning of 'only' or 'just'. だけでなく (or variant forms like だけではなく, だけじゃなくて, etc.) is a negation of this, combining だけ with で (the conjuctive form of だ) and なく (the conjunctive form of ない) to express the meaning of ‘not only (A)...but also (B)’. Unlike だけ, だけでなく will always be followed by a continuation of the sentence providing additional information, e.g. バナナだけでなく、リンゴも食べました (‘I ate not only a banana, but also an apple.’).

related.title: にとどまらず
related.meaning: Not limited to, Not only...but also, Not stopping at
related.comparison: Both add information beyond an initial point, but the nuance differs. にとどまらず means ‘not limited to’ and emphasizes that the scope goes further than expected, often used in formal or written contexts to highlight expansion, as in 影響は国内にとどまらず海外にも及んだ ('The impact extended beyond the country to overseas.'). だけでなく is more neutral and conversational, simply adding another item without emphasizing scale or extension, as in 英語だけでなく日本語も話せる ('He can speak not only English but also Japanese.').

related.title: ばかりでなく
related.meaning: Not only…but also, As well as
related.comparison: だけでなく and ばかりでなく convey the same essential meaning of 'not only'. Both expressions are often followed by another noun + も, which gives the added meaning of 'not only (A), but also (B)'. The main difference is that ばかりでなく is more formal and appears primarily in written or academic language. In everyday speech, the だけ version is more common, particularly in the variant form だけじゃなくて.

related.title: はもちろん
related.meaning: Not only but also, Let alone, Not to mention, Of course
related.comparison: だけでなく and はもちろん both express non-exclusivity, but differ in nuance and emphasis. だけでなく simply means 'not only (A), but also (B)', e.g. 英語だけでなく、日本語も話せる ('He can speak not only English, but also Japanese.'). In contrast, はもちろん implies the first thing is obvious, while the second is more notable, e.g. 英語はもちろん、日本語も話せる ('He can speak English of course, but also Japanese.').

related.title: だけでなく(て)～も
related.meaning: Not only ~ but ~ also, Not just ~ but ~ also
related.comparison: だけでなく (or だけでなくて, だけじゃなくて, etc.) is a common expression that means 'not only'. It is often followed by another noun + も in the expression だけでなく(て)～も, which has the meaning of 'not only (A), but also (B)', e.g. 肉だけでなく、魚も食べる ('I eat not only meat, but also fish.'). In other words, だけでなく(て)～も is an extension of the だけでなく pattern when you want to specify the additional thing or quality.

related.title: に限らず
related.meaning: Not just, Not only… but also
related.comparison: だけでなく and に限らず both express non-exclusivity, but differ in nuance and formality. だけでなく is a straightforward expression meaning 'not only (A), but also (B)', and is often used in everyday conversation (especially in the variant form だけじゃなくて). In contrast, に限らず has a more formal and academic tone, and carries a slightly different nuance, suggesting that whatever follows is 'not limited to' the thing in question.

related.title: をはじめ
related.meaning: Not only...but also..., Starting with
related.comparison: だけでなく and をはじめ both express non-exclusivity, but differ in nuance. だけでなく simply means 'not only (A), but also (B)', e.g. 英語だけでなく、日本語も話せる ('He can speak not only English, but also Japanese.'). In contrast, をはじめ implies that something is the first in a list of many, e.g. 日本語をはじめ、様々な外国語が話せる ('He can speak many foreign languages, starting with Japanese.').

synonyms.title: だけあって
synonyms.meaning: Is only natural, …Being the case, Because, As might be expected
synonyms.comparison: Both express results in line with expectations. だけのことはある is sentence-final and expresses admiration, as in 優勝するなんて、さすが努力しただけのことはある (Winning is just what you’d expect from his effort). だけあって is followed by a phrase stating the result, as in 彼は有名人だけあって態度が堂々としている (As expected of a celebrity, his manner is confident).

related.title: わけだ
related.meaning: For that reason, No wonder, As you'd expect, Naturally, Thus, This is why
related.comparison: Both だけのことはある and わけだ can be used when drawing conclusions, but function differently. だけのことはある affirms that an outcome is appropriate or deserved in a positive sense, e.g. プロだけのことはある ('It's as expected from a pro'), while わけだ signals the speaker's understanding or conclusion, e.g. だから負けたわけだ ('So that's why we lost'). The latter conveys realization, in either a positive or negative sense.

related.title: だけに
related.meaning: As might be expected, Because, All the more because, It's only natural
related.comparison: Both imply results in line with expectations. だけのことはある expresses admiration, always at sentence-end, as in 練習しただけのことはあって優勝した (He won, as you’d expect from his practice). だけに is more flexible, attaching to another phrase that states the result, and can be used positively or negatively.

related.title: せめて
related.meaning: At least
related.comparison: Both can express the meaning of at least, but with different nuance. せめて expresses a minimal hope or consolation in an otherwise unfavorable situation, as in せめて一日は休みたい (I want to take at least one day off). だけましだ evaluates a situation as better than something worse, as in 忙しいけど、土日休めるだけましだ (I’m busy, but at least I can rest on the weekends).

antonyms.title: おわる
antonyms.comparison: おわる and だす both attach to verb stems but express opposite ideas, like finishing vs. starting an action. おわる is used for completing almost any action, e.g. 本を読み終わった ('I finished reading the book.'). だす often describes actions that begin suddenly or unexpectedly, e.g. 雨が降り出した ('It started to rain.').

related.title: はじめる
related.meaning: To start, To begin to
related.comparison: Both はじめる and だす attach to verb stems to mean 'to start doing (A)', but with different nuances. はじめる implies a deliberate or planned action, e.g. 学びはじめた ('I began to learn.'). In contrast, だす conveys a more spontaneous or unexpected beginning, e.g. 雨が降りだした ('It suddenly began to rain.').

related.title: いきなり
related.meaning: For no reason, Suddenly, All of a sudden
related.comparison: Both だす and いきなり express sudden action, but they differ in usage. だす attaches to verb stems to mean 'to suddenly start doing something.' For example, 動き出す 'begin to move'. In contrast, いきなり appears before verbs or phrases, like 'suddenly' in English. They can also be used together, as in いきなり動き出した ('It suddenly started moving.').

related.title: かけ
related.meaning: Half-, Not yet finished, About to, Begin to
related.comparison: かけ and だす both attach to verb stems and can express the start of an action, but their nuance differs. かけ emphasizes that the action has begun but remains incomplete. For example, 書きかけの小説 'a partially written/incomplete novel.' In contrast, だす highlights the beginning of the action, often with a sense of suddenness. For example, 書き出した ('He suddenly started writing.').

related.title: う-Verb (Past)
related.meaning: う - Verbs (Past tense)
related.comparison: The past form of う-Verbs expresses a completed action or an event that took place in the past, similar to the English '-ed.' In contrast, だった and でした are used after nouns to express the past tense, indicating that something 'was' or 'were' in a certain state. While the meaning (something happened or existed in the past) is the same, the grammatical expression differs depending on the word type (verb vs. noun).

related.title: だ
related.meaning: To be, Is
related.comparison: だった・でした and だ are all used to assert or identify something. The difference among them is in formality and tense. だ is casual and present tense (similar to the English 'is'/'am'), while だった・でした is past tense ('was'), with だった being casual (the past tense of だ) and でした being polite (the past tense of です).

related.title: です
related.meaning: To be, Is
related.comparison: です and だった・でした are all forms of 'is' or 'to be,' but they show different tenses. です is used for the present or future, like in あれは猫です ('That is a cat'). だった and でした are past tense—だった is casual, and でした is polite. For example, あれは猫だった means 'That was a cat.'

related.title: やら～やら
related.meaning: Whether or, Things like, And … so on
related.comparison: だの and やら～やら are similar in that both are used to list multiple examples of something, but differ in that だの generally has a negative implication, while やら～やら can just be used to provide examples without any particular implication.

related.title: など
related.meaning: Such as, Things like, Etc., And so on
related.comparison: だの and など are similar in that both are used to list examples. They differ in that だの will often explicitly list multiple examples, while など can be used with a singular example to suggest 'and things like that'. Also だの often is used in statements with a negative implication, while など is more neutral and has no such limitations.

related.title: それでも
related.meaning: But still, And yet, Even so, Nevertheless
related.comparison: だって and それでも both express contradiction. だって is casual and often used at the start of a sentence to offer an objection, e.g., だって疲れたもん ('But I'm tired!'). それでも is less casual and shows contrast with a neutral tone, e.g., 雨だった。それでも行った ('It was rainy, but I still went.').

related.title: んだって
related.meaning: I heard that, He or She said that
related.comparison: Both expressions involve だって, but their usage differs. んだって appears at the end of a sentence and means 'I hear (A)' or 'they say (A)', with an explanatory nuance added by ん. In contrast, だって is more flexible. It can appear at the beginning, middle, or end of a sentence, and has several meanings. It can function as a casual 'because', 'even though', or 'they say (A)', depending on context.

related.title: たって
related.meaning: Even if, Even though, No matter how
related.comparison: These are two expressions that can look very similar, or even identical in some cases, as たって when used with nouns will become だって. As an overall guideline, だって is a casual expression used to express 'I hear (A)' or 'they say (A)', or as a colloquial 'because', while たって is one way to say 'even though (A)' or 'even (A)', similar to ても or でも.

related.title: ということだ
related.meaning: I heard that, Rumor has it that, It is said that, It means that
related.comparison: Both can be used to report hearsay (i.e. 'they say (A)'), but differ in tone and nuance. ということだ is more formal and can carry the nuance of summarizing or confidently reporting what the speaker heard. だって is much more casual and light, and can also be used at the beginning of a sentence with an emphatic 'but I mean (A)' nuance, which is something ということだ cannot do.

related.title: なぜなら〜から
related.meaning: Because, The reason is, That is because
related.comparison: Both can be used to express reasons, but だって is highly casual, colloquial, and emotional, while なぜなら〜から is formal and objective-sounding. Also, なぜなら〜から begins a sentence, and clearly introduces a reason for something just stated, while だって can be used in sentence-final position. Depending on usage, だって can also have the meanings of 'I hear' and 'even though', which なぜなら〜から does not have.

related.title: ときいた
related.meaning: I heard that
related.comparison: Both ときいた and だって express hearsay, but differ in tone and formality. ときいた means 'I heard that (A)' and is neutral and conveys information directly heard by the speaker, while だって is casual and emphatic, often used in conversation to mean something like 'They say (A).' or 'I'm telling you (A).'.

related.title: Verb + だに
related.meaning: Just, Even just, Even merely
related.comparison: Both use だに to express an extremely small degree, but the structure and nuance differ. だに + しない is a fixed negative pattern meaning ‘does not even do (the smallest action),’ often used to emphasize complete lack of attention or effort, as in 挨拶だにしない (‘He doesn’t even greet.’). Verb＋だに is a formal, written construction meaning ‘even (the act of)' and is typically followed by negative or limiting expressions to stress how minimal or unlikely the action is, as in 彼の名前を聞くだに緊張する (‘I get nervous just hearing his name.').

related.title: しか～ない
related.meaning: There's only, Nothing but
related.comparison: だらけ and しか～ない are used differently and express contrasting meanings. だらけ follows nouns and expresses the idea of 'covered with' or 'full of', e.g. 泥だらけ 'covered in mud'. In contrast, しか～ない is used with a noun and negative verb to mean 'only' or 'nothing but', e.g. １００円しかない ('I have only 100 yen.'). They are similar in that both can often carry a negative or undesirable nuance.

related.title: ずくめ
related.meaning: Completely, Entirely, All in, Nothing but, A real streak of, Without exception, Jet
related.comparison: Both indicate that something is filled with many similar elements, but the evaluation differs. だらけ means ‘full of’ with a negative or undesirable nuance, used when the items are unwanted or messy, as in ゴミだらけの部屋だ ('The room is full of trash.'). ずくめ means ‘nothing but’ with a positive or favorable nuance, used when the elements are all good or fortunate, as in いいことずくめの旅行だった ('It was a trip full of good things.').

related.title: にほかならない
related.meaning: Nothing but, None other than, Simply
related.comparison: Both express a strong characterization, but differ in tone and focus. だらけ implies a negative excess of something undesirable, e.g. ミスだらけのレポート 'A report full of mistakes.' にほかならない expresses emphatic certainty or identity, e.g. 犯罪にほかならない 'It is nothing less than a crime.'

related.title: 塗れ
related.meaning: Stained, Covered in, Smeared, Dirty with, Soaked in
related.comparison: だらけ and 塗れ (often written in hiragana as まみれ) both describe being covered in something, but differ in nuance. だらけ suggests an overwhelming or chaotic abundance of unpleasant things, e.g. 傷だらけの手 'Hands full of cuts.' 塗れ emphasizes being soaked or stained, either physically or figuratively, e.g. 泥まみれの靴 'Mud-covered shoes' or 借金まみれの男 'A man drowning in debt.'

related.title: 誰か・どこか・誰も・どこも
related.slug: 誰か-どこか-誰も-どこも
related.meaning: Someone, Somewhere, Not anyone, Not anywhere
related.comparison: 誰 is a noun that refers to an unknown person, similar to the English 'who.' A common use is to ask about someone's identity, as in あの人は誰ですか ('Who is that person?') 誰 can also combine with other particles to express concepts like 誰か ('somebody'), 誰の ('whose'), and 誰も ('everyone' or 'no one' when used with a negative).

synonyms.title: でしょう
synonyms.comparison: Both でしょう and だろう express conjecture (e.g., 'It's probably…') or seek confirmation when used with rising intonation in a question (e.g., 面白いでしょう？ or 面白いだろう？ = 'It's interesting, right?'). でしょう is more polite and used with strangers or those of equal or higher rank, while だろう is casual and blunt, used with close friends, family, and in informal settings.

antonyms.title: まい
antonyms.meaning: Won't, Intend not to, Probably not
antonyms.comparison: まい and だろう are both used to express speculation, but they contrast in polarity. だろう indicates positive speculation or likelihood, as in 雨が降るだろう (It will probably rain), while まい expresses strong negative speculation or denial, as in 雨は降るまい (It probably won’t rain).

related.title: ね
related.meaning: Isn't it?, Right? (Sentence ending particle)
related.comparison: だろう is a form of だ used when the speaker feels uncertain. It's often translated as 'probably,' 'I guess,' or '...right?' when asking a question. In contrast, ね is used to seek confirmation or agreement, often translated as '...isn't it?' These two can combine to express both uncertainty and confirmation, as in 面白いだろうね ('It's probably interesting, isn't it?').

related.title: のではないだろうか
related.meaning: I think ... isn't it?, It might be that..., Don't you agree?
related.comparison: だろう is a form of だ used to express mild uncertainty (like 'It's probably…') or to seek confirmation from the listener ('It's this, right?'). のではないだろうか is similar but expresses much less certainty, with a nuance of wondering or seeking confirmation or approval ('I wonder if it's…?').

related.title: かもしれない
related.meaning: Might, Maybe, Probably
related.comparison: かもしれない (polite form かもしれません) and だろう (polite form でしょう) are both casual phrases used to express uncertainty or possibility, but with different degrees of certainty. かもしれない is less certain, similar to 'maybe' or 'might,' while だろう expresses a higher degree of certainty, like 'probably' or 'I guess.'

related.title: じゃないか
related.meaning: Isn't it?, Is it not?
related.comparison: だろう, in addition to expressing conjecture ('It's probably…'), can seek confirmation or agreement from the listener (e.g., 面白いだろう = 'It's interesting, right?'). じゃないか is more forceful and assertive, suggesting the speaker strongly believes something and expects agreement (e.g., 面白いじゃないか = 'It's interesting, isn't it!'). Both are casual forms and should be avoided in polite conversation.

related.title: どんどん
related.meaning: Progressively, Rapidly increasing, More and more
related.comparison: だんだん and どんどん are similar-sounding adverbs that both describe change over time, but with different nuances. だんだん suggests a slow, step-by-step progression, like 'gradually' or 'bit by bit'. どんどん implies faster, more dynamic change; something happening quickly or in leaps and bounds.

related.title: か
related.meaning: Question marking particle
related.comparison: か is the standard question particle, used at the end of sentences to ask questions (e.g., どんな本が好きですか？ = 'What kind of books do you like?') and also within other grammar patterns. っけ, on the other hand, is a casual sentence-ending particle used when trying to recall something (e.g., 名前はなんだっけ？ = 'What was his name again?').

synonyms.title: そうにない
synonyms.meaning: Unlikely to, Showing no signs of
synonyms.comparison: っこない and そうにない both indicate improbability, but differ in strength. っこない is categorical and emphatic, rejecting any possibility, as in こんなに弱い選手ばかりでは、勝てっこない (There’s no way we can win with such poor athletes). そうにない is more tentative, suggesting something seems unlikely based on current signs, as in 勝てそうにない (It doesn’t seem like we can win).

related.title: ようがない・ようもない
related.slug: ようがない・ようもない
related.meaning: There's no way to, It's impossible to
related.comparison: ようがない・ようもない and っこない both express impossibility, but differ in tone and focus. ようがない・ようもない conveys that there is no way or method to do something, as in 助けようがない (There’s no way to help). っこない is more absolute and emphatic, denying any chance of occurrence, as in 勝てっこない (There’s no way we can win).

related.title: そうもない
related.meaning: Very unlikely to, Does not seem that, Does not appear that
related.comparison: Both そうもない and っこない express unlikelihood, but differ in intensity and tone. そうもない implies doubt but leaves room for possibility, e.g. '間に合いそうもない' ('It doesn't seem like we'll make it in time'). っこない is stronger and more absolute, suggesting impossibility, e.g. 'そんなことできっこない' ('There's no way I could do that'). っこない is also more casual and colloquial, while そうもない is more neutral.

related.title: ときたら
related.meaning: When it comes to, That or this … of mine, That or this darn …, That or this blasted …
related.comparison: Both are used to single out a person or thing and comment on it, often with complaint or emphasis, but the tone differs. ったら・といったら is more direct and emotional, commonly used in spoken Japanese to show irritation or exasperation toward someone’s behavior, as in あの人ったら約束を全然守らない ('That person just never keeps promises.'). と来たら① is slightly more descriptive and observational, often used to highlight someone’s typical behavior or qualities with mild sarcasm or criticism, as in あの上司と来たら文句ばかりだ ('That boss, all he does is complain.').

related.title: なんか・なんて
related.meaning: Such as, Things like, Emphasis
related.comparison: って casually introduces or refers back to a topic, e.g. あの人って優しいよね ('That person is really kind, huh?') なんか・なんて adds a dismissive or emotional tone, often implying downplaying or surprise, e.g. 私なんかにできるかな ('Can someone like me really do it?')

related.title: といえば
related.meaning: Speaking of, If it were the case that
related.comparison: Both can mean 'speaking of (A)', but differ in tone and usage. といえば often refers back to something just mentioned or known to both speaker and listener, e.g. 京都といえば、お寺が有名だね ('Speaking of Kyoto, its temples are famous.') って is a casual form used in everyday conversation, often functioning like は to casually introduce a topic, e.g. この映画って最高！ ('That movie is amazing!')

related.title: は
related.meaning: As for... (Highlights sentence topic)
related.comparison: って is used in casual conversation to introduce a topic informally. It comes from the quotation particle と and can carry the nuance of 'speaking of…'. は is a more standard and flexible topic marker, used in both casual and formal situations, including writing. Unlike って, は can also carry a contrastive nuance.

related.title: ときたら
related.meaning: When it comes to, That or this … of mine, That or this darn …, That or this blasted …
related.comparison: Both are used to complain or express light annoyance, but the tone and target differ. ってば・ったら is a casual, emotional expression often used in spoken language, and can be used after simple phrases, e.g. もう、遅いってば (‘Hey, you’re late, I’m telling you.’). と来たら① is more descriptive and often slightly sarcastic, and is typically used e.g. after a name or description of a person to comment on their typical behavior or qualities, e.g. 田中くんと来たら文句ばかりだ (‘That Tanaka, all he does is complain.’).

related.title: まま(に)
related.meaning: As is, While remaining, Left in a state
related.comparison: まま(に) and っぱなし both describe something left in a certain state, but differ in tone. まま(に) can be neutral or passive, e.g. 電気をつけたまま寝た ('I fell asleep with the light on'). っぱなし adds a careless or irresponsible nuance, as in ドアを開けっぱなしにしないで ('Don't leave the door wide open').

related.title: てある
related.meaning: Something is done, Has been done, Remaining state
related.comparison: てある and っぱなし both describe ongoing states, but differ in tone. てある suggests something was done intentionally for future benefit. For example, 窓が開けてある means 'The window has been opened (for a reason).'. In contrast, っぱなし often implies carelessness. For example, 窓を開けっぱなしにして家を出た means 'I (carelessly) left the house with the window open.'.

related.title: がち
related.meaning: Apt to do, Tend to do, Prone to do
related.comparison: Both がち and っぽい describe tendencies or characteristic qualities, but differ in nuance. がち is neutral and used for tendencies that happen often, e.g. つい忘れがちだ ('I tend to forget.'). っぽい expresses an inherent quality or the appearance of something, similar to the English '~ish', e.g. 子供っぽい性格 ('a childish personality').

related.title: ぎみ
related.meaning: Looking, Feeling a little, Touch of, Tending to, Slightly
related.comparison: Both ぎみ (often written in kanji as 気味) and っぽい describe tendencies or characteristics, but with different nuance. ぎみ suggests a slight or subtle tendency, often related to physical or emotional states, e.g. 疲れ気味 ('a bit tired') or 風邪気味 ('have a bit of a cold'). っぽい implies a more innate trait that gives the appearance or impression of something, e.g. 子どもっぽい ('childish') or 忘れっぽい ('forgetful').

related.title: げ
related.meaning: Seemingly, Giving appearance of, [Expresses emotions or feelings]
related.comparison: Both っぽい and げ describe perceived qualities, but differ in nuance. っぽい refers to innate behaviors or traits that give a certain impression. For example, 子どもっぽい ('childish') or 怒りっぽい ('quick to anger'). In contrast, げ highlights perceived emotions in others. For example, 寂しげな顔 ('a lonely-looking face').

related.title: そう
related.meaning: Look like, Appear, Seem, Have a feeling that
related.comparison: Both っぽい and そう express appearance or impression, but differ in nuance and usage. そう attaches to word stems and is neutral and often based on observation, e.g. 雨が降りそう ('It looks like it'll rain.'). っぽい is casual and attaches to nouns to mean '~ish' or '~like', e.g. 子供っぽい 'childish'. Unlike そう, it often carries a negative or demeaning tone.

related.title: ふりをする
related.meaning: To pretend, To act as if
related.comparison: Both ふりをする and っぽい describe imitation or resemblance, but with a key difference in nuance. ふりをする implies pretending to be or do something one is not, as in 知ったふりをする ('pretend to know'). In contrast, っぽい conveys a natural resemblance or tendency, often unconscious or inherent, as in 子供っぽい ('childish').

related.title: 的
related.meaning: ~ly・~like・~al, Typical
related.comparison: 的 and っぽい are similar in that both can be attached to nouns to turn them into adjectives (or adverbs, in the forms 的に and っぽく). They differ grammatically (的 functions as a な-adjective, while っぽい is an い-adjective), and in nuance. 的 has a wider range of uses, similar to English endings like '-al', '-ly', etc., e.g. 感情 'emotion' vs. 感情的 'emotional'. っぽい is narrower in use, and often carries a derogatory meaning, e.g. 子供っぽい 'childish'.

related.title: らしい ①
related.meaning: Seems like, Apparently, I heard
related.comparison: っぽい and らしい ① both describe being 'like' something, but with different nuances and formality. っぽい, similar to the English '-ish,' refers to having the characteristics of something without being that thing, and is very casual, e.g. 子どもっぽい態度 'childish behavior', e.g. from an adult. In contrast, らしい ① implies embodying a characteristic in a more fitting or expected way, e.g. 子どもらしい笑顔 'a smile just as you'd expect from a child'.

related.title: らしい ②
related.meaning: typical of, -ish・like, appropriate for, becoming of, befitting
related.comparison: っぽい and らしい② both express resemblance. っぽい is casual, like '-ish' in English, e.g. 子どもっぽい 'childish' (usually with a negative nuance). らしい② implies that someone or something embodies expected traits, e.g. 男らしい 'manly' with a nuance of fulfilling cultural expectations of what a man should be.

related.title: いつの間にか
related.meaning: Suddenly, Unnoticed, Without noticing, Too soon
related.comparison: Both describe actions that occur without full conscious control, but they differ in nuance. いつの間にか emphasizes that something happened without the speaker noticing, e.g. いつの間にか春になっていた ('Before I knew it, spring had come.'). つい highlights that an action was done inadvertently or against one's better judgment, and often pairs with 〜てしまう or 〜ちゃう, e.g. つい甘いものを食べてしまった ('I ended up eating sweets without meaning to.').

related.title: てしまう・ちゃう
related.meaning: To do something by accident, To finish completely・totally, Unfortunately
related.comparison: つい and てしまう・ちゃう can both express doing something unintentionally or against one's better judgment. つい appears at the beginning of a sentence or phrase, while てしまう・ちゃう is a verb ending. They are often used together to emphasize the nuance of regret or unintentionally, e.g. つい食べてしまった・つい食べちゃった would imply 'I ended up eating it (even though I didn't mean to).'.

synonyms.title: がてら
synonyms.meaning: On the occasion of, While … as well, While …  (one is) at it, During, Along the way
synonyms.comparison: Both express simultaneous actions, but differ in nuance. ついでに implies convenience, e.g. '買い物のついでに郵便局に寄った' ('I stopped at the post office while shopping'). がてら implies doing something while moving, e.g. '散歩がてら郵便を出した' ('I mailed a letter while taking a walk'). They also differ grammatically: がてら connects directly to nouns, while ついでに requires の.

related.title: の間に
related.meaning: While, During, Between, Period
related.comparison: ついでに and の間に can both express the idea of 'while', but are used in different contexts. ついでに implies taking advantage of one action to do another, e.g. 買い物のついでに銀行に寄った ('I stopped by the bank while shopping.') の間に, by contrast, means 'during/while (A), (B)' without the added nuance of 'while you're at it' that comes with ついでに.

related.title: つつ
related.meaning: While, And
related.comparison: Both ついでに and つつ involve performing multiple actions together, but differ in nuance. ついでに expresses performing a second action while it's convenient or appropriate, e.g. '買い物のついでに郵便局に寄った' ('I stopped at the post office while shopping'). つつ is a formal expression indicating simultaneous actions or states, e.g. 'レシピを見つつ料理をする' ('cook while looking at a recipe').

related.title: ～ところに・～ところへ
related.meaning: At the time, When, While, Timing
related.comparison: Both ついでに and ～ところに・～ところへ relate to timing, but from different angles. ついでに expresses performing a second action while it's convenient, e.g. '買い物のついでに郵便局に寄った' ('I stopped at the post office while shopping'). ～ところに・～ところへ focuses on when something happens, often unexpectedly, e.g. '出かけようとしたところに電話がかかってきた' ('Just as I was about to leave, the phone rang').

related.title: にあたり・にあたって
related.meaning: When, When doing something, On the occasion, At the time
related.comparison: Both connect actions to a larger context, but with different emphasis. ついでに expresses performing a second action while it's convenient, e.g. '買い物のついでに郵便局に寄った' ('I stopped at the post office while shopping'). にあたり・にあたって is more formal and used for actions on a certain occasion, e.g. '開会にあたり、ご挨拶申し上げます' ('On the occasion of the opening, I extend my greetings').

related.title: がけに
related.meaning: On the way to, As you go
related.comparison: ついでに and がけに both relate to doing something while engaged in another action. ついでに expresses a second action done while convenient, e.g. '買い物のついでに郵便局に寄った' ('I stopped at the post office while shopping'). がけに is used with movement verbs and emphasizes doing something during a transition, e.g. '帰りがけに電話した' ('I called on my way home').

related.title: うちに
related.meaning: While, During
related.comparison: Both can be translated as 'while', but with different implications. ついでに means 'while you're at it' and adds a convenient extra action, e.g. 買い物のついでに銀行に寄った ('I stopped by the bank while shopping.') うちに emphasizes doing something before a condition changes, e.g. 熱いうちに食べてね ('Eat it while it's hot.')

related.title: 際に
related.meaning: When, At the time of, In case of, On the occasion of
related.comparison: Both ついでに and 際に refer to additional actions tied to another event, but differ in tone. ついでに is an everyday expression for when something is done while convenient, e.g. '買い物のついでに郵便局に寄った' ('I stopped at the post office while shopping'). 際に is more formal and emphasizes a particular occasion, e.g. '来日の際にご連絡ください' ('Please contact us when you come to Japan').

related.title: ているあいだに
related.meaning: While, During the time that ~
related.comparison: Both can mean 'while,' but the nuance differs. ついでに implies doing something extra during another task, e.g. 買い物のついでに郵便局に寄った ('While shopping, I stopped at the post office.'). ているあいだに just marks that something happened during another action, as in 買い物しているあいだに電話がかかってきた ('I got a call while shopping.').

related.title: ながら
related.meaning: While ~ing, During, As
related.comparison: ながら attaches to verb stems to express performing one action simultaneously with another, e.g. 音楽を聴きながら走る 'listen to music while running'. In contrast, ついでに follows various words or phrases to convey the idea of taking advantage of the opportunity to do another thing at the same time, e.g. 買い物に行くついでに走ってきた ('On the occasion of going shopping, I went for a run.').

related.title: ているところだ
related.meaning: In the process of doing ~ right now/at this moment
related.comparison: ているところだ and ついでに both relate to ongoing actions but differ greatly in usage. ているところだ describes an action in progress at that moment, with the nuance that the speaker is currently occupied (e.g. 昼ご飯を食べているところだ = 'I'm in the middle of eating lunch'). In contrast, ついでに describes performing an 'extra' action on the same occasion as another (e.g. 散歩のついでに昼ご飯を買ってきた = 'While on a walk, I bought lunch').

related.title: ついでに
related.meaning: While you are at it, On the occasion
related.comparison: Both ついでに and つつ involve performing multiple actions together, but differ in nuance. ついでに expresses performing a second action while it's convenient or appropriate, e.g. '買い物のついでに郵便局に寄った' ('I stopped at the post office while shopping'). つつ is a formal expression indicating simultaneous actions or states, e.g. 'レシピを見つつ料理をする' ('cook while looking at a recipe').

related.title: ながら
related.meaning: While ~ing, During, As
related.comparison: ながら and つつ overlap in some uses but not in others. When used to express simultaneous actions, e.g. コーヒーを[飲みながら・飲みつつ]本を読む 'read a book while drinking coffee', they are essentially identical in meaning, with the only difference being that つつ is more formal and literary. There are certain phrases, however, e.g. 生まれながら 'from birth', where only ながら works and つつ cannot be used.

synonyms.title: ものの
synonyms.comparison: Both express contrast between expectation and reality. つつ(も) implies that an action continues over time while another, opposing reality exists, as in 危険だと知りつつも挑戦した (I knew it was dangerous, yet I tried). ものの simply means although/even though, without emphasizing continuation, as in 雨は降ったものの試合は行われた (It rained, but the game was held).

related.title: くせに
related.meaning: Even though, And yet, Despite
