related.comparison: Both くせに and つつ(も) express contradiction, but differ in tone. くせに is a casual expression with a blaming or critical tone, e.g. 知っているくせに何で教えてくれないの？ ('You know, so why won't you tell me!?'). つつ(も) is neutral and used to describe conflicting simultaneous actions, e.g. 危険だと知りつつも行った ('I went despite knowing at the same time that it was dangerous.').

related.title: ながらも
related.meaning: But, Even while, Even though, Despite, Although
related.comparison: Both mean 'while' or 'although' and are often interchangeable, e.g. '何度も休みつつ、ゴールまでたどりついた' ('While/although resting many times, I neared the goal'). This could also be expressed as '何度も休みながらも...'. However, ながらも requires the action to occur over time, so '手紙を開きつつ' ('while opening the letter') would typically not use ながらも.

synonyms.title: よていだ
synonyms.comparison: Both つもりだ and よていだ express future intentions or plans, but with different nuances. つもりだ emphasizes personal intent or decision, such as 'I intend to' or 'I hope to,' without necessarily implying a set timeframe. よていだ focuses more on planning or scheduling, suggesting that the action is expected to happen at a specific time.

related.title: はずだ
related.meaning: Bound to (be), Expected to (be), Should be (able)
related.comparison: つもりだ is used to express personal intentions or plans, similar to 'I hope to' or 'I intend to' in English. In contrast, はずだ expresses an expectation or belief that something will happen, based on logic or evidence. While つもりだ is subjective and often refers to the speaker's own plans, はずだ is more objective and is used for expectations about others or events outside the speaker's control.

related.title: つもりで
related.meaning: With the intention of doing, Instead of..., Act as if or pretend, Believing that..., Thinking that...
related.comparison: つもり basically means 'intention.' When used at the end of a sentence in the form つもりだ, it expresses future plans, similar to 'I intend to' or 'I hope to.' In the form つもりで, it connects to another part of the sentence and means doing something with a certain intention or belief in mind. When preceded by a past tense verb, つもり can also mean 'pretending' or 'acting as if... (though it's not actually the case).'

related.title: 〜ようと思う・〜おうと思う
related.slug: ようと思う-おうと思う
related.meaning: I think I will, I think I shall, Going to, Gonna (Volitional + と思う)
related.comparison: Both つもりだ and 〜ようと思う・〜おうと思う can both be used to express intentions or plans for the future. つもりだ feels slightly more 'official' or definite, similar to the English 'I intend to' or 'I hope to'. In contrast, 〜ようと思う・〜おうと思う has a more casual and less definitive feel, similar to the English 'I'm thinking of (doing something)'.

related.title: ているあいだに
related.meaning: While, During the time that ~
related.comparison: Both forms involve the idea of an ongoing process, but with different nuance and focus. ているあいだに refers to something happening while another action or state is ongoing. For example, 勉強しているあいだに電話がかかってきた ('The phone rang while I was studying.'). In contrast, つつある expresses something in the process of changing, e.g. 病気が治りつつある ('My illness is in the process of healing.').

related.title: ながら
related.meaning: While ~ing, During, As
related.comparison: つつ (when used by itself) and ながら both attach to verbs to express 'while doing', but つつある differs in usage. Specifically, ながら describes two actions performed simultaneously by the same subject, e.g. 音楽を聴きながら走る 'listen to music while running'. In contrast, つつある refers to a continuing process and is not typically used with physical actions, e.g. 物価が上がりつつある ('Prices are continuing to rise.').

related.title: よていだ
related.meaning: Plan to, Intend to
related.comparison: 予定だ expresses a concrete future plan, e.g. 来年日本に行く予定だ ('I plan to go to Japan next year.'). つもり is often less concrete and, in つもりで form, connects actions with intention, e.g. 遊ぶつもりで行った 'went with the intention of having fun'. It can also mean 'believing (A)' as in 俳優になったつもりで話す 'speak as if you were (lit. with the belief that you had become) an actor'.

related.title: つもりだ
related.meaning: To plan to, To intend to
related.comparison: つもり basically means 'intention.' When used at the end of a sentence in the form つもりだ, it expresses future plans, similar to 'I intend to' or 'I hope to.' In the form つもりで, it connects to another part of the sentence and means doing something with a certain intention or belief in mind. When preceded by a past tense verb, つもり can also mean 'pretending' or 'acting as if... (though it's not actually the case).'

related.title: なおす
related.meaning: To do over, To redo
related.comparison: なおす can be attached to the stem of other verbs to express the idea of 're-doing' or doing something over. つづける can be attached to verb stems in the same way, but expresses the meaning of 'continuing' to do something. For example, やりなおす means 'to do over', while やりつづける means 'to keep doing'.

related.title: てくる
related.meaning: To come to, To become, To continue, To be starting to, Has been ~ing
related.comparison: Both つづける and てくる can show the progression of an action over time. つづける attaches to the verb stem and focuses on the continuation of the action itself. てくる uses the て-form and emphasizes that the action started in the past and has continued up to the present.

related.title: ばかりだ
related.meaning: (Only) continue to, Keep on -ing, More and more
related.comparison: つづける and ばかりだ both show continuation, but with different nuances. つづける attaches to verb stems and shows simple ongoing action, e.g. 減り続けている 'is continuing to decrease'. ばかりだ adds the sense of 'more and more,' often negatively, e.g. 減るばかりだ 'keeps on decreasing'.

related.title: ている②
related.meaning: State of being, Has (have) done
related.comparison: ている expresses an ongoing action or state. つづける attached to the verb stem expresses a similar meaning, but with a greater emphasis on the idea of continuation. For example, 増えている means that something is increasing or has increased, while 増えつづけている carries the additional nuance that it is continuing to increase.

related.title: 一方だ
related.meaning: More and more, Continue to, Keep on, -er and -er
related.comparison: つづける and 一方だ both indicate continuation but differ in tone. つづける means a simple ongoing action, e.g. 減り続けている 'is still decreasing'. 一方だ follows dictionary-form verbs and adds a sense of progression in one direction, often negatively, e.g., 減る一方だ 'is getting worse and worse'.

related.title: ている①
related.meaning: Is, Am, Are (~ing)
related.comparison: ている is a common structure in Japanese to express an ongoing action or state. つづける attached to the verb stem expresses a similar meaning, but with a greater emphasis on the idea of continuation. For example, 増えている means that something is increasing or has increased, while 増えつづけている carries the additional nuance that it is continuing to increase.

related.title: 要するに
related.meaning: To sum up, In summary, In short
related.comparison: つまり and 要するに both signal a rephrasing or summary, often translated as 'in other words' or 'to sum up'. つまり is more casual and common in conversation, e.g. つまり、行かないってこと？ ('So, you're not going?'). 要するに is more formal and often used in writing or speeches, e.g. 要するに無理だということです ('To sum up, it's impossible').

related.title: ということは
related.meaning: That means, That is to say
related.comparison: つまり and ということは both rephrase earlier information, but differ in usage. つまり restates or explains something just said, e.g. つまりできないってこと？ ('So you're saying you can't do it?'). ということは introduces a logical inference or consequence, e.g. ということは、試合は中止だね ('So that means the game is canceled').

related.title: 即ち
related.meaning: In other words, That is, Namely
related.comparison: Both つまり and 即ち mean 'in other words', but differ in tone and context. つまり is casual and used in conversation, e.g. つまりしょうがないってこと ('In other words, it can't be helped'). 即ち is formal and often used in academic or written contexts, e.g. 生きることは即ち戦いである ('To live is, in other words, a battle').

related.title: てもらう
related.meaning: To get someone to do, To have someone do
related.comparison: Both use the て form to talk about favors, but with different perspectives. てあげる focuses on the giver (you or someone close to you) doing something for someone else. てもらう focuses on the receiver having something done for them. For example, ケンに教えてあげた ('I taught/told Ken.') vs. ケンに教えてもらった ('Ken taught me.').

related.title: てくれる
related.meaning: To do something for someone (usually you)
related.comparison: Both use the て form with a giving verb to describe doing a favor. てあげる is used when you or someone close to you does something for someone else, e.g. 友達に日本語を教えてあげた ('I taught my friend Japanese.'). In contrast, てくれる is used when someone else does something for you or someone close to you, e.g. 友達が日本語を教えてくれた ('My friend taught me Japanese.').

related.title: あげる
related.meaning: To give (away), To present, To provide
related.comparison: あげる is a basic verb meaning 'to give,' often used when the giver is the speaker or someone in the speaker's in-group, and the receiver is outside that group. For example, プレゼントをあげる means 'to give a present.' When used as てあげる after a verb in the て form, it means doing something for someone's benefit. For example, 英語を教えてあげる means 'to teach English (to someone as a favor).'

related.title: てくる
related.meaning: To come to, To become, To continue, To be starting to, Has been ~ing
related.comparison: てくる and ていく combine the て-form of a verb with 来る or 行く to express movement or change over time. てくる suggests movement toward the speaker or a change that started in the past and leads up to now. ていく implies movement away from the speaker or a change that begins now and continues into the future.

related.title: へいく
related.meaning: To go to, To head toward
related.comparison: The verb いく expresses movement in a direction, literally or figuratively. へいく is used after nouns to indicate moving toward a place (e.g., 学校へいく = 'go to school'). When used as ていく (Verb + て + いく), it implies a continuing action or change moving forward. Note that いく is written in kanji as 行く when used alone, but typically in hiragana in the ていく form.

related.title: Verb + にいく
related.meaning: To go ~, To go in order to ~
related.comparison: Verb + にいく combines the verb stem with にいく to express going somewhere to do something (e.g., 食べに行く = 'go to eat'). In contrast, ていく (Verb + て + いく) expresses sequential actions (e.g., 食べて行く = 'eat, then go') or actions that progress over time (e.g., 上手くなっていく = 'get better over time').

related.title: はじめる
related.meaning: To start, To begin to
related.comparison: ていく expresses either literal movement away from the speaker or a change that begins now and continues into the future. In contrast, はじめる attaches to the verb stem to mean 'to start (A)'. The key difference is nuance: ていく highlights ongoing progression and continuation going forward, while はじめる focuses on the starting point and the idea of commencing an action.

related.title: まま(に)
related.meaning: As is, While remaining, Left in a state
related.comparison: てある and まま(に) both describe continuing states, but differ in nuance. てある implies an action was done for a reason and that the result remains. For example, 窓が開けてある ('The window has been opened.') implies that it has been left open for a purpose. In contrast, まま(に) emphasizes that the state remains unchanged, without implying purpose, e.g. 窓を開けたまま家を出てしまった ('I left the house with the window open.').

related.title: ておく
related.meaning: To do something in advance
related.comparison: Both てある and ておく express actions done for future purposes, but with key differences. てある focuses on the resulting state after the action is performed, e.g. 窓が開けてある ('The window has been opened.', when it remains open for some purpose). In contrast, ておく emphasizes the action itself, focusing on preparation, e.g. 窓を開けておいた ('I opened the window.', implying that it was for future benefit.). Note also the different particles used.

related.title: っぱなし
related.meaning: Leaving something in a certain state, Left as is, Keep on -ing
related.comparison: てある and っぱなし both describe ongoing states, but differ in tone. てある suggests something was done intentionally for future benefit. For example, 窓が開けてある means 'The window has been opened (for a reason).'. In contrast, っぱなし often implies carelessness. For example, 窓を開けっぱなしにして家を出た means 'I (carelessly) left the house with the window open.'.

related.title: Verb + て
related.meaning: And, Then (Linking events)
related.comparison: The Verb + て form connects verbs or parts of a sentence. When combined with いた, it describes a past state. For example, 食べていた means 'I was eating,' and 死んでいた means 'it was dead.' Whether ていた conveys an action ('was -ing') or a state depends on the verb. In casual speech, ていた is often shortened to てた.

related.title: う-Verb (Past)
related.meaning: う - Verbs (Past tense)
related.comparison: The う-Verb (Past) expresses a completed action or one that took place in the past, similar to '-ed' in English. The ていた form conveys an ongoing action or state in the past, similar to 'was -ing' in English. In other words, ていた indicates an action that was continuous or repeated, while the regular past form describes a single, completed action.

synonyms.title: てくれない・てもらえない
synonyms.slug: てくれない-てもらえない
synonyms.meaning: Won't you?, Will you?, Could you?, Can you?, Would you?
synonyms.comparison: All three are used to request something, like 'Can you do (A) for me?', but differ in tone. てくれない and てもらえない are casual and used with friends, as in 日本語を教えてくれない？ ('Can you teach me Japanese?'). ていただけませんか is much more polite, and therefore appropriate when asking someone like a boss or teacher.

synonyms.title: てください
synonyms.comparison: てください is a basic way to express a polite request (e.g., 'Please do…'). It is polite and neutral, and can be used with strangers or people equal in status to you. For more formal situations, ていただけませんか is a more polite and humble request, often used when speaking to clients, customers, or superiors. It softens the tone further—similar to saying 'Would you mind doing...for me?' in English.

antonyms.title: ないでください
antonyms.comparison: ないでください is a polite way to ask someone not to do something. It's neutral and appropriate for strangers or people of equal status. In contrast, 〜ていただけませんか is a more respectful and humble way to ask someone to do something, typically used with clients, customers, or superiors when 〜てください might sound too direct.

related.title: てくれる
related.meaning: To do something for someone (usually you)
related.comparison: てくれる shows someone did something for your benefit, like 教えてくれた ('They taught/told me.'). ていただけませんか is a humble and polite way to make a request, e.g. 教えていただけませんか？ ('Would you kindly teach/tell me?'). Both involve benefits for the speaker but differ in tone and usage.

related.title: てもらう
related.meaning: To get someone to do, To have someone do
related.comparison: てもらう describes getting someone to do something for you, e.g. 教えてもらった ('I had them teach/tell me.'). ていただけませんか is a polite way to ask for such a favor, as in 教えていただけませんか？ ('Would you kindly teach/tell me?'). Both involve receiving help but differ in politeness and usage.

related.title: ては
related.meaning: If, With, Since, Because
related.comparison: ていては shows the continuation or repetition of an action and its negative consequences, often with a nuance of warning, as in 遊んでいては合格できない (If you keep playing, you won’t pass). ては marks a conditional result without the progressive sense, and can imply either positive or negative outcomes, as in 褒めてはやる気を出させた (By praising him, I motivated him).

synonyms.title: ている②
synonyms.comparison: The ている form describes an ongoing action or state. With verbs that describe actions over time, it functions like the English 'is...-ing,' but with state verbs, it means to be (or continue to be) in that state. For example, 食べている means 'is/am eating,' while 死んでいる means 'is/are dead' (not 'is dying').

synonyms.title: ている③
synonyms.comparison: The ている form describes an ongoing action or state. In the ている① form, it expresses something you are currently doing, like 食べている ('I am eating'). In the ている③ form, it refers to a habitual or repeated action, even if you're not currently doing said action, often paired with a time word. For example, 毎朝３０分走っている means 'I run every morning for 30 minutes.'

related.title: Verb + て
related.meaning: And, Then (Linking events)
related.comparison: The Verb + て form is used to connect a verb to another verb, or another part of the sentence. When combined with いる, it describes an ongoing action or state. For example, 食べている means 'I am eating,' and 死んでいる means 'I am dead.' Whether ている conveys an action (like 'is -ing') or a state depends on the verb. In casual speech, ている is often shortened to てる.

related.title: Verb［た・ている］+ Noun
related.meaning: Verb modified noun, Relative clause
related.comparison: The ている① form expresses completed actions or past states. Unlike English, verbs can directly modify nouns, similar to い-Adjectives. For example, 読んでいる本 means 'the book [I] am reading.' However, when describing a noun's state, the past form (た) is often preferred over ている. For example, 死んだネズミ ('a dead mouse') is more common than 死んでいるネズミ.

related.title: ているあいだに
related.meaning: While, During the time that ~
related.comparison: The ている① form describes an ongoing action or state. When combined with あいだに (meaning 'while doing something'), it expresses the idea of 'while doing A, B happened.' This is typically used for events outside the speaker's control. For example, 映画を見ている間に、電話が掛かってきました means 'While I was watching a movie, a phone call came in.'

related.title: ているところだ
related.meaning: In the process of doing ~ right now/at this moment
related.comparison: The ている① form describes an ongoing action or state. ているところだ conveys the same meaning but emphasizes being 'right in the middle' of doing something. For example, 食べている means 'I am eating,' while 食べているところだ emphasizes 'I'm in the middle of eating,' which could explain why you can't talk long on the phone, for example.

related.title: つづける
related.meaning: To continue
related.comparison: ている is a common structure in Japanese to express an ongoing action or state. つづける attached to the verb stem expresses a similar meaning, but with a greater emphasis on the idea of continuation. For example, 増えている means that something is increasing or has increased, while 増えつづけている carries the additional nuance that it is continuing to increase.

synonyms.title: ている①
synonyms.comparison: The ている form describes an ongoing action or state. With verbs that describe actions over time, it functions like the English 'is...-ing,' but with state verbs, it means to be (or continue to be) in that state. For example, 食べている means 'is/am eating,' while 死んでいる means 'is/are dead' (not 'is dying').

related.title: Verb + て
related.meaning: And, Then (Linking events)
related.comparison: The Verb + て form connects verbs or parts of a sentence. When an 'instantaneous' verb (one that expresses a single change in state) is combined with the existence verb いる in the ている form, it expresses an ongoing state. For example, 死んでいる means '(It) is dead.' With other verbs, ている conveys a meaning similar to the English 'is...-ing' (see ている①).

related.title: つづける
related.meaning: To continue
related.comparison: ている expresses an ongoing action or state. つづける attached to the verb stem expresses a similar meaning, but with a greater emphasis on the idea of continuation. For example, 増えている means that something is increasing or has increased, while 増えつづけている carries the additional nuance that it is continuing to increase.

related.title: Verb［た・ている］+ Noun
related.meaning: Verb modified noun, Relative clause
related.comparison: The ている① form expresses completed actions or past states. Unlike English, verbs can directly modify nouns, similar to い-Adjectives. However, unless emphasizing a changing state, the past form (た) is usually preferred over ている when describing nouns. For example, 死んだネズミ ('a dead mouse') is more common than 死んでいるネズミ.

related.title: ている③
related.meaning: Has/have done, Is ~ing (Repetition)
related.comparison: ている expresses an ongoing action or state. The ている② form is used with verbs indicating a single change in state, like 死んでいる ('is dead'), not an action. The ている③ form describes repeated or habitual actions, like 会社で働いている could mean ('I'm working for [that/this] company).

synonyms.title: ている①
synonyms.comparison: The ている form describes an ongoing action or state. In the ている① form, it expresses something you are currently doing, like 食べている ('I am eating'). In the ている③ form, it refers to a habitual or repeated action, even if you're not currently doing said action, often paired with a time word. For example, 毎朝３０分走っている means 'I run every morning for 30 minutes.'

related.title: Verb［た・ている］+ Noun
related.meaning: Verb modified noun, Relative clause
related.comparison: The ている① form expresses completed actions or past states. Unlike English, verbs can directly modify nouns, similar to い-Adjectives. For example, 行っている学校 means 'the school I am going to.' However, when describing a noun's state, the past form is often preferred over ている. For example, 死んだネズミ ('a dead mouse') is more common than 死んでいるネズミ.

related.title: Verb + て
related.meaning: And, Then (Linking events)
related.comparison: The Verb + て form connects verbs or parts of a sentence. One common use of this form is to connect it to the existence verb いる to express continuing or repeated actions, like going to work or going to sleep at the same time every day. Note that the specific nuance of ている can depend on the verb and context (see ている① and ている②).

related.title: ている②
related.meaning: State of being, Has (have) done
related.comparison: ている expresses an ongoing action or state. The ている② form is used with verbs indicating a single change in state, like 死んでいる ('is dead'), not an action. The ている③ form describes repeated or habitual actions, like 会社で働いている could mean ('I'm working for [that/this] company).

related.title: つつある
related.meaning: -ing, Be -ing
related.comparison: Both forms involve the idea of an ongoing process, but with different nuance and focus. ているあいだに refers to something happening while another action or state is ongoing. For example, 勉強しているあいだに電話がかかってきた ('The phone rang while I was studying.'). In contrast, つつある expresses something in the process of changing, e.g. 病気が治りつつある ('My illness is in the process of healing.').

related.title: の間に
related.meaning: While, During, Between, Period
related.comparison: 間(あいだ) means 'period' or 'space', and in addition to its literal meaning, it is used in expressions meaning 'while' or 'between', e.g. 1月と4月の間に 'between January and April'. In the ているあいだに form, the focus is on something happening during another ongoing action. For example, 勉強しているあいだに電話がかかってきた ('The phone rang while I was studying.').

related.title: 中
related.meaning: During, While, Throughout, In the process of
related.comparison: Both patterns express being in the middle of an action, but differ in how they are used. 中 attaches to nouns to indicate 'in the process of' or 'during.' For example, 今、食事中です means 'I'm in the middle of eating.' ているあいだに refers to something happening while another action or state is ongoing. For example, ご飯を食べているあいだに電話が鳴った and 食事中に電話が鳴った are equivalent in meaning.

related.title: とき
related.meaning: When, At the time of
related.comparison: とき expresses the time of an action or event, like the English 'when'. It can be used with nouns as in 雨の時 'when it rains', verbs as in 父が帰ってきた時 'when Dad came home', or adjectives as in 暑い時 'when it's hot'. In contrast, ているあいだに uses the て-form of verbs and refers to a period during which something occurs, e.g. 晩ご飯を食べている間に、父が帰ってきました ('While I was eating dinner, my father came home.').

related.title: ているところだ
related.meaning: In the process of doing ~ right now/at this moment
related.comparison: Both patterns express ongoing actions but differ in focus. ているところだ means being in the middle of something, often with a sense of being occupied. For example, 勉強をしているところだ ('I'm in the middle of studying.').ているあいだに highlights something that happens during another action, e.g. 勉強しているあいだに電話がかかってきた ('The phone rang while I was studying.').

related.title: ながら
related.meaning: While ~ing, During, As
related.comparison: Both forms involve the idea of simultaneous or ongoing actions, but differ in structure and nuance. ているあいだに expresses that something happened during another ongoing action, e.g. 勉強しているあいだに電話がかかってきた ('The phone rang while I was studying.'). ながら shows one subject doing two things at once, e.g. 音楽を聴きながら勉強するのが好き ('I like to study while listening to music.').

related.title: うちに
related.meaning: While, During
related.comparison: Both patterns can express the idea of 'while,' but differ in nuance. ているあいだに refers to something happening during another ongoing action. For example, 勉強しているあいだに電話がかかってきた means 'The phone rang while I was studying.' うちに emphasizes doing something while a particular state still lasts. For example, 早いうちに帰ろう means 'Let's go home while it's still early.'

related.title: ついでに
related.meaning: While you are at it, On the occasion
related.comparison: Both can mean 'while,' but the nuance differs. ついでに implies doing something extra during another task, e.g. 買い物のついでに郵便局に寄った ('While shopping, I stopped at the post office.'). ているあいだに just marks that something happened during another action, as in 買い物しているあいだに電話がかかってきた ('I got a call while shopping.').

related.title: ている①
related.meaning: Is, Am, Are (~ing)
related.comparison: The ている① form describes an ongoing action or state. When combined with あいだに (meaning 'while doing something'), it expresses the idea of 'while doing A, B happened.' This is typically used for events outside the speaker's control. For example, 映画を見ている間に、電話が掛かってきました means 'While I was watching a movie, a phone call came in.'

related.title: ～ところに・～ところへ
related.meaning: At the time, When, While, Timing
related.comparison: Both express something happening during an ongoing action, but differ in focus. Compared to ているあいだに, which states in a straightforward manner one thing happening in the middle of another thing, ～ところに・～ところへplaces more of a focus on the 'scene' or situation, and is therefore often used to express a surprising or disturbing event.

related.title: 途中に・途中で
related.meaning: On the way, Partway, In the middle of
related.comparison: ているところだ conveys that an action is currently in progress, often with a sense of immediate involvement, e.g. 朝ご飯を食べているところだ ('I'm in the middle of eating breakfast.'). In contrast, 途中に・途中で expresses doing something 'on the way' or 'in the middle' of another activity, e.g. 通勤の途中で朝ご飯を食べた ('I ate breakfast on my commute to work.').

related.title: 中
related.meaning: During, While, Throughout, In the process of
related.comparison: ているところだ and 中 are similar in that both express being in the middle of doing something, but they function differently grammatically. ているところだ is used with verbs, e.g. 今、食事をしているところです ('Now I'm in the middle of eating.'). In contrast, 中 attaches to the end of nouns and expresses a similar meaning, e.g. 今、食事中です, which means essentially the same thing but is more concise.

related.title: うちに
related.meaning: While, During
related.comparison: ているところだ conveys that an action is in progress at that moment, with the nuance that the speaker is occupied with it, e.g. 晩ご飯を食べているところだ ('I'm in the middle of eating dinner.'). In contrast, うちに describes doing an action while a certain state is ongoing, often with the nuance that it's the ideal time, e.g. 熱いうちに食べましょう ('Let's eat it while it's hot.').

related.title: とき
related.meaning: When, At the time of
related.comparison: とき expresses the time of an action or event, like 'when' in English. It can be used with nouns as in 雨の時 'when it rains', verbs as in 父が帰ってきた時 'when Dad came home', or adjectives as in 暑い時 'when it's hot'. In contrast, ているところ uses the verb in て-form and emphasizes being in the middle of an action, often with a sense of being busy or focused, e.g. 晩ご飯を食べているところです ('I'm right in the middle of eating dinner.').

related.title: ているあいだに
related.meaning: While, During the time that ~
related.comparison: Both patterns express ongoing actions but differ in focus. ているところだ means being in the middle of something, often with a sense of being occupied. For example, 勉強をしているところだ ('I'm in the middle of studying.').ているあいだに highlights something that happens during another action, e.g. 勉強しているあいだに電話がかかってきた ('The phone rang while I was studying.').

related.title: ながら
related.meaning: While ~ing, During, As
related.comparison: ているところだ and ながら both can express ongoing actions or states, but are used differently. ているところだ focuses on being 'in the middle of' something and implies that the speaker is occupied with that action, e.g. ご飯を食べているところだ ('I'm in the middle of eating.'). In contrast, ながら expresses simultaneous actions, e.g. テレビを観ながらご飯を食べる 'watch TV while eating'.

related.title: ついでに
related.meaning: While you are at it, On the occasion
related.comparison: ているところだ and ついでに both relate to ongoing actions but differ greatly in usage. ているところだ describes an action in progress at that moment, with the nuance that the speaker is currently occupied (e.g. 昼ご飯を食べているところだ = 'I'm in the middle of eating lunch'). In contrast, ついでに describes performing an 'extra' action on the same occasion as another (e.g. 散歩のついでに昼ご飯を買ってきた = 'While on a walk, I bought lunch').

related.title: の間に
related.meaning: While, During, Between, Period
related.comparison: ているところだ conveys that an action is in progress at that moment, with the nuance that the speaker is occupied with it, e.g. 映画を観ているところだ ('I'm in the middle of watching a movie.'). In contrast, の間に is used to express that something else happened at some point during the duration of that action, e.g. 映画を観ている間に父が帰ってきました ('My father came home while I was watching a movie.').

related.title: ～ところに・～ところへ
related.meaning: At the time, When, While, Timing
related.comparison: ているところだ conveys that an action is in progress at that moment, with the nuance that the speaker is occupied with it, e.g. ゲームをしているところだ ('I'm in the middle of playing a video game.'). In contrast, ～ところに・～ところへ describes something happening at the moment of another action, e.g. ゲームをしているところに母に呼ばれた ('I was called by my mother while I was in the middle of playing a game.').

related.title: 最中に
related.meaning: In the middle of
related.comparison: ているところだ conveys that an action is in progress at that moment, with the nuance that the speaker is busy with it, e.g. 晩ご飯を食べているところだ ('I'm in the middle of eating dinner.'). In contrast, 最中に is used to express that something else happened during that action, e.g. 晩ご飯を食べている最中に電話がかかってきた ('The phone rang while I was right in the middle of eating dinner.').

related.title: ている①
related.meaning: Is, Am, Are (~ing)
related.comparison: The ている① form describes an ongoing action or state. ているところだ conveys the same meaning but emphasizes being 'right in the middle' of doing something. For example, 食べている means 'I am eating,' while 食べているところだ emphasizes 'I'm in the middle of eating,' which could explain why you can't talk long on the phone, for example.

related.title: てある
related.meaning: Something is done, Has been done, Remaining state
related.comparison: Both てある and ておく express actions done for future purposes, but with key differences. てある focuses on the resulting state after the action is performed, e.g. 窓が開けてある ('The window has been opened.', when it remains open for some purpose). In contrast, ておく emphasizes the action itself, focusing on preparation, e.g. 窓を開けておいた ('I opened the window.', implying that it was for future benefit.). Note also the different particles used.

related.title: て初めて
related.meaning: For the first time, Not until, Only after, Only when, Only because
related.comparison: て初めて and てから both use the Verb + て form to show actions happening in sequence, but with different nuances. てから simply shows that one event happened after another, while て初めて emphasizes that the second action happened 'only after' or 'not until' meaning 'for the first time after' the previous event.

related.title: てからというもの
related.meaning: After, Since, Ever since then
related.comparison: てからというもの can be seen as a more emphatic, formal, and wordy version of てから. Though both mean 'after/once (A), (B)', てから is often used for even simple actions, e.g. 掃除をしてから出掛けます ('I'll go out after I clean up.'), while てからというもの will be used for more profound/meaningful statements, e.g. 娘が生まれてからというもの、人生観が変わった ('Ever since my daughter was born, my view on life changed.').

related.title: から
related.meaning: From
related.comparison: てから, combining the て form of a verb with から, expresses a sequence of actions, similar to 'A, then B' or 'after A, B' in English (e.g., 食べてから寝る = 'After I eat, I'll sleep'). It can be confused with the past tense た form plus から, which expresses a reason (e.g., 食べたから寝る = 'I ate, so I'll sleep'), focusing on cause rather than sequence.

related.title: 上で
related.meaning: After, Upon
related.comparison: てから and 上で both convey a sequence of events, but their nuances differ. てから simply indicates that one event follows another. In contrast, 上で emphasizes that the first action or event was a necessary precondition for the second event. Also, 上で is more formal than てから, and often used in business situations or writing.

related.title: あとで
related.meaning: After, Later
related.comparison: てから and あとで both convey a sequence of events, but their nuances differ. てから uses the Verb + て form to show that one event follows another. In contrast, あとで follows the past tense (た form) and simply indicates that the second event occurred after the first, without implying any connection between the two.

related.title: Verb + て
related.meaning: And, Then (Linking events)
related.comparison: The Verb + て form is used to connect a verb to another verb, or another part of the sentence. It's commonly used to list actions in sequence, such as 食べて寝る ('I eat, and (then) sleep'). Adding から after the て form emphasizes the sequence, as in 食べてから寝る ('I eat, and (after that) I will sleep').

related.title: たら
related.meaning: When, From when, Just when, If
related.comparison: てから and たら both convey a sequence of events, but their nuances differ. てから simply indicates that one event follows another. In contrast, たら is similar to the English 'when' or 'if' and suggests that the second event is a result of the first. たら can be used in both hypothetical situations ('if A, then B') and past events ('When I did A, B happened').

related.title: てから
related.meaning: Once...is done, Once...happens, After doing
related.comparison: てからというもの can be seen as a more emphatic, formal, and wordy version of てから. Though both mean 'after/once (A), (B)', てから is often used for even simple actions, e.g. 掃除をしてから出掛けます ('I'll go out after I clean up.'), while てからというもの will be used for more profound/meaningful statements, e.g. 娘が生まれてからというもの、人生観が変わった ('Ever since my daughter was born, my view on life changed.').

related.title: ないことには～ない
related.meaning: Unless you do something
related.comparison: Both express necessary conditions, but with different focus. ないことには～ない emphasizes that something cannot happen unless a prerequisite is met, as in 勉強しないことには合格できない (Unless you study, you can’t pass). てからでないと focuses on sequence, meaning the second action cannot occur until the first is done, as in 確認してからでないと承諾できません (I can’t approve it unless I check first).

related.title: て初めて
related.meaning: For the first time, Not until, Only after, Only when, Only because
related.comparison: Both imply 'only after (A) did (B) happen', but differ in nuance. て初めて is typically used in a positive sense, often conveying realization or discovery, e.g. 大人になって初めて分かった ('I first understood after becoming an adult'). In contrast, てからでないと focuses on the negative—the inability to do something until a condition is met, e.g. パスポートを取ってからでないと申し込めない ('You can't apply unless you get a passport first').

synonyms.title: お～願う
synonyms.comparison: てください is a basic way to express a polite request (e.g., 'Please do…'). It is polite and neutral, and can be used with strangers or people equal in status to you. In comparison, お～願う is a very polite and formal expression, often used in professional contexts (such as to a customer or client) or formal announcements.

synonyms.title: お～ください
synonyms.comparison: てください is a polite, neutral way to say 'Please do (something),' and is used with strangers or equals. The honorific form, お〜ください, is used for superiors or customers. However, some verbs like 食べる and 行く have special honorific forms, such as 召し上がる and いらっしゃる, which are used instead.

synonyms.title: ていただけませんか
synonyms.meaning: Won't you, Could you please (Humble request)
synonyms.comparison: てください is a basic way to express a polite request (e.g., 'Please do…'). It is polite and neutral, and can be used with strangers or people equal in status to you. For more formal situations, ていただけませんか is a more polite and humble request, often used when speaking to clients, customers, or superiors. It softens the tone further—similar to saying 'Would you mind doing...for me?' in English.

antonyms.title: な
antonyms.comparison: The てください form is used to make polite requests, similar to the English 'Please do [this]'. In contrast, the dictionary/non-past form of a verb + な expresses an extremely blunt or brusque command not to do something. The two forms have opposite meanings and are completely different in politeness levels (i.e. 'please do' vs. 'don't do!').

antonyms.title: ないで
antonyms.comparison: 〜てください is a polite way to say 'Please do (something),' as in 食べてください ('Please eat'). Its negative form, 〜ないで, means 'Don't do (something),' as in 食べないで ('Don't eat'). To make this negative request polite, simply add ください, as in 食べないでください means 'Please don't eat.'

antonyms.title: ないでください
antonyms.comparison: てください is a basic way to make a polite request, meaning 'Please do (something),' while ないでください is its negative counterpart, meaning 'Please don't do (something).' For example, 食べてください means 'Please eat (this),' while 食べないでください means 'Please don't eat (this).'

related.title: てごらん
related.meaning: (Please) try to, (Please) look
related.comparison: てください is a basic way to express a polite request ('Please do…'). For example, 食べてください would mean 'Please eat (this).' It is polite and neutral, and can be used with strangers or people equal in status to you. てごらん is a gentle request form often used by adults to children, etc., that adds a nuance of 'Give this a try' or 'Try doing this (to see how you like it).'

related.title: なさい
related.meaning: Command to do
related.comparison: てください and なさい are both used to tell or ask someone to do something in Japanese. てください is a polite request, similar to 'Please (do this),' and is appropriate in neutral or formal situations, such as with strangers or people of equal status. なさい, on the other hand, is a firm command, closer to 'Do (this),' and is typically used by someone in a higher position, like teachers to students or adults to children.

related.title: Verb[て]
related.meaning: Please do for me (Casual request)
related.comparison: Both the 〜て  and 〜てください are used to make requests. てください is polite and neutral, and can be used with strangers or people of equal status. In contrast, the 〜て form is more casual and is used when speaking with friends or family. Requests using either form, especially the plain 〜て, can be softened further by adding the sentence-ending particle ね (e.g. 食べてね), which adds a gentle, caring tone.

related.title: てくれる
related.meaning: To do something for someone (usually you)
related.comparison: てください is a basic way to express a polite request (e.g., 'Please do...') For example, 食べてください would mean 'Please eat (this).' It is polite and neutral, and can be used with strangers or people equal in status to you. In comparison, てくれる (when used with rising question intonation), is a casual, gentle way of making a request to a close friend or family member.

related.title: ていく
related.meaning: To ~ and go, To go on, To start
related.comparison: てくる and ていく combine the て-form of a verb with 来る or 行く to express movement or change over time. てくる suggests movement toward the speaker or a change that started in the past and leads up to now. ていく implies movement away from the speaker or a change that begins now and continues into the future.

related.title: ばかりだ
related.meaning: (Only) continue to, Keep on -ing, More and more
related.comparison: ばかりだ attaches to the dictionary (non-past) form of a verb and is used to show that an action, often a negative one, keeps happening or is the only thing happening. In contrast, てくる combines the て-form of a verb with くる to describe an action that began in the past and has continued to the present. Unlike ばかりだ, てくる can be used for both positive and negative situations.

related.title: つづける
related.meaning: To continue
related.comparison: Both つづける and てくる can show the progression of an action over time. つづける attaches to the verb stem and focuses on the continuation of the action itself. てくる uses the て-form and emphasizes that the action started in the past and has continued up to the present.

related.title: くる
related.meaning: To come
related.comparison: The verb くる commonly expresses movement toward the speaker, similar to the English 'come.' When combined with the て form of a verb, it can be used literally (e.g., 持ってくる = 'bring') or figuratively to express progression or change over time, like the English 'come to be' (e.g., 雨が降ってくる = 'it will come to rain').

related.title: ～になる・～くなる
related.meaning: To become (Adjective)
related.comparison: Both ～になる／～くなる and てくる describe changes in state but differ in usage. ～になる／～くなる are used with nouns and adjectives to show something becoming a certain condition. てくる, used with verbs, suggests a gradual change over time. They can also be combined. For example, 面白くなってきた means 'It gradually became interesting.'

antonyms.title: ないでください
antonyms.comparison: ないでください is used to ask someone not to do something in a polite way. It's relatively neutral and can be used with strangers or people of equal status. In contrast, てくれる, when said with a rising question intonation, is a casual and gentle way to ask a favor from someone close, such as a friend or family member. It feels softer and more familiar than standard polite forms.

related.title: くれる
related.meaning: To give (me), To be given
related.comparison: くれる means 'to give' and is used when someone outside the speaker's group gives something to the speaker or their in-group. For example, プレゼントをくれた means '(someone) gave me a present.' As てくれる, it shows someone did something for the speaker's benefit, as in 日本語を教えてくれた ('(someone) taught me Japanese').

related.title: てもらう
related.meaning: To get someone to do, To have someone do
related.comparison: Both express receiving a favor, but from different perspectives. With てくれる, the subject is the giver and they do something for you. With てもらう, the subject is the receiver and they get someone to do something. For example, ケンが教えてくれた frames the action as 'Ken taught/told me.', while ケンに教えてもらった frames it as 'I had Ken teach/tell me.'.

related.title: てくれてありがとう
related.meaning: Thank you for ~ ing
related.comparison: てくれる describes someone doing something that benefits you, like 来てくれた ('He/you/they came for me.'), implying that their coming helped you. てくれてありがとう specifically expresses gratitude for the action, e.g. 来てくれてありがとう ('Thank you for coming.'). The base meaning is the same, but one contains an explicit message of thanks.

related.title: てあげる
related.meaning: To do something for someone, As a favor for
related.comparison: Both use the て form with a giving verb to describe doing a favor. てあげる is used when you or someone close to you does something for someone else, e.g. 友達に日本語を教えてあげた ('I taught my friend Japanese.'). In contrast, てくれる is used when someone else does something for you or someone close to you, e.g. 友達が日本語を教えてくれた ('My friend taught me Japanese.').

related.title: てください
related.meaning: Please do (Polite request)
related.comparison: てください is a basic way to express a polite request (e.g., 'Please do...') For example, 食べてください would mean 'Please eat (this).' It is polite and neutral, and can be used with strangers or people equal in status to you. In comparison, てくれる (when used with rising question intonation), is a casual, gentle way of making a request to a close friend or family member.

related.title: ていただけませんか
related.meaning: Won't you, Could you please (Humble request)
related.comparison: てくれる shows someone did something for your benefit, like 教えてくれた ('They taught/told me.'). ていただけませんか is a humble and polite way to make a request, e.g. 教えていただけませんか？ ('Would you kindly teach/tell me?'). Both involve benefits for the speaker but differ in tone and usage.

antonyms.title: てすみません
antonyms.comparison: These て-form patterns are opposites. てすみません expresses apology for your action, e.g., 遅れてすみません ('Sorry for being late.'), while てくれてありがとう expresses gratitude for someone else's action, e.g., 来てくれてありがとう ('Thank you for coming.'), implying that the person's coming benefitted you.

related.title: てくれる
related.meaning: To do something for someone (usually you)
related.comparison: てくれる describes someone doing something that benefits you, like 来てくれた ('He/you/they came for me.'), implying that their coming helped you. てくれてありがとう specifically expresses gratitude for the action, e.g. 来てくれてありがとう ('Thank you for coming.'). The base meaning is the same, but one contains an explicit message of thanks.

related.title: Verb + て
related.meaning: And, Then (Linking events)
related.comparison: The Verb + て form connects verbs or parts of a sentence. A common use is to express thanks by combining it with the giving verb くれる (also in the Verb + て form) and ありがとう. For example, 教えてくれてありがとう means 'Thank you for teaching me.' This is a casual form, and a more polite version would use honorific verbs (くださる or いただく) and add ございます.

related.title: くれる
related.meaning: To give (me), To be given
related.comparison: くれる is a basic verb meaning 'to give,' usually used when someone from outside the speaker's group gives something to the speaker or their in-group. For example, プレゼントをくれた means '(someone) gave me a present.' It also appears in the pattern てくれてありがとう to thank someone for a favor. For example, 日本語を教えてくれてありがとう means 'Thank you for teaching me Japanese.'

synonyms.title: ていただけませんか
synonyms.meaning: Won't you, Could you please (Humble request)
synonyms.comparison: All three are used to request something, like 'Can you do (A) for me?', but differ in tone. てくれない and てもらえない are casual and used with friends, as in 日本語を教えてくれない？ ('Can you teach me Japanese?'). ていただけませんか is much more polite, and therefore appropriate when asking someone like a boss or teacher.

antonyms.title: ないでください
antonyms.comparison: ないでください is a polite way to ask someone not to do something. It's neutral and appropriate for strangers or people of equal status. In contrast, てごらん is a gentle request form often used by adults to children, etc., that adds a nuance of 'Give this a try' or 'Try doing this (to see how you like it).'

antonyms.title: な
antonyms.comparison: The dictionary/non-past form of a verb + な expresses a blunt command not to do something. In contrast, てごらん is a gentle encouragement to try doing something. For example, 食べるな！ means 'Don't eat it!' while 食べてごらん means 'Try eating it' Both are used toward someone of lower status, such as an adult speaking to a child.

related.title: てください
related.meaning: Please do (Polite request)
related.comparison: てください is a basic way to express a polite request ('Please do…'). For example, 食べてください would mean 'Please eat (this).' It is polite and neutral, and can be used with strangers or people equal in status to you. てごらん is a gentle request form often used by adults to children, etc., that adds a nuance of 'Give this a try' or 'Try doing this (to see how you like it).'

related.title: てみる
related.meaning: To try to
related.comparison: てみる expresses trying something to see what it's like, similar to the English 'try (doing)', e.g. 食べてみる ('I'll try eating it.'). てごらん is a more directive form that encourages someone else to try something, e.g. 食べてごらん ('Try eating it.' as a gentle request). てごらん is often used by adults to children, or superiors to subordinates. Among peers, something like 食べてみて would be more common.

related.title: なさい
related.meaning: Command to do
related.comparison: Both are command forms, but with different tones. なさい is a polite but direct command, often used by teachers or parents, e.g. 食べなさい ('Eat.'). てごらん is softer and gently encourages someone to give something a try, e.g. 食べてごらん ('Try eating it'). Both are typically used from adults to children, or superiors to subordinates.

antonyms.title: はじめる
antonyms.comparison: はじめる attaches to verb stems to mean 'to begin' or 'start' doing something, e.g. 食べはじめる 'to start eating'. In contrast, てしまう・ちゃう follows the て-form and can express doing something unintentionally or completely, e.g. 食べてしまった・食べちゃった ('I ate it unintentionally.' or 'I ate it all.').

antonyms.title: 切れない
antonyms.meaning: Being unable to finish or complete
antonyms.comparison: てしまう・ちゃう and 切れない can express opposite meanings. One nuance of てしまう・ちゃう is to do something completely, e.g. 食べてしまった has one potential meaning of 'I ate it all.'. In contrast, 食べ切れなかった would mean 'I couldn't eat all of it.'. Note that てしまう・ちゃう can also express regret or an unintentional action, which 切れない does not. For example, 食べてしまった could also mean 'I ate it (even though I didn't intend to).'.

related.title: てすみません
related.meaning: I'm sorry for
related.comparison: てすみません is used to apologize for doing something, e.g. 遅れてすみません ('Sorry for being late.'). It often pairs with てしまう・ちゃう, which adds a nuance of regret or accident. For example, 遅れてしまってすみません・遅れちゃってすみません ('I'm sorry I was late.'), which implies that you didn't mean to do so and feel bad it happened.

related.title: おわる
related.meaning: To finish, To end
related.comparison: おわる attaches to verb stems to mean 'to finish doing (A)', e.g. 食べおわった ('I finished eating.'). In contrast, てしまう・ちゃう uses the て-form and can also mean doing something completely, but often adds a nuance of regret, finality, or lack of intention, e.g. 食べてしまった・食べちゃった ('I ate it and now it's gone.' or 'I didn't mean to eat it.').

related.title: 切る
related.meaning: To do something completely to the end, Utterly, Resolutely
related.comparison: 切る conveys the idea of doing something completely, which overlaps with one nuance of てしまう・ちゃう, but their usage differs. てしまう・ちゃう can imply that the action was unintentional or against one's will, which 切る cannot. In other words, 食べ切った simply means 'I ate all of it.', while 食べてしまった・食べちゃった also can have the meaning of mean 'I ended up eating it (even though I didn't intend to).', which 食べ切った cannot.

related.title: つい
related.meaning: Accidentally, Unconsciously, Against one's better judgement
related.comparison: つい and てしまう・ちゃう can both express doing something unintentionally or against one's better judgment. つい appears at the beginning of a sentence or phrase, while てしまう・ちゃう is a verb ending. They are often used together to emphasize the nuance of regret or unintentionally, e.g. つい食べてしまった・つい食べちゃった would imply 'I ended up eating it (even though I didn't mean to).'.

related.title: 上がる・上げる
related.meaning: Something is finished, To finish something, Something has come to an end, To do something completely, To do something through
related.comparison: 上がる・上げる can express the idea of completing an action, which overlaps with てしまう・ちゃう, but they differ in nuance. てしまう・ちゃう can express regret or lack of control, while 上がる・上げる focuses on the act of producing a finished result. For example, 書き上げる 'finish writing'. In contrast, てしまう・ちゃう can be used with any verb, regardless of whether a tangible result is produced.

synonyms.title: てならない
synonyms.meaning: Very, Extremely, Can't help but do
synonyms.comparison: Both express uncontrollable emotion. てならない is formal and written-style, as in 気になってならない (I can’t stop worrying). てしょうがない is common in speech, as in 会いたくてしょうがない (I can’t help wanting to see you).

related.title: ざるを得ない
related.meaning: Can't help doing, Have no choice but
related.comparison: ざるを得ない and てしょうがない both express inevitability, but differ in cause and nuance. ざるを得ない conveys compulsion due to external pressure or lack of alternatives, as in 行かざるを得ない (I have no choice but to go), while てしょうがない expresses an overpowering personal emotion or state, as in 寂しくてしょうがない (I can’t help feeling lonely).

related.title: てたまらない
related.meaning: Can't help but want to, Very, Extremely
related.comparison: Both express intense feelings. てたまらない emphasizes degree, as in かゆくてたまらない (It’s so itchy I can’t stand it). てしょうがない also shows intensity but leans toward inevitability, as in 暇でしょうがない (I’m unbearably bored).

related.title: ことに
related.meaning: Extremely, Especially, To my ...
related.comparison: Both can express emotion, but ことに highlights the emotional tone of a situation, e.g. 嬉しいことに、試験に合格しました 'To my delight, I passed the test.' てしょうがない expresses a strong, almost uncontrollable personal feeling, e.g. 帰りたくてしょうがない 'I want to go home so badly I can't help it.'

related.title: ずにはいられない
related.meaning: Cannot help doing something, Cannot resist doing something
related.comparison: Both express uncontrollable actions or feelings, but differ in nuance. ずにはいられない is about being compelled to act (lit. 'unable not to act'), e.g. '彼を応援せずにはいられない' ('I can't help but root for him'). てしょうがない emphasizes a state or feeling that overwhelms you, e.g. '暑くてしょうがない' ('It's unbearably hot') or '会いたくてしょうがない' ('I want to see you so much I can't bear it').

related.title: てはかなわない
related.meaning: It's unbearable when, It'd be unbearable if, I can't stand it when, I couldn’t stand it if
related.comparison: Both describe situations that feel difficult or unpleasant, and can overlap in some usages. In particular, てはかなわない is used when a situation is practically unmanageable or intolerable, focusing on how an external condition makes normal life or work hard to continue, as in こんなに暑くてはかなわない (‘It’s so hot that I can’t deal with it.’). In contrast, てしょうがない focuses on an internal, uncontrollable emotions, as in 会いたくてinternal, uncontrollable emotions, as in 会いたくてしょうがない (‘I miss you so much I can't bear it.'). てはかなわない also sounds somewhat more formal.

related.title: ざるを得ない
related.meaning: Can't help doing, Have no choice but
related.comparison: ざるを得ない expresses unavoidable action due to external circumstances, as in 行かざるを得ない (I have no choice but to go). By contrast, てたまらない conveys an intense emotional feeling that is hard to endure, as in 会いたくてたまらない (I can’t help wanting to see you). Both imply compulsion, but one is situational, the other emotional.

related.title: てしょうがない
related.meaning: Extremely, Very, Really, It can't be helped
related.comparison: Both express intense feelings. てたまらない emphasizes degree, as in かゆくてたまらない (It’s so itchy I can’t stand it). てしょうがない also shows intensity but leans toward inevitability, as in 暇でしょうがない (I’m unbearably bored).

related.title: ことに
related.meaning: Extremely, Especially, To my ...
related.comparison: Both ことに and てたまらない express emotion, but in different ways. ことに highlights the speaker's emotional response to a situation, usually following an adjective or verb, e.g. 悲しいことに、誰も来なかった 'Sadly, no one came.' てたまらない conveys an overwhelming personal feeling, often physical or emotional, e.g. 会いたくてたまらない 'I want to see you so badly I can't stand it.'

related.title: てならない
related.meaning: Very, Extremely, Can't help but do
related.comparison: Both express strong emotions. てならない often implies natural, uncontrollable feelings and sounds formal, as in 心配でならない (I can’t help but worry). てたまらない emphasizes unbearable intensity, often colloquial, as in 会いたくてたまらない (I’m dying to see you).

related.title: ないわけにはいかない
related.slug: ないわけにはいかない
related.meaning: Can't not, No way to avoid
related.comparison: Both describe situations beyond control, but differ in meaning. てたまらない expresses unbearable feelings or desires, often casually, as in 会いたくてたまらない (I miss you so much I can’t stand it). ないわけにはいかない shows unavoidable obligation due to duty or circumstances, as in 助けないわけにはいかない (I can’t very well not help them).

related.title: Adj限りだ
related.meaning: Extremely, Really, Very, As ... as one can be
related.comparison: Both convey extremes but differ in tone. Adj限りだ attaches to adjectives to show feelings at the utmost, often formal, as in 嬉しい限りだ (I’m extremely happy). てたまらない highlights intense emotions or desires that feel unbearable, used more in casual speech, as in 会いたくてたまらない (I miss you so much I can’t bear it).

related.title: ずにはいられない
related.meaning: Cannot help doing something, Cannot resist doing something
related.comparison: Both express uncontrollable actions or feelings, but differ in nuance. ずにはいられない is about being compelled to act (lit. 'unable not to act'), e.g. '彼を応援せずにはいられない' ('I can't help but root for him'). てたまらない emphasizes a state or feeling that overwhelms you, e.g. '暑くてたまらない' ('It's unbearably hot') or '会いたくてたまらない' ('I want to see you so much I can't bear it').

antonyms.title: てくれてありがとう
antonyms.comparison: These て-form patterns are opposites. てすみません expresses apology for your action, e.g., 遅れてすみません ('Sorry for being late.'), while てくれてありがとう expresses gratitude for someone else's action, e.g., 来てくれてありがとう ('Thank you for coming.'), implying that the person's coming benefitted you.

related.title: てしまう・ちゃう
related.meaning: To do something by accident, To finish completely・totally, Unfortunately
related.comparison: てすみません is used to apologize for doing something, e.g. 遅れてすみません ('Sorry for being late.'). It often pairs with てしまう・ちゃう, which adds a nuance of regret or accident. For example, 遅れてしまってすみません・遅れちゃってすみません ('I'm sorry I was late.'), which implies that you didn't mean to do so and feel bad it happened.

related.title: ようが～まいが
related.meaning: Even if, No matter what, Whether or not, [Emphatic], Volitional + が
related.comparison: Both can show determination against conditions, but differ in use. てでも expresses strong will to do something even at personal cost, as in 雨に濡れてでも行きたい (I want to go even if I get drenched). ようが～まいが lists two alternatives and insists the result won’t change, as in 雨が降ろうが降るまいが試合は行われる (Whether it rains or not, the match will be held).

related.title: にしても
related.meaning: Even though, Even if, Even when, Also
related.comparison: にしても and てでも both express determination in hypothetical or extreme situations. にしても presents a condition with contrast or concession, e.g. 行くにしても気が進まない ('Even if I go, I don't feel like it'). てでも emphasizes doing something even if it requires an extreme measure, e.g. 会うためなら借金してでも行く ('I'd even go into debt just to see them').

related.title: たって
related.meaning: Even if, Even though, No matter how
related.comparison: ては often marks a condition with a negative or cautionary result, as in 無駄遣いしては貯金できない (If you waste money, you can’t save). たって means even if or no matter, used casually like ても but with a rougher tone, as in 誰に聞いたって分からない (Even if you ask anyone, they won’t know).

related.title: ていては
related.meaning: If one keeps doing something, When, If something is...
related.comparison: ていては shows the continuation or repetition of an action and its negative consequences, often with a nuance of warning, as in 遊んでいては合格できない (If you keep playing, you won’t pass). ては marks a conditional result without the progressive sense, and can imply either positive or negative outcomes, as in 褒めてはやる気を出させた (By praising him, I motivated him).

related.title: ようでは・ようじゃ
related.meaning: If, If it is true that
related.comparison: ようでは・ようじゃ expresses concern or dissatisfaction, implying that if things stay as they are, the outcome will be undesirable, as in この成績では合格できないようじゃないか (With these grades, you won’t pass, will you?). ては introduces a condition leading to a result, which can be neutral or negative, as in 遊んでいては合格できない (If you keep playing, you won’t pass).

synonyms.title: てしょうがない
synonyms.meaning: Extremely, Very, Really, It can't be helped
synonyms.comparison: Both express uncontrollable emotion. てならない is formal and written-style, as in 気になってならない (I can’t stop worrying). てしょうがない is common in speech, as in 会いたくてしょうがない (I can’t help wanting to see you).

related.title: ないわけにはいかない
related.slug: ないわけにはいかない
related.meaning: Can't not, No way to avoid
related.comparison: Both suggest lack of control, but differ in usage. てならない expresses strong emotions welling up, as in 残念でならない (I can’t help but feel regret). ないわけにはいかない expresses unavoidable obligation, as in 約束したから行かないわけにはいかない (Since I promised, I can’t not go).

related.title: ざるを得ない
related.meaning: Can't help doing, Have no choice but
related.comparison: Both てならない and ざるを得ない convey inevitability, but differ in source. てならない expresses uncontrollable emotion or sensation, as in 会いたくてならない (I can’t help wanting to see them), while ざるを得ない indicates being forced to act due to circumstance or necessity, as in 行かざるを得ない (I have no choice but to go).

related.title: てやまない
related.meaning: Never cease to, Always, Sincerely, Greatly, From the bottom of one's heart
related.comparison: Both express strong feelings that continue naturally, but the nuance is different. てやまない is used for enduring, often positive feelings such as hope, respect, or wishes that persist over time, typically in written or formal contexts, as in 彼の幸せを祈ってやまない ('I continue to wish for his happiness.'). てならない expresses an intense feeling that arises uncontrollably at the moment, often emotional or physical, e.g. 不安でならない ('I cannot help feeling anxious.').

related.title: ことに
related.meaning: Extremely, Especially, To my ...
related.comparison: ことに and てならない both express emotional intensity, but differ in focus. ことに emphasizes the emotional tone of the situation itself, e.g. 悔しいことに、負けてしまった 'Regretfully, I lost.' てならない expresses a deeply felt, uncontrollable emotion, e.g. 心配でならない 'I can't help but worry.'

related.title: ずにはいられない
related.meaning: Cannot help doing something, Cannot resist doing something
related.comparison: Both express uncontrollable actions or feelings, but differ in nuance. ずにはいられない is about being compelled to act (lit. 'unable not to act'), e.g. '彼を応援せずにはいられない' ('I can't help but root for him'). てならない emphasizes a state or feeling that overwhelms you, e.g. '暑くてならない' ('It's unbearably hot') or '会いたくてならない' ('I want to see you so much I can't bear it').

related.title: てたまらない
related.meaning: Can't help but want to, Very, Extremely
related.comparison: Both express strong emotions. てならない often implies natural, uncontrollable feelings and sounds formal, as in 心配でならない (I can’t help but worry). てたまらない emphasizes unbearable intensity, often colloquial, as in 会いたくてたまらない (I’m dying to see you).

related.title: Adj限りだ
related.meaning: Extremely, Really, Very, As ... as one can be
related.comparison: Both convey extremity of feeling. Adj限りだ attaches to adjectives for maximum intensity, as in 嬉しい限りだ (I’m extremely happy). てならない attaches to adjectives or verbs for uncontrollable emotions, as in 不安でならない (I can’t help but feel uneasy).

related.title: てはかなわない
related.meaning: It's unbearable when, It'd be unbearable if, I can't stand it when, I couldn’t stand it if
related.comparison: Both describe situations that feel difficult or unpleasant, and can overlap in some usages. In particular, てはかなわない is used when a situation is practically unmanageable or intolerable, focusing on how an external condition makes normal life or work hard to continue, as in こんなに暑くてはかなわない (‘It’s so hot that I can’t deal with it.’). In contrast, てならない focuses on an internal, uncontrollable emotions, as in 会いたくてinternal, uncontrollable emotions, as in 会いたくてならない (‘I miss you so much I can't bear it.'). てはかなわない also sounds somewhat more formal.

antonyms.title: なくてはならない
antonyms.comparison: てはいけない expresses prohibition, meaning something 'must not' or 'cannot' be done. In contrast, なくてはならない expresses obligation or something that 'must' be done. These forms can be tricky for learners, as the negative form なくてはならない (literally, 'it's not good if you don't') means 'must,' while the affirmative form てはいけない ('it's not good if you do') means 'must not.'

antonyms.title: なくちゃ・なきゃ
antonyms.comparison: てはいけない expresses prohibition, meaning something 'must not' or 'cannot' be done. In contrast, なくちゃ and なきゃ are casual contractions of なくてはいけない and なければいけない, which express obligation—saying that something 'must' be done. The two forms differ in both formality (formal vs. casual) and meaning ('must not' vs. 'must').

antonyms.title: なくてはいけない
antonyms.comparison: なくてはいけない expresses necessity or obligation, something that 'must' be done. In contrast, てはいけない means something 'must not' or 'cannot' be done. These forms can be tricky for learners: なくてはいけない looks negative but means 'must' (literally, 'it's not good if you don't'), while てはいけない looks affirmative but means 'must not' (literally, 'it's not good if you do').

antonyms.title: なければいけない
antonyms.comparison: てはいけない is used to express prohibition, meaning that something 'must not' or 'cannot' be done. In contrast, なければいけない expresses obligation, something 'must' be done. These forms can be tricky for learners, as なければいけない is negative in form but means 'must' (literally, 'it's not good if you don't'), while てはいけない is affirmative in form but means 'must not' (literally, 'it's not good if you do').

antonyms.title: なければならない
antonyms.comparison: てはいけない is used to express prohibition, meaning that something 'must not' or 'cannot' be done. なければならない expresses the opposite meaning, i.e., that something 'must' be done. These forms can be tricky for learners, as なければならない appears negative but means 'must' (literally, 'it's not good if you don't'), while てはいけない appears affirmative but means 'must not' (literally, 'it's not good if you do'). (いけない and ならない themselves are essentially synonymous, though ならない is somewhat more formal/written-style.)

related.title: てはならない
related.meaning: Must not, Cannot, Should not
related.comparison: Both てはいけない and てはならない express prohibition, saying something 'must not' or 'cannot' be done. The two forms are equivalent in meaning, with the only difference being that ならない is slightly more formal and therefore more often found in the written language, such as in rules or regulations.

related.title: べからず
related.meaning: Do not, Must not, Should not, Cannot (べからざる)
related.comparison: べからず and てはいけない are similar in that they both express prohibition, or the idea that you 'must not' or 'cannot' do something. The difference is in formality and how they connect to other words. べからず is a highly formal and archaic expression that nowadays is found mostly in regulations, proverbs, and so forth. It follows directly after the plain form of a verb, e.g. 入るべからず ('Do not enter.'). In contrast, てはいけない is far more common in the modern language, and connects to the て-form of verbs, e.g. 入ってはいけない ('You must not enter.')

related.title: べきではない
related.meaning: Should not, Must not , Ought not to
related.comparison: てはいけない expresses prohibition, meaning something 'must not' or 'cannot' be done. In contrast, べきではない suggests that something 'should not' be done, often implying it's improper or that there's a better option. While both express negative recommendations, てはいけない is more authoritative and used for rules, whereas べきではない carries a more subjective nuance.

related.title: なくてもいい
related.meaning: Don't have to
related.comparison: なくてもいい means something is optional; 'it's okay not to (do something).' In contrast, てはいけない expresses prohibition; 'you must not' or 'cannot' do something. For example, 食べなくてもいい means 'It's okay not to eat it,' while 食べてはいけない means 'You must not eat it.' The first gives freedom; the second sets a rule.

antonyms.title: ないではいられない
antonyms.meaning: Cannot help doing something, Cannot resist doing something
antonyms.comparison: These are opposite uses of the same pattern with いられない (cannot remain in such a state). てはいられない means cannot afford to (A), as in 休んではいられない (I can’t afford to rest). ないではいられない means cannot help but (A), as in 助けないではいられない (I can’t help but assist them).

related.title: に堪えない
related.meaning: Cannot bear to, Cannot stand to, Too much to, Cannot go on doing, Cannot tolerate
related.comparison: Both express that something cannot be endured, but the reason is different. に堪えない is used to judge that an experience is unbearable in quality or content, often in an evaluative or descriptive sense, as in 見るに堪えなかった ('It was too bad to watch.'). てはいられない expresses urgency or pressure that makes continuing the current state impossible, usually because of time limits, duties, or circumstances that require action, as in レポートがあるから遊んではいられない ('I have a report, so I can’t afford to hang out.').

related.title: わけにはいかない
related.meaning: Cannot afford to, No way, Impossible to do
related.comparison: Both express that an action can't be taken, but for different reasons. わけにはいかない implies restraint due to strong internal or societal reasons, e.g. 泣くわけにはいかない 'I can't very well cry.' てはいられない conveys urgency or pressure, suggesting that one can't allow a situation to continue, e.g. 怠けてはいられない 'I can't afford to slack off.'

related.title: てしょうがない
related.meaning: Extremely, Very, Really, It can't be helped
related.comparison: Both describe situations that feel difficult or unpleasant, and can overlap in some usages. In particular, てはかなわない is used when a situation is practically unmanageable or intolerable, focusing on how an external condition makes normal life or work hard to continue, as in こんなに暑くてはかなわない (‘It’s so hot that I can’t deal with it.’). In contrast, てしょうがない focuses on an internal, uncontrollable emotions, as in 会いたくてinternal, uncontrollable emotions, as in 会いたくてしょうがない (‘I miss you so much I can't bear it.'). てはかなわない also sounds somewhat more formal.

related.title: てならない
related.meaning: Very, Extremely, Can't help but do
related.comparison: Both describe situations that feel difficult or unpleasant, and can overlap in some usages. In particular, てはかなわない is used when a situation is practically unmanageable or intolerable, focusing on how an external condition makes normal life or work hard to continue, as in こんなに暑くてはかなわない (‘It’s so hot that I can’t deal with it.’). In contrast, てならない focuses on an internal, uncontrollable emotions, as in 会いたくてinternal, uncontrollable emotions, as in 会いたくてならない (‘I miss you so much I can't bear it.'). てはかなわない also sounds somewhat more formal.

antonyms.title: なければいけない
antonyms.comparison: These are opposites: なければいけない expresses obligation (i.e. 'must'), while てはならない means prohibition (i.e. 'must not'). For example, 行かなければいけない ('You must go.') vs. 行ってはならない ('You must not go.'). Both are formal and common in writing or structured speech, while in conversation casual forms like なきゃ and ちゃ are more common.

antonyms.title: ことだ
antonyms.comparison: Both carry an authoritative tone, as if stating a rule, but have opposite meanings. ことだ gives strong advice or recommendations, e.g. 約束は守ることだ ('You should keep your promises.') てはならない expresses strict prohibition, e.g. 約束を破ってはならない ('You must not break your promises.')

antonyms.title: なければならない
antonyms.comparison: These express opposites: なければならない 'must' vs. てはならない 'must not'. For example, 行かなければならない 'must go' is the opposite of 行ってはならない 'must not go'. Both are formal and typically used in writing or structured speech. Casual speech favors shorter forms like なきゃ and ちゃ.

related.title: てはいけない
related.meaning: Must not, May not
related.comparison: Both てはいけない and てはならない express prohibition, saying something 'must not' or 'cannot' be done. The two forms are equivalent in meaning, with the only difference being that ならない is slightly more formal and therefore more often found in the written language, such as in rules or regulations.

related.title: なくてもいい
related.meaning: Don't have to
related.comparison: なくてもいい means there is no obligation to do something, like the English 'you don't have to (A)' (or literally, 'it's okay even without').  For example, 食べなくてもいい means 'You don't have to eat it.' In contrast, てはならない expresses prohibition, like 'must not (A)' or 'cannot (A)'. For example, 食べてはならない means 'You must not eat it.'.

related.title: べからず
related.meaning: Do not, Must not, Should not, Cannot (べからざる)
related.comparison: Both indicate prohibition. べからず is old-fashioned and literary, often seen on signs or notices, as in 立入るべからず (Do not enter). てはならない is the standard modern form, still formal but usable in both speech and writing, as in 入ってはならない (You must not enter).

related.title: ように～てほしい
related.meaning: Want ~ to ~ like ~, Want ~ to be able to ~, Want ~ to ~ in the way of ~
related.comparison: Both ように～てほしい and てほしい express wanting someone else to do something, with the key difference being the presence of よう. よう adds a nuance of doing something in a certain way. For example, 忘れないでほしい ('I want you not to forget.') vs. 忘れないようにしてほしい ('I want you to make sure to not forget.', in the sense of doing what needs to be done to ensure you do not forget).

related.title: がる
related.meaning: To feel, To think, To act as if, To want, To desire
related.comparison: てほしい and がる both relate to expressing desire but are used differently. てほしい is used when you want someone to do something, e.g. これを買ってほしい ('I want you to buy this.'). がる describes what another person appears to feel, e.g. 田中さんは車を買いたがっている ('Mr. Tanaka seems to want to buy a car.').

related.title: たがる
related.meaning: To want to (Third person)
related.comparison: てほしい and たがる both express desire but in different ways. てほしい is used when the speaker wants someone else to do something, e.g. これを買ってほしい ('I want you to buy this.'). たがる combines the たい-form with がる to describe a third person's apparent desire, e.g. 田中さんは車を買いたがっている ('Tanaka seems to want to buy a car.').

related.title: がほしい
related.meaning: To want something, To be in need of
related.comparison: Both がほしい and てほしい express a want, but differ in meaning and usage. がほしい follows nouns to express the speaker's desire for something, whether physical, e.g. 金がほしい ('I want money.') or figurative, e.g. 自由がほしい ('I want freedom.'). In contrast, てほしい uses the て form of a verb to express a desire for someone else to do something, e.g. これを見てほしい ('I want you to look at this.').

related.title: たい
related.meaning: Want to do
related.comparison: Both たい and てほしい express a desire, with one key difference. The たい form is used to express your own desires, similar to the English 'I want to...' while てほしい expresses your desires for other people, or in other words, 'I want you to…' For example, 見たい would express your desire to see something, while 見てほしい would be used if you want someone else to take a look.

related.title: てごらん
related.meaning: (Please) try to, (Please) look
related.comparison: てみる expresses trying something to see what it's like, similar to the English 'try (doing)', e.g. 食べてみる ('I'll try eating it.'). てごらん is a more directive form that encourages someone else to try something, e.g. 食べてごらん ('Try eating it.' as a gentle request). てごらん is often used by adults to children, or superiors to subordinates. Among peers, something like 食べてみて would be more common.

related.title: ようにする
related.meaning: To try to, To make sure to, To endeavor to
related.comparison: Both can be translated in English as 'try (doing)', but differ in nuance. ようにする means to make an effort or form a habit, e.g. 毎朝走るようにしている ('I try to run every morning.') has the nuance of making a point of doing so. In contrast, てみる means to try something out to see the result, e.g. 走ってみた ('I tried running.'), which has the nuance of doing so to see what it was like.

related.title: Verb[volitional]とする
related.meaning: Try to, Attempt to, To be about to
related.comparison: Verb[volitional]とする and てみる are both translated as 'try', but the former implies attempting something that may not succeed, e.g. 話そうとした ('I tried to speak.'), which implies that maybe the attempt was unsuccessful. In contrast,てみる focuses on trying something to see the result, as in 話してみた ('I tried speaking.'), which has the nuance of doing so to see what it was like.

related.title: 〜ようと思う・〜おうと思う
related.slug: ようと思う-おうと思う
related.meaning: I think I will, I think I shall, Going to, Gonna (Volitional + と思う)
related.comparison: 〜ようと思う・〜おうと思う expresses intention, similar to 'I'm thinking of doing X,' e.g. 行こうと思います ('I plan to go.') てみる conveys the idea of trying something to see what it's like, e.g. 行ってみる ('I'll go and see.'). These forms can be combined, e.g. 行ってみようと思います ('I plan to go and see what it's like.')

related.title: てもいい
related.meaning: It is alright/fine/okay even if, Can/may, ~ is also okay
related.comparison: てもいい is a common grammatical pattern used to give or ask for permission. At the core of this expression is the ても conditional pattern. Though てもいい is often translated as 'Can I…' or 'May I…' in English, the literal meaning is 'Is it okay even if I...?' In this sense, the basic ても pattern has an even wider range of uses. For example, 負けてもあきらめない means '(I) won't give up even if (I) lose.'

related.title: でも
related.meaning: Or something,  Any... (with question words), No matter (who)
related.comparison: The particle でも can attach to question words to mean 'any~' as in 誰でも 'anyone' or なんでも 'anything', or follow nouns to suggest a casual option, like in お茶でも飲みませんか？ ('Want some tea or something?'). This looks similar to ても, which becomes でも after nouns and な-adjectives, but means 'even if', as in 日本語でも大丈夫です ('It's fine even if it's in Japanese.').

related.title: にせよ・にしろ
related.meaning: Even if, Even though, No matter, Whether… or
related.comparison: Both forms can express the meaning of 'even if'. ても is more versatile and commonly used in both everyday speech and writing, while にせよ・にしろ is more limited in usage, with にせよ sounding especially formal. Note that ても is often used in the pattern てもいい 'it's okay to (A)' to ask or give permission, whereas にせよ・にしろ is not used this way.

related.title: たって
related.meaning: Even if, Even though, No matter how
related.comparison: ても and たって both mean 'even if' or 'even though,' with the main difference being tone and formality. ても is widely used in both everyday speech and writing, while たって is more casual and emphatic, typically found in informal conversation. Aside from tone, their meaning is nearly identical, e.g., 行っても and 行ったって both mean 'Even if I/you/etc. go...'

related.title: たとえ〜ても
related.meaning: Even if…is the case, Supposing that
related.comparison: たとえ～ても is a stronger and more limited version of ても used in hypothetical situations. For example, たとえどこに行っても 'no matter where you might go' is a more emphatic version of どこに行っても 'wherever you go'. However, in cases where ても expresses permission, e.g. てもいい 'you can (A)', たとえ would not be used, since the meaning is not strongly hypothetical.

related.title: にしたところで
related.meaning: Even, Even for, Even from the perspective of, Even in the case of
