related.comparison: Both express concession meaning ‘even if,’ but the tone and strength differ. にしたところで is a more emphatic and formal way to say ても, often used to stress that the result will not change despite the condition, sometimes implying resignation or futility, as in 急いだところで間に合わない ('Even if we were to hurry now, we won’t make it in time.'). ても is the neutral, everyday way to say 'even if', and is used broadly without added emphasis, as in 急いでも間に合わない ('Even if we hurry, we won’t make it.').

related.title: もし
related.meaning: If (For emphasis)
related.comparison: もし and ても both express hypothetical situations, but in different ways. もし appears before a clause to emphasize the condition, similar to 'if' or 'supposing that', while ても is attached to the verb itself. Because they fill different roles, they can be used together, e.g., もし雨が降っても 'even if it rains'. もし can also pair with other conditionals like たら or ば.

related.title: ようが～まいが
related.meaning: Even if, No matter what, Whether or not, [Emphatic], Volitional + が
related.comparison: ても is an extremely versatile expression used to mean 'even if' in a wide variety of contexts, both in everyday speech and writing. In contrast, ようが～まいが is a highly formal expression that specifically means 'whether or not' and occurs primarily in written or academic language. For example 食べようが食べまいが and 食べても食べなくても both mean 'whether or not (you) eat', but the former is far more formal and emphatic.

related.title: さえ
related.meaning: Even
related.comparison: さえ and ても can both express the meaning of 'even' and sometimes overlap, especially with nouns. For example, 子どもでもできる and 子どもさえできる both mean 'Even a child can do it.'. However, they diverge when ても expresses permission, such as in 行ってもいい ('You can go.') or when さえ means 'if only', e.g. お金さえあれば 'as long as you have money'. In these cases, the two expressions are not interchangeable.

related.title: もしも～なら・もしも～でも
related.slug: もしも～なら・もしも～でも
related.meaning: If, Supposing
related.comparison: ても is a highly versatile structure meaning 'even if,' used widely in both speech and writing. In contrast, ようが～まいが is much more formal and mainly found in written or academic contexts. Both forms can express 'whether or not'. For example, 食べても食べなくても and 食べようが食べまいが both mean 'whether or not (you) eat', but the latter is more emphatic and formal.

related.title: としても
related.meaning: Even if (we assume), Assuming,  Also as
related.comparison: Both forms can express the meaning of 'even if' or 'even though.' ても is more versatile and commonly appears in expressions like てもいい 'it's okay to (A)' to ask or give permission. としても has a stronger hypothetical nuance similar to 'Even assuming that…', and can also serve as a more emphatic version of として. For example, 私としても大変だ ('It's tough even for me.').

related.title: にしても
related.meaning: Even though, Even if, Even when, Also
related.comparison: Both forms can mean 'even if' or 'even though,' but differ in nuance. ても is more versatile and often appears in expressions like てもいい 'it's okay to (A)' for asking or giving permission. にしても carries a stronger hypothetical tone, like 'Even supposing that...' which ても alone doesn't necessarily convey. Both are used with question words, e.g. どこに行っても and どこに行くにしても 'no matter where you go', with the latter sounding more hypothetical.

related.title: Verb + てもいい
related.meaning: It's okay to, It's alright to, Can/may
related.comparison: ても has the core meaning of 'even if.' One common use is in Verb + てもいい to ask or give permission. For example, 食べてもいいですか？ means 'Can/May I eat this?' (literally, 'Is it okay even if I eat this?') More broadly, ても can also express contrast. For example, 寒くても行きます means 'I'll go even if it's cold.'

related.title: 〜でも 〜でも
related.meaning: Whether ~ or, Neither ~ or
related.comparison: 〜でも〜でも and ～ても～なくても both express 'whether or not'. 〜でも〜でも shows multiple possibilities, e.g. クッキーでもケーキでも甘いものは食べたい ('I want sweets, whether it's cookies or cake.'). ～ても～なくても shows opposition, e.g. 行っても行かなくても大丈夫 ('It's okay whether you go or not.').

related.title: ないまでも
related.meaning: May not … but (at least), Even though not, Even if not
related.comparison: Both express that something holds regardless of degree, but the framing differs. ないまでも means ‘even if not to that extent’ and concedes a weaker case while still affirming the main point, often implying partial satisfaction, as in 完璧ではないまでも、十分だ ('Even if it’s not perfect, it’s good enough.'). ～ても～なくても states full irrelevance of the condition, meaning the result is the same whether it happens or not, as in 言われても言われなくとも私はやる ('I’ll do it whether I'm told or not.').

related.title: にしても～にしても
related.meaning: Regardless of whether
related.comparison: ～ても～なくても and にしても～にしても are similar in that both can mean 'regardless of whether it's (A) or (B)'. The difference is that ～ても～なくても contrasts doing something with not doing it, e.g. 行っても行かなくても ('whether you go or not'), while にしても～にしても contrasts two opposing words or expressions, e.g. 勝つにしても負けるにしても ('whether we win or lose').

related.title: にしろ～にしろ
related.meaning: Whether… or
related.comparison: ～ても～なくても and にしろ～にしろ can both mean 'whether it's (A) or (B)', but ～ても～なくても contrasts something with its negative, e.g. 食べても食べなくても ('whether you eat or not'), while にしろ～にしろ typically contrasts two opposing words or ideas, e.g. 勝つにしろ負けるにしろ ('whether we win or lose').

related.title: にかかわらず
related.meaning: Regardless of, Whether or, No matter, With or without
related.comparison: ～ても～なくても and にかかわらず both express that something holds regardless of condition. ～ても～なくても contrasts an action with its negative, e.g. 行っても行かなくても ('whether you go or not'), while にかかわらず can attach to a single noun, e.g. 天気にかかわらず ('regardless of the weather'), or a pair of opposing concepts, e.g. 早いか遅いかに関わらず ('whether early or late').

related.title: やら～やら
related.meaning: Whether or, Things like, And … so on
related.comparison: ～ても～なくても and やら～やら both present multiple possibilities, but ～ても～なくても contrasts something with its negative, e.g. 行っても行かなくてもいい ('Go or don't, it's fine'), while やら～やら loosely lists alternatives, which may include opposites, e.g. 行くのやら行かないのやら ('whether he'll go or not'), or not, e.g. 本やら辞書やら ('things like books and dictionaries').

related.title: ようが～まいが
related.meaning: Even if, No matter what, Whether or not, [Emphatic], Volitional + が
related.comparison: ～ても～なくても and ようが～まいが are identical in meaning, both expressing the idea of 'whether or not', with the difference that ～ても～なくても is more casual and colloquial, while ようが～まいが is more formal/literary and emphatic. For example, 行っても行かなくても and 行こうが行くまいが mean the same thing but differ in tone.

related.title: を問わず
related.meaning: Regardless of, Whether or not, No matter, With or without
related.comparison: ～ても～なくても and を問わず both express 'regardless of', but ～ても～なくても contrasts doing something with not doing it, e.g. 雨が降っても降らなくても ('whether it rains or not'), while を問わず states that something holds true regardless of category or attribute, e.g. 年齢を問わず ('regardless of age').

related.title: かどうか
related.meaning: Whether or not, If...or not
related.comparison: かどうか expresses uncertainty, like 'whether or not', as in 行くかどうか分からない ('I don't know whether or not they'll go.'). ～ても～なくても presents two opposite possibilities and says the result is unchanged, like 降っても降らなくても行く ('Whether it rains or not, I'm going.').

related.title: てあげる
related.meaning: To do something for someone, As a favor for
related.comparison: Both use the て form to talk about favors, but with different perspectives. てあげる focuses on the giver (you or someone close to you) doing something for someone else. てもらう focuses on the receiver having something done for them. For example, ケンに教えてあげた ('I taught/told Ken.') vs. ケンに教えてもらった ('Ken taught me.').

related.title: もらう
related.meaning: To receive, To get, To obtain
related.comparison: もらう is a basic verb meaning 'to receive,' often used when the speaker or someone in their in-group receives something from someone outside the group. For example, プレゼントをもらった means 'I received a present.' When used as てもらう after a verb in the て form, it shows that the speaker received the benefit of an action, as in 日本語を教えてもらった ('I had someone teach me Japanese').

related.title: てくれる
related.meaning: To do something for someone (usually you)
related.comparison: Both express receiving a favor, but from different perspectives. With てくれる, the subject is the giver and they do something for you. With てもらう, the subject is the receiver and they get someone to do something. For example, ケンが教えてくれた frames the action as 'Ken taught/told me.', while ケンに教えてもらった frames it as 'I had Ken teach/tell me.'.

related.title: ていただけませんか
related.meaning: Won't you, Could you please (Humble request)
related.comparison: てもらう describes getting someone to do something for you, e.g. 教えてもらった ('I had them teach/tell me.'). ていただけませんか is a polite way to ask for such a favor, as in 教えていただけませんか？ ('Would you kindly teach/tell me?'). Both involve receiving help but differ in politeness and usage.

synonyms.title: てもかまわない
synonyms.meaning: It doesn't matter if, I don't mind
synonyms.comparison: Both てもいい and てもかまわない are used to give or ask for permission, but with slight differences in nuance and tone. てもいい is a straightforward way to say 'It's okay' or 'Is it okay to (do this?)' On the other hand, てもかまわない adds a nuance of 'Do you mind if…' or 'it doesn't matter if…' てもかまわない sounds more polite, especially in it's かまいません form.

synonyms.title: Verb + てもいい
synonyms.meaning: It's okay to, It's alright to, Can/may
synonyms.comparison: The てもいい pattern expresses that something is acceptable or allowed. With verbs, it's used to give or ask for permission, such as 食べてもいい？ (Can I eat this?') It can also be used with nouns or adjectives to ask if a condition is acceptable, like 冷たくてもいい? ('Is it okay if it's cold?) when referring to a drink, for example.

related.title: ても
related.meaning: Even if, Even though
related.comparison: てもいい is a common grammatical pattern used to give or ask for permission. At the core of this expression is the ても conditional pattern. Though てもいい is often translated as 'Can I…' or 'May I…' in English, the literal meaning is 'Is it okay even if I...?' In this sense, the basic ても pattern has an even wider range of uses. For example, 負けてもあきらめない means '(I) won't give up even if (I) lose.'

synonyms.title: Verb + てもいい
synonyms.meaning: It's okay to, It's alright to, Can/may
synonyms.comparison: Both Verb + てもいい and てもかまわない are used to give or ask for permission. The difference is tone and formality. てもいい is a basic, everyday expression, while てもかまわない is slightly more formal and often used in writing or professional contexts to mean 'It doesn't matter if…' or 'It's fine if…'

synonyms.title: てもいい
synonyms.meaning: It is alright/fine/okay even if, Can/may, ~ is also okay
synonyms.comparison: Both てもいい and てもかまわない are used to give or ask for permission, but with slight differences in nuance and tone. てもいい is a straightforward way to say 'It's okay' or 'Is it okay to (do this?)' On the other hand, てもかまわない adds a nuance of 'Do you mind if…' or 'it doesn't matter if…' てもかまわない sounds more polite, especially in it's かまいません form.

related.title: でよければ
related.meaning: If it is alright, If you don't mind, If you would like, If possible
related.comparison: でよければ and てもかまわない both express acceptance, but from different perspectives. でよければ is most often used to modestly present something as an option, e.g. 私でよければお手伝いします ('If I'm acceptable, I'll help'). In contrast, てもかまわない is used to allow or grant permission, e.g. 使ってもかまわない ('I don't mind if you use it').

related.title: てならない
related.meaning: Very, Extremely, Can't help but do
related.comparison: Both express strong feelings that continue naturally, but the nuance is different. てやまない is used for enduring, often positive feelings such as hope, respect, or wishes that persist over time, typically in written or formal contexts, as in 彼の幸せを祈ってやまない ('I continue to wish for his happiness.'). てならない expresses an intense feeling that arises uncontrollably at the moment, often emotional or physical, e.g. 不安でならない ('I cannot help feeling anxious.').

related.title: ばよかった
related.meaning: Should have, I wish, It would have been better to
related.comparison: Though they may look similar, てよかった and ばよかった express opposite feelings. てよかった conveys relief or happiness about something that happened, e.g. 行ってよかった ('I'm glad I went.' or literally 'It was good that I went.'). In contrast, ばよかった expresses regret about something that didn't happen, e.g., 行けばよかった ('I wish I had gone.' or literally, 'It would have been nice if I went.').

related.title: にして①
related.meaning: At, Over, In, Only (after, when, or because)
related.comparison: These expressions are often found together because their meanings complement each other. にして suggests a significant occasion or example of something, while て初めて describes something happening for the first time 'only after' a certain event. Together, they can express an idea like 結婚10年目にして初めて二人で旅行した ('They vacationed together for the first time in their 10th year of marriage.').

related.title: にして①
related.meaning: At, Over, In, Only (after, when, or because)
related.comparison: These expressions are often found together because their meanings complement each other. にして suggests a significant occasion or example of something, while て初めて describes something happening for the first time 'only after' a certain event. Together, they can express an idea like 結婚10年目にして初めて二人で旅行した ('They vacationed together for the first time in their 10th year of marriage.').

related.title: まず
related.meaning: To start with, Firstly
related.comparison: まず means 'first' or 'to begin with' and is used to express something that happens or should happen before something else. In contrast, て初めて means 'not until (A) did (B) happen' or 'for the first time after (A), (B)', e.g. 大人になって初めて分かった ('I understood for the first time after becoming an adult.').

related.title: てから
related.meaning: Once...is done, Once...happens, After doing
related.comparison: て初めて and てから both use the Verb + て form to show actions happening in sequence, but with different nuances. てから simply shows that one event happened after another, while て初めて emphasizes that the second action happened 'only after' or 'not until' meaning 'for the first time after' the previous event.

related.title: 上で
related.meaning: After, Upon
related.comparison: Both show one action leading to another, but with different focus. て初めて marks a first experience or realization, e.g. 大人になって初めて分かった ('I only understood after becoming an adult.') 上で shows a necessary step before the next action, e.g. 社長に相談した上で決めましょう ('Let's decide after consulting the president.')

related.title: てからでないと
related.meaning: (Not) unless ~ cannot, (Not) until ~ cannot
related.comparison: Both imply 'only after (A) did (B) happen', but differ in nuance. て初めて is typically used in a positive sense, often conveying realization or discovery, e.g. 大人になって初めて分かった ('I first understood after becoming an adult'). In contrast, てからでないと focuses on the negative—the inability to do something until a condition is met, e.g. パスポートを取ってからでないと申し込めない ('You can't apply unless you get a passport first').

synonyms.title: で
synonyms.comparison: The particle で has two main uses: the first is indicating the location where an action takes place (e.g., 家で遊ぶ = 'play at home'). The second is marking the means or instrument used to perform an action (e.g., ボールで遊ぶ = 'play with a ball'). These differences can usually be inferred from context, so it's recommended to not stress this too much.

related.title: をもって
related.meaning: As of, With, Effective from
related.comparison: を以て can be though of as a formal version of the basic particle で when used to mean 'at this point in time', e.g. これ（で・を以て）閉会します ('With this, we will end the meeting.'). Note that をもって would not typically be used with the meaning of で indicating 'place of an action', e.g. 大手企業で働く 'to work at a major corporation'.

related.title: を以て
related.meaning: By means of, With, By, Via, In view of
related.comparison: を以て can be though of as a highly formal/written-style version of the basic particle で when used to mean 'with'/'by means of', e.g. 鉛筆で書く 'write with a pencil' vs. 死を以て償う 'to atone (e.g. for a sin) with one's death' or 'at this point in time', e.g. これ（で・を以て）閉会します ('With this, we will end the meeting.'). Note that を以て would not typically be used with the meaning of で indicating 'place of an action', e.g. 大手企業で働く 'to work at a major corporation'.

related.title: Verb[て]・Noun[で] + B
related.meaning: By, With, On, By means of, ~ing with
related.comparison: The particle で follows a noun to describe the means or method of an action (e.g., ペンで書く = 'to write with a pen'). A verb in the て form can express a similar nuance (e.g., ペンを使って書く = 'to write using a pen,' 歩いて行く = 'to go by walking'). The meaning is the same, but the form differs depending on whether the 'method' is expressed with a noun or a verb.

related.title: のも当然だ
related.meaning: It is only natural that, It is no wonder
related.comparison: Both express that something is natural or self-evident. て当然だ is matter-of-fact, as in 負けて当然だ (It’s no wonder you lost). のも当然だ can feel slightly more emphatic, as in 負けるのも当然だ (It’s only natural to lose).

related.title: のももっともだ
related.meaning: May well, Have or has good reason for, No wonder, … Is only natural
related.comparison: Both convey inevitability, but nuance differs. て当然だ emphasizes strong expectation, as in 負けて当然だ (It’s no wonder you lost). のももっともだ shows the speaker’s empathy or understanding, as in 怒るのももっともだ (It makes sense they’d be angry).

synonyms.title: です
synonyms.comparison: です is often used as the polite form of だ, which is similar in meaning to 'is' or 'to be' in English. It's polite but not overly formal, and it's often used in both speech and writing. In contrast, である is a formal (but not polite) expression mainly used in writing or formal speeches. One key difference is that である can appear in front of nouns in modifying clauses, like in 日本人である留学生 ('an exchange student who is Japanese').

synonyms.title: だ
synonyms.comparison: The main difference between だ and である is formality. だ is casual and common in conversation, while である is more formal and mainly used in writing. Both だ and である follow nouns or noun phrases, but である has a more literary tone. である slight differs from the verb ある (to exist) in that である is a fixed connector.

related.title: たる
related.meaning: To be (in a position of), As a, For
related.comparison: Both are formal forms of 'to be' used in writing. である is the standard formal equivalent of だ, e.g. 彼は学生である ('He is a student.') たる emphasizes qualification or the qualities appropriate to a role, e.g. 指導者たる者 ('A person worthy of being a leader')

related.title: がある
related.meaning: To be, There is
related.comparison: がある expresses the existence or possession of inanimate objects, similar to saying 'there is' or 'I have' in English. In contrast, である is comparable to (and is, in fact, an alternate form of) the casual だ or polite です, and is therefore equivalent to the English "it is". In other words, ピアノがある means 'There's a piano' or 'I have a piano', but ピアノである means 'It's a piano' in formal/written language (the spoken polite equivalent would be ピアノです).

synonyms.title: にせよ・にしろ
synonyms.meaning: Even if, Even though, No matter, Whether… or
synonyms.comparison: Both can mean even if or no matter, but nuance differs. にせよ・にしろ stresses the speaker’s strong insistence, as in 学生にせよ社会人にせよ責任はある (Even if you’re a student or a worker, you have responsibility). であれ is more formal and written, as in 理由が何であれ許されない (No matter what the reason, it’s unforgivable).

synonyms.title: にしろ～にしろ
synonyms.comparison: にしろ～にしろ can follow verbs and broader phrases, as in 勉強するにしろ遊ぶにしろ全力でやれ (Whether you study or play, do it wholeheartedly). であれ follows nouns, な-adjectives, or wh-words and is more formal, as in どんな結果であれ受け入れるしかない (Whatever the result, we must accept it).

related.title: であれ〜であれ
related.meaning: Whether or, Or, No matter which, In either case
related.comparison: These are essentially the same grammar point, expressing no matter if one thing is true, the result does not change. The difference is that であれ～であれ is specifically used when comparing multiple options, e.g. 子どもであれ大人であれ 'no matter if they're a child or adult', while であれ is used with single examples, e.g. 大人であれ 'even if they're an adult' or with question expressions, e.g. 誰であれ 'no matter who it is'.

related.title: 何しろ
related.meaning: No wonder, After all, In fact, Because, At any rate
related.comparison: 何しろ appears at the start of a sentence to give a reason, like after all, as in 何しろ忙しいので行けません (After all, I’m busy, so I can’t go). であれ follows nouns, な-adjectives, or wh-words to mean even if or no matter, often in formal contexts, as in 理由が何であれ許されない (No matter what the reason, it’s unforgivable).

related.title: とはいえ
related.meaning: Though, Although (I admit that), Be that as it may, Nonetheless
related.comparison: とはいえ and であれ are similar in that they both express a sort of contrast, but とはいえ 'although (one could say)' specifically qualifies a statement by saying one thing might be true, but there is more to the matter, e.g. 日本人とはいえ、フランスで生まれ育った ('Though she may be Japanese, she was born and raised in France.'). Though they can overlap in some cases, であれ 'even'/'no matter' is often used together with question words, e.g. 理由は何であれ 'no matter the reason', while とはいえ cannot be used this way.

synonyms.title: にせよ・にしろ
synonyms.meaning: Even if, Even though, No matter, Whether… or
synonyms.comparison: Both mean whether…or…, but tone differs. にせよ・にしろ can be used in everyday speech or writing, as in 男にせよ女にせよ参加できる (Whether men or women, they can join). であれ〜であれ is more formal and academic, usually with nouns or な-adjectives, as in 貧乏であれ金持ちであれ人間は同じだ (Whether poor or rich, people are the same).

synonyms.title: にしろ～にしろ
synonyms.comparison: Both list alternatives meaning whether (A) or (B), with the result unchanged. にしろ～にしろ is more common and conversational, as in 雨にしろ雪にしろ試合は行う (Whether rain or snow, the game will be held). であれ〜であれ is more formal, used in writing, as in 学生であれ教師であれ責任は同じだ (Whether student or teacher, the responsibility is the same).

related.title: であれ
related.meaning: Even if, Even, No matter
related.comparison: These are essentially the same grammar point, expressing no matter if one thing is true, the result does not change. The difference is that であれ～であれ is specifically used when comparing multiple options, e.g. 子どもであれ大人であれ 'no matter if they're a child or adult', while であれ is used with single examples, e.g. 大人であれ 'even if they're an adult' or with question expressions, e.g. 誰であれ 'no matter who it is'.

related.title: 何しろ
related.meaning: No wonder, After all, In fact, Because, At any rate
related.comparison: 何しろ introduces reasons or explanations, like after all. であれ〜であれ presents alternatives to show the result is unaffected, as in 男であれ女であれ歓迎する (Whether man or woman, they are welcome).

related.title: とはいえ
related.meaning: Though, Although (I admit that), Be that as it may, Nonetheless
related.comparison: とはいえ 'although (one could say)' specifically qualifies a statement by saying one thing might be true, but there is more to the matter, e.g. 日本人とはいえ、フランスで生まれ育った ('Though she may be Japanese, she was born and raised in France.'). であれ～であれ is specifically used when comparing multiple options, e.g. 日本人であれ外国人であれ、法律は守らないといけない ('No matter if you're Japanese or a foreigner, one must obey the law.').

related.title: なるべく
related.meaning: As ~ as possible, Wherever practicable
related.comparison: なるべく and できれば・できたら both mean 'to the extent possible', but differ in nuance. なるべく implies doing something as much or as often as possible over time, e.g. 'whenever possible.' できれば・できたら typically are used when referring to a single situation or request, like 'if possible'.

related.title: ものなら①
related.meaning: If, If one could, If something would be possible
related.comparison: できれば・できたら and ものなら both express possibilities, but differ in tone and implication. できれば・できたら is a relatively casual and neutral way to say 'if possible', often used for ordinary requests or wishes. ものなら sounds more formal and can convey a stronger emotional desire, often about something difficult or unrealistic, as in できるものなら ('if only it were possible').

synonyms.title: です
synonyms.comparison: です and でございます are both polite forms of 'is' or 'to be.' です is polite but commonly used in everyday conversation. でございます is extremely polite and formal, used in customer service, ceremonial speech, or other situations requiring a high level of respect.

synonyms.title: だ
synonyms.comparison: だ conveys a casual, informal, or blunt tone, except when required by certain grammatical patterns. In contrast, でございます is a highly polite form, primarily used in business settings or when addressing someone of higher social status such as a boss or customer.

related.title: にすぎない
related.meaning: No more than, Just, Merely
related.comparison: Both mean merely or nothing but. にすぎない is neutral and factual, as in 僕は学生にすぎない (I’m just a student). でしかない often carries a negative or limiting nuance, as in 言い訳でしかない (That’s nothing but an excuse).

related.title: しか～ない
related.meaning: There's only, Nothing but
related.comparison: しか～ない uses the particle しか and a verb in the negative (ない) form to convey the idea of 'only' (literally, 'nothing but'), e.g. １００円しかない ('I only have / have nothing but 100 yen.') and バナナしか食べなかった ('I only ate / ate nothing but a banana.'). でしかない is a version of this using a form of だ・です 'is', and means 'it is only' or 'it's nothing but', e.g. ゴミでしかない ('It's nothing but garbage.').

synonyms.title: だろう
synonyms.comparison: Both でしょう and だろう express conjecture (e.g., 'It's probably…') or seek confirmation when used with rising intonation in a question (e.g., 面白いでしょう？ or 面白いだろう？ = 'It's interesting, right?'). でしょう is more polite and used with strangers or those of equal or higher rank, while だろう is casual and blunt, used with close friends, family, and in informal settings.

related.title: じゃないか
related.meaning: Isn't it?, Is it not?
related.comparison: でしょう, in addition to expressing conjecture, can also seek confirmation or agreement from the listener (e.g., 面白いでしょう = 'Interesting, right?'). じゃないか is more forceful and casual, implying the speaker strongly believes something and expects the listener to agree (e.g., 面白いじゃないか = 'Interesting, isn't it!'). Note that でしょう is polite, while じゃないか is very casual.

related.title: のではないだろうか
related.meaning: I think ... isn't it?, It might be that..., Don't you agree?
related.comparison: でしょう is a form of です used to express mild uncertainty (e.g., 'It's probably...') or to soften a statement. When used with a question intonation, it seeks the listener's agreement (e.g., 'It's this, right?'). In contrast, のではないだろうか expresses more doubt and seeks confirmation or approval (e.g., 'I wonder if it's…?'). In polite speech, だろう is replaced with でしょう, as in のではないでしょうか.

related.title: ね
related.meaning: Isn't it?, Right? (Sentence ending particle)
related.comparison: でしょう is a form of です used when the speaker is uncertain, often translated as 'probably,' 'I guess,' or '...right?' when asking a question. In contrast, ね is a sentence-ending particle used to seek confirmation or agreement, similar to '...isn't it?' When combined (e.g., 面白いでしょうね), the sentence expresses both uncertainty and a request for agreement: 'It's probably interesting, isn't it?'

related.title: かもしれない
related.meaning: Might, Maybe, Probably
related.comparison: かもしれない (polite form かもしれません) and でしょう both express uncertainty or possibility, softening a statement. The main difference is the degree of certainty: かもしれない expresses less certainty, similar to 'maybe' or 'might,' while でしょう (casual だろう) expresses a higher degree of certainty, similar to 'probably' or 'I guess.'

related.title: によって・による
related.meaning: By means of, According to, Depending on, Because of, Due to
related.comparison: でできる・からできる expresses that something is made or composed of a certain ingredient, e.g. この家は木でできている ('This house is made from wood.'). In one usage, によって can be used with verbs like 作る or できる with a similar meaning, e.g., 木によって作られた家 'a house made from wood'. In other uses, によって can also mark the person or method by which something is made, while でできる・からできる cannot.

synonyms.title: である
synonyms.meaning: Formal or literary だ, Authoritative, Copula
synonyms.comparison: です is often used as the polite form of だ, which is similar in meaning to 'is' or 'to be' in English. It's polite but not overly formal, and it's often used in both speech and writing. In contrast, である is a formal (but not polite) expression mainly used in writing or formal speeches. One key difference is that である can appear in front of nouns in modifying clauses, like in 日本人である留学生 ('an exchange student who is Japanese').

synonyms.title: だ
synonyms.comparison: だ and です both function like the English 'is.' The main difference is politeness: だ is casual and used in plain speech, while です is polite. だ can't be used after い-adjectives, but です can, acting as a general politeness marker. You may still see だ in set expressions like ～だと思います, even in polite sentences.

synonyms.title: でございます
synonyms.comparison: です and でございます are both polite forms of 'is' or 'to be.' です is polite but commonly used in everyday conversation. でございます is extremely polite and formal, used in customer service, ceremonial speech, or other situations requiring a high level of respect.

antonyms.title: じゃない
antonyms.comparison: です works like 'is' or 'to be' in English and is used to politely state something in everyday conversation. じゃない is the casual negative form meaning 'is not,' and it's often used in relaxed speech or in phrases like ～じゃないと思います ('I don't think it is...').

related.title: ～んです・のです
related.meaning: Explanatory, Emphasis, The fact is
related.comparison: ～んです・のです are used to clarify context, provide background information, give reasons, or express curiosity. In contrast, です without の or ん is a simple declarative form used to state facts or general statements. Using ～んです・のです adds emphasis, conveys intention, or creates a more explanatory tone by suggesting a larger context.

related.title: がある
related.meaning: To be, There is
related.comparison: The phrase ～がある is used to show existence or possession. For example, 本がある means 'There is a book' or 'I have a book.' On the other hand, です is used to identify something, like 本です ('It is a book'). がある is more casual and often used with close friends or in certain grammar patterns, while です is polite.

related.title: だった・でした
related.meaning: Was, Were (Past tense)
related.comparison: です and だった・でした are all forms of 'is' or 'to be,' but they show different tenses. です is used for the present or future, like in あれは猫です ('That is a cat'). だった and でした are past tense—だった is casual, and でした is polite. For example, あれは猫だった means 'That was a cat.'

related.title: なくて
related.meaning: Not ~ and, Not so, Because not (Examples)
related.comparison: ではなくて (formal) and じゃなくて (casual) are used with nouns and な-adjectives, while なくて is used with verbs and い-adjectives. Both connect negative forms to another idea and can express cause or reason. For example, 便利じゃなくて残念です ('It's unfortunate it's not convenient.') vs. 行けなくて残念です ('It's unfortunate I couldn't go.').

related.title: 連用形
related.meaning: Formal Conjunctive
related.comparison: 連用形 is a more formal way to connect phrases or clauses, often used in writing. It shares the same basic meaning as the て form. In the case of ではなくて・じゃなくて, the 連用形 becomes ではなく・じゃなく, e.g. 病院ではなく、図書館です 'It's not a hospital, but a library.' (Same meaning, just more formal in tone.)

related.title: なくて
related.meaning: To not ~ and ~, Not ~ and ~ (Conjugations)
related.comparison: ではなくて (formal) and じゃなくて (casual) are used to connect negative forms of nouns and な-adjectives, while なくて is used with verbs and い-adjectives. For example, 先生ではなくて、学生です ('He's not a teacher, but a student.') and 弟にはあげなくて、妹にあげた ('I didn't give it to my brother, but to my sister.').

related.title: それなら
related.meaning: Then, If that's the case
related.comparison: それなら and それでは can both be used at the start of a sentence as transition markers meaning in that case or well then. それなら expresses a response to a specific condition, as in それなら行きません (In that case, I won’t go). それでは can overlap in usage, but it can also mean well then or and with that, often used to wrap up a topic, as in それでは、失礼します (Well then, excuse me), which それなら cannot convey.3

related.title: さ - Interjection
related.meaning: All right, There we go, Now, You see, Well (I don't know, let me see)
related.comparison: Both can appear at the beginning of a sentence to mark a transition. では・それでは・じゃあ signal a shift or next step based on context, e.g. じゃあ、行こうか ('Well then, shall we go?') さ (often extended as さあ) adds an encouraging or prompting tone, e.g. さあ、そろそろ行こうか！ ('Come on, let's get going!')

related.title: さて
related.meaning: Well, Well then
related.comparison: Both mark transitions, but with slightly different nuance. さて is used to shift to a new topic or proceed to the next step, e.g. さて、次の話題に入りましょう ('Now then, let's move on to the next topic.') では・それでは・じゃあ reference the previous context and often lead into a decision or conclusion, similar to 'well then' or 'in that case', e.g. それでは、始めましょう ('Well then, let's begin.')

related.title: もしも～なら・もしも～でも
related.slug: もしも～なら・もしも～でも
related.meaning: If, Supposing
related.comparison: もしも～なら and もしも～でも emphasize a hypothetical situation, similar to 'supposing that (A)' or 'even if (A)' in English. Separately, でも can attach to question words to mean 'any~', e.g. 誰でも 'anyone' and なんでも 'anything', or follow regular nouns to suggest a casual option, as in お茶でも飲みませんか？ ('Want some tea or something?').

related.title: か
related.meaning: Or
related.comparison: The particle か can follow nouns to mean 'or,' clearly specifying alternatives (e.g., 今日か明日休みます = 'I'll take off today or tomorrow'). On the other hand, でも, which also follows nouns, means 'or something,' suggesting an open-ended alternative (e.g., お茶でも飲みましょうか = 'Shall we drink tea or something?').

related.title: 誰か・どこか・誰も・どこも
related.slug: 誰か-どこか-誰も-どこも
related.meaning: Someone, Somewhere, Not anyone, Not anywhere
related.comparison: The 誰か・どこか・誰も・どこも pattern combines question words like 誰 ('who') and どこ ('where') with particles to express ideas like 'someone' or 'somewhere.' Similarly, でも can also be attached to question words to express the idea of 'anyone' (誰でも), 'anywhere' (どこでも), and so forth.

related.title: ても
related.meaning: Even if, Even though
related.comparison: The particle でも can attach to question words to mean 'any~' as in 誰でも 'anyone' or なんでも 'anything', or follow nouns to suggest a casual option, like in お茶でも飲みませんか？ ('Want some tea or something?'). This looks similar to ても, which becomes でも after nouns and な-adjectives, but means 'even if', as in 日本語でも大丈夫です ('It's fine even if it's in Japanese.').

related.title: か何か
related.meaning: Or something, Or something like that
related.comparison: Both か何か and でも can suggest alternatives, like 'or something' in English. か何か places more emphasis on the idea of other specific options, e.g. お茶か何か飲みませんか？ ('How about some tea or something else?'), while でも is vaguer and implies the suggestion is just one of many possibilities, e.g. お茶でも飲みませんか？ ('How about some tea or something?').

related.title: も
related.meaning: Also, Too, As well, Even, Either, Neither
related.comparison: でもある and も can both express 'also' (the も of でもある is the same as the particle も), but there is a key difference to note. AもB means '(A) is also (B)', while Aでもある means 'it is also (A)'. For example, compare 田中さんは学生です。鈴木さんも学生です ('Tanaka is a student. Suzuki is also a student') vs. 田中さんは学生です。日本人でもあります ('Tanaka is a student. He is also Japanese').

related.title: と同時に
related.meaning: At the same time as, The instant, The moment, As well as
related.comparison: でもある and と同時に can both express the idea of 'also' or '(A) as well as (B)', though they function differently. と同時に follows the first item of information, while でもある follows the additional information. Because they appear in different parts of a sentence, they can also be used together. For example, ピアニストであると同時に、作曲家でもあります ('In addition to being a pianist, he is also a composer').

related.title: にしても～にしても
related.meaning: Regardless of whether
related.comparison: 〜でも〜でも and にしても～にしても both show multiple options and that the result doesn't change. にしても～にしても has a slightly more hypothetical tone and works with verbs as well as nouns, e.g. 犬にしても猫にしても大変だ ('Whether it's a dog or cat, it's tough.').

related.title: かどうか
related.meaning: Whether or not, If...or not
related.comparison: かどうか expresses uncertainty, as in 来るかどうか分からない ('I don't know whether or not they'll come.'). 〜でも〜でも lists multiple alternatives and suggests the outcome is unchanged. For example, 雨でも晴れでも行く ('Whether it rains or shines, I'll go.').

related.title: ～ても～なくても
related.meaning: Whether ~ or not
related.comparison: 〜でも〜でも and ～ても～なくても both express 'whether or not'. 〜でも〜でも shows multiple possibilities, e.g. クッキーでもケーキでも甘いものは食べたい ('I want sweets, whether it's cookies or cake.'). ～ても～なくても shows opposition, e.g. 行っても行かなくても大丈夫 ('It's okay whether you go or not.').

related.title: にしろ～にしろ
related.meaning: Whether… or
related.comparison: 〜でも〜でも and にしろ～にしろ both express 'whether it's (A) or (B)'. にしろ～にしろ sounds a bit more formal and hypothetical and works with a wider range of grammar, including verbs. Both forms show that the result or evaluation doesn't change regardless of the options given.

related.title: なにか・なにも
related.meaning: Something, Nothing, Anything, Not anything, Some kind of, Not any kind of
related.comparison: The なにか・なにも pattern combines the question word なに with particles to express ideas like 'something' or 'nothing.' でもなんでもない is a variation of なんでも used after a noun to emphasize 'not A or anything like it.' It can be used with both positive (迷惑でもなんでもない = 'It's not a bother or anything') and negative (好きでもなんでもない = 'I don't like it or anything') nuance.

related.title: い-Adj[く] + もなんともない
related.meaning: Not... or anything, Not at all, Not, By no means, Definitely not, Nothing like that
related.comparison: The phrase い-Adj（く） + もなんともない commonly follows an adjective to strongly downplay or deny something, e.g. 怖くもなんともない('I'm not scared or anything like it..'). The phrase でもなんでもない is similar but is used with nouns or な-adjectives to convey a similar sense, e.g. 迷惑でもなんでもない ('It's not a bother or anything.').

related.title: てもかまわない
related.meaning: It doesn't matter if, I don't mind
related.comparison: でよければ and てもかまわない both express acceptance, but from different perspectives. でよければ is most often used to modestly present something as an option, e.g. 私でよければお手伝いします ('If I'm acceptable, I'll help'). In contrast, てもかまわない is used to allow or grant permission, e.g. 使ってもかまわない ('I don't mind if you use it').

related.title: Verb + てもいい
related.meaning: It's okay to, It's alright to, Can/may
related.comparison: The ても form means 'even if.' One common use is Verb + てもいい, which gives or asks for permission (e.g. 食べてもいい？ = 'Can I eat this?') A related pattern is でよければ, which uses the conditional form of いい (よければ) to mean 'if it's okay.' It's often used to make polite offers, like 私でよければお手伝いします ('If I'm okay [as a choice], I'll help').

related.title: から言うと
related.meaning: Speaking in terms of, From the point of view of
related.comparison: で言うと and から言うと both present a frame of reference, but differ in nuance. で言うと highlights category or classification, often for comparison, e.g. 車で言うとポルシェみたいなものだ ('If we speak in terms of cars, it's like a Porsche'). から言うと gives a basis for a stated opinion, e.g. 私の経験から言うと、もうどうしようもない ('Speaking from my experience, there's nothing to be done').

related.title: と共に
related.meaning: Together with, At the same time,  As well as
related.comparison: Both と and と共に express mutual action or relation, similar to the English 'with.' と is more common and used in everyday speech and writing, but it only follows nouns (e.g., 友だちと遊ぶ = 'play with a friend'). In contrast, と共に is more formal, appearing in written Japanese or structured speech, and can follow verbs, adjectives, and nouns.

related.title: のなかで～がいちばん～
related.meaning: Out of ~, ~ is the best/most ~
related.comparison: Both of these patterns can be used to express preferences or comparisons. The main difference is that the と～と、どちらが pattern is used when asking about or expressing a choice between two options, while のなかで～がいちばん～ involves comparing a group of three or more options.

related.title: たらいい・といい
related.meaning: It would be nice if, It would be good if, Should, I hope
related.comparison: Both といい and たらいい can express 'if you do (A), it would be good'. but differ in nuance. たら is less certain, often used for things that may or may not happen, e.g. 'It would be nice if (A).'. と is more definite and can imply hope, e.g. 'I hope (A).'. Depending on the context, といい can also suggest 'You should (A).'.

related.title: ばいい
related.meaning: Can, Should, It'd be good if
related.comparison: When followed by いい, both ば and と express 'if you do (A), it would be good', but with different nuances. ば conveys uncertainty and is used for unlikely events, e.g. 'It would be nice if (A)', implying that it probably won't happen. と is stronger, often implying hope, e.g. 'I hope (A) happens.' Depending on context, といい can also suggest 'You should (A).'.

related.title: ということだ
related.meaning: I heard that, Rumor has it that, It is said that, It means that
related.comparison: ということ turns phrases into nouns and often clarifies what was said, e.g. 日本に行くということ 'the fact that he's going to Japan'. Adding だ forms ということだ, which is used to report information second-hand, e.g. 来年から海外に住むということだ ('I hear he'll be living abroad from next year.').

related.title: ということは
related.meaning: That means, That is to say
related.comparison: ということ turns phrases into nouns and is often used to clarify what was said. When followed by は as ということは, it marks the phrase as a topic for further explanation or comment. ということは can also begin a sentence to restate or interpret what came before, like 'So what you're saying is (A)' in English.

related.title: ことなの
related.meaning: (A) is (Description of A), (A) means that (Description of A)
related.comparison: ことなの combines こと with the explanatory ～んです／のです form and is used to give or ask for background with an emphatic, explanatory tone. ということ also turns a phrase into a noun, meaning 'the fact that' or 'the act of (A)', but is typically used to clarify something without the added emphasis of ん／の.

related.title: という
related.meaning: Called, Named, Known as, As much as
related.comparison: ということ turns phrases into nouns and is often used to clarify or restate something, e.g. 日本に行くということ 'the fact that (he) is going to Japan'. という by itself links a phrase or name to a noun, as in ポチという犬 'a dog named Pochi' or 来年日本に行くという学生 'a student who is going to Japan'.

related.title: というのは
related.meaning: ~ Known as ~ is, ~ Called ~ is, ~ Means ~, Because
related.comparison: ということ and というのは both turn phrases into nouns. ということ is often used to clarify what was said, while というのは introduces a topic for further explanation or comment. というのは can also start a sentence to mean 'what I mean is (A)' or 'that is to say (A)'.

related.title: こと
related.meaning: Converting a verb into a noun
related.comparison: こと attaches to the dictionary form of verbs to turn them into nouns, e.g. 行くこと '(the act of) going'. ということ has a similar function, but adds the quoting phrase という, so it's often used to clarify information or reported speech, e.g. 日本に行くということ 'the fact that (he) is going to Japan'.

related.title: とされている
related.meaning: To be considered, To be deemed, Be regarded as, Be accepted as, To be said, To be accredited with
related.comparison: とされている and ということだ both report other people's opinions. ということだ has a nuance of direct hearsay: 'They say that (A)'. On the other hand, とされている presents something as generally believed or accepted. It's less about quoting someone and more about shared public perception.

related.title: ということ
related.meaning: ~ing, The ~ that ~ (Nominalization)
related.comparison: ということ turns phrases into nouns and often clarifies what was said, e.g. 日本に行くということ 'the fact that he's going to Japan'. Adding だ forms ということだ, which is used to report information second-hand, e.g. 来年から海外に住むということだ ('I hear he'll be living abroad from next year.').

related.title: そうだ
related.meaning: I heard that, It is said that
related.comparison: Both convey hearsay, but differ in tone and formality. そうだ is used to directly pass on something you've heard, e.g. 明日は休みだそうだ ('I heard he has tomorrow off.') ということだ is more formal and often sounds more confident or declarative. It can also carry a nuance of explanation or conclusion, e.g. 明日は休みになるということだ ('So that means / They say we'll have tomorrow off.')

related.title: らしい ①
related.meaning: Seems like, Apparently, I heard
related.comparison: ということだ and らしい ① both convey information from another source, but differ in nuance and formality. らしい ① conveys hearsay with a nuance of confidence, e.g. 雨が降るらしい ('Apparently, it's going to rain.'). In contrast, ということだ literally means 'It is said that (A).' or 'The word is that (A).', and sounds more formal and confident than らしい ①.

related.title: とか
related.meaning: I heard that… something like, They said ... or something
related.comparison: Both can report hearsay, but with different tone. とか is casual and vague, often implying uncertainty, similar to 'I heard / They say (A), or something.' ということだ is more formal and declarative, closer to 'The word is (A).' ということだ can also be used to draw conclusions (i.e. 'that means (A)'), which とか cannot.

related.title: ことなの
related.meaning: (A) is (Description of A), (A) means that (Description of A)
related.comparison: Both use こと to summarize information, but with different nuances. ことなの means 'it's that (A)' and is often used to clarify or define something, as in '(A) means (B)' or 'that means (A).' ということだ can also be used in this way, but additionally functions to report hearsay, e.g. 'I hear / They say (A).' ことなの, without the quoting function of という, is not used to report what others have said.

related.title: だって
related.meaning: I heard/was said, Because, But, Even or also, Even if or even though
related.comparison: Both can be used to report hearsay (i.e. 'they say (A)'), but differ in tone and nuance. ということだ is more formal and can carry the nuance of summarizing or confidently reporting what the speaker heard. だって is much more casual and light, and can also be used at the beginning of a sentence with an emphatic 'but I mean (A)' nuance, which is something ということだ cannot do.

related.title: という
related.meaning: Called, Named, Known as, As much as
related.comparison: Both expressions contain the same という, but are used differently. という is used to name or describe something, typically in the form AというB ('a B called A'). In contrast, というのは introduces something as a topic for explanation or comment, e.g. 人生というのは ('This thing we call life') While という is followed by a single noun, というのは is followed by a phrase that elaborates on the topic.

related.title: ということは
related.meaning: That means, That is to say
related.comparison: というのは and ということは are similar in that both take a phrase and turn it into a topic for further explanation. While sometimes interchangeable, というのは is typically followed by a definition or reason. ということは is primarily used to rephrase, clarify, or draw a conclusion from the previous statement.

related.title: いわゆる
related.meaning: What is called, So-called, Generally known
related.comparison: いわゆる and という both express the idea of something being 'called' something, but they are used very differently. いわゆる precedes a noun to mean 'the so-called (A)'. というのは takes a word or phrase and introduces it as a topic to be defined or explained. Because they serve different roles, they can be used together, e.g. いわゆる五月病というのは ('This thing known as the so-called May blues is...')

related.title: ことなの
related.meaning: (A) is (Description of A), (A) means that (Description of A)
related.comparison: ことなの and というのは can both be used in explanations, but differ in position and role. ことなの appears at the end of a sentence to emphasize a conclusion or explanation. Literally, 'it's that it's (A)'. というのは follows a noun or phrase to introduce it as a topic to be explained. They can be used together, e.g. 人生というのはこういうことなんだ ('This thing we call life... this is what it means.')

related.title: ということ
related.meaning: ~ing, The ~ that ~ (Nominalization)
related.comparison: ということ and というのは both turn phrases into nouns. ということ is often used to clarify what was said, while というのは introduces a topic for further explanation or comment. というのは can also start a sentence to mean 'what I mean is (A)' or 'that is to say (A)'.

related.title: ということ
related.meaning: ~ing, The ~ that ~ (Nominalization)
related.comparison: ということ turns phrases into nouns and is often used to clarify what was said. When followed by は as ということは, it marks the phrase as a topic for further explanation or comment. ということは can also begin a sentence to restate or interpret what came before, like 'So what you're saying is (A)' in English.

related.title: というのは
related.meaning: ~ Known as ~ is, ~ Called ~ is, ~ Means ~, Because
related.comparison: というのは and ということは are similar in that both take a phrase and turn it into a topic for further explanation. While sometimes interchangeable, というのは is typically followed by a definition or reason. ということは is primarily used to rephrase, clarify, or draw a conclusion from the previous statement.

related.title: ことなの
related.meaning: (A) is (Description of A), (A) means that (Description of A)
related.comparison: These two expressions are similar in that both can be used when offering or seeking explanations. ことなの is used at the end of a phrase or sentence, often to clarify a particular point, e.g. 今日は来ないということなの？ ('So does that mean he's not coming today?'), while ということは begins a sentence and sets up a conclusion based on what was just said, e.g. ということは、今日は来ないんだ ('So that means he's not coming today').

related.title: つまり
related.meaning: In short, To sum up, In other words
related.comparison: つまり and ということは both rephrase earlier information, but differ in usage. つまり restates or explains something just said, e.g. つまりできないってこと？ ('So you're saying you can't do it?'). ということは introduces a logical inference or consequence, e.g. ということは、試合は中止だね ('So that means the game is canceled').

related.title: 即ち
related.meaning: In other words, That is, Namely
related.comparison: 即ち and ということは both rephrase information, but differ in tone and function. 即ち is formal and used to define or clarify precisely, often in writing, e.g. 日本の首都、即ち東京 ('The capital of Japan—namely, Tokyo'). ということは is conversational and introduces a conclusion, e.g. 明日は休み？ということは、今日は早く帰れるかもね ('Tomorrow's a holiday? So that means we might get to leave early today').

related.title: 何といっても
related.meaning: By any account, All the same, No doubt
related.comparison: Both phrases assert something strongly, but from different angles. ～というのは事実だ affirms something as a literal fact, e.g. 負けたというのは事実だ ('It's a fact that we lost'). 何といっても expresses what the speaker feels is most important, e.g. 何といっても家族が大事だ ('No matter what you say, family is important').

related.title: に違いない
related.meaning: There is no doubt that, I'm sure
related.comparison: Both ～というのは事実だ and に違いない make confident statements, but differ in implied certainty. ～というのは事実だ affirms something as a fact, e.g. 負けたというのは事実だ ('It's a fact that we lost'). に違いない expresses strong conviction, but not absolute certainty, e.g. 彼は疲れているに違いない ('He must be tired').

related.title: もさることながら
related.meaning: It is a fact that, But, Even more than, It goes without saying, Not only but also,
related.comparison: Both emphasize a truth, but differ in nuance and tone. ～というのは事実だ explicitly affirms something as a fact, e.g. 彼がやったというのは事実だ ('It's a fact that he did it'). もさることながら highlights one point while further stressing another, e.g. 見た目もさることながら味も最高だ ('Not only the appearance, but the taste is excellent'). Both are formal, but the former is more clinical, while the latter sounds more literary or poetic.

related.title: どうせ
related.meaning: Anyhow, At any rate, In any case, I have no doubt that, Probably
related.comparison: These two forms both relate in a sense to an unchangeable fact, but differ greatly in usage. ～というのは事実だ literally states that something is a fact, e.g. 何もできないのは事実だ ('It's a fact we can't do anything'). どうせ expresses resignation or futility with a more emotional nuance, e.g. どうせ無理だよ ('It's hopeless anyway').

related.title: ことにはならない
related.meaning: Just because… doesn't mean…
related.comparison: Both mean just because (A), it doesn’t mean (B). ことにはならない emphasizes that one condition doesn’t guarantee a result, as in 勉強したからといって合格することにはならない (Just because you studied doesn’t mean you’ll pass). というものでもない carries a stronger nuance that the claim cannot be taken as an absolute truth, as in お金があれば幸せになれるというものでもない (Having money doesn’t necessarily mean you’ll be happy).

related.title: というわけではない
related.meaning: It is not that, It doesn't mean that, It is not always the case
related.comparison: Both reject absolute statements. というわけではない often counters an assumption with logical reasoning, as in 嫌いというわけではない (It’s not that I dislike it). というものでもない focuses more on the idea that something is not necessarily accepted as true, as in 努力すれば必ず成功するというものでもない (Effort doesn’t necessarily guarantee success).

related.title: わけではない
related.meaning: It doesn't mean that, It is not the case, It's not like
related.comparison: Both というものでもない and わけではない express that something isn't necessarily true, but differ in tone. わけではない directly denies a potential assumption, while というものでもない has a more indirect or reflective nuance, often meaning 'it's not always necessarily the case that (A)'.

related.title: とは限らない
related.meaning: Not necessarily so, Is not always true, There is no guarantee that
related.comparison: These two expressions are similar in meaning, but with a slightly different nuance. とは限らない says that something isn't necessarily the case, e.g. '高いものが必ずしも良いとは限らない' ('Expensive things aren't always good'). というものでもない says that something cannot be stated as a general truth, e.g. '努力すれば必ず成功するというものでもない' ('It's not necessarily true that effort always leads to success').

related.title: 必ずしも
related.meaning: Not always, Not entirely, Not necessarily
related.comparison: Both 必ずしも and というものでもない carry the meaning 'not necessarily', but are used in different ways. 必ずしも precedes a phrase or sentence to emphasize 'not always', and is often used with phrases like とは限らない, e.g. '必ずしもいいとは限らない' ('It's not necessarily good'). というものでもない appears at the end of a sentence and implies a general principle about how things are.

related.title: より
related.meaning: More than, Over
related.comparison: より expresses a general comparison, like 'more than (A)' or '~er,' e.g. 月曜日より水曜日がいい ('Wednesday is better than Monday.') or 走るより野球をするのが好き ('I prefer playing baseball to running.'). In contrast, というより specifically means 'more/rather than saying (A)' and is used primarily when rephrasing or clarifying something.

related.title: といおうか
related.meaning: How to put it, How shall I put it, How should I put it, Or perhaps, Or maybe, Call it … or
related.comparison: Both といおうか and というより are used to adjust or refine what the speaker is saying, but they differ in how that adjustment works. といおうか is used when the speaker hesitates and offers an alternative way of describing something, often softening the statement or showing uncertainty, as in 優しいというか、少し甘い人だ (‘He’s kind, or rather, maybe a bit naive.’). In contrast, というより is used to correct or replace the first expression with a more accurate one, clearly shifting to what the speaker considers the better description, as in 忙しいというより疲れている (‘Rather than saying I'm busy, I’m tired.’).

related.title: むしろ
related.meaning: Rather, Instead, Over
related.comparison: むしろ and というより can both be used when clarifying or rewording something, but function differently. というより attaches to a word or phrase to suggest a more appropriate expression, while むしろ introduces the clarified or preferred wording. Because of this, they often pair together, e.g. 可愛いというより、むしろ美しい ('Rather than cute, she's beautiful').

antonyms.title: ないことはない
antonyms.meaning: Is not impossible, It doesn't mean that it isn't
antonyms.comparison: Both というわけではない and ないことはない introduce nuance or partial disagreement, but in opposite directions. というわけではない denies a conclusion or assumption, e.g. '好きというわけではない' ('It's not that I like it'). ないことはない uses a double negative to express a less confident positive, e.g. '行けないことはない' ('It's not that I can't go'), implying it may be possible but difficult.

related.title: というものでもない
related.meaning: There is no guarantee that…, Not necessarily
related.comparison: Both reject absolute statements. というわけではない often counters an assumption with logical reasoning, as in 嫌いというわけではない (It’s not that I dislike it). というものでもない focuses more on the idea that something is not necessarily accepted as true, as in 努力すれば必ず成功するというものでもない (Effort doesn’t necessarily guarantee success).

related.title: わけではない
related.meaning: It doesn't mean that, It is not the case, It's not like
related.comparison: Both わけではない and というわけではない express the idea of 'it doesn't mean that (A)' or 'it's not necessarily the case that (A)', but differ in usage and nuance. わけではない attaches directly to a verb or adjective phrase to deny a specific assumption or conclusion, while というわけではない uses the quoting particle と and often adds a nuance of clarification or softening of an implied statement.

related.title: とは限らない
related.meaning: Not necessarily so, Is not always true, There is no guarantee that
related.comparison: Both expressions deny absolutes, but differ in usage. とは限らない expresses that something isn't necessarily the case, e.g. '留学すれば英語が上手になるとは限らない' ('Studying abroad doesn't always mean your English improves'). というわけではない clarifies previous statements or assumptions, e.g. '嫌いというわけではない' ('It's not the case I dislike it').

related.title: そのため(に)
related.meaning: For that reason, Because of that, To that end
related.comparison: Both introduce reasons, but そのため(に) shows a direct consequence or effect from what was previously stated, while という理由で introduces a reason more abstractly and with a greater degree of formality. They also differ in how they connect the reason to the consequence, with そのため(に) always appearing at the beginning of a sentence, and という理由で being able to appear either at the beginning or in the middle of a sentence.

related.title: にしたら
related.meaning: From the point of view of
related.comparison: にしたら conveys empathy with how someone might think or feel, as in 彼にしたら迷惑だっただろう (From his perspective, it must have been troublesome). という点から考えると is more analytical, focusing on one aspect logically, as in 経済の面という点から考えると重要だ (From the perspective of the economy, it’s important).

synonyms.title: ふうに
synonyms.meaning: ~ way, In a way that suggests, Like ~
synonyms.comparison: Both mean in the way of, but differ in usage. ふうに often attaches to demonstratives like こんなふうに (in this way). という風に follows whole phrases, as in すべて分かっているというふうに (as if they knew everything), and can sound slightly more indirect.

related.title: ように・ような
related.meaning: As・like, Just like
related.comparison: Both という風に and ように・ような can be used to express similarity or comparison, but their nuance differs. という風に suggests doing something in a way 'as if to say' something else, meaning that the comparison can be uncertain or abstract. In contrast, ように・ような expresses a more direct comparison, implying similar qualities.

related.title: かのようだ
related.meaning: As if, Just like
related.comparison: Both describe how something appears, but differ in tone. かのようだ often conveys imagination or unreality, as in 幻かのようだ (It’s like an illusion). という風に shows how something visibly seems, as in 分かっているというふうに笑った (He smiled as if he knew).

related.title: ところで
related.meaning: By the way, Incidentally
related.comparison: Both can be used to shift the topic, but といえば is used after a noun to bring up something related or comment on that thing, e.g. 京都といえば、有名なお寺が多いね ('Speaking of Kyoto, there many famous temples, aren't there.'). In contrast, ところで typically starts a new sentence to introduce an unrelated topic, e.g. ところで、週末の予定は？ ('By the way, what are your plans for the weekend?').

related.title: は
related.meaning: As for... (Highlights sentence topic)
related.comparison: Both といえば and は can introduce the topic of a sentence. といえば is used when referencing something previously mentioned or known, often implying that more information will follow ('speaking of…'). は is broader and can also suggest contrast, as in 'バナナは食べたけど…' ('I ate the banana, but [not the apple]'). といえば doesn't create contrast in this way.

related.title: ～と言っても
related.meaning: Although ~ might say that ~
related.comparison: While both relate to things that have just been mentioned, ～と言っても softens or qualifies assumptions, while といえば introduces a related topic. For example, 旅行と言っても日帰りだった ('Even though I say vacation, it was just a day trip') vs. 夏といえば海ですね ('If we're speaking of summer, the beach comes to mind').

related.title: そういえば
related.meaning: Speaking of which, By the way, Come to think of it
related.comparison: These expressions are similar in that both can be used to shift the topic, but といえば is typically used following a noun to introduce something connected to that topic, while そういえば recalls something the speaker just remembered. For example, 夏といえば花火だね ('Speaking of summer, fireworks come to mind') vs. そういえば、明日は休みだった ('Oh right, we have a holiday tomorrow').

related.title: って
related.meaning: As for, Speaking of, Casual は (Sentence topic marker)
related.comparison: Both can mean 'speaking of (A)', but differ in tone and usage. といえば often refers back to something just mentioned or known to both speaker and listener, e.g. 京都といえば、お寺が有名だね ('Speaking of Kyoto, its temples are famous.') って is a casual form used in everyday conversation, often functioning like は to casually introduce a topic, e.g. この映画って最高！ ('That movie is amazing!')

related.title: とはいえ
related.meaning: Though, Although (I admit that), Be that as it may, Nonetheless
related.comparison: These are both formal/written-style expressions that have the same fundamental meaning of 'even if one says'. In practice, both are used as a literary way to say 'although’, even if there is no explicit intention of quoting something that was said, e.g. ここは治安がいい（といえども・とはいえ）油断してはいけない ('Even though it's safe here, you mustn't be careless.').

related.title: ～と言っても
related.meaning: Although ~ might say that ~
related.comparison: といえども is essentially a more formal/written-style variant of the more casual/colloquial と言っても, with both meaning 'even if one says'. Though they can overlap in usage, と言っても is perhaps more commonly used when referring to something actually said, e.g. 安いと言っても5000円はする ('Although I say it's cheap, it does cost 5,000 yen.'), while といえども is often used figuratively with no explicit sense of quotation, e.g. 名人(だ)といえども失敗する時もある ('Although he's a master, he sometimes fails.').

related.title: というより
related.meaning: ...is more of a... than a..., Rather than saying that...
related.comparison: Both といおうか and というより are used to adjust or refine what the speaker is saying, but they differ in how that adjustment works. といおうか is used when the speaker hesitates and offers an alternative way of describing something, often softening the statement or showing uncertainty, as in 優しいというか、少し甘い人だ (‘He’s kind, or rather, maybe a bit naive.’). In contrast, というより is used to correct or replace the first expression with a more accurate one, clearly shifting to what the speaker considers the better description, as in 忙しいというより疲れている (‘Rather than saying I'm busy, I’m tired.’).

related.title: なんか・なんて
related.meaning: Such as, Things like, Emphasis
related.comparison: Both express that something is one example of a larger group, but differ in usage. といった is neutral and often used to classify or exemplify within a broader group, e.g. 'ピアノやギターといった楽器' ('musical instruments such as the piano and guitar'). なんか・なんて is more casual and implies a dismissive attitude, e.g. '宿題なんかしたくない' ('I don't want to do homework or anything like that').

related.title: など
related.meaning: Such as, Things like, Etc., And so on
related.comparison: といった and など both give examples, but differ in usage. といった appears in the form AといったB, meaning '(B) such as (A),' e.g. サッカーといったスポーツ ('sports like soccer'). など can be used similarly as AなどのB, but also stands alone, e.g. サッカーなどが好き ('I like soccer and similar things.').

related.title: とか～とか
related.meaning: Among other things, For example, Such as
related.comparison: といった and とか～とか both give examples, but differ in structure and nuance. といった is used in the form AといったB to mean '(B) such as (A)', e.g. サッカーといったスポーツ ('sports such as soccer'). とか～とか follows each noun to list examples, e.g. サッカーとか野球とか ('things like soccer and baseball').

related.title: と
related.meaning: Quotation
related.comparison: と is a quoting particle used to mark the content of direct or indirect speech, thoughts, or feelings. For example, 行くと言っていた means '[He] was saying [he] will go.' といってもいい combines と with 言う and the い-Adjective いい, meaning 'You/one could say' (literally, 'It's fine even if one says...').

related.title: と言える
related.meaning: Would or could say that..., Can probably say that..., Fair to say that...
related.comparison: Both と言える and といってもいい can express a meaning similar to the English 'it can/might be said that (A)' with the difference being the degree of assertiveness. と言える is a firmer statement, literally meaning '(A) can be said.'. In contrast, といってもいい sounds more tentative, implying that it would be fine/acceptable to say the thing in question, but allowing for the possibility that other views/opinions may also be valid.

related.title: Verb + てもいい
related.meaning: It's okay to, It's alright to, Can/may
related.comparison: The ても form has the core meaning of 'even if.' One common use is Verb + てもいい, which gives or asks for permission—for example, 食べてもいいですか？ literally means 'Is it okay even if I eat this?' but works like 'Can/May I eat this?' in English. Similarly, といってもいい means 'It's okay even if you say...' but is used to express 'You could say' or 'One might say.'

related.title: ないでもない
related.meaning: Kind of, Might, Slightly, It’s not (impossible) that, Somewhat, In some respects
related.comparison: These forms are similar in that both can make a statement sound less certain, but their nuance differs. ないでもない uses a double negative, literally 'It's not not (A).', to express a somewhat unsure positive, e.g., 分からないでもない ('I kind of understand.', or literally 'It's not that I don't understand.'). といってもいい suggests 'you could say (A)', e.g. 問題ないといってもいい ('You could say there's no problem.').

related.title: って
related.meaning: Casual quotation
related.comparison: って (a contracted form of と) is a casual quoting particle used to mark direct or indirect speech, thoughts, feelings, or to introduce a topic casually. For example, 行くって言っていた means '[They] were saying [they] will go.' といってもいい combines と with 言う and いい, meaning 'You/one could say' (literally: 'It's fine even if one says...').

related.title: とかんがえられている
related.slug: とかんがえられている
related.meaning: Is considered, Is thought of as
related.comparison: といわれている 'it is said' and とかんがえられている 'it is thought' both soften a statement and make it feel more objective or widely accepted. This can make an idea sound more trustworthy or backed by others. Both are formal and common in writing.

related.title: そうだ
related.meaning: I heard that, It is said that
related.comparison: といわれている and そうだ both report something heard, but differ in nuance. といわれている is more general and formal, e.g. 'It is said that (A)'. On the other hand, そうだ often implies someone specific said it and is more casual, e.g., 雨が降るそうだ ('I heard it's going to rain.').

related.title: という
related.meaning: Called, Named, Known as, As much as
related.comparison: という is versatile: AというB = 'a (B) called (A)' or to show hearsay, e.g. 面白いという本 'a book said to be interesting'. といわれている is more formal and emphasizes that something is generally said or believed by many people, especially in written or academic contexts.

related.title: 遂に
related.meaning: Finally, At last
related.comparison: Both とうとう and 遂に mean 'finally', but with different nuances. とうとう emphasizes the long effort or struggle leading up to the result, while 遂に focuses more on the result itself and what may follow. In other words, both とうとう大学生になった and 遂に大学生になった mean 'I finally became a university student.', but the former is more backward-looking, while the latter is forward-looking.

related.title: いよいよ
related.meaning: At last, Finally, More and more
related.comparison: Both とうとう and いよいよ can mean 'finally', but with different nuances. とうとう is used when something expected happens after effort or waiting, e.g. とうとう大学に入った ('I finally got into university'). いよいよ expresses anticipation for something imminent, e.g. いよいよ夏が来た ('At last, summer has come.').

related.title: 〜ようと思う・〜おうと思う
related.slug: ようと思う-おうと思う
related.meaning: I think I will, I think I shall, Going to, Gonna (Volitional + と思う)
related.comparison: 〜よう・おうと思う uses the volitional form with と思う to express plans or intentions, like 'I intend to (A)' or 'I'm thinking of doing (A)' In contrast, plain と思う expresses a simple thought. For example, この本を読もうと思います means 'I intend to read this book.', while この本は面白いと思います means 'I think this book is interesting.'.

related.title: れる・られる + ままに
related.meaning: Do as (one is told or ordered…), In accordance with, In compliance with, Just like
related.comparison: Both convey the idea of doing something in precisely a certain way, but differ in nuance. れる・られる + ままに implies passivity or being compelled by external forces. For example, 言われるがままにやった ('I did as told'). とおり can be used similarly, as in 言われたとおりにやった ('I did it just as I was told'), but also appears in neutral contexts. For example, レシピのとおりに作った ('I made it exactly according to the recipe').

related.title: まま(に)
related.meaning: As (one likes, feels or pleases), Spur of the moment, Wherever one fancies, Spontaneously, On a whim
related.comparison: Both まま(に) and とおり express doing something in a certain way, but differ in nuance. まま(に) emphasizes leaving things as they are or acting spontaneously. For example, 思うままに話す ('Speak as you feel'). とおり stresses replication or faithful adherence. For example, レシピのとおりに作った ('I made it exactly according to the recipe').

related.title: ように・ような
related.meaning: As・like, Just like
related.comparison: Both とおり and ように・ような can express doing something in a similar way, but their nuance differs. とおり implies doing something exactly as described, while ように・ような suggests general similarity. For example, 言われたとおりにした ('I did exactly as I was told.') vs. 言われたようにした ('I did it as I was told.'), where the latter is less precise and emphatic about how closely the speaker followed instructions.

related.title: ということだ
related.meaning: I heard that, Rumor has it that, It is said that, It means that
related.comparison: Both can report hearsay, but with different tone. とか is casual and vague, often implying uncertainty, similar to 'I heard / They say (A), or something.' ということだ is more formal and declarative, closer to 'The word is (A).' ということだ can also be used to draw conclusions (i.e. 'that means (A)'), which とか cannot.

related.title: そうだ
related.meaning: I heard that, It is said that
related.comparison: Both can relay hearsay, but with different levels of certainty. とか adds a nuance of vagueness or uncertainty, e.g. 来月引っ越すとか ('I heard they might be moving next month, or something.') そうだ also conveys hearsay, but with less ambiguity, e.g. 来月引っ越すそうだ ('I heard they're moving next month.')

related.title: んだって
related.meaning: I heard that, He or She said that
related.comparison: These two expressions are similar in that both can serve as casual expressions of hearsay, i.e. 'they say (A)' or 'I hear (A)'. The difference is that んだって has a slightly more explanatory and emphatic nuance due to the presence of ん, while とか has an uncertain or non-specific tone, i.e. 'I hear (A) or something', because of the presence of the question particle か.

related.title: なんか・なんて
related.meaning: Such as, Things like, Emphasis
related.comparison: なんか・なんて and とか～とか both give examples, but differ in tone and usage. なんか・なんて usually follows a single noun to imply 'things like (A)', often with a dismissive or belittling nuance. とか～とか is used after each item in a list and simply means 'things like (A), (B), and so forth' without any negative implication.

related.title: やら～やら
related.meaning: Whether or, Things like, And … so on
related.comparison: While both やら～やら and とか～とか list examples, they differ in nuance. やら～やら often has an uncertain tone or a presents contradictory possibilities, e.g. 行くのやら行かないのやら ('whether he's going or not'). とか is more neutral and casual, simply listing examples without a strong nuance of uncertainty.

related.title: たり～たりする
related.meaning: Things like ~ and ~
related.comparison: Both たり～たりする and とか～とか are used to give examples of actions, similar to the English '(doing) things like A and B.' The key difference is how they attach to words. たり is made by adding り to the た (past tense) of a verb, while とか follows the dictionary form. Also, とか can follow nouns directly, while たり requires だ to become だったり.

related.title: たとえば
related.meaning: For example
related.comparison: たとえば and とか～とか both introduce examples, but differ in usage. たとえば comes at the start of a sentence or clause, like 'for example'. In contrast, とか～とか is used to list multiple items and follows each noun. Because they differ in function, they can be used together in the same sentence, e.g. たとえば本とか雑誌とか ('For example, things like books, magazines, and so forth.').

related.title: など
related.meaning: Such as, Things like, Etc., And so on
related.comparison: など and とか～とか both list examples, but differ in nuance and structure. など usually follows a single noun or ends a list, implying the items are representative, like '(A) and so on' or 'things like (A).' とか～とか follows each noun and emphasizes specific, individual examples, like '(A) and (B), for example'.

related.title: し～し
related.meaning: And, Giving reasons
related.comparison: し～し and とか～とか both list things, but with a different nuance. し～し lists reasons or causes, like 'because of things like (A) and (B)'. とか～とか simply lists items without implying they are a cause for something else, i.e. 'things like (A), (B), and so forth'. Also, とか～とか is very casual, while し～し will be found in both speech and writing.

related.title: ～なり～なり
related.meaning: Or, Either or
related.comparison: While both ～なり～なり and とか～とか present examples, they differ in formality and nuance. ～なり～なり is more formal and presents possible choices without preference. とか～とか is casual and used in everyday speech to mean 'things like (A) and (B),' often implying there are other possible examples.

related.title: といった
related.meaning: Such as, Like, So much as
related.comparison: といった and とか～とか both give examples, but differ in structure and nuance. といった is used in the form AといったB to mean '(B) such as (A)', e.g. サッカーといったスポーツ ('sports such as soccer'). とか～とか follows each noun to list examples, e.g. サッカーとか野球とか ('things like soccer and baseball').

related.title: や
related.meaning: Things like, And the like
related.comparison: Both や and とか～とか are used to list items non-exhaustively, implying that other, unmentioned items are also included. や is more common in writing or formal speech, while とか is more colloquial. や must follow nouns, while とか can also be used with actions or events involving verbs. Additionally, とか can follow each item in the list, whereas や is not used after the final item.

synonyms.title: 折には
synonyms.comparison: Both とき and 折には express the time of an action or event, but differ in nuance and formality. とき is common in everyday conversation and broadly used, like the English 'when'. 折には is more formal, similar to 'on the occasion of', and is often used for special or less frequent (and often positive) events.

related.title: ながら
related.meaning: While ~ing, During, As
related.comparison: とき expresses the time of an action or event, like the English 'when'. It can be used with nouns as in 雨の時 'when it rains', verbs as in 父が帰ってきた時 'when Dad came home', or adjectives as in 暑い時 'when it's hot'. In contrast, ながら is used when doing something while performing another action. It is used only with verbs, and unlike とき, the subject of both phrases must be the same, e.g. 音楽を聴きながら走る 'listen to music while running'.

related.title: うちに
related.meaning: While, During
related.comparison: とき expresses the time of an action or event, like 'when' in English. It can be used with nouns as in 雨の時 'when it rains', verbs as in 父が帰ってきた時 'when Dad came home', or adjectives as in 暑い時 'when it's hot', and can describe one-time or repeated actions. うちに means 'while' or 'during,' often with the nuance that it's the ideal or only time to act, e.g. 熱いうちに食べてね ('Eat it while it's hot.').

related.title: ことがある
related.meaning: Something is possible, Sometimes, Occasionally, There are times when
related.comparison: とき marks the time of an action or event, like 'when' in English, e.g. 雨の時 or 雨が降るとき 'when it rains'. ことがある expresses something that happens occasionally, e.g. 雨が降ることがある ('There are occasions when it rains.'). Because they express different concepts, the two can be combined, as in: 時間がないとき、コンビニ弁当を食べることがある ('When I don't have time, there are occasions when I eat a convenience store lunch box.').

related.title: の間に
related.meaning: While, During, Between, Period
related.comparison: とき expresses the time of an action or event, like the English 'when'. It can be used with nouns as in 雨の時 'when it rains', verbs as in 父が帰ってきた時 'when Dad came home', or adjectives as in 暑い時 'when it's hot'. In contrast, の間に is used with nouns, and describes a period within which something happens, similar to the English 'while' or 'during', e.g. 冬休みの間に ('during winter break').

related.title: ているところだ
related.meaning: In the process of doing ~ right now/at this moment
related.comparison: とき expresses the time of an action or event, like 'when' in English. It can be used with nouns as in 雨の時 'when it rains', verbs as in 父が帰ってきた時 'when Dad came home', or adjectives as in 暑い時 'when it's hot'. In contrast, ているところ uses the verb in て-form and emphasizes being in the middle of an action, often with a sense of being busy or focused, e.g. 晩ご飯を食べているところです ('I'm right in the middle of eating dinner.').

related.title: 中
related.meaning: During, While, Throughout, In the process of
related.comparison: とき expresses the time of an action or event, like 'when' in English.
