It can be used with nouns as in 雨の時 'when it rains', verbs as in 父が帰ってきた時 'when Dad came home', or adjectives as in 暑い時 'when it's hot', and can describe one-time or repeated actions. 中 attaches to nouns and describes an action or state in progress, e.g. 勉強中 'now studying' or 使用中 'currently in use'.

related.title: と同時に
related.meaning: At the same time as, The instant, The moment, As well as
related.comparison: とき expresses the general time of an action or event, like 'when' in English. と同時に means 'at the same time as' and emphasizes simultaneity. For example, 帰ってきた時 means 'when (I) came home', while 帰ってきたと同時に means 'the moment (I) came home' or 'as soon as (I) came home'.

related.title: 最中に
related.meaning: In the middle of
related.comparison: とき expresses the general time of an action or event, like 'when' in English. It can be used with nouns as in 雨の時 'when it rains', verbs as in 父が帰ってきた時 'when Dad came home', or adjectives as in 暑い時 'when it's hot', and can describe one-time or repeated actions. In contrast, 最中に describes something happening right in the middle of another action/event, e.g. 食事の最中に電話がなった ('My phone rang while I was in the middle of eating.').

related.title: ているあいだに
related.meaning: While, During the time that ~
related.comparison: とき expresses the time of an action or event, like the English 'when'. It can be used with nouns as in 雨の時 'when it rains', verbs as in 父が帰ってきた時 'when Dad came home', or adjectives as in 暑い時 'when it's hot'. In contrast, ているあいだに uses the て-form of verbs and refers to a period during which something occurs, e.g. 晩ご飯を食べている間に、父が帰ってきました ('While I was eating dinner, my father came home.').

related.title: ごろ
related.meaning: Around, About
related.comparison: Both ごろ (or ころ) and とき indicate when something happened, but with key differences. ごろ is less specific and means 'around that time', as in 子どもの頃 'in my childhood days'. It also works as a suffix with time words, e.g. 11時ごろ 'around 11 o'clock', while とき cannot be used this way. By contrast, とき has a more precise nuance, as in 子どもの時 'when I was a child'.

related.title: 際に
related.meaning: When, At the time of, In case of, On the occasion of
related.comparison: Both とき and 際に express the time of an action or event, but with different nuances and levels of formality. とき is common in everyday conversation and broadly used, like the English 'when'. 際に is more formal and often appears in official contexts, such as instructions, advice, or warnings related to specific events.

synonyms.title: とされている
synonyms.meaning: To be considered, To be deemed, Be regarded as, Be accepted as, To be said, To be accredited with
synonyms.comparison: とされている and とかんがえられている both mean 'It is believed/considered (A)' and are used to present ideas objectively. Both make statements sound more neutral or widely accepted, often in formal writing, academic papers, or news reports.

synonyms.title: と考えられる
synonyms.meaning: Can be considered as, Is thought to be, One can think that, It is conceivable that
synonyms.comparison: と考えられる and と考えられている are similar but differ slightly in nuance. と考えられる means 'it can be considered (A)' and implies a basis in evidence or reasoning. On the other hand と考えられている suggests something is a commonly held belief or consensus, like the English 'it is (generally) thought that (A)'.

antonyms.title: 考えられない
antonyms.comparison: These two forms of the verb 考える express opposite sentiments. 考えられない (lit. 'one cannot think') expresses strong disbelief or the idea that something is inconceivable, e.g. 'そんなことは考えられない' ('I can't imagine such a thing'). In contrast, と考えられている indicates a commonly accepted or reasoned belief, often used in formal writing to make statements less direct, e.g. 'これは効果的だと考えられている' ('This is considered effective').

related.title: といわれている
related.meaning: It is said that, Is known as
related.comparison: といわれている 'it is said' and とかんがえられている 'it is thought' both soften a statement and make it feel more objective or widely accepted. This can make an idea sound more trustworthy or backed by others. Both are formal and common in writing.

related.title: ～は～となっている
related.meaning: It is (has been), Have/has become, Happen to be, Has been established
related.comparison: ～は～となっている and と考えられている both express current states or views, but differ in tone. ～は～となっている describes some sort of established fact that is now 'the way things are', e.g. 店は日曜休業となっている ('The store is closed on Sundays'). と考えられている expresses a generally held belief or reasoning and is often used to make a statement sound less direct, e.g. 彼は天才だと考えられている ('He is thought to be a genius').

related.title: ～は～となっている
related.meaning: It is (has been), Have/has become, Happen to be, Has been established
related.comparison: ～は～となっている and と考えられている both express current states or views, but differ in tone. ～は～となっている describes some sort of established fact that is now 'the way things are', e.g. 店は日曜休業となっている ('The store is closed on Sundays'). と考えられている expresses a generally held belief or reasoning and is often used to make a statement sound less direct, e.g. 彼は天才だと考えられている ('He is thought to be a genius').

related.title: ようだ
related.meaning: It seems that, It appears that, It looks like
related.comparison: ようだ expresses that something appears a certain way, based on various cues that are not always explicitly stated, e.g. 雨が降るようだ ('It seems it will rain.', perhaps because people are carrying umbrellas, etc.). In contrast, ときいた specifically means 'I heard (A)', and indicates that the information is hearsay directly obtained by the speaker.

related.title: んだって
related.meaning: I heard that, He or She said that
related.comparison: Both ときいた and んだって express hearsay, but with different nuances and formality. ときいた means 'I heard (A).' and conveys information directly heard by the speaker, e.g. 高いときいた ('I heard it was expensive.'). In contrast, んだって is casual and adds an explanatory nuance, like 'They say (A).' or 'I heard that (A).', e.g. 高いんだって ('They say it's expensive, you know.').

related.title: らしい ①
related.meaning: Seems like, Apparently, I heard
related.comparison: らしい ① and ときいた both convey information obtained from another source, but with different nuances. らしい ① expresses hearsay, with the nuance that the speaker is relatively confident about the information, e.g. 雨が降るらしい ('Apparently, it's going to rain.'). In contrast, ときいた literally means 'I heard (A)', with no implication about the speaker's belief, and it cannot be used for information obtained from e.g. written sources.

related.title: そうだ
related.meaning: I heard that, It is said that
related.comparison: Both ときいた and そうだ express hearsay, but with different nuances. ときいた means 'I heard (A).' and conveys information directly heard by the speaker, e.g., 高いときいた ('I heard it was expensive.'). In contrast, そうだ reports hearsay in a more abstract fashion, similar to 'They say (A).' in English.

related.title: だって
related.meaning: I heard/was said, Because, But, Even or also, Even if or even though
related.comparison: Both ときいた and だって express hearsay, but differ in tone and formality. ときいた means 'I heard that (A)' and is neutral and conveys information directly heard by the speaker, while だって is casual and emphatic, often used in conversation to mean something like 'They say (A).' or 'I'm telling you (A).'.

related.title: ってば・ったら
related.meaning: I said...!, I told you...!, !
related.comparison: Both are used to complain or express light annoyance, but the tone and target differ. ってば・ったら is a casual, emotional expression often used in spoken language, and can be used after simple phrases, e.g. もう、遅いってば (‘Hey, you’re late, I’m telling you.’). と来たら① is more descriptive and often slightly sarcastic, and is typically used e.g. after a name or description of a person to comment on their typical behavior or qualities, e.g. 田中くんと来たら文句ばかりだ (‘That Tanaka, all he does is complain.’).

related.title: ったら・といったら
related.meaning: Cannot tell, describe, say, express or find words for how, Words cannot express, Too … for words, Extremely
related.comparison: Both are used to single out a person or thing and comment on it, often with complaint or emphasis, but the tone differs. ったら・といったら is more direct and emotional, commonly used in spoken Japanese to show irritation or exasperation toward someone’s behavior, as in あの人ったら約束を全然守らない ('That person just never keeps promises.'). と来たら① is slightly more descriptive and observational, often used to highlight someone’s typical behavior or qualities with mild sarcasm or criticism, as in あの上司と来たら文句ばかりだ ('That boss, all he does is complain.').

related.title: ところだった ②
related.meaning: Was just about to do something, Was in the middle of...
related.comparison: ところだった can be used with two closely related nuances. The more literal meaning expresses the idea of 'was just about to (A)', as in: 昼ご飯を食べるところだった ('I was just about to eat lunch.'). The more figurative meaning conveys that something nearly happened but ultimately didn't, similar to 'I almost (A)' or 'I nearly (A)' in English, as in: 死ぬところだった ('I almost died' [but didn't]).

related.title: るところだ
related.meaning: About to, On the verge of
related.comparison: るところだ and ところだった① both express that an action is or was about to happen, but differ in outcome. るところだ means the action is about to occur and likely will. ところだった① means it was on the verge of happening, but didn't in the end.

related.title: それにしても
related.meaning: Nevertheless, Even so
related.comparison: Both express contrast, but with different nuance. ところが introduces an unexpected result, e.g. 試合に勝つと思った。ところが負けた ('I thought we'd win the game. But we lost.') それにしても acknowledges the situation but adds an emotional reaction or further judgment, e.g. 仕方がない。それにしても悔しい ('It can't be helped. Even so, it's frustrating.')

related.title: 却って
related.meaning: All the more, On the contrary, Conversely
related.comparison: Both introduce unexpected outcomes, but differ in type of surprise. 却って means the result was the opposite of what was intended or expected, e.g. 謝ったら却って怒られた ('When I apologized, I was yelled at instead.') ところが highlights a surprising turn of events, though not necessarily the exact opposite, e.g. 田中さんに電話をかけた。ところがもう出発していた ('I called Tanaka. But he had already departed.')

related.title: だが・ですが
related.meaning: But, Still, However, Nevertheless
related.comparison: Both だが・ですが and ところが express contrast, but with different nuances. だが・ですが is a neutral transition similar to 'but' or 'however,' and can appear in various positions within a sentence. ところが usually comes at the start of a sentence and emphasizes an unexpected or surprising result, often appearing in storytelling or narration.

related.title: が
related.meaning: But, However
related.comparison: Both ところが and が express contrast or contradiction. ところが is used at the beginning of a sentence to emphasize a contrast between expectation and reality. In contrast, が is used after a phrase or at the end of a sentence to express simple contrast, or sometimes to add information or context without implying surprise or expectation.

related.title: けど・だけど
related.meaning: But, However
related.comparison: Both ところが and けど・だけど express contrast or contradiction. The key difference is that ところが is used at the beginning of a sentence to emphasize a contrast between the expected result and reality, and is more formal. In contrast, けど・だけど are used after a phrase or at the end of a sentence to express simple contrast or contradiction.

related.title: それでも
related.meaning: But still, And yet, Even so, Nevertheless
related.comparison: それでも and ところが both show contrast, like 'even so' or 'and yet'. それでも has a neutral tone and simply adds something that goes against the previous statement. ところが implies something unexpected or surprising happened, especially outside the speaker's control.

related.title: けれども
related.meaning: But, Although
related.comparison: Both ところが and けれども are linking phrases used to express contrast. けれども (or the casual form けど) shows a simple contrast or transition, similar to 'but' in English, and can appear mid-sentence or at the end. In contrast, ところが is typically used at the beginning of a sentence and adds a strong nuance of surprise or something unexpected.

related.title: ものを
related.meaning: But, Although, If only, I wish I had
related.comparison: These expressions seem similar in that both use a noun followed by を to express that something is 'operating' on a given situation. ところを is often used to express that acknowledge a situation and acknowledge/describe something affecting it, e.g. お忙しいところをすみません ('I'm sorry for interrupting you at a busy time.') while ものを is often used to expect contrary-to-fact wishes about things that should have happened, but didn't, e.g. みんなで協力してやればいいものを ('If only you had cooperated and done it together, but…')

related.title: ところだった ①
related.meaning: Almost, Nearly, About to, On the verge of
related.comparison: ところだった can be used with two closely related nuances. The more literal meaning expresses the idea of 'was just about to (A)', as in: 昼ご飯を食べるところだった ('I was just about to eat lunch.'). The more figurative meaning conveys that something nearly happened but ultimately didn't, similar to 'I almost (A)' or 'I nearly (A)' in English, as in: 死ぬところだった ('I almost died' [but didn't]).

related.title: といえば
related.meaning: Speaking of, If it were the case that
related.comparison: Both can be used to shift the topic, but といえば is used after a noun to bring up something related or comment on that thing, e.g. 京都といえば、有名なお寺が多いね ('Speaking of Kyoto, there many famous temples, aren't there.'). In contrast, ところで typically starts a new sentence to introduce an unrelated topic, e.g. ところで、週末の予定は？ ('By the way, what are your plans for the weekend?').

related.title: そういえば
related.meaning: Speaking of which, By the way, Come to think of it
related.comparison: Both can be used at the beginning of a sentence to introduce a new topic, but with different nuance. ところで marks a clear, unrelated shift, similar to 'by the way' in English, e.g. ところで、週末は何してた？ ('By the way, what did you do this weekend?') そういえば recalls something related to the current topic, e.g. そういえば、田中さんに会ったよ ('Speaking of which, I saw Tanaka.')

related.title: からして
related.meaning: Even..., Judging from, Based on
related.comparison: Both indicate judgment, but differ in form. からして follows nouns, as in 顔つきからして疲れている (Judging from his face, he’s tired). ところを見ると follows verbs, focusing on directly observed actions, as in 帰るところを見ると用事が終わった (Seeing him leave, his business must be finished).

related.title: からすると・からすれば
related.slug: からすると-からすれば
related.meaning: Judging from, Considering
related.comparison: Both describe conclusions based on evidence, but differ in usage. ところを見ると attaches to verbs, drawing inferences from observed action, as in 人が並んでいるところを見ると人気だ (Seeing people lining up, it must be popular). からすると・からすれば follow nouns, as in 成績からすると合格できる (Judging from his grades, he can pass), and can also express personal perspective.

synonyms.title: とかんがえられている
synonyms.slug: とかんがえられている
synonyms.meaning: Is considered, Is thought of as
synonyms.comparison: とされている and とかんがえられている both mean 'It is believed/considered (A)' and are used to present ideas objectively. Both make statements sound more neutral or widely accepted, often in formal writing, academic papers, or news reports.

related.title: ～は～となっている
related.meaning: It is (has been), Have/has become, Happen to be, Has been established
related.comparison: Both present an accepted state or fact, but differ in nuance. ～は～となっている describes that something has been established and is now 'the way things are', e.g. 朝礼は毎朝１０時開始となっている ('The morning meeting is set to start every day at 10 a.m'). とされている conveys that it is generally regarded in a certain way or as something, e.g. この神社は最古のものとされている ('This shrine is considered the oldest one').

related.title: ということだ
related.meaning: I heard that, Rumor has it that, It is said that, It means that
related.comparison: とされている and ということだ both report other people's opinions. ということだ has a nuance of direct hearsay: 'They say that (A)'. On the other hand, とされている presents something as generally believed or accepted. It's less about quoting someone and more about shared public perception.

related.title: ～は～となっている
related.meaning: It is (has been), Have/has become, Happen to be, Has been established
related.comparison: Both present an accepted state or fact, but differ in nuance. ～は～となっている describes that something has been established and is now 'the way things are', e.g. 朝礼は毎朝１０時開始となっている ('The morning meeting is set to start every day at 10 a.m'). とされている conveys that it is generally regarded in a certain way or as something, e.g. この神社は最古のものとされている ('This shrine is considered the oldest one').

related.title: には
related.meaning: In order to, In regard to, For
related.comparison: Both can mean as far as (A) is concerned, but differ in nuance. には emphasizes to/for (A) in a fundamental sense, as in 私には分からない (It’s not clear to me). としては highlights a position or standpoint, as in 私としては許せない (As far as I’m concerned, I can’t forgive them).

related.title: たる
related.meaning: To be (in a position of), As a, For
related.comparison: Both relate to identity or role. たる is classical and formal, emphasizing the qualities expected of that role, as in リーダーたる者は責任感が必要だ (A leader must have responsibility). としては emphasizes perspective from a role or position, as in 教師としては当然だ (As a teacher, it’s natural).

related.title: にしては
related.meaning: (Even) considering, (Even) for
related.comparison: にしては and としては both relate to judgment, but にしては highlights a state or result that departs from what one might expect, e.g. 子どもにしてはおとなしい ('He's quiet for a child'), while としては simply shows perspective, e.g. 親としては反対だ ('As a parent, I'm against it').

related.title: に取って
related.meaning: To, For, Concerning
related.comparison: に取って (usually written in kana as にとって) marks relevance or effect from a certain standpoint, e.g. 学生にとって分かりにくい説明だ ('It's a hard-to-understand explanation for students'). としては expresses viewpoint based on role or identity, e.g. 留学生としては反対だ ('As an exchange student, I disagree').

related.title: なら
related.meaning: Were it, If, If it's the case, As for (Conditional)
related.comparison: Both としたら・とすれば・とすると and なら express 'if,' but with different nuance. としたら (and its variants) emphasize a strongly hypothetical condition, like the English 'if we suppose that (A)'. In contrast, なら can imply that the speaker believes the condition likely to happen, e.g. 'if it's true that (A)', or 'if it's the case that (A)'.

related.title: としても
related.meaning: Even if (we assume), Assuming,  Also as
related.comparison: としたら・とすれば・とすると and としても both introduce hypothetical situations. The former typically presents a scenario as worth considering, e.g. 留学するとしたら ('If I were to study abroad'). The latter introduces an unlikely or extreme case and implies that something will happen regardless, e.g. 行くとしても一人では行かない ('Even if I go, I won't go alone').

related.title: ばあいは
related.meaning: In the event of, In the case of
related.comparison: ばあいは and としたら・とすれば・とすると both express the idea of 'in the case of' or 'in the event that'. In general, ばあいは (often written in kanji as 場合は) is more concrete and used for specific situations that might arise. For example, 行く場合 'in the event that you go'. In contrast, としたら・とすれば・とすると is typically used to discuss more hypothetical scenarios.

related.title: たる
related.meaning: To be (in a position of), As a, For
related.comparison: たる and として both mean 'as (A)' or 'in the role of (A)', but たる is formal and often used when describing what someone in that position should be or do, e.g. 指導者たる者 ('a person in the role of leader'). として is more neutral and can be found in everyday speech, e.g. 学生として ('as a student').

related.title: にしても
related.meaning: Even though, Even if, Even when, Also
related.comparison: にしても and としても both mean 'even if', but にしても can introduce a judgment that doesn't directly follow from the condition, e.g. 行くにしても不安だ ('Even if I go, I'm uneasy'). としても implies that the result stems logically from the assumption, e.g. 行くとしても一度だけだ ('Even if I go, it'll be only once').

related.title: たとえ〜ても
related.meaning: Even if…is the case, Supposing that
related.comparison: Both express hypothetical conditions, but with different implications. たとえ〜ても presents contrast, like 'Even if (A), still (B)', e.g. たとえ雨が降っても行きます 'Even if it rains, I'll go.' としても assumes (A) as a premise for argument or stance, e.g. 行くとしても一人では行かない 'Even if I go, I won't go alone.'

related.title: としたら・とすれば・とすると
related.slug: としたら-とすれば-とすると
related.meaning: If it were the case that, Assuming that...
related.comparison: としたら・とすれば・とすると and としても both introduce hypothetical situations. The former typically presents a scenario as worth considering, e.g. 留学するとしたら ('If I were to study abroad'). The latter introduces an unlikely or extreme case and implies that something will happen regardless, e.g. 行くとしても一人では行かない ('Even if I go, I won't go alone').

related.title: ても
related.meaning: Even if, Even though
related.comparison: Both forms can express the meaning of 'even if' or 'even though.' ても is more versatile and commonly appears in expressions like てもいい 'it's okay to (A)' to ask or give permission. としても has a stronger hypothetical nuance similar to 'Even assuming that…', and can also serve as a more emphatic version of として. For example, 私としても大変だ ('It's tough even for me.').

related.title: ぜんぜん
related.meaning: (Not) at all
related.comparison: Both ぜんぜん (with a negative form) and とても～ない can mean 'not at all', but differ slightly in nuance. とても～ない emphasizes impossibility, e.g. とてもできない and ぜんぜんできない both mean 'I absolutely can't do it.'. With adjectives like 美味しくない, where there is no sense of potential, 全然 is preferred, e.g. 全然美味しくない ('It's not tasty at all.').

related.title: 全く～ない
related.meaning: Not~at all
related.comparison: 全く～ない and とても～ない both express strong negation, but differ in nuance. 全く～ない is used to completely deny or negate something, indicating total absence or lack. とても～ない, on the other hand, implies an extreme state or condition that makes something impossible, adding a nuance of implausibility or incapability.

related.title: すこしも～ない
related.meaning: Not even a little, Not at all, Not in the least, Never
related.comparison: とても～ない is an emphatic negative used to show that something is far beyond one's ability. For example, とてもできない ('I can't do that at all.'). In contrast, すこしも～ない means 'not even a little bit (A)' and isn't limited to ability. For example, すこしも面白くない ('It's not interesting at all.'). Unlike すこしも, とても can be used without ない if the meaning is still negative, e.g. とても無理だ ('It's completely impossible.').

related.title: 別に〜ない
related.meaning: Not really, Not particularly, It's not like...
related.comparison: なんて can overlap in meaning with とは when used in phrases like 優勝できる（とは）・（なんて）夢にも思わなかった ('Never even in my dreams did I think we could win the championship.'). なんて however has a variety of usages that do not apply to とは, such as to comment (often negatively) on 'things like (A)', e.g. 嘘なんて大嫌い ('I hate things like lies.').

related.title: まさか
related.meaning: I never dreamed, Never, No way, Don't tell me, You don't say
related.comparison: Both expressions can be used to convey shock and surprise, though how they function in a sentence differs. まさか precedes whatever the speaker is surprised about, e.g. まさか勝てるなんて ('To think they could win!'), or even on its own as an interjection, i.e. まさか！ ('No way!). In contrast, とは always follows what the speaker is surprised about, and can pair with まさか for further emphasis, e.g. まさか勝てるとは (same meaning as the above example).

related.title: まさか
related.meaning: I never dreamed, Never, No way, Don't tell me, You don't say
related.comparison: まさか and とは are both used to express surprise, but function in different ways. まさか comes at the beginning of a sentence to emphasize that what follows goes against the speaker's expectations, while とは appears at the end of a sentence or phrase to express the same. Because they occupy different positions, they are often used together. For example, まさか勝てるとは！ ('To think we could win!' / 'I never thought we could win').

related.title: といえども
related.meaning: Even (if), Although, Even though
related.comparison: These are both formal/written-style expressions that have the same fundamental meaning of 'even if one says'. In practice, both are used as a literary way to say 'although’, even if there is no explicit intention of quoting something that was said, e.g. ここは治安がいい（といえども・とはいえ）油断してはいけない ('Even though it's safe here, you mustn't be careless.').

related.title: にせよ・にしろ
related.meaning: Even if, Even though, No matter, Whether… or
related.comparison: Both show concession, but differ in nuance. にせよ・にしろ makes a strong assertion that something holds regardless, as in 結果が悪いにしろ挑戦するべきだ (Even if the result is bad, you should try). とはいえ concedes a point but softens with that said, as in 難しいとはいえ不可能ではない (While it may be difficult, it’s not impossible).

related.title: にしろ～にしろ
related.meaning: Whether… or
related.comparison: にしろ～にしろ lists alternatives where the outcome stays the same, as in 成功するにしろ失敗するにしろ挑戦する価値がある (Whether you succeed or fail, it’s worth trying). とはいえ acknowledges something but introduces a contrasting fact, as in 難しいとはいえ不可能ではない (That said, it’s not impossible).

related.title: であれ〜であれ
related.meaning: Whether or, Or, No matter which, In either case
related.comparison: とはいえ 'although (one could say)' specifically qualifies a statement by saying one thing might be true, but there is more to the matter, e.g. 日本人とはいえ、フランスで生まれ育った ('Though she may be Japanese, she was born and raised in France.'). であれ～であれ is specifically used when comparing multiple options, e.g. 日本人であれ外国人であれ、法律は守らないといけない ('No matter if you're Japanese or a foreigner, one must obey the law.').

related.title: であれ
related.meaning: Even if, Even, No matter
related.comparison: とはいえ and であれ are similar in that they both express a sort of contrast, but とはいえ 'although (one could say)' specifically qualifies a statement by saying one thing might be true, but there is more to the matter, e.g. 日本人とはいえ、フランスで生まれ育った ('Though she may be Japanese, she was born and raised in France.'). Though they can overlap in some cases, であれ 'even'/'no matter' is often used together with question words, e.g. 理由は何であれ 'no matter the reason', while とはいえ cannot be used this way.

related.title: に限ったことではない
related.slug: に限ったことではない
related.meaning: Not limited to, Not restricted to, Not only
related.comparison: Both express that something is not exclusive, but with a different nuance. に限ったことではない means ‘not limited to this case’ and is used to say that a problem or tendency applies more broadly than the topic at hand, often generalizing from an example, as in 若者に限ったことではない ('It’s not something limited to young people.'). とは限らない means ‘not necessarily’ and is used to deny certainty about an assumption or conclusion itself, as in 成功するとは限らない ('It won’t necessarily succeed.').

related.title: わけではない
related.meaning: It doesn't mean that, It is not the case, It's not like
related.comparison: Both とは限らない and わけではない challenge assumptions, but in different ways. わけではない denies a specific inference or interpretation, e.g. 'it's not the case that (A)', while とは限らない emphasizes exceptions, e.g. 'just because (A), it's not always (B)'.

related.title: というものでもない
related.meaning: There is no guarantee that…, Not necessarily
related.comparison: These two expressions are similar in meaning, but with a slightly different nuance. とは限らない says that something isn't necessarily the case, e.g. '高いものが必ずしも良いとは限らない' ('Expensive things aren't always good'). というものでもない says that something cannot be stated as a general truth, e.g. '努力すれば必ず成功するというものでもない' ('It's not necessarily true that effort always leads to success').

related.title: 限り
related.meaning: As long as, While… is the case, Limited to
related.comparison: These two expressions derived from 限る contrast in meaning. とは限らない expresses that something is not necessarily the case, e.g. '雨だからといって中止とは限らない' ('Just because it's raining doesn't mean it will be canceled'). 限り indicates a bounded condition or extent, e.g. '知っている限り' ('as far as I know').

related.title: ないことはない
related.meaning: Is not impossible, It doesn't mean that it isn't
related.comparison: Both introduce nuance and uncertainty, but with opposite slants. とは限らない expresses that something isn't necessarily the case, e.g. '有名だからといって面白いとは限らない' ('Just because it's famous doesn't mean it's interesting'). ないことはない uses a double negative to express a less confident positive statement, e.g. '行けないことはない' ('It's not that I can't go').

related.title: というわけではない
related.meaning: It is not that, It doesn't mean that, It is not always the case
related.comparison: Both expressions deny absolutes, but differ in usage. とは限らない expresses that something isn't necessarily the case, e.g. '留学すれば英語が上手になるとは限らない' ('Studying abroad doesn't always mean your English improves'). というわけではない clarifies previous statements or assumptions, e.g. '嫌いというわけではない' ('It's not the case I dislike it').

related.title: 必ずしも
related.meaning: Not always, Not entirely, Not necessarily
related.comparison: 必ずしも and とは限らない often appear together as a pair in statements expressing that something isn't necessarily always the case, e.g. 必ずしも努力が報われるとは限らない ('Effort doesn't always pay off.'). とは限らない alone can express the same meaning without 必ずしも, but the pairing goes together naturally.

related.title: ことにはならない
related.meaning: Just because… doesn't mean…
related.comparison: While both deny expected conclusions, ことにはならない expresses that one thing doesn't necessarily lead to another, e.g. '話し合ったからといって解決したことにはならない' ('Just talking about it doesn't mean it's resolved'). とは限らない expresses that something isn't necessarily the case, e.g. '高いものが必ずしも良いとは限らない' ('Expensive things aren't always good').

related.title: んばかりに
related.meaning: As if, As if it were about to, About to
related.comparison: Both express the idea of 'as if (something is happening)', with とばかり（に） being essentially a shortened form of 言わんばかりに 'as if to say'. んばかりに describes a state or action that appears on the verge of happening, emphasizing intensity or appearance itself, as in 泣かんばかりに訴えた ('She pleaded as if she were about to cry.'). とばかり（に） focuses more on inferred intent or attitude behind an action, often meaning ‘seizing it as a chance’ or ‘as if to say,’ as in 選手はチャンスとばかりに相手を睨んだ ('Seeing it as a chance, the athlete stared at his opponent.').

related.title: んばかりに
related.meaning: As if, As if it were about to, About to
related.comparison: Both express the idea of 'as if (something is happening)', with とばかり（に） being essentially a shortened form of 言わんばかりに 'as if to say'. んばかりに describes a state or action that appears on the verge of happening, emphasizing intensity or appearance itself, as in 泣かんばかりに訴えた ('She pleaded as if she were about to cry.'). とばかり（に） focuses more on inferred intent or attitude behind an action, often meaning ‘seizing it as a chance’ or ‘as if to say,’ as in 選手はチャンスとばかりに相手を睨んだ ('Seeing it as a chance, the athlete stared at his opponent.').

related.title: らしい ①
related.meaning: Seems like, Apparently, I heard
related.comparison: らしい ① and にみえる both express observations, but differ in nuance and formality. らしい ① suggests hearsay or information from a third-party source, e.g. 知っていたらしい ('Apparently he/she knew.'). In contrast, とみえる is more formal and expresses the speaker's deduction based on evidence, e.g. 知っていたと見える ('It appears he/she knew.', as if drawing a conclusion based on evidence).

related.title: ようだ
related.meaning: It seems that, It appears that, It looks like
related.comparison: Both ようだ and と見える express observations, but differ in nuance and formality. ようだ is commonly used to say something appears a certain way, based on various cues. と見える is more formal or literary and implies a deduction based on specific evidence, which is usually mentioned, e.g. 慌てているところを見ると、何かあったと見える ('Judging by how flustered he looks, something must've happened.').

related.title: にみえる
related.meaning: To look, To seem, To appear
related.comparison: にみえる describes appearance based on what's visually perceived, e.g. 暑そうにみえる ('It looks hot.'). とみえる is used for logical inference based on context, as in 暑いとみえる ('It must be hot', e.g. if everyone's taking off their coats). The latter is less about sight, and more about reasoning.

related.title: にしても
related.meaning: Even though, Even if, Even when, Also
related.comparison: にしても and とも both express 'even if', but differ in formality and structure. にしても follows the plain, non-past form and is more common in daily language, e.g. どこに行くにしても ('No matter where you go'). とも follows the volitional form and is more formal and emphatic, often found in set expressions like 何があろうとも ('No matter what happens').

related.title: たとえ〜ても
related.meaning: Even if…is the case, Supposing that
related.comparison: Both express strong contrary hypotheticals, similar to 'Even if (A), still (B)'. たとえ〜ても is less formal and more common in speech, e.g. たとえ一人でも、ちゃんとやる 'Even if it's just me, I'll do it right.' とも is more literary and formal, and takes the volitional form, e.g. 死のうとも構わない 'Even if I die, I don't mind.'

related.title: どうしても
related.meaning: Regardless of, No matter what
related.comparison: どうしても and とも both express the meaning of 'even if' or 'no matter', but differ in formality and placement. どうしても comes at the beginning of a sentence to emphasize determination, as in どうしても勝ちたい ('I want to win no matter what'). とも is a verb ending, often more formal or literary than ても. For example, 彼女が何と言おうとも ('No matter what she says') is equivalent in meaning to 彼女が何と言っても, but more formal.

related.title: ようが～まいが
related.meaning: Even if, No matter what, Whether or not, [Emphatic], Volitional + が
related.comparison: Both mean even if or no matter if. とも states a single condition in a slightly formal style, as in 雨が降ろうとも行く (I’ll go even if it rains). ようが～まいが is more emphatic, presenting contrasting alternatives, as in 雨が降ろうが降るまいが行く (I’ll go whether it rains or not).

related.title: ないで
related.meaning: Without doing, Not ~ and ~
related.comparison: Though these two phrases appear similar in form, both suggesting 'without doing', they differ in that ともなく・ともなしに is used figuratively to express that the action was performed, but in an absentminded manner, or ‘without even so much as the intention of doing (A), (B)’, e.g. 見るともなく見ていたら 'as I was absentmindedly looking...' whereas ないで literally means that action was not performed, e.g. 見ないでやる 'do without looking'.

related.title: まい
related.meaning: Won't, Intend not to, Probably not
related.comparison: Though these two phrases appear similar in form, both suggesting 'without doing', they differ in that ともなく・ともなしに is used figuratively to express that the action was performed, but in an absentminded manner, or ‘without even so much as the intention of doing (A), (B)’ e.g. 見るともなく見ていたら 'as I was absentmindedly looking...', whereas ないで literally means that action was not performed, e.g. 見ずにやる 'do without looking'.

related.title: につれて
related.meaning: As…then, In proportion to, (Along) with
related.comparison: Both につれて and ともなると・にもなると describe changes that arise with conditions, but differ in nuance. につれて describes gradual, parallel change over time, e.g. '年を取るにつれて体力が落ちる' ('As I age, my stamina declines'). ともなると・にもなると marks a turning point or elevated situation, e.g. '社長ともなると責任が重い' ('Once you become a company president, the responsibility is heavy').

related.title: に伴って・に伴い
related.meaning: As, Due,  Along with, Hand-in-hand with, Resulted in, Be consequent upon
related.comparison: Both に伴って and と並んで connect two elements, but differ in meaning. に伴って expresses a causal or accompanying relationship where one thing changes in response to another, e.g. '経済の成長に伴って、生活水準も上がった' ('As the economy grew, the standard of living also rose'). と並んで indicates comparable qualities without implying cause, e.g. 'ドラえもんと並んで人気のアニメ' ('an anime as popular as Doraemon').

related.title: に沿って
related.meaning: Along, In accordance with, In line with
related.comparison: While both と並んで and に沿って indicate some kind of coordination, their core meanings differ. と並んで highlights equivalence or joint status, e.g. 'ドラえもんと並んで人気のアニメ' ('an anime as popular as Doraemon'). に沿って means 'in line with' or 'according to', emphasizing adherence to a path, rule, or guideline, e.g. '規則に沿って行動する' ('act in accordance with the rules').

related.title: と共に
related.meaning: Together with, At the same time,  As well as
related.comparison: と並んで and と共に both suggest pairing, but with different focuses. と並んで stresses equal footing or value, e.g. '村上春樹と並んで人気の日本人作家' ('a Japanese author as popular as Haruki Murakami'). と共に emphasizes accompaniment or simultaneity, e.g. '友人と共に旅に出る' ('go on a trip with a friend') or '厳しいと共に優しい' ('kind as well as strict').

synonyms.title: に伴って・に伴い
synonyms.meaning: As, Due,  Along with, Hand-in-hand with, Resulted in, Be consequent upon
synonyms.comparison: に伴って・に伴い and と共に both express things happening in connection with another event. と共に emphasizes simultaneity or shared experience, e.g. '成長と共に責任も増える' ('Responsibility grows along with maturity'). に伴って・に伴い emphasizes causality or consequential change, often in formal contexts, e.g. '経済の発展に伴って生活水準が向上した' ('As the economy developed, living standards improved').

related.title: と
related.meaning: And
related.comparison: と is a basic particle used to connect nouns, similar to 'and' in English, commonly appearing in both speech and writing. と共に, however, is a formal expression meaning 'along with' or 'at the same time as,' and is used with verbs, adjectives, or nouns. It often suggests a mutual or deeper connection, unlike と, which simply connects items without implying additional nuance.

related.title: 合う
related.meaning: To do for one another, Mutually, With
related.comparison: Both relate to the idea of doing something 'together', but with different nuances and usage. 合う attaches to verb stems to convey mutual interaction, e.g. 助け合う ('help each other'), while と共に means '(A) happens together with (B)' in the sense of co-existence or simultaneity, e.g. 年を取ると共に考え方も変わった ('My thinking changed as I grew up').

related.title: と並んで
related.meaning: In line with, Comparable with, Along with, Just like, As … as
related.comparison: と並んで and と共に both suggest pairing, but with different focuses. と並んで stresses equal footing or value, e.g. '村上春樹と並んで人気の日本人作家' ('a Japanese author as popular as Haruki Murakami'). と共に emphasizes accompaniment or simultaneity, e.g. '友人と共に旅に出る' ('go on a trip with a friend') or '厳しいと共に優しい' ('kind as well as strict').

related.title: につれて
related.meaning: As…then, In proportion to, (Along) with
related.comparison: と共に and につれて are similar in that both can be translated as 'together with', but differ in nuance. と共に can express simultaneous actions or states, or the idea of accompaniment, e.g. '厳しいと共に優しい' ('kind as well as strict'). につれて highlights proportional change over time, e.g. '年を取るにつれて体力が落ちる' ('As I get older, my stamina declines').

related.title: と同時に
related.meaning: At the same time as, The instant, The moment, As well as
related.comparison: と同時に and と共に can both mean 'together with' or 'at the same time as', but their nuance differs. と同時に emphasizes temporal simultaneity in neutral or factual contexts, while と共に often conveys shared action, emotional resonance, or development over time.

related.title: と
related.meaning: With
related.comparison: Both と and と共に express mutual action or relation, similar to the English 'with.' と is more common and used in everyday speech and writing, but it only follows nouns (e.g., 友だちと遊ぶ = 'play with a friend'). In contrast, と共に is more formal, appearing in written Japanese or structured speech, and can follow verbs, adjectives, and nouns.

related.title: 並み
related.meaning: As, On par with, Worthy of
related.comparison: Both expressions are similar in that this refer to something being equivalent to something else. 並み in particular refers to measuring up to a certain standard, e.g. 彼の歌はプロ並みだ ('His singing is on par with a pro.'), while と同じくらい simply expresses an equivalence without implying a certain standard, e.g. 息子と同じぐらいの年 'around the same age as my son'.

related.title: なり
related.meaning: As soon as, The moment, When
related.comparison: Both と同時に and なり follow the plain form of verbs and describe events occurring in close succession, but their nuance differs. と同時に means 'at the same time as' and emphasizes actual simultaneity. In contrast, なり highlights immediate consequence, something happening as soon as the first action occurs, and often implies a reflexive or automatic response.

related.title: たとたんに
related.meaning: The instant or moment, As soon as, Just as
related.comparison: Both と同時に and たとたんに describe closely linked events, but they differ in focus. と同時に emphasizes simultaneity or coordination, while たとたんに conveys a sudden, often unexpected result following a completed action. Their grammar structure also differs: と同時に follows the non-past form, while たとたんに follows the past tense.

related.title: とき
related.meaning: When, At the time of
related.comparison: とき expresses the general time of an action or event, like 'when' in English. と同時に means 'at the same time as' and emphasizes simultaneity. For example, 帰ってきた時 means 'when (I) came home', while 帰ってきたと同時に means 'the moment (I) came home' or 'as soon as (I) came home'.

related.title: と共に
related.meaning: Together with, At the same time,  As well as
related.comparison: と同時に and と共に can both mean 'together with' or 'at the same time as', but their nuance differs. と同時に emphasizes temporal simultaneity in neutral or factual contexts, while と共に often conveys shared action, emotional resonance, or development over time.

related.title: でもある
related.meaning: Be also, It also is
related.comparison: でもある and と同時に can both express the idea of 'also' or '(A) as well as (B)', though they function differently. と同時に follows the first item of information, while でもある follows the additional information. Because they appear in different parts of a sentence, they can also be used together. For example, ピアニストであると同時に、作曲家でもあります ('In addition to being a pianist, he is also a composer').

related.title: かと思ったら・かと思うと
related.slug: かと思ったら-かと思うと
related.meaning: Then again, Just when I thought, No sooner than
related.comparison: Both show overturned expectations. かと思ったら・かと思うと stresses suddenness, as in 会ったかと思ったらすぐ帰った (He came and left immediately). と思いきや focuses on realization after expectation was proven wrong, as in 勝つと思いきや負けた (I thought they would win, but they lost).

related.title: みたいに・みたいな
related.meaning: Like, Similar to, Resembling
related.comparison: みたいに・みたいな and と同じで both express comparison but differ in nuance. みたいに・みたいな suggests similarity or resemblance, e.g. ‘like’ or ‘seems like'. と同じで states something is explicitly the same, showing a stronger degree of similarity. Conversely, と違って means ‘unlike’ or ‘different from'.

synonyms.title: とかんがえられている
synonyms.slug: とかんがえられている
synonyms.meaning: Is considered, Is thought of as
synonyms.comparison: と考えられる and と考えられている are similar but differ slightly in nuance. と考えられる means 'it can be considered (A)' and implies a basis in evidence or reasoning. On the other hand と考えられている suggests something is a commonly held belief or consensus, like the English 'it is (generally) thought that (A)'.

antonyms.title: 考えられない
antonyms.comparison: These two forms of the verb 考える express opposite sentiments. 考えられない (lit. 'one cannot think') expresses strong disbelief or the idea that something is inconceivable, e.g. 'そんなことは考えられない' ('I can't imagine such a thing'). と考えられる, on the other hand, expresses a reasoned or likely conclusion, e.g. 'これは自然な結果だと考えられる' ('This can be considered a natural result').

related.title: といえども
related.meaning: Even (if), Although, Even though
related.comparison: といえども is essentially a more formal/written-style variant of the more casual/colloquial と言っても, with both meaning 'even if one says'. Though they can overlap in usage, と言っても is perhaps more commonly used when referring to something actually said, e.g. 安いと言っても5000円はする ('Although I say it's cheap, it does cost 5,000 yen.'), while といえども is often used figuratively with no explicit sense of quotation, e.g. 名人(だ)といえども失敗する時もある ('Although he's a master, he sometimes fails.').

related.title: にしても
related.meaning: Even though, Even if, Even when, Also
related.comparison: Both ～と言っても and にしても can be translated as 'even though' or 'even if', but they differ in nuance. ～と言っても is used to downplay or soften expectations, e.g. 運動と言ってもウォーキング程度です ('Even though I say exercise, it's just walking'), while にしても has the nuance of 'even considering (A), (B)' or 'no matter if (A), (B)', e.g. 忙しいにしても連絡してほしい ('Even if they're busy, I'd at least like to be contacted').

related.title: といえば
related.meaning: Speaking of, If it were the case that
related.comparison: While both relate to things that have just been mentioned, ～と言っても softens or qualifies assumptions, while といえば introduces a related topic. For example, 旅行と言っても日帰りだった ('Even though I say vacation, it was just a day trip') vs. 夏といえば海ですね ('If we're speaking of summer, the beach comes to mind').

related.title: べきではない
related.meaning: Should not, Must not , Ought not to
related.comparison: ～と言っても typically qualifies or corrects an impression based on a previous statement, while ～というのは explains or defines something more directly. For example, 忙しいと言っても一日中じゃない ('I say I'm busy, but not all day') vs. 留学というのは海外の学校で勉強することです ('What we call study abroad means studying at a school in a foreign country').

related.title: といってもいい
related.meaning: You could say, You might say
related.comparison: Both と言える and といってもいい can express a meaning similar to the English 'it can/might be said that (A)' with the difference being the degree of assertiveness. と言える is a firmer statement, literally meaning '(A) can be said.'. In contrast, といってもいい sounds more tentative, implying that it would be fine/acceptable to say the thing in question, but allowing for the possibility that other views/opinions may also be valid.

related.title: どんなに〜ても
related.meaning: No matter how
related.comparison: どんなに〜ても means 'no matter how' and emphasizes that an outcome remains unchanged even under extreme or difficult conditions, e.g. どんなに努力しても勝てない ('No matter how hard I try, I can't win'). On the other hand, どうしても expresses strong intent or inevitability, often meaning 'no matter what' or 'at any cost', e.g. どうしても行きたい ('I want to go no matter what'). It can reflect either persistence or a lack of alternatives.

related.title: たって
related.meaning: Even if, Even though, No matter how
related.comparison: どうしても and たって are similar in that both express ideas like 'even' or 'no matter', but they are used differently. どうしても appears at the beginning of a sentence to convey 'no matter what'. For example, どうしても勝ちたい ('I want to win no matter what'). たって is a casual verb ending that functions like ても. In fact, どうしても can be expressed more colloquially as どうしたって.

related.title: にかかわらず
related.meaning: Regardless of, Whether or, No matter, With or without
related.comparison: どうしても and にかかわらず both mean 'no matter' or 'regardless of', but differ in usage. どうしても appears at the beginning of a sentence to express strong will or inevitability. For example, どうしても勝ちたい ('I want to win no matter what'). にかかわらず usually follows nouns or question phrases and shows that something remains unchanged regardless of conditions. For example, 年齢にかかわらず参加できます ('You can participate regardless of age').

related.title: を問わず
related.meaning: Regardless of, Whether or not, No matter, With or without
related.comparison: どうしても and を問わず share the meaning of 'no matter' or 'regardless of', but are used differently. どうしても appears at the beginning of a sentence to express persistence or inevitability. For example, どうしても勝ちたい ('I want to win no matter what'). を問わず usually follows nouns or question words and shows that something holds true regardless of conditions. For example, 年齢を問わず参加できます ('You can participate regardless of age').

related.title: とも
related.meaning: Even if, No matter
related.comparison: どうしても and とも both express the meaning of 'even if' or 'no matter', but differ in formality and placement. どうしても comes at the beginning of a sentence to emphasize determination, as in どうしても勝ちたい ('I want to win no matter what'). とも is a verb ending, often more formal or literary than ても. For example, 彼女が何と言おうとも ('No matter what she says') is equivalent in meaning to 彼女が何と言っても, but more formal.

related.title: にしても～にしても
related.meaning: Regardless of whether
related.comparison: どうしても and にしても～にしても both express the idea that something happens regardless of differing conditions. どうしても begins a sentence to show strong intent or inevitability, e.g. どうしても勝ちたい ('I want to win no matter what'). にしても～にしても is used to list contrasting cases and state that the outcome remains unchanged. For example, 上手にしても下手にしても ('Whether you're good or bad at it').

related.title: いくら〜でも
related.meaning: No matter how (much)
related.comparison: どうしても and いくら〜でも both express emphasis but in different ways. どうしても is used when something must happen or when the speaker is determined, e.g. どうしても行きたい ('I want to go no matter what'). いくら〜でも means 'no matter how much' or 'how many', often used with amounts or degrees to show that the outcome won't change, e.g. いくら頼んでも無理です ('No matter how much you ask, it's impossible').

related.title: にせよ・にしろ
related.meaning: Even if, Even though, No matter, Whether… or
related.comparison: どうしても and にせよ・にしろ both express that something happens regardless of conditions, but differ in nuance and structure. どうしても comes at the start of a sentence to show persistence or inevitability, as in どうしても勝ちたい ('I want to win no matter what'). にせよ・にしろ presents hypothetical cases and emphasizes that the outcome won't change. For example, 雨にせよ雪にせよ中止しない ('We won't cancel whether it rains or snows').

related.title: みたい
related.meaning: Like, Similar to, Resembling
related.comparison: どうやら and みたい combine to convey a tentative impression or guess based on observed clues. どうやら adds a tone of cautious inference, while みたい expresses resemblance or appearance. For example: どうやら彼は来ないみたいだ (It seems like he’s not coming).

related.title: ようだ
related.meaning: It seems that, It appears that, It looks like
related.comparison: どうやら and ようだ are frequently used together to express the speaker’s tentative judgment based on evidence or context. どうやら introduces the inference, while ようだ states the conclusion in a neutral or formal tone. For example: どうやら電車が遅れているようだ (It seems the train is delayed).

related.title: らしい ①
related.meaning: Seems like, Apparently, I heard
related.comparison: どうやら and らしい are often used together to express conjecture based on indirect evidence. どうやら softens the statement and suggests uncertainty, while らしい indicates that the information is based on hearsay or external sources. For example: どうやら雨が降るらしい (Apparently, it’s going to rain).

related.title: 何といっても
related.meaning: By any account, All the same, No doubt
related.comparison: どうせ expresses resignation or pessimism about an inevitable outcome, often with a negative tone, as in どうせ失敗するから (It’s bound to fail anyway). In contrast, 何といっても emphasizes something’s outstanding or most important quality, as in 何といっても彼が一番だ (No matter what you say, he’s the best). The former carries a defeatist tone, while the latter asserts something positively.

related.title: 何しろ
related.meaning: No wonder, After all, In fact, Because, At any rate
related.comparison: 何しろ introduces a reason or emphasis, often justifying a statement, as in 何しろ忙しいので行けません (In any case, I can’t go because I’m busy). どうせ conveys resignation or futility, as in どうせ無理だ (It’s hopeless anyway). Both can be translated as ‘in any case’, but 何しろ is explanatory, while どうせ carries a negative, defeatist tone.

related.title: ～というのは事実だ
related.meaning: It is a fact that ~, There is no doubt that
related.comparison: These two forms both relate in a sense to an unchangeable fact, but differ greatly in usage. ～というのは事実だ literally states that something is a fact, e.g. 何もできないのは事実だ ('It's a fact we can't do anything'). どうせ expresses resignation or futility with a more emotional nuance, e.g. どうせ無理だよ ('It's hopeless anyway').

related.title: ここ
related.meaning: Here, This place
related.comparison: どこ is a question word used to ask about locations, similar to 'where' in English. In contrast, ここ refers specifically to a place close to the speaker, either physically or figuratively, equivalent to 'here.' For example, トイレはどこですか？ ('Where is the bathroom?') could be answered by ここです ('It's here'—near the speaker or indicated location).

related.title: そこ
related.meaning: There, That place
related.comparison: どこ is a question word used to ask about locations, similar to 'where' in English. In contrast, そこ refers specifically to a place closer to the listener, physically or figuratively, equivalent to 'there.' For instance, トイレはどこですか？ means 'Where is the bathroom?' and could be answered with そこです, meaning 'It's there (near you, or a place I'm pointing to).'

related.title: あそこ
related.meaning: Over there
related.comparison: どこ is a question word used to inquire about a place, similar to 'where' in English. あそこ, in contrast, refers specifically to a place far from both the speaker and listener, physically or figuratively, similar to 'over there.' For example, トイレはどこですか？ ('Where's the bathroom?') could be answered with あそこです ('It's over there,' indicating a place distant from both speaker and listener).

related.title: どころではない
related.meaning: This is not the time for…, Far from, Out of the question
related.comparison: Both contain どころ and express contrast, but differ in nuance. どころか emphasizes an extreme or surprising contrast. For example, 痩せるどころか、太ってしまった ('Far from slimming down, I actually gained weight'). どころではない conveys that something is completely out of the question due to circumstances. For example, 忙しすぎて年休を取るどころではない ('I'm so busy this is no time to take a day off').

related.title: ものか
related.meaning: Definitely not, Absolutely not, Impossible, Do you really think~?
related.comparison: Both ものか and どころか convey strong denial or contrast, but differ in tone. ものか expresses emphatic denial, often with indignation or disbelief. For example, 負けるものか ('As if I could lose!'). どころか highlights an opposite or unexpected result compared to an initial expectation. For example, 優勝どころか、一回戦で負けた ('Far from winning the championship, they lost in the first round').

related.title: どれ
related.meaning: Which (of three or more)
related.comparison: どれ and どの are both question words used to ask 'which' when given the option of three or more choices. The key difference is that, while どれ stands alone and asks about one specific thing or choice (i.e. 'Which one,' or 'Which thing'), どの must always be followed by a noun. For example, どの本 means 'Which book'?

related.title: この
related.meaning: This
related.comparison: Both どの and この are used before nouns. この refers to something physically or figuratively close to the speaker, similar to 'this' in English (e.g., この色 = 'this color'). In contrast, どの is a question word used to ask 'which' when there are three or more choices (e.g., どの色が好きですか？ = 'Which color do you like?').

related.title: その
related.meaning: That
related.comparison: Both どの and その are used before nouns. その refers to something physically or figuratively closer to the listener than the speaker, or something that was just mentioned, similar to the English 'that.' In contrast, どの is a question word used to ask 'which' when there are three or more choices (e.g., どの色が好きですか？ = 'Which color do you like?').

related.title: あの
related.meaning: That (over there)
related.comparison: Both どの and あの are used before nouns. あの refers to something far from both the speaker and listener, or something familiar to both but not close, similar to 'that' in English. In contrast, どの is a question word meaning 'which' when there are three or more choices (e.g., どの色が好きですか？ = 'Which color do you like?').

antonyms.title: ものか
antonyms.meaning: Definitely not, Absolutely not, Impossible, Do you really think~?
antonyms.comparison: ものか is a strong, indignant denial, meaning Absolutely not! or As if…, as in 勝てるものか (As if you could win!). どころではない means out of the question due to circumstances, often translated as This is no time for… or Far from…, as in それどころじゃない (This is no time to worry about that!).

related.title: どころか
related.meaning: Far from, Anything but
related.comparison: Both contain どころ and express contrast, but differ in nuance. どころか emphasizes an extreme or surprising contrast. For example, 痩せるどころか、太ってしまった ('Far from slimming down, I actually gained weight'). どころではない conveys that something is completely out of the question due to circumstances. For example, 忙しすぎて年休を取るどころではない ('I'm so busy this is no time to take a day off').

related.title: それ
related.meaning: That
related.comparison: どれ is a question word used to ask 'which' or 'which one' when choosing from three or more options. それ, on the other hand, refers to something near the listener or something already mentioned, similar to 'that' in English. Unlike どれ, それ is not a question word.

related.title: どの
related.meaning: Which (of three or more), What (kind)
related.comparison: どれ and どの are both question words used to ask 'which' when given the option of three or more choices. The key difference is that, while どれ stands alone and asks about one specific thing or choice (i.e. 'Which one,' or 'Which thing'), どの must always be followed by a noun. For example, どの本 means 'Which book'?

related.title: あれ
related.meaning: That (over there)
related.comparison: どれ is a question word used to ask 'which' or 'which one' from a group of three or more options. あれ, on the other hand, refers to something far from both the speaker and listener, or something they both know, similar to 'that' or 'that thing' in English. In short, どれ is used to ask a question, while あれ refers to a specific, distant thing.

related.title: これ
related.meaning: This
related.comparison: これ refers to something close to the speaker, like 'this' in English, and can be a physical object or an idea. どれ is a question word that means 'which one?' and is used when choosing from a set of options. In short, これ identifies something specific, while どれ asks about a choice.

related.title: だんだん
related.meaning: Gradually, Little by little, Step by step
related.comparison: だんだん and どんどん are similar-sounding adverbs that both describe change over time, but with different nuances. だんだん suggests a slow, step-by-step progression, like 'gradually' or 'bit by bit'. どんどん implies faster, more dynamic change; something happening quickly or in leaps and bounds.

related.title: ますます
related.meaning: Increasingly, More and more, Decreasingly, Less and less, Keep -ing
related.comparison: どんどん and ますます are both adverbs used to describe change over time. どんどん emphasizes rapid progress or sudden development, often in leaps and bounds. ますます focuses on something already in a certain state becoming even more so, and is often used in polite expressions like wishes for continued health or success.

related.title: 一方だ
related.meaning: More and more, Continue to, Keep on, -er and -er
related.comparison: どんどん is an adverb that implies rapid or dramatic change, often in leaps and bounds, and is common in everyday speech. In contrast, 一方だ is more formal and used to describe ongoing change that continues beyond the speaker's control. Structurally, 一方だ follows the verb, unlike どんどん, which appears before it.

related.title: ば〜ほど
related.meaning: The more…the more
related.comparison: どんどん is an adverb that implies rapid or dramatic change, often in leaps and bounds, e.g. どんどん大きくなる 'grow rapidly'. 〜ば〜ほど is a structure that shows proportional change, like 'the more (A), the more (B)' in English. For example, 大きくなればなるほどいい ('The bigger it gets, the better.').

related.title: いくら〜でも
related.meaning: No matter how (much)
related.comparison: Both express 'no matter how', but どんなに〜ても is used with qualities or actions, e.g. どんなに忙しくても行きます ('No matter how busy I am, I'll go'), while いくら〜でも is used with amounts or quantities, e.g. いくら頼んでも無理です ('No matter how much you ask, it's impossible').

related.title: どうしても
related.meaning: Regardless of, No matter what
related.comparison: どんなに〜ても means 'no matter how' and emphasizes that an outcome remains unchanged even under extreme or difficult conditions, e.g. どんなに努力しても勝てない ('No matter how hard I try, I can't win'). On the other hand, どうしても expresses strong intent or inevitability, often meaning 'no matter what' or 'at any cost', e.g. どうしても行きたい ('I want to go no matter what'). It can reflect either persistence or a lack of alternatives.

related.title: にしても
related.meaning: Even though, Even if, Even when, Also
related.comparison: Both どんなに〜ても and にしても introduce conditions contrary to results, but with different focuses. どんなに〜ても emphasizes an unchanging outcome despite extreme conditions, e.g. どんなに高くても買います ('I'll buy it no matter how expensive it is'), while にしても is used more flexibly to acknowledge a point while suggesting a contrast or reservation, e.g. 行くにしても、今日は無理だ ('Even if I go, today's not possible').

related.title: にも～ない
related.meaning: Cannot... even if one wants or tries, Would like but cannot, Wants or tries but cannot
related.comparison: Both にも〜ない and どんなに〜ても deal with limitations, but in different ways. どんなに〜ても focuses on the resilience or constancy of an outcome despite extreme efforts or conditions, e.g. どんなに頑張っても無理だった ('No matter how hard I tried, it was impossible'), while にも〜ない expresses helplessness or inability despite intent, often paired with verbs of volition or emotion, e.g. 寝ようにも寝られない ('I can't sleep even if I try').

related.title: かたがた
related.meaning: Partly for (the purpose of), In addition to, Along with, By the way, And also to, Incidentally, While
related.comparison: Both are similar in that they describe doing something 'incidentally' to another action. かたがた is particularly formal and used especially in writing, e.g. お礼かたがたお伺いしたく存じます ('I hope to visit you, in part to thank you.'), while ついでに is far more casual and can be used for even simple daily expressions, e.g. 買い物のついでに郵便局に寄る 'to stop at the post office while going out shopping'.

related.title: の
related.meaning: Indicates possession
related.comparison: な-Adjectives describe nouns and connect to them using な (e.g., 好きな本 = 'a book I like'). の also connects nouns, with the first noun describing the second (e.g., 日本人の学生 = 'a Japanese student'). The choice between な and の depends on the type of word, though some words can function as both な-Adjectives or nouns with different nuances (e.g., 幸せな日 vs. 幸せの理由).

related.title: い-Adjective + Noun
related.meaning: Describing a noun
related.comparison: い-Adjectives connect directly to nouns (e.g., 面白い本) and aren't followed by だ in plain form (e.g., この本は面白い). な-Adjectives, on the other hand, connect to nouns using な (e.g., 好きな本) and are followed by だ in plain form (e.g., この本は好きだ). Note that not all adjectives ending in い are い-Adjectives (e.g., 綺麗, written as きれい in hiragana, is a な-Adjective, e.g., きれいな月).

synonyms.title: ないでください
synonyms.comparison: The ないでください form is used to make polite negative requests, similar to the English 'Please don't do [this]'. In comparison, the dictionary/non-past form of a verb + な expresses an extremely blunt or brusque command not to do something. The two forms have similar meanings (telling someone not to do something) but vary greatly in politeness level.

antonyms.title: てください
antonyms.comparison: The てください form is used to make polite requests, similar to the English 'Please do [this]'. In contrast, the dictionary/non-past form of a verb + な expresses an extremely blunt or brusque command not to do something. The two forms have opposite meanings and are completely different in politeness levels (i.e. 'please do' vs. 'don't do!').

antonyms.title: てごらん
antonyms.comparison: The dictionary/non-past form of a verb + な expresses a blunt command not to do something. In contrast, てごらん is a gentle encouragement to try doing something. For example, 食べるな！ means 'Don't eat it!' while 食べてごらん means 'Try eating it' Both are used toward someone of lower status, such as an adult speaking to a child.

antonyms.title: たまえ
antonyms.meaning: (Please) do!, Ask to, Beg to, (Order somebody to do something)
antonyms.comparison: The dictionary/non-past form of a verb + な expresses a blunt command not to do something. In contrast, たまえ expresses a strong, authoritative command to do something. For example, 食べるな！ means 'Don't eat it!' while 食べたまえ means 'I command you: eat it!' な is more casual than the formal たまえ, but both are used toward someone of lower status whom you have power over.

antonyms.title: なさい
antonyms.comparison: The dictionary/non-past form of a verb + な expresses a blunt command not to do something. In contrast, a verb stem + なさい expresses a strong but respectful command. For example, 食べるな means 'Don't eat it!' while 食べなさい means 'Eat it!' Despite being derived from an honorific verb, なさい is still a command and is used with lower-status people or children, not superiors.

related.title: ないで
related.meaning: Without doing, Not ~ and ~
related.comparison: The dictionary/non-past form of a verb + な expresses a blunt command not to do something. Similarly, ないで attached to the negative stem of a verb is a more casual and gentle request. For example, するな is a strong way of saying 'Don't do it!' while しないで is a softer, more casual way of saying '[Please] don't do it.'

related.title: な-Adjectives
related.meaning: Adjectives that are also nouns
related.comparison: な-Adjectives describe the state or appearance of nouns, like 好きな本 ('a book I like'). They can also form the predicate with だ (or the polite form です) to describe the main topic, as in それは大変だ ('That's tough'). Note that な-Adjective だ alone can form a complete sentence (e.g., 大変だ means 'It's tough' without needing an equivalent of 'it').

related.title: Verb［た・ている］+ Noun
related.meaning: Verb modified noun, Relative clause
related.comparison: な-Adjectives describe the state or characteristics of nouns. They can be used at the end of a sentence (followed by だ or です) or precede a noun with な. For example, 大変な仕事 means 'a tough job.' Similarly, Japanese verbs can modify nouns directly, unlike な-Adjectives. For example, 読んだ本 means 'the book (I) read.'

related.title: な-Adjective だ
related.meaning: な-Adjective (Predicate)
related.comparison: な-Adjectives describe the state or appearance of nouns, like 好きな本 ('a book I like'). They can also form the predicate with だ (or the polite form です) to describe the main topic, as in それは大変だ ('That's tough'). Note that な-Adjective だ alone can form a complete sentence (e.g., 大変だ means 'It's tough' without needing an equivalent of 'it').

related.title: い-Adjectives
related.meaning: Adjectives ending in い
related.comparison: い-Adjectives attach directly to nouns (e.g., 面白い本) and don't use だ in plain form (e.g., この本は面白い). な-Adjectives attach to nouns with な (e.g., 好きな本) and do use だ in plain form (e.g., この本は好きだ). Note that not all adjectives ending in い are い-Adjectives—for example, きれい (綺麗) ends in い but is a な-Adjective, as in きれいな月.

related.title: ないことには～ない
related.meaning: Unless you do something
related.comparison: Both forms use a double negative but differ in nuance and usage. ～ない～はない means 'There is no (A) that is not (B).' For example, 書けない漢字はない ('There are no kanji he can't write.') emphasizes complete ability. In contrast, ないことには～ない means 'unless', or literally 'If (A) can't happen, then (B) can't.'. For example, 漢字が書けないことには試験は合格できない ('You can't pass the test unless you can write kanji.').

related.title: ないことはない
related.meaning: Is not impossible, It doesn't mean that it isn't
related.comparison: Both forms use a double negative to convey a positive meaning, but differ in nuance and usage. ～ない～はない means 'There is no (A) that isn't (B).', e.g. 書けない漢字はない ('There are no kanji he can't write.'), strongly affirming his ability. In contrast, ないことはない softens a statement, implying 'It's not that (A) isn't true,' e.g., 書けないことはないけど ('It's not that I can't write.', i.e. I can, but perhaps not easily or well.).

related.title: まえに
related.meaning: Before, In front of
related.comparison: ないうちに and まえに both mean doing something before another event, but with different nuances. まえに simply marks one action happening before another. ないうちに adds the sense of doing something while there's still time. For example, 暗くならないうちに帰ろう means 'Let's go home before it gets dark,' with the nuance that it's better to leave while it's still light out.

related.title: うちに
related.meaning: While, During
related.comparison: うちに means 'while' a state persists, implying that an action is taken during that time, e.g. 明るいうちに帰る ('Go home while it's still light.') ないうちに means 'before' a negative state ends, emphasizing the importance of acting before something happens, e.g. 雨が降らないうちに出かける ('Leave before it rains.') Both suggest taking action while a condition remains unchanged.

related.title: ～ない～はない
related.meaning: There is not ~ that is not/does not
related.comparison: Both forms use a double negative but differ in nuance and usage. ～ない～はない means 'There is no (A) that is not (B).' For example, 書けない漢字はない ('There are no kanji he can't write.') emphasizes complete ability. In contrast, ないことには～ない means 'unless', or literally 'If (A) can't happen, then (B) can't.'. For example, 漢字が書けないことには試験は合格できない ('You can't pass the test unless you can write kanji.').

related.title: てからでないと
related.meaning: (Not) unless ~ cannot, (Not) until ~ cannot
related.comparison: Both express necessary conditions, but with different focus. ないことには～ない emphasizes that something cannot happen unless a prerequisite is met, as in 勉強しないことには合格できない (Unless you study, you can’t pass). てからでないと focuses on sequence, meaning the second action cannot occur until the first is done, as in 確認してからでないと承諾できません (I can’t approve it unless I check first).

antonyms.title: てください
antonyms.comparison: 〜てください is a polite way to say 'Please do (something),' as in 食べてください ('Please eat'). Its negative form, 〜ないで, means 'Don't do (something),' as in 食べないで ('Don't eat'). To make this negative request polite, simply add ください, as in 食べないでください means 'Please don't eat.'

antonyms.title: なさい
antonyms.comparison: なさい is a firm but polite command form, often used by those in authority (like teachers or parents) toward subordinates. In contrast, ないで is a casual way to ask someone not to do something, e.g. 行かないで ('Don't go.'). It is typically used among close friends or family.

related.title: ともなく・ともなしに
related.meaning: Absentmindedly, Mindlessly, Unconsciously, Without paying attention
related.comparison: Though these two phrases appear similar in form, both suggesting 'without doing', they differ in that ともなく・ともなしに is used figuratively to express that the action was performed, but in an absentminded manner, or ‘without even so much as the intention of doing (A), (B)’, e.g. 見るともなく見ていたら 'as I was absentmindedly looking...' whereas ないで literally means that action was not performed, e.g. 見ないでやる 'do without looking'.

related.title: なくて
related.meaning: To not ~ and ~, Not ~ and ~ (Conjugations)
related.comparison: ないで and なくて are both negative connecting forms, but with different uses. ないで is used in negative requests or to mean 'without doing' something, e.g. 食べないでください ('Please don't eat it.'). and 朝ご飯を食べないで学校に行った ('I went to school without eating breakfast.'). なくて connects clauses to describe reasons or states, similar to 'and', e.g. 高くなくて便利です ('It's not expensive, and it's convenient.').

related.title: なくて
related.meaning: Not ~ and, Not so, Because not (Examples)
related.comparison: ないで and なくて are both connecting forms of ない, but with different uses. ないで is used in negative requests or to mean 'without doing' something, e.g. 食べないでください ('Please don't eat it.') and 朝ご飯を食べないで学校に行った ('I went to school without eating breakfast.'). なくて can express a reason or cause, e.g. 行けなくて残念でした ('I couldn't go, which was disappointing.').

related.title: ずに
related.meaning: Without doing
related.comparison: ないで and ずに both express the idea of 'without doing (A)' and are used in similar ways. For example, 朝ご飯を食べないで学校に行った and 朝ご飯を食べずに学校に行った both mean 'I went to school without eating breakfast.', but ずに has a slightly more formal or literary tone, while ないで is more conversational. Unlike ないで, ずに cannot be used as a negative request form to say 'Don't (A).'.

related.title: な
related.meaning: Do not, Don't (Prohibitive)
related.comparison: The dictionary/non-past form of a verb + な expresses a blunt command not to do something. Similarly, ないで attached to the negative stem of a verb is a more casual and gentle request. For example, するな is a strong way of saying 'Don't do it!' while しないで is a softer, more casual way of saying '[Please] don't do it.'

related.title: Verb[ないで]
related.meaning: (Please) do not (for me), Casual request
related.comparison: The ないで form combines a verb in the negative with で, and has two common uses: (1) to make a negative request, e.g. 食べないで ('Don't eat it.'), and (2) to express 'without doing' something, e.g. 晩ご飯を食べないで出かけた ('I went out without eating dinner.').

related.title: ないでください
related.meaning: Please don't (Polite request)
related.comparison: ないでください is a polite way to ask someone not to do something. The shorter form, ないで, is more casual and typically used with close friends or family. While ないでください is appropriate for strangers or people of equal status, ないで has a softer, more informal tone. Adding the sentence-ending particle ね (e.g. 食べないでね) makes the request even gentler and more caring.

antonyms.title: というわけではない
antonyms.meaning: It is not that, It doesn't mean that, It is not always the case
antonyms.comparison: Both というわけではない and ないことはない introduce nuance or partial disagreement, but in opposite directions. というわけではない denies a conclusion or assumption, e.g. '好きというわけではない' ('It's not that I like it'). ないことはない uses a double negative to express a less confident positive, e.g. '行けないことはない' ('It's not that I can't go'), implying it may be possible but difficult.

antonyms.title: そうもない
antonyms.meaning: Very unlikely to, Does not seem that, Does not appear that
antonyms.comparison: Both そうもない and ないことはない express uncertainty, but in opposite directions. そうもない shows strong doubt or improbability, e.g. '雨が降りそうもない' ('It doesn't look like it will rain'). ないことはない uses a double negative to express a less confident positive, e.g. '行けないことはない' ('It's not that I can't go'), which implies that while not impossible, it might be with some difficulty.

antonyms.title: はずがない
antonyms.meaning: Hardly possible, Cannot be, Highly unlikely, Improbable
antonyms.comparison: はずがない strongly denies something, as in そんなことがあるはずがない ('That can't possibly be true.'). In contrast, ないことはない suggests that something is technically possible, but maybe unlikely or difficult, as in 行けないことはない ('It's not that I can't go.').

related.title: とは限らない
related.meaning: Not necessarily so, Is not always true, There is no guarantee that
related.comparison: Both introduce nuance and uncertainty, but with opposite slants. とは限らない expresses that something isn't necessarily the case, e.g. '有名だからといって面白いとは限らない' ('Just because it's famous doesn't mean it's interesting'). ないことはない uses a double negative to express a less confident positive statement, e.g. '行けないことはない' ('It's not that I can't go').

related.title: ～ない～はない
related.meaning: There is not ~ that is not/does not
related.comparison: Both forms use a double negative to convey a positive meaning, but differ in nuance and usage. ～ない～はない means 'There is no (A) that isn't (B).', e.g. 書けない漢字はない ('There are no kanji he can't write.'), strongly affirming his ability. In contrast, ないことはない softens a statement, implying 'It's not that (A) isn't true,' e.g., 書けないことはないけど ('It's not that I can't write.', i.e. I can, but perhaps not easily or well.).

synonyms.title: な
synonyms.comparison: The ないでください form is used to make polite negative requests, similar to the English 'Please don't do [this]'. In comparison, the dictionary/non-past form of a verb + な expresses an extremely blunt or brusque command not to do something. The two forms have similar meanings (telling someone not to do something) but vary greatly in politeness level.

antonyms.title: てごらん
antonyms.comparison: ないでください is a polite way to ask someone not to do something. It's neutral and appropriate for strangers or people of equal status. In contrast, てごらん is a gentle request form often used by adults to children, etc., that adds a nuance of 'Give this a try' or 'Try doing this (to see how you like it).'

antonyms.title: なさい
antonyms.comparison: ないでください is a polite way to ask someone not to do something. It is relatively neutral, and could be used with strangers or people equal in status to you. In contrast, なさい is a command form, similar to the English 'Do (this),' typically used when one person is in a higher position, such as teachers to students, adults to children, etc.

antonyms.title: お～願う
antonyms.comparison: ないでください is used to ask someone not to do something in a polite way. It is relatively neutral, and could be used with strangers or people equal in status to you. In contrast, お～願う is an extremely polite and formal way to make a request, often used in professional contexts (such as to a customer or client) or formal announcements.

antonyms.title: てください
antonyms.comparison: てください is a basic way to make a polite request, meaning 'Please do (something),' while ないでください is its negative counterpart, meaning 'Please don't do (something).' For example, 食べてください means 'Please eat (this),' while 食べないでください means 'Please don't eat (this).'

antonyms.title: てくれる
antonyms.meaning: To do something for someone (usually you)
antonyms.comparison: ないでください is used to ask someone not to do something in a polite way. It's relatively neutral and can be used with strangers or people of equal status. In contrast, てくれる, when said with a rising question intonation, is a casual and gentle way to ask a favor from someone close, such as a friend or family member. It feels softer and more familiar than standard polite forms.

antonyms.title: お～ください
antonyms.comparison: ないでください is a polite way to ask someone not to do something. It's neutral and can be used with strangers or people of equal status. In contrast, お〜ください is an honorific request form used with superiors, customers, or others of higher status. Some verbs, like 食べる and 行く, have special honorific forms such as 召し上がる and いらっしゃる, which are used instead of the お〜ください structure.

antonyms.title: ていただけませんか
antonyms.meaning: Won't you, Could you please (Humble request)
antonyms.comparison: ないでください is a polite way to ask someone not to do something. It's neutral and appropriate for strangers or people of equal status. In contrast, 〜ていただけませんか is a more respectful and humble way to ask someone to do something, typically used with clients, customers, or superiors when 〜てください might sound too direct.

related.title: ないで
related.meaning: Without doing, Not ~ and ~
