related.comparison: While both にかかわる and において・における describe relationships with nouns, their meanings differ. にかかわる expresses significant relevance or impact, often with serious subjects, e.g. '命にかかわる' ('a matter of life'). において・における simply marks context without implying influence, e.g. '歴史において重要な事件' ('an important event in history').

related.title: で
related.meaning: At, In
related.comparison: Both で and において・における indicate the location where an action takes place, but differ significantly in formality. で is common in daily speech and informal writing, whereas において・における appear mainly in formal contexts or written language. Additionally, において・における can express meanings like 'in terms of' or 'with respect to.'

related.title: ために
related.meaning: Due to, Because of, As a result of
related.comparison: Both express reason or cause, but with different constraints. ために connects to nouns with の and means 'due to (A)' or 'for the purpose of (A)'. にあって attaches directly to a noun and is limited to situations, positions, or conditions, e.g. 非常時にあって ('in a state of emergency') It cannot be used with general nouns the way ために can.

related.title: 次第だ・次第で
related.meaning: Depending on, So
related.comparison: にかかっている expresses strong dependence, implying that the outcome rests entirely on something, as in 成功は君にかかっている (Success depends on you). 次第だ・次第で also conveys dependence, but less emphatically, and 次第で can modify the following phrase, as in 調査の結果次第で方針を決める (We will decide the policy based on the results of the investigation).

related.title: 如何
related.meaning: Depending on
related.comparison: Both can express that something is dependent on something, but their usage differs. にかかっている is used at the end of a phrase or sentence, e.g. 開催できるかどうかは天気にかかっている ('Whether or not we can hold the event depends on the weather.'). In contrast, 如何 can be used in mid-sentence in forms like ～の如何で or ～の如何によって, e.g. 君の努力の如何によって 'depending on your effort…'. Because of the overlap in meaning, they can even be used together for further emphasis, i.e. ～如何にかかっている 'depending on'.

synonyms.title: を問わず
synonyms.meaning: Regardless of, Whether or not, No matter, With or without
synonyms.comparison: Both mean regardless of, but differ slightly in register. にかかわらず is more formal or literary, often used even with negative implications, as in 年齢にかかわらず (Regardless of age). を問わず is more common in daily usage, treating conditions equally without preference, as in 学歴を問わず (Regardless of academic background).

synonyms.title: にもかかわらず
synonyms.meaning: Despite, In spite of, Regardless, Nevertheless
synonyms.comparison: にかかわらず means regardless of or whether or not, as in 年齢にかかわらず (Regardless of age). にもかかわらず means in spite of and highlights contradiction between expectation and reality, often with surprise, as in 雨にもかかわらず人が集まった (In spite of the rain, people gathered).

related.title: ～ても～なくても
related.meaning: Whether ~ or not
related.comparison: ～ても～なくても and にかかわらず both express that something holds regardless of condition. ～ても～なくても contrasts an action with its negative, e.g. 行っても行かなくても ('whether you go or not'), while にかかわらず can attach to a single noun, e.g. 天気にかかわらず ('regardless of the weather'), or a pair of opposing concepts, e.g. 早いか遅いかに関わらず ('whether early or late').

related.title: どうしても
related.meaning: Regardless of, No matter what
related.comparison: どうしても and にかかわらず both mean 'no matter' or 'regardless of', but differ in usage. どうしても appears at the beginning of a sentence to express strong will or inevitability. For example, どうしても勝ちたい ('I want to win no matter what'). にかかわらず usually follows nouns or question phrases and shows that something remains unchanged regardless of conditions. For example, 年齢にかかわらず参加できます ('You can participate regardless of age').

related.title: にもかかわらず
related.meaning: Despite, In spite of, Regardless, Nevertheless
related.comparison: These expressions share the verb かかわる but have opposite functions. にかかわる describes a relation or connection, often serious, as in 命にかかわる問題 (A matter of life and death). にもかかわらず is adverbial, expressing contrast or contradiction, as in 年齢にもかかわらず (Regardless of age).

related.title: について
related.meaning: Concerning, About, Regarding
related.comparison: Both patterns relate to a topic, but differ in meaning, tone, and grammatical usage. について means 'about (A)' or 'concerning (A)' and attaches to verb phrases, e.g. スポーツについて話す ('Talk about sports'), while にかかわる is more formal and attaches to nouns, typically describing a more serious or consequential relationship, such as one involving life, health, or major repercussions, e.g. 命にかかわる問題 ('A life-threatening issue').

related.title: に関する・に関して
related.meaning: Related to, About, Regarding
related.comparison: Both follow nouns to express the idea of being related to or having to do with something, but they differ in nuance and structure. に関する・に関して attaches to a noun to mean 'about (A)' or 'concerning (A)', e.g. 歴史に関する本 ('a book about history'). にかかわる also connects to nouns but often implies a more serious or impactful relationship, e.g. 命にかかわる問題 ('a life-threatening issue').

related.title: において・における
related.meaning: In, On, At, Regarding, In terms of
related.comparison: While both にかかわる and において・における describe relationships with nouns, their meanings differ. にかかわる expresses significant relevance or impact, often with serious subjects, e.g. '命にかかわる' ('a matter of life'). において・における simply marks context without implying influence, e.g. '歴史において重要な事件' ('an important event in history').

related.title: 次第
related.meaning: As soon as
related.comparison: 次第 emphasizes timing or sequencing, used to express that one action is a condition for the next, which will follow immediately. For example, 準備ができ次第出発します ('As soon as preparations are done, we'll depart'). にかかわる focuses on relevance or importance, describing how something affects or relates to another matter. For example, 命にかかわる問題 ('a matter of life').

related.title: にわたって
related.meaning: Across, Throughout, Extends, Over (a period of time or range)
related.comparison: にかけて and にわたって both describe range or extent, but differ in specificity. にかけて implies an approximate span in time or space, e.g. '九州から関西にかけて' ('from Kyushu through Kansai'). にわたって emphasizes coverage over a broad, often deliberate range, e.g. '三日間にわたって会議が開かれた' ('The conference was held over three days').

related.title: にかけては
related.meaning: When it comes to, Regarding, Concerning
related.comparison: にかけては and にかけて share the same base expression but serve different functions. にかけて indicates a loose span of time or space, e.g. 九州から関西にかけて ('from Kyushu through Kansai'), while にかけては means 'when it comes to (A)' and introduces a topic that is evaluated positively. For example, 音楽にかけては自信がある ('when it comes to music, I'm confident').

related.title: に関する・に関して
related.meaning: Related to, About, Regarding
related.comparison: Both show a relationship, but differ in nuance. に関する・に関して means 'about/concerning (A)' and attaches to nouns, e.g. 歴史に関する本 ('a book about history') or 歴史に関して書く ('write about history'). In contrast, にかけては means 'when it comes to (A)' and highlights strength or excellence in an area, e.g. 料理にかけては彼が一番だ ('When it comes to cooking, he's the best').

related.title: にかけて
related.meaning: Over (a period), From ~ until, Through
related.comparison: にかけては and にかけて share the same base expression but serve different functions. にかけて indicates a loose span of time or space, e.g. 九州から関西にかけて ('from Kyushu through Kansai'), while にかけては means 'when it comes to (A)' and introduces a topic that is evaluated positively. For example, 音楽にかけては自信がある ('when it comes to music, I'm confident').

related.title: について
related.meaning: Concerning, About, Regarding
related.comparison: Both について and にかけては can be translated as 'about' or 'regarding', but they differ in specificity and usage. について typically marks the topic of a discussion, question, or the like, e.g. 日本文化について話す ('Talk about Japanese culture'), while にかけては highlights someone's strength or expertise in a specific area, often with praise, e.g. 料理にかけては彼が一番 ('When it comes to cooking, he's the best').

related.title: に対して
related.meaning: In contrast to, While, In regard to, Whereas
related.comparison: While both can be used for comparison, their nuance differs. に対して typically contrasts two things in terms of opposing qualities, e.g. 田中先生が厳しいのに対して、鈴木先生は優しい ('Whereas Prof. Tanaka is strict, Prof. Suzuki is lenient'). In contrast, にかけては highlights strength or excellence in a specific area, e.g. 料理にかけては彼が一番だ ('When it comes to cooking, he's the best').

related.title: に取って
related.meaning: To, For, Concerning
related.comparison: に取って (usually written in kana as にとって) shows viewpoint or relevance, often translated as 'to/for (A)' or 'as far as (A) is concerned', e.g. 子供にとって有害だ ('It's harmful to children'). にかけては highlights someone's strength or skill, e.g. 歌にかけては彼が一番うまい ('When it comes to singing, he's the best').

related.title: に至るまで
related.meaning: (Everything) from...to..., From…up until..., Starting with...ending with...
related.comparison: These two patterns can be relatively interchangeable when used in the pattern AからBに至るまで 'from A to B' or AからBにかけては (same meaning). However, にかけては has a separate usage, e.g. スポーツにかけては彼が一番強い ('When it comes to basketball, he's the best’) where it cannot be substituted with に至るまで.

antonyms.title: やすい
antonyms.comparison: やすい and にくい express opposite ideas: 'easy to do' and 'hard to do.' Both attach to verb stems, e.g. 食べやすい 'easy to eat' and 食べにくい 'hard to eat', and function as い-adjectives, meaning they can be seen with tenses like 食べやすかった 'was easy to eat', and modify nouns like with 食べやすい果物 'an easy-to-eat fruit'.

related.title: がたい
related.meaning: Hard to, Difficult to
related.comparison: がたい and にくい both attach to verb stems and express the idea of 'difficult to do', but with different nuance and formality levels. にくい is used in everyday speech and reflects the speaker's personal sense of difficulty or discomfort. がたい is more formal/written-style and implies something is nearly impossible or extremely difficult due to factors beyond the speaker's control.

synonyms.title: に沿って
synonyms.meaning: Along, In accordance with, In line with
synonyms.comparison: When used to mean in line with or in accordance with, にしたがって and に沿って are similar and often interchangeable, as in 規則（にしたがって／に沿って）行動する (act according to the regulations). However, に沿って can also mean along(side), as in 川に沿って歩く (walk alongside the river), while にしたがって cannot.

synonyms.title: に伴って・に伴い
synonyms.meaning: As, Due,  Along with, Hand-in-hand with, Resulted in, Be consequent upon
synonyms.comparison: に伴って・に伴い describes changes in one thing due to another, in a proportional or cause-and-effect manner, as in 人口減少に伴って (along with the declining population). にしたがって can overlap in this usage, but it also extends to a broader sense of following rules or standards, as in 指示に従って (according to instructions).

synonyms.title: につれて
synonyms.comparison: Both express change in tandem and can often be used interchangeably. にしたがって frames it as happening 'in accordance with' another, while につれて emphasizes gradual, proportional change. For example, '話すにしたがって、だんだん打ち解けてきた' and '話すにつれて、だんだん打ち解けてきた' have nearly the same meaning.

related.title: によると・によれば
related.meaning: According to
related.comparison: Both can translate as 'according to', but differ in usage. にしたがって means 'in accordance with' or 'following' something, e.g. 説明書に従って組み立てた ('I assembled it according to the manual.') によると・によれば expresses reported information, e.g. 説明書によると操作は簡単です ('According to the manual, the operation is simple.')

related.title: をもとに
related.meaning: Based on, From, According to
related.comparison: をもとに and にしたがって can both be translated as according to or based on, but differ in nuance. をもとに suggests a foundation or inspiration, as in 実話をもとに書いた小説 (a novel written based on actual events). にしたがって focuses more on being in line with or following something, as in 指示にしたがって (according to instructions). It can also mark proportional changes, as in 普及にしたがって (as it spread).

related.title: に応じて
related.meaning: Depending or based on, According to, In response to, Suitable for or to
related.comparison: に応じて and にしたがって can seem similar, but differ in nuance. に応じて means in response to something, as in アンケートの結果に応じて方針を決める (decide policy in response to the survey results). にしたがって refers to directly following guidelines or standards, as in 規則に従って (in accordance with the regulations).

related.title: にもとづいて
related.meaning: Based on, In accordance with
related.comparison: Both are formal expressions meaning 'in accordance with (A)', but differ in usage. にもとづいて shows the foundation for an action, e.g. 法律にもとづいて判断する 'make a decision based on the law.' にしたがって can overlap, but often shows related change, e.g. 年を取るにしたがって目が悪くなっていく 'Eyesight deteriorates with age.' In this case, もとづいて would not be used.

related.title: に合わせて・に合った
related.meaning: In accordance with, Matching, Fitting, Suitable for, Adjust or tune to
related.comparison: Both relate to the idea of being in accordance with something else, but with different nuances and usage. に合わせて・に合った follows a noun and implies adjustment to suit a person or situation, e.g. 相手に合わせて話し方を変える ('Adjust your way of speaking to the other person'), while にしたがって shows something happening in response or proportion to another change, e.g. 年を取るにしたがって体力が落ちる ('As you age, your stamina declines').

related.title: に則って・に則り
related.meaning: According to, In accordance with, Based on, In line with
related.comparison: Both に則って and にしたがって mean according to or in accordance with. に則って is more formal and often used in technical or legal contexts, as in 法律に則って (in accordance with the law). にしたがって has broader usage: besides according to, it can also express sequential or proportional relationships, as in 普及にしたがって (as it spread).

related.title: れる・られる + ままに
related.meaning: Do as (one is told or ordered…), In accordance with, In compliance with, Just like
related.comparison: れる・られる + ままに implies doing something in accordance with external directions, often with little control, as in 指示されるがままに (exactly as instructed). にしたがって also means in accordance with, as in 規則に従って (in accordance with the regulations), but does not necessarily imply lack of volition.

related.title: ても
related.meaning: Even if, Even though
related.comparison: Both express concession meaning ‘even if,’ but the tone and strength differ. にしたところで is a more emphatic and formal way to say ても, often used to stress that the result will not change despite the condition, sometimes implying resignation or futility, as in 急いだところで間に合わない ('Even if we were to hurry now, we won’t make it in time.'). ても is the neutral, everyday way to say 'even if', and is used broadly without added emphasis, as in 急いでも間に合わない ('Even if we hurry, we won’t make it.').

related.title: から見ると
related.meaning: From the point of view of, By the look of
related.comparison: から見ると is used for objective judgment from a certain perspective, often based on observable facts, as in 専門家から見ると簡単だ (From an expert’s point of view, it’s easy). にしたら emphasizes a personal, subjective perspective, showing how it feels from someone’s position, as in 子供にしたら大問題だ (From a child’s standpoint, it’s a big problem).

related.title: という点から考えると
related.slug: という点から考えると
related.meaning: From the standpoint of, From the perspective of, From the viewpoint of, Think in terms of, Viewed from the angle of
related.comparison: にしたら conveys empathy with how someone might think or feel, as in 彼にしたら迷惑だっただろう (From his perspective, it must have been troublesome). という点から考えると is more analytical, focusing on one aspect logically, as in 経済の面という点から考えると重要だ (From the perspective of the economy, it’s important).

related.title: からすると・からすれば
related.slug: からすると-からすれば
related.meaning: Judging from, Considering
related.comparison: からすると・からすれば are objective, drawing conclusions from evidence, as in 態度からすると本気だ (Judging from his attitude, he’s serious). にしたら highlights a subjective, inner viewpoint, as in 親にしたら心配だろう (From a parent’s perspective, they must be worried).

related.title: からして
related.meaning: Even..., Judging from, Based on
related.comparison: からして judges from a single strong piece of evidence, often critically, as in 言葉遣いからして失礼だ (Even just from his wording, it’s rude). にしたら centers on a person’s standpoint, neutral or sympathetic, as in 子供にしたら楽しい経験だ (From a child’s perspective, it’s a fun experience).

related.title: 点
related.meaning: Aspect, Respect, Point, Way, In terms of, In that, From the point of view
related.comparison: にしたら and 点 are similar in that both can express the idea of a standpoint, but differ in nuance. にしたら expresses an opinion from a personal or empathetic point of view, e.g. 子どもにしたら大事件だ ('For a child, it's a big deal.'). In contrast, 点 will typically highlight a reference point for objective analysis, e.g. 安全性の点では問題がある ('There's a problem from a safety standpoint.').

related.title: から言うと
related.meaning: Speaking in terms of, From the point of view of
related.comparison: から言うと and にしたら both express perspective, but differ in nuance. から言うと gives an opinion or evaluation from a stated viewpoint, e.g. 医者から言うとこれは異常だ ('From a doctor's perspective, this is abnormal'). にしたら puts the speaker in someone else's position to make a subjective judgment, e.g. 子供にしたら怖かっただろう ('It must have been scary from the child's perspective').

related.title: に取って
related.meaning: To, For, Concerning
related.comparison: に取って (usually written in kana as にとって) indicates a perspective or viewpoint, e.g. 社会にとって必要な制度だ ('It's a necessary system for society'). にしたら also means 'for (A)', but often implies a more subjective or emotional stance, e.g. 親にしたら心配だろう ('For a parent, it must be worrying').

related.title: て初めて
related.meaning: For the first time, Not until, Only after, Only when, Only because
related.comparison: These expressions are often found together because their meanings complement each other. にして suggests a significant occasion or example of something, while て初めて describes something happening for the first time 'only after' a certain event. Together, they can express an idea like 結婚10年目にして初めて二人で旅行した ('They vacationed together for the first time in their 10th year of marriage.').

related.title: て初めて
related.meaning: For the first time, Not until, Only after, Only when, Only because
related.comparison: These expressions are often found together because their meanings complement each other. にして suggests a significant occasion or example of something, while て初めて describes something happening for the first time 'only after' a certain event. Together, they can express an idea like 結婚10年目にして初めて二人で旅行した ('They vacationed together for the first time in their 10th year of marriage.').

related.title: にして②
related.meaning: To be ~ and ~, To be ~ as well as ~, Both ~ and ~
related.comparison: These are the same combination of particle に and verb する and on a fundamental level have the same meaning of 'while being (A), (B)'. In practice, they may seem to have different functions, as にしては② expresses that something is two qualities at once, e.g. シンプルにして複雑 'both simple and complex', while にして① expresses something that is a condition for what follows, e.g. 30歳にして初めて海外旅行に行く 'to go on a trip abroad for the first time at thirty'.

related.title: にして①
related.meaning: At, Over, In, Only (after, when, or because)
related.comparison: These are the same combination of particle に and verb する and on a fundamental level have the same meaning of 'while being (A), (B)'. In practice, they may seem to have different functions, as にしては② expresses that something is two qualities at once, e.g. シンプルにして複雑 'both simple and complex', while にして① expresses something that is a condition for what follows, e.g. 30歳にして初めて海外旅行に行く 'to go on a trip abroad for the first time at thirty'.

related.title: にしてみれば
related.meaning: For (A), In the case of (A), From (A)'s point of view, In the eyes of (A)
related.comparison: Though both express a sort of contrast, they are used quite differently in practice. にしてみれば expresses a hypothetical from the point of view of another, e.g. 初心者にしてみればなかなか難しい implies that one might expect something to be simple, but it's difficult from the perspective of a beginner. にしては expresses a surprising or unexpected result given the basis or condition, eg. 初心者にしては上出来だ ('That's a great job for a beginner.')

related.title: に取って
related.meaning: To, For, Concerning
related.comparison: に取って (often written in kana as にとって) and にしては both involve perspective, but にとって expresses personal relevance, e.g. 子供にとっては大問題だ ('It's a big issue for a child'), while にしては shows that a certain quality or characteristic defies expectations, e.g. 子供にしてはおとなしい ('He's quiet for a child').

related.title: としては
related.meaning: As a, For a, As for
related.comparison: にしては and としては both relate to judgment, but にしては highlights a state or result that departs from what one might expect, e.g. 子どもにしてはおとなしい ('He's quiet for a child'), while としては simply shows perspective, e.g. 親としては反対だ ('As a parent, I'm against it').

related.title: からすると・からすれば
related.slug: からすると-からすれば
related.meaning: Judging from, Considering
related.comparison: にしては and からすると・からすれば both express judgment, but にしては highlights a state or result that departs from expectations, e.g. 初心者にしては上手だ ('He's good for a beginner'), while からすると・からすれば shows reasoning from a standpoint, e.g. 私の経験からすると ('Based on my experience').

related.title: からして
related.meaning: Even..., Judging from, Based on
related.comparison: にしては and からして both use a reference point, but にしては highlights a result that defies expectations, e.g. 子供にしては詳しい ('He knows a lot for a child'), while からして draws a conclusion from one part that suggests the whole, e.g. みんなの感想からして面白そう ('From everyone's impressions, it seems interesting').

related.title: 割に
related.meaning: Although, Despite, Rather...for, Unexpectedly, Comparatively, Considering
related.comparison: 割に and にしては both express something contrary to expectations, but 割に suggests mild contrast with what was assumed, e.g. 高いわりに美味しくなかった ('For how expensive it is, it wasn't tasty'), while にしては more clearly emphasizes how something defies expectations for its role or class, e.g. 初心者にしてはうますぎる ('He's too good for a beginner').

related.title: に比べて
related.meaning: Compared to, When compared to
related.comparison: Both are used in making comparisons, but にしては expresses when something is unexpectedly different from what one would assume based on a certain condition, e.g. 子供にしては大人しい ('He is quiet for a child'), while に比べて simply compares or contrasts, e.g. この問題は前のに比べて簡単だ ('This problem is easier than the previous one'). にしては implies something contrary to expectations, while に比べて does not.

related.title: から見ると
related.meaning: From the point of view of, By the look of
related.comparison: にしては and から見ると both reflect viewpoint, but にしては highlights a result that departs from expectations for a category or role, e.g. 新人にしては落ち着いている ('She's calm for a newcomer'), while から見ると gives a neutral assessment from a given perspective, e.g. プロから見ると ('From a professional's viewpoint').

related.title: に取って
related.meaning: To, For, Concerning
related.comparison: にしてみれば and にとって are similar in that both express that something is true from the perspective of a certain person or group. にしてみれば is more hypothetical, e.g. 初心者にしてみればなかなか難しい implies that one might expect something to be simple, but it's difficult from the perspective of a beginner. にとって is a more neutral 'to’ or 'from the perspective of', e.g. 私にとって宝物だ ('To me, it's a prized possession.').

related.title: にしては
related.meaning: (Even) considering, (Even) for
related.comparison: Though both express a sort of contrast, they are used quite differently in practice. にしてみれば expresses a hypothetical from the point of view of another, e.g. 初心者にしてみればなかなか難しい implies that one might expect something to be simple, but it's difficult from the perspective of a beginner. にしては expresses a surprising or unexpected result given the basis or condition, eg. 初心者にしては上出来だ ('That's a great job for a beginner.')

related.title: ～と言っても
related.meaning: Although ~ might say that ~
related.comparison: Both ～と言っても and にしても can be translated as 'even though' or 'even if', but they differ in nuance. ～と言っても is used to downplay or soften expectations, e.g. 運動と言ってもウォーキング程度です ('Even though I say exercise, it's just walking'), while にしても has the nuance of 'even considering (A), (B)' or 'no matter if (A), (B)', e.g. 忙しいにしても連絡してほしい ('Even if they're busy, I'd at least like to be contacted').

related.title: どんなに〜ても
related.meaning: No matter how
related.comparison: Both どんなに〜ても and にしても introduce conditions contrary to results, but with different focuses. どんなに〜ても emphasizes an unchanging outcome despite extreme conditions, e.g. どんなに高くても買います ('I'll buy it no matter how expensive it is'), while にしても is used more flexibly to acknowledge a point while suggesting a contrast or reservation, e.g. 行くにしても、今日は無理だ ('Even if I go, today's not possible').

related.title: にしろ～にしろ
related.meaning: Whether… or
related.comparison: にしても and にしろ～にしろ are similar in that both express 'whether', but にしても tends to focus on a single case and convey contrast or concession, e.g. 遅れるにしても連絡はしてほしい ('Even if you're late, I want you to contact me'). にしろ～にしろ pairs two options and stresses that the result remains the same, e.g. 勝つにしろ負けるにしろ ('Whether we win or lose').

related.title: たとえ〜ても
related.meaning: Even if…is the case, Supposing that
related.comparison: にしても and たとえ〜ても both mean 'even if', but にしても leans toward conditions that are reasonably likely, e.g. 難しいにしてもやってみたほうがいい ('Even if it's difficult, you should give it a try'). たとえ〜ても is far more hypothetical, e.g. たとえ失敗してもあきらめない ('Even if I fail, I won't give up').

related.title: としても
related.meaning: Even if (we assume), Assuming,  Also as
related.comparison: にしても and としても both mean 'even if', but にしても can introduce a judgment that doesn't directly follow from the condition, e.g. 行くにしても不安だ ('Even if I go, I'm uneasy'). としても implies that the result stems logically from the assumption, e.g. 行くとしても一度だけだ ('Even if I go, it'll be only once').

related.title: ながらも
related.meaning: But, Even while, Even though, Despite, Although
related.comparison: にしても and ながらも both express contrast. にしても introduces something counter to the condition, e.g. 難しいにしてもやってみたほうがいい ('Even if it's hard, you should try doing it'). ながらも shows two opposing facts that coexist, e.g. 忙しいながらも楽しんでいる ('I'm busy, but still enjoying it').

related.title: にせよ・にしろ
related.meaning: Even if, Even though, No matter, Whether… or
related.comparison: にしても and にせよ・にしろ both mean 'even if' or 'regardless of', but にせよ・にしろ are stronger and more formal, often used in instruction or insistence, e.g. 行くにせよ行かないにせよ連絡してほしい ('Whether you go or not, I want you to let me know'). にしても is softer and more reflective, e.g. どこに行くにしても携帯は持っていったほうがいい ('Regardless of where you go, you should bring your cell phone').

related.title: たって
related.meaning: Even if, Even though, No matter how
related.comparison: にしても and たって are related in that たって is a more casual form of ても, meaning 'even if'. にしても could be rephrased as にしたって, which sounds more informal and emotional. For example, どこに行くにしても ('No matter where you go') can also be expressed as どこ行くにしたって.

related.title: とも
related.meaning: Even if, No matter
related.comparison: にしても and とも both express 'even if', but differ in formality and structure. にしても follows the plain, non-past form and is more common in daily language, e.g. どこに行くにしても ('No matter where you go'). とも follows the volitional form and is more formal and emphatic, often found in set expressions like 何があろうとも ('No matter what happens').

related.title: のに
related.meaning: Despite, Although, Even though
related.comparison: Both にしても and のに express contrast, but with different nuances. のに is a general contrastive expression, like 'although.' にしても often presents a hypothetical or assumed condition, followed by an unexpected result. Like other ～ても forms, it's commonly used with Wh-words, e.g. どこに行くにしても 'no matter where you go'.

related.title: 何しろ
related.meaning: No wonder, After all, In fact, Because, At any rate
related.comparison: 何しろ and にしても are similar in that both have a general sense of 'no matter' or 'regardless', they are used quite differently. 何しろ comes at the beginning of a sentence to express 'after all, (A)', while にしても follows a noun or phrase to specifically state 'even if (A), still (B)'.

related.title: てでも
related.meaning: Even if I have to, By any or all means, No matter what
related.comparison: にしても and てでも both express determination in hypothetical or extreme situations. にしても presents a condition with contrast or concession, e.g. 行くにしても気が進まない ('Even if I go, I don't feel like it'). てでも emphasizes doing something even if it requires an extreme measure, e.g. 会うためなら借金してでも行く ('I'd even go into debt just to see them').

related.title: ても
related.meaning: Even if, Even though
related.comparison: Both forms can mean 'even if' or 'even though,' but differ in nuance. ても is more versatile and often appears in expressions like てもいい 'it's okay to (A)' for asking or giving permission. にしても carries a stronger hypothetical tone, like 'Even supposing that...' which ても alone doesn't necessarily convey. Both are used with question words, e.g. どこに行っても and どこに行くにしても 'no matter where you go', with the latter sounding more hypothetical.

related.title: かどうか
related.meaning: Whether or not, If...or not
related.comparison: かどうか expresses binary uncertainty, like 'whether or not', as in 行くかどうか分からない ('I don't know whether or not they'll go.'). にしても adds a tone of concession or contrast, like 'even if'. For example, どこに行くにしても 'no matter where (you) go'. The former asks a yes/no-type question, the latter implies acceptance of a situation.

synonyms.title: にしても～にしても
synonyms.comparison: Both list possibilities to show that no matter which is true, the conclusion holds. にしろ～にしろ is stronger and more emphatic, as in 行くにしろ行かないにしろ連絡して (Whether you go or not, contact me). にしても～にしても is more neutral, simply indicating that the result applies in either case, as in 大人にしても子供にしても楽しめる (Whether adults or children, they can enjoy it).

synonyms.title: にせよ・にしろ
synonyms.meaning: Even if, Even though, No matter, Whether… or
synonyms.comparison: にせよ・にしろ follows a single phrase to mean even if (A), as in 値段が高いにしろ欲しい (Even if it’s expensive, I want it). にしろ～にしろ lists two alternatives to mean whether (A) or (B), as in 行くにしろ行かないにしろ連絡して (Whether you go or not, contact me).

synonyms.title: であれ〜であれ
synonyms.meaning: Whether or, Or, No matter which, In either case
synonyms.comparison: Both list alternatives meaning whether (A) or (B), with the result unchanged. にしろ～にしろ is more common and conversational, as in 雨にしろ雪にしろ試合は行う (Whether rain or snow, the game will be held). であれ〜であれ is more formal, used in writing, as in 学生であれ教師であれ責任は同じだ (Whether student or teacher, the responsibility is the same).

synonyms.title: であれ
synonyms.comparison: にしろ～にしろ can follow verbs and broader phrases, as in 勉強するにしろ遊ぶにしろ全力でやれ (Whether you study or play, do it wholeheartedly). であれ follows nouns, な-adjectives, or wh-words and is more formal, as in どんな結果であれ受け入れるしかない (Whatever the result, we must accept it).

related.title: ～ても～なくても
related.meaning: Whether ~ or not
related.comparison: ～ても～なくても and にしろ～にしろ can both mean 'whether it's (A) or (B)', but ～ても～なくても contrasts something with its negative, e.g. 食べても食べなくても ('whether you eat or not'), while にしろ～にしろ typically contrasts two opposing words or ideas, e.g. 勝つにしろ負けるにしろ ('whether we win or lose').

related.title: にしても
related.meaning: Even though, Even if, Even when, Also
related.comparison: にしても and にしろ～にしろ are similar in that both express 'whether', but にしても tends to focus on a single case and convey contrast or concession, e.g. 遅れるにしても連絡はしてほしい ('Even if you're late, I want you to contact me'). にしろ～にしろ pairs two options and stresses that the result remains the same, e.g. 勝つにしろ負けるにしろ ('Whether we win or lose').

related.title: 何しろ
related.meaning: No wonder, After all, In fact, Because, At any rate
related.comparison: 何しろ starts sentences with reasons, like after all. にしろ～にしろ lists two alternatives to stress the conclusion is unchanged, as in 行くにしろ行かないにしろ連絡して (Whether you go or not, let me know).

related.title: とはいえ
related.meaning: Though, Although (I admit that), Be that as it may, Nonetheless
related.comparison: にしろ～にしろ lists alternatives where the outcome stays the same, as in 成功するにしろ失敗するにしろ挑戦する価値がある (Whether you succeed or fail, it’s worth trying). とはいえ acknowledges something but introduces a contrasting fact, as in 難しいとはいえ不可能ではない (That said, it’s not impossible).

related.title: 〜でも 〜でも
related.meaning: Whether ~ or, Neither ~ or
related.comparison: 〜でも〜でも and にしろ～にしろ both express 'whether it's (A) or (B)'. にしろ～にしろ sounds a bit more formal and hypothetical and works with a wider range of grammar, including verbs. Both forms show that the result or evaluation doesn't change regardless of the options given.

related.title: かどうか
related.meaning: Whether or not, If...or not
related.comparison: Both express the sense of 'whether', but in different ways. かどうか is used to present uncertainty, as in 面白いかどうか分からない ('I don't know whether or not it's interesting.'). にしろ～にしろ expresses that something applies regardless of the case, like 面白いにしろつまらないにしろ観たい ('Whether it's interesting or boring, I want to watch.').

synonyms.title: にしろ～にしろ
synonyms.comparison: Both list possibilities to show that no matter which is true, the conclusion holds. にしろ～にしろ is stronger and more emphatic, as in 行くにしろ行かないにしろ連絡して (Whether you go or not, contact me). にしても～にしても is more neutral, simply indicating that the result applies in either case, as in 大人にしても子供にしても楽しめる (Whether adults or children, they can enjoy it).

related.title: 〜でも 〜でも
related.meaning: Whether ~ or, Neither ~ or
related.comparison: 〜でも〜でも and にしても～にしても both show multiple options and that the result doesn't change. にしても～にしても has a slightly more hypothetical tone and works with verbs as well as nouns, e.g. 犬にしても猫にしても大変だ ('Whether it's a dog or cat, it's tough.').

related.title: かどうか
related.meaning: Whether or not, If...or not
related.comparison: Both can suggest contrastive options, but with different usage. かどうか focuses on 'whether or not' something is true. にしても～にしても means 'regardless of which', and is used for comparing two alternatives. For example, 楽しいにしてもつまらないにしても行く ('Whether it's fun or boring, I'll go.').

related.title: 何しろ
related.meaning: No wonder, After all, In fact, Because, At any rate
related.comparison: にしても～にしても structures a conditional to show indifference between possibilities, as in 雨にしても雪にしても中止だ (Whether rain or snow, it’s canceled). 何しろ introduces a reason or explanation, often with resignation, as in 忙しい、何しろ締め切りが近い (I’m busy; after all, the deadline is near).

related.title: ～ても～なくても
related.meaning: Whether ~ or not
related.comparison: ～ても～なくても and にしても～にしても are similar in that both can mean 'regardless of whether it's (A) or (B)'. The difference is that ～ても～なくても contrasts doing something with not doing it, e.g. 行っても行かなくても ('whether you go or not'), while にしても～にしても contrasts two opposing words or expressions, e.g. 勝つにしても負けるにしても ('whether we win or lose').

related.title: どうしても
related.meaning: Regardless of, No matter what
related.comparison: どうしても and にしても～にしても both express the idea that something happens regardless of differing conditions. どうしても begins a sentence to show strong intent or inevitability, e.g. どうしても勝ちたい ('I want to win no matter what'). にしても～にしても is used to list contrasting cases and state that the outcome remains unchanged. For example, 上手にしても下手にしても ('Whether you're good or bad at it').

synonyms.title: ことにする
synonyms.meaning: To decide on, Have decided to
synonyms.comparison: Both にする and ことにする share the same core meaning: making a choice or decision. にする is used when choosing a thing and follows a noun, like ビールにする ('I'll have a beer'). ことにする follows plain-form verbs and expresses the speaker's decision to do or not do something, like 行くことにする or 行かないことにする.

related.title: ことになる
related.meaning: It will end up being, It is the result of, It has been decided that
related.comparison: Both にする and ことになる relate to decisions but differ in agency. にする is used when the speaker actively chooses something, often in daily situations like deciding what to eat, similar to 'I'll go with X.' ことになる, on the other hand, expresses decisions made by others or by circumstance, like saying 'It's been decided that X.'

related.title: に決まっている
related.meaning: Surely, Bound to, Certainly, Be decided
related.comparison: Both にする and に決まっている relate to decisions but differ in use. にする expresses making a choice, often in daily contexts like picking what to eat, similar to 'I'll go with X.' に決まっている, despite using 決まる, often just shows strong belief, meaning 'It's surely X' or 'It's bound to be X,' rather than an actual decision.

antonyms.title: にほかならない
antonyms.meaning: Nothing but, None other than, Simply
antonyms.comparison: にほかならない and にすぎない both express exclusivity but with opposite evaluations. にほかならない emphasizes significance or essential identity, as in それは努力の結果にほかならない (It is nothing but the result of effort). にすぎない downplays importance, as in 僕は一学生にすぎない (I’m just a student).

related.title: でしかない
related.meaning: Merely, Nothing but, No more than
related.comparison: Both mean merely or nothing but. にすぎない is neutral and factual, as in 僕は学生にすぎない (I’m just a student). でしかない often carries a negative or limiting nuance, as in 言い訳でしかない (That’s nothing but an excuse).

related.title: ようにする
related.meaning: To try to, To make sure to, To endeavor to
related.comparison: ～にする・～くする and ようにする both mean making something a certain way, but differ in usage. ～にする・～くする is used with nouns and adjectives to mean ‘make into (A)’, e.g. 綺麗にする ‘make clean’ and 甘くする ‘make sweet’. ようにする is used with verbs and conveys the idea of making sure to do something, e.g. 走るようにする ‘make sure to (or make an effort to) run’.

related.title: ことにする
related.meaning: To decide on, Have decided to
related.comparison: ～にする・～くする and ことにする both involve making something a certain way, but differ in usage. ～にする and ～くする are used with nouns and な-Adjectives, e.g. 綺麗にする 'make clean' and 甘くする 'make sweet'. ことにする, used with verbs, implies deciding on an action, e.g. いくことにする 'decide to go'.

related.title: ～になる・～くなる
related.meaning: To become (Adjective)
related.comparison: ～にする・～くする and ～になる・～くなる express change but differ in focus. ～にする and ～くする imply making something a certain way, e.g. 綺麗にする 'make clean', 甘くする 'make sweet'. ～になる and ～くなる describe something changing on its own, e.g. 綺麗になる 'become clean', 甘くなる 'become sweet'. The difference is between focusing on the agent causing the change, or simply framing it in terms of the thing itself changing.

related.title: にまつわる
related.meaning: Surrounding, Related or relating to, Associated with, Connected to
related.comparison: Both introduce a topic, but the nuance is different. にまつわる means ‘associated with' and is used for things indirectly connected through stories, episodes, or background, as in この神社には狐にまつわる話が多い ('There are many stories associated with foxes at this shrine.'). について is a neutral topic marker meaning 'about' or 'regarding,' used simply to state what is being discussed, as in 日本の歴史について勉強している (‘I am studying about Japanese history.').

related.title: に取って
related.meaning: To, For, Concerning
related.comparison: These two phrases appear similar but are used quite differently. について means 'about (A)' or 'concerning (A)' and indicates the topic of a speech, discussion, etc., e.g. ゲームについて話す ('Speak about games'), while に取って (often written in hiragana as にとって) means 'to/for (A)' or 'as far as (A) is concerned' and is typically used to express perspective or point of view, e.g. 私にとって大切な人 ('An important person to me').

related.title: において・における
related.meaning: In, On, At, Regarding, In terms of
related.comparison: While they appear similar in form, these expressions have different nuance and meaning. について means 'concerning (A)' and often marks a topic of discussion, question, and so on, e.g. 日本文化について話す ('Talk about Japanese culture'), while において・における is a formal equivalent of で and often indicates the location or context something happens, e.g. 会議において発表する ('Announce at the meeting').

related.title: に関する・に関して
related.meaning: Related to, About, Regarding
related.comparison: Both patterns mean 'about (A)' or 'concerning (A)' and show a relationship between (A) and the following phrase, but with some differences. について is neutral in formality and attaches to verb phrases, e.g. 歴史について学ぶ ('Learn about history'), while に関する・に関して is somewhat more formal and can modify verbs (in に関して form) or nouns directly, e.g. 歴史に関する本 ('A book about history'), which について cannot do.

related.title: にかかわる
related.meaning: To relate to, To have to do with, Concerning, Depends on
related.comparison: Both patterns relate to a topic, but differ in meaning, tone, and grammatical usage. について means 'about (A)' or 'concerning (A)' and attaches to verb phrases, e.g. スポーツについて話す ('Talk about sports'), while にかかわる is more formal and attaches to nouns, typically describing a more serious or consequential relationship, such as one involving life, health, or major repercussions, e.g. 命にかかわる問題 ('A life-threatening issue').

related.title: に対して
related.meaning: In contrast to, While, In regard to, Whereas
related.comparison: Both に対して and について express a relationship between (A) and (B), but differ in nuance and usage. について means 'about (A)' and marks the topic of a statement, e.g. 日本について学ぶ ('Learn about Japan'), while に対して means 'toward (A)' and expresses direction or stance, e.g. 親に対する気持ち ('Feelings toward parents') or 優しい人に対して ('Toward kind people').

related.title: にかけては
related.meaning: When it comes to, Regarding, Concerning
related.comparison: Both について and にかけては can be translated as 'about' or 'regarding', but they differ in specificity and usage. について typically marks the topic of a discussion, question, or the like, e.g. 日本文化について話す ('Talk about Japanese culture'), while にかけては highlights someone's strength or expertise in a specific area, often with praise, e.g. 料理にかけては彼が一番 ('When it comes to cooking, he's the best').

related.title: につき
related.meaning: Due to, On account of, Each or every
related.comparison: Though similar in that both use に with a form of the verb つく, these patterns differ in meaning and formality level. について means 'concerning (A)' and typically marks the topic of a discussion, question, or so forth, e.g. 日本文化について話す ('Talk about Japanese culture'), while につき is a formal way to express a reason or condition, often used in official announcements, e.g. 悪天候につき中止する ('Cancel due to bad weather').

synonyms.title: であれ〜であれ
synonyms.meaning: Whether or, Or, No matter which, In either case
synonyms.comparison: Both mean whether…or…, but tone differs. にせよ・にしろ can be used in everyday speech or writing, as in 男にせよ女にせよ参加できる (Whether men or women, they can join). であれ〜であれ is more formal and academic, usually with nouns or な-adjectives, as in 貧乏であれ金持ちであれ人間は同じだ (Whether poor or rich, people are the same).

synonyms.title: たところで
synonyms.meaning: Even if ~ cannot or will not, Even though ~ not
synonyms.comparison: Both share an even if nuance. にせよ・にしろ asserts that the conclusion doesn’t change, as in 成功しないにせよ挑戦すべきだ (Even if you don’t succeed, you should try). たところで adds futility, as in 今さら謝ったところで遅い (Even if you apologize now, it’s too late).

synonyms.title: であれ
synonyms.comparison: Both can mean even if or no matter, but nuance differs. にせよ・にしろ stresses the speaker’s strong insistence, as in 学生にせよ社会人にせよ責任はある (Even if you’re a student or a worker, you have responsibility). であれ is more formal and written, as in 理由が何であれ許されない (No matter what the reason, it’s unforgivable).

synonyms.title: にしろ～にしろ
synonyms.comparison: にせよ・にしろ follows a single phrase to mean even if (A), as in 値段が高いにしろ欲しい (Even if it’s expensive, I want it). にしろ～にしろ lists two alternatives to mean whether (A) or (B), as in 行くにしろ行かないにしろ連絡して (Whether you go or not, contact me).

related.title: ても
related.meaning: Even if, Even though
related.comparison: Both forms can express the meaning of 'even if'. ても is more versatile and commonly used in both everyday speech and writing, while にせよ・にしろ is more limited in usage, with にせよ sounding especially formal. Note that ても is often used in the pattern てもいい 'it's okay to (A)' to ask or give permission, whereas にせよ・にしろ is not used this way.

related.title: を問わず
related.meaning: Regardless of, Whether or not, No matter, With or without
related.comparison: を問わず follows a noun (often a category) to show disregard for distinctions, as in 年齢を問わず (Regardless of age). にせよ・にしろ also implies that something holds true under different conditions, but it typically introduces a possibility that is then contrasted or disputed, as in 行くにせよ行かないにせよ (Whether you go or not…). を問わず highlights inclusiveness, while にせよ・にしろ highlights hypothetical contrasts.

related.title: にしても
related.meaning: Even though, Even if, Even when, Also
related.comparison: にしても and にせよ・にしろ both mean 'even if' or 'regardless of', but にせよ・にしろ are stronger and more formal, often used in instruction or insistence, e.g. 行くにせよ行かないにせよ連絡してほしい ('Whether you go or not, I want you to let me know'). にしても is softer and more reflective, e.g. どこに行くにしても携帯は持っていったほうがいい ('Regardless of where you go, you should bring your cell phone').

related.title: とはいえ
related.meaning: Though, Although (I admit that), Be that as it may, Nonetheless
related.comparison: Both show concession, but differ in nuance. にせよ・にしろ makes a strong assertion that something holds regardless, as in 結果が悪いにしろ挑戦するべきだ (Even if the result is bad, you should try). とはいえ concedes a point but softens with that said, as in 難しいとはいえ不可能ではない (While it may be difficult, it’s not impossible).

related.title: 何しろ
related.meaning: No wonder, After all, In fact, Because, At any rate
related.comparison: Both include しろ but are unrelated in usage. にせよ・にしろ means even if (A), as in 結果が悪いにせよ挑戦するべきだ (Even if the result is bad, you should try). 何しろ appears at the start of a sentence meaning anyway or after all, as in 何しろ忙しいので行けません (Anyway, I can’t go because I’m busy).

related.title: たとえ〜ても
related.meaning: Even if…is the case, Supposing that
related.comparison: Both show that something remains unchanged regardless of conditions. たとえ〜ても presents a contrasting hypothetical, e.g. たとえ雨に降られても気にしない 'Even if I get rained on, I won't care.' にせよ・にしろ is more concrete and neutral, often listing options, e.g. 行くにせよ行かないにせよ、連絡してほしい 'Whether you go or not, I want you to contact me.'

related.title: どうしても
related.meaning: Regardless of, No matter what
related.comparison: どうしても and にせよ・にしろ both express that something happens regardless of conditions, but differ in nuance and structure. どうしても comes at the start of a sentence to show persistence or inevitability, as in どうしても勝ちたい ('I want to win no matter what'). にせよ・にしろ presents hypothetical cases and emphasizes that the outcome won't change. For example, 雨にせよ雪にせよ中止しない ('We won't cancel whether it rains or snows').

related.title: せいで
related.meaning: Because of, Due to, Blame
related.comparison: Both せいで and につき express cause, but with different nuance and tone. せいで is more colloquial and typically introduces a cause for a negative result, e.g. 雨のせいで試合が中止になった ('The game was canceled because of the rain.'). につき states a reason more neutrally and is often used in formal written announcements, e.g. 工事中につき通行止め ('Closed to traffic due to construction.').

related.title: にあたり・にあたって
related.meaning: When, When doing something, On the occasion, At the time
related.comparison: Both are formal expressions where one condition influences another, but with different nuance. につき means on account of or due to, as in 雨天につき中止になる (Cancelled due to rain). にあたり・にあたって means on the occasion of or at the time of, as in 退職にあたり一言挨拶をする (Say a few words on the occasion of retiring).

related.title: おかげで
related.meaning: Thanks to, Because of
related.comparison: Both express cause, but differ in tone and formality. おかげで implies a positive result with gratitude, e.g. 先生のおかげで合格できました ('Thanks to my teacher, I passed.') につき states a neutral reason, often used in formal notices, e.g. 雨天につき中止します ('Due to rain, it is canceled.') It also only follows nouns.

related.title: ばかりに
related.meaning: Simply because, Only because, Just because, Simply on account of
related.comparison: These expressions are loosely related in that both indicate a reason. ばかりに conveys that a specific cause led to a negative outcome, often with a nuance of regret, e.g. 忘れ物をしたばかりに受験できなかった ('Just because I forgot something, I couldn't take the exam'). につき, by contrast, is a formal, neutral expression often used in official contexts, e.g. 工事中につき通行止め ('Road closed due to construction').

related.title: ために
related.meaning: Due to, Because of, As a result of
related.comparison: Both ために and につき can express 'due to (A)', but differ in tone and usage. ために requires の to connect to nouns, e.g. 悪天候のため ('Due to bad weather') につき connects directly and sounds stiffer or more formal, e.g. 悪天候につき (Same meaning) Unlike ために, につき cannot be used to express purpose.

related.title: について
related.meaning: Concerning, About, Regarding
related.comparison: Though similar in that both use に with a form of the verb つく, these patterns differ in meaning and formality level. について means 'concerning (A)' and typically marks the topic of a discussion, question, or so forth, e.g. 日本文化について話す ('Talk about Japanese culture'), while につき is a formal way to express a reason or condition, often used in official announcements, e.g. 悪天候につき中止する ('Cancel due to bad weather').

related.title: まい～のように
related.meaning: Almost every ~, Nearly every ~, On a ~ basis
related.comparison: まい～のように and につき both express frequency, but in different ways. まい～のように is used with 'every~' time expressions to mean 'almost every (A)', e.g. 毎日のように 'almost every day'. In contrast, につき follows time/quantity expressions to mean 'each' or 'every,' e.g. 30分につき 'for every 30 minutes'. につき can also mean 'due to', which is unrelated to frequency.

related.title: ごとに
related.meaning: Each, Every
related.comparison: ごとに and たびに both express frequency, but differ in usage. In phrases like 失敗するごとに or 失敗するにつけ 'every time (you fail)', the meaning is similar, though the grammatical attachment is different. につけ is also more formal and literary, while ごとに is common attaches directly to time or distance expressions, e.g. ３時間ごとに 'every 3 hours'.

related.title: たびに
related.meaning: Each time, Every time, Whenever
related.comparison: These are similar expressions that both express the idea that every time one thing happens, so does something else. While they are interchangeable in some cases, につけ can only be used for naturally occurring events, e.g. '見るにつけ笑ってしまう' ('I laugh every time I see it'). たびに can also be used for volitional actions, e.g. '出張に行くたびにお土産を買う' ('I buy souvenirs every time I go on a business trip').

related.title: おきに
related.meaning: Repeated at intervals, Every other, Every, At intervals of
related.comparison: Both おきに and につけ describe repeated occurrences, but are used differently. おきに attaches to time expressions to mean 'at intervals of (A)', e.g. '５分おきに電車が来る' ('Trains arrive every five minutes'). につけ is used with verbs and nouns to express a habitual response, e.g. '彼の声を聞くにつけ' ('whenever I hear his voice').

synonyms.title: で
synonyms.comparison: で and にて both indicate the location or means of an action, differing mainly in formality. で is used commonly in everyday conversation and informal writing, whereas にて appears primarily in formal contexts such as business, academic documents, or speeches. Historically, で evolved from にて, with their sounds merging over time.

related.title: に
related.meaning: In, At, To, For, On
related.comparison: に is a versatile particle marking direction, destination, the target of an action, or the place of existence. It commonly corresponds to English prepositions like 'to' or 'in,' and is frequently used in everyday conversation and writing. にて, however, is highly formal, appearing mainly in written documents or speeches, and is closer in meaning to で (indicating the place of action or means) rather than に (destination or existence).

related.title: において・における
related.meaning: In, On, At, Regarding, In terms of
related.comparison: Both are formal equivalents of で and can mark place of action, means, or method. において・における can also describe nouns, e.g. '現代社会における問題' ('problems in modern society') or '現代社会においての問題'. にて always connects to the rest of the sentence, not directly to a noun, e.g. '大会にて優勝する' ('win in the tournament').

synonyms.title: にしたがって
synonyms.meaning: As, Following, As then, According to, In accordance with
synonyms.comparison: Both express change in tandem and can often be used interchangeably. にしたがって frames it as happening 'in accordance with' another, while につれて emphasizes gradual, proportional change. For example, '話すにしたがって、だんだん打ち解けてきた' and '話すにつれて、だんだん打ち解けてきた' have nearly the same meaning.

antonyms.title: と
antonyms.comparison: につれて describes changes that occur in proportion to each other, as one progresses, the other does too. と, on the other hand, is a conditional particle used when one event naturally or habitually leads to another. Unlike につれて, と does not imply a proportional relationship between the events.

related.title: と共に
related.meaning: Together with, At the same time,  As well as
related.comparison: と共に and につれて are similar in that both can be translated as 'together with', but differ in nuance. と共に can express simultaneous actions or states, or the idea of accompaniment, e.g. '厳しいと共に優しい' ('kind as well as strict'). につれて highlights proportional change over time, e.g. '年を取るにつれて体力が落ちる' ('As I get older, my stamina declines').

related.title: に伴って・に伴い
related.meaning: As, Due,  Along with, Hand-in-hand with, Resulted in, Be consequent upon
related.comparison: につれて and に伴って・に伴い both express one thing happening as a result of or alongside another. につれて emphasizes gradual and proportional change, e.g. '年を取るにつれて体力が落ちる' ('As I age, my stamina declines'). に伴って・に伴い can also be used with one-time or habitual events, e.g. '津波は地震に伴い発生する' ('Tsunamis occur together with earthquakes').

related.title: ともなると・にもなると
related.slug: ともなると-にもなると
related.meaning: When, If, Once, As
related.comparison: Both につれて and ともなると・にもなると describe changes that arise with conditions, but differ in nuance. につれて describes gradual, parallel change over time, e.g. '年を取るにつれて体力が落ちる' ('As I age, my stamina declines'). ともなると・にもなると marks a turning point or elevated situation, e.g. '社長ともなると責任が重い' ('Once you become a company president, the responsibility is heavy').

related.title: だけでなく
related.meaning: Not only… but also
related.comparison: Both add information beyond an initial point, but the nuance differs. にとどまらず means ‘not limited to’ and emphasizes that the scope goes further than expected, often used in formal or written contexts to highlight expansion, as in 影響は国内にとどまらず海外にも及んだ ('The impact extended beyond the country to overseas.'). だけでなく is more neutral and conversational, simply adding another item without emphasizing scale or extension, as in 英語だけでなく日本語も話せる ('He can speak not only English but also Japanese.').

related.title: てくる
related.meaning: To come to, To become, To continue, To be starting to, Has been ~ing
related.comparison: Both ～になる／～くなる and てくる describe changes in state but differ in usage. ～になる／～くなる are used with nouns and adjectives to show something becoming a certain condition. てくる, used with verbs, suggests a gradual change over time. They can also be combined. For example, 面白くなってきた means 'It gradually became interesting.'

related.title: ～にする・～くする
related.meaning: Adjective + する, To make something/someone (more)
related.comparison: ～にする・～くする and ～になる・～くなる express change but differ in focus. ～にする and ～くする imply making something a certain way, e.g. 綺麗にする 'make clean', 甘くする 'make sweet'. ～になる and ～くなる describe something changing on its own, e.g. 綺麗になる 'become clean', 甘くなる 'become sweet'. The difference is between focusing on the agent causing the change, or simply framing it in terms of the thing itself changing.

related.title: 化する
related.meaning: -ification, -ization, To make something, To change, To become, Transform into
related.comparison: 化する and ～になる・～くなる both express change. 化する attaches to nouns and means '~ify' or '~ize', e.g. データ化する 'to digitize' (literally, 'to turn into data'). ～になる and ～くなる are more general-purpose ways to express becoming something, e.g. 綺麗になる 'become clean' or 白くなる 'to become white'.

related.title: のに
related.meaning: In order to
related.comparison: Both can express purpose, e.g. 日本語を勉強するのに文プロを使っている ('I'm using Bunpro to study Japanese.') 日本語をマスターするには時間がかかる ('To master Japanese it takes time.') のに follows dictionary-form verbs, while には can follow either verbs or nouns. には is also used more broadly to mean 'in regard to' or 'for', where the idea of purpose doesn't apply, e.g. 子どもには難しい ('It's difficult for children.')

related.title: としては
related.meaning: As a, For a, As for
related.comparison: Both can mean as far as (A) is concerned, but differ in nuance. には emphasizes to/for (A) in a fundamental sense, as in 私には分からない (It’s not clear to me). としては highlights a position or standpoint, as in 私としては許せない (As far as I’m concerned, I can’t forgive them).

related.title: に取って
related.meaning: To, For, Concerning
related.comparison: に取って (usually written in kana as にとって) indicates a perspective or viewpoint, e.g. 私にとって大切なことだ ('It's something important to me'). には can be used this way too, e.g. 私には分からない ('It's not understandable to me'), but also has broader uses like indicating purpose, target, or scope, e.g. 成功には努力が必要だ ('To succeed, effort is necessary').

related.title: ため(に)
related.meaning: For, For the sake of, In order to
related.comparison: Both can express purpose, similar to 'in order to' or 'for that reason', e.g. 海外に行くためにパスポートを取った ('I got a passport in order to go abroad.') 海外に行くにはパスポートが必要だ ('A passport is necessary to go abroad.') Unlike ため(に), には also has broader uses, such as marking scope or perspective, e.g. 子どもには難しい ('It's difficult for children.')

related.title: までもない
related.meaning: There is no need to, Do not have to, It is not necessary to, Needless to
related.comparison: Both can serve as emphatic ways to express 'there is no need to'. The main difference is that には及ばない is more formal and can be used following nouns, while までもない will typically always follow verbs, e.g. ご心配には及ばない ('There is no need to worry.'). vs. 心配するまでもない (same meaning, but not honorific).

related.title: までもない
related.meaning: There is no need to, Do not have to, It is not necessary to, Needless to
related.comparison: Both can serve as emphatic ways to express 'there is no need to'. The main difference is that には及ばない is more formal can be used following nouns, while までもない will typically always follow verbs, e.g. ご心配には及ばない ('There is no need to worry.') vs. 心配するまでもない (same meaning, but not honorific).

related.title: ～に値する
related.meaning: Be worthy of, Deserving of, Amounts to
related.comparison: Both expressions judge whether something deserves evaluation, but in opposite directions. には当たらない means 'does not warrant' or 'is not serious enough to justify' a reaction such as praise, criticism, or punishment, often used to tone things down, as in そのミスは責めるには当たらない ('That mistake is not worth blaming.'). に値する means 'is worthy of' and is used to positively judge that something deserves respect, praise, or consideration, as in 彼の努力は評価に値する ('His effort is worthy of being recognized.').

related.title: に足る
related.meaning: To be worthy of, To be enough, To be sufficient
related.comparison: Both assess worthiness, but with opposite conclusions and different tone. には当たらない is used to deny that an action or event reaches the level where judgment or response is needed, as in 批判するには当たらない ('It is not something worth criticizing.'). に足る means 'sufficient to deserve' and is a formal expression used to state that something meets the required standard for trust, evaluation, or action, as in 信頼に足る人物だ ('He is a person worthy of trust.').

related.title: に対して
related.meaning: In contrast to, While, In regard to, Whereas
related.comparison: Both にひきかえ and に対して set things side by side, but they differ in purpose and tone. にひきかえ is used specifically to make a contrast between two situations or people, often highlighting a difference in evaluation or outcome, as in 父が厳しすぎるのに対して、母は優しすぎる (‘My father is too strict, whereas my mother is too kind.’). に対して can be used similarly, but is also a broad and neutral expression meaning “toward,” “regarding,” or “in response to,” and is used for attitudes, actions, or opinions directed at a target without implying comparison or evaluation, as in 彼はみんなに対して優しい人だ (‘He is kind toward everyone.’).

related.title: について
related.meaning: Concerning, About, Regarding
related.comparison: Both introduce a topic, but the nuance is different. にまつわる means ‘associated with' and is used for things indirectly connected through stories, episodes, or background, as in この神社には狐にまつわる話が多い ('There are many stories associated with foxes at this shrine.'). について is a neutral topic marker meaning 'about' or 'regarding,' used simply to state what is being discussed, as in 日本の歴史について勉強している (‘I am studying about Japanese history.').

related.title: みたい
related.meaning: Like, Similar to, Resembling
related.comparison: にみえる and みたい both express that something 'seems' a certain way, but with different nuance. にみえる specifically refers to visual appearance, e.g. 'looks like', and can be used with そうだ or ようだ. みたい means 'is like' or 'seems like' more in a more general sense, not necessarily related to appearance, and is more casual than にみえる.

related.title: ようだ
related.meaning: It seems that, It appears that, It looks like
related.comparison: Both ようだ and に見える express observations, but differ in nuance. ようだ means 'appears to be' based on various cues, e.g. 虫のようだ ('It seems to be an insect.', based on looks, behavior, or context). に見える focuses specifically on visual appearance, e.g. 虫に見える ('It looks like an insect.'), without implying that the speaker thinks it actually is one.

related.title: がする
related.meaning: To give off, Smell, Sound, Taste, Sensation
related.comparison: がする and にみえる (often written in kanji as に見える) both describe sensations, but differ in usage. がする is used with sensory words, e.g. 音がする 'I hear', 匂いがする 'I smell', and 味がする 'I taste' (literally, 'It gives off a sound/smell/taste.'). に見える specifically describes visual sensations and means 'looks like', e.g. 石に見える ('It looks like a rock.'), without implying that it is the thing it visually resembles.

related.title: ～(の)姿
related.meaning: Figure, Shape, Appearance, Looks, Wearing, Dressed in, Presence
related.comparison: Both expressions relate to appearance, but with different nuance and usage. ～(の)姿 refers to someone's outward form, posture, or visual state, e.g. 彼女の頑張っている姿 ('The sight/figure of her working hard'), while ～ように見える expresses how something visually seems or appears to the speaker, e.g. 彼は悲しそうに見える ('He looks sad') or 葉っぱに見える ('It looks like a leaf').

related.title: そう
related.meaning: Look like, Appear, Seem, Have a feeling that
related.comparison: にみえる and そう both express that something 'seems' a certain way, but with different nuance. にみえる specifically refers to visual appearance and can be combined with そう, e.g. 面白そうにみえる ('It looks interesting.'). そう alone expresses general impression and doesn't necessarily rely on visual cues, e.g. 面白そう ('It seems interesting.'), which could be based on hearing about it, etc.

related.title: そうに・そうな
related.meaning: Seem, Look like, Sound
related.comparison: Both そうに・そうな and にみえる express appearance, but with different nuances. そうに・そうな conveys a subjective impression of a state, e.g. 優しそうな目 'kind-seeming eyes', or an event about to happen, e.g. 雨が降りそうな天気 'weather that seems like it's about to rain'. にみえる, used with そう or alone, e.g. 優しそうにみえる ('He/she seems kind.') or 虫にみえる ('It looks like a bug.'), specifically emphasizes visual appearance.

related.title: のように・のような
related.meaning: Like (Noun), Similar to (Noun)
related.comparison: Both のように・のような and にみえる express how something 'appears,' but with different nuances. のように・のような is used for comparisons and connects to nouns or the rest of the sentence, e.g. 春のような天気 'spring-like weather'. にみえる specifically refers to visual appearance and can be used alone, e.g. その虫は葉っぱに見える ('That bug looks like a leaf.').

related.title: らしい ①
related.meaning: Seems like, Apparently, I heard
related.comparison: Both らしい ① and にみえる can convey observations, but with different nuances. らしい ① implies hearsay or information from a third-party source, e.g. 雨が降るらしい ('Apparently it's going to rain.'). In contrast, にみえる expresses a direct visual observation, e.g. 雨が降るようにみえる ('It looks like it's going to rain.', based on visual cues like the sky).

related.title: ように・ような
related.meaning: As・like, Just like
related.comparison: ように・ような and にみえる can both describe similarities in appearance, but differ in usage. ように・ような follows the plain form of a verb or adjective and expresses general similarity. にみえる follows a noun or な-adjective (note: よう functions as a な-adjective) and means 'looks like'. The two can be used together, for example, 台風が来たように見える ('It looks as if a typhoon had come.').

related.title: とみえる
related.meaning: It seems that, It can be deduced, One can conclude that
related.comparison: にみえる describes appearance based on what's visually perceived, e.g. 暑そうにみえる ('It looks hot.'). とみえる is used for logical inference based on context, as in 暑いとみえる ('It must be hot', e.g. if everyone's taking off their coats). The latter is less about sight, and more about reasoning.

antonyms.title: にすぎない
antonyms.comparison: にほかならない and にすぎない both express exclusivity but with opposite evaluations. にほかならない emphasizes significance or essential identity, as in それは努力の結果にほかならない (It is nothing but the result of effort). にすぎない downplays importance, as in 僕は一学生にすぎない (I’m just a student).

related.title: よりほかない
related.meaning: To have no choice but, No other way than, Have to
related.comparison: よりほかない and にほかならない both emphasize exclusivity, but in different ways. よりほかない indicates there is no alternative action and is often used in practical or urgent contexts, as in 頼るよりほかない (I have no choice but to rely on them). にほかならない is assertive and logical, used to affirm a specific identity or conclusion, as in これは運命にほかならない (This is nothing other than fate).

related.title: しか～ない
related.meaning: There's only, Nothing but
related.comparison: しか～ない and にほかならない are similar in that both can be translated as 'nothing but' or 'only', but with a different nuance. しか～ない means 'nothing but' in the sense of 'nothing else exists', e.g. １００円しかない ('I only have 100 yen.'). In contrast, にほかならない means 'nothing but' in the sense of 'simply' or 'definitely', e.g. 彼は犯罪者に他ならない ('He's simply / nothing but a criminal.').

related.title: だらけ
related.meaning: Completely covered in, Full of
related.comparison: Both express a strong characterization, but differ in tone and focus. だらけ implies a negative excess of something undesirable, e.g. ミスだらけのレポート 'A report full of mistakes.' にほかならない expresses emphatic certainty or identity, e.g. 犯罪にほかならない 'It is nothing less than a crime.'

related.title: どんなに〜ても
related.meaning: No matter how
related.comparison: Both にも〜ない and どんなに〜ても deal with limitations, but in different ways. どんなに〜ても focuses on the resilience or constancy of an outcome despite extreme efforts or conditions, e.g. どんなに頑張っても無理だった ('No matter how hard I tried, it was impossible'), while にも〜ない expresses helplessness or inability despite intent, often paired with verbs of volition or emotion, e.g. 寝ようにも寝られない ('I can't sleep even if I try').

synonyms.title: にかかわらず
synonyms.meaning: Regardless of, Whether or, No matter, With or without
synonyms.comparison: にかかわらず means regardless of or whether or not, as in 年齢にかかわらず (Regardless of age). にもかかわらず means in spite of and highlights contradiction between expectation and reality, often with surprise, as in 雨にもかかわらず人が集まった (In spite of the rain, people gathered).

synonyms.title: ものの
synonyms.comparison: Both express concession but differ in tone. にもかかわらず is formal and often used in writing to highlight strong contrast or surprise, as in 雨にもかかわらず試合が行われた (The game was held despite the rain). ものの is less formal and means even though or but, as in 雨が降ったものの試合は行われた (It rained, but the game was held).

related.title: くせに
related.meaning: Even though, And yet, Despite
related.comparison: Both くせに and にもかかわらず convey contradiction between two facts, but with different nuance and tone. くせに is a casual expression with a blaming or critical tone, e.g. 知っているくせに何で教えてくれないの？ ('You know, so why won't you tell me!?'). In contrast, にもかかわらず is a more neutral and formal way to say 'in spite of (A)', e.g. 雨にもかかわらず出かけた ('We went out despite the rain.').

related.title: にかかわる
related.meaning: To relate to, To have to do with, Concerning, Depends on
related.comparison: These expressions share the verb かかわる but have opposite functions. にかかわる describes a relation or connection, often serious, as in 命にかかわる問題 (A matter of life and death). にもかかわらず is adverbial, expressing contrast or contradiction, as in 年齢にもかかわらず (Regardless of age).

related.title: を問わず
related.meaning: Regardless of, Whether or not, No matter, With or without
related.comparison: Both can mean regardless of, but are used differently. にもかかわらず expresses a surprising or unexpected result in spite of circumstances, as in 年齢にもかかわらず元気だ (In spite of his age, he is energetic). を問わず applies more broadly and neutrally, indicating that something holds true in all cases, as in 性別を問わず参加できる (Participation is open regardless of gender).

related.title: ながらも
related.meaning: But, Even while, Even though, Despite, Although
related.comparison: ながらも and にもかかわらず both express contradiction, but differ in nuance and usage. ながらも conveys coexisting contradictory states, e.g. '緊張しながらもわくわくしている' ('While feeling anxious, I'm also excited'). にもかかわらず means 'despite' and is used when something occurs against expectations, e.g. '雨にもかかわらず出かけた' ('He went out despite the rain').

synonyms.title: をもとに
synonyms.comparison: These phrases both mean 'based on' and can overlap, but differ slightly. にもとづいて is more formal and tends to show a concrete or logical foundation for an action or product. をもとに is less formal (but still used in formal contexts) and can indicate a general basis or inspiration where にもとづいて may not fit.

related.title: によると・によれば
related.meaning: According to
related.comparison: によると・によれば reports second-hand information, e.g. 新聞によると事件があったそうです ('According to the newspaper, there was an incident.') にもとづいて indicates that an action is grounded in something like rules or facts, e.g. 法律にもとづいて判断する ('Make a decision based on the law.')

related.title: からして
related.meaning: Even..., Judging from, Based on
related.comparison: While both patterns can be translated into English as 'based on', they have very different meanings. からして means 'based on (A), (B)', and expresses the logical basis for the speaker's judgment or opinion. For example, 匂いからして美味しそう = 'From the smell, it seems tasty.' In contrast, にもとづいて expresses the foundation or basis for another action. For example: 経験に基づいて判断する = 'Make a decision based on experience.'

related.title: に合わせて・に合った
related.meaning: In accordance with, Matching, Fitting, Suitable for, Adjust or tune to
related.comparison: Both express a general sense of basing an action on something, but their specific nuances differ. に合わせて・に合った conveys adapting or matching to something. For example: 季節に合わせて洋服を選ぶ = 'Choose clothes based on the season.' In contrast, にもとづいて typically expresses the grounding or foundation for some action. For example, 経験にもとづいて判断する = 'Make a decision based on experience.'

related.title: にしたがって
related.meaning: As, Following, As then, According to, In accordance with
related.comparison: Both are formal expressions meaning 'in accordance with (A)', but differ in usage. にもとづいて shows the foundation for an action, e.g. 法律にもとづいて判断する 'make a decision based on the law.' にしたがって can overlap, but often shows related change, e.g. 年を取るにしたがって目が悪くなっていく 'Eyesight deteriorates with age.' In this case, もとづいて would not be used.

related.title: を踏まえて
related.meaning: Based on, Considering, Keeping in mind, Taking into account, Therefore
related.comparison: Both have a general meaning of 'based on', but with slightly different nuances. にもとづいて typically shows strict reliance on a fixed source like rules or data, e.g. 法律にもとづいて判断する 'ake a decision based on the law'. を踏まえて means taking something into account as a reference or consideration, e.g. アンケートの結果を踏まえて調節する 'make adjustments taking the survey results into consideration'.

related.title: より
related.meaning: More than, Over
related.comparison: Both express comparison meaning ‘more than,’ and they can overlap in meaning, but the tone differs. にもまして is a more formal, literary variant often used in writing or speeches to emphasize that something exceeds another in degree, as in 昨年にもまして今年は暑い ('This year is even hotter than last year.'). より is the neutral, everyday comparative used broadly in speech and writing without added emphasis, as in 去年より今年の方が暑い ('This year is hotter than last year.').

related.title: に先駆けて
related.meaning: First, Prior to, Pioneer, Ahead of
related.comparison: These are similar in that both mean 'ahead of anything', but differ in that に先駆けて refers to being ahead of others in a temporal sense, while にもまして refers to ahead of others in quality, etc. compared to a certain standpoint.  For example 他の会社に先駆けて実現できた ('They were able to realize it before other companies.') vs. いつもにもまして元気だ ('He's even more energetic than usual.')

related.title: によると・によれば
related.meaning: According to
related.comparison: Both derive from よる ('to be based on'), but are used differently. によると・によれば means 'according to' and is used to report information, e.g. 天気予報によると雨が降るそうです ('According to the weather forecast, it will rain.') によって・による often means 'depending on' and has broader uses, including cause, method, or basis, e.g. 人によって考え方が違う ('Ways of thinking vary depending on the person.')

related.title: ために
related.meaning: Due to, Because of, As a result of
related.comparison: Both can express cause, but with different perspectives. ために often reflects the speaker's point of view, e.g. 試合は台風のために中止になった ('The game was canceled in light of the typhoon.') によって typically conveys an external or objective cause, e.g. 台風によって多くの家が壊れた ('Many houses were damaged due to the typhoon.')

related.title: 次第
related.meaning: As soon as
related.comparison: Both describe how one thing leads to another, but with different nuance. 次第 emphasizes immediacy, e.g. 方法が決まり次第連絡します ('I'll contact you as soon as the method is decided.') によって・による shows variation or dependence, e.g. 方法によって結果が変わる ('The result changes depending on the method.')

related.title: 〜かは〜によって違う
related.meaning: Depends on, Changes depending on, Differ depending on
related.comparison: Both express variation depending on conditions, and 〜かは〜によって違う includes によって as part of its structure. The key difference lies in grammatical structure: 好きか嫌いかは人によって違う ('Whether someone likes it depends on the person.') vs. 好き嫌いは人による ('Likes and dislikes depend on the person.')

related.title: でできる・からできる
related.meaning: Made of, Made from, Produced from, Come from
related.comparison: でできる・からできる expresses that something is made or composed of a certain ingredient, e.g. この家は木でできている ('This house is made from wood.'). In one usage, によって can be used with verbs like 作る or できる with a similar meaning, e.g., 木によって作られた家 'a house made from wood'. In other uses, によって can also mark the person or method by which something is made, while でできる・からできる cannot.

related.title: によって・による
related.meaning: By means of, According to, Depending on, Because of, Due to
related.comparison: Both derive from よる ('to be based on'), but are used differently. によると・によれば means 'according to' and is used to report information, e.g. 天気予報によると雨が降るそうです ('According to the weather forecast, it will rain.') によって・による often means 'depending on' and has broader uses, including cause, method, or basis, e.g. 人によって考え方が違う ('Ways of thinking vary depending on the person.')

related.title: をもとに
related.meaning: Based on, From, According to
related.comparison: Both involve a basis or source, but differ in usage. によると・によれば reports information, e.g. 天気予報によると雨が降るそうです ('According to the weather forecast, it will rain.') をもとに means doing something based on a source or foundation, e.g. 事実をもとに小説のストーリーを考えた ('I thought up a story for a novel based on true events.')

related.title: にもとづいて
related.meaning: Based on, In accordance with
related.comparison: によると・によれば reports second-hand information, e.g. 新聞によると事件があったそうです ('According to the newspaper, there was an incident.') にもとづいて indicates that an action is grounded in something like rules or facts, e.g. 法律にもとづいて判断する ('Make a decision based on the law.')

related.title: にしたがって
related.meaning: As, Following, As then, According to, In accordance with
related.comparison: Both can translate as 'according to', but differ in usage. にしたがって means 'in accordance with' or 'following' something, e.g. 説明書に従って組み立てた ('I assembled it according to the manual.') によると・によれば expresses reported information, e.g. 説明書によると操作は簡単です ('According to the manual, the operation is simple.')

related.title: にかけて
related.meaning: Over (a period), From ~ until, Through
related.comparison: にかけて and にわたって both describe range or extent, but differ in specificity. にかけて implies an approximate span in time or space, e.g. '九州から関西にかけて' ('from Kyushu through Kansai'). にわたって emphasizes coverage over a broad, often deliberate range, e.g. '三日間にわたって会議が開かれた' ('The conference was held over three days').

synonyms.title: にしたがって
synonyms.meaning: As, Following, As then, According to, In accordance with
synonyms.comparison: に伴って・に伴い describes changes in one thing due to another, in a proportional or cause-and-effect manner, as in 人口減少に伴って (along with the declining population). にしたがって can overlap in this usage, but it also extends to a broader sense of following rules or standards, as in 指示に従って (according to instructions).

synonyms.title: と共に
synonyms.meaning: Together with, At the same time,  As well as
synonyms.comparison: に伴って・に伴い and と共に both express things happening in connection with another event. と共に emphasizes simultaneity or shared experience, e.g. '成長と共に責任も増える' ('Responsibility grows along with maturity'). に伴って・に伴い emphasizes causality or consequential change, often in formal contexts, e.g. '経済の発展に伴って生活水準が向上した' ('As the economy developed, living standards improved').

related.title: に沿って
related.meaning: Along, In accordance with, In line with
related.comparison: に沿って and に伴って・に伴い both imply corresponding relationships, but with different nuances. に沿って means in line with in literal or figurative contexts, as in 点線に沿って (along the dotted line) or スケジュールに沿って (in line with the schedule). に伴って・に伴い suggests one change occurring along with another, as in 人口減少に伴って (along with the declining population).

related.title: きっかけ
related.meaning: Lead to, Because of, As a start or trigger of, An opportunity
