related.comparison: Both can describe one event influencing another, but with very different nuance. きっかけ refers to something that triggered or led to a change, as in 入院をきっかけにタバコをやめた (I gave up smoking on the occasion of my hospitalization). に伴って・に伴い is more formal, often expressing a broader causal relationship, as in 高齢化に伴い人口が激減した (The population rapidly declined along with the aging of the population).

related.title: を契機に
related.meaning: Lead to, As a good opportunity to, As a result of, As a trigger
related.comparison: Both are formal expressions describing one event influencing another, but differ slightly in usage. を契機に emphasizes a turning point or opportunity that led to change, as in 入院を契機にタバコをやめた (I gave up smoking on the occasion of my hospitalization). に伴って・に伴い often appears in news reports to describe cause-and-effect relationships.

related.title: と並んで
related.meaning: In line with, Comparable with, Along with, Just like, As … as
related.comparison: Both に伴って and と並んで connect two elements, but differ in meaning. に伴って expresses a causal or accompanying relationship where one thing changes in response to another, e.g. '経済の成長に伴って、生活水準も上がった' ('As the economy grew, the standard of living also rose'). と並んで indicates comparable qualities without implying cause, e.g. 'ドラえもんと並んで人気のアニメ' ('an anime as popular as Doraemon').

related.title: につれて
related.meaning: As…then, In proportion to, (Along) with
related.comparison: につれて and に伴って・に伴い both express one thing happening as a result of or alongside another. につれて emphasizes gradual and proportional change, e.g. '年を取るにつれて体力が落ちる' ('As I age, my stamina declines'). に伴って・に伴い can also be used with one-time or habitual events, e.g. '津波は地震に伴い発生する' ('Tsunamis occur together with earthquakes').

related.title: 代わりに
related.meaning: Instead of,  In place of, In exchange for
related.comparison: Both phrases use 代わる ('to replace') and express substitution or replacement, but differ in usage. 代わりに emphasizes doing something in place of another thing or action. For example, ご飯の代わりにパンを食べた ('I ate bread instead of rice'). に代わって is used when taking over someone's role or acting in their place, often in formal contexts. For example, 部長に代わってご挨拶します ('On behalf of the department head, I greet you').

related.title: には当たらない
related.meaning: No need to/for, Cannot, Not worth it to, Not suitable
related.comparison: Both expressions judge whether something deserves evaluation, but in opposite directions. には当たらない means 'does not warrant' or 'is not serious enough to justify' a reaction such as praise, criticism, or punishment, often used to tone things down, as in そのミスは責めるには当たらない ('That mistake is not worth blaming.'). に値する means 'is worthy of' and is used to positively judge that something deserves respect, praise, or consideration, as in 彼の努力は評価に値する ('His effort is worthy of being recognized.').

related.title: にもまして
related.meaning: Even -er than, Even more than, More than anything, More than anybody, More than ever
related.comparison: These are similar in that both mean 'ahead of anything', but differ in that に先駆けて refers to being ahead of others in a temporal sense, while にもまして refers to ahead of others in quality, etc. compared to a certain standpoint.  For example 他の会社に先駆けて実現できた ('They were able to realize it before other companies.') vs. いつもにもまして元気だ ('He's even more energetic than usual.')

synonyms.title: に沿って
synonyms.meaning: Along, In accordance with, In line with
synonyms.comparison: Both expressions mean in line with or in accordance with. に沿って can be literal or figurative, as in 点線に沿って (along the dotted line) or スケジュールに沿って (in line with the schedule). に則って・に則り is more formal, often implying compliance with official rules or principles, as in 法律に則って (in accordance with the law).

related.title: に即して
related.meaning: In line with, Based on, In accordance with
related.comparison: Both mean ‘based on/in accordance with,’ but the nuance differs slightly. に即して emphasizes adapting closely to actual conditions, facts, or the current situation, often suggesting flexibility or responsiveness, as in 現状に即して判断する ('Make a decision based on the current situation.'). に則って・に則り is more formal and rule-oriented, meaning ‘in accordance with’ established standards, rules, or principles, as in 規則に則って手続きを進める ('Proceed with the procedure in accordance with the rules.').

related.title: にしたがって
related.meaning: As, Following, As then, According to, In accordance with
related.comparison: Both に則って and にしたがって mean according to or in accordance with. に則って is more formal and often used in technical or legal contexts, as in 法律に則って (in accordance with the law). にしたがって has broader usage: besides according to, it can also express sequential or proportional relationships, as in 普及にしたがって (as it spread).

related.title: はもちろん
related.meaning: Not only but also, Let alone, Not to mention, Of course
related.comparison: はもちろん emphasizes the obviousness of the first item before adding another, e.g. 英語はもちろん中国語も話せる ('He speaks not only English but also Chinese'). に加えて simply adds one thing to another without that emphasis, e.g. 雨に加えて風も強い ('In addition to the rain, the wind is also strong').

related.title: をはじめ
related.meaning: Not only...but also..., Starting with
related.comparison: をはじめ introduces a prominent example from a group, e.g. 学生をはじめ多くの人が参加した ('Many people participated, starting with students'). に加えて simply adds another element to the first, e.g. 雨に加えて風も強い ('In addition to the rain, the wind is also strong').

related.title: ばかりでなく
related.meaning: Not only…but also, As well as
related.comparison: Both are formal patterns which add new information or elements, but they differ in nuance. ばかりでなく is a relatively straightforward 'Not only (A), but also (B)', e.g. 日本語ばかりでなく、韓国語も話せる 'He can speak not only Japanese, but also Korean.' In contrast, に加えて has a more specific nuance of 'In addition to (A)', e.g. 日本語に加えて、文学も勉強することになった 'In addition to Japanese, I'll also study literature.'

related.title: に則って・に則り
related.meaning: According to, In accordance with, Based on, In line with
related.comparison: Both mean ‘based on/in accordance with,’ but the nuance differs slightly. に即して emphasizes adapting closely to actual conditions, facts, or the current situation, often suggesting flexibility or responsiveness, as in 現状に即して判断する ('Make a decision based on the current situation.'). に則って・に則り is more formal and rule-oriented, meaning ‘in accordance with’ established standards, rules, or principles, as in 規則に則って手続きを進める ('Proceed with the procedure in accordance with the rules.').

related.title: 逆に
related.meaning: On the contrary
related.comparison: 逆に is colloquial, meaning on the contrary or contrary to expectations, often introducing a reversal, as in 応援したのに逆に怒られた (I cheered them on, but on the contrary, they got angry). に反して means contrary to or against, used with rules, intentions, or expectations, as in 規則に反して行動した (He acted against the rules). 逆に can stand alone, while に反して must follow something.

related.title: むしろ
related.meaning: Rather, Instead, Over
related.comparison: むしろ and に反して both express contrast, but differ in usage. むしろ emphasizes preference or clarification, e.g. 雨でもむしろ外に出たい ('Even in the rain, I'd rather go out'). に反して highlights contradiction or opposition, e.g. 期待に反して失敗した ('He failed, contrary to expectations').

related.title: 却って
related.meaning: All the more, On the contrary, Conversely
related.comparison: Both convey contradiction, but they differ in usage and nuance. 却って connects to the rest of the sentence to emphasize unexpected results, e.g. 慌てて出ようとしたら、却って遅くなった ('I tried to rush out, and I actually ended up running later.'). In contrast, に反して connects to a noun and highlights going against norms or expectations, e.g. 親の期待に反して、大学に進学しなかった ('Against his parents' expectations, he didn't go to college.').

related.title: にしてみれば
related.meaning: For (A), In the case of (A), From (A)'s point of view, In the eyes of (A)
related.comparison: にしてみれば and にとって are similar in that both express that something is true from the perspective of a certain person or group. にしてみれば is more hypothetical, e.g. 初心者にしてみればなかなか難しい implies that one might expect something to be simple, but it's difficult from the perspective of a beginner. にとって is a more neutral 'to’ or 'from the perspective of', e.g. 私にとって宝物だ ('To me, it's a prized possession.').

related.title: ばあいは
related.meaning: In the event of, In the case of
related.comparison: ばあいは (often written in kanji as 場合は) and に取って (often written in kana as にとって) can overlap in some cases, but not all. For example, 私の場合は and 私にとって can both mean 'as far as I'm concerned' or 'in my case'. However, since にとって simply means 'concerning', it can't replace ばあいは for the meaning of 'in the event that', e.g. 行く場合は気をつけたほうがいい ('In the event that you go, you should be careful.').

related.title: について
related.meaning: Concerning, About, Regarding
related.comparison: These two phrases appear similar but are used quite differently. について means 'about (A)' or 'concerning (A)' and indicates the topic of a speech, discussion, etc., e.g. ゲームについて話す ('Speak about games'), while に取って (often written in hiragana as にとって) means 'to/for (A)' or 'as far as (A) is concerned' and is typically used to express perspective or point of view, e.g. 私にとって大切な人 ('An important person to me').

related.title: にしては
related.meaning: (Even) considering, (Even) for
related.comparison: に取って (often written in kana as にとって) and にしては both involve perspective, but にとって expresses personal relevance, e.g. 子供にとっては大問題だ ('It's a big issue for a child'), while にしては shows that a certain quality or characteristic defies expectations, e.g. 子供にしてはおとなしい ('He's quiet for a child').

related.title: において・における
related.meaning: In, On, At, Regarding, In terms of
related.comparison: に取って (usually written in kana as にとって) expresses viewpoint or concern, e.g. 私にとって大切な人だ ('She's an important person to me'). において・における is a formal equivalent of で, conveying setting, context, or means, e.g. 歴史において重要な出来事だ ('It's an important event in history'). Unlike にとって, the latter can directly modify nouns in the における form, e.g. 教育における問題 ('Problems in education').

related.title: にしたら
related.meaning: From the point of view of
related.comparison: に取って (usually written in kana as にとって) indicates a perspective or viewpoint, e.g. 社会にとって必要な制度だ ('It's a necessary system for society'). にしたら also means 'for (A)', but often implies a more subjective or emotional stance, e.g. 親にしたら心配だろう ('For a parent, it must be worrying').

related.title: には
related.meaning: In order to, In regard to, For
related.comparison: に取って (usually written in kana as にとって) indicates a perspective or viewpoint, e.g. 私にとって大切なことだ ('It's something important to me'). には can be used this way too, e.g. 私には分からない ('It's not understandable to me'), but also has broader uses like indicating purpose, target, or scope, e.g. 成功には努力が必要だ ('To succeed, effort is necessary').

related.title: としては
related.meaning: As a, For a, As for
related.comparison: に取って (usually written in kana as にとって) marks relevance or effect from a certain standpoint, e.g. 学生にとって分かりにくい説明だ ('It's a hard-to-understand explanation for students'). としては expresses viewpoint based on role or identity, e.g. 留学生としては反対だ ('As an exchange student, I disagree').

related.title: にかけては
related.meaning: When it comes to, Regarding, Concerning
related.comparison: に取って (usually written in kana as にとって) shows viewpoint or relevance, often translated as 'to/for (A)' or 'as far as (A) is concerned', e.g. 子供にとって有害だ ('It's harmful to children'). にかけては highlights someone's strength or skill, e.g. 歌にかけては彼が一番うまい ('When it comes to singing, he's the best').

related.title: にもとづいて
related.meaning: Based on, In accordance with
related.comparison: Both express a general sense of basing an action on something, but their specific nuances differ. に合わせて・に合った conveys adapting or matching to something. For example: 季節に合わせて洋服を選ぶ = 'Choose clothes based on the season.' In contrast, にもとづいて typically expresses the grounding or foundation for some action. For example, 経験にもとづいて判断する = 'Make a decision based on experience.'

related.title: にしたがって
related.meaning: As, Following, As then, According to, In accordance with
related.comparison: Both relate to the idea of being in accordance with something else, but with different nuances and usage. に合わせて・に合った follows a noun and implies adjustment to suit a person or situation, e.g. 相手に合わせて話し方を変える ('Adjust your way of speaking to the other person'), while にしたがって shows something happening in response or proportion to another change, e.g. 年を取るにしたがって体力が落ちる ('As you age, your stamina declines').

related.title: 向け
related.meaning: Intended for, Aimed at
related.comparison: Both relate to the idea of being suited to something else, but with different nuances. に合わせて・に合った implies active adjustment to suit a person or situation, e.g. 季節に合わせて洋服を選ぶ ('Choose clothes to match the season'), while 向け describes being made for or targeted toward a certain group, e.g. 子供向けのアニメ ('A cartoon for children').

related.title: に応じて
related.meaning: Depending or based on, According to, In response to, Suitable for or to
related.comparison: Both relate to the idea of one thing being dependent on another, but with different nuance and formality level. に応じて is a formal expression and conveys variation or response depending on conditions, e.g. 必要に応じてサポートする ('Support as needed'), while に合わせて・に合った is less formal and implies actively adjusting to match or suit something, e.g. 季節に合わせて洋服を選ぶ ('Choose clothes to match the season').

related.title: 向き
related.meaning: Suitable for, Facing
related.comparison: Both relate to the idea of being suited to something else, but with different nuances. に合わせて・に合った implies active adjustment to suit a person or situation, e.g. 季節に合わせて洋服を選ぶ ('Choose clothes to match the season'), while 向き describes suitability or appropriateness by nature, e.g. 初心者向きの本 ('A book suitable for beginners').

related.title: へ
related.meaning: To, Toward
related.comparison: The particle へ indicates direction or destination, similar to 'to' or 'toward' in English. に向かって and に向けて emphasize direction rather than destination, unlike へ, which can also imply the final goal. For example, 学校へ行く means 'go to school,' while 学校に向かって走る means 'run toward the school.'

related.title: 向き
related.meaning: Suitable for, Facing
related.comparison: These expressions all involve direction or suitability, but differ in usage. に向かって・に向けて describe motion or intent toward a goal or target, e.g. 'ゴールに向かって走る' ('run toward the goal'). 向き attaches directly to a noun and refers to the appropriateness or orientation of something for a group, without necessarily implying targeted action, e.g. '初心者向きのコース' ('a course suited for beginners').

related.title: 向け
related.meaning: Intended for, Aimed at
related.comparison: These expressions all involve direction or targeting, but their usage differs. に向かって・に向けて describe motion or intent toward a goal or target, e.g. 'ゴールに向かって走る' ('run toward the goal'). 向け attaches directly to a noun to describe something targeted at a particular group, e.g. '子ども向けのアニメ' ('an anime for children').

related.title: てはいられない
related.meaning: Can't afford to, Unable to
related.comparison: Both express that something cannot be endured, but the reason is different. に堪えない is used to judge that an experience is unbearable in quality or content, often in an evaluative or descriptive sense, as in 見るに堪えなかった ('It was too bad to watch.'). てはいられない expresses urgency or pressure that makes continuing the current state impossible, usually because of time limits, duties, or circumstances that require action, as in レポートがあるから遊んではいられない ('I have a report, so I can’t afford to hang out.').

related.title: について
related.meaning: Concerning, About, Regarding
related.comparison: Both に対して and について express a relationship between (A) and (B), but differ in nuance and usage. について means 'about (A)' and marks the topic of a statement, e.g. 日本について学ぶ ('Learn about Japan'), while に対して means 'toward (A)' and expresses direction or stance, e.g. 親に対する気持ち ('Feelings toward parents') or 優しい人に対して ('Toward kind people').

related.title: において・における
related.meaning: In, On, At, Regarding, In terms of
related.comparison: Both patterns connect to nouns to show a relationship, but differ in nuance. に対して (which also appears before nouns as に対する) implies a directional or oppositional relationship, like 'toward (A)', e.g. 彼に対する気持ち ('feelings toward him'). In contrast, において・における is a more formal/literary equivalent of で, indicating a context, place, or setting, e.g. 現代社会における宗教 ('religion in modern society').

related.title: 反面
related.meaning: While, On the other hand, Although, However, On the flip side
related.comparison: Both express contrast, but their nuance differs. に対して typically compares two things with opposing qualities, e.g. 田中先生が厳しいのに対して、鈴木先生は優しい ('Whereas Prof. Tanaka is strict, Prof. Suzuki is lenient'). 反面 focuses on two sides of the same situation, e.g. 都会の生活は便利な反面、お金がかかる ('Life in the big city is convenient, but expensive'). に対して also has a 'toward (A)' usage, which 反面 does not.

related.title: にひきかえ
related.meaning: In stark contrast, In comparison, On the other hand, While, In sharp contrast
related.comparison: Both にひきかえ and に対して set things side by side, but they differ in purpose and tone. にひきかえ is used specifically to make a contrast between two situations or people, often highlighting a difference in evaluation or outcome, as in 父が厳しすぎるのに対して、母は優しすぎる (‘My father is too strict, whereas my mother is too kind.’). に対して can be used similarly, but is also a broad and neutral expression meaning “toward,” “regarding,” or “in response to,” and is used for attitudes, actions, or opinions directed at a target without implying comparison or evaluation, as in 彼はみんなに対して優しい人だ (‘He is kind toward everyone.’).

related.title: に関する・に関して
related.meaning: Related to, About, Regarding
related.comparison: Both patterns show a relationship, but differ in nuance and usage. に関する・に関して attaches to a noun to mean 'about (A)' or 'concerning (A)', e.g. 歴史に関する本 ('a book about history'). In contrast, に対して (or に対する before nouns) implies a directional or oppositional relationship like 'toward (A)', e.g. 彼に対する気持ち ('feelings toward him').

related.title: にかけては
related.meaning: When it comes to, Regarding, Concerning
related.comparison: While both can be used for comparison, their nuance differs. に対して typically contrasts two things in terms of opposing qualities, e.g. 田中先生が厳しいのに対して、鈴木先生は優しい ('Whereas Prof. Tanaka is strict, Prof. Suzuki is lenient'). In contrast, にかけては highlights strength or excellence in a specific area, e.g. 料理にかけては彼が一番だ ('When it comes to cooking, he's the best').

related.title: 当たり
related.meaning: Per~, A~, Apiece, Each
related.comparison: Both phrases derive from 当たる, but are used in very different ways. に当たる means 'corresponds to' and often functions as a more formal, indirect way of saying 'is', e.g. '父親に当たる人' ('a person who is [lit. corresponds to] their father'). 当たり is used after number + counter expressions to mean 'per' or 'apiece', e.g. '１個当たり１００円' ('100 yen apiece').

related.title: にあたり・にあたって
related.meaning: When, When doing something, On the occasion, At the time
related.comparison: Both derive from 当たる, but are used in different ways. に当たる means 'corresponds to' and functions as an indirect way of saying 'is', e.g. '父親に当たる人' ('a person who is [lit. corresponds to] their father'). にあたり・にあたって follows nouns or verb phrases and means 'when' or 'on the occasion of', e.g. '就職に当たり' ('on the occasion of getting a job'). Note that に当たる can also appear as に当たり in formal conjunctive form.

synonyms.title: に応えて
synonyms.comparison: に応えて means in response to expectations, requests, or demands, as in 要望に応えて商品を改良した (We improved the product in response to requests). に応じて means in line with or according to conditions, adapting as needed, as in 年齢に応じて料金が変わる (The fee changes according to age).

related.title: に沿って
related.meaning: Along, In accordance with, In line with
related.comparison: に沿って and に応じて can both suggest doing something in line with a standard, but differ in nuance. に沿って means according to, as in 会社の方針に沿って行動する (act according to company policy). に応じて implies acting in response to or suitable for something, as in 客のニーズに応じてサービスを提供する (provide a service suitable for customers’ needs).

related.title: にしたがって
related.meaning: As, Following, As then, According to, In accordance with
related.comparison: に応じて and にしたがって can seem similar, but differ in nuance. に応じて means in response to something, as in アンケートの結果に応じて方針を決める (decide policy in response to the survey results). にしたがって refers to directly following guidelines or standards, as in 規則に従って (in accordance with the regulations).

related.title: 次第だ・次第で
related.meaning: Depending on, So
related.comparison: Both can be translated as according to, but differ in nuance. に応じて emphasizes adapting or responding flexibly, as in 年齢に応じて料金が変わる (The fee changes according to age). 次第だ・次第で stresses that the outcome is directly determined by another factor, as in 成功するかどうかは準備次第である (Success depends on preparation).

related.title: をもとに
related.meaning: Based on, From, According to
related.comparison: をもとに indicates a fixed source or basis for something, as in 実話をもとに小説を書く (Write a novel based on true events). に応じて shows flexible adjustment or response to conditions, as in 状況に応じて対応を決める (Decide the response according to the situation).

related.title: からして
related.meaning: Even..., Judging from, Based on
related.comparison: に応じて shows flexible adjustment in proportion to conditions, as in 状況に応じて対応する (Respond according to the situation). からして emphasizes the basis for judgment, meaning judging from or even just from, often with emphasis, as in 態度からして気に入っていない (Judging from his attitude, he doesn’t like it).

related.title: に合わせて・に合った
related.meaning: In accordance with, Matching, Fitting, Suitable for, Adjust or tune to
related.comparison: Both relate to the idea of one thing being dependent on another, but with different nuance and formality level. に応じて is a formal expression and conveys variation or response depending on conditions, e.g. 必要に応じてサポートする ('Support as needed'), while に合わせて・に合った is less formal and implies actively adjusting to match or suit something, e.g. 季節に合わせて洋服を選ぶ ('Choose clothes to match the season').

synonyms.title: に応じて
synonyms.meaning: Depending or based on, According to, In response to, Suitable for or to
synonyms.comparison: に応えて means in response to expectations, requests, or demands, as in 要望に応えて商品を改良した (We improved the product in response to requests). に応じて means in line with or according to conditions, adapting as needed, as in 年齢に応じて料金が変わる (The fee changes according to age).

related.title: に足る
related.meaning: To be worthy of, To be enough, To be sufficient
related.comparison: Both judge whether something meets a standard, but the nuance differs. に恥じない means ‘not shameful'/'living up to’ a name, role, or expectation, focusing on dignity or appropriateness, as in 名に恥じない仕事をした ('He did work worthy of the name.'). に足る means ‘worthy of’ in a more objective sense, judging whether something sufficiently merits trust, evaluation, or action, as in 信頼に足る人物だ ('He is a person worthy of trust.').

related.title: 割に
related.meaning: Although, Despite, Rather...for, Unexpectedly, Comparatively, Considering
related.comparison: Both are used in making comparisons, but に比べて is used to directly compare and contrast two things, e.g. このスマホはあのスマホに比べて安い ('This smartphone is cheaper than that one'), while 割に expresses a gap between expectation and reality, e.g. 値段の割に美味しい ('It's tasty considering the price'). The former makes a neutral comparison; the latter evaluates something against an assumed standard.

related.title: にしては
related.meaning: (Even) considering, (Even) for
related.comparison: Both are used in making comparisons, but にしては expresses when something is unexpectedly different from what one would assume based on a certain condition, e.g. 子供にしては大人しい ('He is quiet for a child'), while に比べて simply compares or contrasts, e.g. この問題は前のに比べて簡単だ ('This problem is easier than the previous one'). にしては implies something contrary to expectations, while に比べて does not.

synonyms.title: にしたがって
synonyms.meaning: As, Following, As then, According to, In accordance with
synonyms.comparison: When used to mean in line with or in accordance with, にしたがって and に沿って are similar and often interchangeable, as in 規則（にしたがって／に沿って）行動する (act according to the regulations). However, に沿って can also mean along(side), as in 川に沿って歩く (walk alongside the river), while にしたがって cannot.

synonyms.title: に則って・に則り
synonyms.meaning: According to, In accordance with, Based on, In line with
synonyms.comparison: Both expressions mean in line with or in accordance with. に沿って can be literal or figurative, as in 点線に沿って (along the dotted line) or スケジュールに沿って (in line with the schedule). に則って・に則り is more formal, often implying compliance with official rules or principles, as in 法律に則って (in accordance with the law).

related.title: に応じて
related.meaning: Depending or based on, According to, In response to, Suitable for or to
related.comparison: に沿って and に応じて can both suggest doing something in line with a standard, but differ in nuance. に沿って means according to, as in 会社の方針に沿って行動する (act according to company policy). に応じて implies acting in response to or suitable for something, as in 客のニーズに応じてサービスを提供する (provide a service suitable for customers’ needs).

related.title: に伴って・に伴い
related.meaning: As, Due,  Along with, Hand-in-hand with, Resulted in, Be consequent upon
related.comparison: に沿って and に伴って・に伴い both imply corresponding relationships, but with different nuances. に沿って means in line with in literal or figurative contexts, as in 点線に沿って (along the dotted line) or スケジュールに沿って (in line with the schedule). に伴って・に伴い suggests one change occurring along with another, as in 人口減少に伴って (along with the declining population).

related.title: れる・られる + ままに
related.meaning: Do as (one is told or ordered…), In accordance with, In compliance with, Just like
related.comparison: Both convey doing something in accordance with something else, but differ in nuance. れる・られる + ままに implies doing something exactly as told or forced, with little choice, as in 指示されるがままにやった (I did it exactly as instructed). に沿って simply means in line with a tangible or figurative standard, as in 点線に沿って (along the dotted line) or スケジュールに沿って (in line with the schedule), without implying lack of control.

related.title: と並んで
related.meaning: In line with, Comparable with, Along with, Just like, As … as
related.comparison: While both と並んで and に沿って indicate some kind of coordination, their core meanings differ. と並んで highlights equivalence or joint status, e.g. 'ドラえもんと並んで人気のアニメ' ('an anime as popular as Doraemon'). に沿って means 'in line with' or 'according to', emphasizing adherence to a path, rule, or guideline, e.g. '規則に沿って行動する' ('act in accordance with the rules').

related.title: 確かに
related.meaning: Surely, Certainly
related.comparison: 確かに and に決まっている both express a strong degree of certainty, but differ in usage and nuance. 確かに is often used to concede or acknowledge something as true, and can at times be followed by a contrast, as in 確かに高いが、品質もいい (It’s certainly expensive, but the quality is good). に決まっている conveys confident personal judgment or obviousness, as in 彼がやったに決まっている (He definitely did it).3

related.title: にする
related.meaning: To decide on, To choose, To make something into, To view something as
related.comparison: Both にする and に決まっている relate to decisions but differ in use. にする expresses making a choice, often in daily contexts like picking what to eat, similar to 'I'll go with X.' に決まっている, despite using 決まる, often just shows strong belief, meaning 'It's surely X' or 'It's bound to be X,' rather than an actual decision.

related.title: に違いない
related.meaning: There is no doubt that, I'm sure
related.comparison: に違いない and に決まっている both express strong conviction, but differ in tone and basis of judgment. に違いない implies a strong conclusion drawn from evidence or context, e.g. '何かあったに違いない' ('Something must have happened'). に決まっている conveys more assertive confidence, often based on common sense or the obvious, e.g. '美味しいに決まっている' ('Of course it tastes good').

related.title: ことになる
related.meaning: It will end up being, It is the result of, It has been decided that
related.comparison: These patterns are related in that both center around the idea of something being decided. ことになる expresses that something has been decided or will happen, often beyond the speaker's control, e.g. 転勤することになった ('It's been decided that I'll be transferred'), while に決まっている expresses that something is decisively true, e.g. 勝つに決まっている ('They'll definitely win').

related.title: に至っては
related.meaning: When it comes to, As for, In particular, What is worse or more
related.comparison: Both relate to reaching a point, but the function is different. に至って・に至り marks the point at which a result, realization, or change finally occurs after preceding circumstances, often corresponding to ‘not until’ or ‘only when,’ as in 先輩に注意されるに至って、自分の間違いに気づいた (‘I didn’t realize my mistake until I was warned by a senior.’). に至っては is used to single out an extreme or noteworthy example within a broader situation, adding emphasis similar to ‘what’s more’ or ‘even,’ as in 母に至ってはインターネットさえ使えない (‘What’s more, my mother can’t even use the internet.’).

synonyms.title: に違いない
synonyms.meaning: There is no doubt that, I'm sure
synonyms.comparison: Both に相違ない and に違いない express a high degree of certainty, often translated as 'no doubt' or 'surely', but differ in tone and formality. に相違ない is more formal and used in writing or official contexts, e.g. '彼が犯人に相違ない' ('There is no doubt he is the culprit'). に違いない is more common in conversation and conveys strong personal conviction, e.g. 'あの人は日本人に違いない' ('That person must be Japanese').

related.title: に至って・に至り
related.meaning: When it came to, Only when, Only after, Go as far as
related.comparison: Both relate to reaching a point, but the function is different. に至って・に至り marks the point at which a result, realization, or change finally occurs after preceding circumstances, often corresponding to ‘not until’ or ‘only when,’ as in 先輩に注意されるに至って、自分の間違いに気づいた (‘I didn’t realize my mistake until I was warned by a senior.’). に至っては is used to single out an extreme or noteworthy example within a broader situation, adding emphasis similar to ‘what’s more’ or ‘even,’ as in 母に至ってはインターネットさえ使えない (‘What’s more, my mother can’t even use the internet.’).

related.title: に至っても
related.meaning: Even when
related.comparison: Both refer to an extreme point or example, but with opposite nuance. に至っても means ‘even after reaching (that point),’ emphasizing that a situation or attitude does not change despite time, experience, or progress, as in 注意されるに至っても態度は変わらなかった ('Even after being warned, his attitude did not change.'). に至っては singles out a specific case or stage as especially noteworthy or extreme within a broader situation, often with emphasis like ‘when it comes to’ or ‘even,’ as in 母に至ってはインターネットさえ使えない ('When it comes to my mother, she can’t even use the internet.').

related.title: に限って
related.meaning: Particularly when, Those who, Only when
related.comparison: Both expressions can convey the idea of 'particularly', but in different ways. に至っては presents an extreme example of something previously introduced. For example, when talking about a region with a lot of snowfall, A市に至っては60センチも積もることがある ('In particular, city A sometimes has accumulation of 60 cm.'). In contrast, に限って is often used when some unexpected/undesired action happens 'particularly' or 'only' at certain times, e.g. 忙しい時に限って誘ってくる ('He invites me particularly/only when I'm busy.').

related.title: に至っては
related.meaning: When it comes to, As for, In particular, What is worse or more
related.comparison: Both refer to an extreme point or example, but with opposite nuance. に至っても means ‘even after reaching (that point),’ emphasizing that a situation or attitude does not change despite time, experience, or progress, as in 注意されるに至っても態度は変わらなかった ('Even after being warned, his attitude did not change.'). に至っては singles out a specific case or stage as especially noteworthy or extreme within a broader situation, often with emphasis like ‘when it comes to’ or ‘even,’ as in 母に至ってはインターネットさえ使えない ('When it comes to my mother, she can’t even use the internet.').

related.title: からすると・からすれば
related.slug: からすると-からすれば
related.meaning: Judging from, Considering
related.comparison: Both express a viewpoint, but the source and nuance differ. に言わせれば・に言わせると・に言わせたら explicitly attributes an opinion to a specific person and often carries a subjective or critical tone, emphasizing that this is that person’s way of seeing things, as in 専門家に言わせればその計画は甘い ('If you ask an expert, that plan is naive.'). からすると・からすれば presents a perspective based on position, role, or circumstances and sounds more neutral or analytical, focusing on standpoint rather than personal opinion, as in 学生の立場からすると高すぎる ('From a student’s point of view, it’s too expensive.').

related.title: に限る
related.meaning: Nothing better than, There's nothing like, Limited to
related.comparison: Both に限る and に越したことはない express preference, but differ in certainty and nuance. に限る asserts that something is clearly the best option, e.g. '本は紙に限る' ('Books are best on paper'). に越したことはない is softer and suggests a desirable but not essential option, e.g. '安いに越したことはない' ('Inexpensive would be best').

related.title: にかけては
related.meaning: When it comes to, Regarding, Concerning
related.comparison: These two patterns can be relatively interchangeable when used in the pattern AからBに至るまで 'from A to B' or AからBにかけては (same meaning). However, にかけては has a separate usage, e.g. スポーツにかけては彼が一番強い ('When it comes to basketball, he's the best’) where it cannot be substituted with に至るまで.

related.title: に足る
related.meaning: To be worthy of, To be enough, To be sufficient
related.comparison: Both judge whether something meets a required standard, but the evaluation is opposite. に足りない means ‘not sufficient to’ and is used to say that something does not reach the level needed for judgment, trust, or action, as in 証拠としては不十分で、判断に足りない ('The evidence is insufficient and not enough to make a judgment.'). に足る means ‘sufficient to’ or ‘worthy of,’ and is used to state that something does meet the required standard, as in 信頼に足る人物だ ('He is a person worthy of trust.').

related.title: には当たらない
related.meaning: No need to/for, Cannot, Not worth it to, Not suitable
related.comparison: Both assess worthiness, but with opposite conclusions and different tone. には当たらない is used to deny that an action or event reaches the level where judgment or response is needed, as in 批判するには当たらない ('It is not something worth criticizing.'). に足る means 'sufficient to deserve' and is a formal expression used to state that something meets the required standard for trust, evaluation, or action, as in 信頼に足る人物だ ('He is a person worthy of trust.').

related.title: に足りない
related.meaning: To not be worthy, To not be enough, To not be sufficient, To lack
related.comparison: Both judge whether something meets a required standard, but the evaluation is opposite. に足りない means ‘not sufficient to’ and is used to say that something does not reach the level needed for judgment, trust, or action, as in 証拠としては不十分で、判断に足りない ('The evidence is insufficient and not enough to make a judgment.'). に足る means ‘sufficient to’ or ‘worthy of,’ and is used to state that something does meet the required standard, as in 信頼に足る人物だ ('He is a person worthy of trust.').

related.title: に恥じない
related.meaning: Lives up to, Truly to …’s name, Worthy of, Not dishonor, Not putting to shame, Measure to
related.comparison: Both judge whether something meets a standard, but the nuance differs. に恥じない means ‘not shameful'/'living up to’ a name, role, or expectation, focusing on dignity or appropriateness, as in 名に恥じない仕事をした ('He did work worthy of the name.'). に足る means ‘worthy of’ in a more objective sense, judging whether something sufficiently merits trust, evaluation, or action, as in 信頼に足る人物だ ('He is a person worthy of trust.').

related.title: たる
related.meaning: To be (in a position of), As a, For
related.comparison: While these two grammar points appear similar in form, they differ in origin and are used quite differently. たる primarily follows nouns and most often occurs in patterns such as リーダーたる者 'as a leader,' to express qualities or characteristics befitting someone in a certain role. In contrast に足る, can follow nouns or verbs to express the idea of being sufficient or worthy of something, e.g. 信頼に足る友人 'a trustworthy friend' or 語るに足りる発明 'an invention worthy of discussion'.

related.title: について
related.meaning: Concerning, About, Regarding
related.comparison: Both patterns mean 'about (A)' or 'concerning (A)' and show a relationship between (A) and the following phrase, but with some differences. について is neutral in formality and attaches to verb phrases, e.g. 歴史について学ぶ ('Learn about history'), while に関する・に関して is somewhat more formal and can modify verbs (in に関して form) or nouns directly, e.g. 歴史に関する本 ('A book about history'), which について cannot do.

related.title: 関係がある
related.meaning: (Not) involved with, (Not) concerned with, (No) connection with, (No) relationship with, (Not) related to
related.comparison: Both express the idea of connection, but differ in usage and nuance. に関する・に関して attaches to nouns to mean 'about (A)' or 'regarding (A)', e.g. 歴史に関する本 ('a book about history') or 歴史に関して勉強する ('study about history'). 関係がある conveys a looser connection, meaning 'is related to (A)', and unlike に関する・に関して, can be used with と as well as に, in sentence-final position, or with ない to express a lack of connection.

related.title: にかかわる
related.meaning: To relate to, To have to do with, Concerning, Depends on
related.comparison: Both follow nouns to express the idea of being related to or having to do with something, but they differ in nuance and structure. に関する・に関して attaches to a noun to mean 'about (A)' or 'concerning (A)', e.g. 歴史に関する本 ('a book about history'). にかかわる also connects to nouns but often implies a more serious or impactful relationship, e.g. 命にかかわる問題 ('a life-threatening issue').

related.title: にかけては
related.meaning: When it comes to, Regarding, Concerning
related.comparison: Both show a relationship, but differ in nuance. に関する・に関して means 'about/concerning (A)' and attaches to nouns, e.g. 歴史に関する本 ('a book about history') or 歴史に関して書く ('write about history'). In contrast, にかけては means 'when it comes to (A)' and highlights strength or excellence in an area, e.g. 料理にかけては彼が一番だ ('When it comes to cooking, he's the best').

related.title: において・における
related.meaning: In, On, At, Regarding, In terms of
related.comparison: These patterns are similar in that both follow nouns, then attach to either another noun (に関する and における) or the rest of the sentence (に関して or において), but differ in meaning. に関する・に関して means 'about (A)' or 'concerning (A)', while において・における can be considered a more formal/literary equivalent of で indicating a context, place, or setting.

related.title: を巡って
related.meaning: Concerning, In regard to, About, (Split) over
related.comparison: These phrases are similar in that both can be translated as 'about (A)' or 'concerning (A)', but they differ in nuance. に関する・に関して is a more neutral and general-purpose expression that can be used to express 'about (A)' in a wide variety of contexts, while を巡って is more formal and often implies differing opinions or viewpoints surrounding a topic, and is therefore used more narrowly.

related.title: に対して
related.meaning: In contrast to, While, In regard to, Whereas
related.comparison: Both patterns show a relationship, but differ in nuance and usage. に関する・に関して attaches to a noun to mean 'about (A)' or 'concerning (A)', e.g. 歴史に関する本 ('a book about history'). In contrast, に対して (or に対する before nouns) implies a directional or oppositional relationship like 'toward (A)', e.g. 彼に対する気持ち ('feelings toward him').

synonyms.title: に相違ない
synonyms.meaning: Without a doubt, Certainly, Surely, Not mistaken
synonyms.comparison: Both に相違ない and に違いない express a high degree of certainty, often translated as 'no doubt' or 'surely', but differ in tone and formality. に相違ない is more formal and used in writing or official contexts, e.g. '彼が犯人に相違ない' ('There is no doubt he is the culprit'). に違いない is more common in conversation and conveys strong personal conviction, e.g. 'あの人は日本人に違いない' ('That person must be Japanese').

related.title: 確かに
related.meaning: Surely, Certainly
related.comparison: 確かに and に違いない both signal confidence in a statement, but with different structures and emphasis. 確かに is an adverb used to affirm a fact, sometimes paired with a following contrast, as in 確かにそうだ (That’s certainly true). に違いない is a grammatical pattern attached to phrases to express logical certainty or strong inference, as in 彼は犯人に違いない (He must be the culprit).3

related.title: ～というのは事実だ
related.meaning: It is a fact that ~, There is no doubt that
related.comparison: Both ～というのは事実だ and に違いない make confident statements, but differ in implied certainty. ～というのは事実だ affirms something as a fact, e.g. 負けたというのは事実だ ('It's a fact that we lost'). に違いない expresses strong conviction, but not absolute certainty, e.g. 彼は疲れているに違いない ('He must be tired').

related.title: に決まっている
related.meaning: Surely, Bound to, Certainly, Be decided
related.comparison: に違いない and に決まっている both express strong conviction, but differ in tone and basis of judgment. に違いない implies a strong conclusion drawn from evidence or context, e.g. '何かあったに違いない' ('Something must have happened'). に決まっている conveys more assertive confidence, often based on common sense or the obvious, e.g. '美味しいに決まっている' ('Of course it tastes good').

related.title: とは限らない
related.meaning: Not necessarily so, Is not always true, There is no guarantee that
related.comparison: Both express that something is not exclusive, but with a different nuance. に限ったことではない means ‘not limited to this case’ and is used to say that a problem or tendency applies more broadly than the topic at hand, often generalizing from an example, as in 若者に限ったことではない ('It’s not something limited to young people.'). とは限らない means ‘not necessarily’ and is used to deny certainty about an assumption or conclusion itself, as in 成功するとは限らない ('It won’t necessarily succeed.').

related.title: に至っては
related.meaning: When it comes to, As for, In particular, What is worse or more
related.comparison: Both expressions can convey the idea of 'particularly', but in different ways. に至っては presents an extreme example of something previously introduced. For example, when talking about a region with a lot of snowfall, A市に至っては60センチも積もることがある ('In particular, city A sometimes has accumulation of 60 cm.'). In contrast, に限って is often used when some unexpected/undesired action happens 'particularly' or 'only' at certain times, e.g. 忙しい時に限って誘ってくる ('He invites me particularly/only when I'm busy.').

synonyms.title: のみならず
synonyms.meaning: Not only...but, Not just...but, What is more
synonyms.comparison: Both mean not only…but also, but differ in register and nuance. に限らず stresses inclusiveness, as in 日本に限らず世界中で使われている (Used not only in Japan but worldwide). のみならず is more formal and often adds additional, sometimes surprising, information, as in 彼は日本語のみならず英語も話せる (He speaks not only Japanese but also English).

related.title: はもとより
related.meaning: Not only... but also, Let alone, Of course
related.comparison: Both に限らず and はもとより express inclusion beyond an initial example but differ in nuance. に限らず highlights that the mentioned case is not the only one, implying a broader range or scope (not only…but also). はもとより, on the other hand, emphasizes that the stated example is obvious or expected, and contrasts it with less obvious or more significant cases, carrying a nuance closer to 'needless to say…even more so'.

related.title: はもちろん
related.meaning: Not only but also, Let alone, Not to mention, Of course
related.comparison: はもちろん emphasizes something obvious before adding another case, e.g. 日本人はもちろん留学生も来た ('Not only Japanese people but also exchange students came'). に限らず expands the scope of a statement, implying 'not limited to (A), also (B)', e.g. 子供に限らず大人も楽しめる ('Not only children, but also adults can enjoy it').

related.title: ばかりでなく
related.meaning: Not only…but also, As well as
related.comparison: Both are fairly formal patterns which convey that something applies to more than one item, but they differ in nuance. ばかりでなく is a relatively straightforward 'Not only (A), but also (B)', e.g. 日本語ばかりでなく、韓国語も話せる 'He can speak not only Japanese, but also Korean.' In contrast, に限らず has a more specific nuance of 'Not limited to (A)', e.g. 学生に限らず誰でも入場できます 'Not limited to students, anyone can enter.'

related.title: をはじめ
related.meaning: Not only...but also..., Starting with
related.comparison: をはじめ highlights one example among similar ones, e.g. 日本をはじめ多くの国で使われている ('It's used in many countries, starting with Japan'). に限らず expands the scope beyond the first item, e.g. 学生に限らず誰でも参加できる ('Not limited to students, anyone can participate').

related.title: だけでなく
related.meaning: Not only… but also
related.comparison: だけでなく and に限らず both express non-exclusivity, but differ in nuance and formality. だけでなく is a straightforward expression meaning 'not only (A), but also (B)', and is often used in everyday conversation (especially in the variant form だけじゃなくて). In contrast, に限らず has a more formal and academic tone, and carries a slightly different nuance, suggesting that whatever follows is 'not limited to' the thing in question.

related.title: 限り
related.meaning: As long as, While… is the case, Limited to
related.comparison: に限る and 限り both relate to boundaries or constraints, but serve different functions. に限る expresses the speaker's judgment that something is the best or most suitable, e.g. '寒い日は鍋に限る' ('Nothing beats hot pot on a cold day'). 限り sets a condition or scope, meaning 'as long as' or 'while', e.g. '知っている限り' ('as far as I know').

related.title: に越したことはない
related.meaning: There is nothing better than, It is the best, Would be the best
related.comparison: Both に限る and に越したことはない express preference, but differ in certainty and nuance. に限る asserts that something is clearly the best option, e.g. '本は紙に限る' ('Books are best on paper'). に越したことはない is softer and suggests a desirable but not essential option, e.g. '安いに越したことはない' ('Inexpensive would be best').

synonyms.title: にあたり・にあたって
synonyms.meaning: When, When doing something, On the occasion, At the time
synonyms.comparison: Both mark the timing of an event. にあたり・にあたって emphasizes necessary preparations or intentions beforehand, as in 開発にあたり調査を行った (Before development, we conducted research). に際して marks the occasion itself, especially significant or ceremonial ones, as in 入学に際して祝辞を述べる (Give a congratulatory speech on the occasion of enrollment).

related.title: 際に
related.meaning: When, At the time of, In case of, On the occasion of
related.comparison: Both mean at the time of, but differ in nuance. 際に is a formal variant of 時に and applies broadly to past or present, as in 出発の際に写真を撮った (We took a photo at the time of departure). に際して is more formal, limited to present or future occasions, and highlights the special or official nature of the event, as in 契約に際して署名が必要だ (A signature is required at the time of signing a contract).

related.title: だろう
related.meaning: Right?, Probably, Isn't it?
related.comparison: だろう is a form of だ used when the speaker feels uncertain. It's often translated as 'probably,' 'I guess,' or '...right?' when asking a question. In contrast, ね is used to seek confirmation or agreement, often translated as '...isn't it?' These two can combine to express both uncertainty and confirmation, as in 面白いだろうね ('It's probably interesting, isn't it?').

related.title: でしょう
related.meaning: Right?, Probably, Isn't it?
related.comparison: でしょう is a form of です used when the speaker is uncertain, often translated as 'probably,' 'I guess,' or '...right?' when asking a question. In contrast, ね is a sentence-ending particle used to seek confirmation or agreement, similar to '...isn't it?' When combined (e.g., 面白いでしょうね), the sentence expresses both uncertainty and a request for agreement: 'It's probably interesting, isn't it?'

related.title: よ
related.meaning: Emphasis (Sentence ending particle)
related.comparison: よ and ね are common sentence-ending particles that add feeling or nuance. よ adds emphasis and is used when giving new information or asserting something (e.g., 面白いですよ = 'It's interesting, I'm telling you!'). ね is used to seek agreement or confirmation (e.g., 面白いですね = 'It's interesting, isn't it?'). When combined as よね, they express both emphasis and a request for agreement.

related.title: じゃないか
related.meaning: Isn't it?, Is it not?
related.comparison: じゃないか and ね both seek agreement at the end of a sentence, but they have different nuances and levels of politeness. じゃないか is casual and assertive, often strongly insisting on an opinion, and can feel confrontational, making it best suited for use among close friends. In contrast, ね is softer and used widely to seek gentle agreement or confirmation in various contexts.

synonyms.title: なければいけない
synonyms.comparison: Both express obligation ('must do' or 'have to do') with a nuance of necessity. 行かねばならない and 行かなければいけない both express an obligation to go, and while なければいけない is already formal, ねばならない is even more formal and old-fashioned, often seen in academic or historical contexts. In daily speech, the contracted form なきゃ is more common than either.

synonyms.title: なくてはいけない
synonyms.comparison: ねばならない and なくてはいけない express obligation or necessity, saying something 'must' or 'needs to' be done. They are equivalent in meaning, but  ねばならない is a highly formal expression used primarily in the written language and in historical speech. Note that in colloquial, everyday conversation, なくてはいけない is often shortened to なくちゃ.

synonyms.title: なくてはならない
synonyms.comparison: ねばならない and なくてはならない both express obligation or necessity, meaning something 'must' or 'needs' to be done. They are equivalent in meaning, but ねばならない is a highly formal expression used primarily in the written language and in historical speech. Note that in colloquial everyday conversation, なくてはならない is often shortened to なくちゃ.

synonyms.title: なければならない
synonyms.comparison: Both express obligation with a nuance of rule or necessity, e.g. 行かねばならない and 行かなければならない both mean 'must go'. なければならない is formal; ねばならない is even more formal and often appears in academic or historical texts. In conversation, contractions like なきゃ are more common.

synonyms.title: Adjective + の(は)
synonyms.comparison: Both of these patterns use の to turn a phrase into a noun. For example, the verb 走る ('to run') becomes 走るのが好き ('I like running') because the subject marker が must follow a noun. The Adjective + の(は) pattern works similarly, turning adjectives into noun phrases (e.g., 赤い ('red') becomes 赤いのはきれいです ('The red one is pretty')).

related.title: は
related.meaning: As for... (Highlights sentence topic)
related.comparison: The particle の often shows possession, like the English ''s' (e.g., ケンジの本 means 'Kenji's book'). It can also connect two nouns, as in 日本人の学生 ('a student who is Japanese'). In contrast, は marks the topic of the sentence. Unlike の, it doesn't show possession — it introduces what the rest of the sentence will describe or explain.

related.title: Particle + の
related.meaning: Combined particles
related.comparison: In the phrase AのB, の indicates a relationship between A and B, either possession (e.g., ジョンの本 = 'John's book') or description (e.g., 日本人の学生 = 'A Japanese student'). Particle + の is a variation where a phrase with a particle describes another noun (e.g., 先生からの手紙 = 'A letter from the teacher'). Note that particles like に, も, は, が, and を are typically not used in this construction.

related.title: の
related.meaning: Possessive use (Noun omission)
related.comparison: The particle の connects nouns to indicate possession or description. For example, 私の本 means 'my book.' When used with the pronoun の (meaning 'one'), the second の is dropped. For instance, to say 'It's mine,' you would say 私のです, not 私ののです.

related.title: の
related.meaning: Indicates possession
related.comparison: の shows possession, like the English 's' (e.g., ケンジの本 = 'Kenji's book'). が marks the subject of a clause, often the doer of an action or the one with a certain trait. In modifying phrases, の and が are sometimes interchangeable without changing the meaning—for example, 先生が書いた本 and 先生の書いた本 both mean 'the book the teacher wrote.'

related.title: こと
related.meaning: Converting a verb into a noun
related.comparison: Both こと and の turn verbs or adjectives into noun phrases. こと is used for intangible or conceptual ideas, while の is used for more tangible or physical concepts. For example, 走るのが好きです and 走ることが好きです both mean 'I like running,' with の feeling more physical and こと more conceptual. However, they aren't interchangeable in set phrases like ことができる or ことがある.

antonyms.title: のがへた
antonyms.meaning: To be bad at, Poor at, Unskillful
antonyms.comparison: These two opposite terms are used after verbs to express being good or bad at certain actions. のが上手 is used to say someone is good at something, as in 絵を描くのが上手 ('She is good at drawing'). In contrast, 絵を描くのが下手 means 'She is not good at drawing.' Note that in Japanese culture, it's uncommon to use 上手 to describe yourself, as it can come across as boastful.

related.title: のがすき
related.meaning: Like doing, Love doing
related.comparison: These phrases combine the nominalizer, or 'dummy noun,' の with the な-Adjectives 好き and 上手. They are used to express liking something (好き) or being good at something (上手) by attaching them to a verb. In Japanese culture, boasting about one's abilities is rare, so 絵を描くのが好き might be preferred over 上手 when talking about being good at drawing.

antonyms.title: のがへた
antonyms.meaning: To be bad at, Poor at, Unskillful
antonyms.comparison: These phrases combine the nominalizer の with the な-Adjectives 好き and 下手. They are used to express liking something (好き) or not being good at something (下手). In Japanese culture, boasting is rare, so while 下手 can be used to humbly describe a lack of ability, 好き serves as a more modest alternative to the boastful-sounding 上手.

related.title: こと
related.meaning: Converting a verb into a noun
related.comparison: This phrase combines the nominalizer の with the な-Adjective 好き to express liking an activity (e.g., 本を読むのが好き = 'I like to read books'). の can be replaced with こと, another nominalizer. The difference is subtle: の implies a concrete or tangible action, while こと is more abstract (e.g., 本を読むことが好き implies liking the 'act of reading').

related.title: のがじょうず
related.meaning: To be good at, Proficient
related.comparison: These phrases combine the nominalizer, or 'dummy noun,' の with the な-Adjectives 好き and 上手. They are used to express liking something (好き) or being good at something (上手) by attaching them to a verb. In Japanese culture, boasting about one's abilities is rare, so 絵を描くのが好き might be preferred over 上手 when talking about being good at drawing.

antonyms.title: のがすき
antonyms.comparison: These phrases combine the nominalizer の with the な-Adjectives 好き and 下手. They are used to express liking something (好き) or not being good at something (下手). In Japanese culture, boasting is rare, so while 下手 can be used to humbly describe a lack of ability, 好き serves as a more modest alternative to the boastful-sounding 上手.

antonyms.title: のがじょうず
antonyms.comparison: These two opposite terms are used after verbs to express being good or bad at certain actions. のが上手 is used to say someone is good at something, as in 絵を描くのが上手 ('She is good at drawing'). In contrast, 絵を描くのが下手 means 'She is not good at drawing.' Note that in Japanese culture, it's uncommon to use 上手 to describe yourself, as it can come across as boastful.

related.title: かな
related.meaning: I wonder
related.comparison: Both かな and ～のだろうか express doubt or curiosity, like 'I wonder if (A)' かな is casual and common in everyday speech or texting. ～のだろうか also can be used in conversation but conveys a more thoughtful or serious tone, suggesting deeper consideration as compared to かな, which can be used for offhand comments.

related.title: かしら
related.meaning: I wonder
related.comparison: Both express uncertainty or internal questioning, like 'I wonder'. ～のだろうか (or the polite ～のでしょうか) is neutral and used by all genders. かしら has a softer, feminine tone and is rarely used by men. The explanatory の adds a nuance of seeking an answer, which かしら does not.

related.title: じゃないか
related.meaning: Isn't it?, Is it not?
related.comparison: Both ～のだろうか and じゃないか seek confirmation, but with different levels of confidence. のだろうか expresses uncertainty, used for low-confidence guesses or suppositions, e.g. 'I wonder if (A)?'. In contrast, じゃないか is used to confirm something the speaker believes to be true, often with a confrontational or challenging tone, e.g. 'It's (A), isn't it!?'.

related.title: はたして
related.meaning: Wonder if ~ (really)?, Wonder how ~ (actually)?, Sure enough, As expected
related.comparison: ～のだろうか means 'I wonder...' and is used at the end of a sentence to express doubt. はたして adds emphasis and often appears at the start of a sentence to heighten that feeling. They're often used together, as in 果たして勝てるのだろうか？ ('Can we actually win?').

related.title: じゃないか
related.meaning: Isn't it?, Is it not?
related.comparison: Both じゃないか and のではないだろうか are negative questions similar to the English '...isn't it?' but differ in nuance. じゃないか is used to confirm something the speaker believes to be true, often with a confrontational or challenging tone, e.g. 'It's (A), isn't it!?' In contrast, のではないだろうか is more tentative, expressing uncertainty and seeking confirmation, e.g. 'Do you suppose it's (A)?'.

related.title: でしょう
related.meaning: Right?, Probably, Isn't it?
related.comparison: でしょう is a form of です used to express mild uncertainty (e.g., 'It's probably...') or to soften a statement. When used with a question intonation, it seeks the listener's agreement (e.g., 'It's this, right?'). In contrast, のではないだろうか expresses more doubt and seeks confirmation or approval (e.g., 'I wonder if it's…?'). In polite speech, だろう is replaced with でしょう, as in のではないでしょうか.

related.title: だろう
related.meaning: Right?, Probably, Isn't it?
related.comparison: だろう is a form of だ used to express mild uncertainty (like 'It's probably…') or to seek confirmation from the listener ('It's this, right?'). のではないだろうか is similar but expresses much less certainty, with a nuance of wondering or seeking confirmation or approval ('I wonder if it's…?').

synonyms.title: ものだから
synonyms.comparison: Both ので and ものだから give reasons or explanations, but with slightly different nuances. ので is normally more objective and direct, used to state that fact (A) is the reason for (B). ものだから, on the other hand, is more subjective, expressing personal or emotional reasons for the speaker's actions.

synonyms.title: もの・もん
synonyms.comparison: ので expresses a cause or reason, similar to 'A, so B' in English. もの or もん (the latter being more casual) can also express a reason, adding emphasis or emotion, like the English 'because.' The main difference is that ので sounds more objective and can be used mid-sentence, while もの or もん typically comes at the end of a sentence and conveys a more personal or emotional tone.

synonyms.title: ものですから・もので
synonyms.meaning: Because, My excuse is, My explanation is
synonyms.comparison: Both ので and ものですから・もので give reasons or explanations. ので is normally more objective and direct, used to state that fact (A) is the reason for (B). ものですから・もので is used to express more personal or emotional reasons for actions. Note that ですから is primarily used in polite speech.

synonyms.title: から
synonyms.comparison: から and ので both express a cause or reason, similar to the English 'A, so B.' The reason comes first, followed by から or ので, and then the result. から is more colloquial, used in everyday speech, and often for personal or emotional statements. ので is slightly more formal and tends to be used for more objective statements.

related.title: ことだし
related.meaning: (And) so, Because, Since
related.comparison: Both express cause, but the stance is different. ことだし frames the reason as an accepted situation that makes the conclusion feel reasonable or timely, often used when gently proposing or deciding something, as in 時間もないことだし、先に始めよう ('Since we don’t have much time, let’s start first.'). ので is a neutral causal connector focusing on objective explanation, commonly used to state factual reasons without added nuance, as in 雨が降っているので、試合は中止です ('Because it is raining, the game is canceled.').

related.title: なぜなら〜から
related.meaning: Because, The reason is, That is because
related.comparison: Both ので and なぜなら〜から express reasons or causes. ので is more objective and straightforward, used in both casual and formal contexts, similar to 'A, so B' in English. なぜなら〜から emphasizes the reason more strongly, typically used in writing or formal speech, with a nuance of 'As for why, it's because...' and often used for detailed explanations.

related.title: し～し
related.meaning: And, Giving reasons
related.comparison: Both し～し and ので express reasons, similar to 'so' or 'because' in English, with the reason coming before し or ので. The main difference is that ので focuses on a single, objective reason, while し～し lists multiple reasons and often has a more personal or subjective feel. Even with one reason, し implies that there are other unstated or implied reasons.

synonyms.title: ～のうち(で)
synonyms.comparison: ～のうち(で) and のなかで are similar expressions that both mean 'among', but their usage can differ. ～のうち(で) is most often used with number + counter phrases. For example, 5人のうち4人が合格した ('Out of 5 people, 4 passed.'). With non-numerical groupings, like スポーツの中でどれが一番好き？ ('Among sports, which do you like best?'), のなかで is more common, especially in speech.

related.title: なかなか
related.meaning: Very, Quite, Considerably
related.comparison: Though both contain なか, their meanings are different. なかなか is often used in negatives to express difficulty, like 'not easily' (なかなかできません), or in positives to mean 'quite' (なかなかいい). In contrast, のなかで means 'among' and is used when referring to one or more members of a group. For example, 果物の中でりんごが一番好き means 'Among fruits, I like apples the best.'.

related.title: のなかで～がいちばん～
related.meaning: Out of ~, ~ is the best/most ~

related.title: 同士
related.meaning: Fellow, With each other, Mutually, Between, Among, Together, With
related.comparison: のなかで and 同士 can both refer to groups or relationships, but their meanings and usage differ. のなかで means 'among' and is used to highlight one or more members within a group, e.g. サッカー選手の中で誰が好き？ ('Among soccer players, who do you like?'). 同士, on the other hand, emphasizes mutual relationships, e.g. サッカー選手同士で話し合った means 'They talked as fellow soccer players.'.

related.title: と～と、どちらが
related.meaning: Between ~ which one
related.comparison: Both of these patterns can be used to express preferences or comparisons. The main difference is that the と～と、どちらが pattern is used when asking about or expressing a choice between two options, while のなかで～がいちばん～ involves comparing a group of three or more options.

related.title: もっとも
related.meaning: Although, But, However, For all that, Provided that
related.comparison: Both patterns express superlatives ('the best' or 'the most'), but with a key difference. のなかで～がいちばん～ clearly states what group is being compared, while もっとも simply means 'the most X,' with the comparison group left unstated. Also, のなかで～がいちばん～ is more casual, whereas もっとも is more formal.

related.title: のなかで
related.meaning: In, Among, Within

related.title: ～のうち(で)
related.meaning: Of the, Among(st)
related.comparison: Both ～のうち(で) and のなかで express the idea of 'among' or 'out of,' used when selecting or describing something from a group. のうち is often (though not exclusively) used with number + counter phrases, as in 10人のうち5人が車を持っている ('Out of 10 people, 5 own a car'). のなかで is more common and casual in speech, while のうち has a slightly more formal tone.

related.title: ため(に)
related.meaning: For, For the sake of, In order to
related.comparison: Both can mean 'in order to' when expressing purpose and are often interchangeable in that usage, e.g. 健康維持のために運動する / 健康を維持するのに運動する ('I exercise in order to maintain my health.') However, ために can also express cause in certain contexts, which のに cannot, e.g. 雨のために試合が中止になった ('The game was canceled due to rain.')

related.title: には
related.meaning: In order to, In regard to, For
related.comparison: Both can express purpose, e.g. 日本語を勉強するのに文プロを使っている ('I'm using Bunpro to study Japanese.') 日本語をマスターするには時間がかかる ('To master Japanese it takes time.') のに follows dictionary-form verbs, while には can follow either verbs or nouns. には is also used more broadly to mean 'in regard to' or 'for', where the idea of purpose doesn't apply, e.g. 子どもには難しい ('It's difficult for children.')

related.title: ものを
related.meaning: But, Although, If only, I wish I had
related.comparison: ものを and のに both are used to express the sense of 'if only'. ものを is more formal and also typically used when the speaker is expressing their disappointment at something that should have happened, not happening, e.g. 事前に知らせてくれればいいものを ('if only you had informed me in advance'), while のに can be used for general wishes 晴れればいいのに ('if only the weather were nice').

related.title: くせに
related.meaning: Even though, And yet, Despite
related.comparison: Both のに and くせに express contrast, but with different nuance. のに is relatively neutral, like 'although', while くせに adds a strong negative or critical tone. For example, 頭がいいのにやる気がない and 頭がいいくせにやる気がない both mean 'Although (he's) smart, (he) has no motivation.', the のに version is just stating a fact, while the くせに version implies that his lack of motivation is a negative trait and not befitting of him.

related.title: それでも
related.meaning: But still, And yet, Even so, Nevertheless
related.comparison: のに expresses contrast, similar to 'although,' and follows verbs, adjectives, or nouns (with な for nouns and な-adjectives). It's typically used at the end of a clause or sentence. それでも also shows contrast, but appears at the beginning of a sentence. The それ refers to the previous statement, giving it the meaning of 'Even so' or 'But still.'

related.title: にしても
related.meaning: Even though, Even if, Even when, Also
related.comparison: Both にしても and のに express contrast, but with different nuances. のに is a general contrastive expression, like 'although.' にしても often presents a hypothetical or assumed condition, followed by an unexpected result. Like other ～ても forms, it's commonly used with Wh-words, e.g. どこに行くにしても 'no matter where you go'.

related.title: ものの
related.meaning: Although, But, Even Though
related.comparison: のに and ものの both express contrast, but differ in nuance and tone. のに is more casual and often conveys disappointment or regret. It can also end a sentence to imply 'If only (A)!'. ものの is more formal and means 'although (A), (B) didn't follow as expected,' often with an implicit contrast. Unlike のに, it is not generally used alone at the end of a sentence..

related.title: それなのに
related.meaning: And yet, Despite this
related.comparison: のに expresses contrast, similar to 'although,' and follows verbs, adjectives, or nouns (with な for nouns and な-adjectives). It's typically used at the end of a phrase or sentence. それなのに is a related form used at the beginning of a sentence, meaning 'and yet' or 'despite that,' with それ referring back to the previous statement.

related.title: ながらも
related.meaning: But, Even while, Even though, Despite, Although
related.comparison: Both のに and ながらも express contrast, but with different focuses. のに is more general and used widely, like the English 'although.' ながらも is mainly used with state-expressing verbs and conveys surprise that something happened despite that state.

synonyms.title: Adjective + の(は)
synonyms.comparison: Both structures single out and highlight something. That is, expressing '(A) is the one that (B)'. Adjective + の(は) is the simpler form, e.g. 新しいのはどっち？ ('Which one is new?') のはXの方だ expands on this to specify and contrast. A common use is in insistent apologies, e.g. いいえ、謝らないといけないのは僕のほうだ ('No, I'm the one who needs to apologize.')

synonyms.title: わけではない
synonyms.meaning: It doesn't mean that, It is not the case, It's not like
synonyms.comparison: のはXの方だ emphatically states what is true or relevant, often in contrast with something else, e.g. 勝ったのは彼の方だ ('He was the one who won.') わけではない, by contrast, qualifies or softens a statement, e.g. 彼が勝ったわけではない ('It's not that he won.') One affirms directly; the other downplays or denies.

related.title: こそ
related.meaning: For sure, For certain, Even more so, Underlined or bolded text
related.comparison: Both structures single out and highlight something, but in different ways. のはXの方だ highlights contrast between options and is often used in apologies, e.g. 悪いのは僕の方だ ('I'm the one that's at fault.') こそ emphasizes exclusivity or importance, e.g. 私こそ謝るべきだ ('I, and no one else, should apologize.')

synonyms.title: のも当然だ
synonyms.meaning: It is only natural that, It is no wonder
synonyms.comparison: Both mean something is natural or expected. のも当然だ is assertive, stressing inevitability, as in 首になるのも当然だ (It’s no wonder he was fired). のももっともだ adds a nuance of understanding, as in 怒るのももっともだ (It’s understandable they’d be angry).

related.title: て当然だ
related.meaning: Natural, As a matter of course
related.comparison: Both convey inevitability, but nuance differs. て当然だ emphasizes strong expectation, as in 負けて当然だ (It’s no wonder you lost). のももっともだ shows the speaker’s empathy or understanding, as in 怒るのももっともだ (It makes sense they’d be angry).

related.title: を機に
related.meaning: Opportunity, Occasion
related.comparison: These expressions are relatively synonymous in meaning and both refer to a sense of 'on the occasion of' or 'using this as an opportunity'. They are interchangeable in many situations but differ in that を機会に can be found in many variant patterns like を機会（に・と）して, and so forth, while を機に is usually found in a very specific pattern.

synonyms.title: に限らず
synonyms.meaning: Not just, Not only… but also
synonyms.comparison: Both mean not only…but also, but differ in register and nuance. に限らず stresses inclusiveness, as in 日本に限らず世界中で使われている (Used not only in Japan but worldwide). のみならず is more formal and often adds additional, sometimes surprising, information, as in 彼は日本語のみならず英語も話せる (He speaks not only Japanese but also English).

synonyms.title: はもとより
synonyms.meaning: Not only... but also, Let alone, Of course
synonyms.comparison: はもとより and のみならず both mean 'not only... but also'. はもとより is slightly more formal and can add the nuance of 'let alone' for the first part, emphasizing the inclusion of the second. のみならず is still formal but slightly less so, and puts more of the focus on the second thing being a noteworthy addition on top of the first.

synonyms.title: ばかりでなく
synonyms.meaning: Not only…but also, As well as
synonyms.comparison: ばかりでなく and のみならず are both expressions used to convey the meaning of 'Not only (A)'. While both are formal, のみならず is even more literary. Both patterns can also be followed by noun + も to express 'But also (B)'. For example, 日本語のみならず、韓国語も話せる 'He can speak not only Japanese, but also Korean.' vs. 日本語ばかりでなく、韓国語も話せる (Same meaning, but slightly less literary in tone).

synonyms.title: だけでなく(て)～も
synonyms.meaning: Not only ~ but ~ also, Not just ~ but ~ also
synonyms.comparison: のみならず and だけでなく(て)～も are nearly identical in meaning, both meaning 'Not only (A), but also (B)'. のみならず is by far the more formal expression, while だけでなく (especially in the alternate form だけじゃなくて) is more common in everyday speech. For example, 日本語のみならず、韓国語も話せる 'He can speak not only Japanese, but also Korean.' vs. 日本語だけじゃなくて、韓国語も話せる (Same meaning, but far more casual).

synonyms.title: だけでなく
synonyms.comparison: のみならず and だけでなく are essentially synonymous in that they both express non-exclusivity (i.e. 'not only'), but differ in formality. While だけでなく is common in both everyday speech (often appearing as だけじゃなくて) and writing, のみならず is a highly formal expression used primarily in writing and academic contexts. Both phrases are often followed by a noun + も to convey 'not only (A), but also (B)'.

antonyms.title: だけ
antonyms.comparison: だけ conveys 'only' or 'just.' In contrast, のみならず is a formal expression similar to だけじゃなく, meaning 'not only A, but also B.' While だけ expresses limitation, のみならず emphasizes a lack of restriction. Note that while だけ is commonly used in both everyday speech and writing, のみならず is more formal and typically limited to written language or formal speech.

synonyms.title: のももっともだ
synonyms.meaning: May well, Have or has good reason for, No wonder, … Is only natural
synonyms.comparison: Both mean something is natural or expected. のも当然だ is assertive, stressing inevitability, as in 首になるのも当然だ (It’s no wonder he was fired). のももっともだ adds a nuance of understanding, as in 怒るのももっともだ (It’s understandable they’d be angry).

related.title: て当然だ
related.meaning: Natural, As a matter of course
related.comparison: Both express that something is natural or self-evident. て当然だ is matter-of-fact, as in 負けて当然だ (It’s no wonder you lost). のも当然だ can feel slightly more emphatic, as in 負けるのも当然だ (It’s only natural to lose).

related.title: そう
related.meaning: Look like, Appear, Seem, Have a feeling that
related.comparison: のように・のような and そう both express impressions, but with different nuance. のように・のような show direct comparison, like 'like' or 'as if,' e.g. 春のように暖かい ('It's warm like spring.'). そう expresses a perceived impression based on observation, e.g. 暖かそう ('It seems warm.').

related.title: そうに・そうな
related.meaning: Seem, Look like, Sound
related.comparison: Both そうに・そうな and のように・のような describe appearances, but with different nuances. そうに・そうな conveys a subjective impression or an event that seems about to happen, e.g. 優しそうな目 'kind-seeming eyes' or 雨が降りそうな天気 'weather that seems like it's about to rain'. ように・ような, in contrast, expresses comparison, e.g. 春のような天気 'spring-like weather'.

related.title: ように・ような
related.meaning: As・like, Just like
related.comparison: Both のように・のような and ように・ような use よう to express similarity, but with different structures and uses. のように・のような follows nouns for comparisons, e.g. 日本人のような話し方 'a manner of speaking like a Japanese person's'. ように・ような follows verbs and conveys the meaning of doing something 'as if' or 'in a way that is like,' e.g. 日本人が話すように話す 'to speak like a Japanese person speaks'.

related.title: みたいに・みたいな
related.meaning: Like, Similar to, Resembling
related.comparison: のように・のような and みたいに・みたいな both express comparisons, 'like' or 'similar to,' but differ in formality and structure. みたい is more casual and attaches directly after nouns, e.g. 春みたいな天気 'spring-like weather'. よう is more formal and uses の before it, e.g. 春のような天気, which has the same meaning.

related.title: ふうに
related.meaning: ~ way, In a way that suggests, Like ~
related.comparison: のように・のような and ふうに both express similarity, but with different usage and nuance. のように・のような follows nouns, e.g. 春のような天気 'spring-like weather', and expresses a general comparison. ふうに, on the other hand, typically follows verbs or words like こんな and そんな, implying 'in a way' or 'giving the feel of,' e.g. こんなふうに話す 'to speak in this way'.

related.title: にみえる
related.meaning: To look, To seem, To appear
related.comparison: Both のように・のような and にみえる express how something 'appears,' but with different nuances. のように・のような is used for comparisons and connects to nouns or the rest of the sentence, e.g. 春のような天気 'spring-like weather'. にみえる specifically refers to visual appearance and can be used alone, e.g. その虫は葉っぱに見える ('That bug looks like a leaf.').

related.title: にみえる
related.meaning: To look, To seem, To appear
related.comparison: Both expressions relate to appearance, but with different nuance and usage. ～(の)姿 refers to someone's outward form, posture, or visual state, e.g. 彼女の頑張っている姿 ('The sight/figure of her working hard'), while ～ように見える expresses how something visually seems or appears to the speaker, e.g. 彼は悲しそうに見える ('He looks sad') or 葉っぱに見える ('It looks like a leaf').

related.title: らしい ②
related.meaning: typical of, -ish・like, appropriate for, becoming of, befitting
related.comparison: Both relate to appearance, but with quite different nuances. ～(の)姿 refers to someone's outward form, posture, or visual state, e.g. 彼女の頑張っている姿 ('The sight/figure of her working hard'). In contrast, ～らしい② is used to describe traits or appearances typical of a category or role, e.g. 男らしい ('Manly'). While 姿 describes the appearance or form itself, ～らしい② is about how that form embodies social or cultural norms.

related.title: の至り
related.meaning: Utmost, Height of, Extreme, Heartiest, Such a, Very, really, Completely, Deeply
related.comparison: Both are formal expressions meaning ‘the utmost/the extreme degree’ of a feeling or quality, and they are often interchangeable in set phrases. の至り tends to sound slightly more personal and subjective, commonly used by the speaker to express their own feelings, especially in polite or humble contexts, as in 光栄の至りです ('I am deeply honored.'). の極み sounds a bit more fixed and categorical, often used to label something as the absolute extreme of a quality, especially in criticism, as in 無礼の極みだ ('It is the height of rudeness.').

related.title: 極まりない・極まる
related.meaning: Extreme, Extremely
related.comparison: Though they share similar origins and both relate to the idea of extremity, these expressions differ in usage. の極み has more specific use cases, following nouns and coming at the end of a sentence or phrase to mean 'the epitome/peak of', e.g. 幸福の極み 'the peak of happiness'. In contrast, 極まりない and 極まる typically follow adjectives, with the resulting phrase preceding a noun to mean 'extremely', e.g. 失礼（極まる・極まりない）話 'a matter that is extremely (the height of) discourtesy'. Both can be used negatively or positively.

related.title: の極み
related.meaning: Ultimate, Extreme, Utter, Epitome, Peak
related.comparison: Both are formal expressions meaning ‘the utmost/the extreme degree’ of a feeling or quality, and they are often interchangeable in set phrases. の至り tends to sound slightly more personal and subjective, commonly used by the speaker to express their own feelings, especially in polite or humble contexts, as in 光栄の至りです ('I am deeply honored.'). の極み sounds a bit more fixed and categorical, often used to label something as the absolute extreme of a quality, especially in criticism, as in 無礼の極みだ ('It is the height of rudeness.').

related.title: うちに
related.meaning: While, During
related.comparison: Both の間に and うちに can express 'while' or 'during', but are used in different contexts. の間に simply conveys the idea of 'while (A), (B)', e.g. 映画を観ている間に眠ってしまった ('I fell asleep while/during watching a movie.') In contrast, うちに carries the nuance of doing something during an ideal time or condition, e.g. 明るいうちに帰ろう ('Let's go home while it's light out.')

related.title: ついでに
related.meaning: While you are at it, On the occasion
