related.comparison: ついでに and の間に can both express the idea of 'while', but are used in different contexts. ついでに implies taking advantage of one action to do another, e.g. 買い物のついでに銀行に寄った ('I stopped by the bank while shopping.') の間に, by contrast, means 'during/while (A), (B)' without the added nuance of 'while you're at it' that comes with ついでに.

related.title: ～ところに・～ところへ
related.meaning: At the time, When, While, Timing
related.comparison: ～ところに・～ところへ and の間に both express the idea of 'while', but with different nuance. ～ところに／へ emphasizes a scene or moment into which something else happens, e.g. ゲームをしているところへ兄が部屋に入ってきた ('While I was playing a game, my brother entered the room.') の間に, by contrast, simply describes one event taking place during another, without emphasizing a specific situation or setting.

related.title: ているあいだに
related.meaning: While, During the time that ~
related.comparison: 間(あいだ) means 'period' or 'space', and in addition to its literal meaning, it is used in expressions meaning 'while' or 'between', e.g. 1月と4月の間に 'between January and April'. In the ているあいだに form, the focus is on something happening during another ongoing action. For example, 勉強しているあいだに電話がかかってきた ('The phone rang while I was studying.').

related.title: 中
related.meaning: During, While, Throughout, In the process of
related.comparison: Both express a continued state or action, but are used differently. 中 attaches only to nouns and means 'in the middle of (A)', e.g. 会議中 ('in the middle of a meeting'), 使用中 ('in use'). の間に attaches to nouns (as-is), verbs/adjectives (with の removed), and な-adjectives (with な instead of の), and means 'while doing (A), (B)', e.g. 映画を観ている間に電話が鳴った ('While I was watching a movie, the phone rang.')

related.title: とき
related.meaning: When, At the time of
related.comparison: とき expresses the time of an action or event, like the English 'when'. It can be used with nouns as in 雨の時 'when it rains', verbs as in 父が帰ってきた時 'when Dad came home', or adjectives as in 暑い時 'when it's hot'. In contrast, の間に is used with nouns, and describes a period within which something happens, similar to the English 'while' or 'during', e.g. 冬休みの間に ('during winter break').

related.title: ながら
related.meaning: While ~ing, During, As
related.comparison: ながら attaches to verb stems to express performing one action simultaneously with another, e.g. 音楽を聴きながら走る 'listen to music while running'. In contrast, 間に can follow various words or phrases to express doing something while a certain state or condition persists, e.g. 若い間にマラソンを走りたい ('I want to run a marathon while I'm young.').

related.title: 最中に
related.meaning: In the middle of
related.comparison: Both の間に and 最中に express 'while (A), (B)', but with slightly different nuance. 最中に is more emphatic, implying that (B) happened right in the middle of (A). In contrast, の間に simply states that (B) occurred during the general span of (A), and sounds more objective.

related.title: ているところだ
related.meaning: In the process of doing ~ right now/at this moment
related.comparison: ているところだ conveys that an action is in progress at that moment, with the nuance that the speaker is occupied with it, e.g. 映画を観ているところだ ('I'm in the middle of watching a movie.'). In contrast, の間に is used to express that something else happened at some point during the duration of that action, e.g. 映画を観ている間に父が帰ってきました ('My father came home while I was watching a movie.').

related.title: の
related.meaning: Verb nominalizer, The one who..., That which...
related.comparison: The particle の often shows possession, like the English ''s' (e.g., ケンジの本 means 'Kenji's book'). It can also connect two nouns, as in 日本人の学生 ('a student who is Japanese'). In contrast, は marks the topic of the sentence. Unlike の, it doesn't show possession — it introduces what the rest of the sentence will describe or explain.

related.title: といえば
related.meaning: Speaking of, If it were the case that
related.comparison: Both といえば and は can introduce the topic of a sentence. といえば is used when referencing something previously mentioned or known, often implying that more information will follow ('speaking of…'). は is broader and can also suggest contrast, as in 'バナナは食べたけど…' ('I ate the banana, but [not the apple]'). といえば doesn't create contrast in this way.

related.title: って
related.meaning: As for, Speaking of, Casual は (Sentence topic marker)
related.comparison: って is used in casual conversation to introduce a topic informally. It comes from the quotation particle と and can carry the nuance of 'speaking of…'. は is a more standard and flexible topic marker, used in both casual and formal situations, including writing. Unlike って, は can also carry a contrastive nuance.

related.title: が
related.meaning: Subject marking particle, Identification particle
related.comparison: The particle は marks the topic of a sentence, or what the sentence is about. が usually marks the grammatical subject, especially when introducing new information or expressing desire, ability, or necessity. は can also show contrast, while が emphasizes what it marks.

related.title: Number/Amount + は
related.meaning: At least, Sort of, Or so
related.comparison: When は follows a number or amount, it often adds a contrastive nuance like 'at least' or 'no more than.' For example, １回は行ったけど means 'I went once, but no more than that.' This is similar to contrastive uses of は, like in 田中さんは行ったけど ('Tanaka went, but no one else did'). If there's no contrast, the number is usually used without a particle, as in 三つ買った ('I bought three').

related.title: くらい ①
related.meaning: About, Approximately
related.comparison: くらい (or ぐらい in speech) expresses an approximate degree or amount, like the English 'about.' It's commonly used with numbers and counters (the くらい① form), e.g. 100円くらい = 'about 100 yen.' A related pattern, は～くらいです, means 'just about the only X is Y.' For example, 食べ物で嫌いなのはピーマンくらいです = 'The only food I dislike is peppers.'

related.title: ほど
related.meaning: The more, About, To the extent that, So much… that
related.comparison: Both relate to expressing extent, but with different emphasis. は～くらいです highlights something as the only or most notable case, like 'about the only (A) is (B)', e.g. できるのは田中さんくらいです ('Mr. Tanaka's about the only person who can do it.') ほど expresses extent or intensity, e.g. 驚くほど静かだった ('It was quiet to a surprising degree.')

related.title: とされている
related.meaning: To be considered, To be deemed, Be regarded as, Be accepted as, To be said, To be accredited with
related.comparison: Both present an accepted state or fact, but differ in nuance. ～は～となっている describes that something has been established and is now 'the way things are', e.g. 朝礼は毎朝１０時開始となっている ('The morning meeting is set to start every day at 10 a.m'). とされている conveys that it is generally regarded in a certain way or as something, e.g. この神社は最古のものとされている ('This shrine is considered the oldest one').

related.title: とされている
related.meaning: To be considered, To be deemed, Be regarded as, Be accepted as, To be said, To be accredited with
related.comparison: Both present an accepted state or fact, but differ in nuance. ～は～となっている describes that something has been established and is now 'the way things are', e.g. 朝礼は毎朝１０時開始となっている ('The morning meeting is set to start every day at 10 a.m'). とされている conveys that it is generally regarded in a certain way or as something, e.g. この神社は最古のものとされている ('This shrine is considered the oldest one').

related.title: とかんがえられている
related.slug: とかんがえられている
related.meaning: Is considered, Is thought of as
related.comparison: ～は～となっている and と考えられている both express current states or views, but differ in tone. ～は～となっている describes some sort of established fact that is now 'the way things are', e.g. 店は日曜休業となっている ('The store is closed on Sundays'). と考えられている expresses a generally held belief or reasoning and is often used to make a statement sound less direct, e.g. 彼は天才だと考えられている ('He is thought to be a genius').

related.title: とかんがえられている
related.slug: とかんがえられている
related.meaning: Is considered, Is thought of as
related.comparison: ～は～となっている and と考えられている both express current states or views, but differ in tone. ～は～となっている describes some sort of established fact that is now 'the way things are', e.g. 店は日曜休業となっている ('The store is closed on Sundays'). と考えられている expresses a generally held belief or reasoning and is often used to make a statement sound less direct, e.g. 彼は天才だと考えられている ('He is thought to be a genius').

related.title: ～のうち(で)
related.meaning: Of the, Among(st)
related.comparison: Both ～のうち(で) and ～は～の一つだ are used to identify things within a group or category. ～のうち(で) is most often paired with number + counter phrases to mean 'among', for example, ５人のうち４人が合格した ('Out of 5 people, 4 passed.'). In contrast, ～は～の一つだ identifies something as part of a group, e.g., バナナは果物の一つだ ('A banana is one type of fruit.').

related.title: はともかく
related.meaning: Setting aside, Apart from
related.comparison: Both set aside one item to comment on another, but the nuance differs. はおろか emphasizes that something is far beyond expectation, often meaning ‘let alone,’ and highlights an extreme gap, as in 漢字はおろか、ひらがなも読めない ('He can’t read hiragana, let alone kanji.'). はともかく means ‘setting aside’ or ‘regardless of,’ used to temporarily bracket one point and move on without judging degree, as in 値段はともかく、味は悪くない ('Putting the price aside, the taste isn’t bad.').

antonyms.title: てしまう・ちゃう
antonyms.meaning: To do something by accident, To finish completely・totally, Unfortunately
antonyms.comparison: はじめる attaches to verb stems to mean 'to begin' or 'start' doing something, e.g. 食べはじめる 'to start eating'. In contrast, てしまう・ちゃう follows the て-form and can express doing something unintentionally or completely, e.g. 食べてしまった・食べちゃった ('I ate it unintentionally.' or 'I ate it all.').

antonyms.title: おわる
antonyms.comparison: はじめる and おわる can be attached to the stem of verbs to express the contrasting ideas of 'beginning' and 'finishing' an action, respectively. For example, 読みはじめる means 'to begin reading', while 読みおわる means 'to finish reading'.

antonyms.title: 上がる・上げる
antonyms.meaning: Something is finished, To finish something, Something has come to an end, To do something completely, To do something through
antonyms.comparison: はじめる attaches to verb stems to express 'beginning to' do something, e.g. 作りはじめる 'to start making'. In contrast, 上がる・上げる also attach to verb stems but express completing an action, e.g. 作りあげる 'to finish making', often with a sense of effort or finality.

related.title: ていく
related.meaning: To ~ and go, To go on, To start
related.comparison: ていく expresses either literal movement away from the speaker or a change that begins now and continues into the future. In contrast, はじめる attaches to the verb stem to mean 'to start (A)'. The key difference is nuance: ていく highlights ongoing progression and continuation going forward, while はじめる focuses on the starting point and the idea of commencing an action.

related.title: かけ
related.meaning: Half-, Not yet finished, About to, Begin to
related.comparison: はじめる attaches to verb stems to mean 'to begin' or 'start' doing something, e.g. 食べはじめる 'to start eating'. In contrast, かけ also attaches to verb stems but indicates something incomplete or in progress, e.g. 食べかけ 'half-eaten'. Forms with かけ function grammatically as nouns, as in 食べかけのバナナ 'a half-eaten banana'.

related.title: だす
related.meaning: To suddenly begin, To start doing
related.comparison: Both はじめる and だす attach to verb stems to mean 'to start doing (A)', but with different nuances. はじめる implies a deliberate or planned action, e.g. 学びはじめた ('I began to learn.'). In contrast, だす conveys a more spontaneous or unexpected beginning, e.g. 雨が降りだした ('It suddenly began to rain.').

related.title: はともかく
related.meaning: Setting aside, Apart from
related.comparison: Both mean setting (A) aside and are often interchangeable. はさておき・はさておいて is often used to temporarily set something aside to prioritize another point, as in 冗談はさておき本題に入りましょう (Jokes aside, let’s get to the main point). はともかく can dismiss something as irrelevant, as in 値段はともかくデザインが気に入った (Setting the price aside, I like the design).

antonyms.title: はずだ
antonyms.meaning: Bound to (be), Expected to (be), Should be (able)
antonyms.comparison: These forms are opposites. はずだ expresses positive expectation, as in 来るはずだ ('He should come.'), while はずがない strongly denies a possibility based on logic or knowledge, as in 来るはずがない ('There's no way he'll come.'). Both are grounded in reasoning, but the conclusions differ.

antonyms.title: わけだ
antonyms.meaning: For that reason, No wonder, As you'd expect, Naturally, Thus, This is why
antonyms.comparison: はずがない denies something based on logic or evidence, while わけだ draws a conclusion or explains a reason. For example, 来るはずがない ('There's no way he'll come.') vs. 来るわけだ ('So that's why he's coming.'). One rejects a possibility, while the other clarifies it.

antonyms.title: ないことはない
antonyms.meaning: Is not impossible, It doesn't mean that it isn't
antonyms.comparison: はずがない strongly denies something, as in そんなことがあるはずがない ('That can't possibly be true.'). In contrast, ないことはない suggests that something is technically possible, but maybe unlikely or difficult, as in 行けないことはない ('It's not that I can't go.').

related.title: ことはない
related.meaning: There is no need to, Never happens
related.comparison: ことはない has two primary uses: one is to express that there is no need to do something, e.g. 心配することはない ('There's no need to worry.'), and the other is to say that something simply doesn't happen, e.g. 彼と会うことはない ('There are no cases where I meet him.'). In contrast, はずがない expresses the speaker's strong denial or disbelief regarding some possibility, e.g. 負けるはずがない ('There's no way / It's highly unlikely we will lose.').

related.title: ものか
related.meaning: Definitely not, Absolutely not, Impossible, Do you really think~?
related.comparison: Both express strong denial, but ものか is more emphatic and emotional. はずがない states something is unlikely or impossible, based on logic, e.g. 来るはずがない ('There's no way he'll come.'). ものか rejects something outright, as in 君にできるものか！ ('As if you could!').

related.title: まじき
related.meaning: Unbecoming of, Unworthy of, Not proper for, Should not, Must not, Cannot
related.comparison: Both express strong denial, but their usage differs. まじき is a formal, literary expression usually found in fixed phrases such as あるまじき, and is used to judge that something is morally or socially unacceptable rather than logically impossible, as in 指導者としてあるまじき行為だ ('It is behavior unbecoming of a leader.'). はずがない denies possibility based on logic, evidence, or common sense, stating that something cannot be true, as in 彼がそんなことをするはずがない ('There is no way he would do such a thing.').

related.title: ようがない・ようもない
related.slug: ようがない・ようもない
related.meaning: There's no way to, It's impossible to
related.comparison: These both express impossibility, but in different ways. はずがない asserts a strong disbelief based on knowledge, like 雨が降るはずがない ('It can't possibly rain.'). ようがない・ようもない focuses on the lack of a method, as in 連絡のしようがない ('There's no way to contact them.').

related.title: わけではない
related.meaning: It doesn't mean that, It is not the case, It's not like
related.comparison: はずがない strongly rejects something as being true. わけではない denies an assumption while leaving room for nuance. For example, 雨が降るはずがない ('There's no way it will.') vs. 雨が降るわけではない ('It's not that it will rain.'), implying e.g. that the weather might be poor in other ways, etc.

related.title: わけがない
related.meaning: There is no way, There is no reason, It is impossible, Cannot be
related.comparison: はずがない and わけがない are similar in that they both express a strong denial of a possibility, similar to the English 'There's no way that (A)', but with slightly different nuances. はずがない gives the sense that the speaker is drawing a conclusion based on their own reasoning, often with a sense of surprise or disbelief. In contrast, わけがない expresses an even stronger denial, as if the impossibility is an obvious fact.

related.title: そうもない
related.meaning: Very unlikely to, Does not seem that, Does not appear that
related.comparison: そうもない and はずがない both express the improbability of something happening. そうもない is the less confident of the two and carries the nuance that something seems unlikely, e.g. 雨が降りそうもない ('It doesn't appear that it's going to rain.'). In contrast, はずがない is a stronger denial, with a nuance of disbelief or surprise, e.g. 負けるはずがない ('There's no way / It's highly unlikely [we] will lose.').

antonyms.title: べきではない
antonyms.meaning: Should not, Must not , Ought not to
antonyms.comparison: はずだ expresses an assumption or expectation based on reason or context, e.g. 雨が降るはずだ ('It should rain.'). In contrast, べきではない expresses a strong sense of moral or social obligation not to do something, e.g. 嘘をつくべきではない ('You shouldn't lie.').

antonyms.title: はずがない
antonyms.meaning: Hardly possible, Cannot be, Highly unlikely, Improbable
antonyms.comparison: These forms are opposites. はずだ expresses positive expectation, as in 来るはずだ ('He should come.'), while はずがない strongly denies a possibility based on logic or knowledge, as in 来るはずがない ('There's no way he'll come.'). Both are grounded in reasoning, but the conclusions differ.

related.title: べき
related.meaning: Must, Should , Ought to
related.comparison: はずだ and べき can both be translated as 'should', but they differ in nuance. はずだ expresses an expectation based on evidence or reasoning, like 明日来るはずだ ('He should come tomorrow.'). In contrast, べき expresses obligation or social correctness, as in 来るべきだ ('You should come.').

related.title: ことになっている
related.meaning: To be expected to, To be scheduled to
related.comparison: Both can describe future events, but differ in source. はずだ shows the speaker's expectation, like 来るはずだ ('He should come.'). ことになっている refers to something officially decided, often outside the speaker's control. For example, 来ることになっている ('It has been decided that he will come.')

related.title: つもりだ
related.meaning: To plan to, To intend to
related.comparison: つもりだ is used to express personal intentions or plans, similar to 'I hope to' or 'I intend to' in English. In contrast, はずだ expresses an expectation or belief that something will happen, based on logic or evidence. While つもりだ is subjective and often refers to the speaker's own plans, はずだ is more objective and is used for expectations about others or events outside the speaker's control.

related.title: わけだ
related.meaning: For that reason, No wonder, As you'd expect, Naturally, Thus, This is why
related.comparison: Both express logical conclusions, but with different emphasis. はずだ conveys expectation or prediction, as in 来るはずだ ('He should come.'). わけだ explains a reason behind something known or observed, like 来るわけだ ('So that's why he's coming.'). はずだ leans future-oriented, わけだ explanatory.

related.title: ものだ
related.meaning: Supposed to, Should, That's the way it is
related.comparison: はずだ conveys a specific expectation or prediction based on current evidence, like 雨が降るはずだ ('It should rain.'). ものだ expresses a general rule or common tendency, like 雨は春によく降るものだ ('It often rains in spring.'). The former is situational, while the latter is habitual or cultural.

related.title: たらいい・といい
related.meaning: It would be nice if, It would be good if, Should, I hope
related.comparison: はずだ expresses confident expectation, as in 雨が降るはずだ ('It should rain.'). たらいい and といい express hope or desire without implying it will happen, e.g. 雨が降るといい ('I hope it rains.'). The difference lies in expectation vs. wish. Thus, といい is often used even when the speaker is not confident the thing will happen.

related.title: ばいい
related.meaning: Can, Should, It'd be good if
related.comparison: はずだ indicates something is expected based on information or logic, as in 行けるはずだ ('I should be able to go.'). ばいい expresses hope or a hypothetical good outcome, like 行ければいい ('It'd be nice if I could go.'). One is based on reasoning, the other on desire.

related.title: かな
related.meaning: I wonder
related.comparison: Both かな and はたして express doubt, but their usage differs. かな is a casual sentence-ending particle meaning 'I wonder,' e.g. 寒いかな？ ('I wonder if it's cold?'). はたして appears at the beginning of a sentence for emphasis, often with かな or ～のだろうか, e.g. はたして来るのだろうか？ ('Will they really come, I wonder?').

related.title: さすが
related.meaning: as one would expect, only natural, that is just like..., even
related.comparison: はたして conveys doubt or skepticism, questioning whether something is really true, as in はたして成功するだろうか (Will it really succeed?). さすが affirms expectations, showing that someone or something lives up to reputation, as in さすが先生だ (That’s just like the teacher).

related.title: ～のだろうか
related.meaning: I wonder...
related.comparison: ～のだろうか means 'I wonder...' and is used at the end of a sentence to express doubt. はたして adds emphasis and often appears at the start of a sentence to heighten that feeling. They're often used together, as in 果たして勝てるのだろうか？ ('Can we actually win?').

synonyms.title: は別として
synonyms.meaning: Apart from, (Setting) aside, Whether or, Except for, Save for
synonyms.comparison: Both mean setting (A) aside and are often interchangeable. は別として can also mean except for (A), as in 彼を別として全員出席した (Everyone attended except him). はともかく simply sets something aside without excluding it, as in 値段はともかくデザインが好きだ (Setting aside the price, I like the design).

related.title: はおろか
related.meaning: Let alone, Much less, Needless to say, Not to mention, Not only but also
related.comparison: Both set aside one item to comment on another, but the nuance differs. はおろか emphasizes that something is far beyond expectation, often meaning ‘let alone,’ and highlights an extreme gap, as in 漢字はおろか、ひらがなも読めない ('He can’t read hiragana, let alone kanji.'). はともかく means ‘setting aside’ or ‘regardless of,’ used to temporarily bracket one point and move on without judging degree, as in 値段はともかく、味は悪くない ('Putting the price aside, the taste isn’t bad.').

related.title: を除いて
related.meaning: Except, With the exception of, Unless
related.comparison: はともかく and を除いて both suggest setting something aside, but with different nuance. はともかく downplays relevance to the main point, as in 田中さんはともかく、鈴木さんは来ると思う (I don’t know about Tanaka, but I think Suzuki will come). を除いて explicitly excludes something, as in 田中さんを除いて全員来ると思うよ (I think everyone will come except Tanaka).

related.title: はさておき・はさておいて
related.slug: はさておき-はさておいて
related.meaning: (Leaving or putting) aside, Apart
related.comparison: Both mean setting (A) aside and are often interchangeable. はさておき・はさておいて is often used to temporarily set something aside to prioritize another point, as in 冗談はさておき本題に入りましょう (Jokes aside, let’s get to the main point). はともかく can dismiss something as irrelevant, as in 値段はともかくデザインが気に入った (Setting the price aside, I like the design).

related.title: いがい
related.meaning: Except・besides, Other than, With the exception of
related.comparison: はともかく carries the nuance of setting something aside to focus on the rest. For example, 田中さんはともかく、みんな来ると思う means 'Setting Tanaka aside, I think everyone will come.'. In contrast, いがい emphasizes exclusion, 田中さん以外はみんな来ると思う means 'I think everyone but Tanaka will come.' So, はともかく avoids commenting on Tanaka, while いがい explicitly excludes him.

related.title: だけでなく
related.meaning: Not only… but also
related.comparison: だけでなく and はもちろん both express non-exclusivity, but differ in nuance and emphasis. だけでなく simply means 'not only (A), but also (B)', e.g. 英語だけでなく、日本語も話せる ('He can speak not only English, but also Japanese.'). In contrast, はもちろん implies the first thing is obvious, while the second is more notable, e.g. 英語はもちろん、日本語も話せる ('He can speak English of course, but also Japanese.').

related.title: はもとより
related.meaning: Not only... but also, Let alone, Of course
related.comparison: はもちろん and はもとより both mean 'not only... but also'. はもちろん adds the nuance of 'of course (A), and also (B)', e.g. 学校はもちろん家でも勉強する ('He studies at home, not just at school'). はもとより is more formal and emphasizes that (A) goes without saying, e.g. 親はもとより先生も反対した ('Not only his parents but also his teacher opposed it').

related.title: に加えて
related.meaning: Not only...but also, In addition, Not to mention
related.comparison: はもちろん emphasizes the obviousness of the first item before adding another, e.g. 英語はもちろん中国語も話せる ('He speaks not only English but also Chinese'). に加えて simply adds one thing to another without that emphasis, e.g. 雨に加えて風も強い ('In addition to the rain, the wind is also strong').

related.title: をはじめ
related.meaning: Not only...but also..., Starting with
related.comparison: はもちろん highlights an obvious example together with a more unexpected addition, e.g. 寿司はもちろん納豆も食べる ('He eats not only sushi, but also natto'). をはじめ is used to give a leading example from a larger group, e.g. 校長先生をはじめ多くの人が来た ('Many people came, starting with the principal').

related.title: ばかりでなく
related.meaning: Not only…but also, As well as
related.comparison: はもちろん highlights an obvious example together with a more unexpected addition, e.g. 子供はもちろん大人も楽しめる ('Not only kids, but adults too can enjoy it'). ばかりでなく is more neutral, simply listing two things, e.g. 雨ばかりでなく雪も降った ('It not only rained, but also snowed').

related.title: に限らず
related.meaning: Not just, Not only… but also
related.comparison: はもちろん emphasizes something obvious before adding another case, e.g. 日本人はもちろん留学生も来た ('Not only Japanese people but also exchange students came'). に限らず expands the scope of a statement, implying 'not limited to (A), also (B)', e.g. 子供に限らず大人も楽しめる ('Not only children, but also adults can enjoy it').

related.title: だけでなく(て)～も
related.meaning: Not only ~ but ~ also, Not just ~ but ~ also
related.comparison: Both express 'not only (A), but also (B)', but the nuance differs. はもちろん stresses (A) as obvious, e.g. 寿司はもちろん納豆も食べる ('He eats not only sushi, but also natto'). だけでなく gives equal weight to both, e.g. 映画だけでなくドラマも好きだ ('I like not only movies, but also TV dramas').

synonyms.title: はともかく
synonyms.comparison: Both mean setting (A) aside and are often interchangeable. は別として can also mean except for (A), as in 彼を別として全員出席した (Everyone attended except him). はともかく simply sets something aside without excluding it, as in 値段はともかくデザインが好きだ (Setting aside the price, I like the design).

synonyms.title: のみならず
synonyms.meaning: Not only...but, Not just...but, What is more
synonyms.comparison: はもとより and のみならず both mean 'not only... but also'. はもとより is slightly more formal and can add the nuance of 'let alone' for the first part, emphasizing the inclusion of the second. のみならず is still formal but slightly less so, and puts more of the focus on the second thing being a noteworthy addition on top of the first.

synonyms.title: だけでなく(て)～も
synonyms.meaning: Not only ~ but ~ also, Not just ~ but ~ also
synonyms.comparison: Both express that something applies to more than one item, but differ in nuance and tone. だけでなく(て)～も is a neutral 'Not only (A), but also (B)', e.g. 日本語だけでなく、韓国語も話せる 'He can speak not only Japanese, but also Korean.' はもとより is more literary and formal, and suggests the first item is obvious, e.g. 親はもとより先生にも感謝しています 'I'm grateful not only to my parents, but also to my teachers.'

related.title: に限らず
related.meaning: Not just, Not only… but also
related.comparison: Both に限らず and はもとより express inclusion beyond an initial example but differ in nuance. に限らず highlights that the mentioned case is not the only one, implying a broader range or scope (not only…but also). はもとより, on the other hand, emphasizes that the stated example is obvious or expected, and contrasts it with less obvious or more significant cases, carrying a nuance closer to 'needless to say…even more so'.

related.title: はもちろん
related.meaning: Not only but also, Let alone, Not to mention, Of course
related.comparison: はもちろん and はもとより both mean 'not only... but also'. はもちろん adds the nuance of 'of course (A), and also (B)', e.g. 学校はもちろん家でも勉強する ('He studies at home, not just at school'). はもとより is more formal and emphasizes that (A) goes without saying, e.g. 親はもとより先生も反対した ('Not only his parents but also his teacher opposed it').

related.title: ばかりでなく
related.meaning: Not only…but also, As well as
related.comparison: Both formally express that something applies to more than one item, with different nuance. ばかりでなく is a neutral 'Not only (A), but also (B)', e.g. 日本語ばかりでなく、韓国語も話せる 'He can speak not only Japanese, but also Korean.' はもとより is more literary in tone, and implies that the first item is obvious and the second more noteworthy, e.g. 親はもとより先生にも感謝しています 'I'm grateful not only to my parents, but also to my teachers.'

related.title: と
related.meaning: If, When, Whenever, And, [Conditional]
related.comparison: ば and と are both conditional expressions, but differ in nuance. ば emphasizes the condition that leads to a result, often with an 'if and only if' tone. と is used for habitual or automatic outcomes, suggesting that what follows happens naturally or inevitably when the condition is met.

related.title: ば〜ほど
related.meaning: The more…the more
related.comparison: ば is a conditional form that emphasizes the condition needed for a result, e.g. 勉強すればうまくなるよ ('If you study, you'll get better.'). ば〜ほど builds on this to mean 'the more (A), the more (B)' e.g. 勉強すればするほど、うまくなる ('The more you study, the better you'll get.').

related.title: たら
related.meaning: When, From when, Just when, If
related.comparison: ば and たら are both conditional forms, but with different nuances. ば emphasizes the condition itself, often with an 'if and only if' feel, e.g. 田中さんが来てくれれば何とかなる ('If Tanaka comes, it'll work out.'). たら sets up an hypothetical condition, which can be followed by a result, comment, or request, e.g. 田中さんが来てくれたら教えてね ('If/when Tanaka comes, let me know.').

related.title: なら
related.meaning: Were it, If, If it's the case, As for (Conditional)
related.comparison: ば and なら are both conditional forms, but with slightly different nuances. ば focuses on the condition itself, e.g. 田中さんが来てくれれば何とかなる ('If Tanaka comes, it'll work out.'). In contrast, なら has the nuance that the speaker has some reason to believe the condition is likely, e.g. 田中さんが来てくれるなら 'if Tanaka comes (as I expect he might)'.

related.title: もし
related.meaning: If (For emphasis)
related.comparison: もし and ば are both used in conditional expressions, but play different roles. ば is a grammatical ending that turns verbs into 'if' statements, e.g. 食べる 'eat' becoming 食べれば 'if (you) eat'. In contrast, もし is used at the start of sentences or phrases to emphasize that a hypothetical statement follows, and therefore is often paired with ば, たら, or なら, e.g. もし食べれば 'if (you) were to eat...'.

synonyms.title: 言うまでもない ②
synonyms.meaning: It goes without saying, It is needless to say, Of course, Obviously
synonyms.comparison: These are slight variations of the same expression meaning 'to go without saying'. は言うまでもない① follows a topic to say '(A) goes without saying'. 言うまでもない②, often seen in forms like 言うまでもないことだけど or 言うまでもなく, comes at the start of a phrase to mean 'Needless to say, (A)' or 'It goes without saying, but (A).'

related.title: ば
related.meaning: If… then, (Conditional)
related.comparison: ば is a conditional form that emphasizes the condition needed for a result, e.g. 勉強すればうまくなるよ ('If you study, you'll get better.'). ば〜ほど builds on this to mean 'the more (A), the more (B)' e.g. 勉強すればするほど、うまくなる ('The more you study, the better you'll get.').

related.title: 一方だ
related.meaning: More and more, Continue to, Keep on, -er and -er
related.comparison: Both can be used to describe change, but with different patterns. ば〜ほど expresses a proportional relationship between two things, like 'the more (A), the more (B)', e.g. 練習すればするほど上手になる ('The more you practice, the better you get.') 一方だ shows a continuous, one-way change in a single direction, e.g. 物価は上がる一方だ ('Prices just keep rising.')

related.title: どんどん
related.meaning: Progressively, Rapidly increasing, More and more
related.comparison: どんどん is an adverb that implies rapid or dramatic change, often in leaps and bounds, e.g. どんどん大きくなる 'grow rapidly'. 〜ば〜ほど is a structure that shows proportional change, like 'the more (A), the more (B)' in English. For example, 大きくなればなるほどいい ('The bigger it gets, the better.').

related.title: ほど
related.meaning: The more, About, To the extent that, So much… that
related.comparison: ほど (sometimes written in kanji as 程) has the basic meaning of 'extent'. ば〜ほど shows a proportional relationship, similar to 'the more (A), the more (B)', e.g. 練習すればするほど上手になる ('The more you practice, the better you get.') When used alone, ほど means 'so much that (A)' or 'to the extent of (A)', e.g. 驚くほど静かだった ('It was quiet to a surprising degree.' / 'It was so quiet that I was surprised.')

related.title: ますます
related.meaning: Increasingly, More and more, Decreasingly, Less and less, Keep -ing
related.comparison: They are similar in that both describe change, but ば〜ほど specifically expresses a proportional relationship between two things, like the English 'the more (A), the more (B)', e.g. 見れば見るほど好きになる ('The more I look, the more I like it.'). In contrast, ますます simply emphasizes continued intensification, e.g. 彼の人気はますます高まっている ('His popularity is steadily rising.')

related.title: なら
related.meaning: Were it, If, If it's the case, As for (Conditional)
related.comparison: なら and ばあいは (often in kanji as 場合は) both correspond to 'if', but with different nuances. なら expresses 'if it's the case that (A)', often implying the speaker believes it will happen, e.g. 来るなら 'if it's the case that (they) come'. In contrast, ばあいは means 'in the event that (A)', presenting a possible situation without assumption, e.g. 来る場合は 'in the event that (they) come'.

related.title: に取って
related.meaning: To, For, Concerning
related.comparison: ばあいは (often written in kanji as 場合は) and に取って (often written in kana as にとって) can overlap in some cases, but not all. For example, 私の場合は and 私にとって can both mean 'as far as I'm concerned' or 'in my case'. However, since にとって simply means 'concerning', it can't replace ばあいは for the meaning of 'in the event that', e.g. 行く場合は気をつけたほうがいい ('In the event that you go, you should be careful.').

related.title: としたら・とすれば・とすると
related.slug: としたら-とすれば-とすると
related.meaning: If it were the case that, Assuming that...
related.comparison: ばあいは and としたら・とすれば・とすると both express the idea of 'in the case of' or 'in the event that'. In general, ばあいは (often written in kanji as 場合は) is more concrete and used for specific situations that might arise. For example, 行く場合 'in the event that you go'. In contrast, としたら・とすれば・とすると is typically used to discuss more hypothetical scenarios.

related.title: 万が一
related.meaning: Just in case, In the worst-case scenario, In the unlikely event, By any chance
related.comparison: ばあいは and 万が一 are related in that both refer to potential situations. ばあいは (often written in kanji as 場合は) means 'in the case of' or 'in the event that'. For example, 行く場合は 'in the event that you go'. On the other hand, 万が一 is typically used at the start of a sentence to highlight a rare or unlikely case. Because they serve different roles, the two can even be used together, e.g. 万が一の場合は 'in the unlikely event that (A)'.

related.title: と
related.meaning: If, When, Whenever, And, [Conditional]
related.comparison: と and ばあいは can both express conditions, but differ significantly in usage. と describes something that happens habitually or as a natural result, e.g. お酒を飲むとすぐ酔っ払ってしまう ('When I drink alcohol, I get drunk quickly.'). ばあいは is used more for situational instructions, like お酒を飲む場合は運転しないほうがいい ('In the event that you drink alcohol, you shouldn't drive.').

synonyms.title: たほうがいい
synonyms.meaning: It'd be better to, Should do
synonyms.comparison: Both たほうがいい and ばいい are used to give suggestions. たほうがいい is a straightforward 'You should…', while ばいい adds a nuance of 'All you have to do is...' or 'Why don't you just...?' Depending on context, ばいい can sound more friendly, but it can also sound a bit pushy. Unlike たほうがいい, ばいい is commonly to be paired with のに to express 'It would be good if (A), but (A) isn't possible).'

antonyms.title: ないほうがいい
antonyms.meaning: It'd be better not to, Shouldn't do
antonyms.comparison: ないほうがいい is used to advise against doing something, similar to 'You shouldn't…'. It's a common expression that reflects the speaker's personal or subjective opinion. In contrast, ばいい suggests doing something, with a nuance like 'All you have to do is...' or 'Why don't you just...?' Unlike ないほうがいい, ばいい is often paired with のに to express 'It would be good if (A), but (A) isn't possible).'

related.title: ことだ
related.meaning: Should, Ought
related.comparison: Both can be used to give advice or suggestions, but the tone differs. ことだ sounds more formal or authoritative, as if stating a rule or guideline. In contrast, ばいい is more casual and conversational, often used among friends. Additionally, ばいい can be used in contrary-to-fact suggestions, such as 行けばいいのに ('You should go but you won't'). ことだ cannot be used this way.

related.title: たらいい・といい
related.meaning: It would be nice if, It would be good if, Should, I hope
related.comparison: All three expressions share the base meaning of 'it would be good if (A)' and can be used to express wishes or give suggestions. たらいい・といい tend to sound stronger or more assertive than ばいい, which feels lighter or less serious in tone. While often interchangeable, in questions like どうすればいい？・どうしたらいい？ ('What should I do?'), といい is not usable.

related.title: といい
related.meaning: I hope, I wish, You should, It would be good
related.comparison: When followed by いい, both ば and と express 'if you do (A), it would be good', but with different nuances. ば conveys uncertainty and is used for unlikely events, e.g. 'It would be nice if (A)', implying that it probably won't happen. と is stronger, often implying hope, e.g. 'I hope (A) happens.' Depending on context, といい can also suggest 'You should (A).'.

related.title: はずだ
related.meaning: Bound to (be), Expected to (be), Should be (able)
related.comparison: はずだ indicates something is expected based on information or logic, as in 行けるはずだ ('I should be able to go.'). ばいい expresses hope or a hypothetical good outcome, like 行ければいい ('It'd be nice if I could go.'). One is based on reasoning, the other on desire.

antonyms.title: ばかりでなく
antonyms.meaning: Not only…but also, As well as
antonyms.comparison: ばかり means 'only' or 'nothing but', emphasizing exclusivity, e.g. 甘いものばかり食べている ('He eats nothing but sweets.'). ばかりでなく means 'not only…but also', adding inclusivity, e.g. 甘いものばかりでなく野菜も食べなさい ('Don't eat only sweets, but vegetables too.').

related.title: だけ
related.meaning: Only, Just
related.comparison: Both ばかり and だけ express limitation, similar to 'only,' but with different nuances. 漫画だけ読んだ means '(He) read only comic books,' while 漫画ばかり読んだ implies '(He) read nothing but comic books,' with a suggestion that other reading would be better. だけ connects to the dictionary form, while ばかり connects to the verb's て form.

related.title: だけでなく(て)～も
related.meaning: Not only ~ but ~ also, Not just ~ but ~ also
related.comparison: ばかり emphasizes exclusivity, meaning 'only' or 'nothing but', e.g. 肉ばかり食べている ('He eats nothing but meat'). だけでなく～も expresses inclusion, meaning 'not only…but also', e.g. 肉だけでなく野菜も食べている ('He eats not only meat but also vegetables'). Compared to だけ, ばかり often carries a stronger nuance of excess or doing something too much.

related.title: ばかりだ
related.meaning: (Only) continue to, Keep on -ing, More and more
related.comparison: ばかり means 'only' or 'just', often suggesting repetition or excess, e.g. 文句ばかり言っている ('He does nothing but complain.'). ばかりだ builds on that to express that a situation continues to worsen or progress in one direction, e.g. 状況は悪くなるばかりだ ('The situation keeps getting worse.').

related.title: だけしか
related.meaning: To emphasize '~only', Nothing but
related.comparison: ばかり and だけしか are similar in that both can express limitation, but ばかり emphasizes the idea of excessiveness of a single thing, e.g. 漫画ばかり読む 'do nothing but read manga', while だけしか emphatically stresses insufficiency, e.g. 千円だけしかない ('I have only 1,000 yen.').

related.title: わ〜わ（で）
related.meaning: And...so, And...because, Since
related.comparison: Both describe a situation dominated by repeated or excessive actions, but the nuance differs. わ〜わ（で） emphasizes noisy, uncontrolled, or emotionally charged repetition, often highlighting disorder or annoyance, as in 子どもたちが泣くわ叫ぶわで大変だった ('The children were crying and shouting nonstop, and it was a mess.'). ばかり focuses on one action happening repeatedly or continuing in the same direction, often with frustration or resignation, as in 彼は文句言ってばかりいる ('He does nothing but complain.').

related.title: ばかりに
related.meaning: Simply because, Only because, Just because, Simply on account of
related.comparison: ばかり means 'only' or 'just', often with a neutral or excessive nuance, e.g. 寝てばかりいる ('He does nothing but sleep'). ばかりに adds a nuance of regret, expressing that something bad happened solely because of one reason, e.g. 寝坊したばかりに電車に乗り遅れた ('I missed the train just because I overslept').

related.title: しか～ない
related.meaning: There's only, Nothing but
related.comparison: ばかり and しか～ない can both mean 'only' or 'nothing but', but their usage and nuance differ. ばかり is used with affirmative verb forms and can imply excess. For example, 漫画ばかり読む 'to read only manga' has a sense of reading manga all the time. In contrast, しか～ない is used with negative verbs and expresses 'nothing but' in a more objective sense. For example, 漫画しか読まない simply states that nothing else is read.

related.title: てくる
related.meaning: To come to, To become, To continue, To be starting to, Has been ~ing
related.comparison: ばかりだ attaches to the dictionary (non-past) form of a verb and is used to show that an action, often a negative one, keeps happening or is the only thing happening. In contrast, てくる combines the て-form of a verb with くる to describe an action that began in the past and has continued to the present. Unlike ばかりだ, てくる can be used for both positive and negative situations.

related.title: つづける
related.meaning: To continue
related.comparison: つづける and ばかりだ both show continuation, but with different nuances. つづける attaches to verb stems and shows simple ongoing action, e.g. 減り続けている 'is continuing to decrease'. ばかりだ adds the sense of 'more and more,' often negatively, e.g. 減るばかりだ 'keeps on decreasing'.

related.title: ばかり
related.meaning: Just, Only, Nothing but
related.comparison: ばかり means 'only' or 'just', often suggesting repetition or excess, e.g. 文句ばかり言っている ('He does nothing but complain.'). ばかりだ builds on that to express that a situation continues to worsen or progress in one direction, e.g. 状況は悪くなるばかりだ ('The situation keeps getting worse.').

related.title: 一方だ
related.meaning: More and more, Continue to, Keep on, -er and -er
related.comparison: ばかりだ and 一方だ are similar in that both express continuous or progressive change. However, ばかりだ is typically used with negative developments and often carries a more subjective tone, e.g. 状況は悪くなるばかりだ ('The situation keeps getting worse'). 一方だ, on the other hand, sounds more neutral or objective, and doesn't necessarily imply a negative trend, e.g. 人口は増える一方だ ('The population keeps increasing').

synonyms.title: のみならず
synonyms.meaning: Not only...but, Not just...but, What is more
synonyms.comparison: ばかりでなく and のみならず are both expressions used to convey the meaning of 'Not only (A)'. While both are formal, のみならず is even more literary. Both patterns can also be followed by noun + も to express 'But also (B)'. For example, 日本語のみならず、韓国語も話せる 'He can speak not only Japanese, but also Korean.' vs. 日本語ばかりでなく、韓国語も話せる (Same meaning, but slightly less literary in tone).

antonyms.title: ばかり
antonyms.comparison: ばかり means 'only' or 'nothing but', emphasizing exclusivity, e.g. 甘いものばかり食べている ('He eats nothing but sweets.'). ばかりでなく means 'not only…but also', adding inclusivity, e.g. 甘いものばかりでなく野菜も食べなさい ('Don't eat only sweets, but vegetables too.').

related.title: だけでなく
related.meaning: Not only… but also
related.comparison: だけでなく and ばかりでなく convey the same essential meaning of 'not only'. Both expressions are often followed by another noun + も, which gives the added meaning of 'not only (A), but also (B)'. The main difference is that ばかりでなく is more formal and appears primarily in written or academic language. In everyday speech, the だけ version is more common, particularly in the variant form だけじゃなくて.

related.title: はもちろん
related.meaning: Not only but also, Let alone, Not to mention, Of course
related.comparison: はもちろん highlights an obvious example together with a more unexpected addition, e.g. 子供はもちろん大人も楽しめる ('Not only kids, but adults too can enjoy it'). ばかりでなく is more neutral, simply listing two things, e.g. 雨ばかりでなく雪も降った ('It not only rained, but also snowed').

related.title: をはじめ
related.meaning: Not only...but also..., Starting with
related.comparison: ばかりでなく and をはじめ both introduce additional items, but their focus differs. ばかりでなく is neutral and treats both elements as equal, e.g. 彼は英語ばかりでなく日本語も話せる ('He speaks not only English but also Japanese'). をはじめ highlights the first item as the most representative, e.g. 校長先生をはじめ、先生たちが全員参加した ('Starting with the principal, all the teachers joined').

related.title: に限らず
related.meaning: Not just, Not only… but also
related.comparison: Both are fairly formal patterns which convey that something applies to more than one item, but they differ in nuance. ばかりでなく is a relatively straightforward 'Not only (A), but also (B)', e.g. 日本語ばかりでなく、韓国語も話せる 'He can speak not only Japanese, but also Korean.' In contrast, に限らず has a more specific nuance of 'Not limited to (A)', e.g. 学生に限らず誰でも入場できます 'Not limited to students, anyone can enter.'

related.title: に加えて
related.meaning: Not only...but also, In addition, Not to mention
related.comparison: Both are formal patterns which add new information or elements, but they differ in nuance. ばかりでなく is a relatively straightforward 'Not only (A), but also (B)', e.g. 日本語ばかりでなく、韓国語も話せる 'He can speak not only Japanese, but also Korean.' In contrast, に加えて has a more specific nuance of 'In addition to (A)', e.g. 日本語に加えて、文学も勉強することになった 'In addition to Japanese, I'll also study literature.'

related.title: だけでなく(て)～も
related.meaning: Not only ~ but ~ also, Not just ~ but ~ also
related.comparison: ばかりでなく and だけでなく(て)～も are nearly identical in meaning, both meaning 'Not only (A), but also (B)'. ばかりでなく is the more formal expression, while だけでなく (especially in the alternate form だけじゃなくて) is more common in everyday speech. For example, 日本語ばかりでなく、韓国語も話せる 'He can speak not only Japanese, but also Korean.' vs. 日本語だけじゃなくて、韓国語も話せる (Same meaning, but more casual in tone).

related.title: はもとより
related.meaning: Not only... but also, Let alone, Of course
related.comparison: Both formally express that something applies to more than one item, with different nuance. ばかりでなく is a neutral 'Not only (A), but also (B)', e.g. 日本語ばかりでなく、韓国語も話せる 'He can speak not only Japanese, but also Korean.' はもとより is more literary in tone, and implies that the first item is obvious and the second more noteworthy, e.g. 親はもとより先生にも感謝しています 'I'm grateful not only to my parents, but also to my teachers.'

related.title: からといって
related.meaning: Just because
related.comparison: They may seem similar because both can be translated as 'just because', but mean very different things. ばかりに expresses, often with regret, a negative outcome caused by a single reason, e.g. 一回遅刻したばかりにクビになった ('I was fired just because I was late once.'). In contrast, からといって means 'just because (A), it doesn't mean (B)', e.g. お金持ちだからといって幸せとは限らない ('Just because someone is rich doesn't mean they're happy.').

related.title: につき
related.meaning: Due to, On account of, Each or every
related.comparison: These expressions are loosely related in that both indicate a reason. ばかりに conveys that a specific cause led to a negative outcome, often with a nuance of regret, e.g. 忘れ物をしたばかりに受験できなかった ('Just because I forgot something, I couldn't take the exam'). につき, by contrast, is a formal, neutral expression often used in official contexts, e.g. 工事中につき通行止め ('Road closed due to construction').

related.title: ばかり
related.meaning: Just, Only, Nothing but
related.comparison: ばかり means 'only' or 'just', often with a neutral or excessive nuance, e.g. 寝てばかりいる ('He does nothing but sleep'). ばかりに adds a nuance of regret, expressing that something bad happened solely because of one reason, e.g. 寝坊したばかりに電車に乗り遅れた ('I missed the train just because I overslept').

synonyms.title: からこそ
synonyms.comparison: Despite appearing different, ～ばこそ and からこそ are essentially synonymous in meaning, both expressing an reason for something, i.e. 'it is precisely because of (A) that (B)'. The main difference is that ～ばこそ is a highly formal and literary expression found primarily in writing, while からこそ is far more common and used in everyday speech as well as writing.

related.title: ～てこそ
related.meaning: Only if, Because, By, Unless, Until
related.comparison: Both use the emphatic こそ but differ by verb form. ～ばこそ is conditional, meaning only if (A), then (B), as in 忙しければこそ 'precisely because you're busy'. ～てこそ focuses on completion, meaning only after (A) happens, then (B), as in 苦労してこそ成功がある ('Only after hardship comes success'). ～ばこそ can introduce a hypothetical condition, while ～てこそ emphasizes the actual realization of (A).

related.title: てよかった
related.meaning: I'm glad that
related.comparison: Though they may look similar, てよかった and ばよかった express opposite feelings. てよかった conveys relief or happiness about something that happened, e.g. 行ってよかった ('I'm glad I went.' or literally 'It was good that I went.'). In contrast, ばよかった expresses regret about something that didn't happen, e.g., 行けばよかった ('I wish I had gone.' or literally, 'It would have been nice if I went.').

related.title: たらいい・といい
related.meaning: It would be nice if, It would be good if, Should, I hope
related.comparison: ばよかった and たらいい・といい are similar in that both express the speaker's wish or hope. ばよかった is used when expressing regret over something that happened (or didn't happen) in the past, e.g. 行けばよかった ('I wish I had gone.') which implies 'but I didn't'. In contrast, たらいい・といい typically expresses something the speaker hopes will happen in the future, e.g. 明日晴れるといいけど ('I hope it's sunny tomorrow.').

antonyms.title: ことはない
antonyms.meaning: There is no need to, Never happens
antonyms.comparison: These forms are opposites in meaning. ひつようがある shows something must be done, as in 行く必要がある ('You need to go.'). ことはない reassures that something doesn't need to be done, as in 行くことはない ('You don't need to go.'). One expresses necessity, the other expresses lack of it.

related.title: がひつよう
related.meaning: Need, Necessary
related.comparison: Both expressions use 必要 'necessary' to show that something is needed. が必要 attaches to nouns or nominalized verbs, as in 金が必要 ('Money is necessary.') or 覚えることが必要 ('It's necessary to memorize.'). 必要がある connects only to verbs and uses the form 覚える必要がある ('There is a need to memorize.'), which means essentially the same thing but fits more naturally with verb phrases.

related.title: めく・めいた
related.meaning: Like, To show signs of,  To have the appearance of, To come to a (state) similar to, Somewhat, An air of,  Feels almost like
related.comparison: Both describe something beginning to show a certain quality, but the nuance differs. めく focuses on a noticeable change in atmosphere or appearance, often used for seasons or general impressions, as in 秋めいてきた ('It has started to feel autumn-like.'). びる emphasizes the emergence of a specific visible or behavioral trait, often something concrete or character-related, as in 大人びた話し方をする ('He speaks in a grown-up way.').

synonyms.title: という風に
synonyms.meaning: As if to say, As if to suggest that, In such a way that, Things like
synonyms.comparison: Both mean in the way of, but differ in usage. ふうに often attaches to demonstratives like こんなふうに (in this way). という風に follows whole phrases, as in すべて分かっているというふうに (as if they knew everything), and can sound slightly more indirect.

related.title: かのようだ
related.meaning: As if, Just like
related.comparison: Both express resemblance, but with different nuance. かのようだ is used at sentence-end to compare to a hypothetical or exaggerated situation, as in 夢かのようだ (It’s like a dream). ふうに is more neutral and connective, describing manner or appearance, as in こんなふうに書く (Write it like this).

related.title: 如何
related.meaning: Depending on
related.comparison: 如何 indicates that something depends on conditions, often formal, as in 成否は努力いかんだ (Success depends on effort). ふうに describes the manner or style of something, often casually, as in 彼は変わったふうに話す (He speaks in a strange way).

related.title: のように・のような
related.meaning: Like (Noun), Similar to (Noun)
related.comparison: のように・のような and ふうに both express similarity, but with different usage and nuance. のように・のような follows nouns, e.g. 春のような天気 'spring-like weather', and expresses a general comparison. ふうに, on the other hand, typically follows verbs or words like こんな and そんな, implying 'in a way' or 'giving the feel of,' e.g. こんなふうに話す 'to speak in this way'.

related.title: ように・ような
related.meaning: As・like, Just like
related.comparison: ふうに and ように・ような both express similarity or comparison, but their nuance differs. ふうに suggests appearing a certain way or having a certain style (literally, a certain 'air'). In contrast, ように・ような implies a more direct comparison or resemblance. Their usage also differs: ふうに is followed by verbs or phrases, while ように connects to phrases and ような to nouns.

related.title: っぽい
related.meaning: -ish, -like, Characteristic of, Typical of, Tendency to
related.comparison: Both ふりをする and っぽい describe imitation or resemblance, but with a key difference in nuance. ふりをする implies pretending to be or do something one is not, as in 知ったふりをする ('pretend to know'). In contrast, っぽい conveys a natural resemblance or tendency, often unconscious or inherent, as in 子供っぽい ('childish').

related.title: てはいけない
related.meaning: Must not, May not
related.comparison: べからず and てはいけない are similar in that they both express prohibition, or the idea that you 'must not' or 'cannot' do something. The difference is in formality and how they connect to other words. べからず is a highly formal and archaic expression that nowadays is found mostly in regulations, proverbs, and so forth. It follows directly after the plain form of a verb, e.g. 入るべからず ('Do not enter.'). In contrast, てはいけない is far more common in the modern language, and connects to the て-form of verbs, e.g. 入ってはいけない ('You must not enter.')

related.title: てはならない
related.meaning: Must not, Cannot, Should not
related.comparison: Both indicate prohibition. べからず is old-fashioned and literary, often seen on signs or notices, as in 立入るべからず (Do not enter). てはならない is the standard modern form, still formal but usable in both speech and writing, as in 入ってはならない (You must not enter).

related.title: へいく
related.meaning: To go to, To head toward
related.comparison: The へ in both patterns is the same directional particle. While it's commonly used with いく to indicate going somewhere or moving in a direction (e.g., 学校へいく, 西へいく), it can also be used with other verbs (e.g., 家へ帰る, 公園へ歩く) or in certain phrases (e.g., 東京へようこそ！). In all cases, へ expresses directionality or movement toward a location.

related.title: に向かって・に向けて
related.meaning: Towards, To face, To head to
related.comparison: The particle へ indicates direction or destination, similar to 'to' or 'toward' in English. に向かって and に向けて emphasize direction rather than destination, unlike へ, which can also imply the final goal. For example, 学校へ行く means 'go to school,' while 学校に向かって走る means 'run toward the school.'

antonyms.title: くる
antonyms.comparison: くる and いく are both movement verbs often used with directional particles to express moving toward a particular location. The difference lies in the perspective of the speaker and directionality: くる typically expresses movement toward the speaker's location, while いく expresses movement away from the speaker's location.

related.title: へ
related.meaning: To, Toward
related.comparison: The へ in both patterns is the same directional particle. While it's commonly used with いく to indicate going somewhere or moving in a direction (e.g., 学校へいく, 西へいく), it can also be used with other verbs (e.g., 家へ帰る, 公園へ歩く) or in certain phrases (e.g., 東京へようこそ！). In all cases, へ expresses directionality or movement toward a location.

related.title: ていく
related.meaning: To ~ and go, To go on, To start
related.comparison: The verb いく expresses movement in a direction, literally or figuratively. へいく is used after nouns to indicate moving toward a place (e.g., 学校へいく = 'go to school'). When used as ていく (Verb + て + いく), it implies a continuing action or change moving forward. Note that いく is written in kanji as 行く when used alone, but typically in hiragana in the ていく form.

related.title: Verb + にいく
related.meaning: To go ~, To go in order to ~
related.comparison: へいく is used with nouns, typically physical locations or directions, to express 'going' or 'heading' somewhere (e.g., 学校へいく, 西へいく). へ can be replaced with に, which is more colloquial and emphasizes the final destination. にいく can also combine with verb stems to express going to perform an action (e.g., 食べにいく = 'go to eat'). The two patterns can be combined as well (e.g., 店へ食べにいく = 'go to the store to eat').

related.title: いらっしゃる
related.meaning: To be (Honorific), To come (Honorific), To go (Honorific)
related.comparison: Both いらっしゃる and いく express movement away from the speaker, similar to the English 'go.' The key difference is politeness: いく is a neutral verb used in everyday contexts, while いらっしゃる is a polite, honorific verb used to show respect for someone's movement. Note that いらっしゃる also serves as the honorific form of いる, くる, and いく.

synonyms.title: たほうがいい
synonyms.meaning: It'd be better to, Should do
synonyms.comparison: たほうがいい is used to give advice or make a suggestion, often expressing the speaker's subjective opinion on what they believe would be better. It's commonly used in everyday conversation. べき has a similar meaning, but is more formal and has a stronger nuance of expectation about what 'should' be done, rather than a personal opinion.

antonyms.title: べきではない
antonyms.meaning: Should not, Must not , Ought not to
antonyms.comparison: べき expresses strong obligation or moral judgment, as in doing something because it is right or proper, e.g. 約束は守るべきだ ('You should keep your promises.') べきではない is the negative form, used to advise against actions considered wrong or inappropriate, e.g. 人を見た目で判断すべきではない ('You shouldn't judge people by appearances.')

antonyms.title: ないほうがいい
antonyms.meaning: It'd be better not to, Shouldn't do
antonyms.comparison: ないほうがいい is used to advise against doing something, similar 'You shouldn't…' in English. It is a common expression that reflects the speaker's personal or subjective opinion. べき has the opposite meaning ('You should,') and is more formal. It carries the nuance of stating what 'ought to' be done based on social norms, rather than giving personal judgment or advice.

related.title: べく
related.meaning: For the purpose of, In order to, To ensure
related.comparison: Both expressions are similar in that they are both derived from the classical Japanese べし and relate to expectations, but they differ in precise meaning. べく typically expresses an action performed for a particular purpose, e.g. 上達すべく毎日練習しています ('I practice every day in hopes of getting better.'), while べき expresses a societal expectation in the sense of saying that something is how it 'should' be, e.g. 言いたいことがある時は本人に言うべきです ('When you have something to say, you should tell the person concerned.')

related.title: ないと
related.meaning: Must, Have to
related.comparison: ないと and べき both express a sense of necessity, but with different nuances. ないと focuses on immediate or practical obligation, like 行かないと ('I've got to go.'). べき expresses moral or logical duty, as in 行くべきだ ('You should go.').

related.title: ことだ
related.meaning: Should, Ought
related.comparison: Both express obligation, but differ in tone. ことだ is used to offer advice or general suggestions, e.g. 無理をしないことだ ('You should take it easy.') べき is stronger, implying moral or logical necessity, e.g. 約束は守るべきだ ('You should keep your promises.')

related.title: ものだ
related.meaning: Supposed to, Should, That's the way it is
related.comparison: Both express what one 'should' do, but with different nuance. べき reflects personal or moral judgment, e.g. 約束は守るべきだ ('You should keep your promises.') ものだ conveys social or cultural expectations, e.g. 子どもは早く寝るものだ ('Children are supposed to go to bed early.')

related.title: はずだ
related.meaning: Bound to (be), Expected to (be), Should be (able)
related.comparison: はずだ and べき can both be translated as 'should', but they differ in nuance. はずだ expresses an expectation based on evidence or reasoning, like 明日来るはずだ ('He should come tomorrow.'). In contrast, べき expresses obligation or social correctness, as in 来るべきだ ('You should come.').

antonyms.title: べき
antonyms.comparison: べき expresses strong obligation or moral judgment, as in doing something because it is right or proper, e.g. 約束は守るべきだ ('You should keep your promises.') べきではない is the negative form, used to advise against actions considered wrong or inappropriate, e.g. 人を見た目で判断すべきではない ('You shouldn't judge people by appearances.')

antonyms.title: ことだ
antonyms.comparison: ことだ suggests what one should do as advice or a general guideline, e.g. 無理をしないことだ ('You should take it easy.') べきではない expresses the opposite, indicating what one should not do based on moral or social expectations, e.g. 人を見た目で判断すべきではない ('You shouldn't judge people by appearances.')

antonyms.title: ものだ
antonyms.meaning: Supposed to, Should, That's the way it is
antonyms.comparison: ものだ expresses general or societal expectations, e.g. 子どもは早く寝るものだ ('Children are supposed to go to bed early.') べきではない carries the opposite nuance, conveying that one should not do something based on moral or social reasoning, e.g. 人を見た目で判断すべきではない ('You shouldn't judge people by appearances.')

antonyms.title: はずだ
antonyms.meaning: Bound to (be), Expected to (be), Should be (able)
antonyms.comparison: はずだ expresses an assumption or expectation based on reason or context, e.g. 雨が降るはずだ ('It should rain.'). In contrast, べきではない expresses a strong sense of moral or social obligation not to do something, e.g. 嘘をつくべきではない ('You shouldn't lie.').

antonyms.title: なければいけない
antonyms.comparison: These express contrasting meanings: なければいけない 'must' vs. べきではない 'should not'. The former implies necessity or rule, while the latter carries a tone of moral or social advice, e.g. 行かなければいけない ('You must go.') vs. 行くべきではない ('You shouldn't go.').

antonyms.title: たほうがいい
antonyms.meaning: It'd be better to, Should do
antonyms.comparison: たほうがいい is used to give advice or make a suggestion, often expressing the speaker's subjective opinion on what they believe would be better. It's commonly used in everyday conversation. In contrast, べきではない has the opposite meaning and is more formal. It conveys an expectation or rule about what 'should not' be done, rather than a personal opinion.

antonyms.title: なくちゃ・なきゃ
antonyms.comparison: なくちゃ and なきゃ are casual ways to express obligation, similar to 'I need to…' or 'I have to…' in English. In contrast, べきではない has the opposite meaning—'should not' or 'ought not'—and is more formal. It conveys a sense of societal expectation or what is considered proper, rather than reflecting the speaker's immediate or personal needs.

antonyms.title: なければならない
antonyms.comparison: These are opposites: なければならない 'must' vs. べきではない 'should not'. The former suggests a rule or necessity; the latter implies social or moral advice, e.g. 行かなければならない ('You must go.') as compared to 行くべきではない ('You ought not to / shouldn't go.').

related.title: ～と言っても
related.meaning: Although ~ might say that ~
related.comparison: ～と言っても typically qualifies or corrects an impression based on a previous statement, while ～というのは explains or defines something more directly. For example, 忙しいと言っても一日中じゃない ('I say I'm busy, but not all day') vs. 留学というのは海外の学校で勉強することです ('What we call study abroad means studying at a school in a foreign country').

related.title: ないほうがいい
related.meaning: It'd be better not to, Shouldn't do
related.comparison: ないほうがいい is used to advise or suggest not doing something, similar to 'You shouldn't…' It is a common expression that reflects the speaker's personal or subjective opinion. べきではない can carry a similar meaning but is more formal, with a nuance of stating what one 'ought not to' or 'should not' do based on social norms or what is considered proper, rather than personal advice.

related.title: てはいけない
related.meaning: Must not, May not
related.comparison: てはいけない expresses prohibition, meaning something 'must not' or 'cannot' be done. In contrast, べきではない suggests that something 'should not' be done, often implying it's improper or that there's a better option. While both express negative recommendations, てはいけない is more authoritative and used for rules, whereas べきではない carries a more subjective nuance.

related.title: べき
related.meaning: Must, Should , Ought to
related.comparison: Both expressions are similar in that they are both derived from the classical Japanese べし and relate to expectations, but they differ in precise meaning. べく typically expresses an action performed for a particular purpose, e.g. 上達すべく毎日練習しています ('I practice every day in hopes of getting better.'), while べき expresses a societal expectation in the sense of saying that something is how it 'should' be, e.g. 言いたいことがある時は本人に言うべきです ('When you have something to say, you should tell the person concerned.')

related.title: ことだし
related.meaning: (And) so, Because, Since
related.comparison: Both suggest that a result follows naturally, but the viewpoint is different. ことだし presents a reason as a settled circumstance that makes a decision reasonable at the moment, often used for gentle justification or proposals, as in 雨も降っていることだし、今日は家にいよう ('Since it’s raining anyway, let’s stay home today.'). べくして is a formal, written expression meaning 'bound to' or 'inevitably,' describing an outcome that follows naturally from prior conditions, often with hindsight, as in 彼は失敗すべくして失敗した ('He failed as was inevitable.').

related.title: らしい ②
related.meaning: typical of, -ish・like, appropriate for, becoming of, befitting
related.comparison: Both relate actions to what is natural or fitting, but with different nuance. らしく means 'in a way typical of' or 'true to,' judging whether behavior matches a person’s character or role, as in 彼は彼らしく行動した ('He acted in a way true to himself.'). べくして does not evaluate character but inevitability, stating that an outcome was the natural result of circumstances or causes, as in 事故は起こるべくして起こった ('The accident happened as was inevitable.').

related.title: いがい
related.meaning: Except・besides, Other than, With the exception of
related.comparison: Both forms can express the idea of 'other than', but their nuance and usage differ slightly. いがい emphasizes exceptions, like 'except for' or 'other than,' and attaches directly after nouns, e.g. バナナ以外の果物 means 'fruits other than bananas.' In contrast, ほかに highlights the presence of alternatives, takes の when linking to nouns, e.g. バナナの他に 'other than bananas', and can also start a sentence or phrase.

related.title: をおいてほかに〜ない
related.meaning: No...but, Nothing else but, None other than
related.comparison: Both use ほか (他, other) to express contrast or exclusivity. ほかに(も) and ほか(に)は mean 'other than' and are used to compare or list alternatives. をおいてほかに〜ない is a stronger expression meaning 'no one/nothing else but', as in 彼をおいてほかに適任者はいない ('There's no one more suitable than him.').

related.title: めったに〜ない
related.meaning: Hardly, Rarely, Seldom
related.comparison: めったに〜ない and ほとんど can be used similarly, but with key differences. めったに〜ない means 'rarely', much like ほとんど with a negative verb. For example, めったに行かない ('I rarely go.') and ほとんど行かない ('I almost never go.') are close, though めったに suggests even lower frequency. Unlike めったに, ほとんど can also be positive, as in ほとんどみんな 'almost everyone'

related.title: だいたい
related.meaning: Most・mostly, Adequately・generally, For the most part, Roughly・approximately, In the first place
related.comparison: Both だいたい and ほとんど can express 'most' or 'almost all,' but they also have distinct uses. For example, だいたいの人 and ほとんどの人 both mean 'most people', and だいたい一緒 and ほとんど一緒 both mean 'almost the same'. However, they diverge when ほとんど is used negatively as in ほとんどない 'almost none', as well as when だいたい means 'generally' or 'approximately' (だいたい５時に起きる), as ほとんど cannot be used here.

synonyms.title: くらい ①
synonyms.comparison: くらい (often ぐらい in speech) expresses an approximate degree or extent. It's often used with number + counter expressions (the くらい① form), as in 10分くらいかかった = 'It took about ten minutes.' ほど can be used similarly, but is more formal and carries the nuance of an upper limit—e.g., 10分ほど implies 'about, but no more than ten minutes.'

related.title: ほど～ない
related.meaning: Is not as…as, Isn't... like..., Not to the extent of...
related.comparison: ほど (sometimes seen in kanji as 程) has the basic meaning of 'extent'. ほど〜ない is used for comparisons meaning 'not as (A) as (B)', e.g. 彼ほど背が高くない ('I'm not as tall as him.'). When used alone, ほど means 'so much that (A)' or 'to the extent of (A)', e.g. 驚くほど静かだった ('It was quiet to a surprising degree.' or 'It was so quiet that I was surprised.').

related.title: くらい ②
related.meaning: To the extent that, So…that, About the only thing that...
related.comparison: Both くらい and ほど can express extent in the sense of 'so much that (A)' or 'to the extent of (A)', and are often interchangeable, e.g. 驚くくらい静かだった / 驚くほど静かだった ('It was quiet to a surprising degree.') くらい (also written as ぐらい) tends to sound slightly more colloquial, and each word has other uses where they may not be interchangeable.

related.title: は～くらいです
related.meaning: About the only, To the extent that
related.comparison: Both relate to expressing extent, but with different emphasis. は～くらいです highlights something as the only or most notable case, like 'about the only (A) is (B)', e.g. できるのは田中さんくらいです ('Mr. Tanaka's about the only person who can do it.') ほど expresses extent or intensity, e.g. 驚くほど静かだった ('It was quiet to a surprising degree.')

related.title: まで
related.meaning: Even, To even, To the extent of
related.comparison: まで and ほど both mean 'to the extent of,' but with different uses. まで emphasizes inclusion or a surprising limit, e.g. 骨まで食べた ('He even ate the bones.'). ほど means 'so much that' and often describes degree or intensity, e.g. 痛いほど分かる ('I understand to the point that it hurts.').

related.title: ば〜ほど
related.meaning: The more…the more
related.comparison: ほど (sometimes written in kanji as 程) has the basic meaning of 'extent'. ば〜ほど shows a proportional relationship, similar to 'the more (A), the more (B)', e.g. 練習すればするほど上手になる ('The more you practice, the better you get.') When used alone, ほど means 'so much that (A)' or 'to the extent of (A)', e.g. 驚くほど静かだった ('It was quiet to a surprising degree.' / 'It was so quiet that I was surprised.')

related.title: ほど
related.meaning: The more, About, To the extent that, So much… that
related.comparison: ほど (sometimes seen in kanji as 程) has the basic meaning of 'extent'. ほど〜ない is used for comparisons meaning 'not as (A) as (B)', e.g. 彼ほど背が高くない ('I'm not as tall as him.'). When used alone, ほど means 'so much that (A)' or 'to the extent of (A)', e.g. 驚くほど静かだった ('It was quiet to a surprising degree.' or 'It was so quiet that I was surprised.').

related.title: まで
related.meaning: Even, To even, To the extent of
related.comparison: Both grammar points share the idea of 'to the extent of,' but are used differently. ほど～ない makes negative comparisons, e.g. ジョンはトムほど強くない ('John isn't as strong as Tom.'). まで emphasizes something going beyond expectations, e.g. 骨まで食べた ('He even ate the bones.').

related.title: より
related.meaning: More than, Over
related.comparison: Both expressions are used for comparisons. ほど～ない makes negative comparisons, e.g. ジョンはトムほど強くない ('John isn't as strong as Tom.'). より is used for positive comparisons, e.g. トムはジョンより強い ('Tom is stronger than John.'). Note that ほど follows the greater of the two options, while より follows the lesser.

related.title: より～のほうが
related.meaning: To be more ~ than ~
related.comparison: Both ほど～ない and より～のほうが express comparisons, but from different angles. ほど～ない is a negative comparison, meaning 'not as...as' (e.g. Xほどおいしくない = 'not as tasty as X'). より～のほうが is a positive comparison, meaning 'more...than' (e.g. AよりBのほうがおいしい = 'B is tastier than A').

antonyms.title: だろう
antonyms.comparison: まい and だろう are both used to express speculation, but they contrast in polarity. だろう indicates positive speculation or likelihood, as in 雨が降るだろう (It will probably rain), while まい expresses strong negative speculation or denial, as in 雨は降るまい (It probably won’t rain).

antonyms.title: Verb[よう]
antonyms.comparison: まい and the volitional form (〜よう) both express the speaker’s intent or decision, but in opposite directions. 〜よう indicates intention or willingness to do something, as in 行こう (Let’s go), while まい expresses a strong negative intention or refusal, as in 行くまい (I will not go).3

related.title: ともなく・ともなしに
related.meaning: Absentmindedly, Mindlessly, Unconsciously, Without paying attention
related.comparison: Though these two phrases appear similar in form, both suggesting 'without doing', they differ in that ともなく・ともなしに is used figuratively to express that the action was performed, but in an absentminded manner, or ‘without even so much as the intention of doing (A), (B)’ e.g. 見るともなく見ていたら 'as I was absentmindedly looking...', whereas ないで literally means that action was not performed, e.g. 見ずにやる 'do without looking'.

related.title: じゃあるまいし
related.meaning: You are not ~ so..., it isn't ~ so...
related.comparison: まい was previously introduced with verbs as a condensed (and somewhat more formal) way of saying ないだろう. This is that same まい used with the negative form じゃない following nouns, and combined with し to list reasons, resulting in a phrase meaning 'You're/It's not (A), so...' , e.g. 子どもじゃあるまいし  ('You're not a child, so...').

related.title: ように・ような
related.meaning: As・like, Just like
related.comparison: These expressions are related through the ように in まい～のように, which is the same as in ように・ような, both expressing comparison. まい～のように is used with 'every~' time expressions to mean 'almost every (A)', e.g. 毎日のように 'almost every day'. In contrast, ように・ような is used for general comparisons, e.g. 春のように暖かい ('It's warm like spring.').

related.title: ごとに
related.meaning: Each, Every
related.comparison: Both phrases express frequency but differ in usage. ごとに attaches to time expressions, nouns, or verbs to mean 'every (A),' e.g. ３時間ごとに 'every three hours' or 失敗するごとに 'every time (you) fail'. まい～のように is used only with time words that take 毎 and adds emphasis, meaning 'almost every (A)', e.g. 毎日のように 'nearly every day'.

related.title: たびに
related.meaning: Each time, Every time, Whenever
related.comparison: Both まい～のように and たびに express frequency and the idea of 'every,' but with different nuances. まい～のように is used with 'every~' time expressions to mean 'almost every (A)', e.g. 毎日のように 'almost every day'. In contrast, たびに follows verbs or noun phrases to mean 'every time (A)', e.g. 彼に会うたびに 'every time I see him' or 旅行のたびに 'every time I take a trip'.

related.title: おきに
related.meaning: Repeated at intervals, Every other, Every, At intervals of
related.comparison: Both まい～のように and おきに express frequency, but in different ways. まい～のように is used with 'every~' time expressions to mean 'almost every (A)', e.g. 毎日のように 'almost every day'. In contrast, おきに attaches to a number + counter expression to mean 'at intervals of (A),' e.g. 一日おきに 'every other day' or 'at intervals of one day'.

related.title: につき
related.meaning: Due to, On account of, Each or every
related.comparison: まい～のように and につき both express frequency, but in different ways. まい～のように is used with 'every~' time expressions to mean 'almost every (A)', e.g. 毎日のように 'almost every day'. In contrast, につき follows time/quantity expressions to mean 'each' or 'every,' e.g. 30分につき 'for every 30 minutes'. につき can also mean 'due to', which is unrelated to frequency.

antonyms.title: あとで
antonyms.comparison: あとで and まえに are both used to describe the timing of actions, but with opposite meanings. まえに usually follows a plain (non-past) verb and means 'before doing A' (e.g., 食べる前に = 'before eating'). In contrast, あとで typically follows the past tense form of a verb and means 'after doing B' (e.g., 食べたあとで = 'after eating').

related.title: ないうちに
related.meaning: Before it becomes, Before ~ occurs, Without
related.comparison: ないうちに and まえに both mean doing something before another event, but with different nuances. まえに simply marks one action happening before another. ないうちに adds the sense of doing something while there's still time. For example, 暗くならないうちに帰ろう means 'Let's go home before it gets dark,' with the nuance that it's better to leave while it's still light out.

related.title: 前者は・後者は
related.meaning: The former ~ the latter, The first ~ the second
related.comparison: まえに is used to express the relative timing of actions and often follows a plain (non-past) verb to mean 'before doing A.' In contrast, 前者 (the former) and 後者 (the latter) refer to things previously mentioned. They're used to avoid repeating a word or phrase, similar to how we use 'the former' and 'the latter' in English.

related.title: 手前
related.meaning: Before, In front of, From one's standpoint or side
related.comparison: Both まえに and 手前 can express a meaning similar to ''before' or 'in front of' in English. The difference is that まえに simply refers to happening earlier in time, or being physically located in front of something else. In contrast, 手前 adds nuance, often implying that, because one is faced with a certain situation (A), a particular action or outcome (B) follows as a result.

related.title: わ〜わ（で）
related.meaning: And...so, And...because, Since
related.comparison: Both express excess, but they highlight different things. わ〜わ（で） describes a chaotic situation where multiple actions or states occur together, emphasizing the overall atmosphere or commotion, as in 子どもたちが泣くわ騒ぐわで大変だった ('The kids were crying and making noise, and it was chaos.'). まくる focuses on a single action being done repeatedly or excessively by the subject, emphasizing behavior rather than atmosphere, as in 文句を言いまくる ('He keeps complaining over and over.').

related.title: ～ましょうか
related.meaning: Shall we, Shall I
related.comparison: Both ～ましょうか and ましょう are polite expressions used to suggest or propose an action. ましょう is more assertive, similar to 'Let's (do this)' in English. In contrast, ～ましょうか is softer and seeks the listener's approval or agreement, like 'Shall I...?' or 'Shall we...?' ましょう can also function as a gentle command, such as when a teacher says 'Let's be quiet' to mean 'Be quiet.'

related.title: ませんか
related.meaning: Won't you, Would you not, Why don't we
