related.comparison: ましょう and ませんか are both polite expressions based on the ます form that can be used to make suggestions or invitations. ましょう is more assertive and usually implies the speaker and listener will do something together (e.g., 食べましょう = 'Let's eat'). In contrast, ませんか is more of an invitation directed at the listener (e.g., 食べませんか = 'Won't you eat [some]?').

related.title: Verb[よう]
related.meaning: Let's, I shall (Casual volitional)
related.comparison: ましょう and Verb[よう] are both volitional forms that mean 'Let's (do something).' The difference is tone: ましょう is polite, while Verb[よう] is casual. For example, 行きましょう (polite) and 行こう (casual) both mean 'Let's go.' The casual Verb[よう] form is also used in other grammar patterns to express intent, such as 行こうと思う ('I think I'll go' / 'I'm planning to go').

related.title: とは
related.meaning: That..., (Surprised) that..., (Never expected) that..., (Shocked) that...
related.comparison: Both expressions can be used to convey shock and surprise, though how they function in a sentence differs. まさか precedes whatever the speaker is surprised about, e.g. まさか勝てるなんて ('To think they could win!'), or even on its own as an interjection, i.e. まさか！ ('No way!). In contrast, とは always follows what the speaker is surprised about, and can pair with まさか for further emphasis, e.g. まさか勝てるとは (same meaning as the above example).

related.title: とは
related.meaning: That..., (Surprised) that..., (Never expected) that..., (Shocked) that...
related.comparison: まさか and とは are both used to express surprise, but function in different ways. まさか comes at the beginning of a sentence to emphasize that what follows goes against the speaker's expectations, while とは appears at the end of a sentence or phrase to express the same. Because they occupy different positions, they are often used together. For example, まさか勝てるとは！ ('To think we could win!' / 'I never thought we could win').

related.title: なんか・なんて
related.meaning: Such as, Things like, Emphasis
related.comparison: まさか and なんか・なんて are both used to express surprise, but function in different ways. まさか comes at the beginning of a sentence to emphasize that what follows goes against the speaker's expectations, while なんて appears at the end of a sentence or phrase to express the same. Because they fill different roles, they are often used together. For example, まさか勝てるなんて！ ('To think we could win!' / 'I never thought we could win').

related.title: どんどん
related.meaning: Progressively, Rapidly increasing, More and more
related.comparison: どんどん and ますます are both adverbs used to describe change over time. どんどん emphasizes rapid progress or sudden development, often in leaps and bounds. ますます focuses on something already in a certain state becoming even more so, and is often used in polite expressions like wishes for continued health or success.

related.title: 一方だ
related.meaning: More and more, Continue to, Keep on, -er and -er
related.comparison: Both 一方だ and ますます express progressive change, but differ in usage. 一方だ follows verbs, often in formal or objective contexts to describe trends, e.g. '物価は上がる一方だ' ('Prices keep rising'). ますます has a similar nuance, but precedes the phrase or sentence, e.g. '彼の人気はますます高まっている' ('His popularity is increasing more and more').

related.title: ば〜ほど
related.meaning: The more…the more
related.comparison: They are similar in that both describe change, but ば〜ほど specifically expresses a proportional relationship between two things, like the English 'the more (A), the more (B)', e.g. 見れば見るほど好きになる ('The more I look, the more I like it.'). In contrast, ますます simply emphasizes continued intensification, e.g. 彼の人気はますます高まっている ('His popularity is steadily rising.')

related.title: 更に
related.meaning: Even more or further, Again, More and more
related.comparison: 更に and ますます both suggest addition or progression, but with different nuances. 更に emphasizes adding something on top of what already exists, e.g. '更に三人が参加した' ('Three more people joined'). ますます highlights a state intensifying over time, e.g. 'ますます寒くなってきた' ('It's gotten colder and colder').

related.title: ましょう
related.meaning: Let's, Shall we (Polite volitional)
related.comparison: Both ～ましょうか and ましょう are polite expressions used to suggest or propose an action. ましょう is more assertive, similar to 'Let's (do this)' in English. In contrast, ～ましょうか is softer and seeks the listener's approval or agreement, like 'Shall I...?' or 'Shall we...?' ましょう can also function as a gentle command, such as when a teacher says 'Let's be quiet' to mean 'Be quiet.'

related.title: Verb[よう]
related.meaning: Let's, I shall (Casual volitional)
related.comparison: ましょうか and Verb [よう] are both volitional forms, with ましょうか being polite and Verb [よう] (sometimes with か) casual. ～ましょうか is used to make polite suggestions like 'Shall we...?' (e.g. 行きましょうか = 'Shall we go?') 行こうか is more casual and introspective, often expressing the speaker's internal thoughts (e.g. 'Should I go?')

related.title: ませんか
related.meaning: Won't you, Would you not, Why don't we
related.comparison: ましょう and ませんか are both polite forms based on the ます form and used to make suggestions or invitations. ましょう typically suggests doing something together (e.g. 食べましょう = 'Let's eat'), while ませんか invites the listener to join (e.g. 食べませんか = 'Won't you eat [some]?') ましょう is more assertive, while ませんか is softer and more inviting.

related.title: はずがない
related.meaning: Hardly possible, Cannot be, Highly unlikely, Improbable
related.comparison: Both express strong denial, but their usage differs. まじき is a formal, literary expression usually found in fixed phrases such as あるまじき, and is used to judge that something is morally or socially unacceptable rather than logically impossible, as in 指導者としてあるまじき行為だ ('It is behavior unbecoming of a leader.'). はずがない denies possibility based on logic, evidence, or common sense, stating that something cannot be true, as in 彼がそんなことをするはずがない ('There is no way he would do such a thing.').

related.title: たらどう
related.meaning: Why don't you...?, What if you did...?
related.comparison: ませんか is a polite way to extend an invitation, similar to 'Won't you…?' or 'Wouldn't you like to...?' in English. In contrast, たらどう has a stronger tone and is used to suggest or advise a course of action. For example, 何か食べませんか？ is a polite offer (e.g. to a guest), while 何か食べたらどう？ implies the speaker thinks the listener should eat something.

related.title: たい
related.meaning: Want to do
related.comparison: ませんか is a polite way to extend an invitation, similar to 'Wouldn't you like to...?' in English. For example, 何か食べませんか？ ('Won't you eat something?') might be used when politely offering food to a guest. Note that unlike English, the たい form ('Do you want to…?') isn't typically used to invite someone in Japanese—何か食べたいですか？ simply asks if the person wants to eat something.

related.title: ましょう
related.meaning: Let's, Shall we (Polite volitional)
related.comparison: ましょう and ませんか are both polite expressions based on the ます form that can be used to make suggestions or invitations. ましょう is more assertive and usually implies the speaker and listener will do something together (e.g., 食べましょう = 'Let's eat'). In contrast, ませんか is more of an invitation directed at the listener (e.g., 食べませんか = 'Won't you eat [some]?').

related.title: ～ましょうか
related.meaning: Shall we, Shall I
related.comparison: ましょう and ませんか are both polite forms based on the ます form and used to make suggestions or invitations. ましょう typically suggests doing something together (e.g. 食べましょう = 'Let's eat'), while ませんか invites the listener to join (e.g. 食べませんか = 'Won't you eat [some]?') ましょう is more assertive, while ませんか is softer and more inviting.

related.title: て初めて
related.meaning: For the first time, Not until, Only after, Only when, Only because
related.comparison: まず means 'first' or 'to begin with' and is used to express something that happens or should happen before something else. In contrast, て初めて means 'not until (A) did (B) happen' or 'for the first time after (A), (B)', e.g. 大人になって初めて分かった ('I understood for the first time after becoming an adult.').

related.title: Verb[volitional]とする
related.meaning: Try to, Attempt to, To be about to
related.comparison: The grammar point 'Verb[volitional]とする' expresses the speaker's decision or intention to do something, often indicating a plan or goal. In contrast, 'まだ～ていません' is used to express that an action has not yet occurred or a state has not yet been reached, emphasizing the ongoing absence of the action or state.

related.title: まだ
related.meaning: Still
related.comparison: まだ suggests that something remains in a state, or that an action has not yet occurred, similar to the English 'still' or 'not yet.' When used with a verb in the ていません form, it expresses the continued non-occurrence of an action. For example, まだ食べていません means 'I haven't eaten yet' or 'I still haven't eaten.'

related.title: 上に
related.meaning: As well, Besides, In addition to
related.comparison: また and 上に both add information, but differ in usage and tone. また appears at the beginning of a sentence or phrase and means 'moreover' or 'again'. It's common in both speech and writing. 上に follows nouns, verbs, or adjectives and conveys a similar meaning, but with a more formal or written tone.

related.title: 再び
related.meaning: Again, Once more, A second time
related.comparison: また and 再び both mean 'again', but differ in nuance and formality. また is common in speech and writing, used for repetition or to add information, like 'again' or 'additionally'. 再び is more formal and literary, often with a stronger sense of 'for the second time'.

related.title: それに
related.meaning: And, Besides, Moreover, In addition
related.comparison: Both それに and また can be used to provide added information, like the English 'in addition' or 'moreover', but with slightly different nuances. また has a slightly formal tone, and adds information in a neutral sense. それに is more colloquial and has the nuance of adding information that supports or reinforces the previous statement.

related.title: 更に
related.meaning: Even more or further, Again, More and more
related.comparison: また is common in both speech and writing, meaning 'again' or introducing additional information, like 'moreover' or 'additionally'. 更に is more formal and used in writing or structured speech, with the nuance of 'even more' or 'beyond that', often indicating further change or escalation.

antonyms.title: もう
antonyms.comparison: まだ and もう are both adverbs that indicate the timing of an action or state (or lack thereof). まだ implies that something is still in a certain state or that an action has not yet occurred, similar to 'still' or ' not yet' in English. In contrast, もう indicates that a change has occurred, similar to 'already' (in affirmatives) or 'no longer' (in negatives).

related.title: まだ～ていません
related.meaning: Still haven't done (something)
related.comparison: まだ suggests that something remains in a state, or that an action has not yet occurred, similar to the English 'still' or 'not yet.' When used with a verb in the ていません form, it expresses the continued non-occurrence of an action. For example, まだ食べていません means 'I haven't eaten yet' or 'I still haven't eaten.'

related.title: さえ
related.meaning: Even
related.comparison: Both さえ and まで mean 'even,' but with different nuances. さえ is often used with negatives to express 'not even X,' e.g. 食べることさえできなかった ('I couldn't even eat.'). まで is usually affirmative and highlights something surprising or extreme, e.g. 骨まで食べた ('He even ate the bones.').

related.title: Noun + まで
related.meaning: Until, Till,  To (but no further than)
related.comparison: The particle まで has the core meaning of 'as far as.' It's commonly used with time expressions to mean 'until' (e.g. 月曜まで休む = 'rest until Monday') and with places to mean 'up to' or 'to (a location)' (e.g. 駅まで送る = 'see [someone] off to the station'). When used with verbs or non-location nouns, it can mean 'even' or 'as much as' (e.g. 骨まで食べる = 'eat even the bones').

related.title: ほど
related.meaning: The more, About, To the extent that, So much… that
related.comparison: まで and ほど both mean 'to the extent of,' but with different uses. まで emphasizes inclusion or a surprising limit, e.g. 骨まで食べた ('He even ate the bones.'). ほど means 'so much that' and often describes degree or intensity, e.g. 痛いほど分かる ('I understand to the point that it hurts.').

related.title: くらい ②
related.meaning: To the extent that, So…that, About the only thing that...
related.comparison: Both まで and ほど share the core meaning of 'to the extent of,' but are used differently. まで emphasizes inclusivity or a surprising limit, e.g. 骨まで食べた ('He even ate the bones.'). In contrast, くらい② means 'to the point of' and often expresses degree, e.g. 叫びたいくらい怖かった ('It was so scary I wanted to scream.').

related.title: ほど～ない
related.meaning: Is not as…as, Isn't... like..., Not to the extent of...
related.comparison: Both grammar points share the idea of 'to the extent of,' but are used differently. ほど～ない makes negative comparisons, e.g. ジョンはトムほど強くない ('John isn't as strong as Tom.'). まで emphasizes something going beyond expectations, e.g. 骨まで食べた ('He even ate the bones.').

related.title: Verb + まで
related.meaning: Until (something) happens
related.comparison: The particle まで has a core meaning of 'as far as' or 'to the extent of.' With verbs, it often means 'until A happens'—for example, 友達が来るまでテレビを見ていた ('I was watching TV until my friend came'). In other cases, it can mean 'even,' as in 骨まで食べた ('He even ate the bones'). Both uses reflect the core idea of reaching a certain extent or limit.

related.title: Verb + まで
related.meaning: Until (something) happens
related.comparison: The particle まで means 'until' when used with a verb that can describe a progressive or ongoing action, e.g. 8時までテレビを見る 'watch TV until eight o'clock'. In contrast, までに means 'by', 'before', or 'no later than', and is used with verbs that describe actions with a set completion point, e.g. 8時までに終わる 'to finish by/before eight o'clock.'

related.title: Noun + まで
related.meaning: Until, Till,  To (but no further than)
related.comparison: The particle まで means 'until' when used with a time expression and a verb describing an ongoing action. For example, 8時までテレビを見る means 'watch TV until 8 o'clock.' In contrast, までに means 'by' and is used to express that an action is completed before a certain time. For example, 8時までに終わる means 'finish by 8 o'clock'.

related.title: したがって
related.meaning: Therefore, Thus, As a result
related.comparison: まま(に) describes being left in a certain state, often implying lack of freedom or control, as in 言われるままに従った (I obeyed just as I was told). したがって is a formal connector meaning therefore or thus, used to show logical consequence, as in 天気は悪い。したがって中止する (The weather is bad. Therefore, it will be canceled).

related.title: とおり
related.meaning: In that way, Exactly, Precisely, Just as
related.comparison: Both まま(に) and とおり express doing something in a certain way, but differ in nuance. まま(に) emphasizes leaving things as they are or acting spontaneously. For example, 思うままに話す ('Speak as you feel'). とおり stresses replication or faithful adherence. For example, レシピのとおりに作った ('I made it exactly according to the recipe').

related.title: ことはない
related.meaning: There is no need to, Never happens
related.comparison: Both can serve as emphatic ways to express 'there is no need to', e.g. 心配する（までもない/ことはない） ('There is no need to worry.'). Note however that ことはない can also be used to mean that something never happens (lit. 'there are no cases of'), e.g. 彼と会うことはない ('I never meet him.'), while までもない is not used in this sense and will always have the 'no need' meaning.

related.title: には及ばない①
related.meaning: There is no need to, It is fine if you don’t, Unnecessary
related.comparison: Both can serve as emphatic ways to express 'there is no need to'. The main difference is that には及ばない is more formal and can be used following nouns, while までもない will typically always follow verbs, e.g. ご心配には及ばない ('There is no need to worry.'). vs. 心配するまでもない (same meaning, but not honorific).

related.title: には及ばない①
related.meaning: There is no need to, It is fine if you don’t, Unnecessary
related.comparison: Both can serve as emphatic ways to express 'there is no need to'. The main difference is that には及ばない is more formal can be used following nouns, while までもない will typically always follow verbs, e.g. ご心配には及ばない ('There is no need to worry.') vs. 心配するまでもない (same meaning, but not honorific).

related.title: かのようだ
related.meaning: As if, Just like
related.comparison: These are minor variations of the same basic expression meaning 'as if it were (A)'. まるで adds an emphatic nuance, implying it's 'just like' the thing it's being compared to, e.g. まるで夢のようだ 'It's just like a dream.' If the question particle か is present, it adds a nuance of uncertainty, highlighting that though it seems like that thing, the speaker doubts it is (or knows it is not) that thing, e.g. 日本にいるかのようだ 'It's like I'm in Japan (though I'm not).'

related.title: 風
related.meaning: -style, Way, Fashion
related.comparison: 風 (ふう) and みたいに・みたいな both express resemblance or appearance. 風 attaches directly to nouns, e.g. アメリカ風料理 'American-style food' and can sound more formal. みたいな also attaches to nouns, but is more casual and makes a more straightforward comparison, e.g. ハンバーガーみたいな料理 = 'food like a hamburger'.

related.title: みたい
related.meaning: Like, Similar to, Resembling
related.comparison: The みたい in all these patterns means 'seems like (A)' or 'like (A)'. The difference lies in how it connects to other words. Examples include 春みたい or 春みたいだ／です ('It's like spring.'), 春みたいな天気 'spring-like weather', and 春みたいに暖かい ('It's warm like spring.').

related.title: ように・ような
related.meaning: As・like, Just like
related.comparison: ように・ような and みたいに・みたいな both express similarity, meaning 'like' something else. The main difference is formality and structure. みたい is more casual and attaches directly, e.g. 春みたいな天気 'spring-like weather' in a season that is not actually spring. よう is more formal and requires の, e.g. 春のような天気, which has the same meaning.

related.title: らしい ①
related.meaning: Seems like, Apparently, I heard
related.comparison: みたいに・みたいな and らしい① both convey a sense of 'seems', but differ in nuance and usage. みたい compares something to something else, e.g. 春みたいに暖かい ('It's warm like spring.'). らしい expresses hearsay or inference, e.g. 暖かいらしい ('Apparently it's warm.' or 'It seems warm.' based on what was heard or observed.).

related.title: のように・のような
related.meaning: Like (Noun), Similar to (Noun)
related.comparison: のように・のような and みたいに・みたいな both express comparisons, 'like' or 'similar to,' but differ in formality and structure. みたい is more casual and attaches directly after nouns, e.g. 春みたいな天気 'spring-like weather'. よう is more formal and uses の before it, e.g. 春のような天気, which has the same meaning.

related.title: そうに・そうな
related.meaning: Seem, Look like, Sound
related.comparison: そうに・そうな and みたいに・みたいな both attach to verbs and adjectives and express appearance, but differ in nuance. そうに・そうな is based on observable signs, e.g. 雨が降りそうな天気 'weather that looks like it's about to rain'. みたいに・みたいな compares something to another thing, e.g. 春みたいな天気 'spring-like weather' in a season that is not actually spring.

related.title: と同じで・と違って
related.meaning: Like, Similar to, Unlike, Dissimilar to
related.comparison: みたいに・みたいな and と同じで both express comparison but differ in nuance. みたいに・みたいな suggests similarity or resemblance, e.g. ‘like’ or ‘seems like'. と同じで states something is explicitly the same, showing a stronger degree of similarity. Conversely, と違って means ‘unlike’ or ‘different from'.

related.title: らしい ②
related.meaning: typical of, -ish・like, appropriate for, becoming of, befitting
related.comparison: みたいに・みたいな and らしい② both express likeness but differ in nuance. みたい suggests resemblance despite being different, e.g. 春みたいな天気 ('spring-like weather' in another season). らしい describes something that fits the typical image of what it actually is, e.g. 春らしい天気 ('weather that's just like you'd expect in spring', when it is actually spring.).

related.title: げ
related.meaning: Seemingly, Giving appearance of, [Expresses emotions or feelings]
related.comparison: み and げ are adjective endings that serve different roles. み turns adjectives into abstract nouns that describe a quality, e.g. '深み' ('depth') or '苦しみ' ('suffering'). げ turns adjectives or verbs into な-adjectives with the nuance of seeming to possess an emotion, e.g. '嬉しげな顔' ('a happy-seeming face') or '意味ありげな表情' ('a seemingly meaningful expression').

related.title: さ
related.meaning: Converting adjectives to nouns
related.comparison: さ and み both nominalize adjectives, similar to '~ness' in English, but differ in nuance and usage. さ works with both い and な-adjectives and expresses measurable degree, e.g. 高さ 'height' or 大変さ 'difficulty'. み is typically used with い-adjectives and focuses on quality or characteristics rather than degree, e.g. 面白み 'that which makes (something) interesting'.

related.title: どうやら
related.meaning: Apparently, Looks Like, Seems like
related.comparison: どうやら and みたい combine to convey a tentative impression or guess based on observed clues. どうやら adds a tone of cautious inference, while みたい expresses resemblance or appearance. For example: どうやら彼は来ないみたいだ (It seems like he’s not coming).

related.title: みたいに・みたいな
related.meaning: Like, Similar to, Resembling
related.comparison: The みたい in all these patterns means 'seems like (A)' or 'like (A)'. The difference lies in how it connects to other words. Examples include 春みたい or 春みたいだ／です ('It's like spring.'), 春みたいな天気 'spring-like weather', and 春みたいに暖かい ('It's warm like spring.').

related.title: にみえる
related.meaning: To look, To seem, To appear
related.comparison: にみえる and みたい both express that something 'seems' a certain way, but with different nuance. にみえる specifically refers to visual appearance, e.g. 'looks like', and can be used with そうだ or ようだ. みたい means 'is like' or 'seems like' more in a more general sense, not necessarily related to appearance, and is more casual than にみえる.

related.title: そう
related.meaning: Look like, Appear, Seem, Have a feeling that
related.comparison: Both みたい and そう can mean 'seems like', but differ in nuance and usage. みたい compares something to another, e.g. 春みたい ('It's like spring.') or is based on reliable evidence, e.g. 面白いみたい ('Apparently it's interesting.' i.e. based on something you've heard). そう is based on appearance or sensory impression, e.g. 面白そう ('It looks/sounds interesting.').

related.title: そうに・そうな
related.meaning: Seem, Look like, Sound
related.comparison: Both みたい and そうに・そうな can mean 'seems like', but differ in nuance and usage. みたい expresses resemblance to something else, e.g. 春みたいな天気 'spring-like weather'. そうに・そうな is based on sensory cues and often used with verbs, e.g. 雨が降りそうな天気 'weather that looks like it's about to rain'.

related.title: らしい ①
related.meaning: Seems like, Apparently, I heard
related.comparison: Both らしい① and みたい express similarity, but with different nuances. らしい means something is typical or characteristic of what it represents, e.g. 春らしい天気 which implies 'weather that's just like spring should be'. In contrast, みたい suggests resemblance of one thing to something else that it is not, e.g. 春みたいな天気 'spring-like weather' such as an unseasonably warm day in winter.

related.title: ようだ
related.meaning: It seems that, It appears that, It looks like
related.comparison: Both みたい and ようだ express resemblance, like 'seems like' in English. みたい is more casual and common in speech, while ようだ is more formal and used in writing. みたい follows nouns directly (e.g. 春みたい), while ようだ requires の (e.g. 春のようだ). Both function grammatically as な-adjectives.

related.title: ように・ような
related.meaning: As・like, Just like
related.comparison: Both みたい and ように・ような express resemblance, similar to 'seems like' in English. みたい is more casual and common in everyday speech, while ように・ような is more formal and used in writing. みたい attaches directly to nouns, e.g. 春みたいな天気 'spring-like weather'. In contrast, よう requires の, e.g. 春のような天気 (same meaning).

related.title: それとも
related.meaning: Or, Or else, Rather
related.comparison: Both むしろ and それとも involve choices, but in different ways. むしろ is used to clarify or emphasize a preferred option, e.g. ワインよりむしろビールが飲みたい ('I'd rather have beer than wine'). それとも offers neutral choices in questions, e.g. ワインにする？それともビール？ ('Will you have wine? Or beer?').

related.title: 逆に
related.meaning: On the contrary
related.comparison: むしろ and 逆に both express contrast, but with different nuances. むしろ presents a preference or clarification, e.g. 話すよりむしろ相手の話を聞きたい ('I'd rather listen than talk'). 逆に highlights an outcome opposite to what was expected, e.g. 喜んでくれると思ったが逆に怒られた ('I thought they'd be happy, but they got mad instead').

related.title: に反して
related.meaning: Go against, Contrary to
related.comparison: むしろ and に反して both express contrast, but differ in usage. むしろ emphasizes preference or clarification, e.g. 雨でもむしろ外に出たい ('Even in the rain, I'd rather go out'). に反して highlights contradiction or opposition, e.g. 期待に反して失敗した ('He failed, contrary to expectations').

related.title: 却って
related.meaning: All the more, On the contrary, Conversely
related.comparison: Both むしろ and 却って  express contrast with expectation, but differ in nuance. むしろ introduces a preferred or clearer option, e.g. ワインよりむしろビールが飲みたい ('Rather than wine, I'd rather drink beer'). 却って highlights ironic reversal, e.g. 手伝ってあげたのに却って怒られた ('I helped, but they got angry instead').

related.title: 割に
related.meaning: Although, Despite, Rather...for, Unexpectedly, Comparatively, Considering
related.comparison: むしろ and 割に both involve contrast, but from different perspectives. むしろ introduces a preferred or more accurate option, e.g. 寝るよりむしろ本を読みたい ('Rather than sleep, I'd rather read'). 割に shows discrepancy between expectation and reality, e.g. 安い割に美味しい ('It's good for how cheap it is').

related.title: 代わりに
related.meaning: Instead of,  In place of, In exchange for
related.comparison: Both expressions involve alternatives, but with different meanings. 代わりに indicates a direct substitution or exchange, often physical or role-based, e.g. 泳ぐ代わりに走った ('I ran instead of swimming'). むしろ introduces a more suitable or preferred option, e.g. 泳ぐよりむしろ走りたい ('I'd rather run than swim').

related.title: というより
related.meaning: ...is more of a... than a..., Rather than saying that...
related.comparison: むしろ and というより can both be used when clarifying or rewording something, but function differently. というより attaches to a word or phrase to suggest a more appropriate expression, while むしろ introduces the clarified or preferred wording. Because of this, they often pair together, e.g. 可愛いというより、むしろ美しい ('Rather than cute, she's beautiful').

related.title: あまり～ない
related.meaning: Not very, Not much, Not really, Hardly
related.comparison: Both めったに〜ない and あまり〜ない are used to qualify negative expressions, but differ in nuance. めったに〜ない means 'rarely' or 'almost never' and focuses on frequency, e.g. めったに行かない ('I rarely go.'). あまり〜ない is less strong, e.g. あまり行かない ('I don't go often.'). It can also qualify a degree or amount, e.g. あまり寒くない ('It's not very cold.'), while めったに cannot.

related.title: ほとんど
related.meaning: Almost all, Most, Hardly any, Few (Adverbial noun)
related.comparison: めったに〜ない and ほとんど can be used similarly, but with key differences. めったに〜ない means 'rarely', much like ほとんど with a negative verb. For example, めったに行かない ('I rarely go.') and ほとんど行かない ('I almost never go.') are close, though めったに suggests even lower frequency. Unlike めったに, ほとんど can also be positive, as in ほとんどみんな 'almost everyone'

related.title: 陸に～ない
related.meaning: Not well or properly, Not much or enough, Barely, Hardly
related.comparison: Both ろくに〜ない and めったに〜ない express the idea of scarcity, but differ in nuance and context. めったに〜ない refers to the rarity of an action or event, e.g. 'めったに外出しない' ('rarely go out'). ろくに〜ない emphasizes inadequacy or insufficiency, often judging something as not measuring up, e.g. 'ろくに話せない' ('barely able to speak').

related.title: なかなか～ない
related.meaning: Not really, Hardly, Not easily or readily, By no means, Far from
related.comparison: めったに〜ない and なかなか〜ない both express rarity, but with different nuance. めったに〜ない means something rarely happens and carries a neutral or negative tone. なかなか〜ない, while also indicating rarity, emphasizes difficulty or persistence, often despite effort. It suggests that the situation is hard to bring about, rather than simply uncommon.

related.title: すら
related.meaning: Even
related.comparison: すら and も are fundamentally similar in that both can serve to add emphasis. In cases like 子どもですらできる 'even a child can do it' 子どもでもできる (same meaning) they are essentially interchangeable except that すら is more formal and more emphatic. In examples where も is simply serving to add additional information, e.g. 田中さんも来た ('Mr. Tanaka also came.'), も could not be replaced with すら (unless there is some reason to believe that Tanaka coming would be an extremely rare or unexpected happening).

related.title: でもある
related.meaning: Be also, It also is
related.comparison: でもある and も can both express 'also' (the も of でもある is the same as the particle も), but there is a key difference to note. AもB means '(A) is also (B)', while Aでもある means 'it is also (A)'. For example, compare 田中さんは学生です。鈴木さんも学生です ('Tanaka is a student. Suzuki is also a student') vs. 田中さんは学生です。日本人でもあります ('Tanaka is a student. He is also Japanese').

related.title: と
related.meaning: And
related.comparison: も and と are two different particles with different meanings. も means 'also' or 'too' and is used to add information, like in 田中さんも来ました ('Tanaka came, too'). と can be used to connect nouns like 'and' (バナナとりんご = 'a banana and an apple') or to mean 'with' (友だちと遊ぶ = 'to play with a friend').

related.title: らしい ②
related.meaning: typical of, -ish・like, appropriate for, becoming of, befitting
related.comparison: Both describe something becoming like something else, but the nuance is different. めく・めいた means ‘showing signs of’ and refers to a noticeable change in atmosphere or appearance, without judging whether it is typical, as in 秋めいてきた ('It has started to feel autumn-like.'). らしい②, especially in らしくなってきた, focuses on becoming more typical or expected for that thing, emphasizing how well it matches the usual image, as in 秋らしくなってきた ('It has started to feel properly autumn-like.').

related.title: びる
related.meaning: -like, Looking, Feeling, With an air of, To become
related.comparison: Both describe something beginning to show a certain quality, but the nuance differs. めく focuses on a noticeable change in atmosphere or appearance, often used for seasons or general impressions, as in 秋めいてきた ('It has started to feel autumn-like.'). びる emphasizes the emergence of a specific visible or behavioral trait, often something concrete or character-related, as in 大人びた話し方をする ('He speaks in a grown-up way.').

synonyms.title: すでに
synonyms.comparison: Both もう and すでに express the idea of 'already.' The difference is their level of formality and tone. While もう is commonly used in everyday conversation and can have a personal or emotional nuance, すでに is more objective and found primarily in writing or formal speech. もう can also be used alone as an expression of exasperation or frustration, while すでに cannot.

antonyms.title: まだ
antonyms.comparison: まだ and もう are both adverbs that indicate the timing of an action or state (or lack thereof). まだ implies that something is still in a certain state or that an action has not yet occurred, similar to 'still' or ' not yet' in English. In contrast, もう indicates that a change has occurred, similar to 'already' (in affirmatives) or 'no longer' (in negatives).

related.title: と
related.meaning: If, When, Whenever, And, [Conditional]
related.comparison: と is used to express natural or habitual results, as in 春が来ると花が咲く ('When spring comes, flowers bloom.'). もし adds emphasis to conditionals and can appear before と or other conditionals like たら and ば. For example, もし雨が降ったら 'if it rains' has a more hypothetical tone than if もし were not present.

related.title: たら
related.meaning: When, From when, Just when, If
related.comparison: もし and たら are often used together to form conditional ('if') sentences. もし typically comes at the start of a sentence to emphasize the hypothetical nature of what follows, while たら gives the verb its conditional form. For example, もし宝くじが当たったら車を買おうと思います means 'If I win the lottery, I think I'll buy a car.'.

related.title: もしも～なら・もしも～でも
related.slug: もしも～なら・もしも～でも
related.meaning: If, Supposing
related.comparison: もし is typically used at the beginning of a sentence to emphasize that what follows is a hypothetical situation or condition, similar to the English 'if' or 'supposing that (A)'. もしも is a more emphatic version of this, and sentences with もしも almost always contain a conditional expression, as seen in the もしも～なら・もしも～でも pattern. The specific meaning is determined by the conditional, e.g. もしも～なら 'if (A)' vs. もしも～でも 'even if (A)'.

related.title: たとえ〜ても
related.meaning: Even if…is the case, Supposing that
related.comparison: もし and たとえ are similar in that both can be used at the beginning of a sentence to emphasize a hypothetical situation or condition. The primary difference is that while もし can be followed by a wide variety of conditionals, including なら, たら, ば, and ても, たとえ is almost always followed by a ても expression, giving it the meaning of 'even if (A)' or 'even supposing that (A)'.

related.title: ても
related.meaning: Even if, Even though
related.comparison: もし and ても both express hypothetical situations, but in different ways. もし appears before a clause to emphasize the condition, similar to 'if' or 'supposing that', while ても is attached to the verb itself. Because they fill different roles, they can be used together, e.g., もし雨が降っても 'even if it rains'. もし can also pair with other conditionals like たら or ば.

related.title: ば
related.meaning: If… then, (Conditional)
related.comparison: もし and ば are both used in conditional expressions, but play different roles. ば is a grammatical ending that turns verbs into 'if' statements, e.g. 食べる 'eat' becoming 食べれば 'if (you) eat'. In contrast, もし is used at the start of sentences or phrases to emphasize that a hypothetical statement follows, and therefore is often paired with ば, たら, or なら, e.g. もし食べれば 'if (you) were to eat...'.

related.title: なら
related.meaning: Were it, If, If it's the case, As for (Conditional)
related.comparison: なら and もし are both used in conditional expressions but have different roles. なら follows phrases to mean 'If it's the case that (A)', often implying the speaker believes it will happen. もし is used at the start of sentences or phrases to emphasize that a hypothetical statement follows, and is often paired with conditionals like なら, ば, or たら, e.g. もし雨なら行かない ('If it rains, I won't go.').

related.title: ～というのは事実だ
related.meaning: It is a fact that ~, There is no doubt that
related.comparison: Both emphasize a truth, but differ in nuance and tone. ～というのは事実だ explicitly affirms something as a fact, e.g. 彼がやったというのは事実だ ('It's a fact that he did it'). もさることながら highlights one point while further stressing another, e.g. 見た目もさることながら味も最高だ ('Not only the appearance, but the taste is excellent'). Both are formal, but the former is more clinical, while the latter sounds more literary or poetic.

synonyms.title: おそらく
synonyms.comparison: Both express possibility, but differ in how likely something seems. おそらく suggests a high chance and is often used with だろう or でしょう, e.g. おそらく来るでしょう 'Most likely, he will come.' もしかしたら suggests a lower chance or speculation, and pairs with かもしれない or かもしれません, e.g. もしかしたら雨が降るかもしれない 'Perhaps it will rain.'

related.title: あるいは
related.meaning: Or, Possibly, Maybe, Perhaps, Or possibly
related.comparison: あるいは and もしかしたら can overlap in one usage. あるいは is usually used mid-sentence to present alternatives, e.g. 月曜日あるいは火曜日 'Monday or Tuesday.' However, it can also appear at the start of a sentence to mean 'Perhaps,' e.g. あるいはそうかもしれない 'Perhaps that might be so.' もしかしたら can be used the same way, as in もしかしたらそうかもしれない, though あるいは carries a stronger nuance of weighing other possibilities.

related.title: かもしれない
related.meaning: Might, Maybe, Probably
related.comparison: かもしれない and もしかしたら both express possibility, but differ in placement and nuance. かもしれない appears at the end of sentences or phrases and means 'might' or 'may', e.g. 行くかもしれない ('I might go.'). もしかしたら comes at the beginning to emphasize the uncertainty, e.g. もしかしたら行くかもしれない ('Perhaps I might go.').

related.title: でも
related.meaning: Or something,  Any... (with question words), No matter (who)
related.comparison: もしも～なら and もしも～でも emphasize a hypothetical situation, similar to 'supposing that (A)' or 'even if (A)' in English. Separately, でも can attach to question words to mean 'any~', e.g. 誰でも 'anyone' and なんでも 'anything', or follow regular nouns to suggest a casual option, as in お茶でも飲みませんか？ ('Want some tea or something?').

related.title: なら
related.meaning: Were it, If, If it's the case, As for (Conditional)
related.comparison: なら is a conditional that follows phrases to mean 'if it's the case that (A)', often implying the condition is likely or assumed. もしも adds emphasis to the hypothetical nature of a situation and is placed at the beginning, e.g. もしも雨なら 'if it were to rain'. It can also appear with でも, e.g. もしも雨でも 'even if it rains' (implying 'I'll still go', etc.).

related.title: もし
related.meaning: If (For emphasis)
related.comparison: もし is typically used at the beginning of a sentence to emphasize that what follows is a hypothetical situation or condition, similar to the English 'if' or 'supposing that (A)'. もしも is a more emphatic version of this, and sentences with もしも almost always contain a conditional expression, as seen in the もしも～なら・もしも～でも pattern. The specific meaning is determined by the conditional, e.g. もしも～なら 'if (A)' vs. もしも～でも 'even if (A)'.

related.title: たとえ〜ても
related.meaning: Even if…is the case, Supposing that
related.comparison: Both introduce hypothetical situations, but with different nuances. たとえ〜ても sets up contrast, like 'Even if (A), still (B)', e.g. たとえ負けても悔いはない 'Even if I lose, I'll have no regrets.' もしも～なら・～でも expresses a basic hypothetical and its result, e.g. もしも彼が来たら、話します 'If he comes, I'll talk to him.'

related.title: ても
related.meaning: Even if, Even though
related.comparison: ても is a highly versatile structure meaning 'even if,' used widely in both speech and writing. In contrast, ようが～まいが is much more formal and mainly found in written or academic contexts. Both forms can express 'whether or not'. For example, 食べても食べなくても and 食べようが食べまいが both mean 'whether or not (you) eat', but the latter is more emphatic and formal.

related.title: のなかで～がいちばん～
related.meaning: Out of ~, ~ is the best/most ~
related.comparison: Both patterns express superlatives ('the best' or 'the most'), but with a key difference. のなかで～がいちばん～ clearly states what group is being compared, while もっとも simply means 'the most X,' with the comparison group left unstated. Also, のなかで～がいちばん～ is more casual, whereas もっとも is more formal.

related.title: それなのに
related.meaning: And yet, Despite this
related.comparison: Both can begin a sentence to introduce contrast, but differ in tone and nuance. もっとも is more formal and adds a condition that qualifies the previous statement, e.g. 全員来ました。もっとも、田中さんは遅れましたが 'Everyone came. That said, Tanaka was late.' それなのに is more casual and expresses surprise at an unexpected outcome, e.g. 雨だった。それなのに、試合は行われた 'It rained. And yet, the match was held.'

related.title: しかしながら
related.meaning: However, Nevertheless, But
related.comparison: Both もっとも and しかしながら are formal expressions that can begin a sentence to convey contrast, but differ in nuance. しかしながら is a literary and emphatic variant of しかし, e.g. 'しかしながら、例外があります' ('Nevertheless, there are exceptions'). もっとも adds a qualification to the previous statement, e.g. '全員来ました。もっとも、田中さんは遅れましたが' ('Everyone came. However, Tanaka was late').

synonyms.title: ので
synonyms.meaning: Because, So, Since, The reason being
synonyms.comparison: ので expresses a cause or reason, similar to 'A, so B' in English. もの or もん (the latter being more casual) can also express a reason, adding emphasis or emotion, like the English 'because.' The main difference is that ので sounds more objective and can be used mid-sentence, while もの or もん typically comes at the end of a sentence and conveys a more personal or emotional tone.

synonyms.title: から
synonyms.comparison: から expresses a cause or reason, similar to 'A, so B' in English. もの or もん (with もん being more casual) can also express an emphatic or emotional reason, similar to 'because' in English. The main difference is that から can be used mid-sentence, while もの or もん typically appears at the end of a sentence and conveys a more personal or emotional tone.

related.title: ことだから
related.meaning: It is exactly because
related.comparison: Both もの・もん and ことだから can be used to give reasons, but they differ in tone and intent. もの・もん is casual, colloquial, and subjective, used to express personal feelings or justifications, like 'because (A)'. ことだから, on the other hand, has a more objective and emphatic feel, often meaning 'it is precisely because (A) that (B)'. In some contexts, ことだから can also imply 'considering (A), then (B)'.

related.title: ものだから
related.meaning: Because, The reason is
related.comparison: These two are closely related and use the same もの (with もん as a more casual variant) to express reason. もの・もん is often used at the end of a sentence in a casual or even childish tone to give a personal reason, e.g. 'because (A)'. ものだから is a slightly more formal expression, often used to explain or justify an action or situation in a softer, less direct way.

related.title: ものですから・もので
related.meaning: Because, My excuse is, My explanation is
related.comparison: These expressions also share the same root as もの・もん, but differ in tone and formality. もの・もん is casual and subjective, typically used for personal justifications like 'cause (A)'. ものですから or もので adds a more objective or polite tone, and is commonly used in formal or semi-formal situations when offering explanations or justifications for one's actions or circumstances.

related.title: なぜなら〜から
related.meaning: Because, The reason is, That is because
related.comparison: なぜなら〜から and もの・もん are similar in that both can express a reason, but they differ significantly in tone and formality. なぜなら～から is a formal and objective way of stating a reason, similar to saying, 'as for why, it is because (A)'. In contrast, もの・もん is casual or colloquial, sometimes even childish, and conveys a personal, subjective tone, like saying, 'because' or ''cause (A)'.

antonyms.title: どころではない
antonyms.meaning: This is not the time for…, Far from, Out of the question
antonyms.comparison: ものか is a strong, indignant denial, meaning Absolutely not! or As if…, as in 勝てるものか (As if you could win!). どころではない means out of the question due to circumstances, often translated as This is no time for… or Far from…, as in それどころじゃない (This is no time to worry about that!).

related.title: どころか
related.meaning: Far from, Anything but
related.comparison: Both ものか and どころか convey strong denial or contrast, but differ in tone. ものか expresses emphatic denial, often with indignation or disbelief. For example, 負けるものか ('As if I could lose!'). どころか highlights an opposite or unexpected result compared to an initial expectation. For example, 優勝どころか、一回戦で負けた ('Far from winning the championship, they lost in the first round').

related.title: ことか
related.meaning: How…, What..., God knows...
related.comparison: Both ことか and ものか express strong emotion, but differ in purpose and tone. ことか is a rhetorical exclamation used to convey deep feelings like admiration or regret, e.g. どんなに嬉しかったことか 'How happy I was!' ものか expresses emphatic denial or rejection, e.g. 行くものか 'As if I'd go!' ものか can also appear as the casual contraction もんか, while ことか is typically more formal or literary.

related.title: はずがない
related.meaning: Hardly possible, Cannot be, Highly unlikely, Improbable
related.comparison: Both express strong denial, but ものか is more emphatic and emotional. はずがない states something is unlikely or impossible, based on logic, e.g. 来るはずがない ('There's no way he'll come.'). ものか rejects something outright, as in 君にできるものか！ ('As if you could!').

antonyms.title: べきではない
antonyms.meaning: Should not, Must not , Ought not to
antonyms.comparison: ものだ expresses general or societal expectations, e.g. 子どもは早く寝るものだ ('Children are supposed to go to bed early.') べきではない carries the opposite nuance, conveying that one should not do something based on moral or social reasoning, e.g. 人を見た目で判断すべきではない ('You shouldn't judge people by appearances.')

related.title: ものだから
related.meaning: Because, The reason is
related.comparison: Both expressions use the same ものだ, with the only difference being the presence or absence of から 'because/since'. Therefore, ものだ is used at the end of a sentence to express the idea of how something is or should be. In contrast, ものだから presents a reason or an explanation for something, and is often used when justifying a certain action or situation. ものだ can also be used to express obligation, while ものだから is not used this way.

related.title: ものですから・もので
related.meaning: Because, My excuse is, My explanation is
related.comparison: While both expressions contain もの, they are used with very different nuances. ものですから・もので offers a reason, and is often used to explain or justify one's personal actions or situation, e.g. 忙しかったものですから 'I was busy, so...'. In contrast, ものだ is used to express a natural tendency or general truth, e.g. １２月は仕事が忙しいものです ('Work is busy in December.', implying that's just the way it is).

related.title: ことだ
related.meaning: Should, Ought
related.comparison: Both express what should be done, but with different nuance. ことだ is used to give firm advice, e.g. 諦めずに続けることだ ('You should keep going without giving up.') ものだ expresses general expectations or norms, e.g. 仕事は地道に頑張り続けるものだ ('One is expected to keep working steadily.')

related.title: べき
related.meaning: Must, Should , Ought to
related.comparison: Both express what one 'should' do, but with different nuance. べき reflects personal or moral judgment, e.g. 約束は守るべきだ ('You should keep your promises.') ものだ conveys social or cultural expectations, e.g. 子どもは早く寝るものだ ('Children are supposed to go to bed early.')

related.title: はずだ
related.meaning: Bound to (be), Expected to (be), Should be (able)
related.comparison: はずだ conveys a specific expectation or prediction based on current evidence, like 雨が降るはずだ ('It should rain.'). ものだ expresses a general rule or common tendency, like 雨は春によく降るものだ ('It often rains in spring.'). The former is situational, while the latter is habitual or cultural.

synonyms.title: ので
synonyms.meaning: Because, So, Since, The reason being
synonyms.comparison: Both ので and ものだから give reasons or explanations, but with slightly different nuances. ので is normally more objective and direct, used to state that fact (A) is the reason for (B). ものだから, on the other hand, is more subjective, expressing personal or emotional reasons for the speaker's actions.

synonyms.title: から
synonyms.comparison: から expresses a reason or cause, similar to 'because' or 'so' in English, with the structure (A) から (B). In contrast, ものだから is more emphatic or emotional, often used to explain or justify actions. Both can be used in subjective statements, where the speaker presents their perspective or judgment.

related.title: ことだから
related.meaning: It is exactly because
related.comparison: Both give reasons, but differ in tone. ことだから emphasizes a reason that is obvious or characteristic, as in 田中さんのことだから大丈夫だろう (Since it’s Tanaka, it should be fine). ものだから is often used to explain or excuse, as in 熱があるものだから休みます (I have a fever, so I’ll take a day off).

related.title: ものですから・もので
related.meaning: Because, My excuse is, My explanation is
related.comparison: All mean because, but nuance differs. ものですから is the most polite. ものだから is plain and often explanatory. もので is slightly softer and less direct. For example: 遅れたのは事故にあったものですから (I was late because there was an accident).

related.title: もの・もん
related.meaning: Because, Cause
related.comparison: These two are closely related and use the same もの (with もん as a more casual variant) to express reason. もの・もん is often used at the end of a sentence in a casual or even childish tone to give a personal reason, e.g. 'because (A)'. ものだから is a slightly more formal expression, often used to explain or justify an action or situation in a softer, less direct way.

related.title: ものだ
related.meaning: Supposed to, Should, That's the way it is
related.comparison: Both expressions use the same ものだ, with the only difference being the presence or absence of から 'because/since'. Therefore, ものだ is used at the end of a sentence to express the idea of how something is or should be. In contrast, ものだから presents a reason or an explanation for something, and is often used when justifying a certain action or situation. ものだ can also be used to express obligation, while ものだから is not used this way.

related.title: なぜなら〜から
related.meaning: Because, The reason is, That is because
related.comparison: These phrases are related in that both can be used to express a reason, though they differ in tone, nuance, and usage. なぜなら〜から is more formal and objective, and is used at the beginning of a sentence to provide a reason for something previously said. In contrast, ものだから is used at the end of a phrase or sentence, and typically has a nuance of providing a justification or excuse for one's behavior or the situation.

related.title: からこそ
related.meaning: Emphasized because, Precisely because
related.comparison: からこそ and ものだから are similar in that both can be used in expressing a reason. They differ in that からこそ is used to specifically emphasize the reason, like saying 'it is precisely because of (A) that (B)', while ものだから is not as emphatic, and is generally used when providing an explanation or excuse for one's actions or a situation.

synonyms.title: ので
synonyms.meaning: Because, So, Since, The reason being
synonyms.comparison: Both ので and ものですから・もので give reasons or explanations. ので is normally more objective and direct, used to state that fact (A) is the reason for (B). ものですから・もので is used to express more personal or emotional reasons for actions. Note that ですから is primarily used in polite speech.

synonyms.title: から
synonyms.comparison: Both から and ものですから・もので express reasons or explanations. から is more straightforward and commonly used, similar to '(A), so (B)' in English. ものですから・もので places more emphasis on the reason, often used to explain one's own actions. Note that ものですから is more polite, while から is used in more casual speech.

related.title: ものだから
related.meaning: Because, The reason is
related.comparison: All mean because, but nuance differs. ものですから is the most polite. ものだから is plain and often explanatory. もので is slightly softer and less direct. For example: 遅れたのは事故にあったものですから (I was late because there was an accident).

related.title: もの・もん
related.meaning: Because, Cause
related.comparison: These expressions also share the same root as もの・もん, but differ in tone and formality. もの・もん is casual and subjective, typically used for personal justifications like 'cause (A)'. ものですから or もので adds a more objective or polite tone, and is commonly used in formal or semi-formal situations when offering explanations or justifications for one's actions or circumstances.

related.title: ものだ
related.meaning: Supposed to, Should, That's the way it is
related.comparison: While both expressions contain もの, they are used with very different nuances. ものですから・もので offers a reason, and is often used to explain or justify one's personal actions or situation, e.g. 忙しかったものですから 'I was busy, so...'. In contrast, ものだ is used to express a natural tendency or general truth, e.g. １２月は仕事が忙しいものです ('Work is busy in December.', implying that's just the way it is).

related.title: なぜなら〜から
related.meaning: Because, The reason is, That is because
related.comparison: These phrases are similar in that both express a reason, but they differ in tone and usage. なぜなら〜から provides a clear, objective explanation for something just mentioned. ことだから is often used in expressions like '彼のことだから', meaning 'because it's him', highlighting a reason based on someone's typical behavior or the nature of a situation.

related.title: ものと思っていた
related.meaning: Was under the impression that, (Always) the thought used to think
related.comparison: Both expressions state an assumption, but they differ in time perspective and speaker stance. ものと思っていた looks back on a past assumption and is often used when the speaker realizes that the belief was mistaken or no longer holds true, frequently carrying a nuance of surprise or correction upon learning the reality. By contrast, ものと思う expresses a present assumption or belief that the speaker currently holds, often with confidence, and is used to state what the speaker thinks is true or likely at the time of speaking.

related.title: ものと思う
related.meaning: Think that, Believe that, Expect that, Have confidence that, Absolutely
related.comparison: Both expressions state an assumption, but they differ in time perspective and speaker stance. ものと思っていた looks back on a past assumption and is often used when the speaker realizes that the belief was mistaken or no longer holds true, frequently carrying a nuance of surprise or correction upon learning the reality. By contrast, ものと思う expresses a present assumption or belief that the speaker currently holds, often with confidence, and is used to state what the speaker thinks is true or likely at the time of speaking.

related.title: できれば・できたら
related.meaning: If possible
related.comparison: できれば・できたら and ものなら both express possibilities, but differ in tone and implication. できれば・できたら is a relatively casual and neutral way to say 'if possible', often used for ordinary requests or wishes. ものなら sounds more formal and can convey a stronger emotional desire, often about something difficult or unrealistic, as in できるものなら ('if only it were possible').

synonyms.title: が
synonyms.comparison: ものの expresses contrast, similar to 'although' in English, showing that while one thing is true, another contradicts it. が also expresses contrast, but its nuance is weaker, and in some cases, it merely introduces a statement without implying contrast. が is more general, while ものの is more specific. Both are formal and more common in writing than in casual speech.

synonyms.title: けれども
synonyms.comparison: Both ものの and けれども can appear at the end of a clause to show contrast. けれども (or the casual けど) usually expresses a simple contrast or adds background information, and is used in a wide range of situations. In contrast, ものの has the specific nuance of 'While A is true, B is not,' and is less colloquial, more commonly found in writing or formal speech.

synonyms.title: にもかかわらず
synonyms.meaning: Despite, In spite of, Regardless, Nevertheless
synonyms.comparison: Both express concession but differ in tone. にもかかわらず is formal and often used in writing to highlight strong contrast or surprise, as in 雨にもかかわらず試合が行われた (The game was held despite the rain). ものの is less formal and means even though or but, as in 雨が降ったものの試合は行われた (It rained, but the game was held).

synonyms.title: つつ(も)
synonyms.meaning: Even while ~ing, Even though ~ing, Despite ~ing, Although ~ing
synonyms.comparison: Both express contrast between expectation and reality. つつ(も) implies that an action continues over time while another, opposing reality exists, as in 危険だと知りつつも挑戦した (I knew it was dangerous, yet I tried). ものの simply means although/even though, without emphasizing continuation, as in 雨は降ったものの試合は行われた (It rained, but the game was held).

related.title: ものを
related.meaning: But, Although, If only, I wish I had
related.comparison: ものを and ものの are similar in that both can express contrast (i.e. 'although'), but ものを specifically contrasts something that was expected to happen with what actually happened because the action was not done, e.g. みんなで協力すればいいものを ('although it would have been good if you had all cooperated'), while ものの can be used for general contrasts 毎朝走っているものの、足はあまり速くない ('Although I run every morning, I'm not that fast.'). Additionally, ものを will most often come at the end of a sentence, while ものの will be followed by the reason or unexpected result.

related.title: のに
related.meaning: Despite, Although, Even though
related.comparison: のに and ものの both express contrast, but differ in nuance and tone. のに is more casual and often conveys disappointment or regret. It can also end a sentence to imply 'If only (A)!'. ものの is more formal and means 'although (A), (B) didn't follow as expected,' often with an implicit contrast. Unlike のに, it is not generally used alone at the end of a sentence..

related.title: 割に
related.meaning: Although, Despite, Rather...for, Unexpectedly, Comparatively, Considering
related.comparison: Both ものの and 割に express contrast, but in different ways. ものの links two opposing facts to mean 'despite', e.g. '話せるものの、字は読めない' ('Despite the fact that he can speak, he can't read characters'). 割に describes a quality being out of proportion with something else, e.g. '高い割にはあまり美味しくない' ('For being expensive, it's not very tasty').

related.title: てもらう
related.meaning: To get someone to do, To have someone do
related.comparison: もらう is a basic verb meaning 'to receive,' often used when the speaker or someone in their in-group receives something from someone outside the group. For example, プレゼントをもらった means 'I received a present.' When used as てもらう after a verb in the て form, it shows that the speaker received the benefit of an action, as in 日本語を教えてもらった ('I had someone teach me Japanese').

related.title: あげる
related.meaning: To give (away), To present, To provide
related.comparison: あげる means 'to give' and is used when the speaker or someone in their in-group gives something to someone outside the group. The subject, marked by が, is the giver. In contrast, もらう means 'to receive' and describes the act from the receiver's perspective. With もらう, the subject marked by が is the one receiving.

related.title: くれる
related.meaning: To give (me), To be given
related.comparison: くれる means 'to give' and is used when someone from the speaker's out-group gives something to the speaker or their in-group. The subject, marked with が, is the giver. In contrast, もらう means 'to receive' and describes the action from the receiver's perspective. With もらう, the subject marked with が is the one receiving.

synonyms.title: と
synonyms.comparison: と and や are particles used to connect nouns, similar to 'and' in English, but differ slightly. と is used when listing all items explicitly (e.g., りんごとバナナ = 'apples and bananas [only]'). In contrast, や indicates examples from a larger set (e.g., りんごやバナナ = 'things like apples and bananas,' implying there are others).

related.title: ～ら
related.meaning: We, Them, They, These, Those
related.comparison: ら is a pluralizing suffix that attaches to nouns to mean '(A) and others'. や is a particle that links multiple nouns, similar to 'and' in English. While ら can follow a single noun, や must connect two or more. Both imply the presence of other people or things not explicitly mentioned.

related.title: および
related.meaning: And, As well as
related.comparison: Both および and や are used to list items, but they differ in nuance and formality. および is a highly formal expression similar to と, often implying a complete list. It is used almost exclusively in writing and structured, formal speech. In contrast, や is less formal and suggests a partial list, such as 'A, B, and things like that.'

related.title: やら～やら
related.meaning: Whether or, Things like, And … so on
related.comparison: Both や and やら～やら are used to list items or examples from a larger set, and are more common in writing or formal speech than in casual conversation. や is a straightforward way to suggest other similar examples, as in 'A, B, and things like that.' In contrast, やら～やら carries a stronger nuance of uncertainty or possibility, as if the speaker is listing examples that may or may not apply.

related.title: とか～とか
related.meaning: Among other things, For example, Such as
related.comparison: Both や and とか～とか are used to list items non-exhaustively, implying that other, unmentioned items are also included. や is more common in writing or formal speech, while とか is more colloquial. や must follow nouns, while とか can also be used with actions or events involving verbs. Additionally, とか can follow each item in the list, whereas や is not used after the final item.

related.title: たり～たりする
related.meaning: Things like ~ and ~
related.comparison: Both たり～たりする and や can be used to provide or list examples, similar to the English 'things like A and B.' The main difference is that たり can only be used with verbs, adjectives, or noun phrases, while や can only be used to list nouns. Note that both grammar points suggest that the speaker is listing just some of many possible actions or things.

related.title: のに
related.meaning: Despite, Although, Even though
related.comparison: ものを and のに both are used to express the sense of 'if only'. ものを is more formal and also typically used when the speaker is expressing their disappointment at something that should have happened, not happening, e.g. 事前に知らせてくれればいいものを ('if only you had informed me in advance'), while のに can be used for general wishes 晴れればいいのに ('if only the weather were nice').

related.title: ものの
related.meaning: Although, But, Even Though
related.comparison: ものを and ものの are similar in that both can express contrast (i.e. 'although'), but ものを specifically contrasts something that was expected to happen with what actually happened because the action was not done, e.g. みんなで協力すればいいものを ('although it would have been good if you had all cooperated'), while ものの can be used for general contrasts 毎朝走っているものの、足はあまり速くない ('Although I run every morning, I'm not that fast.'). Additionally, ものを will most often come at the end of a sentence, while ものの will be followed by the reason or unexpected result.

related.title: ところを
related.meaning: When, While, In spite of (the circumstances), Regardless of (the circumstances)
related.comparison: These expressions seem similar in that both use a noun followed by を to express that something is 'operating' on a given situation. ところを is often used to express that acknowledge a situation and acknowledge/describe something affecting it, e.g. お忙しいところをすみません ('I'm sorry for interrupting you at a busy time.') while ものを is often used to expect contrary-to-fact wishes about things that should have happened, but didn't, e.g. みんなで協力してやればいいものを ('If only you had cooperated and done it together, but…')

antonyms.title: にくい
antonyms.comparison: やすい and にくい express opposite ideas: 'easy to do' and 'hard to do.' Both attach to verb stems, e.g. 食べやすい 'easy to eat' and 食べにくい 'hard to eat', and function as い-adjectives, meaning they can be seen with tenses like 食べやすかった 'was easy to eat', and modify nouns like with 食べやすい果物 'an easy-to-eat fruit'.

related.title: だの
related.meaning: Things like, And so on, Or something, And whatnot
related.comparison: だの and やら～やら are similar in that both are used to list multiple examples of something, but differ in that だの generally has a negative implication, while やら～やら can just be used to provide examples without any particular implication.

related.title: とか～とか
related.meaning: Among other things, For example, Such as
related.comparison: While both やら～やら and とか～とか list examples, they differ in nuance. やら～やら often has an uncertain tone or a presents contradictory possibilities, e.g. 行くのやら行かないのやら ('whether he's going or not'). とか is more neutral and casual, simply listing examples without a strong nuance of uncertainty.

related.title: ～ても～なくても
related.meaning: Whether ~ or not
related.comparison: ～ても～なくても and やら～やら both present multiple possibilities, but ～ても～なくても contrasts something with its negative, e.g. 行っても行かなくてもいい ('Go or don't, it's fine'), while やら～やら loosely lists alternatives, which may include opposites, e.g. 行くのやら行かないのやら ('whether he'll go or not'), or not, e.g. 本やら辞書やら ('things like books and dictionaries').

related.title: たり～たりする
related.meaning: Things like ~ and ~
related.comparison: Both たり～たりする and やら～やら list examples of actions, like saying 'doing things like A and B' in English. たり is more neutral, while やら adds a nuance of uncertainty or a wider, less-defined range, suggesting the speaker is listing just some of many possible actions or things.

related.title: や
related.meaning: Things like, And the like
related.comparison: Both や and やら～やら are used to list items or examples from a larger set, and are more common in writing or formal speech than in casual conversation. や is a straightforward way to suggest other similar examples, as in 'A, B, and things like that.' In contrast, やら～やら carries a stronger nuance of uncertainty or possibility, as if the speaker is listing examples that may or may not apply.

related.title: が早いか
related.meaning: As soon as, No sooner than, The instant that, No sooner had
related.comparison: Both express that one action is followed immediately by another, meaning ‘no sooner than,’ and are often interchangeable. や否や is more formal and literary, commonly used in written narratives to describe near-simultaneous events, as in ベルが鳴るや否や生徒が教室を飛び出した ('No sooner had the bell rung than the students rushed out of the classroom.'). が早いか is slightly more vivid and action-focused, often used to emphasize the suddenness of the second action, as in ドアを開けるが早いか子どもが走り出した ('No sooner had I opened the door than a child ran off.').

related.title: したがって
related.meaning: Therefore, Thus, As a result
related.comparison: Both express logical consequence, but the tone and register differ. ゆえに is a formal, written connector meaning ‘therefore’ that presents a reason–result relation in a stiff, explanatory style, as in 天候が悪化した。ゆえに計画を変更した (‘The weather worsened; therefore, we changed the plan.’). したがって also means ‘therefore’ but is more neutral and common in explanations or academic writing, focusing on logical progression rather than formality, as in 条件が満たされない。したがって実行できない (‘The conditions are not met; therefore, it cannot be carried out.’).

related.title: ために
related.meaning: Due to, Because of, As a result of
related.comparison: Both relate cause and result, but their structures are different. ゆえに can be used as a sentence-level connector meaning ‘therefore’, drawing a conclusion a stated reason, as in 予算が不足している。ゆえに延期した (‘The budget was insufficient; therefore, it was postponed.’). ために is a grammatical form meaning ‘because of' or 'due to’ comes within a sentence to similarly express purpose, e.g. 雨のために試合が中止になった (‘The game was canceled due to the rain.’).

antonyms.title: か
antonyms.comparison: よ is a common sentence-ending particle used for emphasis, often indicating that the speaker is providing new information (e.g., 面白いですよ = 'It's interesting, I'm telling you!'). In contrast, か is the standard question particle, which has the opposite function, asking for information (e.g., 面白いですか？ = 'Is it interesting?').

related.title: ぜ
related.meaning: Strong emphasis (friendly or familiar), !
related.comparison: よ is a sentence-ending particle used to add emphasis and indicate the speaker is sharing new information (e.g., 面白いですよ = 'It's interesting, I'm telling you!'). ぜ is a casual, masculine particle that also adds strong emphasis. Unlike ぞ, it can sound a bit more friendly, but it's still informal and should be avoided in polite situations or with people you don't know well.

related.title: だ
related.meaning: To be, Is
related.comparison: よ is a common sentence-ending particle used to add emphasis, often indicating the speaker is sharing new information (e.g., 面白いよ = 'It's interesting, I'm telling you!'). だ is a grammatical form expressing assertion, similar to 'is' or 'am.' These two are often combined, especially in casual or masculine speech (元気だよ = 'I'm feeling good!'). In softer or more feminine speech, the だ is frequently omitted (e.g., 元気よ).

related.title: ぞ
related.meaning: Strong emphasis (toward anyone), !
related.comparison: よ is a sentence-ending particle used to add emphasis and indicate the speaker is sharing something new (e.g., 面白いですよ = 'It's interesting, I'm telling you!'). ぞ is a rougher, more masculine sentence-ending particle that adds strong emphasis. It has a blunt tone and should be avoided in polite conversation or with strangers, as it lacks the softness or friendliness of よ.

related.title: ね
related.meaning: Isn't it?, Right? (Sentence ending particle)
related.comparison: よ and ね are common sentence-ending particles that add feeling or nuance. よ adds emphasis and is used when giving new information or asserting something (e.g., 面白いですよ = 'It's interesting, I'm telling you!'). ね is used to seek agreement or confirmation (e.g., 面白いですね = 'It's interesting, isn't it?'). When combined as よね, they express both emphasis and a request for agreement.

antonyms.title: まい
antonyms.meaning: Won't, Intend not to, Probably not
antonyms.comparison: まい and the volitional form (〜よう) both express the speaker’s intent or decision, but in opposite directions. 〜よう indicates intention or willingness to do something, as in 行こう (Let’s go), while まい expresses a strong negative intention or refusal, as in 行くまい (I will not go).3

related.title: 〜ようとしない
related.meaning: Not willing to, Not make an effort to, To not attempt or try to, Volitional + としない
related.comparison: Both of these use Verb [よう], the volitional form. By itself, it expresses a suggestion, e.g. 食べよう ('Let's eat'.). Combined with としない, it means 'doesn't even try to,' e.g. 野菜を食べようとしない ('He/she doesn't even make an effort to eat vegetables.'). The key difference is between making a proposal vs. showing a lack of intent or effort.

related.title: ～ましょうか
related.meaning: Shall we, Shall I
related.comparison: ましょうか and Verb [よう] are both volitional forms, with ましょうか being polite and Verb [よう] (sometimes with か) casual. ～ましょうか is used to make polite suggestions like 'Shall we...?' (e.g. 行きましょうか = 'Shall we go?') 行こうか is more casual and introspective, often expressing the speaker's internal thoughts (e.g. 'Should I go?')

related.title: Verb[volitional]とする
related.meaning: Try to, Attempt to, To be about to
related.comparison: Both of these use Verb [よう], the volitional form. On its own, it expresses a suggestion, e.g. 食べよう ('Let's eat.'). When combined with とする, it shows an attempt to do something, often one that fails, e.g. 食べようとしたけど ('I tried to eat it, but...'). The key difference is suggestion/proposal vs. attempted action.

related.title: 〜ようではないか
related.meaning: Let's do something, Why don't we
related.comparison: Both of these use Verb [よう], the volitional form, to express suggestions like 'Let's do (A).' The core meaning is the same, but they differ in tone. Verb [よう] is casual and used in everyday speech among friends. In contrast, ～ようではないか has a tone that sounds more formal and grandiose, and will be found in media or situations where the speaker is looking for dramatic effect.

related.title: 〜ようと思う・〜おうと思う
related.slug: ようと思う-おうと思う
related.meaning: I think I will, I think I shall, Going to, Gonna (Volitional + と思う)
related.comparison: Both use the volitional Verb [よう] form. On its own, it expresses a suggestion, e.g. 食べよう ('Let's eat.'). When combined with と思う, it expresses an intention or plan, e.g. 食べようと思う ('I'm thinking of eating.'). The key difference is that Verb [よう] includes others in the action, while ～ようと思う expresses the speaker's personal intent.

related.title: ましょう
related.meaning: Let's, Shall we (Polite volitional)
related.comparison: ましょう and Verb[よう] are both volitional forms that mean 'Let's (do something).' The difference is tone: ましょう is polite, while Verb[よう] is casual. For example, 行きましょう (polite) and 行こう (casual) both mean 'Let's go.' The casual Verb[よう] form is also used in other grammar patterns to express intent, such as 行こうと思う ('I think I'll go' / 'I'm planning to go').

related.title: っこない
related.meaning: There is no chance of, There is no way of, Impossible
related.comparison: ようがない・ようもない and っこない both express impossibility, but differ in tone and focus. ようがない・ようもない conveys that there is no way or method to do something, as in 助けようがない (There’s no way to help). っこない is more absolute and emphatic, denying any chance of occurrence, as in 勝てっこない (There’s no way we can win).

related.title: わけがない
related.meaning: There is no way, There is no reason, It is impossible, Cannot be
related.comparison: わけがない and ようがない・ようもない are similar in that both express impossibility. わけがない rejects something as logically unthinkable, e.g. 彼がそんなことをするわけがない ('There's no way he'd do that'). ようがない emphasizes that there is no way or means to do something, e.g. 助けようがない ('There's no way to help').

related.title: そうもない
related.meaning: Very unlikely to, Does not seem that, Does not appear that
related.comparison: While both express impossibility, そうもない focuses on visual or situational cues suggesting unlikelihood, e.g. '雨が降りそうもない' ('It doesn't look like it'll rain'). ようがない・ようもない implies there is no method or way to perform the action, e.g. '連絡のしようがない' ('There's no way to contact them').

related.title: がたい
related.meaning: Hard to, Difficult to
related.comparison: Both ようがない・ようもない and がたい express difficulty or unfeasibility of an action, but differ in nuance. ようがない・ようもない conveys that there is no way or means to do something. For example, 連絡のしようがない ('There's no way to contact him'). がたい, on the other hand, indicates that something is very difficult to do, often due to factors beyond the speaker's control. For example, 信じがたい話だ ('It's a story that is hard to believe').

related.title: はずがない
related.meaning: Hardly possible, Cannot be, Highly unlikely, Improbable
related.comparison: These both express impossibility, but in different ways. はずがない asserts a strong disbelief based on knowledge, like 雨が降るはずがない ('It can't possibly rain.'). ようがない・ようもない focuses on the lack of a method, as in 連絡のしようがない ('There's no way to contact them.').

related.title: ようだ
related.meaning: It seems that, It appears that, It looks like
related.comparison: While both use よう, their meanings differ. ようだ expresses a neutral observation, e.g. 雨が降るようだ ('It seems it will rain.'). ようでは／ようじゃ introduces a hypothetical or ongoing situation that leads to an undesirable outcome, e.g. 雨が降るようじゃ、試合ができない ('If it rains, we can't hold the game.').

related.title: ては
related.meaning: If, With, Since, Because
related.comparison: ようでは・ようじゃ expresses concern or dissatisfaction, implying that if things stay as they are, the outcome will be undesirable, as in この成績では合格できないようじゃないか (With these grades, you won’t pass, will you?). ては introduces a condition leading to a result, which can be neutral or negative, as in 遊んでいては合格できない (If you keep playing, you won’t pass).

related.title: かのようだ
related.meaning: As if, Just like
related.comparison: Both ようだ and かのようだ express appearance or resemblance, but with different nuance. ようだ means 'It seems' or 'It appears,' and can be used whether the speaker believes the thing is actually as it appears or not. かのようだ adds a stronger, often figurative tone like 'just as if (A)', and is typically used when the speaker knows it is it not actually that thing.

related.title: そうだ
related.meaning: I heard that, It is said that
related.comparison: Both forms express observations similar to 'seems' or 'appears', but differ in nuance. そうだ is based on sensory clues, e.g. 雨が降りそうだ ('It looks like it'll rain.', because the sky is dark and the air is damp). ようだ is based on more concrete information or experience, e.g. 雨が降るようだ ('It seems it'll rain.' because people are carrying umbrellas, or the weather report said so).

related.title: ときいた
related.meaning: I heard that
related.comparison: ようだ expresses that something appears a certain way, based on various cues that are not always explicitly stated, e.g. 雨が降るようだ ('It seems it will rain.', perhaps because people are carrying umbrellas, etc.). In contrast, ときいた specifically means 'I heard (A)', and indicates that the information is hearsay directly obtained by the speaker.

related.title: そうに・そうな
related.meaning: Seem, Look like, Sound
related.comparison: そうに・そうな and ようだ can both mean 'seems' or 'appears', but differ in nuance and usage. そうに・そうな is based on sensory observation and attaches to verbs/adjectives (in the case of そうに) or nouns (in the case of そうな). In contrast, ようだ is based on information or inference and typically appears at the end of a sentence, e.g. 雨が降るようだ ('It looks like it will rain.').

related.title: にみえる
related.meaning: To look, To seem, To appear
related.comparison: Both ようだ and に見える express observations, but differ in nuance. ようだ means 'appears to be' based on various cues, e.g. 虫のようだ ('It seems to be an insect.', based on looks, behavior, or context). に見える focuses specifically on visual appearance, e.g. 虫に見える ('It looks like an insect.'), without implying that the speaker thinks it actually is one.

related.title: ようでは・ようじゃ
related.meaning: If, If it is true that
related.comparison: While both use よう, their meanings differ. ようだ expresses a neutral observation, e.g. 雨が降るようだ ('It seems it will rain.'). ようでは／ようじゃ introduces a hypothetical or ongoing situation that leads to an undesirable outcome, e.g. 雨が降るようじゃ、試合ができない ('If it rains, we can't hold the game.').

related.title: らしい ①
related.meaning: Seems like, Apparently, I heard
related.comparison: Both forms express observations similar to 'seems' or 'appears,' but differ in nuance. ようだ is based on direct information or experience, e.g. 雨が降るようだ ('It seems it will rain.', based on people carrying umbrellas). らしい① implies indirect or secondhand information, e.g. 雨が降るらしい ('I heard it will rain.' or 'Apparently, it will rain.')

related.title: みたい
related.meaning: Like, Similar to, Resembling
related.comparison: Both みたい and ようだ express resemblance, like 'seems like' in English. みたい is more casual and common in speech, while ようだ is more formal and used in writing. みたい follows nouns directly (e.g. 春みたい), while ようだ requires の (e.g. 春のようだ). Both function grammatically as な-adjectives.

related.title: どうやら
related.meaning: Apparently, Looks Like, Seems like
related.comparison: どうやら and ようだ are frequently used together to express the speaker’s tentative judgment based on evidence or context. どうやら introduces the inference, while ようだ states the conclusion in a neutral or formal tone. For example: どうやら電車が遅れているようだ (It seems the train is delayed).

related.title: ように・ような
related.meaning: As・like, Just like
related.comparison: ようだ and ように・ような share the meaning of 'seems' or 'appears', but differ in usage. ようだ is used at the end of a sentence to express observations or inferences, e.g. 雨が降るようだ ('It looks like it will rain.'). ように・ような connect to verbs or nouns to show similarity, e.g. 春のように暖かい ('It's warm like spring.') and 春のような天気 'spring-like weather'.

related.title: とみえる
related.meaning: It seems that, It can be deduced, One can conclude that
