related.comparison: Both ようだ and と見える express observations, but differ in nuance and formality. ようだ is commonly used to say something appears a certain way, based on various cues. と見える is more formal or literary and implies a deduction based on specific evidence, which is usually mentioned, e.g. 慌てているところを見ると、何かあったと見える ('Judging by how flustered he looks, something must've happened.').

related.title: Verb[よう]
related.meaning: Let's, I shall (Casual volitional)
related.comparison: Both of these use Verb [よう], the volitional form, to express suggestions like 'Let's do (A).' The core meaning is the same, but they differ in tone. Verb [よう] is casual and used in everyday speech among friends. In contrast, ～ようではないか has a tone that sounds more formal and grandiose, and will be found in media or situations where the speaker is looking for dramatic effect.

related.title: とおもう
related.meaning: I think
related.comparison: 〜よう・おうと思う uses the volitional form with と思う to express plans or intentions, like 'I intend to (A)' or 'I'm thinking of doing (A)' In contrast, plain と思う expresses a simple thought. For example, この本を読もうと思います means 'I intend to read this book.', while この本は面白いと思います means 'I think this book is interesting.'.

related.title: Verb[よう]
related.meaning: Let's, I shall (Casual volitional)
related.comparison: Both use the volitional Verb [よう] form. On its own, it expresses a suggestion, e.g. 食べよう ('Let's eat.'). When combined with と思う, it expresses an intention or plan, e.g. 食べようと思う ('I'm thinking of eating.'). The key difference is that Verb [よう] includes others in the action, while ～ようと思う expresses the speaker's personal intent.

related.title: つもりだ
related.meaning: To plan to, To intend to
related.comparison: Both つもりだ and 〜ようと思う・〜おうと思う can both be used to express intentions or plans for the future. つもりだ feels slightly more 'official' or definite, similar to the English 'I intend to' or 'I hope to'. In contrast, 〜ようと思う・〜おうと思う has a more casual and less definitive feel, similar to the English 'I'm thinking of (doing something)'.

related.title: てみる
related.meaning: To try to
related.comparison: 〜ようと思う・〜おうと思う expresses intention, similar to 'I'm thinking of doing X,' e.g. 行こうと思います ('I plan to go.') てみる conveys the idea of trying something to see what it's like, e.g. 行ってみる ('I'll go and see.'). These forms can be combined, e.g. 行ってみようと思います ('I plan to go and see what it's like.')

related.title: そう
related.meaning: Look like, Appear, Seem, Have a feeling that
related.comparison: ような気がする and そう both express impressions, but with different nuance. ような気がする conveys personal intuition or vague feeling, i.e. something you sense even without clear reason. On the other hand, そう is typically based on observable clues or appearance. In that sense, ような気がする is more subjective and less confident than そう.

related.title: ように・ような
related.meaning: As・like, Just like
related.comparison: ように・ような follows the plain form of a verb or adjective, and expresses similarity in a general sense. For example, 春が来たような日 'a day that is like as if spring had come'. ような気がする is a common idiom that uses this pattern, meaning 'have a feeling.' For example, 誰かが来たような気がする ('I have a feeling that someone came.').

related.title: ぎみ
related.meaning: Looking, Feeling a little, Touch of, Tending to, Slightly
related.comparison: Both ような気がする and ぎみ (from 気味) use 気 expressions to convey subtle states or impressions, but with different nuance and usage. ような気がする follows a phrase to express a subjective hunch like 'Have a feeling that (A)', e.g. 来るような気がする 'I have a feeling they'll come.' ぎみ attaches to nouns to express a slight tendency or hint of something observable, often (but not always) negative, e.g. 疲れ気味だ 'I'm feeling a bit tired.'

related.title: 気
related.meaning: Feel like ~, Spirit
related.comparison: Both ような気がする and 気 use 気 'spirit' expressions to convey subtle states or impressions, but with different nuance and usage. ような気がする follows a phrase to express a subjective hunch like 'Have a feeling that (A)', e.g. 来るような気がする 'I have a feeling they'll come.' 気 itself can be used after verbs mid-sentence or at the end of a sentence to express 'Feel like (doing) (A)', e.g. 行く気満々だよ 'I have a real mind to go!'

related.title: げ
related.meaning: Seemingly, Giving appearance of, [Expresses emotions or feelings]
related.comparison: Both ような気がする and げ (from 気) use 気 expressions to convey subtle states or impressions, but with different nuance and usage. ような気がする follows a phrase to express a subjective hunch like 'Have a feeling that (A)', e.g. 来るような気がする 'I have a feeling they'll come.' げ attaches to emotion-related stems to describe appearance based on sensory perception, e.g. 悲しげな顔 'A face with a sorrowful air.'

related.title: ようになる
related.meaning: To reach the point that, To come to be that, To turn into
related.comparison: ように means 'in such a way' and expresses doing something to achieve a certain result, e.g. 忘れないようにメモした ('I took a memo so I wouldn't forget.'). ようになる, on the other hand, describes a situation changing to a certain state, without specifying the cause, e.g. 話せるようになった ('I became able to speak.').

related.title: ようにする
related.meaning: To try to, To make sure to, To endeavor to
related.comparison: The core meaning of ように is 'in such a way'. It's used to show an action done to achieve a result, e.g. 勉強できるように新しい本を買った ('I bought a new book so I can study.'). ようにする builds on this to express making a habit or effort, like 毎日、日本語を勉強するようにしている ('I try to study / make a habit of studying Japanese every day.').

related.title: ように～てほしい
related.meaning: Want ~ to ~ like ~, Want ~ to be able to ~, Want ~ to ~ in the way of ~
related.comparison: The core meaning of ように is 'so that' or 'in order to', and it is commonly used in patterns like ようにする 'make an effort to (A)' or ようにいう 'ask someone to (A)'. When used together with the ～てほしい form, it expresses wanting someone else to do the action. For example, 忘れないようにする ('I'll try not to forget.') vs. 忘れないようにしてほしい ('I want you to try not to forget.').

related.title: ようにいう
related.meaning: To tell, request, or order to do (in such a way)
related.comparison: The core meaning of ように is 'in such a way.' It shows doing something to achieve a result, e.g. 間に合うように早めに出かけた ('I left early so I could be on time.'). ようにいう builds on this and means 'to instruct someone to do something', e.g. 間に合うように言っておいた ('I told them to be on time.').

related.title: ようにいのる
related.meaning: Pray that/for, Hope that/for, Keep fingers crossed that
related.comparison: The core meaning of ように is 'in such a way'. In general usage, this conveys performing a certain action in order to achieve a stated result, e.g. 合格できるように毎日勉強した ('I studied every day so I could pass.'). ようにいのる (often written in kanji as 祈る 'pray') is one form of this and expresses a wish or hope, e.g. 合格できるように祈っているよ ('I'm hoping/praying you'll pass.').

related.title: 如何
related.meaning: Depending on
related.comparison: 如何 (read as いかん) is a formal term meaning 'how' or 'in what way', used mainly in formal or academic writing. It appears after nouns, often in the pattern 如何によって ('depending on'). 状況の如何で決まる = 'It will be decided depending on the situation.' ように is a more common, casual form, e.g. 忘れないようにメモした ('I took a memo so I wouldn't forget.').

related.title: れる・られる + ままに
related.meaning: Do as (one is told or ordered…), In accordance with, In compliance with, Just like
related.comparison: Both structures express doing something in a certain way. れる・られる + ままに is used when someone acts as they're told, e.g. 言われるがままに 'just as told'. ように can express acting of one's own accord, e.g. 間に合うように早めに出た ('I left early so I could make it on time.').

related.title: みたいに・みたいな
related.meaning: Like, Similar to, Resembling
related.comparison: ように・ような and みたいに・みたいな both express similarity, meaning 'like' something else. The main difference is formality and structure. みたい is more casual and attaches directly, e.g. 春みたいな天気 'spring-like weather' in a season that is not actually spring. よう is more formal and requires の, e.g. 春のような天気, which has the same meaning.

related.title: そうに・そうな
related.meaning: Seem, Look like, Sound
related.comparison: Both そうに・そうな and ように・ような describe appearances, but with different nuances. そうに・そうな conveys a subjective impression or an event about to happen, e.g. 優しそうな目 'kind-seeming eyes' or 雨が降りそうな天気 'weather that looks like it's about to rain'. ように・ような, in contrast, expresses comparison, e.g. 春が来たような天気 'weather that feels like spring has arrived'.

related.title: のように・のような
related.meaning: Like (Noun), Similar to (Noun)
related.comparison: Both のように・のような and ように・ような use よう to express similarity, but with different structures and uses. のように・のような follows nouns for comparisons, e.g. 日本人のような話し方 'a manner of speaking like a Japanese person's'. ように・ような follows verbs and conveys the meaning of doing something 'as if' or 'in a way that is like,' e.g. 日本人が話すように話す 'to speak like a Japanese person speaks'.

related.title: まい～のように
related.meaning: Almost every ~, Nearly every ~, On a ~ basis
related.comparison: These expressions are related through the ように in まい～のように, which is the same as in ように・ような, both expressing comparison. まい～のように is used with 'every~' time expressions to mean 'almost every (A)', e.g. 毎日のように 'almost every day'. In contrast, ように・ような is used for general comparisons, e.g. 春のように暖かい ('It's warm like spring.').

related.title: ような気がする
related.meaning: Have a feeling that
related.comparison: ように・ような follows the plain form of a verb or adjective, and expresses similarity in a general sense. For example, 春が来たような日 'a day that is like as if spring had come'. ような気がする is a common idiom that uses this pattern, meaning 'have a feeling.' For example, 誰かが来たような気がする ('I have a feeling that someone came.').

related.title: という風に
related.meaning: As if to say, As if to suggest that, In such a way that, Things like
related.comparison: Both という風に and ように・ような can be used to express similarity or comparison, but their nuance differs. という風に suggests doing something in a way 'as if to say' something else, meaning that the comparison can be uncertain or abstract. In contrast, ように・ような expresses a more direct comparison, implying similar qualities.

related.title: そう
related.meaning: Look like, Appear, Seem, Have a feeling that
related.comparison: ように・ような and そう both convey impressions, but with different nuance. ように・ような expresses direct comparison or similarity, like 'as if' or 'like,' e.g. 春が来たように暖かい ('It's warm as if spring had come.'). そう is based on the speaker's observation or feeling, e.g. 暖かそう ('It seems warm.').

related.title: ように～てほしい
related.meaning: Want ~ to ~ like ~, Want ~ to be able to ~, Want ~ to ~ in the way of ~
related.comparison: ように・ような follows the plain form of a verb or adjective and expresses general similarity. For example, 日本人のように話す 'speak like a Japanese person'. The same よう appears in ように～てほしい, which expresses wanting someone to act a certain way. For example, 日本人のように話してほしい ('I want you to speak like a Japanese person.').

related.title: みたい
related.meaning: Like, Similar to, Resembling
related.comparison: Both みたい and ように・ような express resemblance, similar to 'seems like' in English. みたい is more casual and common in everyday speech, while ように・ような is more formal and used in writing. みたい attaches directly to nouns, e.g. 春みたいな天気 'spring-like weather'. In contrast, よう requires の, e.g. 春のような天気 (same meaning).

related.title: ようだ
related.meaning: It seems that, It appears that, It looks like
related.comparison: ようだ and ように・ような share the meaning of 'seems' or 'appears', but differ in usage. ようだ is used at the end of a sentence to express observations or inferences, e.g. 雨が降るようだ ('It looks like it will rain.'). ように・ような connect to verbs or nouns to show similarity, e.g. 春のように暖かい ('It's warm like spring.') and 春のような天気 'spring-like weather'.

related.title: 風
related.meaning: -style, Way, Fashion
related.comparison: 風 (ふう) and ように・ような both express resemblance. 風 attaches directly to nouns, e.g. アメリカ風料理 'American-style food', while ような requires の before nouns, e.g. ハンバーガーのような料理 'food like a hamburger'. 風 often has a more stylistic nuance, while ような is a more neutral comparison.

related.title: とおり
related.meaning: In that way, Exactly, Precisely, Just as
related.comparison: Both とおり and ように・ような can express doing something in a similar way, but their nuance differs. とおり implies doing something exactly as described, while ように・ような suggests general similarity. For example, 言われたとおりにした ('I did exactly as I was told.') vs. 言われたようにした ('I did it as I was told.'), where the latter is less precise and emphatic about how closely the speaker followed instructions.

related.title: ふうに
related.meaning: ~ way, In a way that suggests, Like ~
related.comparison: ふうに and ように・ような both express similarity or comparison, but their nuance differs. ふうに suggests appearing a certain way or having a certain style (literally, a certain 'air'). In contrast, ように・ような implies a more direct comparison or resemblance. Their usage also differs: ふうに is followed by verbs or phrases, while ように connects to phrases and ような to nouns.

related.title: かのようだ
related.meaning: As if, Just like
related.comparison: かのようだ and ように・ような both use よう, meaning 'seems', but have different specific uses. かのようだ carries a strong hypothetical nuance and is often used to mean 'just like' or 'as if' when comparing to obviously different things. In contrast, ように・ような expresses comparison in a more direct or neutral way and can be used whether the similarity is hypothetical or real.

related.title: にみえる
related.meaning: To look, To seem, To appear
related.comparison: ように・ような and にみえる can both describe similarities in appearance, but differ in usage. ように・ような follows the plain form of a verb or adjective and expresses general similarity. にみえる follows a noun or な-adjective (note: よう functions as a な-adjective) and means 'looks like'. The two can be used together, for example, 台風が来たように見える ('It looks as if a typhoon had come.').

related.title: てほしい
related.meaning: I want you to
related.comparison: Both ように～てほしい and てほしい express wanting someone else to do something, with the key difference being the presence of よう. よう adds a nuance of doing something in a certain way. For example, 忘れないでほしい ('I want you not to forget.') vs. 忘れないようにしてほしい ('I want you to make sure to not forget.', in the sense of doing what needs to be done to ensure you do not forget).

related.title: がほしい
related.meaning: To want something, To be in need of
related.comparison: These two patterns using ほしい to express a 'want' differ in meaning and usage. がほしい follows nouns to express the speaker's desire for something, whether physical (金がほしい = 'I want money.') or figurative (自由がほしい = 'I want freedom.'). ようにしてほしい combines ようにする and てほしい to express a desire for someone to act in a certain way, e.g. 忘れないようにしてほしい ('I want you to make sure not to forget.').

related.title: ように・ような
related.meaning: As・like, Just like
related.comparison: ように・ような follows the plain form of a verb or adjective and expresses general similarity. For example, 日本人のように話す 'speak like a Japanese person'. The same よう appears in ように～てほしい, which expresses wanting someone to act a certain way. For example, 日本人のように話してほしい ('I want you to speak like a Japanese person.').

related.title: ように
related.meaning: So that, In order to, In such a way that
related.comparison: The core meaning of ように is 'so that' or 'in order to', and it is commonly used in patterns like ようにする 'make an effort to (A)' or ようにいう 'ask someone to (A)'. When used together with the ～てほしい form, it expresses wanting someone else to do the action. For example, 忘れないようにする ('I'll try not to forget.') vs. 忘れないようにしてほしい ('I want you to try not to forget.').

related.title: ようにする
related.meaning: To try to, To make sure to, To endeavor to
related.comparison: ようにする means 'to make an effort to ensure something happens a certain way' and expresses intention or habit. For example, 遅れないようにする ('I'll try not to be late.'). In contrast, ように～てほしい expresses a desire for someone else to behave a certain way. For example, 遅れないようにしてほしい ('I want you to make an effort not to be late.').

related.title: ようにいう
related.meaning: To tell, request, or order to do (in such a way)
related.comparison: ようにいう means 'to tell or ask someone to do something.' It is often used for indirect or general requests. For example, 来るようにいう 'tell (someone) to come'. When paired with てほしい, it shows a desire for someone else to make the request. For example, 来るようにいってほしい ('I want you to tell him/her/them to come.').

related.title: ように
related.meaning: So that, In order to, In such a way that
related.comparison: The core meaning of ように is 'in such a way.' It shows doing something to achieve a result, e.g. 間に合うように早めに出かけた ('I left early so I could be on time.'). ようにいう builds on this and means 'to instruct someone to do something', e.g. 間に合うように言っておいた ('I told them to be on time.').

related.title: ように～てほしい
related.meaning: Want ~ to ~ like ~, Want ~ to be able to ~, Want ~ to ~ in the way of ~
related.comparison: ようにいう means 'to tell or ask someone to do something.' It is often used for indirect or general requests. For example, 来るようにいう 'tell (someone) to come'. When paired with てほしい, it shows a desire for someone else to make the request. For example, 来るようにいってほしい ('I want you to tell him/her/them to come.').

related.title: ように
related.meaning: So that, In order to, In such a way that
related.comparison: The core meaning of ように is 'in such a way'. In general usage, this conveys performing a certain action in order to achieve a stated result, e.g. 合格できるように毎日勉強した ('I studied every day so I could pass.'). ようにいのる (often written in kanji as 祈る 'pray') is one form of this and expresses a wish or hope, e.g. 合格できるように祈っているよ ('I'm hoping/praying you'll pass.').

antonyms.title: 〜ようとしない
antonyms.meaning: Not willing to, Not make an effort to, To not attempt or try to, Volitional + としない
antonyms.comparison: ようにする (literally, 'do things in such a way as to X') can express the idea of taking care to perform a certain action and often describes intentions or habits. 〜ようとしない conveys the opposite, not making an effort to do something. For example, 毎日野菜を食べるようにしている ('I'm making a habit of eating vegetables every day.') vs. うちの子どもは野菜を食べようとしない ('My child doesn't even try to eat vegetables.').

related.title: Verb[volitional]とする
related.meaning: Try to, Attempt to, To be about to
related.comparison: ようにする (literally, 'do things in such a way as to X') expresses taking care to perform a certain action and often indicates a habitual effort. In contrast, Verb[volitional] + とする implies a one-time attempt to do something, often implying the attempt may not succeed. For example, 毎朝走るようにしている ('I'm making a habit of running every morning.') vs. １０キロ走ろうとしたけど ('I tried to run 10k, but...' implying 'but I couldn't finish', etc.).

related.title: ように
related.meaning: So that, In order to, In such a way that
related.comparison: The core meaning of ように is 'in such a way'. It's used to show an action done to achieve a result, e.g. 勉強できるように新しい本を買った ('I bought a new book so I can study.'). ようにする builds on this to express making a habit or effort, like 毎日、日本語を勉強するようにしている ('I try to study / make a habit of studying Japanese every day.').

related.title: ～にする・～くする
related.meaning: Adjective + する, To make something/someone (more)
related.comparison: ～にする・～くする and ようにする both mean making something a certain way, but differ in usage. ～にする・～くする is used with nouns and adjectives to mean ‘make into (A)’, e.g. 綺麗にする ‘make clean’ and 甘くする ‘make sweet’. ようにする is used with verbs and conveys the idea of making sure to do something, e.g. 走るようにする ‘make sure to (or make an effort to) run’.

related.title: てみる
related.meaning: To try to
related.comparison: Both can be translated in English as 'try (doing)', but differ in nuance. ようにする means to make an effort or form a habit, e.g. 毎朝走るようにしている ('I try to run every morning.') has the nuance of making a point of doing so. In contrast, てみる means to try something out to see the result, e.g. 走ってみた ('I tried running.'), which has the nuance of doing so to see what it was like.

related.title: ようになる
related.meaning: To reach the point that, To come to be that, To turn into
related.comparison: Both ようになる and ようにする express something becoming a certain way, but differ in nuance. ようになる describes a situation becoming that way, e.g. 行けるようになった ('I became able to go.', implying external or internal factors allowed it). ようにする focuses on someone actively making it possible, e.g. 行けるようにする ('I'll make sure I can go.').

related.title: ように～てほしい
related.meaning: Want ~ to ~ like ~, Want ~ to be able to ~, Want ~ to ~ in the way of ~
related.comparison: ようにする means 'to make an effort to ensure something happens a certain way' and expresses intention or habit. For example, 遅れないようにする ('I'll try not to be late.'). In contrast, ように～てほしい expresses a desire for someone else to behave a certain way. For example, 遅れないようにしてほしい ('I want you to make an effort not to be late.').

related.title: ことにする
related.meaning: To decide on, Have decided to
related.comparison: ことにする and ようにする are closely related but have different nuances. ことにする is used to express a resolution or decision, e.g. 来年、マラソンを走ることにした ('I decided to run a marathon next year.'). In contrast, ようにする expresses making an effort to carry out an action and is often used for ongoing intentions or habits, e.g. 毎朝走るようにしている ('I'm trying to run every morning.').

related.title: ように
related.meaning: So that, In order to, In such a way that
related.comparison: ように means 'in such a way' and expresses doing something to achieve a certain result, e.g. 忘れないようにメモした ('I took a memo so I wouldn't forget.'). ようになる, on the other hand, describes a situation changing to a certain state, without specifying the cause, e.g. 話せるようになった ('I became able to speak.').

related.title: ようにする
related.meaning: To try to, To make sure to, To endeavor to
related.comparison: Both ようになる and ようにする express something becoming a certain way, but differ in nuance. ようになる describes a situation becoming that way, e.g. 行けるようになった ('I became able to go.', implying external or internal factors allowed it). ようにする focuses on someone actively making it possible, e.g. 行けるようにする ('I'll make sure I can go.').

related.title: ことになる
related.meaning: It will end up being, It is the result of, It has been decided that
related.comparison: Both ようになる and ことになる express something becoming a certain way, but with different nuances. ようになる describes a change in state, e.g. 話せるようになった ('I became able to speak.') ことになる, on the other hand, conveys a decision made, often outside the speaker's control, e.g. 辞めることになった ('It was decided that I/he/she would quit.').

synonyms.title: つもりだ
synonyms.meaning: To plan to, To intend to
synonyms.comparison: Both つもりだ and よていだ express future intentions or plans, but with different nuances. つもりだ emphasizes personal intent or decision, such as 'I intend to' or 'I hope to,' without necessarily implying a set timeframe. よていだ focuses more on planning or scheduling, suggesting that the action is expected to happen at a specific time.

related.title: つもりで
related.meaning: With the intention of doing, Instead of..., Act as if or pretend, Believing that..., Thinking that...
related.comparison: 予定だ expresses a concrete future plan, e.g. 来年日本に行く予定だ ('I plan to go to Japan next year.'). つもり is often less concrete and, in つもりで form, connects actions with intention, e.g. 遊ぶつもりで行った 'went with the intention of having fun'. It can also mean 'believing (A)' as in 俳優になったつもりで話す 'speak as if you were (lit. with the belief that you had become) an actor'.

related.title: 如何
related.meaning: Depending on
related.comparison: Both express that results depend on conditions, but the nuance and construction differ. ようによっては means ‘depending on how something is seen, used, or handled,’ and highlights that the same thing can lead to different outcomes based on circumstances or perspective, as in この映画は見ようによってはコメディーだと感じる人もいるだろう ('Some people may see this movie as a comedy, depending on how they watch it.'). 如何 is a formal, written expression meaning ‘depending on,’ used to state that an outcome is determined by a specific factor in an objective or structural way, as in 対応如何で結果が変わる ('The outcome will change depending on the response.').

synonyms.title: 以上に
synonyms.meaning: More than, No less than, Even more
synonyms.comparison: Both より and 以上に are used for comparisons. In some cases, their meaning and usage can overlap, e.g. 思っていたより and 思っていた以上に both mean 'more than I thought'. In other cases, they can differ in that 以上に suggests a shared quality between the two things being compared. For example, AはBより強い simply states that A is stronger than B, while AはB以上に強い suggests that A is even stronger than B (who is also strong).

synonyms.title: より～のほうが
synonyms.comparison: より is used to make comparisons, with Xより meaning 'more than X.' It often appears in the pattern より～のほうが, as in 'More than X, Y is...' but can also be used without のほうが. In any case, より always follows the thing being compared as 'less.' It can also directly precede adjectives—for example, より高い ('more expensive [than something]'), even if the item being compared isn't stated.

related.title: というより
related.meaning: ...is more of a... than a..., Rather than saying that...
related.comparison: より expresses a general comparison, like 'more than (A)' or '~er,' e.g. 月曜日より水曜日がいい ('Wednesday is better than Monday.') or 走るより野球をするのが好き ('I prefer playing baseball to running.'). In contrast, というより specifically means 'more/rather than saying (A)' and is used primarily when rephrasing or clarifying something.

related.title: にもまして
related.meaning: Even -er than, Even more than, More than anything, More than anybody, More than ever
related.comparison: Both express comparison meaning ‘more than,’ and they can overlap in meaning, but the tone differs. にもまして is a more formal, literary variant often used in writing or speeches to emphasize that something exceeds another in degree, as in 昨年にもまして今年は暑い ('This year is even hotter than last year.'). より is the neutral, everyday comparative used broadly in speech and writing without added emphasis, as in 去年より今年の方が暑い ('This year is hotter than last year.').

related.title: こそ
related.meaning: For sure, For certain, Even more so, Underlined or bolded text
related.comparison: より expresses a general comparison, like 'more than (A)' or '~er,' e.g. 月曜日より水曜日がいい ('Wednesday is better than Monday.') or 走るより野球をするのが好き ('I prefer playing baseball to running.'). In contrast, こそ adds emphasis, highlighting something as especially important, e.g. 月曜日こそ図書館に行きたい ('Monday is precisely when I want to go to the library.').

related.title: 以上 ①
related.meaning: More than (or equal to), Over・(and) above, Any more・Greater, Exceeding
related.comparison: Both 以上① and より express comparisons like 'more than (A)', but with different nuances. 以上 follows numbers or nouns like これ, and means '(A) or more,' e.g. ２時間以上かかる ('It will take two or more hours.'). より makes general comparisons and does not necessarily have a nuance of measuring some quantity.

related.title: ほど～ない
related.meaning: Is not as…as, Isn't... like..., Not to the extent of...
related.comparison: Both expressions are used for comparisons. ほど～ない makes negative comparisons, e.g. ジョンはトムほど強くない ('John isn't as strong as Tom.'). より is used for positive comparisons, e.g. トムはジョンより強い ('Tom is stronger than John.'). Note that ほど follows the greater of the two options, while より follows the lesser.

synonyms.title: より
synonyms.comparison: より is used to make comparisons, with Xより meaning 'more than X.' It often appears in the pattern より～のほうが, as in 'More than X, Y is...' but can also be used without のほうが. In any case, より always follows the thing being compared as 'less.' It can also directly precede adjectives—for example, より高い ('more expensive [than something]'), even if the item being compared isn't stated.

related.title: たほうがいい
related.meaning: It'd be better to, Should do
related.comparison: These are two common expressions using ほう ('alternative'). より～のほうが is used for comparisons—for example, XよりYのほうが means 'Y is more... than X.' In contrast, たほうがいい is used to give advice or suggestions. 野菜を食べたほうがいい literally means 'It would be better if you ate vegetables,' but carries the nuance of 'You should eat vegetables.'

related.title: ほど～ない
related.meaning: Is not as…as, Isn't... like..., Not to the extent of...
related.comparison: Both ほど～ない and より～のほうが express comparisons, but from different angles. ほど～ない is a negative comparison, meaning 'not as...as' (e.g. Xほどおいしくない = 'not as tasty as X'). より～のほうが is a positive comparison, meaning 'more...than' (e.g. AよりBのほうがおいしい = 'B is tastier than A').

synonyms.title: よりしかたがない
synonyms.meaning: There is no choice but, Can't be helped, Inevitable
synonyms.comparison: よりほかない and よりしかたがない both express a lack of alternatives, but differ in nuance. よりほかない emphasizes personal obligation or resolve, often used when one feels compelled to act despite reluctance, as in 行くよりほかない (I have no choice but to go). よりしかたがない conveys resignation to external forces or inevitability, as in 待つよりしかたがない (There’s nothing to do but wait).

related.title: にほかならない
related.meaning: Nothing but, None other than, Simply
related.comparison: よりほかない and にほかならない both emphasize exclusivity, but in different ways. よりほかない indicates there is no alternative action and is often used in practical or urgent contexts, as in 頼るよりほかない (I have no choice but to rely on them). にほかならない is assertive and logical, used to affirm a specific identity or conclusion, as in これは運命にほかならない (This is nothing other than fate).

related.title: しかない
related.meaning: Have no choice but, Only, Just
related.comparison: しかない and よりほかない are similar in that both mean 'there's no choice but to (A)', but よりほかない is considerably more formal than the colloquial しかない, and can carry a nuance that other options exist but aren't feasible due to external pressure or circumstance.

synonyms.title: よりほかない
synonyms.meaning: To have no choice but, No other way than, Have to
synonyms.comparison: よりほかない and よりしかたがない both express a lack of alternatives, but differ in nuance. よりほかない emphasizes personal obligation or resolve, often used when one feels compelled to act despite reluctance, as in 行くよりほかない (I have no choice but to go). よりしかたがない conveys resignation to external forces or inevitability, as in 待つよりしかたがない (There’s nothing to do but wait).

related.title: しかない
related.meaning: Have no choice but, Only, Just
related.comparison: While しかない and よりしかたがない both express having no choice, しかない is more neutral and conversational, while よりしかたがない is more literary and formal, often adding a nuance of resignation. Compare: そうするしかない ('We have no choice but to do that') and 待つよりしかたがない ('There's nothing to be done but wait').

related.title: と
related.meaning: And
related.comparison: ら is often called a pluralizing suffix, and is used after nouns to mean '(A) and others'. と, on the other hand, is a particle that links nouns like 'and' in English. ら can follow a single noun and implies a group with the other members not mentioned, while と connects multiple items and lists all members explicitly.

related.title: や
related.meaning: Things like, And the like
related.comparison: ら is a pluralizing suffix that attaches to nouns to mean '(A) and others'. や is a particle that links multiple nouns, similar to 'and' in English. While ら can follow a single noun, や must connect two or more. Both imply the presence of other people or things not explicitly mentioned.

related.title: そう
related.meaning: Look like, Appear, Seem, Have a feeling that
related.comparison: The main difference between そう and らしい① lies in the source of information. そう implies speculation based on personal observation, e.g. 面白そう ('It looks interesting.'). らしい is based on secondhand information or inference, e.g. 面白いらしい ('I hear it's interesting.' or 'Apparently, it's interesting.').

related.title: とみえる
related.meaning: It seems that, It can be deduced, One can conclude that
related.comparison: らしい ① and にみえる both express observations, but differ in nuance and formality. らしい ① suggests hearsay or information from a third-party source, e.g. 知っていたらしい ('Apparently he/she knew.'). In contrast, とみえる is more formal and expresses the speaker's deduction based on evidence, e.g. 知っていたと見える ('It appears he/she knew.', as if drawing a conclusion based on evidence).

related.title: ということだ
related.meaning: I heard that, Rumor has it that, It is said that, It means that
related.comparison: ということだ and らしい ① both convey information from another source, but differ in nuance and formality. らしい ① conveys hearsay with a nuance of confidence, e.g. 雨が降るらしい ('Apparently, it's going to rain.'). In contrast, ということだ literally means 'It is said that (A).' or 'The word is that (A).', and sounds more formal and confident than らしい ①.

related.title: んだって
related.meaning: I heard that, He or She said that
related.comparison: んだって and らしい ① both convey information from another source, but with different nuances and levels of formality. らしい ① suggests hearsay with a nuance of certainty, indicating the speaker believes it to be true, e.g. 'Apparently it's (A).'. In contrast, んだって is highly casual and includes an explanatory nuance, similar to 'He/she/they said/say (A), you know.'.

related.title: みたいに・みたいな
related.meaning: Like, Similar to, Resembling
related.comparison: みたいに・みたいな and らしい① both convey a sense of 'seems', but differ in nuance and usage. みたい compares something to something else, e.g. 春みたいに暖かい ('It's warm like spring.'). らしい expresses hearsay or inference, e.g. 暖かいらしい ('Apparently it's warm.' or 'It seems warm.' based on what was heard or observed.).

related.title: ようだ
related.meaning: It seems that, It appears that, It looks like
related.comparison: Both forms express observations similar to 'seems' or 'appears,' but differ in nuance. ようだ is based on direct information or experience, e.g. 雨が降るようだ ('It seems it will rain.', based on people carrying umbrellas). らしい① implies indirect or secondhand information, e.g. 雨が降るらしい ('I heard it will rain.' or 'Apparently, it will rain.')

related.title: みたい
related.meaning: Like, Similar to, Resembling
related.comparison: Both らしい① and みたい express similarity, but with different nuances. らしい means something is typical or characteristic of what it represents, e.g. 春らしい天気 which implies 'weather that's just like spring should be'. In contrast, みたい suggests resemblance of one thing to something else that it is not, e.g. 春みたいな天気 'spring-like weather' such as an unseasonably warm day in winter.

related.title: っぽい
related.meaning: -ish, -like, Characteristic of, Typical of, Tendency to
related.comparison: っぽい and らしい ① both describe being 'like' something, but with different nuances and formality. っぽい, similar to the English '-ish,' refers to having the characteristics of something without being that thing, and is very casual, e.g. 子どもっぽい態度 'childish behavior', e.g. from an adult. In contrast, らしい ① implies embodying a characteristic in a more fitting or expected way, e.g. 子どもらしい笑顔 'a smile just as you'd expect from a child'.

related.title: らしい ②
related.meaning: typical of, -ish・like, appropriate for, becoming of, befitting
related.comparison: らしい has two related but distinct functions. らしい ① conveys information heard from a third-party source with a nuance that the speaker believes it to be true, e.g. 雨が降るらしい ('Apparently, it's going to rain.'). らしい ② serves as an adjective, describing a typical characteristic, e.g. 春らしい天気 'spring-like weather'.

related.title: どうやら
related.meaning: Apparently, Looks Like, Seems like
related.comparison: どうやら and らしい are often used together to express conjecture based on indirect evidence. どうやら softens the statement and suggests uncertainty, while らしい indicates that the information is based on hearsay or external sources. For example: どうやら雨が降るらしい (Apparently, it’s going to rain).

related.title: にみえる
related.meaning: To look, To seem, To appear
related.comparison: Both らしい ① and にみえる can convey observations, but with different nuances. らしい ① implies hearsay or information from a third-party source, e.g. 雨が降るらしい ('Apparently it's going to rain.'). In contrast, にみえる expresses a direct visual observation, e.g. 雨が降るようにみえる ('It looks like it's going to rain.', based on visual cues like the sky).

related.title: そうだ
related.meaning: I heard that, It is said that
related.comparison: らしい ① and そうだ both convey information heard from another source, but with different nuances. らしい ① suggests hearsay with some degree of certainty, implying the speaker believes it to be true, e.g. 'Apparently it's (A)'. In contrast, そうだ is more neutral, simply meaning 'I heard (A).' or 'They say (A).'. They also connect differently to nouns and な-Adjectives, e.g. 大変らしい vs. 大変だそうだ (both meaning 'It seems difficult.').

related.title: ときいた
related.meaning: I heard that
related.comparison: らしい ① and ときいた both convey information obtained from another source, but with different nuances. らしい ① expresses hearsay, with the nuance that the speaker is relatively confident about the information, e.g. 雨が降るらしい ('Apparently, it's going to rain.'). In contrast, ときいた literally means 'I heard (A)', with no implication about the speaker's belief, and it cannot be used for information obtained from e.g. written sources.

related.title: 的
related.meaning: ~ly・~like・~al, Typical
related.comparison: 的 and らしい  both describe being 'like' something, but differ in usage and nuance. 的 is highly versatile, converting nouns into な-Adjectives, e.g. 感情的な 'emotional' or 定期的に 'regularly'. In contrast, らしい  expresses embodying the ideal or typical qualities of something, e.g. 春らしい天気 'spring-like weather'.

related.title: なりに
related.meaning: In one's own way, Of someone's own, Do one's best in one's own way, As much as one can
related.comparison: Both なりに and らしい② can connect to the idea of 'being appropriate to someone' but in different ways. なりに means “in one’s own way/to the extent of one’s ability or circumstances,” and it evaluates an action or result as the best (or at least reasonable) within those limits, as in 自分なりに考えた (‘I thought as best I could.'), and can be used in a self-deprecating sense. らしい② means 'typical' or 'true to (someone or something)', focusing on whether a behavior or style fits a person’s usual character, not on limits or 'doing one’s best', as in 彼らしくやった ('He did it how you'd expect from him.')

related.title: 風
related.meaning: -style, Way, Fashion
related.comparison: 風 and らしい② both express resemblance but differ in nuance. 風 means something has the 'air' or appearance of a type, e.g. 日本風建築 'Japanese-style architecture'. らしい② implies it embodies the ideal of that category, e.g. 日本らしい建築 implies 'quintessential Japanese architecture'.

related.title: べくして
related.meaning: As expected, Meant to, Supposed to, Destined to, Sure to
related.comparison: Both relate actions to what is natural or fitting, but with different nuance. らしく means 'in a way typical of' or 'true to,' judging whether behavior matches a person’s character or role, as in 彼は彼らしく行動した ('He acted in a way true to himself.'). べくして does not evaluate character but inevitability, stating that an outcome was the natural result of circumstances or causes, as in 事故は起こるべくして起こった ('The accident happened as was inevitable.').

related.title: Noun＋型
related.meaning: Model, Type, Pattern, Kind, Shape, Size(d)
related.comparison: Both describe attributes, but with different focuses. 型 emphasizes form or category, e.g. 家族型の会社 ('A family-style company') らしい expresses what seems typical or characteristic of something, often based on impression, e.g. 家族らしい雰囲気 ('A family-like atmosphere', warm and loving, as one might expect from a family)

related.title: めく・めいた
related.meaning: Like, To show signs of,  To have the appearance of, To come to a (state) similar to, Somewhat, An air of,  Feels almost like
related.comparison: Both describe something becoming like something else, but the nuance is different. めく・めいた means ‘showing signs of’ and refers to a noticeable change in atmosphere or appearance, without judging whether it is typical, as in 秋めいてきた ('It has started to feel autumn-like.'). らしい②, especially in らしくなってきた, focuses on becoming more typical or expected for that thing, emphasizing how well it matches the usual image, as in 秋らしくなってきた ('It has started to feel properly autumn-like.').

related.title: ならでは
related.meaning: Unique to, Only, Impossible if not, Expected only of
related.comparison: These expressions can be used in similar cases to describe something that is characteristic of a certain person, place, situation, etc., but with slightly different nuances. ならでは implies uniqueness, e.g. 彼ならではの意見 'an opinion only he would have'. In contrast, らしい② suggests something is typical/representative of that thing, but not necessarily unique, e.g. 彼らしい意見 'an opinion you'd expect from him'.

related.title: みたいに・みたいな
related.meaning: Like, Similar to, Resembling
related.comparison: みたいに・みたいな and らしい② both express likeness but differ in nuance. みたい suggests resemblance despite being different, e.g. 春みたいな天気 ('spring-like weather' in another season). らしい describes something that fits the typical image of what it actually is, e.g. 春らしい天気 ('weather that's just like you'd expect in spring', when it is actually spring.).

related.title: らしい ①
related.meaning: Seems like, Apparently, I heard
related.comparison: らしい has two related but distinct functions. らしい ① conveys information heard from a third-party source with a nuance that the speaker believes it to be true, e.g. 雨が降るらしい ('Apparently, it's going to rain.'). らしい ② serves as an adjective, describing a typical characteristic, e.g. 春らしい天気 'spring-like weather'.

related.title: っぽい
related.meaning: -ish, -like, Characteristic of, Typical of, Tendency to
related.comparison: っぽい and らしい② both express resemblance. っぽい is casual, like '-ish' in English, e.g. 子どもっぽい 'childish' (usually with a negative nuance). らしい② implies that someone or something embodies expected traits, e.g. 男らしい 'manly' with a nuance of fulfilling cultural expectations of what a man should be.

related.title: ～(の)姿
related.meaning: Figure, Shape, Appearance, Looks, Wearing, Dressed in, Presence
related.comparison: Both relate to appearance, but with quite different nuances. ～(の)姿 refers to someone's outward form, posture, or visual state, e.g. 彼女の頑張っている姿 ('The sight/figure of her working hard'). In contrast, ～らしい② is used to describe traits or appearances typical of a category or role, e.g. 男らしい ('Manly'). While 姿 describes the appearance or form itself, ～らしい② is about how that form embodies social or cultural norms.

related.title: Verbs (Non-past)
related.meaning: (Non-past)
related.comparison: In Japanese, the dictionary form and the non-past form of verbs are identical. For example, 食べる is both the dictionary form and the non-past form of the verb 'to eat.' Unlike in English, this form can be used as is to express future actions ('I will eat'), habitual actions ('I regularly eat / I eat everyday'), or general truths ('Lions eat meat').

related.title: る-Verb (Past)
related.meaning: る - Verbs (Past tense)
related.comparison: The dictionary form of a る-verb is its base form, expressing current, habitual, or future actions (e.g., 食べる = 'I eat' or 'I will eat'). The past form (-た form) describes completed actions or past states (e.g., 食べた = 'I ate'). Both forms are commonly used in casual speech and various grammar patterns requiring plain verb forms, such as ～と思います ('I think').

related.title: う-Verb (Dictionary)
related.meaning: う + ます (Godan Verbs)
related.comparison: る-Verbs and う-Verbs are the two main verb types in Japanese. Their dictionary forms are the basic forms used in dictionaries and casual non-past sentences (e.g., 食べる = 'I eat' or 'I will eat'). The difference between these verb types is in their conjugation patterns, not their meaning or nuance. Be careful: not all verbs ending in る are る-Verbs (for example, 帰る 'to return' is actually an う-Verb).

related.title: る-Verb (Negative)
related.meaning: Will/Does/Do (not)
related.comparison: The dictionary form is the base form of the verb, used for habitual or future actions. The negative form expresses that an action doesn't happen or a state doesn't exist. For る-Verbs, the negative is formed by changing る to ない (e.g., 食べる → 食べない). う-Verbs have different conjugation patterns. Be careful: not all verbs ending in る are る-Verbs—for example, 帰る ('to return') is actually an う-Verb.

related.title: Verb + て
related.meaning: And, Then (Linking events)
related.comparison: The dictionary form of a る-Verb (e.g., 食べる) is its basic, informal form used to describe present or future actions (e.g., 食べる = 'I eat' or 'I will eat'). In contrast, the て form (verb + て) connects verbs, describing sequential actions or appearing in requests and other grammar patterns (e.g., 食べてください = 'Please eat').

related.title: う-Verb (Past)
related.meaning: う - Verbs (Past tense)
related.comparison: The dictionary form of verbs is their base form, expressing habitual or future actions. The past form indicates completed actions or past states. The difference between る-Verbs and う-Verbs is how they conjugate into the past, not in their meaning. For example, る-Verbs change る to た (食べる → 食べた), while う-Verbs follow different patterns (読む → 読んだ). Be aware that not all verbs ending in る are る-Verbs—帰る ('to return') is an う-Verb.

related.title: る-Verb (Negative)
related.meaning: Will/Does/Do (not)
related.comparison: These forms express the negative ('does not' or 'will not') of る-Verbs, with a difference in tense. The る-Verb (Negative) expresses a lack of future or habitual action (e.g., 食べない = 'I don't eat' or 'I won't eat'). The る-Verb (Negative-Past) expresses a lack of past action (e.g., 食べなかった = 'I didn't eat'). Both are plain forms used in casual speech or other grammatical patterns.

related.title: う-Verb (Negative-Past)
related.meaning: う - Verbs (Negative-past tense)
related.comparison: Both forms express the negative past ('did not'), but the formation differs depending on whether the verb is a る-Verb or う-Verb. For example, 食べなかった (for 食べる, a る-Verb) means 'I did not eat,' while 買わなかった (for 買う, a う-Verb) means 'I did not buy.' Both are plain forms used in casual speech or other grammatical patterns.

related.title: る-Verb (Past)
related.meaning: る - Verbs (Past tense)
related.comparison: These forms are used with る-Verbs to express the idea of having done or not having done a particular action in the past. The difference is whether the meaning is affirmative ('did') or negative ('did not'). For example, 食べた means 'I ate,' while 食べなかった means 'I did not eat.' Both are plain forms used in casual speech or as part of other grammatical forms.

related.title: Verbs (Non-past)
related.meaning: (Non-past)
related.comparison: The non-past (or dictionary) form expresses habitual or future actions, while the past form expresses completed actions or past states. For る-Verbs, change the final る to た to form the past tense. Both the non-past and past forms are casual, non-polite forms used in conversation or other grammar patterns.

related.title: る-Verb (Dictionary)
related.meaning: る + ます (Ichidan Verbs)
related.comparison: The dictionary form of a る-verb is its base form, expressing current, habitual, or future actions (e.g., 食べる = 'I eat' or 'I will eat'). The past form (-た form) describes completed actions or past states (e.g., 食べた = 'I ate'). Both forms are commonly used in casual speech and various grammar patterns requiring plain verb forms, such as ～と思います ('I think').

related.title: Verb［た・ている］+ Noun
related.meaning: Verb modified noun, Relative clause
related.comparison: The る-Verb (Past) expresses completed actions or past states. Unlike English, verbs can modify nouns directly, similar to い-Adjectives. For example, 食べたケーキ means 'the cake [I] ate.' This applies mainly to the plain/casual form, while the polite ます form is used at the end of clauses or sentences.

related.title: う-Verb (Past)
related.meaning: う - Verbs (Past tense)
related.comparison: The past form of verbs expresses a completed action or an action that took place in the past, similar to the English '-ed.' It is a plain form often used in casual conversation or as part of other grammatical structures. The past form differs for る-Verbs and う-Verbs (e.g., 食べる becomes 食べた, and 買う becomes 買った), but the meaning of the conjugation is the same.

related.title: る-Verb (Negative-Past)
related.meaning: る - Verbs (Negative-past tense)
related.comparison: These forms are used with る-Verbs to express the idea of having done or not having done a particular action in the past. The difference is whether the meaning is affirmative ('did') or negative ('did not'). For example, 食べた means 'I ate,' while 食べなかった means 'I did not eat.' Both are plain forms used in casual speech or as part of other grammatical forms.

synonyms.title: じゃない
synonyms.comparison: The negative form of a る-verb indicates 'does not' or 'will not' do something (e.g., 食べない = 'I don't eat' or 'I won't eat'). In contrast, じゃない (a casual form of ではない, the negative of だ) negates nouns and な-adjectives (e.g., 元気じゃない = 'not feeling good'). While both express negation, they apply to different types of words.

antonyms.title: う-Verb (Past)
antonyms.comparison: The past form of a verb expresses completed actions or past states, while the negative form expresses actions or states that do not occur. る-Verbs form their negative by changing る to ない (e.g., 食べる → 食べない), whereas う-Verbs form the past tense differently (e.g., 買う → 買った). Remember, not all verbs ending in る are る-Verbs, for example, 帰る ('to return') is an う-verb.

related.title: る-Verb (Negative-Past)
related.meaning: る - Verbs (Negative-past tense)
related.comparison: These forms express the negative ('does not' or 'will not') of る-Verbs, with a difference in tense. The る-Verb (Negative) expresses a lack of future or habitual action (e.g., 食べない = 'I don't eat' or 'I won't eat'). The る-Verb (Negative-Past) expresses a lack of past action (e.g., 食べなかった = 'I didn't eat'). Both are plain forms used in casual speech or other grammatical patterns.

related.title: Verb + て
related.meaning: And, Then (Linking events)
related.comparison: The negative form of a る-verb expresses that someone does not or will not perform an action (e.g., 食べない = 'I do not eat' or 'I will not eat'). The Verb + て form, on the other hand, connects verbs with other sentence elements, expressing sequences, requests, or permission. For example, 食べて can mean 'Eat, please' in a casual request.

related.title: Verbs (Non-past)
related.meaning: (Non-past)
related.comparison: The non-past (or dictionary) form is the base form of a verb, used to express habitual or future actions (e.g., 食べる = 'I eat' or 'I will eat'). To express the negative, use the negative form. For most える/いる-Verbs, change the final る to ない (e.g., 食べない = 'I do not eat' or 'I will not eat'). Both the non-past and negative forms are plain forms used in casual conversation or other grammar patterns.

related.title: る-Verb (Dictionary)
related.meaning: る + ます (Ichidan Verbs)
related.comparison: The dictionary form is the base form of the verb, used for habitual or future actions. The negative form expresses that an action doesn't happen or a state doesn't exist. For る-Verbs, the negative is formed by changing る to ない (e.g., 食べる → 食べない). う-Verbs have different conjugation patterns. Be careful: not all verbs ending in る are る-Verbs—for example, 帰る ('to return') is actually an う-Verb.

related.title: う-Verb (Dictionary)
related.meaning: う + ます (Godan Verbs)
related.comparison: The dictionary form is the base form of the verb, used for habitual or future actions. The negative form expresses that an action doesn't happen or a state doesn't exist. For る-Verbs, the negative is formed by changing る to ない (e.g., 食べる → 食べない). う-Verbs have different conjugation patterns. Be careful: not all verbs ending in る are る-Verbs—for example, 帰る ('to return') is actually an う-Verb.

related.title: たところだ
related.meaning: Just finished doing, Was just doing
related.comparison: たところだ and るところだ share the same structure, with the tense indicating whether the action is complete or not. るところだ uses the non-past form to mean 'about to do' something, e.g. 晩ご飯を食べるところだ ('I'm just about to eat dinner.'). たところだ uses the past form to mean 'just did' something, e.g. 晩ご飯を食べたところだ ('I just ate dinner.').

related.title: かけ
related.meaning: Half-, Not yet finished, About to, Begin to
related.comparison: ところだ and かけ both express incomplete actions, but with a key difference. るところだ emphasizes that the action is just about to happen, e.g. リンゴを食べるところだ ('I'm about to eat an apple.'). かけ indicates the action has started but isn't finished, e.g. 食べかけのリンゴ ('a half-eaten apple').

related.title: Verb[volitional]とする
related.meaning: Try to, Attempt to, To be about to
related.comparison: Both can describe actions about to happen, but with different nuance. るところだ simply states that something is on the verge of occurring. Verb[volitional] + とする adds a sense of intent or effort, and is often used when the action may not actually happen, e.g. 子どもはなんでも食べようとする ('Kids will try to eat anything.'), where 食べるところだ wouldn't be appropriate.

related.title: ところだった ①
related.meaning: Almost, Nearly, About to, On the verge of
related.comparison: るところだ and ところだった① both express that an action is or was about to happen, but differ in outcome. るところだ means the action is about to occur and likely will. ところだった① means it was on the verge of happening, but didn't in the end.

related.title: まくる
related.meaning: Like crazy, Keep doing (without a second thought), Nothing but, On and on, Do … all around, To do a lot of, One after another, All over, Keep, Everything, Constantly, All the time
related.comparison: Both express excess, but they highlight different things. わ〜わ（で） describes a chaotic situation where multiple actions or states occur together, emphasizing the overall atmosphere or commotion, as in 子どもたちが泣くわ騒ぐわで大変だった ('The kids were crying and making noise, and it was chaos.'). まくる focuses on a single action being done repeatedly or excessively by the subject, emphasizing behavior rather than atmosphere, as in 文句を言いまくる ('He keeps complaining over and over.').

related.title: ばかり
related.meaning: Just, Only, Nothing but
related.comparison: Both describe a situation dominated by repeated or excessive actions, but the nuance differs. わ〜わ（で） emphasizes noisy, uncontrolled, or emotionally charged repetition, often highlighting disorder or annoyance, as in 子どもたちが泣くわ叫ぶわで大変だった ('The children were crying and shouting nonstop, and it was a mess.'). ばかり focuses on one action happening repeatedly or continuing in the same direction, often with frustration or resignation, as in 彼は文句言ってばかりいる ('He does nothing but complain.').

related.title: ようがない・ようもない
related.slug: ようがない・ようもない
related.meaning: There's no way to, It's impossible to
related.comparison: わけがない and ようがない・ようもない are similar in that both express impossibility. わけがない rejects something as logically unthinkable, e.g. 彼がそんなことをするわけがない ('There's no way he'd do that'). ようがない emphasizes that there is no way or means to do something, e.g. 助けようがない ('There's no way to help').

related.title: そうもない
related.meaning: Very unlikely to, Does not seem that, Does not appear that
related.comparison: そうもない and わけがない are similar in that both express the unlikelihood of an event. そうもない suggests low probability based on observation, e.g. 雨が降りそうもない ('It seems unlikely that it'll rain'), while わけがない is an emphatic denial based on the speaker's logical conclusion, e.g. 勝てるわけがない ('There's no way we can win').

related.title: ことはない
related.meaning: There is no need to, Never happens
related.comparison: These expressions are similar in that both emphatically express negation, but they differ in nuance. ことはない implies something never happens, or literally 'there are no cases of (A)', e.g. 二度と会うことはない ('I'll never talk to him again / a second time.'). In contrast, わけがない expresses strong denial or impossibility, e.g. 負けるわけがない ('There's no way I can lose.')

related.title: わけではない
related.meaning: It doesn't mean that, It is not the case, It's not like
related.comparison: Both of these patterns with わけ express negation, but with different nuances. わけではない denies a possible interpretation or assumption, e.g. 'it's not the case that (A)', while わけがない is a stronger, categorical denial, meaning 'there's no way that (A)' or '(A) is impossible'.

related.title: はずがない
related.meaning: Hardly possible, Cannot be, Highly unlikely, Improbable
related.comparison: はずがない and わけがない are similar in that they both express a strong denial of a possibility, similar to the English 'There's no way that (A)', but with slightly different nuances. はずがない gives the sense that the speaker is drawing a conclusion based on their own reasoning, often with a sense of surprise or disbelief. In contrast, わけがない expresses an even stronger denial, as if the impossibility is an obvious fact.

antonyms.title: はずがない
antonyms.meaning: Hardly possible, Cannot be, Highly unlikely, Improbable
antonyms.comparison: はずがない denies something based on logic or evidence, while わけだ draws a conclusion or explains a reason. For example, 来るはずがない ('There's no way he'll come.') vs. 来るわけだ ('So that's why he's coming.'). One rejects a possibility, while the other clarifies it.

related.title: ため(に)
related.meaning: For, For the sake of, In order to
related.comparison: わけだ and ため(に) are similar in that both can be used when expressing a reason, but they mean quite different things. わけだ is used when drawing a conclusion, like the English 'so that's why (A)', while ため(に) on the other hand means 'for the sake of (A)' or 'due to (A)'.

related.title: だけに
related.meaning: As might be expected, Because, All the more because, It's only natural
related.comparison: Both だけに and わけだ can be used to draw conclusions, but differ in tone and focus. だけに emphasizes an expected result based on a known condition, e.g. 忙しい人だけに来ないと思った ('Precisely because he's a busy man, I thought he wouldn't come'), while わけだ indicates the speaker's realization based on something previously expressed or observed, e.g. だから来ないわけだ ('So that's why he's not coming').

related.title: だけのことはある
related.meaning: No wonder, Not surprising that, As expected
related.comparison: Both だけのことはある and わけだ can be used when drawing conclusions, but function differently. だけのことはある affirms that an outcome is appropriate or deserved in a positive sense, e.g. プロだけのことはある ('It's as expected from a pro'), while わけだ signals the speaker's understanding or conclusion, e.g. だから負けたわけだ ('So that's why we lost'). The latter conveys realization, in either a positive or negative sense.

related.title: ことだ
related.meaning: Should, Ought
related.comparison: Both express reasoning in a broad sense, but are used differently. わけだ explains why something is the case, e.g. 暖房が壊れているのか。だから寒いわけだ ('So the heater's broken. That explains why it's cold.') ことだ gives strong advice or instruction, e.g. 寒いなら暖房をつけることだ ('If it's cold, you should turn on the heater.')

related.title: はずだ
related.meaning: Bound to (be), Expected to (be), Should be (able)
related.comparison: Both express logical conclusions, but with different emphasis. はずだ conveys expectation or prediction, as in 来るはずだ ('He should come.'). わけだ explains a reason behind something known or observed, like 来るわけだ ('So that's why he's coming.'). はずだ leans future-oriented, わけだ explanatory.

synonyms.title: のはXの方だ
synonyms.meaning: It is...that..., It is...who..., ...is the one that..., ...is the one who...
synonyms.comparison: のはXの方だ emphatically states what is true or relevant, often in contrast with something else, e.g. 勝ったのは彼の方だ ('He was the one who won.') わけではない, by contrast, qualifies or softens a statement, e.g. 彼が勝ったわけではない ('It's not that he won.') One affirms directly; the other downplays or denies.

related.title: というわけではない
related.meaning: It is not that, It doesn't mean that, It is not always the case
related.comparison: Both わけではない and というわけではない express the idea of 'it doesn't mean that (A)' or 'it's not necessarily the case that (A)', but differ in usage and nuance. わけではない attaches directly to a verb or adjective phrase to deny a specific assumption or conclusion, while というわけではない uses the quoting particle と and often adds a nuance of clarification or softening of an implied statement.

related.title: とは限らない
related.meaning: Not necessarily so, Is not always true, There is no guarantee that
related.comparison: Both とは限らない and わけではない challenge assumptions, but in different ways. わけではない denies a specific inference or interpretation, e.g. 'it's not the case that (A)', while とは限らない emphasizes exceptions, e.g. 'just because (A), it's not always (B)'.

related.title: というものでもない
related.meaning: There is no guarantee that…, Not necessarily
related.comparison: Both というものでもない and わけではない express that something isn't necessarily true, but differ in tone. わけではない directly denies a potential assumption, while というものでもない has a more indirect or reflective nuance, often meaning 'it's not always necessarily the case that (A)'.

related.title: わけがない
related.meaning: There is no way, There is no reason, It is impossible, Cannot be
related.comparison: Both of these patterns with わけ express negation, but with different nuances. わけではない denies a possible interpretation or assumption, e.g. 'it's not the case that (A)', while わけがない is a stronger, categorical denial, meaning 'there's no way that (A)' or '(A) is impossible'.

related.title: はずがない
related.meaning: Hardly possible, Cannot be, Highly unlikely, Improbable
related.comparison: はずがない strongly rejects something as being true. わけではない denies an assumption while leaving room for nuance. For example, 雨が降るはずがない ('There's no way it will.') vs. 雨が降るわけではない ('It's not that it will rain.'), implying e.g. that the weather might be poor in other ways, etc.

antonyms.title: ないわけにはいかない
antonyms.slug: ないわけにはいかない
antonyms.meaning: Can't not, No way to avoid
antonyms.comparison: These are opposites in meaning. わけにはいかない expresses an inability to do something, often out of obligation or social pressure, e.g. 行きたくないけど、断るわけにはいかない 'I don't want to go, but I can't refuse.' ないわけにはいかない means the opposite: 'I have no choice but to (A),' e.g. 助けないわけにはいかない 'I can't not help them.'

related.title: かねる
related.meaning: Cannot, Be unable to, Hard to, Hesitant to
related.comparison: Both express inability to do something, but differ in tone and usage. わけにはいかない follows the non-past form of a verb and implies a moral or social reason for being unable to act, e.g. 約束を破るわけにはいかない 'I can't break my promise.' かねる attaches to the verb stem and is more formal, often used in business settings for polite refusals or hesitations, e.g. 私には分かりかねます 'I'm afraid I'm not sure.'

related.title: ずにはいられない
related.meaning: Cannot help doing something, Cannot resist doing something
related.comparison: These express internal conflict, but from opposite angles. わけにはいかない implies being unable to act due to duty or social constraint, e.g. 断るわけにはいかない 'I just can't refuse.' ずにはいられない expresses an inability to stop oneself from acting, e.g. 笑わずにはいられない 'I couldn't help but laugh.'

related.title: ずにはすまない
related.meaning: Won't get away without, Won't get off without, Have to, Have no choice but to, Can’t not
related.comparison: Both express that an action cannot be avoided for similar but slightly different reasons. ずにはすまない means ‘cannot avoid doing’ due to moral obligation, responsibility, or consequences, often implying inner pressure or remorse, as in 迷惑をかけた以上、謝らずにはすまない ('Since I caused trouble, I can’t avoid apologizing.'). わけにはいかない also means ‘cannot do,’ but focuses on social rules, common sense, or one’s position making the action inappropriate, rather than moral necessity, as in 今日は大事な会議があるから休むわけにはいかない ('I can’t take the day off because there is an important meeting.').

related.title: てはいられない
related.meaning: Can't afford to, Unable to
related.comparison: Both express that an action can't be taken, but for different reasons. わけにはいかない implies restraint due to strong internal or societal reasons, e.g. 泣くわけにはいかない 'I can't very well cry.' てはいられない conveys urgency or pressure, suggesting that one can't allow a situation to continue, e.g. 怠けてはいられない 'I can't afford to slack off.'

related.title: 折角
related.meaning: With trouble, At great pains, Take the trouble, Might as well..., Rare/long-awaited occasion
related.comparison: 折角 and わざわざ both convey effort or intentionality, but differ in nuance. 折角 highlights the value of an effort or opportunity, often implying it would be wasteful not to use it. For example, 折角来たのだから食べましょう ('We came all this way, so let's eat it'). わざわざ emphasizes that someone deliberately went out of their way. For example, わざわざ届けてくれてありがとう ('Thank you for going out of your way to deliver it to me').

related.title: に比べて
related.meaning: Compared to, When compared to
related.comparison: Both are used in making comparisons, but に比べて is used to directly compare and contrast two things, e.g. このスマホはあのスマホに比べて安い ('This smartphone is cheaper than that one'), while 割に expresses a gap between expectation and reality, e.g. 値段の割に美味しい ('It's tasty considering the price'). The former makes a neutral comparison; the latter evaluates something against an assumed standard.

related.title: にしては
related.meaning: (Even) considering, (Even) for
related.comparison: 割に and にしては both express something contrary to expectations, but 割に suggests mild contrast with what was assumed, e.g. 高いわりに美味しくなかった ('For how expensive it is, it wasn't tasty'), while にしては more clearly emphasizes how something defies expectations for its role or class, e.g. 初心者にしてはうますぎる ('He's too good for a beginner').

related.title: さえ
related.meaning: Even
related.comparison: Both さえ and 割に convey, in some sense, a characteristic or event contrary to expectations. さえ is a strong 'even' that shows emphatic extremity or surprise, e.g. 子どもさえ分かる ('Even a child understands'). 割に expresses contrast with expectations, e.g. 年の割に元気だ ('He's energetic for his age').

related.title: むしろ
related.meaning: Rather, Instead, Over
related.comparison: むしろ and 割に both involve contrast, but from different perspectives. むしろ introduces a preferred or more accurate option, e.g. 寝るよりむしろ本を読みたい ('Rather than sleep, I'd rather read'). 割に shows discrepancy between expectation and reality, e.g. 安い割に美味しい ('It's good for how cheap it is').

related.title: ものの
related.meaning: Although, But, Even Though
related.comparison: Both ものの and 割に express contrast, but in different ways. ものの links two opposing facts to mean 'despite', e.g. '話せるものの、字は読めない' ('Despite the fact that he can speak, he can't read characters'). 割に describes a quality being out of proportion with something else, e.g. '高い割にはあまり美味しくない' ('For being expensive, it's not very tasty').

synonyms.title: に
synonyms.comparison: を marks the direct object, the thing an action is done to (e.g., リンゴを食べた = '[I] ate an apple'). に has multiple functions, including marking the destination of movement or the target of an action. Be aware that grammar patterns can differ between English and Japanese: for example, 'I passed the test' uses 試験に合格する in Japanese, not 試験を合格する.

related.title: が
related.meaning: Subject marking particle, Identification particle
related.comparison: が and を are basic Japanese particles. が marks the subject—the one doing the action, while を marks the direct object, the thing being acted upon. For example, ジョンがリンゴを食べた means 'John ate an apple.' が is also used in expressions of preference or ability, like コーヒーが好きです ('I like coffee') or 英語がわかります ('I understand English').

related.title: を契機に
related.meaning: Lead to, As a good opportunity to, As a result of, As a trigger
related.comparison: を契機に is used when an event serves as a trigger or catalyst for change, often formal and neutral/positive, as in 留学を契機に視野が広がった (Studying abroad became a catalyst for broadening my horizons). をいいことに means taking advantage of a situation for one’s own benefit, usually with a negative nuance, as in 留守をいいことに泥棒が入った (A thief took advantage of the absence to break in).

related.title: はもちろん
related.meaning: Not only but also, Let alone, Not to mention, Of course
related.comparison: はもちろん highlights an obvious example together with a more unexpected addition, e.g. 寿司はもちろん納豆も食べる ('He eats not only sushi, but also natto'). をはじめ is used to give a leading example from a larger group, e.g. 校長先生をはじめ多くの人が来た ('Many people came, starting with the principal').

related.title: ばかりでなく
related.meaning: Not only…but also, As well as
related.comparison: ばかりでなく and をはじめ both introduce additional items, but their focus differs. ばかりでなく is neutral and treats both elements as equal, e.g. 彼は英語ばかりでなく日本語も話せる ('He speaks not only English but also Japanese'). をはじめ highlights the first item as the most representative, e.g. 校長先生をはじめ、先生たちが全員参加した ('Starting with the principal, all the teachers joined').

related.title: だけでなく(て)～も
related.meaning: Not only ~ but ~ also, Not just ~ but ~ also
related.comparison: をはじめ gives a representative example and implies that others follow, e.g. 東京をはじめ、全国でイベントが行われた ('The event was held across the country, starting with Tokyo'). だけでなく(て)～も lists multiple elements without suggesting one is more representative, e.g. 東京だけでなく大阪でも人気だ ('It's popular not only in Tokyo but also in Osaka').

related.title: に加えて
related.meaning: Not only...but also, In addition, Not to mention
related.comparison: をはじめ introduces a prominent example from a group, e.g. 学生をはじめ多くの人が参加した ('Many people participated, starting with students'). に加えて simply adds another element to the first, e.g. 雨に加えて風も強い ('In addition to the rain, the wind is also strong').

related.title: を皮切りに
related.meaning: Beginning of, Starting with
related.comparison: を皮切りに marks the beginning of a sequence of events, e.g. 東京公演を皮切りに全国ツアーが始まった ('With the Tokyo performance as the start, their national tour began'). をはじめ lists a representative example from a group, focusing on inclusion rather than sequence, e.g. 田中先生をはじめ、皆さんに感謝している ('Beginning with Prof. Tanaka, I'm grateful to everyone').

related.title: に限らず
related.meaning: Not just, Not only… but also
related.comparison: をはじめ highlights one example among similar ones, e.g. 日本をはじめ多くの国で使われている ('It's used in many countries, starting with Japan'). に限らず expands the scope beyond the first item, e.g. 学生に限らず誰でも参加できる ('Not limited to students, anyone can participate').

related.title: だけでなく
related.meaning: Not only… but also
related.comparison: だけでなく and をはじめ both express non-exclusivity, but differ in nuance. だけでなく simply means 'not only (A), but also (B)', e.g. 英語だけでなく、日本語も話せる ('He can speak not only English, but also Japanese.'). In contrast, をはじめ implies that something is the first in a list of many, e.g. 日本語をはじめ、様々な外国語が話せる ('He can speak many foreign languages, starting with Japanese.').

related.title: しか～ない
related.meaning: There's only, Nothing but
related.comparison: Both convey the idea of 'nothing but,' but with different nuances. をおいてほかに～ない almost always has a positive nuance, suggesting that the thing in question is the only one that possesses some good quality, e.g. 日本語を学ぶなら文プロをおいてほかにない ('If you want to learn Japanese, there's nothing but Bunpro.'). In contrast, しか～ない emphasizes lack and is often used with a negative nuance, e.g. １００円しかない ('I only have 100 yen.').

related.title: いがい
related.meaning: Except・besides, Other than, With the exception of
related.comparison: Both forms express exclusion, but with key differences. いがい (often written in kanji as 以外)  attaches directly to nouns, e.g. サッカー以外 'other than soccer'. It's common in both speech and writing. In contrast, をおいてほかに〜ない is much more formal and academic. It's also used almost exclusively to positively highlight something, while いがい carries no such nuance.

related.title: ほかに(も)・ほか(に)は
related.meaning: Other, Another, Any other, Anything else, As well as, Besides, In addition
related.comparison: Both use ほか (他, other) to express contrast or exclusivity. ほかに(も) and ほか(に)は mean 'other than' and are used to compare or list alternatives. をおいてほかに〜ない is a stronger expression meaning 'no one/nothing else but', as in 彼をおいてほかに適任者はいない ('There's no one more suitable than him.').

related.title: で
related.meaning: With, By (using)
related.comparison: を以て can be though of as a formal version of the basic particle で when used to mean 'at this point in time', e.g. これ（で・を以て）閉会します ('With this, we will end the meeting.'). Note that をもって would not typically be used with the meaning of で indicating 'place of an action', e.g. 大手企業で働く 'to work at a major corporation'.

synonyms.title: にもとづいて
synonyms.comparison: These phrases both mean 'based on' and can overlap, but differ slightly. にもとづいて is more formal and tends to show a concrete or logical foundation for an action or product. をもとに is less formal (but still used in formal contexts) and can indicate a general basis or inspiration where にもとづいて may not fit.

related.title: によると・によれば
related.meaning: According to
related.comparison: Both involve a basis or source, but differ in usage. によると・によれば reports information, e.g. 天気予報によると雨が降るそうです ('According to the weather forecast, it will rain.') をもとに means doing something based on a source or foundation, e.g. 事実をもとに小説のストーリーを考えた ('I thought up a story for a novel based on true events.')

related.title: にしたがって
related.meaning: As, Following, As then, According to, In accordance with
related.comparison: をもとに and にしたがって can both be translated as according to or based on, but differ in nuance. をもとに suggests a foundation or inspiration, as in 実話をもとに書いた小説 (a novel written based on actual events). にしたがって focuses more on being in line with or following something, as in 指示にしたがって (according to instructions). It can also mark proportional changes, as in 普及にしたがって (as it spread).

related.title: に応じて
related.meaning: Depending or based on, According to, In response to, Suitable for or to
related.comparison: をもとに indicates a fixed source or basis for something, as in 実話をもとに小説を書く (Write a novel based on true events). に応じて shows flexible adjustment or response to conditions, as in 状況に応じて対応を決める (Decide the response according to the situation).

related.title: を前提に
related.meaning: On the premise of
related.comparison: Both indicate a basis, but the type of basis differs. を前提に sets a condition or assumption that must hold true, often before action is taken, as in 合意があることを前提に話を進める ('We proceed with the discussion on the assumption that there is agreement.'). をもとに means ‘based on’ and refers to concrete materials, data, or sources actually used to create or decide something, as in 実話をもとに映画が作られた ('The movie was made based on a true story.').

related.title: をよそに
related.meaning: Ignoring, Disregarding, Despite
related.comparison: Both expressions describe acting despite surrounding circumstances, but the attitude differs. をものともせず means ‘in defiance of’ or ‘undeterred by,’ emphasizing that an obstacle has no effect on the action, as in 雨をものともせず試合は続けられた (‘The game continued undeterred by the rain.’). をよそに means ‘ignoring’ or ‘regardless of' highlighting indifference to what others are doing or feeling rather than resistance to difficulty, as in 周囲の心配をよそに彼は一人で決めた (‘Ignoring everyone’s concerns, he decided on his own.’).

related.title: をよそに
related.meaning: Ignoring, Disregarding, Despite
related.comparison: Both expressions describe acting despite surrounding circumstances, but the attitude differs. をものともせず means ‘in defiance of’ or ‘undeterred by,’ emphasizing that an obstacle has no effect on the action, as in 雨をものともせず試合は続けられた (‘The game continued undeterred by the rain.’). をよそに means ‘ignoring’ or ‘regardless of' highlighting indifference to what others are doing or feeling rather than resistance to difficulty, as in 周囲の心配をよそに彼は一人で決めた (‘Ignoring everyone’s concerns, he decided on his own.’).

related.title: をものともせず
related.meaning: Making nothing of, Ignoring, In defiance of, Regardless of, Disregarding
related.comparison: Both expressions describe acting despite surrounding circumstances, but the attitude differs. をものともせず means ‘in defiance of’ or ‘undeterred by,’ emphasizing that an obstacle has no effect on the action, as in 雨をものともせず試合は続けられた (‘The game continued undeterred by the rain.’). をよそに means ‘ignoring’ or ‘regardless of' highlighting indifference to what others are doing or feeling rather than resistance to difficulty, as in 周囲の心配をよそに彼は一人で決めた (‘Ignoring everyone’s concerns, he decided on his own.’).

related.title: をものともせず
related.meaning: Making nothing of, Ignoring, In defiance of, Regardless of, Disregarding
related.comparison: Both expressions describe acting despite surrounding circumstances, but the attitude differs. をものともせず means ‘in defiance of’ or ‘undeterred by,’ emphasizing that an obstacle has no effect on the action, as in 雨をものともせず試合は続けられた (‘The game continued undeterred by the rain.’). をよそに means ‘ignoring’ or ‘regardless of' highlighting indifference to what others are doing or feeling rather than resistance to difficulty, as in 周囲の心配をよそに彼は一人で決めた (‘Ignoring everyone’s concerns, he decided on his own.’).

related.title: で
related.meaning: With, By (using)
related.comparison: を以て can be though of as a highly formal/written-style version of the basic particle で when used to mean 'with'/'by means of', e.g. 鉛筆で書く 'write with a pencil' vs. 死を以て償う 'to atone (e.g. for a sin) with one's death' or 'at this point in time', e.g. これ（で・を以て）閉会します ('With this, we will end the meeting.'). Note that を以て would not typically be used with the meaning of で indicating 'place of an action', e.g. 大手企業で働く 'to work at a major corporation'.

related.title: ざるを得ない
related.meaning: Can't help doing, Have no choice but
related.comparison: Both express an obligation, but from different perspectives. を余儀なくさせる implies forcing someone into a difficult situation, e.g. 退去を余儀なくさせる 'it (e.g. the situation) forced them to leave' vs. 退去せざるを得ない '(we/I) have no choice but to leave'.

related.title: を余儀なくされる
related.meaning: To be forced to do something
related.comparison: These are the same basic expression, except that one is causative (forcing someone else to do something) while one is passive (expressing being forced to do something), e.g. 入院を余儀なくさせる 'to force someone to be hospitalized' vs. 入院を余儀なくされる 'to be forced to be hospitalized'.

related.title: しか～ない
related.meaning: There's only, Nothing but
related.comparison: を余儀なくされる and しかない are similar in that both express having no choice but to do something, though their usage differs. を余儀なくされる follows nouns and has a nuance of being forced by circumstances, e.g. 中止を余儀なくされる 'be forced to cancel.' In contrast, しかない follows verbs and focuses on lack of choice rather than external pressure, e.g. 行くしかない ('There's no choice but to go.').

related.title: を余儀なくさせる
related.meaning: Force to, To compel, Make unavoidable
related.comparison: These are the same basic expression, except that one is causative (forcing someone else to do something) while one is passive (expressing being forced to do something), e.g. 入院を余儀なくさせる 'to force someone to be hospitalized' vs. 入院を余儀なくされる 'to be forced to be hospitalized'.

related.title: かねる
related.meaning: Cannot, Be unable to, Hard to, Hesitant to
