related.comparison: を兼ねて and かねる come from the same verb, but their uses differ. を兼ねて expresses that something serves two purposes simultaneously, as in 運動を兼ねて散歩してきた (I went for a walk, also to get some exercise). かねる attaches to verb stems to express inability politely or indirectly, as in 私には分かりかねます (I’m afraid I don’t understand).

related.title: かたがた
related.meaning: Partly for (the purpose of), In addition to, Along with, By the way, And also to, Incidentally, While
related.comparison: Both are similar in that they describe doing something 'along with' or 'incidentally' to another action. かたがた is particularly formal and used especially in writing, e.g. お礼かたがたお伺いしたく存じます ('I hope to visit you, in part to thank you.'), while を兼ねて (and variant forms like も兼ねて) are still somewhat formal but used more widely, e.g. ダイエットも兼ねて毎朝散歩する 'to walk every morning, in part for purposes of a diet'.

synonyms.title: にかかわらず
synonyms.meaning: Regardless of, Whether or, No matter, With or without
synonyms.comparison: Both mean regardless of, but differ slightly in register. にかかわらず is more formal or literary, often used even with negative implications, as in 年齢にかかわらず (Regardless of age). を問わず is more common in daily usage, treating conditions equally without preference, as in 学歴を問わず (Regardless of academic background).

related.title: にもかかわらず
related.meaning: Despite, In spite of, Regardless, Nevertheless
related.comparison: Both can mean regardless of, but are used differently. にもかかわらず expresses a surprising or unexpected result in spite of circumstances, as in 年齢にもかかわらず元気だ (In spite of his age, he is energetic). を問わず applies more broadly and neutrally, indicating that something holds true in all cases, as in 性別を問わず参加できる (Participation is open regardless of gender).

related.title: にせよ・にしろ
related.meaning: Even if, Even though, No matter, Whether… or
related.comparison: を問わず follows a noun (often a category) to show disregard for distinctions, as in 年齢を問わず (Regardless of age). にせよ・にしろ also implies that something holds true under different conditions, but it typically introduces a possibility that is then contrasted or disputed, as in 行くにせよ行かないにせよ (Whether you go or not…). を問わず highlights inclusiveness, while にせよ・にしろ highlights hypothetical contrasts.

related.title: どうしても
related.meaning: Regardless of, No matter what
related.comparison: どうしても and を問わず share the meaning of 'no matter' or 'regardless of', but are used differently. どうしても appears at the beginning of a sentence to express persistence or inevitability. For example, どうしても勝ちたい ('I want to win no matter what'). を問わず usually follows nouns or question words and shows that something holds true regardless of conditions. For example, 年齢を問わず参加できます ('You can participate regardless of age').

related.title: ～ても～なくても
related.meaning: Whether ~ or not
related.comparison: ～ても～なくても and を問わず both express 'regardless of', but ～ても～なくても contrasts doing something with not doing it, e.g. 雨が降っても降らなくても ('whether it rains or not'), while を問わず states that something holds true regardless of category or attribute, e.g. 年齢を問わず ('regardless of age').

related.title: に伴って・に伴い
related.meaning: As, Due,  Along with, Hand-in-hand with, Resulted in, Be consequent upon
related.comparison: Both are formal expressions describing one event influencing another, but differ slightly in usage. を契機に emphasizes a turning point or opportunity that led to change, as in 入院を契機にタバコをやめた (I gave up smoking on the occasion of my hospitalization). に伴って・に伴い often appears in news reports to describe cause-and-effect relationships.

related.title: をいいことに
related.meaning: To take advantage of
related.comparison: を契機に is used when an event serves as a trigger or catalyst for change, often formal and neutral/positive, as in 留学を契機に視野が広がった (Studying abroad became a catalyst for broadening my horizons). をいいことに means taking advantage of a situation for one’s own benefit, usually with a negative nuance, as in 留守をいいことに泥棒が入った (A thief took advantage of the absence to break in).

related.title: きっかけ
related.meaning: Lead to, Because of, As a start or trigger of, An opportunity
related.comparison: Both indicate events that lead to change, but differ in register. を契機に is formal and often marks major turning points, as in 結婚を契機に転職した (Marriage became the occasion for changing jobs). きっかけ is casual and broad, used for any kind of trigger, as in 友達の紹介がきっかけで知り合った (We met thanks to a friend’s introduction).

related.title: を踏まえて
related.meaning: Based on, Considering, Keeping in mind, Taking into account, Therefore
related.comparison: Both refer to using information as a basis, but the stance is different. を前提に means ‘on the assumption that’ and treats something as a condition that must be accepted before proceeding, often hypothetical or conditional, as in 雨が降ることを前提に計画を立てる ('We make the plan assuming it will rain.'). を踏まえて means ‘taking into account’ and is used after facts or results that are already known, emphasizing reflection or consideration rather than assumption, as in 調査結果を踏まえて方針を決めた ('We decided the policy taking the survey results into account.').

related.title: をもとに
related.meaning: Based on, From, According to
related.comparison: Both indicate a basis, but the type of basis differs. を前提に sets a condition or assumption that must hold true, often before action is taken, as in 合意があることを前提に話を進める ('We proceed with the discussion on the assumption that there is agreement.'). をもとに means ‘based on’ and refers to concrete materials, data, or sources actually used to create or decide something, as in 実話をもとに映画が作られた ('The movie was made based on a true story.').

related.title: に関する・に関して
related.meaning: Related to, About, Regarding
related.comparison: These phrases are similar in that both can be translated as 'about (A)' or 'concerning (A)', but they differ in nuance. に関する・に関して is a more neutral and general-purpose expression that can be used to express 'about (A)' in a wide variety of contexts, while を巡って is more formal and often implies differing opinions or viewpoints surrounding a topic, and is therefore used more narrowly.

related.title: のももっともだ
related.meaning: May well, Have or has good reason for, No wonder, … Is only natural
related.comparison: These expressions are relatively synonymous in meaning and both refer to a sense of 'on the occasion of' or 'using this as an opportunity'. They are interchangeable in many situations but differ in that を機会に can be found in many variant patterns like を機会（に・と）して, and so forth, while を機に is usually found in a very specific pattern.

related.title: をはじめ
related.meaning: Not only...but also..., Starting with
related.comparison: を皮切りに marks the beginning of a sequence of events, e.g. 東京公演を皮切りに全国ツアーが始まった ('With the Tokyo performance as the start, their national tour began'). をはじめ lists a representative example from a group, focusing on inclusion rather than sequence, e.g. 田中先生をはじめ、皆さんに感謝している ('Beginning with Prof. Tanaka, I'm grateful to everyone').

related.title: を通じて・を通して
related.meaning: Through, Via, Throughout
related.comparison: These are both formal expressions that use the て form of a verb to express the idea of 'through' or 'after'. を経て (as might be expected from its derivation from the verb 経る（へる） ’to pass’ or 経つ（たつ） ‘to pass (specifically of time)’ can be used with time expressions, e.g. 2年の交際を経て結婚しました ('They got married after a two-year relationship.'). In contrast, を通じて・を通して can be used for experiences or other avenues that do not necessarily imply a passage of time, e.g. 弁護士を通じて相手に連絡する 'contact someone through a lawyer'.

related.title: にもとづいて
related.meaning: Based on, In accordance with
related.comparison: Both have a general meaning of 'based on', but with slightly different nuances. にもとづいて typically shows strict reliance on a fixed source like rules or data, e.g. 法律にもとづいて判断する 'ake a decision based on the law'. を踏まえて means taking something into account as a reference or consideration, e.g. アンケートの結果を踏まえて調節する 'make adjustments taking the survey results into consideration'.

related.title: を前提に
related.meaning: On the premise of
related.comparison: Both refer to using information as a basis, but the stance is different. を前提に means ‘on the assumption that’ and treats something as a condition that must be accepted before proceeding, often hypothetical or conditional, as in 雨が降ることを前提に計画を立てる ('We make the plan assuming it will rain.'). を踏まえて means ‘taking into account’ and is used after facts or results that are already known, emphasizing reflection or consideration rather than assumption, as in 調査結果を踏まえて方針を決めた ('We decided the policy taking the survey results into account.').

related.title: を経て
related.meaning: Via, Through, After (experience at job, university, etc.), Pass through
related.comparison: These are both formal expressions that use the て form of a verb to express the idea of 'through' or 'after'. を経て (as might be expected from its derivation from the verb 経る（へる） ’to pass’ or 経つ（たつ） ‘to pass (specifically of time)’ can be used with time expressions, e.g. 2年の交際を経て結婚しました ('They got married after a two-year relationship.'). In contrast, を通じて・を通して can be used for experiences or other avenues that do not necessarily imply a passage of time, e.g. 弁護士を通じて相手に連絡する 'contact someone through a lawyer'.

related.title: きり
related.meaning: Only, Just, Since
related.comparison: Both を限りに  and きり mark a point after which something stops or changes, but they differ in formality and nuance. を限りに is a fixed, formal expression used mainly in written or public contexts to indicate a clear and definitive final point, often for events, careers, or official actions, as in 本日を限りに閉店します (‘As of today, we will close for good.’). In contrast, きり is more casual and flexible, commonly used in everyday speech to say that something has not happened again since a certain point, and often used with number + counter expressions such as 一度きりしか会っていない ('I haven't met him except that one time.')

related.title: いがい
related.meaning: Except・besides, Other than, With the exception of
related.comparison: いがい and を除いて both express exclusion, similar to 'other than' or 'with the exception of' in English. Their meanings are very close and often interchangeable. The main difference is that を除いて is more formal and mainly used in writing, while いがい appears in both casual speech and writing. 土日以外毎日働いています and 土日を除いて毎日働いています both mean 'I work every day except Saturday and Sunday.'.

related.title: はともかく
related.meaning: Setting aside, Apart from
related.comparison: はともかく and を除いて both suggest setting something aside, but with different nuance. はともかく downplays relevance to the main point, as in 田中さんはともかく、鈴木さんは来ると思う (I don’t know about Tanaka, but I think Suzuki will come). を除いて explicitly excludes something, as in 田中さんを除いて全員来ると思うよ (I think everyone will come except Tanaka).

related.title: ため(に)
related.meaning: For, For the sake of, In order to
related.comparison: んがため(に) can be considered an old-fashioned, literary form of ため(に). While both can express the idea of 'for the purpose of (A)' or 'in order to (A)', for example 生きんがために食べる 'eat (in order) to live' sounds like a much more formal and poetic version of 生きるために食べる (same meaning).

related.title: が
related.meaning: But, However
related.comparison: Both express contrast, but が is more formal and used in writing, like 面白いが難しい ('It's interesting, but difficult'). When combined with ～んだ・～んです, as in 行きたいんですが, it softens the sentence and invites a response or action from the listener.

related.title: けど・だけど
related.meaning: But, However
related.comparison: Both introduce contrast, like the English 'but'. けど・だけど are common in speech, as in 難しいけど面白い ('It's difficult, but interesting.'). Adding ～ん to this pattern adds an explanatory nuance, often used to imply a request or hesitation, e.g. 難しいんですけど 'It's difficult, but...(do you want me to do it anyway?, etc.)'.

related.title: ～んです・のです
related.meaning: Explanatory, Emphasis, The fact is
related.comparison: ～んです・のです adds an explanatory tone to a statement, like 難しいんです ('It's difficult, I'm telling you.'). When paired with contrastive particles like けど or が, the speaker often implies something left unsaid, as in 難しいんですけど ('It's hard, but...', implying 'and that's why I can't do it', etc.).

related.title: らしい ①
related.meaning: Seems like, Apparently, I heard
related.comparison: んだって and らしい ① both convey information from another source, but with different nuances and levels of formality. らしい ① suggests hearsay with a nuance of certainty, indicating the speaker believes it to be true, e.g. 'Apparently it's (A).'. In contrast, んだって is highly casual and includes an explanatory nuance, similar to 'He/she/they said/say (A), you know.'.

related.title: ときいた
related.meaning: I heard that
related.comparison: Both ときいた and んだって express hearsay, but with different nuances and formality. ときいた means 'I heard (A).' and conveys information directly heard by the speaker, e.g. 高いときいた ('I heard it was expensive.'). In contrast, んだって is casual and adds an explanatory nuance, like 'They say (A).' or 'I heard that (A).', e.g. 高いんだって ('They say it's expensive, you know.').

related.title: そうだ
related.meaning: I heard that, It is said that
related.comparison: Both express hearsay, but with different tone and usage. んだって is very casual, often used in everyday conversation, and carries an explanatory tone due to んだ, e.g. 明日休みなんだって ('They said tomorrow's a day off, you know.') そうだ is more neutral and indirect, with no explanatory nuance, e.g. 明日休みだそうだ ('I heard tomorrow's a day off.')

related.title: って
related.meaning: Casual quotation
related.comparison: って (a contracted form of と) is a casual quoting particle used to mark direct or indirect speech, thoughts, feelings, or to introduce a topic. んだって, which combines って with the explanatory ん・の, is used to express 'I heard' or 'He/she/they said.' While って can follow any word, んだって requires な before な-Adjectives or nouns.

related.title: だって
related.meaning: I heard/was said, Because, But, Even or also, Even if or even though
related.comparison: Both expressions involve だって, but their usage differs. んだって appears at the end of a sentence and means 'I hear (A)' or 'they say (A)', with an explanatory nuance added by ん. In contrast, だって is more flexible. It can appear at the beginning, middle, or end of a sentence, and has several meanings. It can function as a casual 'because', 'even though', or 'they say (A)', depending on context.

related.title: とか
related.meaning: I heard that… something like, They said ... or something
related.comparison: These two expressions are similar in that both can serve as casual expressions of hearsay, i.e. 'they say (A)' or 'I hear (A)'. The difference is that んだって has a slightly more explanatory and emphatic nuance due to the presence of ん, while とか has an uncertain or non-specific tone, i.e. 'I hear (A) or something', because of the presence of the question particle か.

related.title: とばかり（に）
related.meaning: As if (to say), Seeming that
related.comparison: Both express the idea of 'as if (something is happening)', with とばかり（に） being essentially a shortened form of 言わんばかりに 'as if to say'. んばかりに describes a state or action that appears on the verge of happening, emphasizing intensity or appearance itself, as in 泣かんばかりに訴えた ('She pleaded as if she were about to cry.'). とばかり（に） focuses more on inferred intent or attitude behind an action, often meaning ‘seizing it as a chance’ or ‘as if to say,’ as in 選手はチャンスとばかりに相手を睨んだ ('Seeing it as a chance, the athlete stared at his opponent.').

related.title: とばかり（に）
related.meaning: As if (to say), Seeming that
related.comparison: Both express the idea of 'as if (something is happening)', with とばかり（に） being essentially a shortened form of 言わんばかりに 'as if to say'. んばかりに describes a state or action that appears on the verge of happening, emphasizing intensity or appearance itself, as in 泣かんばかりに訴えた ('She pleaded as if she were about to cry.'). とばかり（に） focuses more on inferred intent or attitude behind an action, often meaning ‘seizing it as a chance’ or ‘as if to say,’ as in 選手はチャンスとばかりに相手を睨んだ ('Seeing it as a chance, the athlete stared at his opponent.').

related.title: ことか
related.meaning: How…, What..., God knows...
related.comparison: 一体 and ことか both add emphasis to questions, but differ in nuance. 一体 strengthens genuine questions with a tone of surprise or frustration, like 'What in the world?', e.g. 一体何があったんですか？ 'What in the world happened?' ことか appears in rhetorical questions or exclamations to express strong emotion, e.g. 彼がどんなに努力したことか 'How hard he worked!'

related.title: 万が一
related.meaning: Just in case, In the worst-case scenario, In the unlikely event, By any chance
related.comparison: 万が一 and 一応 both express precaution, but differ in severity and tone. 万が一 is used when considering rare or worst-case scenarios, often with serious implications, as in 万が一事故が起きたら (In the unlikely event of an accident). 一応 implies taking an action just to be safe, even if it’s probably unnecessary, as in 一応持っていく (I’ll bring it just in case).

related.title: 一応 ②
related.meaning: more or less, for the time being, tentatively, roughly
related.comparison: 一応① and 一応② share the same form but differ in meaning. 一応 ① refers to doing something as a precaution, as in 一応確認した (I checked just in case). 一応 ② expresses something provisional, partial, or minimal, as in 一応終わった (It’s done, more or less).

related.title: どんどん
related.meaning: Progressively, Rapidly increasing, More and more
related.comparison: どんどん is an adverb that implies rapid or dramatic change, often in leaps and bounds, and is common in everyday speech. In contrast, 一方だ is more formal and used to describe ongoing change that continues beyond the speaker's control. Structurally, 一方だ follows the verb, unlike どんどん, which appears before it.

related.title: ば〜ほど
related.meaning: The more…the more
related.comparison: Both can be used to describe change, but with different patterns. ば〜ほど expresses a proportional relationship between two things, like 'the more (A), the more (B)', e.g. 練習すればするほど上手になる ('The more you practice, the better you get.') 一方だ shows a continuous, one-way change in a single direction, e.g. 物価は上がる一方だ ('Prices just keep rising.')

related.title: ばかりだ
related.meaning: (Only) continue to, Keep on -ing, More and more
related.comparison: ばかりだ and 一方だ are similar in that both express continuous or progressive change. However, ばかりだ is typically used with negative developments and often carries a more subjective tone, e.g. 状況は悪くなるばかりだ ('The situation keeps getting worse'). 一方だ, on the other hand, sounds more neutral or objective, and doesn't necessarily imply a negative trend, e.g. 人口は増える一方だ ('The population keeps increasing').

related.title: ますます
related.meaning: Increasingly, More and more, Decreasingly, Less and less, Keep -ing
related.comparison: Both 一方だ and ますます express progressive change, but differ in usage. 一方だ follows verbs, often in formal or objective contexts to describe trends, e.g. '物価は上がる一方だ' ('Prices keep rising'). ますます has a similar nuance, but precedes the phrase or sentence, e.g. '彼の人気はますます高まっている' ('His popularity is increasing more and more').

related.title: いよいよ
related.meaning: At last, Finally, More and more
related.comparison: Both 一方だ and いよいよ express change, but differ in usage. 一方だ follows verbs, often in formal or objective contexts to describe trends, e.g. '物価は上がる一方だ' ('Prices keep rising'). いよいよ suggests something is finally or imminently happening, often with anticipation, e.g. 'いよいよ試合が始まる' ('The match is about to begin').

related.title: 一方で
related.meaning: On the other hand, At the same time, While
related.comparison: While they look similar, 一方で and 一方だ serve different functions. 一方で shows contrast between two ideas or situations, e.g. '一方で反対する人もいる' ('On the other hand, some people are against it'). 一方だ follows verbs to describe trends, e.g. '物価は上がる一方だ' ('Prices keep rising').

related.title: つづける
related.meaning: To continue
related.comparison: つづける and 一方だ both indicate continuation but differ in tone. つづける means a simple ongoing action, e.g. 減り続けている 'is still decreasing'. 一方だ follows dictionary-form verbs and adds a sense of progression in one direction, often negatively, e.g., 減る一方だ 'is getting worse and worse'.

related.title: 一応 ①
related.meaning: Just in case, Just to be sure
related.comparison: 一応① and 一応② share the same form but differ in meaning. 一応 ① refers to doing something as a precaution, as in 一応確認した (I checked just in case). 一応 ② expresses something provisional, partial, or minimal, as in 一応終わった (It’s done, more or less).

related.title: 一方だ
related.meaning: More and more, Continue to, Keep on, -er and -er
related.comparison: While they look similar, 一方で and 一方だ serve different functions. 一方で shows contrast between two ideas or situations, e.g. '一方で反対する人もいる' ('On the other hand, some people are against it'). 一方だ follows verbs to describe trends, e.g. '物価は上がる一方だ' ('Prices keep rising').

related.title: 反面
related.meaning: While, On the other hand, Although, However, On the flip side
related.comparison: 一方で and 反面 both show contrast, but differ in tone. 一方で is neutral and presents parallel or opposing facts, e.g. '一方で経済は成長している' ('On the other hand, the economy is growing'). 反面 highlights a downside or contradiction, e.g. '便利な反面、依存しやすい' ('While convenient, it's also easy to become dependent').

related.title: かたわら
related.meaning: While, Besides, In addition to
related.comparison: かたわら and 一方で both describe two actions or conditions, but differ in nuance. かたわら implies doing something secondary alongside a main activity, e.g. '彼は本業のかたわら小説を書いている' ('He writes novels alongside his main job'). 一方で marks contrast or coexistence between broader situations, e.g. '一方で都市化が進んでいる' ('Meanwhile, urbanization is advancing').

related.title: 一応 ①
related.meaning: Just in case, Just to be sure
related.comparison: 万が一 and 一応 both express precaution, but differ in severity and tone. 万が一 is used when considering rare or worst-case scenarios, often with serious implications, as in 万が一事故が起きたら (In the unlikely event of an accident). 一応 implies taking an action just to be safe, even if it’s probably unnecessary, as in 一応持っていく (I’ll bring it just in case).

related.title: ばあいは
related.meaning: In the event of, In the case of
related.comparison: ばあいは and 万が一 are related in that both refer to potential situations. ばあいは (often written in kanji as 場合は) means 'in the case of' or 'in the event that'. For example, 行く場合は 'in the event that you go'. On the other hand, 万が一 is typically used at the start of a sentence to highlight a rare or unlikely case. Because they serve different roles, the two can even be used together, e.g. 万が一の場合は 'in the unlikely event that (A)'.

related.title: 上に
related.meaning: As well, Besides, In addition to
related.comparison: 上 and 上に share the same kanji but serve distinct functions. 上に adds reasons or qualities in a cumulative way, as in 綺麗な上に頭もいい (In addition to being pretty, she’s also smart). 上 by itself expresses viewpoint, as in 法律上の問題 (a problem from a legal standpoint). The former is additive, the latter indicates perspective. Both are relatively formal expressions.

related.title: から言うと
related.meaning: Speaking in terms of, From the point of view of
related.comparison: から言うと and 上 both indicate viewpoint, but から言うと introduces a subjective opinion from a specific angle, e.g. 学生の立場から言うと反対だ ('Speaking from a student's viewpoint, I'm against it'). 上 expresses evaluation based on a defined category, e.g. 法律上問題はない ('There's no problem in terms of the law').

related.title: からすると・からすれば
related.slug: からすると-からすれば
related.meaning: Judging from, Considering
related.comparison: 上 and からすると・からすれば both indicate a perspective or basis for judgment, but differ in tone and scope. 上 is formal and often tied to institutional or thematic standpoints, as in 理論上の問題 (a problem from a theoretical standpoint). からすると・からすれば is more subjective and flexible, used with personal or situational cues, as in 表情からすると彼は怒っている (Judging from his expression, he’s angry).

antonyms.title: はじめる
antonyms.comparison: はじめる attaches to verb stems to express 'beginning to' do something, e.g. 作りはじめる 'to start making'. In contrast, 上がる・上げる also attach to verb stems but express completing an action, e.g. 作りあげる 'to finish making', often with a sense of effort or finality.

antonyms.title: 切れない
antonyms.meaning: Being unable to finish or complete
antonyms.comparison: These phrases are opposites—one expresses a completed action, the other, incompleteness. 上がる・上げる is typically used when the action results in a finished product, e.g. '論文を書き上げる' ('finish writing a thesis'). 切れない expresses the inability to finish something fully, e.g. '食べ切れなかった' ('I couldn't finish eating it').

related.title: 抜く
related.meaning: All~, Completely, See through, Overcome, To the bitter end
related.comparison: Both can attach to verb stems to express the completion of an action, but differ in nuance. 上がる・上げる is typically used when the action results in a finished product, e.g. '論文を書き上げる' ('finish writing a thesis'). 抜く highlights the effort or perseverance involved in following something through to the end, e.g. 'やり抜く' ('see it through').

related.title: おわる
related.meaning: To finish, To end
related.comparison: While おわる and 上がる・上げる both attach to verb stems to express finishing an action, their nuances differ. おわる can describe completing any action, e.g. ケーキを食べおわった ('I finished eating the cake.') or 映画を観おわった ('I finished watching a movie.'). 上がる・上げる is typically used when the action results in some completed product, e.g. 論文を書きあげた ('I finished writing the paper.').

related.title: 切る
related.meaning: To do something completely to the end, Utterly, Resolutely
related.comparison: Both can attach to verb stems to express the completion of an action, but differ in nuance. 上がる・上げる is typically used when the action results in a finished product, e.g. '論文を書き上げる' ('finish writing a thesis'). 切る expresses that something has been done thoroughly or completely, but doesn't necessarily imply a final product, e.g. '読み切る' ('read all the way through') or '使い切る' ('use up').

related.title: てしまう・ちゃう
related.meaning: To do something by accident, To finish completely・totally, Unfortunately
related.comparison: 上がる・上げる can express the idea of completing an action, which overlaps with てしまう・ちゃう, but they differ in nuance. てしまう・ちゃう can express regret or lack of control, while 上がる・上げる focuses on the act of producing a finished result. For example, 書き上げる 'finish writing'. In contrast, てしまう・ちゃう can be used with any verb, regardless of whether a tangible result is produced.

related.title: あとで
related.meaning: After, Later
related.comparison: Both 上で and あとで express that one action follows another, but with different nuances. あとで simply shows sequence, like 'after doing (A)'. 上で implies the first action is a necessary step for the second, similar to 'upon doing (A)', and is more formal, often used in writing or business contexts.

related.title: た末・の末
related.meaning: After, As a conclusion of
related.comparison: Both can express the idea of 'after doing (A), (B)', but 上で focuses on process or sequence (lit. 'upon' doing), e.g. 話し合った上で決めましょう ('Let's decide after discussing it.'). In contrast, た末・の末 emphasizes an outcome after prolonged effort or struggle, e.g. 悩んだ末に転職した ('After much deliberation, I changed jobs.').

related.title: て初めて
related.meaning: For the first time, Not until, Only after, Only when, Only because
related.comparison: Both show one action leading to another, but with different focus. て初めて marks a first experience or realization, e.g. 大人になって初めて分かった ('I only understood after becoming an adult.') 上で shows a necessary step before the next action, e.g. 社長に相談した上で決めましょう ('Let's decide after consulting the president.')

related.title: 上に
related.meaning: As well, Besides, In addition to
related.comparison: Both use 上 but differ quite a bit in meaning. 上に adds information, like '(B) in addition to (or 'on top of') (A)', typically within the same general category, e.g. 彼は親切な上に頭もいい ('He's smart in addition to being kind.'). In contrast, 上で expresses an action happening after (lit. 'upon') another action or condition being realized, e.g. 社長に相談した上で決めましょう ('Let's decide after consulting the president.').

related.title: たら
related.meaning: When, From when, Just when, If
related.comparison: Both 上で and たら can describe sequences or conditions, but with different nuance. 上で implies that one action is a prerequisite for another, such as 社長と相談した上で決めましょう ('Let's decide after discussing it with the president.'). たら is more general and covers a wider range of temporal and conditional uses, as in 晴れたら散歩に行こう ('If it's sunny, let's go for a walk.').

related.title: てから
related.meaning: Once...is done, Once...happens, After doing
related.comparison: てから and 上で both convey a sequence of events, but their nuances differ. てから simply indicates that one event follows another. In contrast, 上で emphasizes that the first action or event was a necessary precondition for the second event. Also, 上で is more formal than てから, and often used in business situations or writing.

related.title: 上に
related.meaning: As well, Besides, In addition to
related.comparison: 上に and 上は share the same kanji but serve different grammatical roles. 上に expresses accumulation of traits or actions, as in 忙しい上に風邪もひいた (I’m busy, and on top of that I caught a cold). 上は introduces a conditional or resolved stance, as in 引き受けた上は最後までやる (Since I accepted it, I’ll see it through).

related.title: 以上 ②
related.meaning: Since, Now that, As long as, If
related.comparison: 以上② and 上は both express a conditional premise leading to a natural or expected outcome. Their meanings are very similar and often interchangeable, but 上は feels somewhat old-fashioned, while 以上② is more common in modern usage. Both are relatively formal and typical of written language or structured speech.

related.title: からには
related.meaning: As long as, Since, Given
related.comparison: 上は and からには both present a situation or condition, then express something deemed necessary or natural based on that situation. For example: 日本に来たからには、たくさんの日本人と話したい (Since I came to Japan, I want to speak to many Japanese people). The primary difference is formality: 上は is used almost exclusively in formal writing, while からには is common in both writing and everyday conversation.

related.title: 以来
related.meaning: Since, Henceforth
related.comparison: 以来 and 上は can both be translated as since, but their usage is quite different. 以来 means since in the sense of time, as in 日本に来て以来、毎日日本語を使っている (Since coming to Japan, I use Japanese every day). 上は means since as in because of the given situation, as in 日本に来た上は、たくさんの日本人と話したい (Since I came to Japan, I want to speak to many Japanese people).

related.title: 上
related.meaning: From the standpoint of
related.comparison: 上 and 上に share the same kanji but serve distinct functions. 上に adds reasons or qualities in a cumulative way, as in 綺麗な上に頭もいい (In addition to being pretty, she’s also smart). 上 by itself expresses viewpoint, as in 法律上の問題 (a problem from a legal standpoint). The former is additive, the latter indicates perspective. Both are relatively formal expressions.

related.title: また
related.meaning: Also, As well, Moreover, Again, Additionally
related.comparison: また and 上に both add information, but differ in usage and tone. また appears at the beginning of a sentence or phrase and means 'moreover' or 'again'. It's common in both speech and writing. 上に follows nouns, verbs, or adjectives and conveys a similar meaning, but with a more formal or written tone.

related.title: おまけに
related.meaning: In addition, On top of that, What is more, To make matters worse
related.comparison: 上に and おまけに both introduce additional information, but differ in tone. 上に is neutral and formal, as in 彼は親切な上に、頭もいい (He is kind, and on top of that, smart). おまけに often emphasizes worsening conditions, as in 雨だった。おまけに風も強かった (It was raining. On top of that, the wind was strong).

related.title: 更に
related.meaning: Even more or further, Again, More and more
related.comparison: 上に and 更に both add information, but their nuance differs. 上に connects additive facts or qualities, often with a static or descriptive tone, as in 彼は真面目な上に礼儀正しい (He is serious, and also polite). 更に emphasizes progression or intensification, as in 更に速く走った (He ran even faster).

related.title: 上は
related.meaning: Once, Since, Now that, As long as
related.comparison: 上に and 上は share the same kanji but serve different grammatical roles. 上に expresses accumulation of traits or actions, as in 忙しい上に風邪もひいた (I’m busy, and on top of that I caught a cold). 上は introduces a conditional or resolved stance, as in 引き受けた上は最後までやる (Since I accepted it, I’ll see it through).

related.title: その上
related.meaning: Besides, In addition, Furthermore
related.comparison: その上 and 上に both convey the idea of in addition, but differ in structure and tone. その上 is a conjunctive adverb used between sentences, as in 彼は遅刻した。その上、宿題も忘れた (He was late. On top of that, he forgot his homework). 上に functions within a sentence to link clauses, and is often used in more structured or descriptive contexts.

related.title: 上で
related.meaning: After, Upon
related.comparison: Both use 上 but differ quite a bit in meaning. 上に adds information, like '(B) in addition to (or 'on top of') (A)', typically within the same general category, e.g. 彼は親切な上に頭もいい ('He's smart in addition to being kind.'). In contrast, 上で expresses an action happening after (lit. 'upon') another action or condition being realized, e.g. 社長に相談した上で決めましょう ('Let's decide after consulting the president.').

related.title: し～し
related.meaning: And, Giving reasons
related.comparison: 上に (literally, 'on top of') can be used as a slightly formal way to express 'in addition to (A), (B)', e.g. 安い上に、美味しい ('In addition to being inexpensive, it's tasty.'). While し～し is similar in that it lists multiple things, it specifically does so in the context of giving reasons for something, e.g. 安いし、美味しいし、一番好きなお店です ('It's inexpensive, it's tasty too, and so it's my favorite shop.').

related.title: うちに
related.meaning: While, During
related.comparison: Both expressions refer to a continued state, but in different ways. うちに means 'do (B) while (A),' that is, during the ideal time or condition for (A), e.g. 熱いうちに食べてね ('Eat it while it's hot.') In contrast, 中 attaches to nouns and simply means 'during (A)' or 'in the middle of (A)', e.g. 会議中 ('in the middle of a meeting'), 授業中に携帯が鳴った ('My phone rang during class.')

related.title: ているあいだに
related.meaning: While, During the time that ~
related.comparison: Both patterns express being in the middle of an action, but differ in how they are used. 中 attaches to nouns to indicate 'in the process of' or 'during.' For example, 今、食事中です means 'I'm in the middle of eating.' ているあいだに refers to something happening while another action or state is ongoing. For example, ご飯を食べているあいだに電話が鳴った and 食事中に電話が鳴った are equivalent in meaning.

related.title: ながら
related.meaning: While ~ing, During, As
related.comparison: ながら attaches to verb stems to express performing one action simultaneously with another, e.g. 音楽を聴きながら勉強する 'listen to music while studying'. In contrast, 中 attaches to nouns to express 'while doing X,' and there is no requirement that what follows must be an action performed by the same subject, e.g. 会議中に電話がかかってきた ('A phone call came in while I was in a meeting.').

related.title: ているところだ
related.meaning: In the process of doing ~ right now/at this moment
related.comparison: ているところだ and 中 are similar in that both express being in the middle of doing something, but they function differently grammatically. ているところだ is used with verbs, e.g. 今、食事をしているところです ('Now I'm in the middle of eating.'). In contrast, 中 attaches to the end of nouns and expresses a similar meaning, e.g. 今、食事中です, which means essentially the same thing but is more concise.

related.title: の間に
related.meaning: While, During, Between, Period
related.comparison: Both express a continued state or action, but are used differently. 中 attaches only to nouns and means 'in the middle of (A)', e.g. 会議中 ('in the middle of a meeting'), 使用中 ('in use'). の間に attaches to nouns (as-is), verbs/adjectives (with の removed), and な-adjectives (with な instead of の), and means 'while doing (A), (B)', e.g. 映画を観ている間に電話が鳴った ('While I was watching a movie, the phone rang.')

related.title: とき
related.meaning: When, At the time of
related.comparison: とき expresses the time of an action or event, like 'when' in English. It can be used with nouns as in 雨の時 'when it rains', verbs as in 父が帰ってきた時 'when Dad came home', or adjectives as in 暑い時 'when it's hot', and can describe one-time or repeated actions. 中 attaches to nouns and describes an action or state in progress, e.g. 勉強中 'now studying' or 使用中 'currently in use'.

related.title: 最中に
related.meaning: In the middle of
related.comparison: Both express a continued state or action, but are used differently. 中 attaches only to nouns and means 'in the middle of (A)', e.g. 会議中 ('in the middle of a meeting'), 使用中 ('in use'). 最中に is used with verbs in the ている form or with nouns (using の) to mean 'right in the middle of (A), (B)', e.g. 食べている最中に話し掛けられた ('I was spoken to when I was right in the middle of eating.')

related.title: と同じくらい
related.meaning: About the same as, About as, No less
related.comparison: Both expressions are similar in that this refer to something being equivalent to something else. 並み in particular refers to measuring up to a certain standard, e.g. 彼の歌はプロ並みだ ('His singing is on par with a pro.'), while と同じくらい simply expresses an equivalence without implying a certain standard, e.g. 息子と同じぐらいの年 'around the same age as my son'.

related.title: に代わって
related.meaning: In place of, On behalf of, To succeed
related.comparison: Both phrases use 代わる ('to replace') and express substitution or replacement, but differ in usage. 代わりに emphasizes doing something in place of another thing or action. For example, ご飯の代わりにパンを食べた ('I ate bread instead of rice'). に代わって is used when taking over someone's role or acting in their place, often in formal contexts. For example, 部長に代わってご挨拶します ('On behalf of the department head, I greet you').

related.title: むしろ
related.meaning: Rather, Instead, Over
related.comparison: Both expressions involve alternatives, but with different meanings. 代わりに indicates a direct substitution or exchange, often physical or role-based, e.g. 泳ぐ代わりに走った ('I ran instead of swimming'). むしろ introduces a more suitable or preferred option, e.g. 泳ぐよりむしろ走りたい ('I'd rather run than swim').

antonyms.title: いか
antonyms.meaning: Less than (or equal to), Under・(and) below, Any less・fewer, Not exceeding
antonyms.comparison: 以上① and いか (written as 以下) both attach to amounts or nouns like それ, but express opposite meanings: 以上① means 'X or more', while いか means 'X or less'. For example, １００円以上 '100 yen or more' vs. １００円以下 means '100 yen or less'. In both cases, the stated number is included. 以上 and 以下 can also refer to earlier or later content in a document.

related.title: からある
related.meaning: As much as, As many as, At least
related.comparison: からある and 以上① both emphasize large numbers or amounts, but differ in nuance and usage. からある means 'as many as', usually with inanimate objects (due to the use of ある), and attaches directly to nouns, e.g. ５００枚からあるチケット ('as many as 500 tickets'). 以上① means 'X or more', can be used with people, and attaches to nouns with の, e.g. 500人以上の学生 = '500 or more students'.

related.title: より
related.meaning: More than, Over
related.comparison: Both 以上① and より express comparisons like 'more than (A)', but with different nuances. 以上 follows numbers or nouns like これ, and means '(A) or more,' e.g. ２時間以上かかる ('It will take two or more hours.'). より makes general comparisons and does not necessarily have a nuance of measuring some quantity.

related.title: 以上に
related.meaning: More than, No less than, Even more
related.comparison: 以上① and 以上に are related patterns that both mean 'more than or equal to', but are used differently. 以上① typically attaches to amounts or nouns like それ, meaning 'X or more'. For example, １００円以上 means '100 yen or more'. In contrast, 以上に follows nouns, verbs, or adjectives, and means 'more than (A)'. For example, 必要以上に means 'more than necessary'.

related.title: 上は
related.meaning: Once, Since, Now that, As long as
related.comparison: 以上② and 上は both express a conditional premise leading to a natural or expected outcome. Their meanings are very similar and often interchangeable, but 上は feels somewhat old-fashioned, while 以上② is more common in modern usage. Both are relatively formal and typical of written language or structured speech.

related.title: からには
related.meaning: As long as, Since, Given
related.comparison: 以上② and からには both mean since or now that, but differ slightly in nuance. からには adds a stronger sense of personal resolve or obligation, as in 約束したからには守らなければならない (Since I made a promise, I must keep it). 以上② is more neutral and objective, as in 参加する以上、遅刻するわけにはいかない (If we’re going to participate, we can’t be late).

related.title: 以来
related.meaning: Since, Henceforth
related.comparison: 以来 and 以上② can both be translated as since, but function differently. 以来 marks a continuous state from a past point, as in 事故以来、運転していない (I haven’t driven since the accident). 以上② expresses conditional logic or justification, as in 勝負する以上、全力でやってみたい (Since I’m going to compete, I want to give it my all).

synonyms.title: より
synonyms.comparison: Both より and 以上に are used for comparisons. In some cases, their meaning and usage can overlap, e.g. 思っていたより and 思っていた以上に both mean 'more than I thought'. In other cases, they can differ in that 以上に suggests a shared quality between the two things being compared. For example, AはBより強い simply states that A is stronger than B, while AはB以上に強い suggests that A is even stronger than B (who is also strong).

related.title: 以上 ①
related.meaning: More than (or equal to), Over・(and) above, Any more・Greater, Exceeding
related.comparison: 以上① and 以上に are related patterns that both mean 'more than or equal to', but are used differently. 以上① typically attaches to amounts or nouns like それ, meaning 'X or more'. For example, １００円以上 means '100 yen or more'. In contrast, 以上に follows nouns, verbs, or adjectives, and means 'more than (A)'. For example, 必要以上に means 'more than necessary'.

related.title: たら最後
related.meaning: Once, Now that, As soon as
related.comparison: Both mark a point after which something changes, but the meaning and outlook are very different. たら最後 expresses a strong, often negative inevitability, meaning “once (A) happens, that’s it,” and implies that things will go badly or cannot be undone, as in 一度言ったら最後、もう取り消せない (‘Once you say it, that’s it, you can’t take it back.’). By contrast, 以来 is a neutral, factual expression meaning “since,” used to state that a situation has continued from a certain point in the past without emotional judgment, as in 日本に来て以来、日本語を勉強している (‘I’ve been studying Japanese since I came to Japan.’).

related.title: 以上 ②
related.meaning: Since, Now that, As long as, If
related.comparison: 以来 and 以上② can both be translated as since, but function differently. 以来 marks a continuous state from a past point, as in 事故以来、運転していない (I haven’t driven since the accident). 以上② expresses conditional logic or justification, as in 勝負する以上、全力でやってみたい (Since I’m going to compete, I want to give it my all).

related.title: 上は
related.meaning: Once, Since, Now that, As long as
related.comparison: 以来 and 上は can both be translated as since, but their usage is quite different. 以来 means since in the sense of time, as in 日本に来て以来、毎日日本語を使っている (Since coming to Japan, I use Japanese every day). 上は means since as in because of the given situation, as in 日本に来た上は、たくさんの日本人と話したい (Since I came to Japan, I want to speak to many Japanese people).

related.title: どうせ
related.meaning: Anyhow, At any rate, In any case, I have no doubt that, Probably
related.comparison: 何しろ introduces a reason or emphasis, often justifying a statement, as in 何しろ忙しいので行けません (In any case, I can’t go because I’m busy). どうせ conveys resignation or futility, as in どうせ無理だ (It’s hopeless anyway). Both can be translated as ‘in any case’, but 何しろ is explanatory, while どうせ carries a negative, defeatist tone.

related.title: にしても～にしても
related.meaning: Regardless of whether
related.comparison: にしても～にしても structures a conditional to show indifference between possibilities, as in 雨にしても雪にしても中止だ (Whether rain or snow, it’s canceled). 何しろ introduces a reason or explanation, often with resignation, as in 忙しい、何しろ締め切りが近い (I’m busy; after all, the deadline is near).

related.title: 何といっても
related.meaning: By any account, All the same, No doubt
related.comparison: Both can mean after all, but differ in nuance. 何といっても emphasizes the decisive point, as in 何といっても経験が大事だ (Above all, experience matters). 何しろ introduces a reason or background explanation, as in 忙しいんです。何しろ締め切りが近い (I’m busy, after all the deadline is near).

related.title: にせよ・にしろ
related.meaning: Even if, Even though, No matter, Whether… or
related.comparison: Both include しろ but are unrelated in usage. にせよ・にしろ means even if (A), as in 結果が悪いにせよ挑戦するべきだ (Even if the result is bad, you should try). 何しろ appears at the start of a sentence meaning anyway or after all, as in 何しろ忙しいので行けません (Anyway, I can’t go because I’m busy).

related.title: であれ
related.meaning: Even if, Even, No matter
related.comparison: 何しろ appears at the start of a sentence to give a reason, like after all, as in 何しろ忙しいので行けません (After all, I’m busy, so I can’t go). であれ follows nouns, な-adjectives, or wh-words to mean even if or no matter, often in formal contexts, as in 理由が何であれ許されない (No matter what the reason, it’s unforgivable).

related.title: であれ〜であれ
related.meaning: Whether or, Or, No matter which, In either case
related.comparison: 何しろ introduces reasons or explanations, like after all. であれ〜であれ presents alternatives to show the result is unaffected, as in 男であれ女であれ歓迎する (Whether man or woman, they are welcome).

related.title: にしろ～にしろ
related.meaning: Whether… or
related.comparison: 何しろ starts sentences with reasons, like after all. にしろ～にしろ lists two alternatives to stress the conclusion is unchanged, as in 行くにしろ行かないにしろ連絡して (Whether you go or not, let me know).

related.title: にしても
related.meaning: Even though, Even if, Even when, Also
related.comparison: 何しろ and にしても are similar in that both have a general sense of 'no matter' or 'regardless', they are used quite differently. 何しろ comes at the beginning of a sentence to express 'after all, (A)', while にしても follows a noun or phrase to specifically state 'even if (A), still (B)'.

related.title: どうせ
related.meaning: Anyhow, At any rate, In any case, I have no doubt that, Probably
related.comparison: どうせ expresses resignation or pessimism about an inevitable outcome, often with a negative tone, as in どうせ失敗するから (It’s bound to fail anyway). In contrast, 何といっても emphasizes something’s outstanding or most important quality, as in 何といっても彼が一番だ (No matter what you say, he’s the best). The former carries a defeatist tone, while the latter asserts something positively.

related.title: ～というのは事実だ
related.meaning: It is a fact that ~, There is no doubt that
related.comparison: Both phrases assert something strongly, but from different angles. ～というのは事実だ affirms something as a literal fact, e.g. 負けたというのは事実だ ('It's a fact that we lost'). 何といっても expresses what the speaker feels is most important, e.g. 何といっても家族が大事だ ('No matter what you say, family is important').

related.title: 何より
related.meaning: Be glad, Be relieved, Most, Best, Above all else, More than anything
related.comparison: Both highlight priority or importance. 何より stresses value or priority, as in 健康が何より大切だ (Health is more important than anything). 何といっても emphasizes undeniability or decisiveness, as in 何といっても彼が一番だ (No matter what, he’s the best).3

related.title: 何しろ
related.meaning: No wonder, After all, In fact, Because, At any rate
related.comparison: Both can mean after all, but differ in nuance. 何といっても emphasizes the decisive point, as in 何といっても経験が大事だ (Above all, experience matters). 何しろ introduces a reason or background explanation, as in 忙しいんです。何しろ締め切りが近い (I’m busy, after all the deadline is near).

related.title: きらいがある
related.meaning: Have a tendency to, Tend to, Be slightly~
related.comparison: Both mean to have a tendency, but differ in nuance. 傾向がある is neutral and objective, used for groups or broad trends, as in 日本人は魚を食べる傾向がある (Japanese people tend to eat fish). きらいがある is often used about individuals and carries a critical tone, as in 彼は決断が遅いきらいがある (He has a tendency to be slow in making decisions).

related.title: 何といっても
related.meaning: By any account, All the same, No doubt
related.comparison: Both highlight priority or importance. 何より stresses value or priority, as in 健康が何より大切だ (Health is more important than anything). 何といっても emphasizes undeniability or decisiveness, as in 何といっても彼が一番だ (No matter what, he’s the best).3

related.title: たった(の)
related.meaning: Only, Just, No more than
related.comparison: Both express small amounts, but differ in usage. 僅かに is adverbial, meaning barely, as in 僅かに聞こえる (It’s barely audible). たった(の) precedes quantity to stress insignificance, as in たったの100円しかない (I have only 100 yen).

related.title: また
related.meaning: Also, As well, Moreover, Again, Additionally
related.comparison: また and 再び both mean 'again', but differ in nuance and formality. また is common in speech and writing, used for repetition or to add information, like 'again' or 'additionally'. 再び is more formal and literary, often with a stronger sense of 'for the second time'.

related.title: 更に
related.meaning: Even more or further, Again, More and more
related.comparison: Both 再び and 更に can involve repetition or continuation, but they differ in meaning. 再び means 'again' or 'once more', and is used when something is done a second time after a pause, e.g. '再び会う' ('to meet again'). 更に means 'further' or 'more', and emphasizes intensification or accumulation, e.g. '更に深まる' ('to deepen further').

related.title: ぜんぜん
related.meaning: (Not) at all
related.comparison: Both ぜんぜん (with a negative verb) and 全く～ない mean 'not at all,' e.g. ぜんぜんできない and 全くできない both mean 'I can't do it at all.', with ぜんぜん being more casual in tone. In modern speech, both can occasionally appear with affirmative forms, e.g. ぜんぜん大丈夫 ('It's completely fine.'), though this was traditionally considered improper use.

related.title: あまり～ない
related.meaning: Not very, Not much, Not really, Hardly
related.comparison: Both 全く～ない and あまり～ない express negation, but differ in degree. 全く～ない indicates a complete absence, e.g. 全く寒くない ('It's not cold at all.'). あまり～ない suggests a lesser degree, e.g. あまり寒くない ('It's not very cold.').

related.title: なかなか～ない
related.meaning: Not really, Hardly, Not easily or readily, By no means, Far from
related.comparison: Both express rarity or lack of frequency, but with different focus. 全く〜ない means something happens infrequently, e.g. 全く外食しない ('I hardly ever eat out.') なかなか〜ない suggests it doesn't happen easily or readily, often due to circumstances, e.g. 忙しくてなかなか外食できない ('I can't easily eat out because I'm so busy.')

related.title: 決して〜ない
related.meaning: Never, By no means
related.comparison: While both 決して〜ない and 全く〜ない express negation, their nuances differ. 決して〜ない is a stronger, more emphatic expression meaning 'never' or 'by no means', and often carries an emotional or resolute tone. 全く〜ない translates as 'not at all' and conveys a more general or objective negation. The choice between them depends on context and the speaker's emotional stance.

related.title: とても～ない
related.meaning: Not ~ at all, Cannot ~ at all
related.comparison: 全く～ない and とても～ない both express strong negation, but differ in nuance. 全く～ない is used to completely deny or negate something, indicating total absence or lack. とても～ない, on the other hand, implies an extreme state or condition that makes something impossible, adding a nuance of implausibility or incapability.

related.title: すこしも～ない
related.meaning: Not even a little, Not at all, Not in the least, Never
related.comparison: 全く～ない and すこしも～ない are emphatic negatives that can both mean 'not at all' or 'not even a little'. For example, 全く飲まない and 少しも飲まない both mean 'I don't drink at all.' or 'I don't drink even a little.'. While often interchangeable, すこしも may sound unnatural when there's no nuance of amount or degree. For example, 全く行ったことがない ('I haven't gone at all.'), which wouldn't fit with すこしも.

antonyms.title: 切れない
antonyms.meaning: Being unable to finish or complete
antonyms.comparison: These two expressions are opposite in meaning. 切る conveys resolution or the complete execution of an action, e.g. やり切る 'do it all the way' or 使い切る 'use up'. 切れない is its antonym, used to express not being able to see an action through to the end, e.g. 食べ切れない ('I can't finish eating it.').

related.title: おわる
related.meaning: To finish, To end
related.comparison: Both 切る and おわる attach to verb stems to express finishing an action, but with different nuances. おわる simply marks completion, e.g. 使いおわった ('I finished using it.'). In contrast, 切る often implies using something up, e.g. バターを使い切った ('I used up all the butter.') or doing something to the very end with full effort, e.g. ゴールまで走り切った ('I ran all the way to the finish line.').

related.title: てしまう・ちゃう
related.meaning: To do something by accident, To finish completely・totally, Unfortunately
related.comparison: 切る conveys the idea of doing something completely, which overlaps with one nuance of てしまう・ちゃう, but their usage differs. てしまう・ちゃう can imply that the action was unintentional or against one's will, which 切る cannot. In other words, 食べ切った simply means 'I ate all of it.', while 食べてしまった・食べちゃった also can have the meaning of mean 'I ended up eating it (even though I didn't intend to).', which 食べ切った cannot.

related.title: 込む ②
related.meaning: Remain, To do thoroughly, Deeply, Intensely
related.comparison: Both can attach to verbs to form compound words. 切る emphasizes full completion or decisiveness, e.g. '言い切る' ('say definitively') or '読み切る' ('finish reading'), focusing on doing something thoroughly to its end. 込む conveys depth or intensity of action, e.g. '考え込む' ('get lost in thought'), highlighting depth rather than finality.

related.title: 抜く
related.meaning: All~, Completely, See through, Overcome, To the bitter end
related.comparison: Both can attach to verbs to form compound words. 切る implies thorough completion and intensity, while 抜く emphasizes perseverance and sustained effort through to the end. For example, やり切る suggests 'do it thoroughly', while 考え抜く adds the nuance of overcoming difficulty in the thinking process.

related.title: 上がる・上げる
related.meaning: Something is finished, To finish something, Something has come to an end, To do something completely, To do something through
related.comparison: Both can attach to verb stems to express the completion of an action, but differ in nuance. 上がる・上げる is typically used when the action results in a finished product, e.g. '論文を書き上げる' ('finish writing a thesis'). 切る expresses that something has been done thoroughly or completely, but doesn't necessarily imply a final product, e.g. '読み切る' ('read all the way through') or '使い切る' ('use up').

related.title: ～になる・～くなる
related.meaning: To become (Adjective)
related.comparison: 化する and ～になる・～くなる both express change. 化する attaches to nouns and means '~ify' or '~ize', e.g. データ化する 'to digitize' (literally, 'to turn into data'). ～になる and ～くなる are more general-purpose ways to express becoming something, e.g. 綺麗になる 'become clean' or 白くなる 'to become white'.

related.title: あまり～ない
related.meaning: Not very, Not much, Not really, Hardly
related.comparison: Both 別に〜ない and あまり〜ない are used to qualify negative statements, but differ in nuance. あまり〜ない expresses a lesser degree, e.g. あまり痛くない ('It doesn't hurt much.'). In contrast, 別に〜ない adds a tone of indifference or dismissal, e.g. 別に痛くない ('It's not like it hurts.', implying that there is no need to worry, etc.).

related.title: なかなか～ない
related.meaning: Not really, Hardly, Not easily or readily, By no means, Far from
related.comparison: Both negate expectations, but in different ways. 別に〜ない downplays something as not particularly important, e.g. 別に怒ってないよ ('I'm not especially mad.') なかなか〜ない indicates that something doesn't happen easily, e.g. なかなか怒らない人だね ('He's not someone who gets angry easily.')

related.title: とは
related.meaning: That..., (Surprised) that..., (Never expected) that..., (Shocked) that...
related.comparison: なんて can overlap in meaning with とは when used in phrases like 優勝できる（とは）・（なんて）夢にも思わなかった ('Never even in my dreams did I think we could win the championship.'). なんて however has a variety of usages that do not apply to とは, such as to comment (often negatively) on 'things like (A)', e.g. 嘘なんて大嫌い ('I hate things like lies.').

antonyms.title: 切る
antonyms.meaning: To do something completely to the end, Utterly, Resolutely
antonyms.comparison: These two expressions are opposite in meaning. 切る conveys resolution or the complete execution of an action, e.g. やり切る 'do it all the way' or 使い切る 'use up'. 切れない is its antonym, used to express not being able to see an action through to the end, e.g. 食べ切れない ('I can't finish eating it.').

antonyms.title: 上がる・上げる
antonyms.meaning: Something is finished, To finish something, Something has come to an end, To do something completely, To do something through
antonyms.comparison: These phrases are opposites—one expresses a completed action, the other, incompleteness. 上がる・上げる is typically used when the action results in a finished product, e.g. '論文を書き上げる' ('finish writing a thesis'). 切れない expresses the inability to finish something fully, e.g. '食べ切れなかった' ('I couldn't finish eating it').

antonyms.title: てしまう・ちゃう
antonyms.meaning: To do something by accident, To finish completely・totally, Unfortunately
antonyms.comparison: てしまう・ちゃう and 切れない can express opposite meanings. One nuance of てしまう・ちゃう is to do something completely, e.g. 食べてしまった has one potential meaning of 'I ate it all.'. In contrast, 食べ切れなかった would mean 'I couldn't eat all of it.'. Note that てしまう・ちゃう can also express regret or an unintentional action, which 切れない does not. For example, 食べてしまった could also mean 'I ate it (even though I didn't intend to).'.

related.title: かねる
related.meaning: Cannot, Be unable to, Hard to, Hesitant to
related.comparison: Both 切れない and かねる express inability to do something, but differ in tone and nuance. 切れない describes physical or practical inability to complete an action, e.g. '食べ切れない' ('I can't finish eating it'). かねる conveys emotional or psychological reluctance and often appears in formal or polite refusals, e.g. '分かりかねます' ('I'm afraid I don't know').

related.title: 〜得ない
related.meaning: Unable to, Cannot
related.comparison: Both 切れない and 得ない describe types of impossibility, but with different focuses. 切れない emphasizes physical or circumstantial inability to complete an action, e.g. '読み切れなかった' ('I couldn't finish reading it'). In contrast, 得ない expresses abstract or conceptual impossibility, e.g. '起こり得ない' ('impossible to happen'), and is used for events that cannot realistically occur.

related.title: まえに
related.meaning: Before, In front of
related.comparison: まえに is used to express the relative timing of actions and often follows a plain (non-past) verb to mean 'before doing A.' In contrast, 前者 (the former) and 後者 (the latter) refer to things previously mentioned. They're used to avoid repeating a word or phrase, similar to how we use 'the former' and 'the latter' in English.

related.title: あとで
related.meaning: After, Later
related.comparison: 前者は and 後者は, like あとで, relate to sequence, but in different ways. あとで (also written in kanji as 後で) refers to the order of actions, like 'after doing (A)'. 前者 and 後者 mean 'the former' and 'the latter', pointing back to the previously mentioned items.

related.title: ということは
related.meaning: That means, That is to say
related.comparison: 即ち and ということは both rephrase information, but differ in tone and function. 即ち is formal and used to define or clarify precisely, often in writing, e.g. 日本の首都、即ち東京 ('The capital of Japan—namely, Tokyo'). ということは is conversational and introduces a conclusion, e.g. 明日は休み？ということは、今日は早く帰れるかもね ('Tomorrow's a holiday? So that means we might get to leave early today').

related.title: 要するに
related.meaning: To sum up, In summary, In short
related.comparison: 即ち and 要するに both help clarify or summarize a statement, but they differ in function. 即ち gives a formal restatement or definition, e.g. 日本の首都、即ち東京 'the capital of Japan, that is to say Tokyo'. 要するに summarizes the main point and can be used in speech or semi-formal writing, e.g. 要するに時間がない ('To sum it up, we have no time.').

related.title: つまり
related.meaning: In short, To sum up, In other words
related.comparison: Both つまり and 即ち mean 'in other words', but differ in tone and context. つまり is casual and used in conversation, e.g. つまりしょうがないってこと ('In other words, it can't be helped'). 即ち is formal and often used in academic or written contexts, e.g. 生きることは即ち戦いである ('To live is, in other words, a battle').

related.title: しかも
related.meaning: Moreover, Furthermore
related.comparison: Both express addition like the English 'moreover', but differ in usage. しかも starts a sentence to add information that emphasizes the previous point, e.g. '彼は優秀だ。しかも努力家だ' ('He's talented. Moreover, he's hardworking'). 又〜も, because of も, must be used with another noun that also applies and adds to the previous statement, e.g. '姉はピアノが弾ける。また、歌も歌える' ('My older sister can play piano. She can also sing songs').

related.title: ところが
related.meaning: Even so, However, Even though, But
related.comparison: Both introduce unexpected outcomes, but differ in type of surprise. 却って means the result was the opposite of what was intended or expected, e.g. 謝ったら却って怒られた ('When I apologized, I was yelled at instead.') ところが highlights a surprising turn of events, though not necessarily the exact opposite, e.g. 田中さんに電話をかけた。ところがもう出発していた ('I called Tanaka. But he had already departed.')

related.title: むしろ
related.meaning: Rather, Instead, Over
related.comparison: Both むしろ and 却って  express contrast with expectation, but differ in nuance. むしろ introduces a preferred or clearer option, e.g. ワインよりむしろビールが飲みたい ('Rather than wine, I'd rather drink beer'). 却って highlights ironic reversal, e.g. 手伝ってあげたのに却って怒られた ('I helped, but they got angry instead').

related.title: 逆に
related.meaning: On the contrary
related.comparison: 却って and 逆に both express something being counter to expectations, like the English 'on the contrary'. 却って is more formal, and occurs primarily in writing in the context of a full sentence or explanation. In contrast, 逆に is quite casual, and can be used in the same way as 却って, but also as an interjection at the beginning of a sentence or phrase, e.g. 逆にたかし君はどう思いますか？ ('On the contrary, what do you think, Takashi?').

related.title: に反して
related.meaning: Go against, Contrary to
related.comparison: Both convey contradiction, but they differ in usage and nuance. 却って connects to the rest of the sentence to emphasize unexpected results, e.g. 慌てて出ようとしたら、却って遅くなった ('I tried to rush out, and I actually ended up running later.'). In contrast, に反して connects to a noun and highlights going against norms or expectations, e.g. 親の期待に反して、大学に進学しなかった ('Against his parents' expectations, he didn't go to college.').

related.title: に対して
related.meaning: In contrast to, While, In regard to, Whereas
related.comparison: Both express contrast, but their nuance differs. に対して typically compares two things with opposing qualities, e.g. 田中先生が厳しいのに対して、鈴木先生は優しい ('Whereas Prof. Tanaka is strict, Prof. Suzuki is lenient'). 反面 focuses on two sides of the same situation, e.g. 都会の生活は便利な反面、お金がかかる ('Life in the big city is convenient, but expensive'). に対して also has a 'toward (A)' usage, which 反面 does not.

related.title: 一方で
related.meaning: On the other hand, At the same time, While
related.comparison: 一方で and 反面 both show contrast, but differ in tone. 一方で is neutral and presents parallel or opposing facts, e.g. '一方で経済は成長している' ('On the other hand, the economy is growing'). 反面 highlights a downside or contradiction, e.g. '便利な反面、依存しやすい' ('While convenient, it's also easy to become dependent').

related.title: それぞれ
related.meaning: Each, Respectively
related.comparison: Both 各 and それぞれ express the idea of 'each' or 'every', but they differ in usage. 各 always goes before a noun, for example, 各部屋 'each room', 各階 'each floor', and so on. それぞれ, on the other hand, can stand alone at the start of a sentence or be attached to a noun with の. For example, 人はそれぞれ違う意見がある ('People each have differing opinions.').

related.title: ～ずつ
related.meaning: One at a time, Per, Each, Gradually
related.comparison: Both mean 'each', but with different usage. ～ずつ follows quantity expressions to mean 'each' or 'at a time', e.g. 一つずつください 'Give me one of each.' or ちょっとずつよくなってきた 'It's gotten better a little at a time.' 各 appears before nouns and means 'every' or 'each', e.g. この電車は各駅にとまります 'This train stops at every station.'

related.title: と共に
related.meaning: Together with, At the same time,  As well as
related.comparison: Both relate to the idea of doing something 'together', but with different nuances and usage. 合う attaches to verb stems to convey mutual interaction, e.g. 助け合う ('help each other'), while と共に means '(A) happens together with (B)' in the sense of co-existence or simultaneity, e.g. 年を取ると共に考え方も変わった ('My thinking changed as I grew up').

related.title: ため(に)
related.meaning: For, For the sake of, In order to
related.comparison: Both can be translated as 'for (A)', but differ in nuance. 向き attaches directly to a noun and suggests suitability, e.g. 子ども向きの本 ('A book suitable for children') ため(に) connects to nouns with の and suggests intended benefit or purpose, e.g. 子どものための本 ('A book for children [designed to benefit them]')

related.title: に向かって・に向けて
related.meaning: Towards, To face, To head to
related.comparison: These expressions all involve direction or suitability, but differ in usage. に向かって・に向けて describe motion or intent toward a goal or target, e.g. 'ゴールに向かって走る' ('run toward the goal'). 向き attaches directly to a noun and refers to the appropriateness or orientation of something for a group, without necessarily implying targeted action, e.g. '初心者向きのコース' ('a course suited for beginners').

related.title: に合わせて・に合った
related.meaning: In accordance with, Matching, Fitting, Suitable for, Adjust or tune to
related.comparison: Both relate to the idea of being suited to something else, but with different nuances. に合わせて・に合った implies active adjustment to suit a person or situation, e.g. 季節に合わせて洋服を選ぶ ('Choose clothes to match the season'), while 向き describes suitability or appropriateness by nature, e.g. 初心者向きの本 ('A book suitable for beginners').

related.title: 向け
related.meaning: Intended for, Aimed at
related.comparison: Both 向け and 向き describe suitability or orientation, but differ in nuance and intent. 向け refers to something explicitly designed or intended for a specific group or purpose, e.g. '子供向けの番組' ('a program for children'). 向き describes natural suitability without implying deliberate targeting, e.g. '初心者向きのコース' ('a course suited for beginners').

related.title: ～のうち(で)
related.meaning: Of the, Among(st)
related.comparison: Both ～のうち(で) and 同士 describe relationships within a group, but differ in nuance. ～のうち(で) indicates selection from a group, e.g. ３人のうちで誰が一番早い？ ('Who is the fastest among the three?'). 同士 emphasizes mutual relationships or shared characteristics, e.g. 学生同士で助け合う ('Help each other as students').

related.title: のなかで
related.meaning: In, Among, Within
related.comparison: のなかで and 同士 can both refer to groups or relationships, but their meanings and usage differ. のなかで means 'among' and is used to highlight one or more members within a group, e.g. サッカー選手の中で誰が好き？ ('Among soccer players, who do you like?'). 同士, on the other hand, emphasizes mutual relationships, e.g. サッカー選手同士で話し合った means 'They talked as fellow soccer players.'.

related.title: に合わせて・に合った
related.meaning: In accordance with, Matching, Fitting, Suitable for, Adjust or tune to
related.comparison: Both relate to the idea of being suited to something else, but with different nuances. に合わせて・に合った implies active adjustment to suit a person or situation, e.g. 季節に合わせて洋服を選ぶ ('Choose clothes to match the season'), while 向け describes being made for or targeted toward a certain group, e.g. 子供向けのアニメ ('A cartoon for children').

related.title: に向かって・に向けて
related.meaning: Towards, To face, To head to
related.comparison: These expressions all involve direction or targeting, but their usage differs. に向かって・に向けて describe motion or intent toward a goal or target, e.g. 'ゴールに向かって走る' ('run toward the goal'). 向け attaches directly to a noun to describe something targeted at a particular group, e.g. '子ども向けのアニメ' ('an anime for children').

related.title: 向き
related.meaning: Suitable for, Facing
related.comparison: Both 向け and 向き describe suitability or orientation, but differ in nuance and intent. 向け refers to something explicitly designed or intended for a specific group or purpose, e.g. '子供向けの番組' ('a program for children'). 向き describes natural suitability without implying deliberate targeting, e.g. '初心者向きのコース' ('a course suited for beginners').

synonyms.title: なさい
synonyms.comparison: The imperative or command form  (命令形) gives strong, direct commands, e.g. 食べろ ('Eat!'). On its own, it sounds harsh, so it's often softened. なさい is a more polite alternative, often used by parents or teachers toward children, e.g. 野菜を食べなさい ('Eat your vegetables!'). Both forms command action but differ in tone.

synonyms.title: Verb[て]
synonyms.comparison: The imperative or command form (命令形) gives blunt commands, e.g. 止まれ ('Stop!'), often sounding forceful. Verb[て] form is softer and used for casual requests, e.g. 見て 'Look!'. Adding よ or ね softens it further, e.g. 見てね ('Take a look, okay?'), which is commonly done in friendly, everyday speech.

related.title: ことだ
related.meaning: Should, Ought
related.comparison: The imperative or command form  (命令形) gives direct, blunt commands, e.g. 止まれ ('Stop!'). It can sound harsh unless softened with particles like よ, e.g. 頑張れよ ('Try harder, okay?'). In contrast, ことだ offers soft advice, e.g. 早起きすることだ ('You should wake up early.'), and is often used when giving recommendations or suggestions.

related.title: たらどう
related.meaning: Why don't you...?, What if you did...?
related.comparison: The imperative or command form  (命令形) is used for blunt orders, e.g. 止まれ ('Stop!'), which can be harsh. In contrast, たらどう is a soft suggestion, similar to 'Why don't you (A)?' in English, e.g. 行ってみたらどう？ ('Why don't you try going?'). It gives the listener more freedom and therefore sounds more gentle.

related.title: まま(に)
related.meaning: As is, While remaining, Left in a state
related.comparison: まま(に) can suggest a condition continuing unchanged or doing something exactly a certain way, e.g. 指示されたままに動く ('Act exactly as instructed'). 如く・如き・如し express resemblance, e.g. 夢の如く消えた ('It vanished like a dream'). Both can involve comparison, but only 如く emphasizes similarity, and is far more formal/literary than まま(に).

related.title: だらけ
related.meaning: Completely covered in, Full of
related.comparison: だらけ and 塗れ (often written in hiragana as まみれ) both describe being covered in something, but differ in nuance. だらけ suggests an overwhelming or chaotic abundance of unpleasant things, e.g. 傷だらけの手 'Hands full of cuts.' 塗れ emphasizes being soaked or stained, either physically or figuratively, e.g. 泥まみれの靴 'Mud-covered shoes' or 借金まみれの男 'A man drowning in debt.'

related.title: 次第だ・次第で
related.meaning: Depending on, So
related.comparison: 次第だ・次第で indicates a cause-and-effect relationship where one outcome depends on another factor, as in 成功するかどうかは努力次第だ (Whether you succeed depends on your effort). 如何 means depending on more broadly, without implying a direct causal link, as in 天気如何で決まる (It will be decided depending on the weather).

related.title: 次第だ・次第で
related.meaning: Depending on, So
related.comparison: Both expressions mean 'depending on' and can be used in similar contexts. For example, 実験の結果の如何で研究資金が決まる ('Depending on the results of the experiment, funding will be decided.') can be rephrased as 実験の結果次第で～ with no change in meaning. Note, however, that 次第 always attaches directly to nouns, while 如何 can connect either directly or with の. Also, 如何 is used in certain set expressions like ～の如何を問わず 'without considering' where 次第 is typically not used.

related.title: ふうに
related.meaning: ~ way, In a way that suggests, Like ~
related.comparison: 如何 indicates that something depends on conditions, often formal, as in 成否は努力いかんだ (Success depends on effort). ふうに describes the manner or style of something, often casually, as in 彼は変わったふうに話す (He speaks in a strange way).

related.title: ようによっては
related.meaning: Depending on the way, Depending on how
related.comparison: Both express that results depend on conditions, but the nuance and construction differ. ようによっては means ‘depending on how something is seen, used, or handled,’ and highlights that the same thing can lead to different outcomes based on circumstances or perspective, as in この映画は見ようによってはコメディーだと感じる人もいるだろう ('Some people may see this movie as a comedy, depending on how they watch it.'). 如何 is a formal, written expression meaning ‘depending on,’ used to state that an outcome is determined by a specific factor in an objective or structural way, as in 対応如何で結果が変わる ('The outcome will change depending on the response.').

related.title: にかかっている
related.meaning: To depend on
related.comparison: Both can express that something is dependent on something, but their usage differs. にかかっている is used at the end of a phrase or sentence, e.g. 開催できるかどうかは天気にかかっている ('Whether or not we can hold the event depends on the weather.'). In contrast, 如何 can be used in mid-sentence in forms like ～の如何で or ～の如何によって, e.g. 君の努力の如何によって 'depending on your effort…'. Because of the overlap in meaning, they can even be used together for further emphasis, i.e. ～如何にかかっている 'depending on'.

related.title: ように
related.meaning: So that, In order to, In such a way that
related.comparison: 如何 (read as いかん) is a formal term meaning 'how' or 'in what way', used mainly in formal or academic writing. It appears after nouns, often in the pattern 如何によって ('depending on'). 状況の如何で決まる = 'It will be decided depending on the situation.' ように is a more common, casual form, e.g. 忘れないようにメモした ('I took a memo so I wouldn't forget.').

related.title: ことに
related.meaning: Extremely, Especially, To my ...
related.comparison: 幸いなことに (sometimes shortened to just 幸い) means 'Fortunately', and is a fixed phrase, e.g. 幸い(なことに)、大事には至らなかった 'Fortunately, it didn't become serious.' It's a variation of ことに, which highlights emotional reactions. While 幸い expresses a positive outcome, ことに can also appear with other emotion words, like 驚いたことに 'To my surprise' or 悲しいことに 'Sadly'.

related.title: ～ずつ
related.meaning: One at a time, Per, Each, Gradually
related.comparison: Both express the idea of 'each', but with different nuance and usage. ～ずつ attaches to quantity expressions like numbers or 少し to mean 'each' or 'at a time', e.g. 一つずつください 'Give me one of each.' or ちょっとずつよくなってきた 'It's gotten better a little at a time.' 当たり also attaches to Number + Counter, but means 'per' or 'apiece', e.g. １個当たり５００円です 'They're 500 yen apiece.'

related.title: に当たる
related.meaning: Correspond to, Amounts to, Equals to, Is appropriate
related.comparison: Both phrases derive from 当たる, but are used in very different ways. に当たる means 'corresponds to' and often functions as a more formal, indirect way of saying 'is', e.g. '父親に当たる人' ('a person who is [lit. corresponds to] their father'). 当たり is used after number + counter expressions to mean 'per' or 'apiece', e.g. '１個当たり１００円' ('100 yen apiece').

related.title: それぞれ
related.meaning: Each, Respectively
related.comparison: 当たり and それぞれ can both convey a sense of 'each', but their nuances are different. 当たり attaches to numbers to mean 'each' in the sense of 'apiece', e.g. １個当たり１００円 ('100 yen each' / '100 yen apiece'). In contrast, それぞれ means 'each' in the sense of 'to each their own', e.g. 人はそれぞれ個性がある ('People each have unique traits').

related.title: かねる
related.meaning: Cannot, Be unable to, Hard to, Hesitant to
related.comparison: Both 〜得ない and かねる express inability, but differ in nuance. 〜得ない indicates objective impossibility, as in あり得ない話 (an unthinkable or impossible story). かねる reflects personal or emotional hesitation, often used in polite refusals in business contexts, as in お答えできかねます (I’m unable to answer).

related.title: 切れない
related.meaning: Being unable to finish or complete
related.comparison: Both 切れない and 得ない describe types of impossibility, but with different focuses. 切れない emphasizes physical or circumstantial inability to complete an action, e.g. '読み切れなかった' ('I couldn't finish reading it'). In contrast, 得ない expresses abstract or conceptual impossibility, e.g. '起こり得ない' ('impossible to happen'), and is used for events that cannot realistically occur.

related.title: 得る・得る
related.meaning: Is able to, Can, Possible
related.comparison: 〜得ない (cannot) and 得る (can) are formal expressions that attach to the stem of verbs and function as antonyms. 得る expresses possibility or attainability, as in 考え得る方法 ('a conceivable method'), while 〜得ない denies such possibility, often in abstract or evaluative contexts, as in 信じ得ない光景 (an unbelievable sight).

related.title: 〜得ない
related.meaning: Unable to, Cannot
related.comparison: 〜得ない (cannot) and 得る (can) are formal expressions that attach to the stem of verbs and function as antonyms. 得る expresses possibility or attainability, as in 考え得る方法 ('a conceivable method'), while 〜得ない denies such possibility, often in abstract or evaluative contexts, as in 信じ得ない光景 (an unbelievable sight).

related.title: れる・られる (Potential)
related.meaning: To be able, Can (Potential)
related.comparison: 得る・得る and the れる・られる (Potential) form both indicate possibility or ability, but differ in nuance. れる・られる is used for general ability, as in 話せる 'can speak', and is conversational. 得る is more formal and written-style, and is typically that something is possible or conceivable, like 起こり得る ('It could happen.').

related.title: かねない
related.meaning: Might, Could be, Capable of, Possible
related.comparison: かねない is a polite expression often used in business settings to indicate a possible undesirable outcome, as in 誤解を招きかねない (It might invite misunderstanding). 恐れがある also expresses possibility, but with a more objective focus on risk, as in 誤解を招く恐れがある (There’s a danger it will invite misunderstanding). The former feels more deferential, the latter more factual.

related.title: とは限らない
related.meaning: Not necessarily so, Is not always true, There is no guarantee that
related.comparison: 必ずしも and とは限らない often appear together as a pair in statements expressing that something isn't necessarily always the case, e.g. 必ずしも努力が報われるとは限らない ('Effort doesn't always pay off.'). とは限らない alone can express the same meaning without 必ずしも, but the pairing goes together naturally.

related.title: というものでもない
related.meaning: There is no guarantee that…, Not necessarily
