related.comparison: Both 必ずしも and というものでもない carry the meaning 'not necessarily', but are used in different ways. 必ずしも precedes a phrase or sentence to emphasize 'not always', and is often used with phrases like とは限らない, e.g. '必ずしもいいとは限らない' ('It's not necessarily good'). というものでもない appears at the end of a sentence and implies a general principle about how things are.

related.title: まえに
related.meaning: Before, In front of
related.comparison: Both まえに and 手前 can express a meaning similar to ''before' or 'in front of' in English. The difference is that まえに simply refers to happening earlier in time, or being physically located in front of something else. In contrast, 手前 adds nuance, often implying that, because one is faced with a certain situation (A), a particular action or outcome (B) follows as a result.

synonyms.title: とき
synonyms.comparison: Both とき and 折には express the time of an action or event, but differ in nuance and formality. とき is common in everyday conversation and broadly used, like the English 'when'. 折には is more formal, similar to 'on the occasion of', and is often used for special or less frequent (and often positive) events.

related.title: 際に
related.meaning: When, At the time of, In case of, On the occasion of
related.comparison: Both mark occasions, but differ in nuance. 折には emphasizes opportunity or timing, often with verbs of judgment or decision, as in 来日の折にはぜひお立ち寄りください (Please visit when you come to Japan). 際に is more neutral, simply indicating when or at the time of, without implying seizing an opportunity.

related.title: うちに
related.meaning: While, During
related.comparison: Both can be translated as 'while', but differ in nuance. うちに emphasizes doing something before a condition changes, e.g. 熱いうちに食べてね ('Eat it while it's hot.') 最中に highlights something occurring in the very middle of another action, e.g. 会議の最中に電話が鳴った ('The phone rang in the middle of the meeting.')

related.title: 途中に・途中で
related.meaning: On the way, Partway, In the middle of
related.comparison: 最中に and 途中に・途中で both mean 'in the middle of', but differ in nuance. 最中に emphasizes being right in the middle of something, e.g. 晩ご飯を作っている最中に電話が鳴った ('The phone rang when I was right in the middle of making dinner.') 途中に・途中で can overlap but also means 'on the way', e.g. 学校に行く途中で友達に会った ('I ran into a friend on the way to school.')

related.title: とき
related.meaning: When, At the time of
related.comparison: とき expresses the general time of an action or event, like 'when' in English. It can be used with nouns as in 雨の時 'when it rains', verbs as in 父が帰ってきた時 'when Dad came home', or adjectives as in 暑い時 'when it's hot', and can describe one-time or repeated actions. In contrast, 最中に describes something happening right in the middle of another action/event, e.g. 食事の最中に電話がなった ('My phone rang while I was in the middle of eating.').

related.title: 中
related.meaning: During, While, Throughout, In the process of
related.comparison: Both express a continued state or action, but are used differently. 中 attaches only to nouns and means 'in the middle of (A)', e.g. 会議中 ('in the middle of a meeting'), 使用中 ('in use'). 最中に is used with verbs in the ている form or with nouns (using の) to mean 'right in the middle of (A), (B)', e.g. 食べている最中に話し掛けられた ('I was spoken to when I was right in the middle of eating.')

related.title: の間に
related.meaning: While, During, Between, Period
related.comparison: Both の間に and 最中に express 'while (A), (B)', but with slightly different nuance. 最中に is more emphatic, implying that (B) happened right in the middle of (A). In contrast, の間に simply states that (B) occurred during the general span of (A), and sounds more objective.

related.title: ～ところに・～ところへ
related.meaning: At the time, When, While, Timing
related.comparison: Both describe an interruption during an action, but with different focus. ～ところに・～ところへ highlights timing or the moment of interruption, e.g. 出かけようとしたところに電話がかかってきた ('Just as I was about to leave, the phone rang.') 最中に stresses that the action was in full progress, e.g. 晩ご飯を作っている最中に電話が鳴った ('The phone rang when I was right in the middle of making dinner.')

related.title: ているところだ
related.meaning: In the process of doing ~ right now/at this moment
related.comparison: ているところだ conveys that an action is in progress at that moment, with the nuance that the speaker is busy with it, e.g. 晩ご飯を食べているところだ ('I'm in the middle of eating dinner.'). In contrast, 最中に is used to express that something else happened during that action, e.g. 晩ご飯を食べている最中に電話がかかってきた ('The phone rang while I was right in the middle of eating dinner.').

related.title: なし
related.meaning: Without, No
related.comparison: Both なし and 抜きで express the idea of 'without' in Japanese, but with a slight difference in nuance. なし indicates the absence or lack of something, e.g. '砂糖なしでお願いします' ('I'll have it without sugar'). 抜きで implies the exclusion of something that would typically be expected, e.g. 'ワサビ抜きのお寿司' ('sushi without wasabi') or 'ランチ抜きで仕事する' ('work without eating lunch').

related.title: 上がる・上げる
related.meaning: Something is finished, To finish something, Something has come to an end, To do something completely, To do something through
related.comparison: Both can attach to verb stems to express the completion of an action, but differ in nuance. 上がる・上げる is typically used when the action results in a finished product, e.g. '論文を書き上げる' ('finish writing a thesis'). 抜く highlights the effort or perseverance involved in following something through to the end, e.g. 'やり抜く' ('see it through').

related.title: 切る
related.meaning: To do something completely to the end, Utterly, Resolutely
related.comparison: Both can attach to verbs to form compound words. 切る implies thorough completion and intensity, while 抜く emphasizes perseverance and sustained effort through to the end. For example, やり切る suggests 'do it thoroughly', while 考え抜く adds the nuance of overcoming difficulty in the thinking process.

related.title: の極み
related.meaning: Ultimate, Extreme, Utter, Epitome, Peak
related.comparison: Though they share similar origins and both relate to the idea of extremity, these expressions differ in usage. の極み has more specific use cases, following nouns and coming at the end of a sentence or phrase to mean 'the epitome/peak of', e.g. 幸福の極み 'the peak of happiness'. In contrast, 極まりない and 極まる typically follow adjectives, with the resulting phrase preceding a noun to mean 'extremely', e.g. 失礼（極まる・極まりない）話 'a matter that is extremely (the height of) discourtesy'. Both can be used negatively or positively.

related.title: によって・による
related.meaning: By means of, According to, Depending on, Because of, Due to
related.comparison: Both describe how one thing leads to another, but with different nuance. 次第 emphasizes immediacy, e.g. 方法が決まり次第連絡します ('I'll contact you as soon as the method is decided.') によって・による shows variation or dependence, e.g. 方法によって結果が変わる ('The result changes depending on the method.')

related.title: たとたんに
related.meaning: The instant or moment, As soon as, Just as
related.comparison: Both describe sequences where one action quickly follows another, but differ in tone and formality. 次第 is formal and indicates that an action will follow as soon as another is completed, e.g. '会議が終わり次第、報告します' ('I'll report as soon as the meeting ends'). たとたんに emphasizes sudden or unexpected results, often in conversation, e.g. 'ドアを開けたとたんに猫が飛び出した' ('The moment I opened the door, the cat jumped out').

related.title: にかかわる
related.meaning: To relate to, To have to do with, Concerning, Depends on
related.comparison: 次第 emphasizes timing or sequencing, used to express that one action is a condition for the next, which will follow immediately. For example, 準備ができ次第出発します ('As soon as preparations are done, we'll depart'). にかかわる focuses on relevance or importance, describing how something affects or relates to another matter. For example, 命にかかわる問題 ('a matter of life').

related.title: 次第だ・次第で
related.meaning: Depending on, So
related.comparison: Both of these expressions use 次第 but differ slightly in usage. 次第 alone attaches to verb stems to emphasize timing or sequencing, expressing that one action is a condition for the next. For example, 終わり次第連絡します ('I'll contact you as soon as I'm finished'). 次第だ・次第で, by contrast, introduces conditional relationships or dependence. For example, 努力次第で結果は変わる ('The results will depend on your effort').

related.title: なり
related.meaning: As soon as, The moment, When
related.comparison: Both なり and 次第 indicate immediacy but differ in tone and nuance. なり is a neutral expression suggesting a spontaneous or automatic reaction. For example, 布団に入るなり寝た ('He fell asleep as soon as he got in his futon'). 次第 has a more formal tone and is often used in business contexts to show that something happens immediately after a condition is met. For example, 終わり次第ご連絡します ('I'll contact you as soon as I'm finished').

related.title: 直ちに
related.meaning: At once, Immediately, As soon as
related.comparison: Both 直ちに and 次第 can imply prompt timing, but differ in grammatical usage and nuance. 直ちに is used before phrases and emphasizes urgency, e.g. '直ちに避難してください' ('Please evacuate immediately'). 次第 attaches to verb stems to suggest one action happening immediately after another, e.g. '準備ができ次第、出発します' ('We'll leave as soon as we're ready').

related.title: その結果
related.meaning: As a result
related.comparison: Both expressions use 結果 to express a resulting situation or condition, like the English 'as a result', but differ in how they connect to the cause. その結果 is used at the beginning of a sentence and points back to something just mentioned, while 結果・の結果 is used in mid-sentence and connects directly to an explicitly mentioned cause, either by attaching to a verb or to a noun with の.

antonyms.title: と考えられる
antonyms.meaning: Can be considered as, Is thought to be, One can think that, It is conceivable that
antonyms.comparison: These two forms of the verb 考える express opposite sentiments. 考えられない (lit. 'one cannot think') expresses strong disbelief or the idea that something is inconceivable, e.g. 'そんなことは考えられない' ('I can't imagine such a thing'). と考えられる, on the other hand, expresses a reasoned or likely conclusion, e.g. 'これは自然な結果だと考えられる' ('This can be considered a natural result').

antonyms.title: とかんがえられている
antonyms.slug: とかんがえられている
antonyms.meaning: Is considered, Is thought of as
antonyms.comparison: These two forms of the verb 考える express opposite sentiments. 考えられない (lit. 'one cannot think') expresses strong disbelief or the idea that something is inconceivable, e.g. 'そんなことは考えられない' ('I can't imagine such a thing'). In contrast, と考えられている indicates a commonly accepted or reasoned belief, often used in formal writing to make statements less direct, e.g. 'これは効果的だと考えられている' ('This is considered effective').

related.title: がみられる
related.meaning: Can see, Can be seen, Can observe, Can be observed, Be evident, Chance to see something
related.comparison: Both みられる and 見える mean 'can see' (potential form) but differ in nuance. 見える means something is visible, while 見られる refers to the ability to watch or view. For example, stars on a clear night would be 見える, but being able to watch a movie on a streaming service would be 見られる.

related.title: れる・られる (Potential)
related.meaning: To be able, Can (Potential)
related.comparison: Both 見える and 見られる mean 'can see' (potential form) but their nuances differ. 見える describes something being visible, while 見られる refers to the ability to view something. For example, stars in the sky are 見える, but being able to watch a movie on a streaming service would be 見られる.

related.title: つまり
related.meaning: In short, To sum up, In other words
related.comparison: つまり and 要するに both signal a rephrasing or summary, often translated as 'in other words' or 'to sum up'. つまり is more casual and common in conversation, e.g. つまり、行かないってこと？ ('So, you're not going?'). 要するに is more formal and often used in writing or speeches, e.g. 要するに無理だということです ('To sum up, it's impossible').

related.title: 即ち
related.meaning: In other words, That is, Namely
related.comparison: 即ち and 要するに both help clarify or summarize a statement, but they differ in function. 即ち gives a formal restatement or definition, e.g. 日本の首都、即ち東京 'the capital of Japan, that is to say Tokyo'. 要するに summarizes the main point and can be used in speech or semi-formal writing, e.g. 要するに時間がない ('To sum it up, we have no time.').

related.title: れる・られる (Potential)
related.meaning: To be able, Can (Potential)
related.comparison: Both 聞こえる and 聞ける can mean 'can hear,' but differ in nuance. 聞こえる refers to a sound being audible, while 聞ける indicates the ability to hear or listen. For example, a bird chirping nearby would be 聞こえる, but being able to stream a song would be 聞ける.

related.title: がする
related.meaning: To give off, Smell, Sound, Taste, Sensation
related.comparison: がする and 聞こえる both express sensory experiences, but differ in nuance.  がする is used with sensory words, e.g. 音がする 'I hear', 匂いがする 'I smell', and 味がする 'I taste' (literally, 'It gives off a sound/smell/taste.'). 聞こえる focuses specifically on sound and means 'to be heard' or 'to sound like', e.g. 音楽が聞こえる ('I hear music.') or 音楽に聞こえる ('It sounds like music.').

synonyms.title: は言うまでもない ①
synonyms.meaning: It goes without saying (that), (It is) needless to say, I don't need to remind you
synonyms.comparison: These are slight variations of the same expression meaning 'to go without saying'. は言うまでもない① follows a topic to say '(A) goes without saying'. 言うまでもない②, often seen in forms like 言うまでもないことだけど or 言うまでもなく, comes at the start of a phrase to mean 'Needless to say, (A)' or 'It goes without saying, but (A).'

related.title: でも
related.meaning: Or something,  Any... (with question words), No matter (who)
related.comparison: The 誰か・どこか・誰も・どこも pattern combines question words like 誰 ('who') and どこ ('where') with particles to express ideas like 'someone' or 'somewhere.' Similarly, でも can also be attached to question words to express the idea of 'anyone' (誰でも), 'anywhere' (どこでも), and so forth.

related.title: 誰
related.meaning: Who
related.comparison: 誰 is a noun that refers to an unknown person, similar to the English 'who.' A common use is to ask about someone's identity, as in あの人は誰ですか ('Who is that person?') 誰 can also combine with other particles to express concepts like 誰か ('somebody'), 誰の ('whose'), and 誰も ('everyone' or 'no one' when used with a negative).

related.title: Question-phrase + か
related.meaning: Including questions within sentences
related.comparison: The 誰か・どこか・誰も・どこも pattern combines question words like 誰 ('who') and どこ ('where') with particles to express ideas like 'someone' or 'somewhere.' These forms are also related to how the particle か is used with question phrases in embedded questions. For example, 誰が来るか分かりますか？ means 'Do you know who will come?'

related.title: なん + counter + か
related.meaning: Some, Several, A few
related.comparison: なん + counter + か is a pattern that combines the question word 何 (なん or なに) with a counter to mean 'a few (A)' or 'some amount of (A)'. For example, 何人か 'a few people.' This is essentially the same as the question word + particle, or 誰か・どこか・誰も・どこも, structure, which is used in expressions such as 何か 'something', 誰か 'someone', and so forth.

related.title: なにか・なにも
related.meaning: Something, Nothing, Anything, Not anything, Some kind of, Not any kind of
related.comparison: Both なにか・なにも and 誰か・どこか・誰も・どこも combine question words (like なに, 誰, どこ) with particles to express ideas such as 'something,' 'nothing,' 'someone,' or 'somewhere.' Their meanings shift depending on whether the sentence is affirmative or negative.

related.title: 込む ②
related.meaning: Remain, To do thoroughly, Deeply, Intensely
related.comparison: 込む can be added to verb stems to express two nuances. The first is inward or inserted movement, as in 放り込む ('throw in') or 読み込む ('read in', such as reading data into a computer). The second suggests intensity, depth, or duration of an action, as in 考え込む ('think deeply') or 座り込む ('sit down and refuse to move').

related.title: 切る
related.meaning: To do something completely to the end, Utterly, Resolutely
related.comparison: Both can attach to verbs to form compound words. 切る emphasizes full completion or decisiveness, e.g. '言い切る' ('say definitively') or '読み切る' ('finish reading'), focusing on doing something thoroughly to its end. 込む conveys depth or intensity of action, e.g. '考え込む' ('get lost in thought'), highlighting depth rather than finality.

related.title: 込む ①
related.meaning: To go into, To put into
related.comparison: 込む can be added to verb stems to express two nuances. The first is inward or inserted movement, as in 放り込む ('throw in') or 読み込む ('read in', such as reading data into a computer). The second suggests intensity, depth, or duration of an action, as in 考え込む ('think deeply') or 座り込む ('sit down and refuse to move').

related.title: おわる
related.meaning: To finish, To end
related.comparison: Both 込む and おわる attach to verb stems but express different nuances. おわる simply marks the end of an action, e.g. ケーキを食べおわった ('I finished eating the cake.') or 映画を観おわった ('I finished watching a movie.'). 込む adds the sense of doing something thoroughly and staying in that state, e.g. 黙り込んだ ('He fell completely silent and stayed that way.').

related.title: に反して
related.meaning: Go against, Contrary to
related.comparison: 逆に is colloquial, meaning on the contrary or contrary to expectations, often introducing a reversal, as in 応援したのに逆に怒られた (I cheered them on, but on the contrary, they got angry). に反して means contrary to or against, used with rules, intentions, or expectations, as in 規則に反して行動した (He acted against the rules). 逆に can stand alone, while に反して must follow something.

related.title: むしろ
related.meaning: Rather, Instead, Over
related.comparison: むしろ and 逆に both express contrast, but with different nuances. むしろ presents a preference or clarification, e.g. 話すよりむしろ相手の話を聞きたい ('I'd rather listen than talk'). 逆に highlights an outcome opposite to what was expected, e.g. 喜んでくれると思ったが逆に怒られた ('I thought they'd be happy, but they got mad instead').

related.title: 却って
related.meaning: All the more, On the contrary, Conversely
related.comparison: 却って and 逆に both express something being counter to expectations, like the English 'on the contrary'. 却って is more formal, and occurs primarily in writing in the context of a full sentence or explanation. In contrast, 逆に is quite casual, and can be used in the same way as 却って, but also as an interjection at the beginning of a sentence or phrase, e.g. 逆にたかし君はどう思いますか？ ('On the contrary, what do you think, Takashi?').

related.title: Verb + て
related.meaning: And, Then (Linking events)
related.comparison: Both 連用形 and the て form connect sequential actions, but 連用形 sounds more formal and is often used in writing. For example, 起きて becomes 起き, e.g. ８時に起きて、朝ご飯を食べました 'I got up at 8 o'clock, then ate breakfast.' → ８時に起き、朝ご飯を食べました. (Same meaning, different register.)

related.title: Adjective + て・Noun + で
related.meaning: And... (Conjunctive)
related.comparison: 連用形 also replaces て or で after adjectives and nouns in formal writing. For example, 優しくて becomes 優しく, e.g. とても優しくて、綺麗です 'She's very kind, and pretty.' → とても優しく、綺麗です. (Same meaning, but more formal and literary.)

related.title: ではなくて・じゃなくて
related.slug: ではなくて-じゃなくて
related.meaning: Not A but B, Conjunctive
related.comparison: 連用形 is a more formal way to connect phrases or clauses, often used in writing. It shares the same basic meaning as the て form. In the case of ではなくて・じゃなくて, the 連用形 becomes ではなく・じゃなく, e.g. 病院ではなく、図書館です 'It's not a hospital, but a library.' (Same meaning, just more formal in tone.)

related.title: がけに
related.meaning: On the way to, As you go
related.comparison: がけに and 途中に・途中で are close in meaning and often interchangeable. The main difference is grammatical connection. がけに attaches to the stem of verbs, as in 帰りがけに (on the way home). 途中に・途中で attaches to the dictionary form of verbs, as in 帰る途中で (on the way home), or to nouns with の, as in 会議の途中に (in the middle of the meeting).

related.title: ているところだ
related.meaning: In the process of doing ~ right now/at this moment
related.comparison: ているところだ conveys that an action is currently in progress, often with a sense of immediate involvement, e.g. 朝ご飯を食べているところだ ('I'm in the middle of eating breakfast.'). In contrast, 途中に・途中で expresses doing something 'on the way' or 'in the middle' of another activity, e.g. 通勤の途中で朝ご飯を食べた ('I ate breakfast on my commute to work.').

related.title: 最中に
related.meaning: In the middle of
related.comparison: 最中に and 途中に・途中で both mean 'in the middle of', but differ in nuance. 最中に emphasizes being right in the middle of something, e.g. 晩ご飯を作っている最中に電話が鳴った ('The phone rang when I was right in the middle of making dinner.') 途中に・途中で can overlap but also means 'on the way', e.g. 学校に行く途中で友達に会った ('I ran into a friend on the way to school.')

related.title: いよいよ
related.meaning: At last, Finally, More and more
related.comparison: Both 遂に and いよいよ can mean 'finally', but with different focus. 遂に indicates that something has occurred at last, often after struggle or delay, e.g. '遂に大学に入った' ('I finally entered university'). いよいよ emphasizes an approaching climax or moment of anticipation, e.g. 'いよいよ卒業の日が来た' ('Graduation day has finally arrived').

related.title: とうとう
related.meaning: Finally, After all
related.comparison: Both とうとう and 遂に mean 'finally', but with different nuances. とうとう emphasizes the long effort or struggle leading up to the result, while 遂に focuses more on the result itself and what may follow. In other words, both とうとう大学生になった and 遂に大学生になった mean 'I finally became a university student.', but the former is more backward-looking, while the latter is forward-looking.

related.title: に関する・に関して
related.meaning: Related to, About, Regarding
related.comparison: Both express the idea of connection, but differ in usage and nuance. に関する・に関して attaches to nouns to mean 'about (A)' or 'regarding (A)', e.g. 歴史に関する本 ('a book about history') or 歴史に関して勉強する ('study about history'). 関係がある conveys a looser connection, meaning 'is related to (A)', and unlike に関する・に関して, can be used with と as well as に, in sentence-final position, or with ない to express a lack of connection.

related.title: に限る
related.meaning: Nothing better than, There's nothing like, Limited to
related.comparison: に限る and 限り both relate to boundaries or constraints, but serve different functions. に限る expresses the speaker's judgment that something is the best or most suitable, e.g. '寒い日は鍋に限る' ('Nothing beats hot pot on a cold day'). 限り sets a condition or scope, meaning 'as long as' or 'while', e.g. '知っている限り' ('as far as I know').

related.title: とは限らない
related.meaning: Not necessarily so, Is not always true, There is no guarantee that
related.comparison: These two expressions derived from 限る contrast in meaning. とは限らない expresses that something is not necessarily the case, e.g. '雨だからといって中止とは限らない' ('Just because it's raining doesn't mean it will be canceled'). 限り indicates a bounded condition or extent, e.g. '知っている限り' ('as far as I know').

related.title: からには
related.meaning: As long as, Since, Given
related.comparison: Both express conditional relationships but with different focus. からには conveys cause-and-effect or obligation, as in 約束したからには守るべきだ (Since I made a promise, I should keep it). 限り emphasizes temporal or situational boundaries, meaning as long as or while, as in 生きている限り希望はある (As long as you live, there is hope).

related.title: さえ〜ば
related.meaning: If only…then, As long as..., Provided..., Only thing ... needs for ... is ...
related.comparison: Both expressions can be translated as 'as long as (A)', but they differ in nuance. さえ〜ば indicates that a single condition is sufficient, e.g. 努力さえすればうまくいく ('As long as you try, things will go well'). 限り means 'as long as' in the sense of duration or ongoing state, e.g. 生きている限り夢を追いたい ('As long as I live, I want to chase my dream').

related.title: めったに〜ない
related.meaning: Hardly, Rarely, Seldom
related.comparison: Both ろくに〜ない and めったに〜ない express the idea of scarcity, but differ in nuance and context. めったに〜ない refers to the rarity of an action or event, e.g. 'めったに外出しない' ('rarely go out'). ろくに〜ない emphasizes inadequacy or insufficiency, often judging something as not measuring up, e.g. 'ろくに話せない' ('barely able to speak').

related.title: に際して
related.meaning: On the occasion of, At the time of
related.comparison: Both mean at the time of, but differ in nuance. 際に is a formal variant of 時に and applies broadly to past or present, as in 出発の際に写真を撮った (We took a photo at the time of departure). に際して is more formal, limited to present or future occasions, and highlights the special or official nature of the event, as in 契約に際して署名が必要だ (A signature is required at the time of signing a contract).

related.title: にあたり・にあたって
related.meaning: When, When doing something, On the occasion, At the time
related.comparison: にあたり・にあたって is used for important occasions that involve preparation or intention, as in 出発にあたり挨拶をした (I gave a greeting on the occasion of departure). 際に simply means when or at the time of, describing something that happens concurrently, as in 出発の際に見送ってくれた (They saw me off at the time of departure).

related.title: とき
related.meaning: When, At the time of
related.comparison: Both とき and 際に express the time of an action or event, but with different nuances and levels of formality. とき is common in everyday conversation and broadly used, like the English 'when'. 際に is more formal and often appears in official contexts, such as instructions, advice, or warnings related to specific events.

related.title: ついでに
related.meaning: While you are at it, On the occasion
related.comparison: Both ついでに and 際に refer to additional actions tied to another event, but differ in tone. ついでに is an everyday expression for when something is done while convenient, e.g. '買い物のついでに郵便局に寄った' ('I stopped at the post office while shopping'). 際に is more formal and emphasizes a particular occasion, e.g. '来日の際にご連絡ください' ('Please contact us when you come to Japan').

related.title: 折には
related.meaning: When, Occasion, Opportunity, Time
related.comparison: Both mark occasions, but differ in nuance. 折には emphasizes opportunity or timing, often with verbs of judgment or decision, as in 来日の折にはぜひお立ち寄りください (Please visit when you come to Japan). 際に is more neutral, simply indicating when or at the time of, without implying seizing an opportunity.

related.title: みたいに・みたいな
related.meaning: Like, Similar to, Resembling
related.comparison: 風 (ふう) and みたいに・みたいな both express resemblance or appearance. 風 attaches directly to nouns, e.g. アメリカ風料理 'American-style food' and can sound more formal. みたいな also attaches to nouns, but is more casual and makes a more straightforward comparison, e.g. ハンバーガーみたいな料理 = 'food like a hamburger'.

related.title: 的
related.meaning: ~ly・~like・~al, Typical
related.comparison: 風 (ふう) and 的 (てき) are suffixes that turn nouns into descriptors. 風 suggests a style or 'air,' and is more limited in use, e.g. 日本風 or 和風 'Japanese-style'. 的 is broader, used for both adjectives and adverbs, e.g. 感情的 'emotional' and 定期的に 'regularly'.

related.title: らしい ②
related.meaning: typical of, -ish・like, appropriate for, becoming of, befitting
related.comparison: 風 and らしい② both express resemblance but differ in nuance. 風 means something has the 'air' or appearance of a type, e.g. 日本風建築 'Japanese-style architecture'. らしい② implies it embodies the ideal of that category, e.g. 日本らしい建築 implies 'quintessential Japanese architecture'.

related.title: Noun＋型
related.meaning: Model, Type, Pattern, Kind, Shape, Size(d)
related.comparison: Both describe style or appearance, but with different nuance. 型 refers to a concrete type, model, or category, e.g. 新型のスマホ ('A new model of smartphone') 風 suggests resemblance or outward style, often subjective or superficial, e.g. 昔風の家 ('A house in the old-fashioned style')

related.title: ように・ような
related.meaning: As・like, Just like
related.comparison: 風 (ふう) and ように・ような both express resemblance. 風 attaches directly to nouns, e.g. アメリカ風料理 'American-style food', while ような requires の before nouns, e.g. ハンバーガーのような料理 'food like a hamburger'. 風 often has a more stylistic nuance, while ような is a more neutral comparison.

related.title: の間に
related.meaning: While, During, Between, Period
related.comparison: ～ところに・～ところへ and の間に both express the idea of 'while', but with different nuance. ～ところに／へ emphasizes a scene or moment into which something else happens, e.g. ゲームをしているところへ兄が部屋に入ってきた ('While I was playing a game, my brother entered the room.') の間に, by contrast, simply describes one event taking place during another, without emphasizing a specific situation or setting.

related.title: が早いか
related.meaning: As soon as, No sooner than, The instant that, No sooner had
related.comparison: Both can express the meaning of 'as soon as (A), (B)', but with different usage and nuance. が早いか marks an action that is followed immediately by another, often sudden or dramatic, meaning ‘no sooner than,’ as in ドアを開けるが早いか、子どもが飛び出してきた ('No sooner had I opened the door than a child burst out.'). ～ところに・～ところへ describes another event occurring at a certain stage of an action, focusing on coincidence in time rather than instant reaction, as in 出かけようとしたところに電話がかかってきた ('Just as I was about to go out, I got a phone call.').

related.title: ついでに
related.meaning: While you are at it, On the occasion
related.comparison: Both ついでに and ～ところに・～ところへ relate to timing, but from different angles. ついでに expresses performing a second action while it's convenient, e.g. '買い物のついでに郵便局に寄った' ('I stopped at the post office while shopping'). ～ところに・～ところへ focuses on when something happens, often unexpectedly, e.g. '出かけようとしたところに電話がかかってきた' ('Just as I was about to leave, the phone rang').

related.title: 最中に
related.meaning: In the middle of
related.comparison: Both describe an interruption during an action, but with different focus. ～ところに・～ところへ highlights timing or the moment of interruption, e.g. 出かけようとしたところに電話がかかってきた ('Just as I was about to leave, the phone rang.') 最中に stresses that the action was in full progress, e.g. 晩ご飯を作っている最中に電話が鳴った ('The phone rang when I was right in the middle of making dinner.')

related.title: ているあいだに
related.meaning: While, During the time that ~
related.comparison: Both express something happening during an ongoing action, but differ in focus. Compared to ているあいだに, which states in a straightforward manner one thing happening in the middle of another thing, ～ところに・～ところへplaces more of a focus on the 'scene' or situation, and is therefore often used to express a surprising or disturbing event.

related.title: ているところだ
related.meaning: In the process of doing ~ right now/at this moment
related.comparison: ているところだ conveys that an action is in progress at that moment, with the nuance that the speaker is occupied with it, e.g. ゲームをしているところだ ('I'm in the middle of playing a video game.'). In contrast, ～ところに・～ところへ describes something happening at the moment of another action, e.g. ゲームをしているところに母に呼ばれた ('I was called by my mother while I was in the middle of playing a game.').

synonyms.title: ～たまでだ
synonyms.comparison: These are essentially the same grammar pattern, with the difference in nuance coming from the verb tense. ～たまでだ is typically used to downplay a completed action, e.g. 自分の意見を述べたまでだ ('All I did was express my opinion.), while ～るまでだ generally implies the speaker will 'just do (something)' in the face of a bad situation, e.g. 誰も教えてくれないなら、自分で調べるまでだ ('If no one will teach me, I'll just look it up myself'). Both are typically used to refer to one's own actions or those of someone in one's in-group.

related.title: それまでだ
related.meaning: If… then it is all in vain, It's done, It is useless, No point, It is a waste, That's it, It is all gone,
related.comparison: Both involve a limit, but the type of limit is different. それまでだ is an evaluative expression meaning “that’s the end,” used to judge that nothing more can be done if a crucial condition is lost, as in 信頼を失ったらそれまでだ (‘If you lose trust, that’s the end.’). By contrast, Vるまでだ (and までのことだ) expresses resignation, meaning “one can only but (B),” often used with なら to show that (B) is the only remaining option in a negative situation, as in 誰にも教えてもらえないなら、ネットで調べてみるまでだ (‘If no one will teach me, I’ll just have to look it up online.’).
